**Interpretation:** Each unit of time a process spends in the **Ready** state (waiting for CPU) directly inflates its Turnaround Time. State transitions to/from the **Ready** queue are the primary lever for optimizing user-perceived latency.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State Transition Triggers

The following table provides an exhaustive mapping of every legal state transition, the **triggering event**, and the **kernel subsystem** responsible.

| # | Transition | Triggering Event | Kernel Component |
|---|------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | New → Ready | Long-term scheduler admits the job | Job Scheduler |
| 2 | Ready → Running | Dispatcher loads PCB onto CPU | Short-term Scheduler / Dispatcher |
| 3 | Running → Ready | Timer interrupt fires (preemption) OR voluntary yield | Interrupt Handler |
| 4 | Running → Blocked | System call for I/O, `wait()` on semaphore, page fault | Device Driver / VMM |
| 5 | Blocked → Ready | I/O completion interrupt, signal, VMM page-in complete | Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) |
| 6 | Running → Terminated | `exit()` syscall, fatal signal (SIGKILL), unhandled exception | Process Termination Module |
| 7 | Ready → Suspended-Ready | Memory pressure; medium-term scheduler decides to swap | Medium-term Scheduler |
| 8 | Blocked → Suspended-Blocked | Swap out a blocked process to reclaim more memory | Medium-term Scheduler |
| 9 | Suspended-Ready → Ready | Swap-in completes | Medium-term Scheduler |
| 10 | Suspended-Blocked → Suspended-Ready | Awaited event occurs while process is on disk | Event Notification Service |
| 11 | New → Terminated (Abort) | Memory allocation failure, invalid executable | Loader / Process Init |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: State Traversal of a Single Process

**Problem:** A process arrives at time $t = 0$ ms. It executes on the CPU for 8 ms, performs 12 ms of disk I/O, executes for 4 more ms, then performs 4 ms of network I/O, and finally terminates. Calculate the total time spent in each state.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Time in New state:} \quad T_{new} &\approx 0 \text{ ms (instantaneous admission)} \\
\text{Time in Ready (initial):} \quad T_{ready,1} &= 0 \text{ ms (dispatched immediately)} \\
\text{Time in Running (1st):} \quad T_{run,1} &= 8 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Time in Blocked (1st):} \quad T_{block,1} &= 12 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Time in Ready (2nd):} \quad T_{ready,2} &= 1 \text{ ms (other process in queue)} \\
\text{Time in Running (2nd):} \quad T_{run,2} &= 4 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Time in Blocked (2nd):} \quad T_{block,2} &= 4 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Time in Ready (3rd):} \quad T_{ready,3} &= 2 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Time in Running (3rd):} \quad T_{run,3} &\approx 0 \text{ ms (just exits)} \\
\text{Total TAT:} \quad TAT &= 8 + 12 + 1 + 4 + 4 + 2 = 31 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sanity Check:** The process traversed states: New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Ready → Running → Blocked → Ready → Running → Terminated. This is a 9-edge path through the state graph, consistent with the triggers in the matrix above.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical 7-State Process Diagram

The following Mermaid state diagram depicts the **complete process state model** (with Suspended states) that is the gold-standard answer for KTU Part B questions on this topic.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sNew
    sNew --> sReady : LongTerm Scheduler admits
    sReady --> sRunning : ShortTerm Scheduler dispatches
    sRunning --> sReady : Preemption / Time quantum expiry
    sRunning --> sBlocked : I/O request / wait on event
    sRunning --> sTerminated : Exit / Fatal signal
    sBlocked --> sReady : I/O completion / event occurs
    sReady --> sSusReady : MediumTerm swaps out
    sSusReady --> sReady : MediumTerm swaps in
    sBlocked --> sSusBlocked : MediumTerm swaps out
    sSusBlocked --> sSusReady : Event occurs while swapped
    sSusReady --> sRunning : Direct dispatch on swap-in
    sSusBlocked --> sBlocked : Swap-in before event resolves
    sTerminated --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of State Management

This diagram maps the **subsystems and queues** that physically implement the abstract state machine. It is useful when answering questions on the "internal mechanism" of process state management.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Newly Created Process] --> B{Long-Term Scheduler}
    B -->|Admits| C[Ready Queue in Main Memory]
    C -->|Dispatch| D[CPU Core]
    D -->|Preempted / Yielded| C
    D -->|I/O request| E[Device Wait Queue]
    D -->|Exit syscall| F[Termination Handler]
    E -->|I/O Complete| C
    C -->|Memory Pressure| G[Medium-Term Swapper]
    E -->|Memory Pressure| G
    G -->|Swap Out| H[Suspended Ready Queue on Disk]
    G -->|Swap Out| I[Suspended Blocked Queue on Disk]
    H -->|Swap In| C
    I -->|Event Resolved| H
    F --> J[Process Table Cleanup]
```

## 4.3 Simplified 5-State Diagram (For Quick Viva Answers)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stNew
    stNew --> stReady : Admit
    stReady --> stRunning : Dispatch
    stRunning --> stReady : Preempt / Timeout
    stRunning --> stBlocked : I/O or Event Wait
    stRunning --> stExit : Terminate
    stBlocked --> stReady : I/O Complete
    stExit --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Use the **7-state diagram** for full 14-mark answers. The **5-state diagram** is acceptable for 7-mark sub-parts. Avoid the 2-state (Running / Not Running) model — it is considered too primitive for university examinations and loses 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 1]`
**Define a process. List the various states of a process.**

**Model Answer:**

A **process** is a program in execution. It is more than just the program code; it includes the current activity, program counter, CPU registers, stack, heap, data section, and the Process Control Block (PCB).

The various states of a process are:
1. **New** — Process is being created.
2. **Ready** — Process is waiting in main memory to be assigned to the CPU.
3. **Running** — Instructions are being executed by the CPU.
4. **Blocked / Waiting** — Process is waiting for some event (typically I/O completion) to occur.
5. **Suspended Ready** — Process has been swapped out of main memory but is ready to execute.
6. **Suspended Blocked** — Process is swapped out and is also waiting for an event.
7. **Terminated** — Process has finished execution and is being deallocated.

*(Full 3 marks awarded only if the PCB is briefly mentioned and at least 5 states are listed with one-line descriptions.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series 2]`
**Differentiate between a program and a process.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Program | Process |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Definition** | Static set of instructions | Program in execution |
| **Storage** | Resides on disk (passive) | Resides in memory (active) |
| **Lifetime** | Exists until deleted | Exists from admit to terminate |
| **Resources** | None allocated | Has allocated PCB, memory, I/O |
| **Entity Type** | Passive | Active |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat state transition diagram, explain the **seven-state process model**. Discuss the role of the **medium-term scheduler** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Process Control Block (PCB)** and the steps involved in a **context switch**. Why is context switching considered a pure overhead? **(7 marks)**

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **five-state process model** with a state transition diagram. Explain how the **long-term, short-term, and medium-term schedulers** differ in their functionality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has 4 processes $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ with the following arrival times and CPU bursts (in ms). Compute the **average Turnaround Time** and **average Waiting Time** under **FCFS** scheduling. Also draw the **Gantt chart** and state which state each process occupies at time $t = 5$ ms. **(7 marks)**

| Process | Arrival Time | CPU Burst (ms) | I/O Wait (ms) |
|---------|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| P1      | 0            | 5              | 3              |
| P2      | 1            | 3              | 0              |
| P3      | 2            | 2              | 4              |
| P4      | 3            | 4              | 2              |

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

### Solution to Question A(a):

**State Transition Diagram:** *(Refer to the 7-state Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)*

**Explanation:**

1. **New → Ready:** Long-term scheduler admits the process into the ready queue after allocating memory.
2. **Ready → Running:** Short-term scheduler dispatches the process to the CPU.
3. **Running → Ready:** Preemption due to time quantum expiry.
4. **Running → Blocked:** Process performs an I/O request or waits on a semaphore.
5. **Blocked → Ready:** The awaited I/O or event completes.
6. **Running → Terminated:** Normal exit or kill signal.
7. **Ready ↔ Suspended-Ready / Blocked ↔ Suspended-Blocked:** The medium-term scheduler swaps processes to optimize memory usage.

**Role of Medium-Term Scheduler:**
- Handles the **suspension and resumption** of processes.
- **Swapping out:** Removes a process from main memory to secondary storage, freeing memory for other processes.
- **Swapping in:** Restores the swapped process when conditions (e.g., memory available, event completed) are met.
- Improves the **degree of multiprogramming** and overall system throughput.
- Particularly useful in systems with **insufficient physical memory**.

### Solution to Question A(b):

**Process Control Block (PCB) — Definition:** A data structure maintained by the OS for every process. Contains:
- Process ID, State, Program Counter, CPU Registers
- Memory Limits, Open File Table, Scheduling Information, I/O Status

**Steps in Context Switch:**

1. The OS receives a **trigger** (timer interrupt, system call, I/O completion).
2. The CPU **saves** the current process's registers, PC, and stack pointer into its PCB.
3. The process state is updated to **Ready** or **Blocked** as applicable.
4. The **scheduler** selects the next process from the ready queue.
5. The **dispatcher** loads the new process's PCB context (registers, PC, page table base) into the CPU.
6. Execution **resumes** for the new process.

**Why is it pure overhead?** Because the CPU does **no useful user work** during the switch. The time is spent entirely saving and loading state, invalidating caches, and updating memory maps. Hence, a high context-switch rate can severely degrade performance.

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B

### Solution to Question B(a):

*(Diagram from Section 4.3 applies.)* The **five-state model** includes New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Terminated.

**Differences between Schedulers:**

| Criterion | Long-Term | Short-Term | Medium-Term |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| **Frequency** | Low | Very high | Moderate |
| **Function** | Job admission | CPU dispatch | Swap in/out |
| **Controls** | Multiprogramming degree | CPU allocation | Memory contention |
| **Speed Required** | Moderate | Extremely fast | Moderate |
| **Where Used** | Batch systems | All systems | Memory-constrained systems |

### Solution to Question B(b):

**FCFS Gantt Chart Construction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Order of execution (by arrival):} \quad P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4 \\
\text{Gantt Chart:} \quad [P_1: 0-5] \; [P_2: 5-8] \; [P_3: 8-10] \; [P_4: 10-14]
\end{aligned}$$

**Completion and Turnaround Times:**

| Process | Arrival | Burst | Completion | TAT = C - A | Waiting Time = TAT - Burst |
|---------|---------|-------|------------|-------------|----------------------------|
| P1      | 0       | 5     | 5          | 5           | 0                          |
| P2      | 1       | 3     | 8          | 7           | 4                          |
| P3      | 2       | 2     | 10         | 8           | 6                          |
| P4      | 3       | 4     | 14         | 11          | 7                          |

**Averages:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{5 + 7 + 8 + 11}{4} = \frac{31}{4} = 7.75 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 4 + 6 + 7}{4} = \frac{17}{4} = 4.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**State of each process at $t = 5$ ms:**
- **P1** — Just completed → **Terminated**.
- **P2** — Currently executing → **Running**.
- **P3** — Has arrived (at $t=2$) and is queued → **Ready**.
- **P4** — Has arrived (at $t=3$) and is queued → **Ready**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "Suspended" with "Blocked":** Suspended = swapped to disk; Blocked = in main memory waiting for I/O. Mixing them loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Preemption arrow:** Many students omit `Running → Ready` (timeout). The complete diagram **must** include it.
> 3. **Arithmetic mistakes in Gantt charts:** Double-check boundaries. P1 ending at $t=5$ means P2 starts at exactly $t=5$, not $t=6$.
> 4. **Not labeling the queues:** Always mention that the Ready queue is a *circular linked list* and Blocked processes are placed in *device-specific queues*. This shows depth.
> 5. **Confusing PCB with Process Table:** The **Process Table** is an array of PCB pointers; each **PCB** is one entry.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process = Program in execution**; tracked via a **PCB**.
- Six (or seven with suspended) canonical states: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended-Ready, Suspended-Blocked, Terminated**.
- The **short-term scheduler** drives the most frequent transition: Ready ↔ Running.
- The **medium-term scheduler** uniquely handles the Suspended states.
- A process in the **Running** state **cannot directly** go to a Suspended state — it must first become Ready or Blocked.
- **Context switching** is pure overhead; it consumes CPU cycles without doing useful user work.
- For university answers, always include the **state transition diagram** and label every arrow with the trigger.
- Know the formulas for **TAT, WT, RT, and CPU Utilization** — they are tested alongside state questions.
- Linux maps these states into `task_struct` flags: `TASK_RUNNING`, `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE`, `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE`, etc.
- Real-world analogy: think of the OS scheduler as a hospital triage nurse managing patients (processes) across multiple rooms (CPU, I/O, waiting area).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Data Structures (in the Context of Operating Systems)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Data Structure** is a systematic way of organizing, storing, managing, and accessing data in computer memory so that it can be utilized efficiently by the Operating System (OS) for tasks such as **process scheduling, memory management, file system organization, and resource allocation**.

In an Operating System, every subsystem relies on carefully chosen data structures. The choice of data structure directly determines the **performance, scalability, and reliability** of the OS kernel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OS Engineers Care About Data Structures:**
> An OS must handle thousands of processes, I/O requests, and memory allocations **per second**. The wrong data structure choice can degrade system throughput from **millions of operations/sec** to mere **thousands**. Hence, the *Art of OS Design* is inseparable from the *Science of Data Structures*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **massive library** (the computer) with millions of books (data):

| Library Component | Data Structure Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Shelf of books in order | **Array** | Fast access if position is known |
| Books linked by index cards | **Linked List** | Easy insertion/deletion of books |
| Stack of trays in a cafeteria | **Stack (LIFO)** | Last plate placed is taken first |
| Queue at a billing counter | **Queue (FIFO)** | First person in line is served first |
| Library catalog folders | **Tree** | Hierarchical search by category |
| Barcode lookup system | **Hash Table** | O(1) book retrieval by barcode |

Just as a librarian picks the right organization system to retrieve books quickly, the OS kernel picks the right data structure to manage processes, memory, and files efficiently.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Data Structures

Data structures are broadly classified into two categories:

### A. **Primitive Data Structures**
These are basic, built-in types supported directly by hardware/language:
- `Integer`, `Float`, `Character`, `Boolean`, `Pointer`

### B. **Non-Primitive Data Structures**
These are derived from primitive types and are the focus of OS design:

1. **Linear Data Structures** — Elements arranged sequentially
   - Arrays
   - Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular)
   - Stacks
   - Queues

2. **Non-Linear Data Structures** — Elements arranged hierarchically or in networks
   - Trees (Binary, BST, AVL, B-Trees)
   - Graphs
   - Heaps

3. **Hash-Based Structures**
   - Hash Tables
   - Direct Access Tables

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — OS Subsystem ↔ Data Structure Mapping**
> - **Process Management** → Linked Lists, Queues
> - **Memory Management** → Arrays, Linked Lists, Trees
> - **File System** → Trees (directory hierarchy), Linked Lists (FAT)
> - **CPU Scheduling** → Queues, Heaps, Trees
> - **Symbol Table / Cache** → Hash Tables
> - **System Call Stack** → Stacks

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Data Structure Analysis

The following standard metrics are used universally to evaluate any data structure:

- **Time Complexity** — Number of operations performed (Big-O notation: $O(1)$, $O(\log n)$, $O(n)$, $O(n \log n)$, $O(n^2)$).
- **Space Complexity** — Amount of auxiliary memory required.
- **Cache Locality** — How well the structure utilizes CPU cache lines.
- **Contention** — Locking overhead in concurrent (multi-threaded) OS kernels.

---

## 1.5 Foundational Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Node** | A basic unit containing data and pointer(s) |
| **Pointer** | A variable storing the memory address of another node |
| **Head** | Pointer to the first node in a linked list |
| **Tail** | Pointer to the last node in a linked list |
| **Top** | Index of the topmost element in a stack |
| **Front / Rear** | Pointers for insertion/deletion in a queue |
| **Root** | Topmost node of a tree |
| **Leaf** | A node with no children in a tree |
| **Hash Function** | Maps keys to indices in a hash table |
| **Collision** | When two keys map to the same index |
| **Load Factor** ($\alpha$) | Ratio of stored elements to table size |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Data Structure

### 🔹 2.1.1 Arrays

An **Array** is a contiguous block of memory storing homogeneous elements accessed via an index.

**Operational Logic:**
- Memory is allocated as a single contiguous block.
- Random access using index in **O(1)** time.
- Insertion and deletion in the middle requires shifting elements → **O(n)**.

**In Operating Systems:**
Arrays are used in:
- **Process Control Blocks (PCB) tables** — Fixed-size array of process descriptors.
- **Page Tables** — Indexed by page number.
- **Disk Block Buffers** — Fixed-size I/O cache.

> [!NOTE]
> Arrays offer **O(1) access** but suffer from poor **O(n) insertion/deletion** cost and inflexible fixed sizing — a major reason OS kernels prefer linked lists for dynamic structures like ready queues.

---

### 🔹 2.1.2 Linked Lists

A **Linked List** is a linear collection of nodes, where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node.

**Variants:**
1. **Singly Linked List** — Each node points to the next only.
2. **Doubly Linked List** — Each node points to both next and previous.
3. **Circular Linked List** — Last node points back to the head.

**In Operating Systems:**
- **Linux Kernel** uses a doubly linked list (`list_head`) extensively to chain processes, files, devices, and modules.
- The **Free Memory List** (used in linked allocation) is implemented as a linked list of free blocks.
- **FAT (File Allocation Table)** uses linked allocation on disk.

**Why Linked Lists Dominate OS Design:**
- **Dynamic size** — no need to declare capacity in advance.
- **O(1) insertion/deletion** at known positions.
- **No memory fragmentation issues** like arrays.

---

### 🔹 2.1.3 Stacks

A **Stack** is a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** linear data structure supporting two primary operations:
- `PUSH` — Insert an element on top.
- `POP` — Remove the topmost element.

**In Operating Systems:**
- **Function call management** — Each function call creates a stack frame.
- **Recursion** — The OS uses the call stack to track recursive invocations.
- **Interrupt handling** — Context is saved on a stack.
- **Expression evaluation** — Postfix, prefix conversions.
- **Memory management** — Stack-based memory allocation (automatic variables).

---

### 🔹 2.1.4 Queues

A **Queue** is a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** linear data structure.
- `ENQUEUE` — Insert at the rear.
- `DEQUEUE` — Remove from the front.

**Variants:**
- **Circular Queue** — Rear wraps around to front; avoids wasted space.
- **Priority Queue** — Elements dequeued by priority (not arrival order).
- **Double-Ended Queue (Deque)** — Insertion/deletion at both ends.

**In Operating Systems:**
- **Ready Queue** — Holds processes waiting for CPU.
- **I/O Wait Queue** — Holds processes waiting for I/O devices.
- **Job Queue** — Holds all processes in the system.
- **Spooling Buffers** — Print queues, mail queues.
- **CPU Scheduling Algorithms** — FCFS, Round-Robin all use queues.

---

### 🔹 2.1.5 Trees

A **Tree** is a non-linear, hierarchical data structure with a root node and child subtrees.

**Important Variants for OS:**
- **Binary Search Tree (BST)** — For symbol tables, dictionary lookups.
- **B-Tree / B+ Tree** — Used in **file systems (NTFS, ext4, BTRFS)** and **database indexing** because they minimize disk I/O.
- **Heap** — A complete binary tree used in **priority queues** for CPU scheduling (used by `Priority Scheduling` and `Heap Sort`).
- **Red-Black Tree** — Self-balancing BST used in Linux kernel's CFS scheduler (via `rbtree`).

**Why Trees Matter in OS:**
- **File System Hierarchy** — Directories are organized as trees (or DAGs with hard links).
- **Process Tree** — Every Unix process has a parent; the entire system forms a tree rooted at `init` (PID 1).
- **Decision Trees** — Used in some OS algorithms like banker's algorithm visualization.

---

### 🔹 2.1.6 Hash Tables

A **Hash Table** stores key-value pairs using a **hash function** $h(k)$ that maps a key $k$ to an index in an array (called a bucket array).

**Collision Resolution Techniques:**
1. **Chaining (Open Hashing)** — Each bucket holds a linked list of collided entries.
2. **Open Addressing (Closed Hashing)** — Linear Probing, Quadratic Probing, Double Hashing.

**In Operating Systems:**
- **Symbol Tables** in compilers and loaders.
- **Page Table** — Hashed page tables for large address spaces.
- **Inode Tables** in file systems — Map filename to inode.
- **Process Descriptors** — Hashing by PID for O(1) lookup.
- **ARP Caches** in networking stack.

---

## 2.2 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU University Exams.**

### A. Array Address Calculation (1-D)

For an array $A$ of size $n$, declared at base address $B$, with each element occupying $w$ bytes:

$$\text{Address}(A[i]) = B + (i - \text{lower\_bound}) \times w$$

For zero-based indexing (most common in C/C++):

$$\text{Address}(A[i]) = B + i \times w$$

---

### B. Array Address Calculation (2-D)

**Row-Major Order** (used in C, C++, Python):

$$\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + ((i - r_1) \times n + (j - c_1)) \times w$$

where:
- $B$ = base address
- $r_1, c_1$ = lower bounds of row and column indices
- $n$ = number of columns
- $w$ = element size in bytes

**Column-Major Order** (used in FORTRAN, MATLAB):

$$\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + ((j - c_1) \times m + (i - r_1)) \times w$$

where $m$ = number of rows.

---

### C. Hash Table Performance

$$\alpha = \frac{n}{m} \quad \text{(Load Factor)}$$

where $n$ = number of stored keys, $m$ = number of slots.

**Average Search Complexity (Successful Search):**

$$T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}\right) \quad \text{[Linear Probing]}$$

$$T_{\text{avg}} = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} \quad \text{[Chaining]}$$

**Ideal Load Factor:** $\alpha < 0.7$ for good performance.

---

### D. Stack Operations — Overflow / Underflow

$$\text{Stack Full Condition (Overflow)}: \quad \text{top} = N - 1$$

$$\text{Stack Empty Condition (Underflow)}: \quad \text{top} = -1$$

After `PUSH`: $\text{top} = \text{top} + 1$

After `POP`: $\text{top} = \text{top} - 1$

---

### E. Circular Queue Conditions

Let `F` = front index, `R` = rear index, `N` = capacity.

**Full:** $\quad (R + 1) \mod N = F$

**Empty:** $\quad F = R = -1$

**Enqueue Rear:** $\quad R = (R + 1) \mod N$

**Dequeue Front:** $\quad F = (F + 1) \mod N$

---

### F. Tree Height & Balance

For a tree with $n$ nodes:
- **Minimum height** (perfect tree): $h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$
- **Maximum height** (skewed tree): $h_{\max} = n$
- **Balanced tree condition:** $\vert h_{\text{left}} - h_{\text{right}} \vert \leq 1$

---

### G. Master Time Complexity Table

| Data Structure | Access | Search | Insertion | Deletion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Array** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Linked List** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Stack** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Queue** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Hash Table** | — | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg |
| **BST** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Balanced Tree (AVL)** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **B-Tree** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| **Heap** | $O(1)$ for max/min | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Data Structure | Real OS / Software Using It |
|---|---|
| Doubly Linked List | Linux kernel `list.h`, FreeBSD process lists |
| Red-Black Tree | Linux CFS scheduler, Java `TreeMap` |
| B+ Tree | NTFS, ext4, MySQL InnoDB indexes |
| Hash Table | Linux inode cache, ARP cache, DNS cache |
| Stack | x86 call stack, Java Virtual Machine stack |
| Queue | Print spooler, packet queues in network stack |
| Heap | Linux process priority queue, `malloc` binning |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Address Calculation — 1-D Array

### 📌 Problem Statement
An integer array `A` of size **10** is declared with base address `B = 1000`. Each integer occupies **4 bytes**. Indices run from `0` to `9`. Compute the address of `A[5]`.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- Base address $B = 1000$
- Element size $w = 4$ bytes
- Index $i = 5$
- Lower bound $\text{LB} = 0$ (zero-based indexing)

**Step 2:** Apply the address formula for 1-D array.

$$
\text{Address}(A[i]) = B + (i - \text{LB}) \times w
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the known values.

$$
\text{Address}(A[5]) = 1000 + (5 - 0) \times 4
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the offset.

$$
(5 - 0) \times 4 = 5 \times 4 = 20
$$

**Step 5:** Add offset to base address.

$$
\text{Address}(A[5]) = 1000 + 20 = 1020
$$

**✅ Final Answer:** $A[5]$ is stored at memory location **1020**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Address Calculation — 2-D Array (Row-Major)

### 📌 Problem Statement
A 2-D integer array `A[10][20]` is stored in **row-major order** starting at base address `B = 2000`. Each element is **2 bytes**. Compute the address of element `A[3][5]`. Assume both row and column indices start from `0`.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- Base address $B = 2000$
- Number of rows $m = 10$
- Number of columns $n = 20$
- Element size $w = 2$ bytes
- Row index $i = 3$
- Column index $j = 5$
- Lower bounds $r_1 = c_1 = 0$

**Step 2:** Apply the row-major address formula.

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + \left[(i - r_1) \times n + (j - c_1)\right] \times w
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute all known values.

$$
\text{Address}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + \left[(3 - 0) \times 20 + (5 - 0)\right] \times 2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the bracket term.

$$
(3 - 0) \times 20 = 60
$$

$$
60 + (5 - 0) = 60 + 5 = 65
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by element size.

$$
65 \times 2 = 130
$$

**Step 6:** Add to base address.

$$
\text{Address}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + 130 = 2130
$$

**✅ Final Answer:** $A[3][5]$ is stored at memory location **2130**.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Address Calculation — 2-D Array (Column-Major)

### 📌 Problem Statement
A 2-D integer array `A[10][20]` is stored in **column-major order** starting at base address `B = 2000`. Each element is **2 bytes**. Compute the address of element `A[3][5]`.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Recall column-major formula.

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + \left[(j - c_1) \times m + (i - r_1)\right] \times w
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\text{Address}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + \left[(5 - 0) \times 10 + (3 - 0)\right] \times 2
$$

**Step 3:** Compute bracket term.

$$
(5 - 0) \times 10 = 50
$$

$$
50 + (3 - 0) = 50 + 3 = 53
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by element size.

$$
53 \times 2 = 106
$$

**Step 5:** Add to base.

$$
\text{Address}(A[3][5]) = 2000 + 106 = 2106
$$

**✅ Final Answer:** $A[3][5]$ is stored at memory location **2106** (note: differs from row-major because columns are stored contiguously).

---

## 3.4 Hash Table — Load Factor and Search Complexity

### 📌 Problem Statement
A hash table has $m = 100$ slots. Currently $n = 75$ keys are stored. Compute the **load factor** $\alpha$ and the **average number of comparisons for a successful search using linear probing**.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply load factor formula.

$$
\alpha = \frac{n}{m} = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75
$$

**Step 2:** Apply linear probing successful search formula.

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\alpha = 0.75$.

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{1 - 0.75}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator.

$$
1 - 0.75 = 0.25
$$

**Step 5:** Compute reciprocal.

$$
\frac{1}{0.25} = 4
$$

**Step 6:** Compute bracket term.

$$
1 + 4 = 5
$$

**Step 7:** Apply outer half.

$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 5 = 2.5
$$

**✅ Final Answer:**
- Load factor $\alpha = 0.75$
- Average comparisons for successful search $= 2.5$

> [!WARNING]
> Since $\alpha = 0.75$ exceeds the recommended threshold of **0.7**, performance will degrade. The OS should resize the table (rehash) immediately.

---

## 3.5 Circular Queue — State Computation

### 📌 Problem Statement
A circular queue of capacity $N = 5$ is implemented using an array indexed `0` to `4`. Initially `F = R = -1` (empty). The following operations are performed in sequence:
- ENQUEUE 10
- ENQUEUE 20
- ENQUEUE 30
- ENQUEUE 40
- DEQUEUE
- ENQUEUE 50
- ENQUEUE 60

**Find the final state of the queue (F, R) and determine if it is Full or Overflow.**

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Initial state: $F = R = -1$ → **Empty**.

**Step 2:** ENQUEUE 10. $F = R = 0$, queue: `[10, _, _, _, _]`

**Step 3:** ENQUEUE 20. $R = (0+1) \mod 5 = 1$, queue: `[10, 20, _, _, _]`

**Step 4:** ENQUEUE 30. $R = (1+1) \mod 5 = 2$, queue: `[10, 20, 30, _, _]`

**Step 5:** ENQUEUE 40. $R = (2+1) \mod 5 = 3$, queue: `[10, 20, 30, 40, _]`

**Step 6:** DEQUEUE. $F = (0+1) \mod 5 = 1$, queue: `[_ , 20, 30, 40, _]`

**Step 7:** ENQUEUE 50. Check fullness: $(R+1) \mod 5 = (3+1) \mod 5 = 4 \neq F$. $R = 4$, queue: `[_ , 20, 30, 40, 50]`

**Step 8:** ENQUEUE 60. Check fullness: $(R+1) \mod 5 = (4+1) \mod 5 = 0 = F$. **OVERFLOW!**

**✅ Final Answer:**
- Before ENQUEUE 60: $F = 1$, $R = 4$, queue is **Full**.
- The ENQUEUE 60 operation results in **Queue Overflow**.

> [!NOTE]
> Although slot 0 is empty, the circular queue treats it as **Full** because the standard "waste one slot" technique is used. This trade-off avoids the ambiguity between Full and Empty states.

---

## 3.6 Tree Height Calculation

### 📌 Problem Statement
A complete binary tree contains $n = 1000$ nodes. Find the **minimum and maximum possible height**.

### 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply minimum height formula.

$$
h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 2:** Compute.

$$
\log_2 1000 \approx 9.966
$$

$$
\lfloor 9.966 \rfloor = 9
$$

$$
h_{\min} = 9 + 1 = 10
$$

**Step 3:** Apply maximum height formula.

$$
h_{\max} = n = 1000
$$

**✅ Final Answer:** $10 \leq h \leq 1000$.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Data Structure Selection for OS Subsystems

| OS Subsystem | Best Data Structure | Reason (Engineering Logic) |
|---|---|---|
| Ready Queue (RR scheduling) | Circular Queue | FIFO with constant-time enqueue/dequeue; no memory waste |
| Process Hierarchy (parent-child) | Tree | Each process has exactly one parent; `ps -ejH` displays it |
| File System Directories | B+ Tree | Multi-level indexing minimizes disk seeks |
| Symbol Table (linker/loader) | Hash Table | O(1) symbol resolution is critical at link time |
| Free Memory Block List | Linked List | Variable-sized holes; frequent insertion/removal |
| Priority-based CPU scheduling | Heap | O(1) extract-min for highest-priority process |
| Function call chain | Stack | LIFO matches call/return semantics naturally |
| Network routing table | Trie / Hash | Prefix matching (Trie) or exact match (Hash) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Array Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["Index 0"]:::arr --> A2["Index 1"]:::arr --> A3["Index 2"]:::arr --> A4["Index 3"]:::arr --> A5["Index 4"]:::arr
    classDef arr fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Block-Level Address Mapping:**
> Base: 1000 → A[0]=1000, A[1]=1004, A[2]=1008, A[3]=1012, A[4]=1016 (each cell = 4 bytes)

---

## 4.2 Singly Linked List Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEAD["HEAD"]:::head --> N1["Data 10, Next"]:::node --> N2["Data 20, Next"]:::node --> N3["Data 30, Next"]:::node --> NULL1["NULL"]:::null
    classDef head fill:#E94E77,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef null fill:#999,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Doubly Linked List Structure (Used in Linux Kernel)

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEAD2["HEAD"]:::head2 --> DN1["Prev, Data A, Next"]:::dnode
    DN1 <--> DN2["Prev, Data B, Next"]:::dnode
    DN2 <--> DN3["Prev, Data C, Next"]:::dnode
    DN3 <--> TAIL2["TAIL"]:::tail2
    classDef head2 fill:#E94E77,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef tail2 fill:#E94E77,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef dnode fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Stack (LIFO) — Push and Pop Operations

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Push Operation"]:::op --> S2["Step 1: Check Overflow top less than N minus 1"]:::step
    S2 --> S3["Step 2: Increment Top"]:::step
    S3 --> S4["Step 3: Place item at new Top position"]:::step
    S5["Pop Operation"]:::op2 --> S6["Step 1: Check Underflow top greater than minus 1"]:::step2
    S6 --> S7["Step 2: Retrieve item at Top"]:::step2
    S7 --> S8["Step 3: Decrement Top"]:::step2
    classDef op fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef op2 fill:#E94E77,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.5 Circular Queue — Front-Rear Movement

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1["F=0"]:::ptr --> Q0["Slot 0: 10"]:::slot
    Q0 --> Q1["Slot 1: 20"]:::slot
    Q1 --> Q2["Slot 2: 30"]:::slot
    Q2 --> Q3["Slot 3: 40"]:::slot
    Q3 --> Q4["Slot 4: EMPTY"]:::empty
    Q4 -.-> Q0
    R1["R=3"]:::ptr --> Q3
    classDef ptr fill:#E94E77,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef slot fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef empty fill:#999,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.6 Binary Search Tree (BST) — OS Symbol Table

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["Root: M"]:::root --> LEFT["L: Left Subtree"]:::left
    ROOT --> RIGHT["N: Right Subtree"]:::right
    LEFT --> LL["A"]:::leaf
    LEFT --> LR["J"]:::leaf
    RIGHT --> RL["P: Right"]:::node
    RL --> RLL["O"]:::leaf
    RL --> RLR["Q"]:::leaf
    classDef root fill:#E94E77,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef left fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef right fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#9B59B6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Property:** For every node, all keys in the left subtree are **less than** the node's key, and all keys in the right subtree are **greater than** the node's key. This enables **O(log n)** search.

---

## 4.7 Hash Table with Chaining (Collision Resolution)

```mermaid
graph TD
    HT["Hash Table Array Index 0 to 9"]:::ht
    HT --> IDX0["Index 0: NULL"]:::empty
    HT --> IDX2["Index 2"]:::bucket
    HT --> IDX5["Index 5"]:::bucket
    HT --> IDX8["Index 8"]:::bucket
    IDX2 --> C2A["Key 12"]:::chain
    C2A --> C2B["Key 22"]:::chain
    IDX5 --> C5A["Key 15"]:::chain
    IDX8 --> C8A["Key 28"]:::chain
    C8A --> C8B["Key 38"]:::chain
    C8B --> C8C["Key 48"]:::chain
    classDef ht fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef bucket fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef empty fill:#999,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef chain fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hashing:**
> **Key → Hash Function h(k) → Index i → Bucket → Linked List of Collisions**

---

## 4.8 Complete OS Data Structure Architecture Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    OS["Operating System Kernel"]:::os
    OS --> PM["Process Management"]:::sub
    OS --> MM["Memory Management"]:::sub
    OS --> FS["File System"]:::sub
    OS --> SCH["CPU Scheduling"]:::sub
    OS --> SYM["Symbol Table"]:::sub
    PM --> LL1["Doubly Linked List for PCB list"]:::ds
    MM --> LL2["Linked List for Free Block List"]:::ds
    MM --> HASH1["Hash Table for Hashed Page Table"]:::ds
    FS --> BTREE["B Plus Tree for Directory Index"]:::ds
    SCH --> PQ["Priority Queue implemented as Heap"]:::ds
    SCH --> CQ["Circular Queue for Round Robin"]:::ds
    SYM --> HASH2["Hash Table for Symbol Resolution"]:::ds
    classDef os fill:#E94E77,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ds fill:#50C878,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Key Insight:** This block-level map shows how **a single OS kernel** simultaneously maintains **multiple data structures**, each chosen specifically to optimize its subsystem's performance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define a Stack. List any two applications of stacks in Operating Systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Stack is a linear data structure that follows the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, where insertion (`PUSH`) and deletion (`POP`) occur at the same end called the **top**.

> **Two Applications (2 marks):**
> 1. **Function Call Management** — The OS maintains a call stack to store return addresses, local variables, and parameters for each active function call.
> 2. **Interrupt Handling** — When an interrupt occurs, the CPU state (registers, program counter) is pushed onto a stack. When the interrupt handler finishes, the state is popped to resume execution.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**
**What is a Hash Table? Explain the term "collision" with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Hash Table is a data structure that maps **keys to values** using a **hash function** $h(k)$, which computes an index into an array of buckets for fast **O(1) average-case** lookup.

> **Collision (2 marks):** A collision occurs when **two different keys** produce the **same hash index** under the hash function. **Example:** Let $h(k) = k \mod 10$. For $k_1 = 25$ and $k_2 = 35$, both produce $h(k) = 5$. This is a collision.

> **Resolution:** Handled via **chaining** (linked list at each bucket) or **open addressing** (linear/quadratic probing, double hashing).

---

## 5.2 🎯 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, S7 B.Tech CSE]**

**(a)** Explain the following data structures with respect to their use in Operating Systems: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Linked List
   (ii) Circular Queue
   (iii) B-Tree

**(b)** An array `A[20][30]` is stored in **row-major order** in memory. The base address is `B = 1000` and each element occupies `4 bytes`. Compute the address of `A[5][8]`. Assume indices start from 0. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Linked List (2 marks):**
A Linked List is a linear data structure where each **node** contains a data field and a pointer to the next node. **OS Use:** The Linux kernel uses a doubly linked list (`struct list_head`) to maintain process lists, device drivers, and file descriptors. **Advantage over arrays:** Dynamic size and O(1) insertion/deletion.

**(ii) Circular Queue (2 marks):**
A Circular Queue is a FIFO structure where the **rear** index wraps around to the **front** after reaching the end. **OS Use:** Used in **Round-Robin CPU scheduling** to manage the ready queue, allowing fair CPU allocation without wasting memory slots. **Operations:** ENQUEUE at rear, DEQUEUE at front — both O(1).

**(iii) B-Tree (3 marks):**
A B-Tree is a **self-balancing search tree** where each node can have multiple keys and children. It minimizes **disk I/O** by storing many keys per node, matching disk block size. **OS Use:** File systems like **NTFS, ext4, ReiserFS, BTRFS** use **B+ Trees** (a B-Tree variant) to index directories, allowing fast file lookup even with millions of files. **Complexity:** O(log n) for search, insert, delete.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Base address $B = 1000$
- Rows $m = 20$, Columns $n = 30$
- Element size $w = 4$ bytes
- Index: $i = 5$, $j = 8$
- Lower bounds $r_1 = c_1 = 0$

**Row-Major Formula:**

$$
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) = B + \left[(i - r_1) \times n + (j - c_1)\right] \times w
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute values.

$$
\text{Address}(A[5][8]) = 1000 + \left[(5 - 0) \times 30 + (8 - 0)\right] \times 4
$$

**Step 2:** Compute row offset.

$$
(5 - 0) \times 30 = 150
$$

**Step 3:** Add column offset.

$$
150 + (8 - 0) = 158
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by element size.

$$
158 \times 4 = 632
$$

**Step 5:** Add to base address.

$$
\text{Address}(A[5][8]) = 1000 + 632 = 1632
$$

**✅ Final Answer:** $A[5][8]$ is stored at memory location **1632**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, S7 B.Tech CSE]**

**(a)** Compare Arrays, Linked Lists, and Hash Tables in terms of access time, insertion time, and search time. State **one OS use-case** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular queue of capacity **6** is implemented using an array. Perform the following sequence of operations and **state the front, rear, and full/empty status after each step**: **(7 marks)**
   - ENQUEUE 5, ENQUEUE 10, ENQUEUE 15, DEQUEUE, ENQUEUE 20, ENQUEUE 25, ENQUEUE 30, ENQUEUE 35

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| Operation | Array | Linked List | Hash Table |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access by Index/Key** | $O(1)$ — direct indexing | $O(n)$ — must traverse | $O(1)$ avg — direct hash |
| **Insertion** | $O(n)$ — shifting required | $O(1)$ — at head/tail | $O(1)$ avg |
| **Search (unsorted)** | $O(n)$ — linear scan | $O(n)$ — linear traversal | $O(1)$ avg — hash lookup |

**OS Use-Cases (1 mark each):**
- **Array** → Page Table indexed by page number for fast address translation.
- **Linked List** → Free memory block list in linked memory allocation.
- **Hash Table** → Inode table in file systems (filename → inode mapping).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Capacity** $N = 6$ (slots 0 to 5). Initial: $F = R = -1$ (Empty).

| Step | Operation | F | R | Status | Queue Contents |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ENQUEUE 5 | 0 | 0 | Not Empty | [5, -, -, -, -, -] |
| 2 | ENQUEUE 10 | 0 | 1 | Not Empty | [5, 10, -, -, -, -] |
| 3 | ENQUEUE 15 | 0 | 2 | Not Empty | [5, 10, 15, -, -, -] |
| 4 | DEQUEUE | 1 | 2 | Not Empty | [-, 10, 15, -, -, -] |
| 5 | ENQUEUE 20 | 1 | 3 | Not Empty | [-, 10, 15, 20, -, -] |
| 6 | ENQUEUE 25 | 1 | 4 | Not Empty | [-, 10, 15, 20, 25, -] |
| 7 | ENQUEUE 30 | 1 | 5 | Not Empty | [-, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30] |
| 8 | ENQUEUE 35 | 1 | (5+1) mod 6 = 0 | OVERFLOW | Cannot insert |

**Check at Step 8:** Full condition $(R+1) \mod N = (5+1) \mod 6 = 0 = F$. **Queue is Full. ENQUEUE 35 fails with Overflow.**

**✅ Final State after Step 7:** $F = 1$, $R = 5$, Queue is **Full** with elements `[10, 15, 20, 25, 30]`.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the lower bound** in address calculation — students often write $B + (i \times n + j) \times w$ directly, losing **2 marks** if lower bound is not 0.
> 2. **Confusing row-major vs column-major** — always check whether the problem specifies it explicitly. KTU mostly uses row-major (C-style).
> 3. **Ignoring element size $w$** — forgetting to multiply the offset by $w$ loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Wrong full/empty logic in circular queue** — many students forget the "waste one slot" technique and confuse Full with Empty.
> 5. **Writing only the definition, not the OS application** — KTU specifically asks for OS context; a perfect definition without application loses **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Skipping steps in derivation** — write every step; partial marking demands clear transitions.
> 7. **Mixing up Stack (LIFO) and Queue (FIFO)** applications — function calls use **Stack**, scheduling uses **Queue**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Data Structures for OS**

- ✅ **Array** → Contiguous, O(1) access, fixed size, used in page tables.
- ✅ **Linked List** → Dynamic, O(1) insertion at head, used in PCB lists, free block list.
- ✅ **Doubly Linked List** → Used in Linux kernel via `list_head`.
- ✅ **Circular Linked List** → Used in Round-Robin scheduling.
- ✅ **Stack (LIFO)** → Function calls, recursion, interrupt handling, expression evaluation.
- ✅ **Queue (FIFO)** → Ready queue, I/O wait queue, job queue, spooling.
- ✅ **Circular Queue** → Saves memory by wrapping rear to front.
- ✅ **Priority Queue / Heap** → O(1) extract-min for highest-priority process scheduling.
- ✅ **Binary Search Tree** → Symbol table, average O(log n) operations.
- ✅ **B-Tree / B+ Tree** → File system directory indexing (NTFS, ext4), database indexes.
- ✅ **Red-Black Tree** → Self-balancing BST used in Linux CFS scheduler.
- ✅ **Hash Table** → O(1) average lookup for symbol tables, inode tables, caches.
- ✅ **Hash Collision** → Resolved by **chaining** or **open addressing** (linear/quadratic probing).
- ✅ **Load Factor $\alpha = n/m$** — Keep below **0.7** for good performance.
- ✅ **1-D Array Address:** $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + (i - \text{LB}) \times w$
- ✅ **2-D Row-Major Address:** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + \left[(i - r_1) \times n + (j - c_1)\right] \times w$
- ✅ **2-D Column-Major Address:** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + \left[(j - c_1) \times m + (i - r_1)\right] \times w$
- ✅ **Stack Overflow** when $\text{top} = N - 1$; **Underflow** when $\text{top} = -1$.
- ✅ **Circular Queue Full** when $(R + 1) \mod N = F$.
- ✅ **Tree minimum height** for $n$ nodes: $h_{\min} = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$.

> **🎓 Final Exam Tip:** KTU loves asking **"Explain with OS application"** style questions. Never give a generic data structure answer — always tie it back to a **specific OS subsystem** like scheduling, memory management, or file system. This single habit can elevate your score from average to distinction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process API
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process API

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process API (Application Programming Interface):** The Process API refers to the collection of **system calls** provided by the operating system kernel that allow user programs and applications to create, control, synchronize, and terminate processes. It is the primary interface through which a process interacts with the OS process management subsystem.

In UNIX/Linux (which is the reference OS architecture in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEAMT595), the Process API is centered around **four foundational system calls**:

| # | System Call | Header File | Purpose |
|---|------------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | `fork()` | `<unistd.h>` | Creates a new (child) process by duplicating the calling process |
| 2 | `exec()` family | `<unistd.h>` | Replaces the current process image with a new program |
| 3 | `wait()` / `waitpid()` | `<sys/wait.h>` | Parent waits for child termination (synchronization) |
| 4 | `exit()` | `<stdlib.h>` | Terminates a process normally and returns status |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍳

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a head chef (the **parent process**) running a busy kitchen. To serve more customers efficiently:
> - **`fork()`** → The head chef trains an **assistant chef** who copies every recipe, ingredient, and technique the head chef knows. Both work independently from that moment onward.
> - **`exec()`** → The assistant chef decides to throw away the soup recipe and instead follow a completely new pastry recipe — same chef (process), but now doing a **different job**.
> - **`wait()`** → The head chef **pauses** and waits at the kitchen door until the assistant chef reports back that a dish is ready.
> - **`exit()`** → When the assistant chef finishes, they **clock out**, clean their station, and report the final dish status to the head chef.

In this analogy, the **kitchen manager** is the **Operating System Kernel**, the **time card** is the **Process Control Block (PCB)**, and the **menu** is the **Process Image**.

---

## 1.3 Why Process API Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Every modern application — from a Chrome browser spawning multiple tabs to an Apache web server handling thousands of clients — is fundamentally built using the Process API. Understanding `fork-exec-wait-exit` is the foundation for **multiprogramming**, **process schedulers**, **shells (bash, zsh)**, **containers (Docker)**, and **microservices orchestration**.

**Standard metrics to remember:**
- **PID (Process Identifier):** A unique **non-negative integer** assigned by the kernel to every process.
- **PPID (Parent PID):** The PID of the process that created the current process.
- **Init Process PID:** Always **1** in UNIX systems.
- **Maximum PID:** Typically **32,768** or **4,194,304** depending on kernel configuration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Process API — Detailed Breakdown

### 🔹 Pillar 1: `fork()` — Process Creation

**Definition:** `fork()` creates a new process by **duplicating** the calling process. The new process is called the **child process**, and the original is the **parent process**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- The child is an **almost exact copy** of the parent.
- Both processes execute the **same code** from the **return point** of `fork()`.
- The child gets a **new unique PID**.
- `fork()` returns:
  - **Child PID (> 0)** in the parent process
  - **0** in the child process
  - **Negative value (-1)** on failure

**Logic Steps (How it works inside the kernel):**
1. Kernel allocates a new **PID** for the child.
2. Kernel creates a new **PCB (Process Control Block)** by duplicating the parent's PCB.
3. Kernel assigns new memory pages to the child using **Copy-on-Write (COW)** optimization.
4. File descriptor table is **shared** (reference counted).
5. Parent returns child's PID; child returns 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** `fork()` is called **once** but returns **twice** — this is one of the most frequently asked conceptual questions in KTU exams.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 2: `exec()` Family — Program Replacement

**Definition:** The `exec()` family of functions **replaces** the current process image with a new program. After a successful `exec()`, the process **PID remains the same**, but the executing program is entirely new.

**Common Variants:**
- `execl(path, arg0, arg1, ..., NULL)` — list arguments
- `execv(path, argv[])` — vector (array) of arguments
- `execlp(file, arg0, ..., NULL)` — searches in PATH
- `execvp(file, argv[])` — vector + PATH search
- `execle(path, arg0, ..., envp[])` — list + custom environment

**Why use `exec` after `fork`?** The classic UNIX pattern is `fork()` + `exec()`:
- `fork()` creates a duplicate process.
- One of them (usually the child) calls `exec()` to run a different program.
- The other (usually the parent) calls `wait()` to synchronize.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 3: `wait()` — Process Synchronization

**Definition:** `wait()` makes the **parent process block** (sleep) until one of its child processes terminates. It is essential to prevent **zombie processes** and to **reap** exit status.

**Key Behaviors:**
- `wait(&status)` — blocks until **any** child terminates.
- `waitpid(pid, &status, options)` — blocks until a **specific** child terminates.
- `WIFEXITED(status)` — macro to check normal exit.
- `WEXITSTATUS(status)` — macro to extract exit code.
- `WIFSIGNALED(status)` — macro to check signal-based termination.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 4: `exit()` — Process Termination

**Definition:** `exit()` terminates the calling process **normally** and returns an exit status (0 = success, non-zero = error) to the parent.

**Important Variants:**
- `exit(status)` — performs cleanup (flushes stdio buffers, calls atexit handlers).
- `_exit(status)` — **immediate** kernel-level termination, no cleanup.
- `return n` in `main()` — equivalent to `exit(n)`.
- Returning from `main()` does **not** flush buffers; `exit()` does.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your **one-page rapid revision** reference for Process API in KTU exams.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| Return value of `fork()` in parent | $\text{return} = \text{child\_pid} > 0$ | Parent receives child's PID |
| Return value of `fork()` in child | $\text{return} = 0$ | Child receives 0 |
| Return on failure | $\text{return} = -1$ | No child created; `errno` set |
| Total processes after `n` forks | $P(n) = 2^n$ | Each fork doubles the process count |
| Child PID relationship | $\text{PPID}_{\text{child}} = \text{PID}_{\text{parent}}$ | Parent-child linkage |
| Total processes with `n` parent forks | $P = n + 1$ | n children + 1 parent |
| Time complexity of fork | $O(1)$ to $O(\text{address space})$ | With COW optimization: $O(1)$ |
| `wait()` return | PID of terminated child | On success |
| Exit status extraction | $WEXITSTATUS(s) = (s >> 8) \ \text{AND} \ 0xFF$ | Bit-shift technique |
| Max concurrent processes | $\text{PID}_{\max} = 32768$ (default) | Linux `/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max` |

**Engineering Real-World Utility:**
- **Web servers (nginx, Apache):** Use `fork()` per incoming connection.
- **Shells (bash):** Use the `fork-exec-wait` sequence for every command.
- **Docker/Containers:** Layered process isolation builds on `fork()` + `namespaces`.
- **Google Chrome:** Each tab is a separate process started via Process API.

---

## 2.3 Process States & Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** A process moves through these states during its lifetime:

| State | Symbol | Description |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| New | $S_0$ | Process is being created |
| Ready | $S_1$ | Waiting in the ready queue for CPU |
| Running | $S_2$ | Currently executing on CPU |
| Blocked / Waiting | $S_3$ | Waiting for I/O or event |
| Terminated | $S_4$ | Finished execution, awaiting cleanup |
| Zombie | $S_z$ | Terminated but PCB not yet reaped by parent |
| Orphan | $S_o$ | Parent terminated before child |

**Zombie vs Orphan — The Classic KTU Question:**
- **Zombie:** Child has finished, but parent hasn't called `wait()` yet. The PCB still holds the exit status.
- **Orphan:** Parent has terminated before the child. The child is "adopted" by **init (PID 1)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Processes Created by `n` Consecutive `fork()` Calls

This is a classic KTU problem. We derive the formula **mathematically** below.

### Setup
Let $P(n)$ denote the **total number of processes** (including the original) that exist after $n$ consecutive `fork()` calls, assuming no `exec()` or `exit()` interrupts the sequence.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Base Case**
At the start (before any fork), there is exactly 1 process (the original parent):
$$P(0) = 1$$

**Step 2: Recursive Relation**
Each existing process that executes `fork()` creates **exactly one additional** child process. So every fork call **doubles** the count of processes currently active at that moment.

If $k$ processes exist before the $n$-th fork, then after the fork there are:
$$k_{\text{after}} = 2 \cdot k_{\text{before}}$$

This gives the recurrence:
$$P(n) = 2 \cdot P(n-1)$$

**Step 3: Solving the Recurrence**
Expanding recursively:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P(n) &= 2 \cdot P(n-1) \\
     &= 2 \cdot 2 \cdot P(n-2) \\
     &= 2^2 \cdot P(n-2) \\
     &= 2^3 \cdot P(n-3) \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= 2^n \cdot P(0) \\
     &= 2^n \cdot 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final Result**
$$\boxed{P(n) = 2^n}$$

**Interpretation for KTU:**
- After **1 fork**: $2^1 = 2$ processes (1 parent + 1 child)
- After **2 forks**: $2^2 = 4$ processes
- After **3 forks**: $2^3 = 8$ processes
- After **n forks**: $2^n$ total processes

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Caveat:** The formula $P(n) = 2^n$ holds only when **every process** executes the next `fork()`. If only the parent forks (and the child does not), then $P(n) = n + 1$ (linear).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Exit Status Encoding (Bit-Level)

The `wait()` system call stores the child's exit information in a 32-bit `int status`. We derive how the exit code is encoded.

**Step 1: Status Word Layout**
The kernel packs the exit code into the **upper 8 bits** of the status word.

**Step 2: Bit-Shift Equation**
The exit code (0–255) is encoded as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{status} &= (\text{exit\_code} \ll 8) \ \text{OR} \ 0 \\
              &= \text{exit\_code} \times 256
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Decoding (used by `WEXITSTATUS`)**
To extract the exit code back:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{exit\_code} &= \text{WEXITSTATUS}(\text{status}) \\
                  &= (\text{status} \gg 8) \ \text{AND} \ 0\text{xFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If a child calls `exit(42)`:
- Encoded status = $42 \times 256 = 10752$ (decimal) = $0\text{x2A00}$ (hex)
- Parent decodes: $(10752 \gg 8) \ \text{AND} \ 255 = 42$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process API Decision Table

This is a **high-yield KTU table** that examiners love to test in viva and theory.

| Scenario | `fork()` Ret (Parent) | `fork()` Ret (Child) | Process Count | Action |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------|--------|
| Single fork, no exec | child PID > 0 | 0 | 2 | Both run same code |
| Single fork, child exec | child PID > 0 | 0 (then never returns) | 2 | Child runs new program |
| Single fork, parent waits | child PID > 0 | 0 | 2 (sequentially) | Synchronized |
| Fork fails | -1 | (not called) | 1 | Error handling |
| Double fork, both fork again | > 0 (twice) | 0 (twice) | 4 | Exponential growth |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** Consider the following C code snippet. How many distinct processes are created in total, including the original?

```c
int main() {
    fork();
    fork();
    fork();
    return 0;
}
```

**Solution using the formula:**

Using $P(n) = 2^n$ with $n = 3$:
$$P(3) = 2^3 = 8$$

**Step-by-step trace:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before any fork:} \quad & P = 1 \quad (\text{parent}) \\
\text{After 1st fork:} \quad & P = 2 \\
\text{After 2nd fork:} \quad & P = 4 \\
\text{After 3rd fork:} \quad & P = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **8 processes** are created, including the original. Out of these, **1 is the original parent**, and **7 are descendants** (children, grandchildren, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Creation Tree (fork-exec-wait Pattern)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **classic UNIX process creation flow** used by shells like `bash` when you type a command.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types command in shell] --> B[Shell calls fork]
    B --> C{Return value of fork}
    C -->|PID > 0 Parent| D[Parent process continues]
    C -->|Return 0 Child| E[Child process created]
    D --> F[Parent calls wait]
    F --> G[Parent blocks until child exits]
    E --> H[Child calls exec to load new program]
    H --> I[New program begins execution]
    I --> J[New program runs and eventually calls exit]
    J --> K[Kernel sends SIGCHLD to parent]
    G --> L[wait returns with child exit status]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Parent reads exit status and continues]
    M --> N[Shell prints next prompt]
```

---

## 4.2 Process State Transition Diagram

The following diagram shows how a process transitions between states during its lifetime, which is critical for understanding `wait()`, `exit()`, and zombie/orphan concepts.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: Process creation
    New --> Ready: Admitted to ready queue
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Time slice expired
    Running --> Blocked: I/O or event wait
    Blocked --> Ready: I/O or event completion
    Running --> Zombie: Process calls exit
    Zombie --> [*]: Parent calls wait reaps PCB
    Running --> [*]: Parent terminated child becomes orphan
    [*] --> Adopted: Init PID 1 becomes parent
    Adopted --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 fork-exec-wait-exit Functional Block Architecture

This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing the interaction between user space, kernel space, and the Process API system calls.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[User Space]
        A1[Application Program]
        A2[Shell bash sh]
        A3[Server Process]
    end

    subgraph LIBC[Standard C Library libc]
        B1[glibc wrapper for fork]
        B2[glibc wrapper for exec]
        B3[glibc wrapper for wait]
        B4[glibc wrapper for exit]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL[Kernel Space]
        C1[Process Management Subsystem]
        C2[Process Control Block PCB]
        C3[Scheduler]
        C4[Memory Manager Copy on Write]
        C5[Signal Handler SIGCHLD]
    end

    subgraph HARDWARE[Hardware]
        D1[CPU]
        D2[RAM]
        D3[Disk I/O]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C5 --> D3
```

---

## 4.4 Zombie vs Orphan Process Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph NORMAL[Normal Process Lifecycle]
        N1[Parent P1] -->|fork| N2[Child C1]
        N2 -->|exit| N3[Child terminated]
        N3 -->|parent calls wait| N4[PCB reaped and cleaned up]
        N1 -->|continues| N5[Parent keeps running]
    end

    subgraph ZOMBIE[Zombie Process Scenario]
        Z1[Parent P2] -->|fork| Z2[Child C2]
        Z2 -->|exit| Z3[Child terminated but PCB remains]
        Z1 -->|NO wait called| Z4[Parent never reaps child]
        Z3 --> Z5[Zombie Z state entry in process table]
        Z4 --> Z5
    end

    subgraph ORPHAN[Orphan Process Scenario]
        O1[Parent P3] -->|fork| O2[Child C3]
        O1 -->|parent exits first| O3[Parent terminated]
        O2 -->|still running| O4[Child C3 becomes orphan]
        O4 -->|adopted by| O5[Init Process PID 1]
        O5 -->|waits for orphan| O6[Init reaps orphan when it exits]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - **Normal:** Clean parent-child synchronization via `wait()`.
> - **Zombie:** Child is dead but parent didn't reap — wastes a slot in the process table.
> - **Orphan:** Parent died first — child is re-parented to `init` (PID 1) which becomes its new parent.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List any four fields stored in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores all the information about a specific process. It is the **identity record** of a process in the OS.

Four essential PCB fields:
1. **Process ID (PID):** Unique identifier for the process.
2. **Process State:** Current state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated).
3. **Program Counter (PC):** Address of the next instruction to execute.
4. **CPU Registers:** Saved values of general-purpose and special-purpose registers.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid fields include memory management info (page tables), I/O status info (open file descriptors), accounting info (CPU time used), and parent PID.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the difference between `fork()` and `exec()` system calls.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `fork()` | `exec()` |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Purpose | Creates a **new process** | **Replaces** the current process image |
| PID | Child gets a **new PID** | **Same PID** is retained |
| Number of processes | **Increases** by 1 | **Remains same** |
| Code execution | Both run **same code** | Runs **entirely new program** |
| Return on success | Child returns 0, parent returns child PID | **Does not return** on success |

> [!NOTE]
> `fork()` creates a duplicate; `exec()` transforms. Together they enable the classic **fork-exec pattern** used by every shell.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five-state process model**. Discuss the role of `wait()` and `exit()` system calls in process termination. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **five-state process model** describes the lifecycle of a process in an OS. The states are:

1. **New:** Process is being created.
2. **Ready:** Process is waiting in the ready queue to be assigned to the CPU.
3. **Running:** Instructions are being executed by the CPU.
4. **Blocked (Waiting):** Process cannot continue until some event occurs (e.g., I/O completion).
5. **Terminated:** Process has finished execution.

**State Transitions:**
$$
\text{New} \rightarrow \text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Ready} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{Terminated}
$$

**Role of `exit()`:**
- `exit()` is called by the process to terminate itself.
- It flushes I/O buffers, calls registered `atexit()` handlers, and notifies the kernel.
- The kernel then changes the process state to **Zombie** and stores the exit status in the PCB.
- The PCB remains until the parent reaps it.

**Role of `wait()`:**
- The parent calls `wait(&status)` to block until any child terminates.
- When a child terminates, the kernel sends a **SIGCHLD** signal to the parent.
- The blocked parent wakes up, and `wait()` reaps the child's PCB.
- The exit status is returned via the `status` argument.

**Significance:** Without `wait()`, terminated children become **zombie processes** that consume kernel resources. The pair `exit()` + `wait()` ensures **clean process cleanup**.

---

**Q. (b)** Write a C program that uses `fork()` to create a child process. The child should print its PID and PPID, then call `execlp()` to run the `ls -l` command. The parent should call `wait()` and print the exit status of the child. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>

int main() {
    pid_t pid;
    int status;

    pid = fork();

    if (pid < 0) {
        fprintf(stderr, "fork failed\n");
        exit(1);
    }
    else if (pid == 0) {
        /* Child process */
        printf("Child: PID = %d, PPID = %d\n", getpid(), getppid());
        execlp("ls", "ls", "-l", NULL);
        /* execlp returns only on error */
        perror("execlp failed");
        exit(1);
    }
    else {
        /* Parent process */
        printf("Parent: Waiting for child (PID = %d)...\n", pid);
        wait(&status);
        if (WIFEXITED(status)) {
            printf("Parent: Child exited with status %d\n",
                   WEXITSTATUS(status));
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines:**
- `fork()` — creates the child; returns 0 in child, child PID in parent.
- `execlp("ls", "ls", "-l", NULL)` — replaces child image with `ls -l`.
- `wait(&status)` — parent blocks until child terminates.
- `WIFEXITED(status)` — checks if child terminated normally.
- `WEXITSTATUS(status)` — extracts the actual exit code.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** What is a **zombie process**? How is it different from an **orphan process**? Explain with examples how they are created and how the OS handles them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zombie Process:** A process that has **finished execution** but still has an entry in the **process table** because its parent has not yet read its exit status via `wait()`.

**Creation of Zombie:**
```c
if (fork() == 0) {
    exit(0);     /* Child finishes immediately */
}
/* Parent does NOT call wait() — child becomes zombie */
sleep(60);
```

**OS Handling:** The zombie persists until the parent calls `wait()` or until the parent itself terminates (then init reaps the zombie).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orphan Process:** A process whose **parent has terminated** before it. The orphan is automatically **adopted by `init` (PID 1)**, which waits on it.

**Creation of Orphan:**
```c
if (fork() == 0) {
    sleep(30);   /* Child runs after parent exits */
}
/* Parent exits immediately */
return 0;
```

**OS Handling:** When a parent's parent dies, the kernel re-parents all its children to `init`, which periodically calls `wait()` to clean them up.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Zombie | Orphan |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Definition | Terminated but not reaped | Running with dead parent |
| Resource use | Holds PCB slot | Normal resource use |
| Created when | Child exits, parent doesn't `wait` | Parent exits before child |
| Cleaned by | Parent's `wait()` call | `init` reaps it |
| Harmful? | Wastes process table entries | Generally harmless |

---

**Q. (b)** Consider the following C program. **How many total processes** (including the original parent) will be created when this program runs? Justify your answer mathematically. **(7 marks)**

```c
int main() {
    fork();
    fork();
    fork();
    return 0;
}
```

**Model Answer:**

**Mathematical Justification:**

Using the formula $P(n) = 2^n$ where $n$ is the number of `fork()` calls:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(3) &= 2^3 \\
     &= 2 \times 2 \times 2 \\
     &= 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step trace:**
- **Start:** 1 process (the original parent)
- **After fork #1:** 1 + 1 = 2 processes (parent + child1)
- **After fork #2:** 2 + 2 = 4 processes (each of the above forks once)
- **After fork #3:** 4 + 4 = 8 processes

**Final Answer: 8 total processes** are created, of which:
- 1 is the original parent
- 7 are descendants (children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren)

**Alternative formula derivation:**
$$
P(n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} 2 = 2^n
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting that `fork()` returns twice** — Students often write "fork creates a new process" without mentioning the two return values (child PID in parent, 0 in child).
> 2. **Confusing `exit()` with `_exit()`** — `exit()` flushes stdio buffers; `_exit()` does not. This is a frequent 1-mark trick question.
> 3. **Applying $P(n) = 2^n$ incorrectly** — This formula only works if **every process** executes every `fork()`. If only the parent forks each time, the answer is $n + 1$, not $2^n$.
> 4. **Mixing up Zombie and Orphan** — Zombie = child dead, parent alive. Orphan = parent dead, child alive. Reversing this loses full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting header files** — `fork()`, `exec()` need `<unistd.h>`; `wait()` needs `<sys/wait.h>`; `exit()` needs `<stdlib.h>`. Missing headers in code = mark deduction.
> 6. **Not explaining Copy-on-Write (COW)** — Modern `fork()` uses COW optimization. Mentioning this adds extra credit.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.

- ✅ **Process API = `fork()` + `exec()` + `wait()` + `exit()`** — the four pillars.
- ✅ **`fork()` returns twice:** child PID (>0) in parent, 0 in child, -1 on failure.
- ✅ **`exec()` does not return on success** — it replaces the process image but keeps the PID.
- ✅ **`wait()` blocks the parent** until a child terminates; reaps zombies.
- ✅ **`exit(status)`** terminates the calling process; status is in range 0–255.
- ✅ **Number of processes after `n` forks (all process):** $P(n) = 2^n$.
- ✅ **Number of processes after `n` forks (only parent):** $P(n) = n + 1$.
- ✅ **Zombie = dead child, alive parent, no `wait()` called.**
- ✅ **Orphan = alive child, dead parent, re-parented to `init` (PID 1).**
- ✅ **PCB = Process Control Block** — kernel data structure holding process metadata.
- ✅ **Copy-on-Write (COW):** Modern optimization where `fork()` shares pages until write.
- ✅ **Headers:** `<unistd.h>` for `fork`/`exec`, `<sys/wait.h>` for `wait`, `<stdlib.h>` for `exit`.
- ✅ **Exit status decoding:** `WEXITSTATUS(status) = (status >> 8) & 0xFF`.
- ✅ **The classic shell pattern:** `fork()` → child does `exec()` → parent does `wait()`.
- ✅ **`SIGCHLD` signal** is sent to the parent when a child terminates.
- ✅ **PID 1 = `init`** — the ancestor of all processes; adopts all orphans.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sharing Processor Among Processes - User and Kernel Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sharing Processor Among Processes - User and Kernel Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sharing Processor Among Processes

In a multiprogramming environment, the operating system must efficiently allocate the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** among multiple competing processes. Since a single CPU core can execute only **one instruction stream at a time**, the OS employs **time-sharing** or **multiplexing** techniques to create the illusion that multiple processes are running concurrently.

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> **Processor Sharing** is the OS mechanism whereby the CPU is multiplexed among several ready processes by allocating each a small **time slice** (or **quantum**) in a round-robin or priority-based fashion, achieved through **context switching** triggered by interrupts, system calls, or I/O events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes *dual-mode operation* as the foundational protection mechanism enabling safe processor sharing. Every KTU question on this topic tests both the *scheduling concept* AND the *mode-transition mechanism*.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Single Cashier at a Supermarket

Imagine a supermarket with **only one billing counter** but **50 customers** waiting in line. The cashier does not serve any single customer until they finish all their shopping. Instead, the cashier serves Customer A for **2 minutes** (scans their items), then politely pauses, remembers the exact spot of every item in Customer A's cart, switches to Customer B for 2 minutes, then Customer C, and so on. From each customer's perspective, it *appears* the cashier is dedicated to them, but in reality the cashier is being **shared** by rapidly switching attention.

This is **exactly** how a CPU is shared among processes:
- **Customers** → Processes
- **Cashier** → CPU
- **2-minute slot** → Time Quantum
- **Pausing and remembering cart state** → Context Save
- **Resuming the next customer** → Context Restore

## 1.2 User Mode and Kernel Mode

Modern CPUs support at least **two distinct execution modes** to enforce protection between user applications and the operating system itself.

| Mode | Also Known As | Privilege Level | What Can Execute Here |
|------|---------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **User Mode** | Non-privileged mode, Problem state | Low (Ring 3 on x86) | User applications, libraries, non-privileged instructions |
| **Kernel Mode** | Privileged mode, Supervisor mode, System mode | High (Ring 0 on x86) | OS kernel, device drivers, privileged instructions |

### The Mode Bit

A special hardware register called the **Program Status Word (PSW)** or **Machine Status Word (MSW)** contains a single-bit flag — the **Mode Bit** — that indicates the current execution mode:

$$\text{Mode Bit} = \begin{cases} 0 & \Rightarrow \text{Kernel/Supervisor Mode (privileged)} \\ 1 & \Rightarrow \text{User Mode (non-privileged)} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The convention (0 = kernel, 1 = user) is the most widely adopted, but some architectures reverse it. KTU expects you to know the conceptual role of the mode bit, not memorize a specific bit value.

## 1.3 Privileged vs Non-Privileged Instructions

### Privileged Instructions (Kernel-Only)

These instructions can **only execute in kernel mode**. Attempting them in user mode triggers a **trap** (hardware exception) that transfers control to the OS:
