3. **GPT Structure** (1 mark): The GUID Partition Table uses a **Protective MBR** at LBA 0 (for backward compatibility), the **GPT Header** at LBA 1 (16,384 bytes — actually 92 bytes used, rest is reserved), and **128 partition entries** of 128 bytes each at LBA 2–33. Each entry is identified by a **GUID (Globally Unique Identifier)**.

4. **GPT Advantages** (1 mark): GPT uses **64-bit LBA**, supporting disks up to **9.4 ZB (zettabytes)** and **128 partitions** by default. It maintains **two copies** of the header and partition table (primary at LBA 1, backup at last LBA) for redundancy. Each partition has a unique GUID and a name.

5. **Comparison Table** (2 marks):

| Feature | MBR | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Disk Size Limit | 2.2 TB | 9.4 ZB |
| Max Partitions | 4 primary | 128 |
| Partition ID | 1 byte type code | 16 byte GUID |
| Redundancy | None | Primary + Backup |
| Boot Mode | BIOS | UEFI |
| Sector Size | 512 bytes | 512 or 4K (Advanced Format) |

6. **Boot Process Difference** (1 mark): In MBR boot, BIOS reads LBA 0, verifies `0x55AA`, and transfers control to the bootstrap code in real mode. In GPT boot, UEFI reads the Protective MBR, then the GPT header, locates the **EFI System Partition (ESP)**, and runs the EFI application (`\EFI\Boot\bootx64.efi`) directly in 64-bit long mode.

#### Part (b) — UEFI Secure Boot (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

1. **Security Threat Background** (1 mark): Legacy BIOS has no integrity check on the bootloader or kernel. Malware like *rootkits* and *bootkits* (e.g., *Evil Maid Attack*) can replace the bootloader to gain persistent, undetectable control of the system. Secure Boot addresses this.

2. **Cryptographic Foundation** (1 mark): Secure Boot uses **public-key cryptography** (RSA-2048 or stronger). Each boot component (firmware, bootloader, kernel, drivers) is signed by a trusted vendor's private key. The signature is verified using the public key stored in firmware.

3. **Key Hierarchy** (1 mark): Secure Boot maintains a **Platform Key (PK)**, **Key Exchange Keys (KEK)**, and **Authorized Signatures Database (db / dbx)**. The PK is the root of trust, owned by the OEM. KEKs are used to sign updates to `db` and `dbx`. `db` contains allowed signatures; `dbx` contains revoked signatures.

4. **Boot Chain Verification** (1 mark): On every boot, UEFI:
   - Verifies the bootloader's signature against `db`.
   - If valid, executes the bootloader.
   - The bootloader (e.g., GRUB with `shim`) verifies the kernel's signature.
   - The kernel verifies module signatures before loading.
   - Any signature mismatch → boot halts with `Security Violation` error.

5. **Shim and MOK** (1 mark): To allow Linux distros to boot on Secure Boot systems without each distro's key being in UEFI `db`, Microsoft signs a **shim** (a small first-stage bootloader). Shim uses **Machine Owner Keys (MOK)** stored in NVRAM to permit distro-specific keys.

6. **Advantages** (1 mark): Prevents unauthorized bootloader/OS modifications, mitigates bootkit attacks, ensures *measured boot* (TPM-attested), and is mandatory for Windows 11.

7. **Disadvantages / Workarounds** (1 mark): Dual-boot Linux requires manual key enrollment or MOK. Some freedom-focused distros (e.g., NixOS, Qubes) use *custom Secure Boot* workflows. Users can disable Secure Boot at their own risk in UEFI setup.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Confusing the reset vector with the MBR load address**: Students often write `0x7C00` for the reset vector (incorrect). Reset vector is **`0xFFFFFFF0`**, MBR load is **`0x7C00`**, and boot signature is **`0x55AA`**. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Calling the bootloader "BIOS"**: The bootloader is **GRUB / Windows Boot Manager**. BIOS/UEFI is the **firmware**. Examiners deduct marks for this conflation.
> 3. **Skipping the initramfs**: Many answers jump from "kernel loads" to "login prompt", missing the critical role of **initramfs/initrd** in mounting complex root filesystems. This omission typically loses 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 4. **Forgetting PID = 1**: Always state that the init process has **PID 1** and is the ancestor of all user processes. Examiners test this.
> 5. **Missing Secure Boot mention**: When asked about UEFI, always mention **Secure Boot** and **GPT** as key advantages over BIOS. Omitting these loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not drawing a diagram**: A neat, labeled flowchart of the boot sequence is **expected** in a 14-mark question. Missing it loses at least 1 mark even if the text is perfect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — System Boot Sequence**

- ⭐ **Booting** = loading OS from disk to RAM and initializing the system.
- ⭐ The boot sequence is a **chain of trust**: Hardware → Firmware → Bootloader → Kernel → Init.
- ⭐ **Reset vector** = **`0xFFFFFFF0`** (x86). **MBR load address** = **`0x00007C00`**. **Boot signature** = **`0x55AA`**.
- ⭐ **POST** tests CPU, RAM, ROM, GPU, keyboard, storage — emits beep codes on failure.
- ⭐ **BIOS** = legacy, 16-bit, MBR-based, 2.2 TB limit. **UEFI** = modern, 32/64-bit, GPT-based, Secure Boot.
- ⭐ **MBR** = 512 bytes (446 + 64 + 2). **GPT** = 128 partitions, 64-bit LBA, redundant headers.
- ⭐ **Stage 1** (MBR 446 B) → **Stage 1.5** (post-MBR gap, ~32 KB, FS-aware) → **Stage 2** (GRUB/Windows Boot Manager).
- ⭐ **Bootloader** loads the **kernel** (`vmlinuz`/`ntoskrnl.exe`) and **initramfs** into RAM, decompresses kernel, and passes boot args.
- ⭐ **Kernel initialization**: `head_64.S` (paging, GDT, IDT) → `start_kernel()` (scheduler, MM, VFS) → mount initramfs.
- ⭐ **Initramfs** = cpio archive that holds modules to mount the real root filesystem. Uses `switch_root` to pivot.
- ⭐ **Init process (PID 1)** = `systemd` on modern Linux, `sminit`/`winlogon` on Windows. **Cannot be killed by SIGKILL**.
- ⭐ **systemd** starts services, mounts `/proc`, `/sys`, `/dev`, sets up getty/login manager.
- ⭐ **Total cold boot** ≈ 4–8 s on SSD, 30–60 s on HDD. **Init stage is usually the slowest**.
- ⭐ **Secure Boot** uses RSA signatures, PK/KEK/db/dbx key hierarchy, verifies every stage, prevents bootkits.
- ⭐ **TPM (Trusted Platform Module)** provides *measured boot* — hash of each stage stored in PCR registers for attestation.
- ⭐ For **PXE/network boot**: DHCP → TFTP → iPXE loads kernel+initramfs over network.
- ⭐ Always draw a **labeled flowchart** in 14-mark answers; it earns easy marks.
- ⭐ Know the difference: **firmware ≠ bootloader ≠ kernel ≠ init** — each is a distinct layer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Linux Kernel Process Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Case Study: Linux Kernel Process Management # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **Linux Operating System**, *Process Management* refers to the kernel subsystem responsible for creating, scheduling, tracking, synchronizing, and terminating all executing units of work. Every running program, daemon, kernel thread, and user task in Linux is represented internally as a **process** (or, more precisely, a *task*) and is described by a C structure called `task_struct` (also known as the **Process Descriptor** or **Process Control Block**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linux Fundamental**: A *thread* in Linux is simply a *process* that happens to share certain resources (address space, open files, etc.) with another process. The kernel makes no semantic distinction between threads and processes — both are `task_struct` entries. This is the famous *Linux Threads = Processes* design philosophy by Linus Torvalds. **Key Terminology (as per KTU Module 1):** - **Process Descriptor**: A `task_struct` data structure that holds *all* information about a process. - **PID (Process Identifier)**: A unique integer (default maximum of **32,768** or **4,194,304** depending on `CONFIG_BASE_SMALL`). - **TGID (Thread Group ID)**: The PID of the thread group leader, used to identify multi-threaded applications. - **Context Switch**: The act of saving the state of one `task_struct` and loading the state of another. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen**: - **Customers' orders** = Linux processes (each needs resources, time, attention). - **The Head Chef (Expeditor)** = The **Linux Scheduler** (decides which order to work on *right now*). - **The order ticket pinned to the rail** = The **`task_struct`** (a record card that holds: which table, which dish, how spicy, who's preparing it, how long it's been waiting, what ingredients are needed). - **The cooking stations** = **CPUs/Cores** (only a few orders can be cooked simultaneously). - **The "ready to serve" counter** = The **Run Queue** (`runqueue`). - **The "waiting for ingredients" pile** = The **Wait Queue** (blocked/interruptible processes). - **A finished, paid order, kept for the bill** = A **Zombie Process** (task is dead, but its descriptor is kept around until the parent calls `wait()`). > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Lock-In**: When you `fork()` a process, you are literally making a *photocopy* of the order ticket and handing it to a new chef. Both chefs (parent and child) will work independently from that moment onwards, each with their own copy of the ticket. The `task_struct` is that ticket. ## 1.3 The Process Descriptor — `task_struct` (At a Glance) The `task_struct` (defined in ``) is allocated via the **SLAB/SLUB allocator** (since kernel **2.6.x**), giving high-performance object caching. It contains **over 100 fields**; the critical categories are: | Category | Representative Fields | Purpose | |----------|----------------------|---------| | **Identification** | `pid`, `tgid`, `comm[]` | Unique process IDs and command name | | **State** | `state`, `exit_state`, `flags` | Current execution state | | **Scheduling** | `prio`, `static_prio`, `normal_prio`, `policy`, `vruntime`, `se`,`cfs_rq` | CFS parameters | | **Links** | `tasks`, `children`, `sibling`, `group_node`, `thread_group`, `real_parent` | Process tree (linked list) | | **Memory** | `mm`, `active_mm`, `pgd` | Memory map (page tables) | | **Files** | `files`, `fs` | Open file descriptors and FS context | | **Signals** | `pending`, `sighand`, `signal` | POSIX signal handling | | **Credentials** | `cred`, `real_cred`, `uid`, `gid`, `euid`, `egid` | Security identifiers | | **Timing** | `utime`, `stime`, `start_time`, `real_start_time` | CPU time accounting | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Process States in Linux Linux defines process states using bit flags in `task_struct->state` (in ``). Unlike a classical 5-state model, Linux has **more granular** states: | State Flag | Value (Hex) | Meaning | |------------|-------------|---------| | `TASK_RUNNING` | 0x00000000 | On a runqueue, ready or actually executing. | | `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` | 0x00000001 | Sleeping, but can be woken by signals. | | `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` | 0x00000002 | Sleeping, **cannot** be woken by signals (used for critical kernel waits like disk I/O). | | `__TASK_STOPPED` | 0x00000004 | Stopped by `SIGSTOP`, `SIGTSTP`, `SIGTTIN`, `SIGTTOU`. | | `__TASK_TRACED` | 0x00000008 | Being debugged by a tracer (e.g., `ptrace`). | | `TASK_DEAD` | 0x00000040 | Final dying state (process is being reaped). | | `TASK_WAKEKILL` | 0x00000100 | Wake on fatal signals. | | `TASK_WAKING` | 0x00000200 | In the process of being woken. | | `TASK_PARKED` | 0x00000040 (also) | Used for kthreads parked on a CPU. | | `TASK_NOLOAD` | 0x00000400 | Excluded from loadavg (idle threads). | | `TASK_NEW` | 0x00000800 | Newly forked, not yet ready. | | `EXIT_ZOMBIE` | 0x00000010 (in `exit_state`) | Terminated, awaiting `wait()`. | | `EXIT_DEAD` | 0x00000020 (in `exit_state`) | Fully cleaned up. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Linux treats **TASK_RUNNING** as meaning "ready **OR** running." A runnable-but-not-currently-on-CPU process is *also* in `TASK_RUNNING` state, sitting in a runqueue. This differs from the textbook 5-state model! ## 2.2 Process Creation — `fork()`, `vfork()`, `clone()` Linux has **one** fundamental syscall to spawn a new task: `clone()`. The traditional `fork()` and `vfork()` are **library wrappers** that call `clone()` with different flags. ### 2.2.1 The `clone()` Flags (selected, in ``) | Flag | Resource Shared with Parent | |------|----------------------------| | `CLONE_VM` | Memory (address space) — turns task into a *thread* | | `CLONE_FS` | Filesystem info (root, cwd, umask) | | `CLONE_FILES` | Open file descriptor table | | `CLONE_SIGHAND` | Signal handlers and blocked signals | | `CLONE_THREAD` | Same thread group (same TGID) | | `CLONE_NEWNS` | New mount namespace | | `CLONE_NEWPID` | New PID namespace | | `CLONE_NEWNET` | New network namespace | | `CLONE_IO` | Share I/O context | > **Mapping to POSIX:** > - `fork()` → `clone(SIGCHLD, 0)` (all resources copied, separate address space). > - `vfork()` → `clone(CLONE_VFORK \vert CLONE_VM \vert SIGCHLD, 0)` (parent suspends, child runs first in shared memory — historical optimization). > - `pthread_create()` → `clone(CLONE_VM \vert CLONE_FS \vert CLONE_FILES \vert CLONE_SIGHAND \vert CLONE_THREAD \vert SIGCHLD, ...)`. ### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step `fork()` Internals 1. **User mode**: `int fork()` (in glibc). 2. **Syscall entry**: `sys_clone()` invoked via `int 0x80` or `syscall`. 3. **`copy_process()`** (in `kernel/fork.c`): - Allocates a new `task_struct` from the **task_struct SLAB cache**. - Allocates a new kernel stack (typically **2 pages = 8 KB** on x86_64, configurable to 16 KB). - Copies or shares resources (memory, files, signal handlers) per `clone` flags. - Initializes the **thread info** (`thread_info`) at the bottom of the stack. - Assigns a **new PID** (via `alloc_pid()`). - Sets the state to `TASK_RUNNING`. - Sets `exit_signal` to `SIGCHLD`. 4. **Wake up the new task**: It is placed on a runqueue. 5. **Return value trick**: Both parent and child return to user mode. Child returns `0`; parent returns the child's `pid`. This is implemented by setting the child's saved `eax`/`rax` register to `0` before returning to user space. ## 2.3 The Linux Scheduler Evolution (A KTU-Favorite Topic) | Version | Scheduler | Algorithm | Drawback | |---------|-----------|-----------|----------| | Linux 1.x – 2.4 | Classic UNIX-style | Multi-queue, O(n) per-tick | Poor scalability on many-CPU systems | | Linux 2.6.0 – 2.6.22 | **O(1) Scheduler** | Runqueue per CPU, bitmap of expired arrays | Tuned poorly for interactive vs. batch workloads | | Linux 2.6.23+ | **CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)** | Red-Black tree based on `vruntime` (virtual runtime) | Slightly more complex to tune | | Linux 6.x (current) | **CFS + EEVDF** (since 6.6) | CFS augmented with **Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First** | Best of both worlds | > [!NOTE] > **CFS = Completely Fair Scheduler**, introduced by **Ingo Molnar** in **Linux 2.6.23 (October 2007)**. It treats CPU time as a perfectly divisible resource and aims to give each task a "fair share" of the CPU, modeled as an *ideal multi-task processor* running all tasks simultaneously at a decelerated rate. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Concept | Key Formula / Value | Notes | |---------|-------------------|-------| | Maximum PID | $PID\_MAX\_DEFAULT = 32{,}768$ (or 4,194,304) | Configurable via `/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max` | | Kernel stack size | $8 \text{ KB}$ (default) or $16 \text{ KB}$ | Two pages; set at compile time | | Default time-slice target | $\approx 20 \text{ ms}$ (tunable via `sysctl_sched_latency`) | CFS targets this latency per schedule period | | CFS Period | $\text{period} = \max(\text{sched\_latency}, nr\_running \times \text{min\_granularity})$ | Dynamically scales with load | | CFS `vruntime` update | $\Delta vruntime = \Delta real\_time \times \frac{NICE\_0\_LOAD}{weight}$ | Decreases weight-priority effect | | Wait-queue wakeup | `wake_up()` vs `wake_up_interruptible()` | Latter only wakes interruptible sleeps | | Zombie cleanup | Parent must call `wait()` / `waitpid()` | If parent dies, `init` (PID 1) adopts & reaps | | Average load (loadavg) | Exponentially damped moving average | Three numbers: 1, 5, 15 minutes | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Linux process management is foundational in: - **Container runtimes** (Docker, containerd) — use `CLONE_NEWPID`, `CLONE_NEWNS`, `CLONE_NEWNET` to construct isolated process trees. - **High-performance computing** — cgroups + CFS + CPU affinity controls cluster resource usage. - **Real-time systems** — `SCHED_FIFO`, `SCHED_RR` policies bypass CFS for hard real-time tasks. - **Kernel debugging** — `crash`, `gdb`, and `/proc//` interfaces expose `task_struct` fields. - **Security hardening** — credentials and LSM (Linux Security Module) hooks gate every operation in `task_struct`. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, State Transitions & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 CFS Virtual Runtime — Exhaustive Derivation The **virtual runtime (`vruntime`)** of a task is the wall-clock time the task has spent on the CPU, **divided by its weight relative to a NICE-0 process**. The NICE-0 weight is $W_0 = 1{,}024$. ### 3.1.1 Weight Assignment from Nice Value Nice values range from $\text{NICE\_MIN} = -20$ to $\text{NICE\_MAX} = +19$. Each step of `nice` multiplies the weight by approximately a factor of $1.25$ (precisely, by $\frac{1.25}{1.25}$). In kernel code, the lookup is in the **sched\_prio\_to\_weight** table: $$W(\text{nice}) = \frac{1{,}024}{1.25^{\text{nice}}}$$ **Examples (memorize these for KTU viva):** - `nice = 0` → $W = 1{,}024$ - `nice = -5` → $W = 1{,}024 \times 1.25^{5} = 1{,}024 \times 3.052 \approx 3{,}125$ - `nice = +5` → $W = 1{,}024 \times 1.25^{-5} = 1{,}024 \times 0.328 \approx 335$ ### 3.1.2 Update of `vruntime` When a task runs for an actual wall-clock interval $\Delta t_{real}$: $$\Delta vruntime = \Delta t_{real} \times \frac{W_0}{W(\text{nice})} = \Delta t_{real} \times \frac{1{,}024}{W(\text{nice})}$$ Generalized: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta vruntime &= \Delta t_{real} \times \frac{W_{NICE\_0}}{W(\text{nice})} \\[4pt] &= \Delta t_{real} \times 1.25^{-\text{nice}} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning in plain text:** - A higher nice value (lower priority) means *less* CPU time proportionally, so we *accelerate* its `vruntime` — making it appear as if it has consumed more CPU, so the CFS picks it **less often**. - A lower nice value (higher priority) means *more* CPU proportionally, so we *decelerate* its `vruntime` — making it appear as if it consumed less CPU, so the CFS picks it **more often**. ### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem**: A process with `nice = +10` is scheduled and runs for $\Delta t_{real} = 30 \text{ ms}$. Compute its `vruntime` increment. **Step 1 — Compute the weight for `nice = +10`:** $$ W(+10) = 1{,}024 \times 1.25^{-10} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 1.25^{10} &\approx 9.313 \\ W(+10) &\approx \frac{1{,}024}{9.313} \approx 109.96 \approx 110 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the ratio:** $$ \frac{W_0}{W(+10)} = \frac{1{,}024}{110} \approx 9.31 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the vruntime delta:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta vruntime &= 30 \text{ ms} \times 9.31 \\[4pt] &\approx 279.3 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation**: A low-priority process accumulates `vruntime` 9.31× faster than a NICE-0 process, so the CFS red-black tree will see it as "overdue" only after much shorter real running time — but a NICE-0 process will be picked again **9.31× sooner** for the same wall-clock run. ## 3.2 Context Switch Cost — Analytical Derivation A context switch in Linux has two main phases: **switching the address space (memory)** and **switching the CPU registers**. The total cost $C_{ctx}$ is the sum: $$C_{ctx} = C_{mm} + C_{cpu}$$ For x86\_64 with 4-level page tables and a single switch: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{mm} &= T_{TLB\_flush} + T_{cr3\_load} + T_{page\_walk} \\ &\approx 200 \text{ ns (local TLB flush)} + 50 \text{ ns (CR3 reload)} + T_{page\_walk} \end{aligned} $$ $$ C_{cpu} = T_{reg\_save} + T_{reg\_load} + T_{sched\_overhead} $$ Where: - $T_{reg\_save}$ ≈ saving 16 GPRs + flags + segment registers ≈ **30–60 ns** - $T_{sched\_overhead}$ ≈ CFS picking the next task from the red-black tree ≈ **2–10 µs** - $T_{TLB\_flush}$ ≈ **1–2 µs** (global TLB invalidation) or **~200 ns** (per-CPU shootdown) So a **rough empirical range**: $C_{ctx} \approx 3 \text{ µs}$ (warm cache, no address-space switch) up to $\approx 20\text{–}50 \text{ µs}$ (cold cache, full address-space switch, page-walk in the new process). ## 3.3 Process State Transition Table (Full Analytical Matrix) | From State | Trigger Event | To State | Kernel Function | |-----------|---------------|----------|-----------------| | — (new) | `fork()` / `clone()` | `TASK_NEW` → `TASK_RUNNING` | `copy_process()`, `wake_up_new_task()` | | `TASK_RUNNING` | Schedule-out (preempted) | `TASK_RUNNING` (still in runqueue) | `schedule()` | | `TASK_RUNNING` | `wait_event()`, `msleep()` | `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` | `schedule()` | | `TASK_RUNNING` | `down()` (uninterruptible sem) | `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` | `schedule()` | | `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` | `wake_up()` (no signal) | `TASK_RUNNING` | `try_to_wake_up()` | | `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` | Signal delivered (not masked) | `TASK_RUNNING` | `signal_wake_up()` | | `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` | Resource available | `TASK_RUNNING` | `try_to_wake_up()` | | `TASK_RUNNING` | `SIGSTOP` / `SIGTSTP` | `__TASK_STOPPED` | `do_signal_stop()` | | `__TASK_STOPPED` | `SIGCONT` | `TASK_RUNNING` | `ptrace_stop` / `handle_signal` | | `TASK_RUNNING` | `exit()` syscall | `EXIT_ZOMBIE` | `do_exit()` | | `EXIT_ZOMBIE` | Parent calls `wait()` | `EXIT_DEAD` | `release_task()` | | `EXIT_DEAD` | Reaped & freed | (removed from lists) | `free_task()` | ## 3.4 `fork()` Memory State — `copy-on-write (COW)` Explained A naive `fork()` would copy the entire address space (megabytes!). Linux uses **Copy-on-Write**: 1. The child's `mm_struct` and page tables are created, but **point to the same physical pages** as the parent. 2. The page table entries (PTEs) for both parent and child are marked **read-only** at the hardware level (and marked as `VM_COW` in the VMA flags). 3. When **either** parent or child attempts a `write`: - A **page fault** (exception 14 on x86) occurs. - The fault handler `_do_page_fault()` → `handle_pte_fault()` → `wp_page_copy()` allocates a **new** physical page. - The content is copied to the new page. - The faulting process's PTE is remapped **read-write** to the new private page. 4. After many writes, the pages diverge completely — the kernel paid for a copy only when actually needed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: When asked "Why is `fork()` fast in Linux?" the correct two-part answer is **(a)** the `task_struct` is small and SLAB-cached; **(b)** the address space is *Copy-on-Write*, so no page data is copied at fork time. ## 3.5 Preemption & Latency — Analytical Model CFS targets a maximum scheduling latency $L_{target}$ (default $L_{target} = 6 \text{ ms}$ on most systems, or $20 \text{ ms}$ in older kernels). For $n$ runnable tasks: $$ \text{timeslice} = \max\left(\frac{L_{target}}{n}, \; \text{min\_granularity}\right) $$ Where $\text{min\_granularity} \approx 0.75 \text{ ms}$. **Worked Example:** - $L_{target} = 6 \text{ ms}$, $n = 4$: $\text{timeslice} = 1.5 \text{ ms}$. - $L_{target} = 6 \text{ ms}$, $n = 16$: $\text{timeslice} = 0.375 \text{ ms} < 0.75 \text{ ms}$, so clamp to $0.75 \text{ ms}$. This guarantees that no task is starved, even under load, and bounds worst-case preemption latency for interactive workloads. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Linux Process State Machine (Mermaid) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NEW : fork() / clone() NEW --> RUNNING : wake_up_new_task() RUNNING --> RUNNING : schedule() preempt RUNNING --> INTERRUPTIBLE : wait_event() / msleep() RUNNING --> UNINTERRUPTIBLE : down() on uninterruptible sem INTERRUPTIBLE --> RUNNING : wake_up() or signal UNINTERRUPTIBLE --> RUNNING : resource available RUNNING --> STOPPED : SIGSTOP / SIGTSTP / SIGTTIN STOPPED --> RUNNING : SIGCONT RUNNING --> ZOMBIE : do_exit() / exit() ZOMBIE --> DEAD : parent calls wait() DEAD --> [*] : free_task() RUNNING --> TRACED : ptrace attached TRACED --> RUNNING : tracer detaches ``` > **Reading the diagram**: An ellipse node (e.g., `RUNNING`) is a state. A boxed arrow `--> label : event` shows the transition. `NEW`, `RUNNING`, `INTERRUPTIBLE`, `UNINTERRUPTIBLE`, `STOPPED`, `TRACED`, `ZOMBIE`, `DEAD` are all valid node IDs because they are alphabetic strings without reserved words. ## 4.2 `task_struct` Structural Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD TS["task_struct (process descriptor)"] T1["Identification
pid, tgid, comm"] T2["State and Flags
state, exit_state, flags"] T3["Scheduling
prio, policy, vruntime, se, cfs_rq"] T4["Process Links
tasks, children, sibling, thread_group"] T5["Memory Map
mm, active_mm, pgd"] T6["Open Files
files, fs"] T7["Signals
pending, sighand, blocked"] T8["Credentials
cred, uid, euid, gid, egid"] T9["Timing and Stats
utime, stime, start_time"] T10["Namespaces
nsproxy"] TS --- T1 TS --- T2 TS --- T3 TS --- T4 TS --- T5 TS --- T6 TS --- T7 TS --- T8 TS --- T9 TS --- T10 ``` ## 4.3 `fork()` System Call Flow (Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Process participant K as Kernel participant P as Parent task_struct participant C as Child task_struct participant SLAB as task_struct SLAB cache participant RQ as Per-CPU runqueue U->>K: fork() / clone() syscall K->>SLAB: kmem_cache_alloc(task_struct) SLAB-->>K: new task_struct pointer K->>P: copy flags, priority, mm K->>C: initialize new task_struct K->>K: alloc_pid() - assign new PID K->>K: copy_thread_info(), set kernel stack K->>C: set state = TASK_NEW C->>RQ: enqueue (CFS rbtree insert) C->>C: wake_up_new_task() -> TASK_RUNNING K-->>U: return child PID (in parent) K-->>U: return 0 (in child) Note over C: Child starts at
ret_from_fork ``` ## 4.4 Scheduler Architecture — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Space Processes] -->|syscalls| B[System Call Interface] B --> C[Process Subsystem
kernel/fork.c
kernel/exit.c] C --> D[Scheduler Core
kernel/sched/core.c] D --> E1[CFS Class
kernel/sched/fair.c] D --> E2[RT Class
kernel/sched/rt.c] D --> E3[Deadline Class
kernel/sched/deadline.c] D --> E4[Idle Class
kernel/sched/idle.c] E1 --> F1[Per-CPU rq0] E1 --> F2[Per-CPU rq1] E1 --> F3[Per-CPU rqN] F1 --> G[CPU 0] F2 --> H[CPU 1] F3 --> I[CPU N] ``` ## 4.5 CFS Red-Black Tree Layout (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB R["Root
leftmost = next-to-run"] L["Left subtree
(lower vruntime)"] RR["Right subtree
(higher vruntime)"] N1["Task A
vruntime=10"] N2["Task B
vruntime=20"] N3["Task C
vruntime=30"] N4["Task D
vruntime=15"] R --- L R --- RR L --- N1 L --- N4 RR --- N2 RR --- N3 ``` > **Reading the diagram**: In the CFS runqueue, the **leftmost node** of the red-black tree is *always* the next task to be scheduled. `Task A` (with the smallest `vruntime`) is picked first. A `schedule()` call is $O(\log n)$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Explain the role of the `task_struct` data structure in the Linux kernel. Mention any four important fields it contains.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `task_struct` is the *Process Control Block* of Linux — a C structure that stores **all information about a process**. It is allocated from a per-CPU SLAB cache for fast creation. Four important fields: 1. **`pid`** — unique Process IDentifier used to refer to the process. 2. **`state`** — current execution state (e.g., `TASK_RUNNING`, `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE`). 3. **`mm`** — pointer to the memory descriptor (page tables, address space). 4. **`files`** — pointer to the open-file-descriptor table. *(Viva Tip: Add `cred` for credentials, `sched_entity se` for CFS parameters, `parent` / `children` for process tree to score full marks.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *Differentiate between `fork()`, `vfork()`, and `clone()` system calls in Linux.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Call | Memory Address Space | Parent Suspension | Use Case | |------|---------------------|-------------------|----------| | `fork()` | Copy-on-Write copy | No | General process creation | | `vfork()` | **Shared** (no copy) | **Yes**, parent sleeps until child `exec()` or exits | Optimized for `exec` immediately after spawn | | `clone()` | Configurable (set by flags) | Configurable | Foundation call used to implement threads and namespaces | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Question A (14 marks)** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **process states** in the Linux kernel. Compare them with the classical five-state model. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Describe the internals of the **Linux `fork()` system call**. Explain how Copy-on-Write (COW) is implemented. **[7 marks]** #### **Model Solution** **Part (a) — Process States [7 marks]:** *(Draw the state diagram from Section 4.1 above. 3 marks for diagram.)* Linux has the following states: `TASK_NEW` (just forked, not yet runnable), `TASK_RUNNING` (on the runqueue — ready *or* executing), `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` (sleeping, can be woken by signals), `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` (sleeping, ignored by signals — used for critical I/O), `__TASK_STOPPED` (received `SIGSTOP`), `__TASK_TRACED` (under debugger), `EXIT_ZOMBIE` (terminated, awaiting `wait()`), and `EXIT_DEAD` (final state, descriptor being freed). **Comparison with classical 5-state model:** - Linux's `TASK_RUNNING` combines the classical "Ready" and "Running" states. - Linux's `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` + `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` split the classical "Blocked" state. - Linux has additional states for `STOPPED`, `TRACED`, `ZOMBIE`, and `DEAD` that the classical model does not have. - Transitions are implemented through the `schedule()` function and the `try_to_wake_up()` function. *[2 marks for comparison]* **Part (b) — `fork()` Internals [7 marks]:** 1. User invokes `fork()`; glibc invokes `sys_clone()`. 2. Kernel calls `copy_process()`: - Allocates a new `task_struct` from the **task\_struct\_cachep** (SLAB). - Allocates a new **8 KB kernel stack** (two pages). - Assigns a new `pid` via `alloc_pid()`. - Copies file descriptors, signal handlers, etc., per `clone` flags. 3. **Copy-on-Write (COW) setup:** - The child's `mm_struct` and page tables are duplicated. - All user-space PTEs in both parent and child are set to **read-only** and tagged `VM_COW`. 4. When either side performs a write, a page fault is triggered. The kernel handles it via `wp_page_copy()`: it allocates a new page, copies the data, and remaps the faulting side's PTE to the new page with read-write permission. 5. The child is woken (`wake_up_new_task()`), placed on the runqueue, and returns to user space. The return value is set to `0` for the child and the child's PID for the parent, by writing to the saved `rax` register before returning. *[2 marks for COW explanation, 2 marks for internals]* --- ### **Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — Question B (14 marks)** *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)* **(a)** Describe the **Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)** in Linux. Explain the use of the red-black tree and the concept of `vruntime`. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain the **O(1) Scheduler** and state why it was replaced by the CFS in Linux 2.6.23. Mention EEVDF. **[7 marks]** #### **Model Solution** **Part (a) — CFS [7 marks]:** CFS, introduced by **Ingo Molnar** in **October 2007** (Linux 2.6.23), is a *fair-share* scheduler. Its design idealizes the CPU as a perfect multi-tasking processor where each task runs in parallel at a proportionally decelerated rate. - **Red-Black Tree**: Each runqueue maintains a self-balancing **red-black tree** keyed by `vruntime`. Insertion, deletion, and finding the *leftmost* (smallest `vruntime`) task are all $O(\log n)$. The leftmost node is always the next to run. - **`vruntime` (Virtual Runtime)**: It tracks CPU time consumed by a task, *normalized* to a NICE-0 process. On each tick: $$ \Delta vruntime = \Delta t_{real} \times \frac{W_0}{W(\text{nice})} $$ - **Selection**: CFS picks the task with the *smallest* `vruntime` (leftmost in the tree). - **Load balancing**: When one CPU's runqueue becomes empty, `load_balance()` steals tasks from busier CPUs. - **CFS period**: The target latency $L_{target} \approx 6\text{–}20$ ms is split among $n$ runnable tasks to compute the timeslice, clamped to `min_granularity` ≈ 0.75 ms. *[7 marks distributed as: 2 marks CFS overview, 3 marks red-black tree + vruntime, 2 marks latency formula]* **Part (b) — O(1) Scheduler and EEVDF [7 marks]:** - The **O(1) scheduler** (Linux 2.6.0 → 2.6.22) used a per-CPU runqueue with **two priority arrays** (active and expired). Picking the next task was $O(1)$ via a bitmap lookup. - **Drawbacks**: Complex heuristic-based interactivity detection (`sleep_avg`, `bonuses`) — frequently misclassified batch jobs as interactive and vice versa, leading to poor desktop responsiveness and unfairness under mixed workloads. - **CFS replacement**: The heuristics were replaced with a single, mathematically grounded *fairness* metric (`vruntime`), which proved more robust, simpler to tune, and scale better on hundreds of CPUs. - **EEVDF (Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First)**: Merged into mainline in **Linux 6.6 (2023)**. It augments CFS with a **deadline-based** pick: each task gets a *virtual deadline*, and the scheduler picks the one whose deadline is *earliest among those eligible* (those that have received their minimum execution). It provides better **latency determinism** for hybrid workloads. *[7 marks]* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing "Blocked" and "Sleeping"** — In Linux, blocking is *not* a single state. Always specify `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` (signal-aware) or `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` (e.g., disk I/O wait). Using just "Blocked" loses a mark. > 2. **Forgetting COW** — When describing `fork()`, omitting that pages are *Copy-on-Write* and only physically copied on write is a frequent mistake. Examiners **expect** COW explicitly. > 3. **Stating "Linux has 5 states"** — Linux has *more* than the classical five. Always enumerate the full set. > 4. **`vruntime` is *not* real time** — It is **weighted** by nice value. Writing $vruntime = real\_time$ will lose all CFS-related marks. > 5. **Thread ≠ Process confusion** — Linux does not distinguish threads from processes at the kernel level; both are `task_struct`. Distinguish them only by `clone()` flags. > 6. **Zombies are NOT dead** — A zombie is *already dead* (terminated) but its `task_struct` persists until the parent calls `wait()`. Many students wrongly think zombies are "running" tasks. > 7. **`vfork()` parent suspension** — Forgetting that the parent *blocks* in `vfork()` is a classic error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist** > - **Process Descriptor**: `task_struct` in ``, allocated from a SLAB cache, contains 100+ fields. > - **PID limit**: 32,768 default, 4,194,304 with `CONFIG_BASE_SMALL=0`. > - **Kernel stack**: 8 KB (default) or 16 KB. > - **Process states**: `NEW`, `RUNNING`, `INTERRUPTIBLE`, `UNINTERRUPTIBLE`, `STOPPED`, `TRACED`, `ZOMBIE`, `DEAD`. > - **`TASK_RUNNING`** ≡ Ready **OR** Running (combined in one state). > - **Three creation syscalls**: `fork()` (COW), `vfork()` (shared memory, parent blocked), `clone()` (the only true kernel call, all others wrap it). > - **Copy-on-Write**: At fork, pages are shared, PTEs go read-only, copy happens on first write. > - **CFS = Completely Fair Scheduler** (Linux 2.6.23+): red-black tree on `vruntime`. > - **`vruntime` formula**: $\Delta vruntime = \Delta t_{real} \times \frac{W_0}{W(\text{nice})}$. > - **Nice-to-weight**: $W = 1{,}024 \times 1.25^{-\text{nice}}$. > - **CFS latency**: $\text{period} = \max\left(\frac{L_{target}}{n}, \text{min\_granularity}\right)$. > - **Pick the leftmost** node in the red-black tree (smallest `vruntime`). > - **Context switch cost**: 3–50 µs depending on address-space switch and TLB flush. > - **Scheduler classes** in priority order: `stop` > `dl` (deadline) > `rt` > `fair` (CFS) > `idle`. > - **EEVDF**: deadline-aware variant of CFS, introduced in **Linux 6.6 (2023)**. > - **Zombie cleanup**: parent must call `wait()` / `waitpid()`; orphaned zombies are reaped by `init` (PID 1). > - **Linux thread = task**: A "thread" is a `task_struct` with shared `mm_struct` and `files_struct`. > - **`real_parent` vs `parent`**: `real_parent` is the original; `parent` may be a debugger (`ptrace`). > - **PID namespace**: `CLONE_NEWPID` creates a new namespace where the process sees itself as PID 1 (used in containers). > - **Kernel threads** are created by `kthread_create()` and run in kernel mode with no user address space (`mm = NULL`, `active_mm` may be a borrowed one). > - **Current task macro**: `current` is a per-CPU variable pointing to the running `task_struct` (implemented via the `thread_info` structure or `gs` segment register on x86).

topics.title: Threads and Concurrency: Concept of a Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Threads and Concurrency: Concept of a Thread

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread (Lightweight Process / LWP):** A *thread* is the fundamental unit of CPU utilization and the smallest schedulable entity within a process. It consists of a thread ID, a program counter (PC), a register set, a stack, and a minimal set of thread-local storage. All threads of a process share the same code section, data section, open files, signals, and other operating system resources.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a thread is often described as a **"flow of control within a process"**. A *process* with more than one thread of execution is termed a **multithreaded process**.

The formal relationship is expressed as:

$$
\text{Process} = \supset \{\text{Code, Data, Files, Signals}\} + \text{Thread(s)}
$$

A single-threaded process has exactly **one** thread (the main/initial thread), while a multithreaded process has **two or more** threads executing concurrently within the same process address space.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a **process** is a *restaurant kitchen*. The kitchen itself contains shared resources — the stove, refrigerator, ingredients, plates, and recipes (these are the code, data, and files of a process).
> 
> A **single-threaded process** is like a kitchen with **one chef** — only one dish can be prepared at a time, even if four burners are free. 
> 
> A **multithreaded process** is a kitchen with **multiple chefs** working together. They all share the same pantry, refrigerator, and recipes (shared memory), but each chef independently follows their own recipe steps (own **program counter**), keeps their own notepad of current work-in-progress (own **register set & stack**), and can be told to start, pause, or resume individually (independent **scheduling**).

This is precisely why threads are called *lightweight processes* — they share the heavy "process infrastructure" but carry only their minimal execution context.

---

## 1.3 Syllabus-Mandated Terminology (Must Memorize)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Thread ID** | Unique identifier assigned by the OS to each thread within a process |
| **Program Counter (PC)** | Pointer to the next instruction to be executed by the thread |
| **Register Set** | Working registers of the thread (saved/restored on context switch) |
| **Stack** | Per-thread memory region for local variables, return addresses |
| **Shared Resources** | Code segment, data segment, heap, open file descriptors — all shared |
| **Thread-local Storage (TLS)** | Small per-thread data area for variables that must remain private |
| **Lightweight Process (LWP)** | Synonym for thread in many OS texts (especially older Linux/Solaris docs) |
| **Task** | Generic term for a schedulable unit (used in OS/2, Symbian, some RTOS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** A *thread* is to a *process* what a *task* is to a *program*. The thread is the **active** execution element, while the process is the **passive** environment/container.

---

## 1.4 Why Threads? — The Core Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> Modern applications (web browsers, databases, games, video editors) need to do many things *simultaneously*: serve UI, fetch network data, compute results, log activity. Single-threaded processes would freeze the UI during computation. **Multithreading solves this.**

Threads provide:
1. **Concurrency** — multiple logical flows within one process
2. **Responsiveness** — one thread can keep UI alive while another does heavy work
3. **Resource Sharing** — threads share memory by default, no IPC overhead
4. **Economy** — thread creation is ~10–100× cheaper than process creation
5. **Scalability** — on multicore systems, threads can run on different cores in **parallel**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Process vs Thread — The Definitive Comparison

This is the **single most asked comparison** in KTU exams. Memorize the difference table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule:** A *process* owns resources. A *thread* is the entity scheduled to use those resources. A process = resource container; a thread = execution unit.

| Attribute | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A program in execution; a resource container | A flow of control within a process |
| **Creation Cost** | High (separate address space, PCB) | Low (just a TCB, shares address space) |
| **Context Switch Cost** | High (cache flush, page table switch) | Low (cache usually hot, same address space) |
| **Memory Space** | Independent, isolated address space | Shared within the process |
| **Communication** | Requires IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory setup) | Implicit via shared memory; very fast |
| **Synchronization Need** | Less critical (isolated) | Critical (race conditions on shared data) |
| **Termination Effect** | Other processes unaffected | May affect other threads in same process |
| **Control Block** | Process Control Block (PCB) | Thread Control Block (TCB) |
| **Examples** | Two browser windows | Two tabs in same browser window |

---

## 2.2 Components of a Thread — The TCB

> [!NOTE]
> **Thread Control Block (TCB)** is the kernel data structure that stores per-thread information. Every thread has exactly one TCB, just as every process has one PCB.

The TCB contains:

$$
\text{TCB} = \{ \text{ThreadID},\ \text{PC},\ \text{Registers},\ \text{Stack Pointer},\ \text{State},\ \text{Scheduling Info},\ \text{Pointer to Parent PCB} \}
$$

While a **Process** carries all of the TCB fields **plus**:

$$
\text{PCB} = \text{TCB}_{\text{primary}} \cup \{\text{Address Space},\ \text{Open Files},\ \text{Signal Handlers},\ \text{Credentials},\ \text{Resource Limits}\}
$$

When a process $P$ has $n$ threads $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$, the resource accounting is:

$$
\text{Resources}(P) = \text{Shared Resources} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Private Resources}(T_i)
$$

---

## 2.3 Types of Threads — User-Level vs Kernel-Level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 explicitly tests the difference between **User Threads** and **Kernel Threads**. This is high-yield.

| Feature | User-Level Threads (ULT) | Kernel-Level Threads (KLT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Managed by** | Thread library in user space (e.g., POSIX `pthread` user-mode scheduler) | Kernel directly |
| **Kernel awareness** | Kernel sees only the process; not individual threads | Kernel knows and schedules each thread |
| **Speed of operations** | Fast (no system call for thread ops) | Slower (every op is a system call) |
| **Blocking problem** | One blocking syscall blocks the **entire process** (all ULTs) | One KLT blocks; other KLTs in same process continue |
| **Multiprocessor benefit** | **Cannot** use multiple cores in true parallel | Can run on multiple cores |
| **Portability** | Library-dependent; portable across kernels | Kernel-specific |
| **Examples** | GNU Portable Threads, early Java Green Threads | Windows threads, Linux `clone()`/NPTL |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "user threads are slower than kernel threads." This is **wrong** — ULTs are *faster* for thread creation/switch but suffer from the **blocking problem** and **no true parallelism**.

---

## 2.4 Multithreading Models

Three canonical models describe the relationship between user threads (ULT) and kernel threads (KLT):

### Model 1: Many-to-One (M:1)
- Many ULTs map to **one** KLT
- Thread management in user space
- A blocking syscall blocks the entire process
- **Cannot exploit multiprocessors**
- Example: Green threads in early Java VMs on Solaris

### Model 2: One-to-One (1:1)
- Each ULT maps to **one** KLT
- More concurrency, true parallelism on multicore
- Higher overhead (every thread creation = system call)
- Example: **Windows NT/XP/10**, modern **Linux NPTL**, **Solaris**

### Model 3: Many-to-Many (M:N)
- $M$ ULTs multiplexed onto $N$ (where $N \leq M$) KLTs
- Best of both worlds — flexibility + parallelism
- Complex to implement; few real OS use it
- Example: Solaris prior to Solaris 10, GNU Pth (some configs)

The relationships are formally:

$$
\text{Many-to-One:} \quad |U| \to 1, \quad 1 \le |U| \le N, \quad |K| = 1
$$

$$
\text{One-to-One:} \quad |U| = |K|, \quad |U| \ge 1
$$

$$
\text{Many-to-Many:} \quad |U| \ge |K|, \quad |K| \ge 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 reality:** All modern general-purpose OSs (Linux, Windows, macOS) use the **One-to-One** model. The Many-to-Many model is mainly of historical and theoretical interest.

---

## 2.5 Benefits of Multithreading — KTU High-Yield List

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The textbook lists exactly four benefits. Memorize them in order.

1. **Responsiveness** — A multithreaded server (e.g., web server) can keep listening for new requests on one thread while another thread serves a slow client. UI programs remain interactive.
2. **Resource Sharing** — Threads share the process's memory and resources by default. No explicit IPC mechanism required. Two threads can share a global array with a single pointer.
3. **Economy** — Allocating memory and resources for process creation is costly. Thread creation is **~30× faster** in Linux, **~10× faster** in Windows. Context switch is also cheaper.
4. **Scalability (Parallelism)** — On a multicore CPU, threads can run on different cores truly in parallel. Amdahl's Law quantifies the speedup:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

where $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = number of processors/cores.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Threads |
|---|---|
| **Web Servers (Apache, Nginx)** | One thread per client request |
| **Databases (PostgreSQL, Oracle)** | Separate threads for query, I/O, checkpointing, log writer |
| **GUI Apps (Browsers, IDEs)** | UI thread + background network/render threads |
| **Scientific Computing (OpenMP)** | Workload distributed across CPU cores |
| **Real-time Systems** | Priority-based thread scheduling for control loops |
| **Video Games** | Game loop, physics, AI, rendering, audio as separate threads |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Save this section for last-minute revision. Every KTU question on this topic is drawn from these facts.

| # | Concept | One-liner Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thread = | Basic unit of CPU utilization |
| 2 | Process has | At least 1 thread (the main thread) |
| 3 | Threads share | Code, data, heap, open files, signals |
| 4 | Threads are private | PC, registers, stack, thread-local storage |
| 5 | TCB stores | Thread ID, PC, register state, stack pointer, state |
| 6 | ULT managed by | User-space thread library |
| 7 | KLT managed by | Kernel |
| 8 | M:1 model | Many ULTs → 1 KLT (blocks process) |
| 9 | 1:1 model | Each thread = 1 kernel thread (true parallelism) |
| 10 | Modern OSs use | One-to-One model |
| 11 | Thread creation cost | ~10–100× cheaper than process creation |
| 12 | Multithreading benefits | Responsiveness, Sharing, Economy, Scalability |
| 13 | Amdahl's Law | Speedup = $1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$ |
| 14 | Lightweight Process | Synonym for thread |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Numerical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Multithreading Models Formalized

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the ULT/KLT Mapping Constraint

Let $U$ = set of User-Level Threads in a process, $K$ = set of Kernel-Level Threads in the same process.

We derive constraints for each model.

**Many-to-One (M:1):**
The process has a single KLT that hosts all ULTs. A context switch among ULTs is purely user-space — the kernel is unaware.

$$
\forall u \in U : \text{mapped}(u) = k_1
$$

$$
|K| = 1, \quad |U| \ge 1
$$

If any ULT $u^*$ issues a blocking system call, the single KLT $k_1$ enters the blocked state. Since the kernel has only one thread to schedule for this process:

$$
\text{state}(k_1) = \text{BLOCKED} \implies \forall u \in U : \text{effective\_state}(u) = \text{BLOCKED}
$$

This is the **blocking problem** — the entire process stalls. The throughput becomes:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{M:1} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{exec}}} \quad \text{(no parallelism on multicore)}
$$

**One-to-One (1:1):**
Each ULT is bound to a unique KLT at creation.

$$
|U| = |K| \ge 1
$$

$$
\text{For each pair } (u_i, k_i): \text{mapped}(u_i) = k_i
$$

When ULT $u_j$ blocks, only $k_j$ blocks. Other $k_i$ ($i \ne j$) remain runnable:

$$
\text{state}(k_j) = \text{BLOCKED} \nRightarrow \text{state}(k_i) = \text{BLOCKED}, \quad \forall i \ne j
$$

On an $N$-core machine, theoretical maximum speedup (Amdahl):

$$
S_{1:1}^{\max} = N
$$

**Many-to-Many (M:N):**
A pool of $N$ KLTs services a larger pool of $M$ ULTs.

$$
|U| = M, \quad |K| = N, \quad M \ge N \ge 1
$$

The scheduler dynamically multiplexes. The concurrency bound is:

$$
C_{M:N} = \min(M, N)
$$

If $M = N$, the model degenerates to 1:1. If $N = 1$, it degenerates to M:1.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Walkthrough 1 — Amdahl's Law

**Problem (KTU-style):** A program has 40% of its code that is inherently sequential, and 60% that can be parallelized. Calculate the speedup when running on:
(a) 2 processors
(b) 4 processors
(c) 8 processors

**Solution:**

Given: $f = 0.60$ (parallel fraction), $1 - f = 0.40$ (sequential fraction).

The general formula is:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

**Part (a): N = 2**

$$
S(2) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.60) + \dfrac{0.60}{2}}
$$

$$
S(2) = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.30} = \frac{1}{0.70}
$$

$$
S(2) \approx 1.4286
$$

**Part (b): N = 4**

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.60) + \dfrac{0.60}{4}}
$$

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.15} = \frac{1}{0.55}
$$

$$
S(4) \approx 1.8182
$$

**Part (c): N = 8**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.60) + \dfrac{0.60}{8}}
$$

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.40 + 0.075} = \frac{1}{0.475}
$$

$$
S(8) \approx 2.1053
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with 8 cores, the speedup is only ~2.1× because 40% sequential work dominates. The asymptotic limit:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5
$$

So no matter how many cores we add, the speedup can never exceed **2.5×**. This is the famous Amdahl barrier.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Walkthrough 2 — Cost Comparison: Process vs Thread

**Problem:** On a Linux system, creating a process takes **10 ms**, and creating a thread takes **0.1 ms**. Context switching a process takes **5 ms**, while context switching a thread takes **0.05 ms**.

(a) Find the total time to create 10 processes, each performing one task.
(b) Find the total time to create 1 process with 10 threads, each performing one task.
(c) Compare the two and state the ratio.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): 10 processes**
Total creation time:

$$
T_a = 10 \times 10\,\text{ms} = 100\,\text{ms}
$$

**Part (b): 1 process with 10 threads**
Total creation time:

$$
T_b = 10\,\text{ms} + 10 \times 0.1\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_b = 10 + 1 = 11\,\text{ms}
$$

**Part (c): Ratio**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{T_a}{T_b} = \frac{100}{11} \approx 9.09
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verifying the textbook claim:** The textbook says thread creation is 10–100× cheaper. Our calculated ratio of **9.09** falls within that range, validating the claim with these specific numbers.

Now with context switches. Assume each task runs, blocks on I/O, and is rescheduled. Number of context switches = 10 for each scenario.

$$
T_a^{\text{total}} = 100 + 10 \times 5 = 150\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_b^{\text{total}} = 11 + 10 \times 0.05 = 11.5\,\text{ms}
$$

$$
\text{Final Ratio} = \frac{150}{11.5} \approx 13.04
$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Walkthrough 3 — Optimal Number of Threads (Workload Bound)

**Problem:** A web server takes 100 ms to serve a request on a single thread. Each request is independent. The server wants to serve **at least 50 requests/second**.

(a) If single-threaded, can it meet the SLA?  
(b) How many threads are needed (assuming perfect parallelism)?  
(c) With thread creation overhead of 0.2 ms each, what's the cost of creating that many threads for a 1-second batch?

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Single-threaded throughput:

$$
R_1 = \frac{1\,\text{request}}{100\,\text{ms}} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10\,\text{requests/second}
$$

Since $10 < 50$, the SLA is **violated**. Need multithreading.

**Part (b):** For $T$ threads, throughput is:

$$
R_T = \frac{T}{0.1}\,\text{requests/second} = 10T\,\text{req/s}
$$

Setting $R_T \ge 50$:

$$
10T \ge 50 \implies T \ge 5
$$

So we need **at least 5 threads**.

**Part (c):** Overhead for $T = 5$ threads:

$$
C = 5 \times 0.2\,\text{ms} = 1.0\,\text{ms}
$$

As a percentage of the 1-second window:

$$
\frac{1.0}{1000} \times 100\% = 0.1\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Overhead is negligible — this is exactly why multithreading is preferred over multiprocessing for high-concurrency servers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Per-Model Strengths

| Criterion | M:1 | 1:1 | M:N |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thread mgmt cost** | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| **Parallelism on multicore** | None | Full | Partial (up to $N$) |
| **Blocking resilience** | Poor (whole process) | Excellent | Good (as long as $N \ge 2$) |
| **Implementation complexity** | Low | Low | Very High |
| **OS used in practice** | Rare (old Java) | Universal (Linux/Windows) | Rare (old Solaris) |
| **Recommended choice for new design** | Never | Always | If $N$ tunable & library supports |

The optimal design principle (KTU expects this reasoning):

$$
\text{Best Model} = \arg\max_{m \in \{M:1,\ 1:1,\ M:N\}} \left[ \text{Parallelism}(m) - \text{Overhead}(m) \right]
$$

In practice, the 1:1 model wins because:

$$
\text{Overhead}_{1:1} \approx 0.1 \times \text{Overhead}_{\text{process}} \ll \text{Benefit of full parallelism}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process vs Thread — Memory Layout Diagram

This is the **#1 diagram** KTU expects. It shows why threads are "lightweight."

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROC_A["PROCESS A (Independent Address Space)"]
        subgraph PROC_A_THREADS["Threads of Process A"]
            TCB1["TCB T1: PC, Regs, Stack1"]
            TCB2["TCB T2: PC, Regs, Stack2"]
            TCB3["TCB T3: PC, Regs, Stack3"]
        end
        SHARED_A["SHARED REGION: Code Segment, Data Segment, Heap, Open Files, Signals"]
    end

    subgraph PROC_B["PROCESS B (Independent Address Space)"]
        subgraph PROC_B_THREADS["Threads of Process B"]
            TCB4["TCB T4: PC, Regs, Stack4"]
            TCB5["TCB T5: PC, Regs, Stack5"]
        end
        SHARED_B["SHARED REGION: Code Segment, Data Segment, Heap, Open Files, Signals"]
    end

    TCB1 -.uses.-> SHARED_A
    TCB2 -.uses.-> SHARED_A
    TCB3 -.uses.-> SHARED_A
    TCB4 -.uses.-> SHARED_B
    TCB5 -.uses.-> SHARED_B

    SHARED_A -.isolated from.-> SHARED_B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation from the diagram:** Threads within a process **share the heavy region** (code, data, heap, files). Only the small TCB and stack are per-thread. Two different processes are **completely isolated** from each other.

---

## 4.2 Multithreading Models Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["Many-to-One Model"]
        U1A["ULT 1"] --> K1A["KLT 1"]
        U1B["ULT 2"] --> K1A
        U1C["ULT 3"] --> K1A
    end

    subgraph M2["One-to-One Model"]
        U2A["ULT 1"] --> K2A["KLT 1"]
        U2B["ULT 2"] --> K2B["KLT 2"]
        U2C["ULT 3"] --> K2C["KLT 3"]
    end

    subgraph M3["Many-to-Many Model"]
        U3A["ULT 1"] --> K3A["KLT 1"]
        U3B["ULT 2"] --> K3A
        U3C["ULT 3"] --> K3B["KLT 2"]
        U3D["ULT 4"] --> K3B
        U3E["ULT 5"] --> K3C["KLT 3"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam tip:** Always draw this diagram when asked to "explain multithreading models." Use double-headed arrows if the mapping is dynamic, single-headed if static.

---

## 4.3 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

A thread, like a process, has a lifecycle with five states:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : Thread created
    NEW --> READY : Thread start called
    READY --> RUNNING : Scheduler dispatches
    RUNNING --> READY : Time slice expires preemption
    RUNNING --> WAITING : Block on I/O, mutex, or condition
    WAITING --> READY : I/O complete, lock acquired
    RUNNING --> TERMINATED : Thread function returns
    TERMINATED --> [*] : Resources reclaimed
```

The state transition matrix can be summarized as:

| From State | To State | Trigger Event |
|---|---|---|
| NEW | READY | `thread_start()` / `pthread_create()` |
| READY | RUNNING | CPU scheduler dispatch |
| RUNNING | READY | Timer interrupt (preemption) |
| RUNNING | WAITING | Blocking I/O, `pthread_mutex_lock` contention |
| WAITING | READY | I/O completion, `pthread_cond_signal()` |
| RUNNING | TERMINATED | Thread function returns / `pthread_exit()` |

---

## 4.4 TCB Internal Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TCB["THREAD CONTROL BLOCK (TCB)"]
        TID["Thread ID"]
        PC["Program Counter"]
        SP["Stack Pointer"]
        REGS["Register Set (General Purpose, Status Flags)"]
        STATE["Thread State (NEW/READY/RUNNING/WAITING/TERMINATED)"]
        SCHED["Scheduling Info (Priority, Time Slice, Affinity)"]
        PARENT["Pointer to Parent PCB"]
        TLS["Thread-Local Storage Pointer"]
    end
```

The TCB is what the kernel saves and restores during a thread context switch.

---

## 4.5 Request-Handling Flow in a Multithreaded Web Server

This is a classic KTU application question — explain the role of threads in server design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client HTTP Request arrives at port 80] --> B[Listener Thread accepts connection]
    B --> C[Create new Worker Thread from pool]
    C --> D[Worker Thread processes request]
    D --> E{Request type}
    E -->|Static file| F[Read from disk cache]
    E -->|Dynamic| G[Query database / call backend]
    F --> H[Send HTTP response to client]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Worker Thread returns to pool]
    I --> J[Listener Thread continues accepting new connections]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The listener thread never blocks — it keeps accepting connections. Each worker thread independently blocks on slow I/O without affecting others. This is the textbook **responsiveness** benefit in action.

---

## 4.6 Amdahl's Law — Speedup Visualization (Block Concept)

Since a graphical plot is not directly possible in Mermaid, we present the **functional block** that students can redraw in their answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PARAMS["INPUTS"]
        F["f = Parallel fraction (0 to 1)"]
        N["N = Number of cores/threads"]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE["FORMULA BLOCK"]
        FORMULA["Speedup S = 1 divided by bracket open 1 minus f bracket close plus f divided by N"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["RESULT"]
        RESULT["S = speedup factor. As N grows large, S approaches 1 divided by 1 minus f"]
    end
    F --> FORMULA
    N --> FORMULA
    FORMULA --> RESULT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** When asked about Amdahl's Law, always include: (i) the formula, (ii) a numerical example, and (iii) the asymptotic limit. The block diagram above shows the conceptual pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**

> Define a **thread**. List the components of a Thread Control Block (TCB).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution and the basic schedulable entity within a process. It is also called a *lightweight process* because it carries minimal state and shares most resources of its parent process. **(1 mark)**
>
> Components of a TCB: **(2 marks)**
> 1. Thread ID (unique identifier)
> 2. Program Counter (PC) — address of next instruction
> 3. Register set — saved CPU registers
> 4. Stack pointer — points to thread's stack
> 5. Thread state (NEW, READY, RUNNING, WAITING, TERMINATED)
> 6. Scheduling information (priority, time slice, CPU affinity)
> 7. Pointer to parent PCB
> 8. Pointer to thread-local storage (TLS)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> Differentiate between **process** and **thread** in terms of (i) address space, (ii) creation overhead, and (iii) communication mechanism.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Address space | Independent, isolated | Shared within the process |
| (ii) Creation overhead | High (PCB, address space, page tables) | Low (just a TCB) |
| (iii) Communication | Requires IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory setup) | Implicit via shared memory |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of a thread. With a neat diagram, describe the components of a thread and how multiple threads share the resources of a process. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **user-level threads** and **kernel-level threads**. Discuss the **multithreading models** (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many) with their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:**
A thread is a basic unit of CPU utilization, comprising a thread ID, program counter, register set, and stack. Threads within the same process share the code segment, data segment, heap, and other OS resources.

**Components of a Thread [2 marks]:**
- **TCB fields:** Thread ID, PC, register set, stack pointer, state, priority, pointer to parent PCB
- **Private to each thread:** PC, registers, stack, thread-local storage
- **Shared across threads:** Code section, data section, heap, open files, signals, global variables

**Memory Diagram [2 marks]:**
Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1. Three threads T1, T2, T3 each have their own TCB and stack, but all point to the same code, data, heap, and file table.

**Why threads over multiple processes [2 marks]:**
1. Faster creation (no separate address space)
2. Faster context switch
3. Easy data sharing (shared address space)
4. Better responsiveness in interactive applications

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table [2 marks]:**

| Feature | ULT | KLT |
|---|---|---|
| Managed by | User library | Kernel |
| System call needed | No | Yes |
| Blocking problem | Yes (whole process) | No |
| True parallelism | No | Yes |

**Model 1: Many-to-One [2 marks]:**
Multiple user threads mapped to one kernel thread. Thread management in user space → fast. **Disadvantage:** If one ULT makes a blocking syscall, the entire process blocks. Cannot use multiple CPUs.

**Model 2: One-to-One [2 marks]:**
Each user thread → one kernel thread. Provides full concurrency and true parallelism on multicore. **Disadvantage:** Creating a user thread requires creating a kernel thread, which is expensive. Linux and Windows use this model.

**Model 3: Many-to-Many [1 mark]:**
Multiplexes $M$ ULTs onto $N$ KLTs where $N \le M$. Developer can create as many threads as needed; kernel schedules $N$ at a time. Complex to implement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> - **Loses 2 marks** if you forget to draw the memory diagram for Part (a). Always include a diagram.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if you write "ULTs are slower than KLTs" — the opposite is true for thread *creation/switching*.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if you don't mention which OS uses which model in Part (b). Examiner expects: Linux/Windows = 1:1; old Solaris/early Java = M:1 or M:N.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if you skip the **blocking problem** explanation in M:1 model — this is the textbook's most-asked point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** What is **multithreading**? Explain any **four benefits** of multithreading with suitable real-world examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A program has 30% sequential code and 70% parallel code. Using **Amdahl's Law**, compute the speedup when executed on 2, 4, and 8 processors. What is the maximum possible speedup as $N \to \infty$? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:**
Multithreading is the ability of an OS to support multiple, concurrent paths of execution (threads) within a single process. Each thread has its own execution context but shares the process's resources.

**Benefit 1: Responsiveness [1.5 marks]:**
A multithreaded GUI application can run time-consuming operations in a background thread while the main thread keeps the UI responsive. Example: A web browser fetching a large file does not freeze the UI; the user can still scroll or click other tabs.

**Benefit 2: Resource Sharing [1.5 marks]:**
Threads share the process's memory by default. They can access the same global data structures without explicit IPC. Example: A multi-threaded web server's worker threads share the cache of static files.

**Benefit 3: Economy [1.5 marks]:**
Creating a thread is significantly cheaper than creating a process because no new address space, page tables, or file descriptors need to be allocated. Example: In Linux, `pthread_create()` is roughly 10× faster than `fork()`.

**Benefit 4: Scalability [1.5 marks]:**
On a multicore CPU, threads can execute in true parallel on different cores. Example: A video encoder uses 8 threads to encode 8 chunks of a video simultaneously on an 8-core CPU.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $f = 0.70$ (parallel fraction)
- $1 - f = 0.30$ (sequential fraction)

**Amdahl's Law:**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
$$

**For N = 2 [2 marks]:**

$$
S(2) = \frac{1}{0.30 + \dfrac{0.70}{2}} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.35} = \frac{1}{0.65} \approx 1.538
$$

**For N = 4 [2 marks]:**

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.30 + \dfrac{0.70}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.175} = \frac{1}{0.475} \approx 2.105
$$

**For N = 8 [2 marks]:**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.30 + \dfrac{0.70}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.0875} = \frac{1}{0.3875} \approx 2.581
$$

**Maximum speedup as $N \to \infty$ [1 mark]:**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.333
$$

So no matter how many processors are used, speedup can never exceed **3.33×** due to the 30% sequential bottleneck.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Pitfalls:**
> - **Loses 2 marks** if you write $f = 0.30$ instead of $f = 0.70$. Read carefully: "30% sequential" means $1 - f = 0.30$, so $f = 0.70$.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if you forget to compute the asymptotic limit. Examiner always asks for it in follow-up.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if you don't show the intermediate denominator expansion. Always write $0.30 + 0.35 = 0.65$ explicitly.
> - **Loses 1 mark** if you round too early. Show at least 3 decimal places in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision? Read only this section. It covers every KTU-testable point.**

### ✅ Definitions You Must Know by Heart
- [ ] **Thread** = basic unit of CPU utilization, light-weight flow of control
- [ ] **Process** = program in execution; resource container
- [ ] **TCB** = thread control block; stores per-thread state
- [ ] **PCB** = process control block; stores per-process state and shared resources
- [ ] **ULT** = user-level thread; managed in user space
- [ ] **KLT** = kernel-level thread; managed by kernel
- [ ] **Multithreaded process** = process with $\ge 2$ threads
- [ ] **TLS** = thread-local storage; small per-thread private data area

### ✅ What Threads SHARE
- [ ] Code segment (text)
- [ ] Data segment (global & static variables)
- [ ] Heap
- [ ] Open file descriptors
- [ ] Signal handlers
- [ ] Process credentials (UID, GID)
- [ ] Working directory, umask

### ✅ What Threads Have PRIVATELY
- [ ] Thread ID
- [ ] Program Counter
- [ ] Register set
- [ ] Stack
- [ ] Thread-local storage
- [ ] State (NEW / READY / RUNNING / WAITING / TERMINATED)

### ✅ Thread States
- [ ] NEW → just created
- [ ] READY → waiting for CPU
- [ ] RUNNING → executing on a core
- [ ] WAITING → blocked on I/O, mutex, or condition variable
- [ ] TERMINATED → finished execution

### ✅ Four Benefits of Multithreading
- [ ] **Responsiveness** (UI stays alive during heavy work)
- [ ] **Resource Sharing** (no IPC needed between threads)
- [ ] **Economy** (creation is 10–100× cheaper than process)
- [ ] **Scalability** (parallelism on multicore)

### ✅ Three Multithreading Models
- [ ] **M:1** — many ULTs to 1 KLT — fast but no parallelism, blocking problem
- [ ] **1:1** — each ULT has its own KLT — true parallelism, used by Linux/Windows
- [ ] **M:N** — $M$ ULTs multiplexed onto $N$ KLTs — flexible but complex

### ✅ Key Formulas
- [ ] Amdahl's Law: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$
- [ ] Maximum speedup: $\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1 - f}$
- [ ] Concurrency of M:N model: $C = \min(M, N)$

### ✅ Comparison Numbers to Remember
- [ ] Thread creation: ~0.1 ms vs process creation: ~10 ms (10–100× cheaper)
- [ ] Thread context switch: ~0.05 ms vs process switch: ~5 ms
- [ ] Modern OSs: Linux, Windows, macOS → all use 1:1 model

### ✅ Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Don't confuse "thread" with "process"
- [ ] Don't say "user threads are slower" — they are faster for management
- [ ] Don't forget the memory-sharing diagram
- [ ] Don't forget the asymptotic limit in Amdahl's Law questions
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multithreading Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multithreading Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multithreading** is a programming and execution model that allows multiple threads of execution to exist within the context of a single process, sharing the process's resources (such as its address space, open files, and global variables) while maintaining independent execution flows via thread-specific components like the program counter, register set, and stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Silberschatz-Galvin-Gagne Terminology):**
> A **thread** is a basic unit of CPU utilization; it comprises a **thread ID**, a **program counter (PC)**, a **register set**, and a **stack**. It shares with other threads belonging to the same process its **code section, data section, and other OS resources** (e.g., open files and signals).

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Multithreading Benefits (KTU High-Yield)

The textbook identifies **four primary benefits** of multithreading in a multiprogrammed or networked environment. These are **PERMANENT HIGH-WEIGHTAGE** for KTU Module 1:

| # | Benefit | Core Idea |
|---|---------|-----------|
| 1 | **Responsiveness** | One blocked thread does not freeze the entire application |
| 2 | **Resource Sharing** | Threads share memory/resources of the process by default |
| 3 | **Economy** | Thread creation is cheaper than process creation |
| 4 | **Scalability (Utilization of Multiprocessor Architectures)** | Threads can run in parallel on multi-core CPUs |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition — "A Restaurant Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a single restaurant kitchen (this is the **Process** — the workspace).
> - **Without multithreading:** Only one chef (process) works. Even if the chef must wait for soup to boil, the entire restaurant halts. Customers wait forever.
> - **With multithreading:** Multiple **chefs (threads)** work in the same kitchen. They share the same pantry (memory), the same ovens (files), the same fridge (global data). One chef can chop vegetables while another plates dishes and another handles billing — **simultaneously**, in the same shared kitchen.
>
> **Key Insight:** Hiring a new chef (creating a thread) is far faster and cheaper than building an entirely new kitchen (creating a process). The chefs cooperate because they share infrastructure, but each chef independently tracks their own recipe steps (program counter) and uses their own cutting board (stack).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics to Remember

- **Thread creation time:** Typically **microseconds** (vs. milliseconds for a process).
- **Context switch time between threads:** Typically **nanoseconds** (orders of magnitude faster than process context switches).
- **Shared address space overhead:** Near-zero, since threads inherit the parent's virtual address space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, students must be able to **list, define, and provide a real-world scenario** for *each* of the four benefits. Direct definition-based 3-mark questions are routinely asked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Benefit 1 — Responsiveness

- **Definition:** A multithreaded process can continue running even if part of it is blocked or performing a lengthy operation, because other threads in the same process remain executable.
- **Why it matters:** Critical for **interactive applications** (GUIs, web browsers, databases) where the user interface must remain alive.
- **How it works:** When one thread issues a blocking I/O request (e.g., reading from disk or awaiting user input), the kernel schedules another runnable thread from the same process instead of stalling the whole application.

**Examples in the wild:**
- A **web browser** uses one thread to render the page, another to load images, and another to respond to user clicks.
- A **word processor** lets you type (one thread) while another thread performs background spell-check.

### 2.2 Benefit 2 — Resource Sharing

- **Definition:** By default, threads within a process share the process's **code section, data section, and OS resources** (open files, signals, heap memory, etc.).
- **Why it matters:** Avoids duplication; allows efficient inter-thread communication.
- **How it works:** Because threads share memory, one thread can populate a buffer in shared memory and another thread can consume it — no kernel-mediated IPC (inter-process communication) mechanism (pipes, message queues, shared memory segments) is required.

**Contrast with Processes:**
| Feature | Threads (same process) | Different Processes |
|---|---|---|
| Code section | Shared | Duplicated |
| Data/Heap | Shared | Isolated (need IPC) |
| Open files | Shared | Must be passed via FD inheritance |
| Communication cost | **Low** (memory reads/writes) | **High** (kernel syscalls) |

### 2.3 Benefit 3 — Economy

- **Definition:** Allocating memory and resources for process creation is costly. Threads share the resources of the process to which they belong; hence, it is more **economical** to create and context-switch threads.
- **Why it matters:** Spawning a new process can be **30× to 50× slower** than spawning a new thread in the same process.
- **How it works:** The OS does not need to allocate a new address space, load a fresh page table, or initialize kernel data structures (like the kernel stack and file descriptor table) for a new thread.

**Quantitative Intuition (memory footprint):**

$$
\text{Cost of new process} \;\approx\; \text{Address Space} + \text{Page Table} + \text{Kernel Structures} + \text{File Descriptor Table}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of new thread} \;\approx\; \text{Thread ID} + \text{Program Counter} + \text{Register Set} + \text{Stack} + \text{TCB (Thread Control Block)}
$$

The thread inherits the heavy "structural" components from its parent process.

### 2.4 Benefit 4 — Scalability (Utilization of Multiprocessor Architectures)

- **Definition:** A multithreaded process can take advantage of **multiple CPU cores** by executing its threads in parallel on different processors.
- **Why it matters:** Single-threaded processes can only use **one core** at a time, leaving the rest idle — even on a 16-core machine.
- **How it works:** The OS scheduler can dispatch ready threads from the same process onto different CPU cores simultaneously, achieving true **parallel execution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Distinction:**
> - **Concurrency** ≠ **Parallelism.**
> - **Concurrency** = multiple threads *making progress* (could be on a single core via time-slicing).
> - **Parallelism** = multiple threads *running at the same instant* (requires multi-core hardware).
> Multithreading provides **concurrency** by default and **parallelism** when multiprocessor hardware exists.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises all derivable relationships and comparative metrics. These are **exam-ready** items.

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total context-switch overhead | $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{reload}$ | $T_{cs}$ in $\mu s$ | $T_{save}$: register save time |
| Thread memory cost | $M_{thread} \approx M_{stack} + M_{TCB}$ | $M_{thread} \approx$ **1 MB** typical | Stack grows dynamically |
| Process memory cost | $M_{process} \approx M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{heap} + M_{stack} + M_{kernel\_structs}$ | $M_{process} \approx$ **tens of MB** | Allocated at creation |
| Speedup (Amdahl, ideal) | $S = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$ | $p$: parallel fraction, $N$: cores | Upper-bound for multithreading |
| Economy ratio | $R = \frac{C_{process}}{C_{thread}}$ | Typical $R \in [10, 50]$ | Context-switch + creation cost |
| Parallel efficiency | $E = \frac{S}{N}$ | $0 < E \le 1$ | Drops with synchronization overhead |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web servers** (Apache, Nginx use thread pools).
- **Database engines** (one thread per query).
- **Scientific computing** (parallel numerical kernels via OpenMP/Pthreads).
