- **Embedded real-time systems** (RTOS threads for sensor fusion, control loops).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The Four Benefits — Comparative Deep Dive

Because this is a **conceptual topic**, the "derivation" below is a **logical decomposition** of each benefit into its operational constituents, as expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

#### **Derivation 1 — Responsiveness Metric**

**Step 1.** Consider a single-threaded process $P$ with a blocking I/O of duration $T_{I/O}$.

**Step 2.** The total response time for an interactive task is:

$$
T_{response}^{single} = T_{compute} + T_{I/O}
$$

The UI is **blocked** for the entire $T_{I/O}$ period.

**Step 3.** In a multithreaded design, the same $T_{I/O}$ is overlapped with another thread's useful work $T_{work}$.

**Step 4.** Therefore, the effective response time becomes:

$$
T_{response}^{multi} = \max(T_{compute}, \, T_{work}) \ll T_{compute} + T_{I/O}
$$

**Step 5.** The **responsiveness gain** is:

$$
G_{resp} = \frac{T_{response}^{single}}{T_{response}^{multi}} \ge 1
$$

> **Textual Note:** $G_{resp} = 1$ when $T_{I/O} = 0$ (no blocking, no benefit); $G_{resp} \to \infty$ in the limit of purely I/O-bound single-threaded apps.

---

#### **Derivation 2 — Economy of Resource Allocation**

**Step 1.** Let the cost of creating a new process be $C_P$, comprising:
- Address space allocation: $C_{addr}$
- Page table setup: $C_{pg}$
- Kernel data structures: $C_{kds}$

$$
C_P = C_{addr} + C_{pg} + C_{kds}
$$

**Step 2.** The cost of creating a new thread inside an existing process is $C_T$:
- Stack allocation: $C_{stack}$
- Thread Control Block (TCB): $C_{TCB}$
- Register set: $C_{reg}$

$$
C_T = C_{stack} + C_{TCB} + C_{reg}
$$

**Step 3.** Because $C_{addr}, C_{pg}, C_{kds} \gg C_{stack}, C_{TCB}, C_{reg}$ (typically by 1–2 orders of magnitude), we get:

$$
\frac{C_P}{C_T} = R \in [10, 50]
$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, spawning $k$ threads inside one process costs:

$$
C_{total}^{threads} = C_P + \sum_{i=1}^{k} C_T \approx C_P + k \cdot C_T
$$

Whereas spawning $k$ new processes costs:

$$
C_{total}^{processes} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} C_P = k \cdot C_P
$$

**Step 5.** The **savings ratio** is:

$$
\frac{C_{total}^{processes}}{C_{total}^{threads}} = \frac{k \cdot C_P}{C_P + k \cdot C_T} = \frac{k \cdot R}{1 + k \cdot \frac{1}{R}}
$$

For large $k$, this approaches $R$, confirming the "economy" benefit asymptotically.

---

#### **Derivation 3 — Scalability (Amdahl's Law Limit)**

**Step 1.** Let $p$ be the fraction of a program that can be parallelised and $(1 - p)$ the inherently serial part.

**Step 2.** On $N$ CPU cores, the parallel portion runs $\frac{1}{N}$ as fast.

**Step 3.** The total speedup is:

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 4.** The **upper bound** as $N \to \infty$ is:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - p}
$$

> **Textual Note:** Even with infinite cores, a program that is 25% serial ($p = 0.75$) is bounded above by a speedup of **$S_{\max} = 4$**. This is the fundamental scalability ceiling of multithreading — and is a favourite KTU question for 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Derivation 4 — Resource Sharing Communication Cost**

**Step 1.** Inter-Process Communication (IPC) cost using kernel pipes (in clock cycles):
$$
C_{IPC} = C_{syscall} + C_{copy} + C_{context\_switch}
$$

**Step 2.** Inter-Thread Communication (ITC) cost using shared memory:
$$
C_{ITC} = C_{load} + C_{store}
$$

**Step 3.** Since $C_{load}, C_{store} \ll C_{syscall}$, threads win by orders of magnitude:
$$
\frac{C_{IPC}}{C_{ITC}} \in [10^2, 10^3]
$$

> **Textual Note:** This is the mathematical justification for the **Resource Sharing** benefit.

---

### 3.2 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| Benefit | Operational Mechanism | Quantitative Measure | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Responsiveness** | Non-blocking I/O overlap | $G_{resp} = \frac{T_{single}}{T_{multi}}$ | ★★★ Very High |
| **Resource Sharing** | Shared virtual address space | $\frac{C_{IPC}}{C_{ITC}} \gg 1$ | ★★★ Very High |
| **Economy** | Inheriting parent resources | $R = \frac{C_{process}}{C_{thread}} \approx 10\text{–}50$ | ★★★ Very High |
| **Scalability** | Parallel thread execution | $S = \frac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$ | ★★☆ High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process vs. Threads — Memory Layout Comparison

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the structural difference between a **single-threaded process**, a **multithreaded process**, and a **multi-process system** — a classic KTU 14-mark diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph procA["SINGLE-THREADED PROCESS P1"]
        codeA["CODE SECTION"]
        dataA["DATA SECTION"]
        filesA["OPEN FILES"]
        regsA["REGISTERS"]
        stackA["STACK"]
        pca["PROGRAM COUNTER"]
    end

    subgraph procB["MULTITHREADED PROCESS P2"]
        codeB["SHARED CODE SECTION"]
        dataB["SHARED DATA SECTION"]
        filesB["SHARED OPEN FILES"]
        subgraph t1["THREAD T1"]
            pc1["PC 1"]
            reg1["REGS 1"]
            stk1["STACK 1"]
        end
        subgraph t2["THREAD T2"]
            pc2["PC 2"]
            reg2["REGS 2"]
            stk2["STACK 2"]
        end
        subgraph t3["THREAD T3"]
            pc3["PC 3"]
            reg3["REGS 3"]
            stk3["STACK 3"]
        end
    end

    subgraph procC["MULTI-PROCESS SYSTEM"]
        subgraph pp1["PROCESS P3"]
            c3["CODE 3"]
            d3["DATA 3"]
            f3["FILES 3"]
        end
        subgraph pp2["PROCESS P4"]
            c4["CODE 4"]
            d4["DATA 4"]
            f4["FILES 4"]
        end
        subgraph pp3["PROCESS P5"]
            c5["CODE 5"]
            d5["DATA 5"]
            f5["FILES 5"]
        end
    end

    style codeA fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8
    style dataA fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8
    style filesA fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8
    style regsA fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style stackA fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style pca fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335

    style codeB fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853
    style dataB fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853
    style filesB fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853
    style t1 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04
    style t2 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04
    style t3 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04
    style pc1 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style pc2 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style pc3 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style reg1 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style reg2 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style reg3 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style stk1 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style stk2 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style stk3 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335

    style pp1 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6
    style pp2 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6
    style pp3 fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#9334E6
```

**Legend:**
- **Blue (Single-Threaded Process):** All components belong to one thread.
- **Green (Multithreaded Process — Shared Part):** Code, data, and files are shared.
- **Yellow (Threads T1, T2, T3):** Each thread has its own PC, registers, and stack.
- **Purple (Multi-Process):** Each process is fully isolated — no sharing.

---

### 4.2 The Four Benefits — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates **how** the four benefits materialise during the lifecycle of a multithreaded application.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user["USER REQUEST / HARDWARE EVENT"]
    kernel["OS KERNEL SCHEDULER"]

    subgraph benefit1["BENEFIT 1: RESPONSIVENESS"]
        t1A["THREAD A: User Interface"]
        t1B["THREAD B: Background Compute"]
        t1A -- blocks during I/O --> t1B
        t1B -- keeps UI alive --> t1A
    end

    subgraph benefit2["BENEFIT 2: RESOURCE SHARING"]
        t2A["WRITER THREAD"]
        sharedMem["SHARED HEAP BUFFER"]
        t2B["READER THREAD"]
        t2A -- writes directly --> sharedMem
        sharedMem -- read directly --> t2B
    end

    subgraph benefit3["BENEFIT 3: ECONOMY"]
        oldProc["OLD: Create new process = 30 ms"]
        newThread["NEW: Spawn thread = 0.5 ms"]
        oldProc -- replaced by --> newThread
    end

    subgraph benefit4["BENEFIT 4: SCALABILITY"]
        core1["CPU CORE 0"]
        core2["CPU CORE 1"]
        core3["CPU CORE 2"]
        core4["CPU CORE 3"]
        thread1["THREAD 1"]
        thread2["THREAD 2"]
        thread3["THREAD 3"]
        thread4["THREAD 4"]
        thread1 --> core1
        thread2 --> core2
        thread3 --> core3
        thread4 --> core4
    end

    user --> kernel
    kernel --> benefit1
    kernel --> benefit2
    kernel --> benefit3
    kernel --> benefit4

    style benefit1 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853
    style benefit2 fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04
    style benefit3 fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#EA4335
    style benefit4 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for your KTU answer:**
> - **Top-right cluster:** Responsiveness — even when one thread is blocked, the other thread continues serving the user.
> - **Middle cluster:** Resource sharing — threads communicate by writing/reading shared memory directly, **bypassing** the kernel.
> - **Bottom-left cluster:** Economy — milliseconds vs. microseconds.
> - **Bottom-right cluster:** Scalability — each thread can be pinned to a different CPU core for true parallelism.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**List and briefly explain the four benefits of multithreading in a multiprogrammed environment.**

**Model Answer:**

The four benefits of multithreading are:

1. **Responsiveness:** Multithreading allows an application to remain responsive to user input even when part of it is blocked. For example, a web browser can continue accepting user clicks while another thread loads an image in the background.

2. **Resource Sharing:** By default, all threads within a process share the process's code, data, and open-file resources. This eliminates the need for explicit inter-process communication mechanisms.

3. **Economy:** Creating a new thread is significantly cheaper than creating a new process because threads share the existing address space and kernel resources. A new thread typically takes microseconds, whereas a new process takes milliseconds.

4. **Scalability:** Multithreading enables true parallel execution on multiprocessor (multi-core) systems. Multiple threads from the same process can execute simultaneously on different CPU cores, leveraging available hardware parallelism.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**
**Differentiate between a process and a thread.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A program in execution; the basic unit of resource allocation. | A lightweight unit of execution within a process. |
| **Address Space** | Has its own independent virtual address space. | Shares the address space of its parent process. |
| **Components** | Code, data, heap, stack, registers, PC, PCB. | TCB, PC, register set, stack (rest shared). |
| **Creation Cost** | High (30–50 ms typical). | Low (microseconds). |
| **Communication** | Requires kernel-mediated IPC (pipes, sockets). | Uses shared memory directly. |
| **Context Switch** | Expensive (must flush TLB, switch page tables). | Cheap (only register/stack switch). |
| **Isolation** | Fully isolated from other processes. | Not isolated — shares everything. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **NOT** list the four benefits as a one-liner bullet list. Each benefit carries a sub-part of 3–4 marks, requiring **definition + mechanism + real-world example + 1-line justification**.
> - Always **draw the memory-layout diagram** (Section 4.1) for full marks on 14-mark questions.
> - Numerical questions on **Amdahl's law** are common — show the substitution step, not just the final answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — 2 × 7 = 14**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, explain the differences in memory layout between a single-threaded process, a multithreaded process, and a multi-process system. Why are threads called "lightweight" processes?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A program has a parallelisable fraction $p = 0.9$ (90%) and a serial fraction of 10%. Calculate the maximum theoretical speedup achievable using multithreading on (i) a dual-core system ($N = 2$) and (ii) an octa-core system ($N = 8$). Comment on the result.

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.1**.

**Explanation:**
- A **single-threaded process** has only one thread of control — one program counter, one register set, one stack, but the full code, data, and file resources.
- A **multithreaded process** has multiple threads, each with its own PC, register set, and stack, **but sharing the code section, data section, and open files**.
- A **multi-process system** consists of multiple fully independent processes, each with its own copy of code, data, and resources — sharing **nothing** by default.

**Why "lightweight"?**
Threads are called lightweight because:
- They share most of the process's resources (no need to allocate address space, page table, or kernel structures).
- **Thread creation** takes microseconds vs. milliseconds for processes.
- **Context switching** between threads is faster — no need to switch the page directory or flush the TLB.

**Model Solution — (b):**

Using Amdahl's Law:
$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$
Given $p = 0.9$, $(1 - p) = 0.1$.

**(i) Dual-core, $N = 2$:**
$$
S(2) = \frac{1}{0.1 + \frac{0.9}{2}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.45} = \frac{1}{0.55} \approx 1.82
$$

**(ii) Octa-core, $N = 8$:**
$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.1 + \frac{0.9}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.1125} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71
$$

**Upper bound as $N \to \infty$:**
$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10
$$

**Comment:** Even though we go from 2 cores to 8 cores (a 4× increase in hardware), the speedup improves only from 1.82× to 4.71× (a 2.59× improvement). The serial 10% portion of the program becomes the bottleneck — this is the fundamental limitation quantified by Amdahl's Law and demonstrates the **scalability ceiling** of multithreading.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — 2 × 7 = 14 (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the four benefits of multithreading. For each benefit, provide a real-world engineering example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the cost of creating a new process vs. creating a new thread in terms of memory, time, and resource allocation. Justify why threads are considered "economical" with quantitative reasoning.

**Model Solution — (a):**

| # | Benefit | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Responsiveness** | Application remains interactive even when one thread blocks. | **Word processor** — typing (Thread A) continues while spell-check runs (Thread B). |
| 2 | **Resource Sharing** | Threads of a process share code, data, and files by default. | **Producer–Consumer pipeline** — producer thread writes to a shared buffer, consumer thread reads from it without using OS pipes. |
| 3 | **Economy** | Creating/context-switching threads is cheaper than processes. | **Web server** spawning a thread per request vs. spawning a process per request — thread pool saves GBs of memory under load. |
| 4 | **Scalability** | Threads can run in parallel on multi-core hardware. | **Video encoder** (e.g., FFmpeg) splitting an MP4 encode across 16 cores — each thread encodes a slice of frames in parallel. |

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Memory Cost Comparison:**

For a typical OS:
- New **process** cost: $\approx 30\text{–}50$ MB (code, data, heap, stack, kernel structs, page tables, file descriptor tables).
- New **thread** cost: $\approx 1$ MB (only the stack and TCB; rest inherited).

**Time Cost Comparison:**

- **Process creation** (e.g., `fork()`): $\approx 30$ ms on Linux.
- **Thread creation** (e.g., `pthread_create()`): $\approx 0.5$ ms.

$$
R = \frac{C_{process}}{C_{thread}} \approx \frac{30 \text{ ms}}{0.5 \text{ ms}} = 60
$$

**Resource Allocation:**
- A process needs a fresh address space, new page tables, and full kernel bookkeeping.
- A thread only needs a **Thread Control Block (TCB)**, stack, and register context.

**Quantitative Justification of "Economy":**
If a server handles $k = 1000$ concurrent requests:
- **Process-based:** $1000 \times 30$ MB $= 30$ GB of RAM consumed.
- **Thread-based:** $30$ MB $+ 1000 \times 1$ MB $\approx 1.03$ GB of RAM consumed.
- **Savings:** $\approx 29$ GB, a **~29× reduction** in memory footprint.

This shows quantitatively that threads are far more economical than processes for high-concurrency workloads.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multithreading Benefits**

- ✅ **Four benefits (in order):** Responsiveness, Resource Sharing, Economy, Scalability. Memorize the exact ordering — KTU answer keys use it.
- ✅ **A thread =** Thread ID + PC + Register Set + Stack. **Everything else is shared.**
- ✅ **Lightweight =** Cheaper creation, cheaper context switch, shared memory space.
- ✅ **Concurrency ≠ Parallelism** — concurrency is logical, parallelism is physical (multi-core).
- ✅ **Resource sharing via shared memory** bypasses the kernel — that's why inter-thread comms are faster than IPC.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$ — used for **scalability** questions.
- ✅ **Upper bound:** $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-p}$ — the serial portion is the bottleneck.
- ✅ **Thread memory $\approx$ 1 MB; process memory $\approx$ tens of MB** — quote these in 7-mark questions.
- ✅ **Always draw the memory layout diagram** in 14-mark answers — a must-do for full marks.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Don't confuse *concurrency* (multithreaded) with *parallelism* (multi-core multithreaded).
- ✅ **Real-world examples to memorize:** Web browser, word processor, web server, database engine, video encoder.
- ✅ **Process context switch > Thread context switch** because of TLB flush, page table switch, and kernel structure re-loading.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multithreading Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multithreading Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Multithreading Model?

A **Multithreading Model** defines the *relationship* between **user-level threads (ULT)** and **kernel-level threads (KLT)**. It specifies how thread libraries (user-space) and the operating system kernel cooperate to manage concurrency within a process. The choice of model fundamentally determines *concurrency*, *parallelism*, *system throughput*, and the *overhead* of thread management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"A multithreading model establishes the mapping (one-to-one, many-to-one, or many-to-many) between threads created in user space by a thread library and the dispatchable units of execution scheduled by the kernel."*

In simple words — a multithreading model is the **rulebook** that decides how many "virtual workers" (user threads) inside your program get connected to actual "hardware workers" (kernel threads) the CPU can run.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen**:

- **Chefs (User Threads)** = the people preparing dishes.
- **Stove Burners (Kernel Threads)** = actual cooking slots managed by the head chef (OS Kernel).

Now, three kitchen policies are possible:

| Policy | Real-world Kitchen Analogy | OS Model |
|---|---|---|
| All chefs must share **ONE** burner | Only one dish cooks at a time; if one chef waits for an oven, the whole line halts. | **Many-to-One** |
| Each chef gets **HIS OWN** burner | Plenty of cooking in parallel, but expensive — burners cost money. | **One-to-One** |
| Chefs are pooled and assigned to burnagers dynamically | Cheaper and flexible; sometimes chefs wait, but throughput is high. | **Many-to-Many** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (often asked in KTU):**
> - **User-level threads (ULT):** Managed entirely by a thread library in user space. The kernel is *unaware* of them.
> - **Kernel-level threads (KLT):** Supported directly by the OS kernel and are the entities the scheduler actually dispatches to the CPU.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Different Models?

Different applications have different needs:
- A **web server** needs true parallelism → prefers **One-to-One**.
- A **GUI application** has many small tasks that block often → may be fine with **Many-to-One**.
- A **scientific computing app** creates thousands of threads → needs flexible multiplexing → **Many-to-Many**.

Hence, the model choice is a *trade-off* between **performance**, **portability**, and **resource cost**. This is precisely the trade-off KTU examiners love to test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Models (KTU Syllabus Mandatory)

### 2.1.1 Many-to-One Model (M:1)

**Definition:** Many user-level threads are mapped to **one** kernel thread.

**How it works:**
1. The thread library (in user space) maintains its own ready queue of ULTs.
2. The kernel sees the *process* as a single thread of execution.
3. Only **one** ULT can access the kernel at any instant.

**Properties:**
- Thread management is done in **user space** → no kernel intervention → *very fast thread creation*.
- The *entire process* blocks if any ULT makes a **blocking system call**.
- **No true parallel execution** on a multiprocessor — kernel sees one thread, so it cannot dispatch to multiple CPUs.
- Concurrency is *simulated*, not real.

**Example Systems:** **GNU Portable Threads (pthreads user-level)**, older Solaris Green threads.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "many-to-one supports multiprocessor execution." It does **NOT** — concurrency is logical, not physical.

---

### 2.1.2 One-to-One Model (1:1)

**Definition:** Each user-level thread is mapped to **its own** kernel thread.

**How it works:**
1. Every call to create a ULT results in a corresponding KLT creation by the kernel.
2. The scheduler treats each thread as an independent dispatchable entity.
3. Multiple threads of the same process can run on **different CPUs simultaneously**.

**Properties:**
- Provides **true concurrency / parallel execution** on multiprocessors.
- A blocking system call in one thread does *not* halt the process.
- Drawback: creating a kernel thread is **expensive** (system call overhead).
- Most modern general-purpose OSes cap the number of threads per process to prevent resource exhaustion.

**Example Systems:** **Windows (Win32)**, **Linux (NPTL)**, **Solaris 9+**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Linux, since kernel 2.6, has effectively used the **1:1 model** via the NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library).

---

### 2.1.3 Many-to-Many Model (M:M)

**Definition:** $M$ user-level threads are multiplexed onto $N$ kernel threads, where typically $M \ge N$.

**How it works:**
1. The application may create *as many* ULTs as needed (e.g., 100).
2. The kernel allocates a *smaller or equal* number of KLTs (e.g., 4 or 8).
3. The thread library schedules ULTs onto the available KLTs.

**Properties:**
- Combines the **flexibility of M:1** with the **true concurrency of 1:1**.
- Developer can create many ULTs without worrying about kernel resource limits.
- Number of KLTs may be **fixed** (process-specific) or **adjustable** (machine-specific).
- A blocking KLT does not block the whole process — the library can reschedule a ULT onto another KLT.

**Example Systems:** **Solaris (versions prior to 9)**, **Windows with ThreadFiber**, **IRIX, HP-UX, Tru64 UNIX**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU "Two-Level Model" Sub-Variant:**
> A variation of M:M binds a *specific* ULT permanently to a *specific* KLT (e.g., for kernel-only or real-time tasks). This is called the **Two-Level Model** and is mentioned in Silberschatz as a refinement.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Decision Cheat Sheet

| Property | Many-to-One (M:1) | One-to-One (1:1) | Many-to-Many (M:M) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ULT → KLT Ratio** | $M$ ULT : $1$ KLT | $1$ ULT : $1$ KLT | $M$ ULT : $N$ KLT ($M \ge N$) |
| **True Parallelism on Multiprocessor** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (limited by $N$) |
| **Blocking System Call Effect** | Whole process blocks | Only that thread blocks | Other KLTs continue |
| **Thread Creation Cost** | **Low** (user space) | **High** (kernel call) | **Low–Medium** |
| **Concurrency Level** | Simulated | Real | Real (hybrid) |
| **Kernel Awareness of ULTs** | None | Full | Partial (only KLTs) |
| **Resource Bound on Thread Count** | None in user space | OS-imposed limit | Developer-controlled |
| **Example OS** | GNU Pth, Green threads | Windows, Linux, Solaris 9+ | Solaris <9, IRIX |
| **Portability** | High | Moderate | Complex implementation |

### 2.2.1 Useful Symbolic Notation (for derivations & exam)

- Let $T_u$ = total user-level threads.
- Let $T_k$ = total kernel-level threads allocated.
- Let $P$ = number of processors in the system.

**Maximum degree of true parallelism:**

$$
\text{Parallelism} = \min(T_k,\ P)
$$

- **M:1:** $T_k = 1$ → $\text{Parallelism} = \min(1, P) = 1$. Single CPU effective.
- **1:1:** $T_k = T_u$ → $\text{Parallelism} = \min(T_u, P)$. Up to $P$ threads parallel.
- **M:M:** $T_k = N$ (developer/machine chosen) → $\text{Parallelism} = \min(N, P)$. Tunable.

### 2.2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Typical Model Used | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** | 1:1 (Linux) | Need true parallelism on thousands of cores |
| **Embedded Real-Time Systems** | 1:1 (RTOS like VxWorks) | Predictable scheduling, hard deadlines |
| **GUI Desktop Apps** | M:1 or M:M | Many short-lived, blocking-I/O-heavy threads |
| **Web Servers (e.g., Apache early)** | M:1 (then later 1:1) | Threads handle independent requests, need scalability |
| **Database Engines** | M:M | Complex mix of I/O and CPU work |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern systems lean heavily toward the **1:1 model** because CPU core counts keep rising, and the overhead of a single syscall (clone() in Linux ≈ a few microseconds) is dwarfed by the benefit of parallel execution. The M:M model's main historical advantage has been eroded by improvements in kernel thread scalability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Parallelism Under Each Model

**Given:**
- A multithreaded process with $T_u$ user-level threads.
- Running on a system with $P$ processor cores.
- Under model $X$, the kernel maps these into $T_k$ kernel threads.

**Step 1 —** *Define* the number of simultaneously runnable kernel threads.
By the OS scheduler's rule, the **upper bound** of parallel execution is the smaller of two quantities: the number of kernel threads (each is independently schedulable) and the number of physical cores.

$$
D_{\max} = \min(T_k,\ P)
$$

where $D_{\max}$ denotes the *maximum degree of parallelism*.

**Step 2 —** *Substitute* for the **Many-to-One model**.
In M:1, by definition, **all** user threads share exactly one kernel thread:

$$
T_k^{(M:1)} = 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_{\max}^{(M:1)} = \min(1, P) = 1 \quad \text{(for any } P \ge 1\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 —** *Substitute* for the **One-to-One model**.
In 1:1, each user thread corresponds to one kernel thread:

$$
T_k^{(1:1)} = T_u
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_{\max}^{(1:1)} = \min(T_u, P)
$$

If $T_u \ge P$, the system is **CPU-saturated**; otherwise the process is **thread-starved**.

**Step 4 —** *Substitute* for the **Many-to-Many model**.
In M:M, the developer or system chooses $N$ kernel threads (where $N \le T_u$):

$$
T_k^{(M:M)} = N
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_{\max}^{(M:M)} = \min(N, P)
$$

**Step 5 —** *Interpret* the result.
- M:1 always yields $D_{\max} = 1$ regardless of $P$. **Adding cores does not help.**
- 1:1 yields the highest $D_{\max}$ but with high cost.
- M:M gives a **tunable** trade-off, controlled by $N$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing a Model

The following decision logic is derived from the trade-offs above and is the kind of reasoning KTU examiners award **full marks (7/7)** for in a 14-mark question.

$$
\text{Choose Model} =
\begin{cases}
\text{M:1} & \text{if } \text{ThreadCreationCost} \gg \text{ParallelismRequired} \\
\text{1:1} & \text{if } P \text{ is large AND } T_u \text{ is small-to-moderate} \\
\text{M:M} & \text{if } T_u \text{ is large AND } \text{kernel thread overhead matters}
\end{cases}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Question:** A server application creates 200 user-level threads. The host has 8 CPU cores. Under each of the three models (M:1, 1:1, M:M with $N=12$), compute the maximum parallelism and the *thread-to-core utilization ratio*.

**Step 1 —** Compute $D_{\max} = \min(T_k, P)$ for each model, with $P = 8$.

- M:1: $T_k = 1$ → $D_{\max} = \min(1, 8) = 1$.
- 1:1: $T_k = 200$ → $D_{\max} = \min(200, 8) = 8$.
- M:M (N=12): $T_k = 12$ → $D_{\max} = \min(12, 8) = 8$.

**Step 2 —** Compute utilization $U = D_{\max} / P$.

- M:1: $U = 1 / 8 = 0.125$ (12.5 %).
- 1:1: $U = 8 / 8 = 1.00$ (100 %).
- M:M: $U = 8 / 8 = 1.00$ (100 %).

**Step 3 —** Compute thread creation overhead index $C$ (relative units).
- M:1: $C = 1$ (cheapest — user-space).
- 1:1: $C = T_u = 200$ (200 kernel calls).
- M:M: $C = N = 12$ (only 12 kernel threads created).

**Step 4 —** Final comparative table (model answer-grade).

| Model | $D_{\max}$ | Utilization $U$ | Creation Cost $C$ | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| M:1 | 1 | 12.5 % | 1 | Wastes 7 of 8 cores |
| 1:1 | 8 | 100 % | 200 | Maximum parallelism, highest cost |
| M:M (N=12) | 8 | 100 % | 12 | **Best trade-off** for this workload |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "which model is best?", the answer is **never a single one** — it is *conditional*. KTU expects you to write: *"The best model depends on the ratio of user threads to CPU cores, blocking I/O frequency, and kernel resource limits."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Many-to-One Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserSpace["USER SPACE - Process P"]
        U1["ULT 1"]
        U2["ULT 2"]
        U3["ULT 3"]
        U4["ULT M"]
    end
    subgraph KernelSpace["KERNEL SPACE"]
        K1["KLT 1 (only one)"]
    end
    U1 --> K1
    U2 --> K1
    U3 --> K1
    U4 --> K1
    K1 --> CPU["CPU Core 1"]
```

> The kernel sees only **one** thread. All ULTs must compete for that single KLT — concurrency is *simulated* by the thread library.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: One-to-One Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserSpace2["USER SPACE - Process P"]
        V1["ULT 1"]
        V2["ULT 2"]
        V3["ULT 3"]
        V4["ULT M"]
    end
    subgraph KernelSpace2["KERNEL SPACE"]
        W1["KLT 1"]
        W2["KLT 2"]
        W3["KLT 3"]
        W4["KLT M"]
    end
    V1 --> W1
    V2 --> W2
    V3 --> W3
    V4 --> W4
    W1 --> C1["CPU 1"]
    W2 --> C2["CPU 2"]
    W3 --> C3["CPU 3"]
    W4 --> C4["CPU M"]
```

> Each ULT has its own KLT. The kernel scheduler can dispatch **M** threads to **M** cores in parallel — *true* concurrency.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Many-to-Many Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USpace3["USER SPACE - Process P - M ULTs"]
        A1["ULT 1"]
        A2["ULT 2"]
        A3["ULT 3"]
        A4["ULT 4"]
        A5["ULT 5"]
        A6["ULT M"]
    end
    subgraph KSpace3["KERNEL SPACE - N KLTs (N less than M)"]
        B1["KLT 1"]
        B2["KLT 2"]
        B3["KLT N"]
    end
    A1 -.-> B1
    A2 -.-> B1
    A3 -.-> B2
    A4 -.-> B2
    A5 -.-> B3
    A6 -.-> B3
    B1 --> P1["CPU 1"]
    B2 --> P2["CPU 2"]
    B3 --> P3["CPU N"]
```

> The thread library multiplexes M ULTs onto N KLTs. Blocking one KLT does not block the others — better resilience than M:1.

---

## 4.4 Two-Level Model (Sub-Variant of M:M)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TLSpace["USER SPACE - Two-Level Model"]
        L1["ULT 1 - bound"]
        L2["ULT 2 - pooled"]
        L3["ULT 3 - pooled"]
        L4["ULT 4 - bound"]
        L5["ULT 5 - pooled"]
    end
    subgraph TLKern["KERNEL SPACE"]
        M1["KLT 1 - dedicated to ULT 1"]
        M2["KLT 2 - shared pool"]
        M3["KLT 3 - shared pool"]
        M4["KLT 4 - dedicated to ULT 4"]
    end
    L1 ==> M1
    L4 ==> M4
    L2 -.-> M2
    L3 -.-> M3
    L5 -.-> M2
    M1 --> C1["CPU 1"]
    M4 --> C4["CPU 4"]
    M2 --> C2["CPU 2"]
    M3 --> C3["CPU 3"]
```

> **Bold arrows** = permanent 1:1 binding (critical threads).
> **Dashed arrows** = pooled multiplexing (ordinary threads).

---

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — All Three Models

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Legend["LEGEND"]
        LG1["M ULT - User Level Threads"]
        LG2["N KLT - Kernel Level Threads"]
        LG3["P CPU - Processor Cores"]
    end
    subgraph ModelBlock["THREE MODELS COMPARED"]
        R1["M to 1 - All ULTs share 1 KLT - Parallelism equals 1"]
        R2["1 to 1 - Each ULT has its own KLT - Parallelism equals min M, P"]
        R3["M to N - M ULTs multiplexed onto N KLTs - Parallelism equals min N, P"]
    end
    R1 --> Outcome1["Result - No true parallelism"]
    R2 --> Outcome2["Result - True parallelism, high cost"]
    R3 --> Outcome3["Result - Tunable, balanced"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual takeaway:** All three diagrams share the same legend (ULTs, KLTs, CPUs), but the *wiring* between them changes. The KTU question *"Draw the three multithreading models"* expects exactly this kind of layered block diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | User-Level Thread (ULT) | Kernel-Level Thread (KLT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Managed by** | Thread library in user space | OS kernel |
| **Kernel awareness** | Kernel is unaware of ULTs | Kernel has full knowledge |
| **Creation cost** | Low (no syscall) | High (requires syscall) |
| **Context switch speed** | Fast (within process) | Slower (kernel involved) |
| **Blocking behavior** | Entire process blocks if one ULT blocks a syscall | Only that thread blocks |
| **Multiprocessor support** | Generally no (in M:1) | Yes |

*(1 mark for each correct point; full table earns 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the Many-to-One multithreading model? Why is it unsuitable for multiprocessor systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In the **Many-to-One model**, multiple user-level threads within a process are mapped to a **single kernel thread**. The thread library handles all scheduling in user space. It is unsuitable for multiprocessor systems because the kernel sees only **one kernel thread** for the entire process, so the OS scheduler can dispatch **at most one thread** to one CPU at a time. The remaining processor cores remain idle — **no true parallelism** is achieved, regardless of how many user threads exist. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**Explain the three multithreading models (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many) with neat diagrams. Compare their merits and demerits.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain Many-to-One and One-to-One Models

**Model Solution:**

**1. Many-to-One Model (M:1)** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Definition:** All user-level threads of a process are multiplexed onto a single kernel thread.
- **Mechanism:** The thread library (in user space) maintains a thread table and performs its own scheduling. The kernel is unaware of the multiple ULTs.
- **Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 above — block diagram showing M ULTs → 1 KLT → CPU.)*
- **Advantages:**
  - Very fast thread creation/switching — no kernel mode entry.
  - Portable across operating systems (since the kernel is uninvolved).
- **Disadvantages:**
  - **No parallel execution** on multiprocessor.
  - **Entire process blocks** if any thread makes a blocking system call.
- **Example:** GNU Portable Threads (GNU Pth), early Java Green Threads.

**2. One-to-One Model (1:1)** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Definition:** Each user-level thread corresponds to exactly one kernel-level thread.
- **Mechanism:** Creating a ULT results in a corresponding kernel thread creation via a system call (e.g., `clone()` in Linux, `CreateThread()` in Windows).
- **Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 above.)*
- **Advantages:**
  - True parallel execution — multiple threads of the same process can run on **different CPUs simultaneously**.
  - Blocking one thread does not block others.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Creating a kernel thread is **expensive** (system call overhead).
  - Most OSes limit the number of threads per process to avoid resource exhaustion.
- **Example:** **Windows (Win32)**, **Linux with NPTL**, **Solaris 9 and above**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain Many-to-Many Model and Comparison

**Model Solution:**

**1. Many-to-Many Model (M:M)** *(3.5 marks)*

- **Definition:** $M$ user-level threads are multiplexed onto $N$ kernel threads, where $M \ge N$ and $N$ is typically small (often equal to the number of CPU cores or a fixed value).
- **Mechanism:** The thread library schedules ULTs onto a pool of KLTs. The kernel schedules the KLTs onto CPUs.
- **Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 above.)*
- **Advantages:**
  - Developer can create **as many ULTs as needed** without exhausting kernel resources.
  - True parallel execution on multiprocessors (because $N \ge 2$ typically).
  - Blocking of one KLT does **not** block the entire process.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Complex to implement — requires coordination between user library and kernel.
  - True concurrency is **limited by $N$**, not by the number of ULTs.
- **Variations:**
  - **Two-Level Model:** Specific ULTs can be *bound* to specific KLTs (e.g., for real-time tasks).
- **Example:** **Solaris (versions before 9)**, **IRIX**, **HP-UX**, **Tru64 UNIX**, **Windows with ThreadFiber**.

**2. Comparison Table** *(3.5 marks)*

| Feature | M:1 | 1:1 | M:M |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thread management | User space | Kernel | Hybrid |
| Parallelism on multicore | No | Yes | Yes |
| Creation cost | Low | High | Medium |
| Blocking impact | Whole process | Per thread | Per KLT |
| Examples | GNU Pth | Windows, Linux | Solaris <9, IRIX |

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **14 Marks (Alternative)**

**Discuss thread libraries in detail. Explain Pthreads, Win32 threads, and Java threads. Also describe implicit threading with thread pools.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Thread Libraries (Pthreads, Win32, Java)

**Model Solution:**

A **thread library** provides the programmer with an API for creating and managing threads. Two main approaches exist:
- **Library entirely in user space** (no kernel support beyond existing process).
- **Kernel-level library** (supported by OS system calls).

**1. Pthreads (POSIX Threads — IEEE 1003.1c)** *(2.5 marks)*

- A **standardized** API for thread creation and synchronization (specified by POSIX standard).
- May be provided as either **user-level** or **kernel-level** library depending on implementation.
- On Linux/Solaris/macOS, Pthreads is implemented **in kernel space** (1:1 model).
- Functions: `pthread_create()`, `pthread_exit()`, `pthread_join()`, `pthread_mutex_lock()`, etc.
- **Portable** across UNIX-like systems — the de-facto standard.

**2. Win32 Threads (Windows API)** *(2.5 marks)*

- Provided by the Windows OS kernel; **kernel-level** threads only (1:1 model).
- Functions: `CreateThread()`, `ExitThread()`, `WaitForSingleObject()`, `WaitForMultipleObjects()`.
- Uses objects like **mutexes, semaphores, events, critical sections** for synchronization.
- **Not portable** outside Windows family.

**3. Java Threads** *(2 marks)*

- Built-in support via `java.lang.Thread` class and `java.lang.Runnable` interface.
- On most JVMs, Java threads map to **native OS threads** (using 1:1 model).
- Methods: `start()`, `run()`, `sleep()`, `join()`, `yield()`, `interrupt()`.
- **Cross-platform** — JVM abstracts the underlying thread library.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Implicit Threading & Thread Pools

**Model Solution:**

**1. Why Implicit Threading?** *(1 mark)*

Modern apps create hundreds/thousands of threads. Manual management is error-prone (race conditions, deadlocks). **Implicit threading** transfers thread creation/management to the compiler, runtime, or libraries.

**2. Thread Pools** *(3 marks)*

- **Concept:** Create a *fixed number* of worker threads at startup. Submit tasks to a queue. Idle threads pick up tasks from the queue.
- **Advantages:**
  - Faster service — thread already exists when a request arrives.
  - Bounds resource use — number of threads is capped.
  - Decouples task submission from thread mechanics.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Fixed size may not match dynamic load → possible queuing.
- **Pseudocode logic** (descriptive, not language-specific):

  $$\text{Submit}(T_i):\ \text{Enqueue}(T_i) \longrightarrow \text{Worker thread wakes} \longrightarrow \text{Dequeue \& execute}(T_j)$$

**3. Other Implicit Threading Techniques** *(3 marks)*

- **OpenMP:** Compiler directives (e.g., `#pragma omp parallel`) for parallelizing loops in C/C++/Fortran. Used in HPC.
- **Grand Central Dispatch (GCD):** Apple's technology for macOS/iOS — manages a thread pool and lets developers submit tasks via "blocks" and queues.
- **Threading Building Blocks (TBB):** Intel's C++ template library for parallel programming.
- **Fork-Join Parallelism:** Master thread forks child tasks, which join back (used in Java's `ForkJoinPool`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Drawing the model wrong:** Students often draw the same number of KLTs in M:1 as in 1:1. In M:1, **only one KLT box** should exist. *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
> 2. **Confusing "concurrency" with "parallelism":** Concurrency = logical simultaneity (M:1 has it); Parallelism = physical simultaneity on multiple cores (M:1 lacks it). *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 3. **Forgetting the formula:** $D_{\max} = \min(T_k, P)$ — this single line in a 14-mark answer boosts your score by 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not citing examples:** Always give OS examples (Windows, Linux, Solaris). KTU rewards *applied* knowledge.
> 5. **Ignoring the Two-Level Model:** It is a frequently-asked 3-mark sub-question. Mention it whenever discussing M:M.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield 30-Second Recall Sheet — KTU Module 1**

- ✅ A **multithreading model** defines the **mapping** between **user-level threads** and **kernel-level threads**.
- ✅ **Many-to-One (M:1):** $M$ ULTs → **1** KLT. Fast, but no parallelism; whole process blocks. Example: **GNU Portable Threads**.
- ✅ **One-to-One (1:1):** **1** ULT → **1** KLT. True parallelism, but expensive creation. Examples: **Windows, Linux, Solaris 9+**.
- ✅ **Many-to-Many (M:M):** $M$ ULTs → $N$ KLTs ($M \ge N$). Tunable, balanced. Example: **Solaris < 9, IRIX**.
- ✅ **Two-Level Model:** A variant of M:M where some ULTs are *permanently bound* to specific KLTs.
- ✅ **Maximum parallelism formula:** $D_{\max} = \min(T_k, P)$.
- ✅ M:1 ⇒ $D_{\max} = 1$ (cores wasted).
- ✅ 1:1 ⇒ $D_{\max} = \min(T_u, P)$.
- ✅ M:M ⇒ $D_{\max} = \min(N, P)$.
- ✅ **Thread libraries** provide APIs: **Pthreads (POSIX)**, **Win32**, **Java Threads**.
- ✅ **Implicit threading** shifts thread management from developer to runtimes/libraries.
- ✅ **Thread pools** = pre-created thread pool + task queue. Saves creation cost, bounds resources.
- ✅ Other implicit tools: **OpenMP, GCD (Apple), TBB, Fork-Join**.
- ✅ Modern general-purpose OSes predominantly use the **1:1 model** for scalability on multi-core systems.
- ✅ KTU exam keywords to memorize: *dispatchable unit*, *thread table*, *lightweight process*, *bound thread*.

> **Final Note:** The M:1 / 1:1 / M:M trio is one of the **most repeated** question patterns in KTU OS papers. A clean diagram + the $D_{\max}$ formula + a comparison table is a guaranteed 12+ marks answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: The Linux Implementation of Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study: The Linux Implementation of Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In traditional operating system literature, a **thread** is defined as the smallest unit of CPU execution, comprising a thread ID, program counter (PC), register set, and a stack, sharing the code section, data section, and operating system resources of its parent **process**. However, **Linux breaks this classical abstraction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> *"In Linux, threads are implemented as **lightweight processes (LWPs)**, not as separate entities from processes. Linux does not distinguish between a process and a thread — every execution context is a `task`, represented by the kernel data structure `struct task_struct`. Threads are simply tasks that share resources with other tasks via the `clone()` system call."*

This radical design choice is rooted in the **Unix philosophy** — Linux treats threads as **processes that share resources** rather than as a fundamentally different execution unit.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Shared Family Kitchen

Imagine a large family living in a **single house** (the process), with several family members (the threads) working independently:

- **Each family member has** their own toothbrush, school bag, and to-do list → *unique PC, registers, stack, and PID-like identifier (`task_struct`)*.
- **The whole family shares** the kitchen, living room, pantry, and Wi-Fi password → *shared code, data, heap, and open files*.
- **When a new baby is born**, the family doesn't build a new house — they just add a new member to the same house, sharing most resources → *this is what `clone()` does in Linux: it creates a new task that shares specific resources with its parent*.

If the family were to be split into separate apartments (which is what Windows and older Unix systems do), each apartment would need its own kitchen, Wi-Fi, and pantry — wasteful and slow to set up. Linux's approach is **economical and fast**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Vocabulary for Module 1 (Threads):**

| Term | Meaning in Linux Context |
|---|---|
| **Task** | The fundamental scheduling unit in Linux (a process OR a thread). |
| **`struct task_struct`** | The kernel's PCB/TCB — used for both processes and threads uniformly. |
| **Lightweight Process (LWP)** | Linux's name for a "thread" — a task sharing resources with another task. |
| **`clone()`** | The system call used to create threads in Linux (analogous to `fork()` but with selective sharing flags). |
| **`fork()`** | Traditional Unix system call — creates a new task sharing **virtually nothing** with parent. |
| **`pthread_create()`** | POSIX wrapper around `clone()` used by user-space thread libraries (e.g., NPTL). |
| **NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library)** | The current GNU C Library thread implementation, used on modern Linux. |
| **Kernel Thread** | A task that runs entirely in kernel mode (no user address space). |

## 1.4 Why Linux Took This Approach

The Linux kernel developers (led by **Linus Torvalds** in the early 1990s) deliberately chose this design for three engineering reasons:

1. **Simplicity of the scheduler** — one scheduling entity (`task_struct`) for both processes and threads.
2. **Efficiency of `fork()`/`clone()`** — the same kernel path is reused; only the sharing flags differ.
3. **Avoidance of a "thread subsystem"** — there is no separate thread manager in the Linux kernel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Architectural Insight: `task_struct` as the Universal Abstraction

The **heart** of Linux's thread implementation is `struct task_struct` (defined in `<linux/sched.h>`). Every thread — whether a heavy-weight process, a user thread, or a kernel thread — is described by exactly one `task_struct`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** Linux has **no separate TCB (Thread Control Block)**. The PCB and TCB are unified into a single structure — `task_struct`.

### 2.1.1 Key Fields of `task_struct` (Thread-Relevant Subset)

| Field | Purpose | Thread Implication |
|---|---|---|
| `pid` | Task ID | Each thread gets a **unique PID** (visible via `gettid()`) |
| `tgid` | Thread Group ID | All threads of a process share the **same `tgid`** (the "main" PID) |
| `thread_info` | Low-level architecture data | Per-task CPU state |
| `mm` (pointer) | Memory descriptor | Points to shared `mm_struct` for threads |
| `files` (pointer) | Open file table | Shared across threads of same process |
| `signal` / `sighand` | Signal handling | Shared handlers, per-thread pending mask |
| `stack` | Kernel stack | **Per-thread** (each thread has its own kernel stack) |
| `sched_entity` | Scheduling data | Per-thread — threads are **independently scheduled** |
| `files_struct *files` | File descriptor table | Shared pointer for threads |
| `nsproxy` | Namespace | Often shared with leader |

## 2.2 The `clone()` System Call: The Heart of Thread Creation

Linux creates threads using `clone()`, **not** `fork()` or a Windows-style `CreateThread()`.

### 2.2.1 `clone()` Signature

```c
#include <sched.h>

int clone(int (*fn)(void *), void *child_stack,
          int flags, void *arg, ...);
```

### 2.2.2 The `flags` Argument — The Sharing Selector

The `flags` argument is the **soul** of Linux's thread model. It is a **bitmask** that tells the kernel which resources to **share** between parent and child.

| Flag (Macro) | Resource Shared | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| `CLONE_VM` | Virtual memory (address space) | **Threads (must-have)** |
| `CLONE_FS` | File system info (root, cwd, umask) | **Threads** |
| `CLONE_FILES` | Open file descriptor table | **Threads** |
| `CLONE_SIGHAND` | Signal handlers and blocked signals | **Threads** |
| `CLONE_THREAD` | Thread group (puts child in same `tgid`) | **Threads (defining flag)** |
| `CLONE_NEWNS` | New mount namespace | Containers |
| `CLONE_SYSVSEM` | System V semaphore undo | Threads |
| `CLONE_SETTLS` | Set Thread-Local Storage descriptor | Threads |
| `CLONE_PARENT_SETTID` | Write child TID into parent's memory | Threads |
| `CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID` | Clear child's TID on exit | Threads |
| `CLONE_IO` | Shared I/O context | Threads |

### 2.2.3 The Canonical "Create a Thread" Flag Set

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favourite — the standard Linux thread creation flags:**
> 
> ```
> CLONE_VM | CLONE_FS | CLONE_FILES | CLONE_SIGHAND |
> CLONE_THREAD | CLONE_SETTLS | CLONE_PARENT_SETTID |
> CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID
> ```
> 
> This bitmask tells the kernel: *"Create a new task that shares the address space, file system context, open files, and signal handlers with me, and place it in my thread group."*

## 2.3 `fork()` vs `clone()` vs `pthread_create()`

| Aspect | `fork()` | `clone()` | `pthread_create()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer** | System call | System call | POSIX library call |
| **Sharing** | Almost nothing | Configurable via flags | Thread-appropriate flags |
| **Result** | Heavy-weight process | Anything (process or thread) | POSIX thread |
| **Caller** | User programs | C library / kernel | User thread programs |
| **Modern Linux reality** | Implemented via `clone()` | **The real creation mechanism** | Wraps `clone()` (NPTL) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Since Linux 2.3.3 (2000), `fork()` itself is implemented as a `clone()` call with **all sharing flags cleared** (specifically, with `SIGCHLD` as the only flag). This proves that `clone()` is the **single primitive** for task creation in Linux.

## 2.4 Linux Thread vs Classical Thread: The Conceptual Comparison

| Classical OS Concept | Linux Implementation |
|---|---|
| Process Control Block (PCB) | `task_struct` |
| Thread Control Block (TCB) | **Also `task_struct`** (no separate TCB) |
| Thread creation | `clone()` with sharing flags |
| Process creation | `clone()` with no sharing flags (i.e., `fork()`) |
| Thread ID | `pid` field of `task_struct` |
| Process ID | `tgid` (thread group ID) — same for all threads |
| Scheduler entity | `sched_entity` inside `task_struct` |
| Per-thread kernel stack | Yes, allocated during `clone()` |
| Shared address space | All threads point to the **same `mm_struct`** |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a conceptual case study, the "formulas" are the **invariants** and **resource-sharing rules** that govern Linux threads.

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of unique `mm_struct` per thread group | $\vert \text{mm} \vert = 1$ | Single shared memory descriptor |
| Number of `task_struct` per thread group | $\vert \text{task} \vert = n$ | $n$ = number of threads |
| Number of kernel stacks | $n$ | One per thread |
| Number of PID namespaces | $\geq 1$ | At least 1, shared or per-thread |
| Thread group identity | $\forall t_i, t_j \in \text{group}: \text{tgid}(t_i) = \text{tgid}(t_j)$ | All threads share `tgid` |
| `getpid()` return value | $\text{getpid}() = \text{tgid}$ | Returns thread group ID, not individual `pid` |
| `gettid()` return value | $\text{gettid}() = \text{pid}$ | Returns the **unique** thread ID |
| Relation: `pid` to `tgid` | $\text{pid} \neq \text{tgid}$ (for non-main threads) | Main thread has $\text{pid} = \text{tgid}$ |
| Address space sharing (CLONE_VM) | $\text{addr}_{\text{parent}} = \text{addr}_{\text{child}}$ | Virtual memory map is identical |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Linux Thread Model |
|---|---|
| **Web Servers (Nginx, Apache)** | Each request handled by a thread sharing the event loop's address space |
| **Database Systems (PostgreSQL)** | Worker processes use threads via `clone()` for connection pooling |
| **Container Runtimes (Docker, runC)** | Use `clone()` with `CLONE_NEWNS`, `CLONE_NEWPID`, etc., for OS-level virtualization |
| **High-Performance Computing** | MPI + pthreads leverage `clone()` for hybrid parallelism |
| **Linux Kernel Itself** | Kernel threads (`kthreadd`, `kworker/*`) created via `kthread_create()` (also `clone()` based) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between `fork()` and `clone()`

This derivation shows **mathematically and operationally** how `fork()` is a special case of `clone()`.

### 3.1.1 Logical Foundation

Let $T_{\text{parent}}$ be a task with `task_struct` $\tau_p$. A child task $T_{\text{child}}$ is created via a kernel primitive $C$:

$$T_{\text{child}} = C(\tau_p, F, A)$$

where:
- $F$ is the set of sharing flags (bitmask),
- $A$ is the argument passed to the child.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Reduction

**Step 1 — Define the resource set $R$ of a task.**

$$R = \{ \text{mm}, \text{fs}, \text{files}, \text{sighand}, \text{thread\_group}, \text{io}, \text{sysvsem}, \ldots \}$$

**Step 2 — Define "Heavy-weight Process Creation" (the `fork()` semantics).**

A heavy-weight process shares **no** resources with its parent:

$$\forall r \in R: r_{\text{child}} \leftarrow \text{copy\_of}(r_{\text{parent}})$$

i.e., every resource is **duplicated**, not shared.

**Step 3 — Express `fork()` as `clone()` with all sharing flags cleared.**

$$\text{fork}() \equiv \text{clone}(\text{fn}, \text{stack}, 0, \text{arg})$$

In Linux source (`kernel/fork.c`):

$$\text{fork}() \rightarrow \text{\_do\_fork}(\text{SIGCHLD}, 0, 0, NULL, NULL)$$

$$\text{clone}(\text{flags}) \rightarrow \text{\_do\_fork}(\text{flags}, 0, 0, NULL, NULL)$$

The difference is solely in the `flags` argument. Setting `flags = SIGCHLD` (which is `0x11`) is the **only sharing flag** — and `SIGCHLD` does not actually share anything, it merely tells the kernel to send `SIGCHLD` to the parent on termination.

**Step 4 — Define "Thread Creation" (the `pthread_create()` semantics).**

A thread shares a specific subset $R_{\text{shared}} \subset R$:

$$R_{\text{shared}} = \{ \text{mm}, \text{fs}, \text{files}, \text{sighand}, \text{thread\_group} \}$$

$$\forall r \in R_{\text{shared}}: r_{\text{child}} \leftarrow r_{\text{parent}} \quad \text{(shared, not copied)}$$

$$\forall r \in R \setminus R_{\text{shared}}: r_{\text{child}} \leftarrow \text{new\_instance}()$$

**Step 5 — Express `pthread_create()` as `clone()` with the canonical flag set.**

$$\text{pthread\_create}() \equiv \text{clone}(\text{fn}, \text{stack}, F_{\text{thread}}, \text{arg})$$

where:

$$F_{\text{thread}} = \text{CLONE\_VM} \mid \text{CLONE\_FS} \mid \text{CLONE\_FILES} \mid \text{CLONE\_SIGHAND} \mid \text{CLONE\_THREAD} \mid \text{CLONE\_SETTLS} \mid \text{CLONE\_PARENT\_SETTID} \mid \text{CLONE\_CHILD\_CLEARTID}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion of the derivation.**

We have shown:

$$\boxed{\text{fork}() = \text{clone}(F = \text{SIGCHLD}) \quad \text{and} \quad \text{pthread\_create}() = \text{clone}(F = F_{\text{thread}})}$$

Therefore, **`clone()` is the single, unified creation primitive** in Linux. The distinction between a "process" and a "thread" is purely a **matter of which flags are passed** — there is no architectural separation in the kernel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Address Space Sharing Semantics (CLONE_VM)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Prove that when `CLONE_VM` is set, parent and child share the **same** virtual-to-physical memory mappings, and that any memory modification by one is immediately visible to the other.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Memory representation in Linux.**

Each task has a pointer `task->mm` of type `struct mm_struct *`. This struct holds:
- The page tables (PGD, PUD, PMD, PTE),
- List of VMAs (Virtual Memory Areas),
- Reference count `mm->mm_users` and `mm->mm_count`.

**Step 2 — Without `CLONE_VM` (the `fork()` case).**

In `dup_mm()` (kernel function):

$$\text{mm}_{\text{child}} = \text{copy\_mm}(\text{mm}_{\text{parent}})$$

The page tables are **deep-copied** using **Copy-on-Write (COW)**:

$$\text{PTE}_{\text{child, page}} = \text{PTE}_{\text{parent, page}} \cdot \text{COW\_FLAG}$$

Modifications by either side trigger a page fault and a private copy.

**Step 3 — With `CLONE_VM` (the thread case).**

The kernel function `copy_mm()` is **skipped**, and:

$$\text{task}_{\text{child}} \rightarrow \text{mm} = \text{task}_{\text{parent}} \rightarrow \text{mm}$$

Increment the reference count:

$$\text{mm} \rightarrow \text{mm\_users} \mathrel{+}= 1$$

**Step 4 — Consequence: instant visibility of writes.**

Let $v$ be a virtual address mapped to physical frame $p$ in the shared `mm_struct`. After a write by the parent:

$$\text{PTE}_{\text{parent}}(v) = \text{PTE}_{\text{child}}(v) \quad \text{(same PTE, same physical frame)}$$

Therefore, a subsequent read by the child returns the parent's value:

$$\text{read}_{\text{child}}(v) = \text{write}_{\text{parent}}(v)$$

**Q.E.D.** — Memory writes by one thread are **immediately** visible to all threads in the same thread group.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Resource Ownership in Linux Threads

| Resource | Heavy Process (`fork()`) | Thread (`clone()` with thread flags) | Kernel Thread |
|---|---|---|---|
| `task_struct` | New (per process) | New (per thread) | New (per kernel thread) |
| `mm_struct` (address space) | New (COW copy) | **Shared** (same pointer) | **None** (kernel threads have no user mm) |
| Kernel stack | New | **New** (per thread) | New |
| User stack | New | New (each thread gets one) | N/A |
| Open file table (`files_struct`) | New (COW) | **Shared** | N/A |
| File system context (`fs_struct`) | New (COW) | **Shared** | N/A |
| Signal handlers (`sighand_struct`) | New (COW) | **Shared** | Per-kernel-thread |
| Pending signals mask | Per-task | **Per-task** (each thread has its own) | Per-kernel-thread |
| PID | Unique | Unique `pid`, **shared `tgid`** | Unique |
| Scheduling parameters | Per-task | Per-task (threads independently scheduled) | Per-task |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The only resources that are **truly per-thread** (not shared) are:
> 1. The `task_struct` itself (registers, kernel stack, scheduling data).
> 2. The user-mode stack.
> 3. The pending signal mask.
> 4. The thread-local storage (TLS) pointer.
> 
> Everything else is **shared** with the thread group leader.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Lifecycle of a Linux Thread

Let us trace the complete lifecycle of a thread created by `pthread_create()`:

**Step 1 — Library call.** `pthread_create()` in `glibc`/`NPTL` is invoked by the user program.

**Step 2 — Stack allocation.** NPTL allocates a new user-mode stack (typically **8 MB** virtual, **4 KB** initially committed) using `mmap()`.

**Step 3 — TLS setup.** A Thread-Local Storage block is initialized and its address will be passed via `CLONE_SETTLS`.

**Step 4 — `clone()` invocation.** NPTL issues:

$$\text{clone}(\text{start\_routine}, \text{new\_stack}, F_{\text{thread}}, \text{arg})$$

**Step 5 — Kernel's `do_fork()`.** The kernel:
- Allocates a new `struct pid` (via `alloc_pid()`).
- Allocates a new kernel stack (typically **8 KB** or **16 KB**).
- Copies the parent's `task_struct` (`copy_process()`).
- Sets `task->mm = current->mm` (because of `CLONE_VM`).
- Sets `task->files = current->files` (because of `CLONE_FILES`).
- Sets `task->tgid = current->tgid` (because of `CLONE_THREAD`).
- Writes the child's `pid` into the parent's memory (because of `CLONE_PARENT_SETTID`).
- Adds the new task to the scheduler's runqueue.
- The new task is now **runnable**.

**Step 6 — Thread begins execution.** The child starts at `start routine`, which calls the user's thread function.

**Step 7 — Thread termination.** When the function returns, NPTL calls `__pthread_exit()`, which:
- Sets the thread's exit value.
- Atomically clears the `pid` at the address given by `CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID` (this is the **futex wake** that joins the thread).
- Calls `sys_exit()` → `do_exit()`.

**Step 8 — Cleanup.** The kernel releases the `task_struct`, decrements `mm_struct` reference count, frees the kernel stack, and notifies the parent (or thread group) via signals.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Linux vs Windows vs Solaris

| Feature | **Linux** | **Windows** | **Solaris (Classic)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thread = Process? | **Yes** (lightweight process) | No (separate `ETHREAD`) | No (LWP is one of many models) |
| Kernel-level threads? | Yes (`kernel threads`) | Yes | Yes |
| Native thread API | `clone()` | `CreateThread()` / `NtCreateThread()` | `thread_create()` |
| POSIX support | Yes (NPTL) | Via Cygwin/WSL | Yes |
| Thread = schedulable entity? | Yes (independent `sched_entity`) | Yes | Yes (LWP) |
| Process = collection of threads | Yes (thread group) | Yes (job / process) | Yes |
| Scheduler structure | CFS runqueue (per-CPU) | Multilevel feedback queue | Multilevel feedback with dispatch classes |
| Lightweight Process concept | **Native** | Not used | Used (intermediate layer) |

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Thread Creation via `clone()`

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Program
    participant P as pthread_create (NPTL)
    participant K as Linux Kernel
    participant S as Scheduler
    participant N as New Thread

    U->>P: pthread_create(&tid, attr, fn, arg)
    activate P
    P->>P: Allocate user stack via mmap (8 MB virtual, 4 KB committed)
    P->>P: Initialize TLS block
    P->>K: clone(fn, new_stack, F_thread, arg)
    activate K
    K->>K: copy_process(flags)
    K->>K: alloc_pid() -> new pid
    K->>K: Allocate kernel stack (8 KB)
    K->>K: task->mm = current->mm (CLONE_VM)
    K->>K: task->files = current->files (CLONE_FILES)
    K->>K: task->sighand = current->sighand (CLONE_SIGHAND)
    K->>K: task->tgid = current->tgid (CLONE_THREAD)
    K->>K: Write tid to parent memory (CLONE_PARENT_SETTID)
    K->>K: Set up TLS descriptor (CLONE_SETTLS)
    K->>S: Add new task_struct to runqueue
    deactivate K
    P-->>U: Return 0 (success)
    deactivate P
    S->>N: Wake up new thread
    activate N
    N->>N: Execute start_thread -> fn(arg)
    N->>K: __pthread_exit (via syscall)
    K->>K: do_exit() -> release task_struct
    K->>P: Futex wake on CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID address
    deactivate N
```
