# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Explain how Linux implements threads. How does this differ from the classical thread model used in other operating systems?**

**Model Answer (10-12 lines):**

Linux does not treat threads as fundamentally different from processes. Both are represented by the same kernel data structure — `struct task_struct` — and are referred to as **tasks**. A thread is simply a task that **shares selected resources** (such as the address space, file descriptor table, and signal handlers) with another task.

Threads in Linux are created using the `clone()` system call, which is a generalization of the traditional `fork()` call. By specifying flags such as `CLONE_VM`, `CLONE_FILES`, `CLONE_SIGHAND`, and `CLONE_THREAD`, the programmer controls exactly which resources the new task shares with its parent. The `clone()` call used internally by the NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library) for `pthread_create()` uses the **canonical flag set** to create a POSIX-compliant thread.

In contrast, the classical thread model (used in Windows, Solaris, and textbooks like Silberschatz) distinguishes clearly between a **process** (with a PCB) and a **thread** (with a separate TCB). Linux's approach is more uniform: there is no separate TCB, and the same scheduler handles both processes and threads identically. This is the key architectural difference.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

**What is the role of the `clone()` system call in Linux? List the flags typically used to create a thread.**

**Model Answer (8-10 lines):**

The `clone()` system call is the **fundamental task creation primitive** in Linux. It is used to create both processes and threads — the difference lies entirely in the flags passed. `clone()` is a generalization of `fork()` and is more flexible because the caller can specify which resources (memory, file descriptors, signal handlers, etc.) should be shared between parent and child.

The flags typically used to create a thread are:

- `CLONE_VM` — share the virtual address space (memory).
- `CLONE_FS` — share the file system context (root, CWD).
- `CLONE_FILES` — share the open file descriptor table.
- `CLONE_SIGHAND` — share signal handlers and blocked-signal mask.
- `CLONE_THREAD` — place the new task in the parent's thread group (same `tgid`).
- `CLONE_SETTLS` — set up Thread-Local Storage.
- `CLONE_PARENT_SETTID` — write the child's TID to a parent-specified location.
- `CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID` — clear the child's TID on exit (used for joining via futex).

In modern glibc (NPTL), `pthread_create()` is implemented as a `clone()` call with these flags.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **Linux thread architecture**. Describe the role of `struct task_struct` and list the resources that are **shared** versus those that are **per-thread**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **`clone()` system call** in detail. Explain how it is used to implement both `fork()` and `pthread_create()`. List the major sharing flags and explain the significance of `CLONE_VM` and `CLONE_THREAD`. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The Linux thread architecture is based on the principle that **threads and processes are unified at the kernel level**. Both are represented by `struct task_struct` (the kernel's process/thread control block), and the same scheduler treats them identically.

**Diagram description** (refer to Section 4.1 above): Multiple `task_struct` entries belong to the same **thread group** (identified by a common `tgid`). Each task has its own kernel stack, user stack, register set, and TLS, but they all point to the **same shared resources**: `mm_struct` (address space), `fs_struct` (file system context), `files_struct` (open file table), and `sighand_struct` (signal handlers).

**Shared resources** (with corresponding `clone` flag):
- Address space → `CLONE_VM`
- File system context → `CLONE_FS`
- Open file table → `CLONE_FILES`
- Signal handlers → `CLONE_SIGHAND`
- Thread group identity → `CLONE_THREAD`

**Per-thread resources**:
- `task_struct` itself (PC, register snapshot, scheduling data)
- Kernel stack (allocated per thread, typically 8–16 KB)
- User-mode stack
- Thread-Local Storage (TLS)
- Pending signal mask
- Unique `pid` (though `getpid()` returns the shared `tgid`)

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

The `clone()` system call is the **single, unified task creation mechanism** in Linux. Its signature is:

```c
int clone(int (*fn)(void *), void *child_stack,
          int flags, void *arg, ...);
```

The **`flags`** argument is a bitmask that determines which resources the new task shares with its parent.

**`fork()` is a special case of `clone()`**: In the Linux kernel source (`kernel/fork.c`), `fork()` is implemented as a call to `_do_fork(SIGCHLD, 0, 0, NULL, NULL)`, which is exactly what `clone()` with `flags = SIGCHLD` does. With these flags, no resources are shared — every resource is duplicated (with COW where applicable). Therefore, `fork()` produces a **heavy-weight process**.

**`pthread_create()` is another special case**: NPTL (the GNU C Library's thread implementation) implements `pthread_create()` as:

```c
clone(start_routine, new_stack, F_thread, arg)
```

where `F_thread` is the canonical flag set listed in Section 2.2.3. This produces a **thread** — a task that shares the parent's address space, file descriptors, signal handlers, and thread group identity.

**Significance of `CLONE_VM`**: When set, the new task uses the **same `mm_struct`** as the parent. This means:
- Both tasks share the same virtual-to-physical memory mappings.
- Writes by one are immediately visible to the other (no COW for shared pages).
- The `mm->mm_users` reference count is incremented.

**Significance of `CLONE_THREAD`**: When set, the new task is placed in the **same thread group** as the parent. This means:
- The new task inherits the parent's `tgid` (thread group ID).
- `getpid()` called from the new task returns the parent's `tgid`, not the new task's `pid`.
- The new task's `pid` is still unique and obtained via `gettid()`.
- Signal delivery to the thread group is handled collectively.

**Conclusion:** `clone()` is the architectural primitive that unifies processes and threads in Linux. There is no separate "thread creation" path in the kernel.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **relationship between `fork()`, `clone()`, and `pthread_create()`** in Linux. How does Linux's thread implementation differ from the classical process–thread model? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **per-thread and shared resources** in a Linux thread group. What is the role of `struct task_struct`, and how are `pid` and `tgid` used to identify threads and processes? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

In the Linux kernel, **`fork()` and `pthread_create()` are both implemented using the `clone()` system call**. The differences between them are entirely determined by the **flags** passed to `clone()`.

**`fork()`**: Since kernel version 2.3.3, `fork()` is implemented in glibc via a call to `clone()` with `flags = SIGCHLD` (and other flags cleared). With these flags, the new task does **not** share any resources with the parent — it receives a copy of the parent's `mm_struct` (Copy-on-Write), `files_struct`, `fs_struct`, etc. The new task is given a **new `tgid`** (which is the same as its new `pid`). The result is a **heavy-weight process** in the classical Unix sense.

**`pthread_create()`**: This POSIX function is implemented by NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library) as a call to `clone()` with the canonical thread flag set (`CLONE_VM | CLONE_FS | CLONE_FILES | CLONE_SIGHAND | CLONE_THREAD | CLONE_SETTLS | CLONE_PARENT_SETTID | CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID`). The new task shares the parent's address space, file descriptors, file system context, and signal handlers. Critically, the new task is placed in the **same thread group** as the parent (same `tgid`). The result is a **POSIX thread** (also called a **lightweight process** in Linux terminology).

**`clone()`**: This is the **underlying primitive**. It exposes the kernel's actual task creation mechanism to user space. Programmers can use `clone()` directly to create processes, threads, or hybrid execution contexts (e.g., tasks that share memory but not file descriptors — used in some parallel computing frameworks).

**Difference from the classical model**: In the classical process–thread model (as described in Silberschatz, Tanenbaum, and Windows internals), a **process** is a heavyweight unit with a Process Control Block (PCB), and a **thread** is a lightweight unit with a separate Thread Control Block (TCB). The OS provides distinct APIs (`CreateProcess` and `CreateThread` on Windows) and distinct kernel data structures.

Linux's model is **radically simpler**: there is **only one data structure** (`task_struct`) for both. The distinction between a "process" and a "thread" is **purely a user-space concept**; the kernel sees only tasks. This design simplifies the scheduler, the memory manager, and the signal subsystem. It also means `fork()` and `pthread_create()` share most of the kernel code path, which improves performance and maintainability.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

In a Linux thread group, resources are divided into **two categories**: those that are **per-thread** (unique to each task) and those that are **shared** (common to all tasks in the thread group).

**Per-thread resources** (held by the individual `task_struct`):
1. **Thread ID (`pid`)** — unique across the system; obtained via `gettid()`.
2. **Register set** — saved/restored during context switches.
3. **Kernel stack** — typically 8 KB or 16 KB; used for syscall handling and kernel-mode execution.
4. **User-mode stack** — provided by the thread library (NPTL allocates 8 MB virtual, 4 KB committed).
5. **Thread-Local Storage (TLS)** — a per-thread data region set up via `CLONE_SETTLS`.
6. **Pending signal mask** — each thread has its own set of pending signals.
7. **Scheduling parameters** — each thread is independently scheduled (`sched_entity` in CFS).
8. **CPU affinity** — can differ per thread (set via `sched_setaffinity()`).
9. **Robust futex list head** — for `pthread_mutex` deadlock recovery.

**Shared resources** (pointed to by each `task_struct`):
1. **Address space (`mm_struct`)** — shared via `CLONE_VM`; same virtual-to-physical mappings for all threads.
2. **File system context (`fs_struct`)** — root directory, current working directory, umask; shared via `CLONE_FS`.
3. **Open file descriptor table (`files_struct`)** — shared via `CLONE_FILES`; opening a file in one thread makes it visible to all.
4. **Signal handlers (`sighand_struct`)** — shared via `CLONE_SIGHAND`; the handler for a given signal is the same for all threads.
5. **Process ID (`tgid`)** — the thread group ID; `getpid()` returns this value.
6. **Process credentials** — UID, GID, capabilities (shared unless explicitly changed).
7. **Supplicant privileges** — seccomp filters, AppArmor profiles (process-level).

**Role of `struct task_struct`**: This is the **central data structure** of the Linux kernel for representing any schedulable entity. It contains:
- Identification: `pid`, `tgid`, `comm` (name).
- State: `state` (running, sleeping, stopped, zombie).
- Scheduling: `sched_entity`, `prio`, `policy`, `cpus_allowed`.
- Memory: pointer `mm` to the address space.
- Files: pointer `files` to the file descriptor table.
- Signals: `pending`, `blocked`, `sighand`.
- Relationships: `real_parent`, `children`, `sibling`, `thread_group` list head.

**`pid` vs `tgid`**: In a multi-threaded process, each thread has a **unique `pid`** (visible to the kernel and via `gettid()`), but they all share the **same `tgid`** (the PID of the original process, returned by `getpid()`). The `tgid` is what external tools (e.g., `ps`, `top`) display as the "process ID". The `task_struct->thread_group` is a linked list that connects all threads of a process through their `thread_node` field. The leader of the thread group is the first task (typically the one created by `fork()` or the main thread); it has `pid == tgid`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing `pid` with `tgid`**: Students often claim "threads share the same PID". This is technically false at the kernel level. They share the same `tgid`, and `getpid()` (the POSIX API) returns the `tgid`. The internal `pid` is unique per thread.
> 2. **Saying Linux uses a separate TCB**: Linux has **no separate TCB**; `task_struct` is the unified PCB/TCB.
> 3. **Forgetting per-thread kernel stacks**: Each thread needs its own kernel stack because threads can independently enter the kernel (e.g., via system calls) without blocking each other.
> 4. **Confusing `CLONE_VM` with shared memory**: `CLONE_VM` is much more than POSIX shared memory (`shm_open`); it shares the **entire** address space.
> 5. **Saying `fork()` is independent of `clone()`**: In modern Linux, `fork()` is literally a `clone()` call with no sharing flags. This is a high-yield point.
> 6. **Mixing up user threads and kernel threads**: A **kernel thread** in Linux is a task that runs only in kernel mode (no user `mm_struct`), created via `kthread_create()`. It is **not** the same as a "user-space thread" running in kernel mode.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linux Thread Implementation (Module 1):**

- **Core Thesis**: Linux does **not** distinguish between a process and a thread at the kernel level. Both are **tasks** represented by `struct task_struct`.

- **The Magic Word is `clone()`**: This is the **single primitive** for creating any execution context in Linux. `fork()` and `pthread_create()` are both wrappers around `clone()` with different flags.

- **Sharing Flags for Threads** (must remember): `CLONE_VM`, `CLONE_FS`, `CLONE_FILES`, `CLONE_SIGHAND`, `CLONE_THREAD`. Additional: `CLONE_SETTLS`, `CLONE_PARENT_SETTID`, `CLONE_CHILD_CLEARTID`.

- **`CLONE_VM`** → shares address space (`mm_struct`). All threads in a group point to the **same** memory descriptor.

- **`CLONE_THREAD`** → places the new task in the parent's thread group. This is the **defining flag** that turns a `clone()` call into "thread creation".

- **`pid` vs `tgid`**: `pid` is unique per thread; `tgid` is shared by all threads of a process. `getpid()` returns `tgid`; `gettid()` returns `pid`.

- **Per-thread (unique)**:
  - `task_struct`
  - Kernel stack
  - User stack
  - Register set
  - TLS
  - Pending signal mask
  - `pid`

- **Shared (common to thread group)**:
  - `mm_struct` (address space)
  - `fs_struct` (file system info)
  - `files_struct` (file descriptor table)
  - `sighand_struct` (signal handlers)
  - `tgid` (thread group ID)
  - Credentials (UID, GID)

- **Why This Design?**: Simplicity, code reuse, scheduler uniformity, and efficiency. There is **no separate thread manager** in the kernel.

- **NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library)**: The modern glibc thread implementation. It uses `clone()` with the canonical flag set. It replaced the older **LinuxThreads** (which used separate processes for threads and was non-POSIX-compliant).

- **Kernel Threads**: Created by `kthread_create()`. Have **no user `mm_struct`**. They run entirely in kernel mode and perform system-level work (e.g., `kworker`, `kthreadd`, `migration`).

- **Container Namespaces**: `clone()` with flags like `CLONE_NEWNS`, `CLONE_NEWPID`, `CLONE_NEWNET` is what powers Docker, Kubernetes, and other container technologies.

- **Signal Handling Caveat**: While signal **handlers** are shared, signal **delivery** is per-thread (or per-process, via `tgid`). The `tgid` is used by the kernel to deliver process-targeted signals.

- **Scheduling Independence**: Each thread has its own `sched_entity` and is **independently scheduled** by the CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler). Threads of the same process can run on **different CPU cores** simultaneously.

- **Historical Note**: Before NPTL (Linux 2.4), Linux used **LinuxThreads**, which had many problems (signals, `getpid()`, PIDs in `/proc`). NPTL (introduced around Linux 2.6) fixed these by using the modern `clone()`-based model.

- **KTU Favourite Comparison**: Be ready to compare Linux's unified model with **Windows** (separate `ETHREAD`/`KTHREAD`) and **Solaris** (LWP as intermediate layer between user threads and kernel threads).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Scheduling: Concepts and Basic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Process Scheduling: Concepts and Basic Algorithms # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Process Scheduling** is the fundamental activity of the operating system (specifically the **CPU Scheduler** / **Short-Term Scheduler**) that selects one of the processes in the **Ready Queue** and dispatches it to the CPU for execution. The selection is governed by a **Scheduling Algorithm** whose goal is to make the most efficient use of the CPU while satisfying user-centric performance objectives. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, process scheduling is studied as a **resource-allocation problem** in multiprogrammed and time-sharing systems. Since the CPU is a **non-preemptible shared resource** (in non-preemptive mode) or a **preemptible resource** (in preemptive mode), the OS must make a *scheduling decision* at four key moments: 1. A process switches from **Running → Waiting** (e.g., I/O request). 2. A process switches from **Running → Ready** (e.g., interrupt, preemption). 3. A process switches from **Waiting → Ready** (e.g., I/O completion). 4. A process **terminates**. > [!NOTE] > **Preemptive Scheduling**: The OS can forcibly remove the CPU from a running process. **Non-Preemptive (Cooperative) Scheduling**: Once the CPU is given to a process, it runs until it terminates or voluntarily waits. KTU examiners expect students to clearly state the preemption boundary of every algorithm. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Single Doctor, Many Patients" Model Imagine a **single doctor (CPU)** in a government clinic and **50 patients (processes)** waiting in the lobby (Ready Queue). Each patient has: - An arrival time (when they entered the lobby). - A burst time (consultation duration). - A priority (emergency case vs. routine check-up). - A tolerance for waiting. The doctor cannot treat all 50 simultaneously. The **receptionist (OS Scheduler)** must decide: - *Who goes in next?* — This is the **scheduling algorithm**. - *Can a patient be pulled out mid-consultation if a more critical case arrives?* — This is **preemption**. If the receptionist uses **First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)**, the patient who came earliest goes first, even if a heart-attack case is behind them. If **Round Robin (RR)** is used, each patient gets a fixed time-slot — fair, but possibly slow. If **Shortest Job First (SJF)** is used, the patient with the shortest consultation goes first, minimizing *average waiting*. If **Priority Scheduling** is used, emergencies jump the queue. This analogy maps directly to: - **Doctor** ↔ **CPU** - **Patient** ↔ **Process** - **Consultation** ↔ **CPU Burst** - **Receptionist** ↔ **Short-Term Scheduler** - **Lobby** ↔ **Ready Queue** ## 1.3 CPU Scheduling Criteria — The "Five Performance Goals" > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists these **five scheduling criteria** as high-yield exam material. Every algorithm is evaluated *against* these criteria. | # | Criterion | Definition | Optimization Goal | |---|-----------|------------|-------------------| | 1 | **CPU Utilization** | Percentage of time the CPU is busy doing useful work. | **Maximize** | | 2 | **Throughput** | Number of processes completed per unit time. | **Maximize** | | 3 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | Total time from process arrival to completion: $TAT = CT - AT$. | **Minimize** | | 4 | **Waiting Time (WT)** | Total time a process spent in the Ready Queue: $WT = TAT - BT$. | **Minimize** | | 5 | **Response Time (RT)** | Time from arrival to first CPU allocation: $RT = ST - AT$. | **Minimize** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metrics (must be memorized):** > - $BT$ = Burst Time (CPU execution time). > - $AT$ = Arrival Time. > - $ST$ = Start Time (first time CPU is allocated). > - $CT$ = Completion Time. > - $TAT = CT - AT$ > - $WT = TAT - BT$ > - $RT = ST - AT$ ## 1.4 Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive — The Core Distinction | Property | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive | |----------|----------------|------------| | Interrupt mid-burst? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Algorithms | FCFS, SJF (non-preemptive), Priority (non-preemptive) | SRTF, Priority (preemptive), Round Robin | | Complexity | Low | Higher (requires context-switch handling) | | KTU Verdict | "Easier to implement" | "Better for time-sharing systems" | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Scheduling Decision Pipeline The CPU scheduler follows a precise 4-stage logical flow every time a scheduling event occurs: 1. **Event Detection** — A scheduling trigger occurs (process termination, I/O wait, preemption timer expiry, I/O completion). 2. **Ready Queue Update** — Affected processes are moved between Running, Ready, and Waiting queues by the **Dispatcher** (software). 3. **Algorithm Invocation** — The scheduling algorithm scans the Ready Queue and applies its selection rule. 4. **Context Switch & Dispatch** — The selected process is loaded into the CPU; the old process's state is saved in its **PCB (Process Control Block)**. > [!NOTE] > **Dispatcher Latency** (KTU frequently asked): The time taken by the dispatcher to stop one process and start another. It includes saving state, loading new state, and switching to user mode. **Dispatch Latency must be minimized** for a responsive system. ## 2.2 Detailed Scheduling Criteria (The "Why" Behind Each Metric) - **CPU Utilization (Maximize)** → In a real system, the ideal is **40% (light load) to 90% (heavy load)**. Below 40% indicates under-utilization; above 90% indicates a starvation / queue-buildup danger zone. - **Throughput (Maximize)** → Depends on process length. For long processes, throughput is inherently low. The scheduler cannot artificially inflate throughput — it can only *not waste* cycles. - **Turnaround Time (Minimize)** → Includes waiting time, execution time, and I/O time. $TAT = CT - AT$. - **Waiting Time (Minimize)** → Pure time spent *not running*. This is the **only metric a CPU scheduler directly controls**. $WT = TAT - BT$. - **Response Time (Minimize)** → Critical for **interactive systems** (e.g., GUI). $RT = ST - AT$. RR excels here. ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Algorithms ### A. First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) - **Rule**: Select the process that arrived earliest. - **Logic**: Simplest possible queue discipline (FIFO). The Ready Queue is a plain FIFO. - **Strength**: Trivial to implement, fair in the temporal sense. - **Weakness**: Suffers from the **Convoy Effect** — one long process delays all short ones behind it. - **Preemption**: Non-preemptive. ### B. Shortest Job First (SJF) / Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) - **Rule**: Select the process with the shortest CPU burst (SJF) or the shortest *remaining* burst (SRTF). - **Logic**: Minimizes the *average* waiting time — this is **provably optimal** for the WT metric when all arrivals are at time 0. - **Strength**: Provably optimal average WT. - **Weakness**: **Starvation** of long processes; requires knowledge of future burst time (impractical in real systems — usually estimated using the exponential averaging formula: $\tau_{n+1} = \alpha t_n + (1-\alpha)\tau_n$). - **Preemption**: SJF is non-preemptive; SRTF is preemptive. ### C. Priority Scheduling - **Rule**: Each process has a priority number; select the process with the highest priority (lowest number if 0 is highest, or vice-versa — **KTU convention: lower number = higher priority**). - **Logic**: Reflects real-world urgency (kernel > user, real-time > batch). - **Strength**: Supports the "importance" hierarchy. - **Weakness**: **Starvation / Indefinite Blocking** of low-priority processes. **Solution**: **Aging** — gradually increase the priority of long-waiting processes. - **Preemption**: Both versions exist. ### D. Round Robin (RR) - **Rule**: Each process gets a fixed time slice called a **Time Quantum (TQ)**. After TQ expires, the process is preempted and placed at the back of the Ready Queue. - **Logic**: Designed for **time-sharing systems**. Balances fairness and responsiveness. - **Strength**: Excellent Response Time, no starvation, fair CPU share. - **Weakness**: Performance depends heavily on TQ choice. - If **TQ is very large** → RR degenerates into FCFS. - If **TQ is very small** → Excessive context-switching overhead, lower throughput. - **Rule of thumb**: TQ should be slightly larger than the time required to handle a typical interaction (~10–100 ms). - **Preemption**: Preemptive (TQ-based). ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table before any numerical question. KTU ESE numericals are 80% formula application, 20% arithmetic.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes | |---|----------|---------|------|-------| | 1 | Turnaround Time | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | seconds / ms | Always non-negative | | 2 | Waiting Time | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | seconds / ms | This is what we minimize | | 3 | Response Time | $RT_i = ST_i - AT_i$ | seconds / ms | Only for first CPU allocation | | 4 | Average TAT | $\overline{TAT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | seconds / ms | Standard performance metric | | 5 | Average WT | $\overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | seconds / ms | **SJ(S)F is optimal here** | | 6 | CPU Utilization | $\frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Higher is better | | 7 | Throughput | $\frac{n}{\text{Total Time}}$ | processes/unit time | Higher is better | | 8 | Burst Time Estimation (Exp. Avg.) | $\tau_{n+1} = \alpha \cdot t_n + (1-\alpha)\tau_n$ | seconds | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | | 9 | Normalized TAT | $NTAT_i = \frac{TAT_i}{BT_i}$ | dimensionless | Useful for comparing different workloads | | 10 | CPU–I/O Burst Cycle | — | — | Alternating sequence; scheduler only sees CPU bursts | **Real-world utility**: These metrics are the **foundation of every modern OS scheduler** — from the Linux **CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)** to Windows' **MLFQ (Multilevel Feedback Queue)**. Real-time operating systems (RTOS) like VxWorks and QNX use variants of priority + RR for hard deadlines in avionics, medical devices, and automotive ECUs. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Gantt Charts & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Universal Problem-Solving Method (7-Step KTU-Approved Recipe) > [!NOTE] > Use this exact 7-step procedure for every scheduling numerical in the KTU 2024 ESE. Examiners look for these steps when awarding partial marks. 1. **Tabulate** the given $(AT_i, BT_i, Priority_i)$ for all processes. 2. **Identify** the scheduling algorithm and preemption mode. 3. **Construct the Gantt Chart** by applying the algorithm's rule at *every* time unit boundary. 4. **Record** the Start Time ($ST_i$) and Completion Time ($CT_i$) for each process from the Gantt chart. 5. **Compute** $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ for every process. 6. **Compute** $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ for every process. 7. **Compute** Averages: $\overline{TAT}$ and $\overline{WT}$. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — FCFS (Non-Preemptive) **Problem:** Consider the following 4 processes: | Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) | |---------|-------------------|-----------------| | P1 | 0 | 7 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | | P3 | 2 | 2 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | **Step 1 — Apply FCFS (non-preemptive).** Order = P1 → P2 → P3 → P4. **Step 2 — Gantt Chart Construction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Time:} \quad & 0 \longrightarrow 7 \longrightarrow 11 \longrightarrow 13 \longrightarrow 14 \\ \text{Process:} \quad & \vert\;\; P1 \;\;\vert\;\; P2 \;\;\vert\;\; P3 \;\;\vert\;\; P4 \;\;\vert \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Extract CT, then compute TAT and WT:** | Process | AT | BT | ST | CT | TAT = CT−AT | WT = TAT−BT | |---------|----|----|----|----|-------------|-------------| | P1 | 0 | 7 | 0 | 7 | 7 | 0 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 7 | 11 | 10 | 6 | | P3 | 2 | 2 | 11 | 13 | 11 | 9 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | 13 | 14 | 11 | 10 | **Step 4 — Averages:** $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{7 + 10 + 11 + 11}{4} = \frac{39}{4} = 9.75 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 6 + 9 + 10}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation (KTU frequently asked)**: P1 has WT=0, but P4 (which arrived only 3 ms later) has WT=10. This is the **Convoy Effect** — a single long process blocks all short ones behind it. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — SJF (Non-Preemptive) **Problem:** Same process table as Example 1. **Step 1 — At t=0, only P1 is in the ready queue → schedule P1 (BT=7).** No choice. **Step 2 — P1 runs from t=0 to t=7.** At t=7, ready queue = {P2, P3, P4}. Pick shortest BT → P4 (BT=1). **Step 3 — P4 runs from t=7 to t=8.** Ready queue = {P2, P3}. Pick shortest BT → P3 (BT=2). **Step 4 — P3 runs from t=8 to t=10.** Ready queue = {P2}. P2 runs from t=10 to t=14. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Time:} \quad & 0 \longrightarrow 7 \longrightarrow 8 \longrightarrow 10 \longrightarrow 14 \\ \text{Process:} \quad & \vert\;\; P1 \;\;\vert\;\; P4 \;\;\vert\;\; P3 \;\;\vert\;\; P2 \;\;\vert \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Compute metrics:** | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 0 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 14 | 13 | 9 | | P3 | 2 | 2 | 10 | 8 | 6 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | 8 | 5 | 4 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{7 + 13 + 8 + 5}{4} = \frac{33}{4} = 8.25 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 9 + 6 + 4}{4} = \frac{19}{4} = 4.75 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison with FCFS**: $\overline{WT}$ dropped from **6.25 → 4.75**. SJF is provably optimal for minimizing average waiting time when all jobs are available at time 0. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — SRTF (Preemptive SJF) **Problem:** Same process table as Example 1. **Step 1 — At t=0: only P1 in queue (remaining = 7). Schedule P1.** **Step 2 — At t=1: P2 arrives (BT=4). Compare: P1 remaining = 6, P2 = 4. P2 is shorter → preempt P1.** **Step 3 — At t=2: P3 arrives (BT=2). Compare: P1=6, P2=3, P3=2. P3 shortest → preempt P2.** **Step 4 — At t=3: P4 arrives (BT=1). Compare: P1=6, P2=3, P3=1, P4=1. Tie between P3 and P4. Convention: pick the one that arrived first → P3. P3 runs from t=3 to t=4.** **Step 5 — At t=4: P3 completes. Ready: {P1, P2, P4} with remaining {6, 3, 1}. Pick P4 (remaining=1).** **Step 6 — P4 runs t=4 to t=5 and completes. Ready: {P1=6, P2=3}. Pick P2.** **Step 7 — P2 runs t=5 to t=8 and completes. P1 runs t=8 to t=14 and completes.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Time:} \quad & 0\;1\;2\;3\;4\;5\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;8\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;14 \\ \text{Process:} \quad & P1 \vert P2 \vert P3 \vert P3 \vert P4 \vert P2 \vert\;\;\; P1 \;\; \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall**: When a process is preempted and resumed later, the **CT** is the *last* completion time, and the **BT is the original (full) burst**, not the remaining. **Step 8 — Compute metrics:** | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 7 | 14 | 14 | 7 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 8 | 7 | 3 | | P3 | 2 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 0 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | 5 | 2 | 1 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{14 + 7 + 2 + 2}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{7 + 3 + 0 + 1}{4} = \frac{11}{4} = 2.75 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **SRTF is OPTIMAL for average waiting time among all CPU scheduling algorithms.** The WT dropped further from 4.75 (SJF) to **2.75 (SRTF)**. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Priority Scheduling (Non-Preemptive) **Problem:** (Lower number = higher priority) | Process | AT | BT | Priority | |---------|----|----|----------| | P1 | 0 | 4 | 2 | | P2 | 1 | 3 | 1 | | P3 | 2 | 1 | 4 | | P4 | 3 | 5 | 3 | **Step 1 — t=0: only P1 → run P1 (0→4).** **Step 2 — t=4: ready = {P2(prio 1), P3(prio 4), P4(prio 3)}. Pick P2 (highest priority = 1).** **Step 3 — t=7: ready = {P3, P4}. Pick P4 (prio 3 < prio 4).** **Step 4 — t=12: ready = {P3}. Run P3 (12→13).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Time:} \quad & 0 \longrightarrow 4 \longrightarrow 7 \longrightarrow 12 \longrightarrow 13 \\ \text{Process:} \quad & \vert\;\; P1 \;\;\vert\;\; P2 \;\;\vert\;\; P4 \;\;\vert\;\; P3 \;\;\vert \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 0 | | P2 | 1 | 3 | 7 | 6 | 3 | | P3 | 2 | 1 | 13 | 11 | 10 | | P4 | 3 | 5 | 12 | 9 | 4 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{4 + 6 + 11 + 9}{4} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.50 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 3 + 10 + 4}{4} = \frac{17}{4} = 4.25 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Starvation Warning**: P3 (lowest priority) has WT = 10 ms despite arriving at t=2. In a heavily loaded system, P3 would be **starved indefinitely**. The KTU-preferred solution is **Aging** — increment P3's priority by 1 every 5 ms of waiting. --- ## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Round Robin (TQ = 2) **Problem:** Same as Example 1. TQ = 2. **Step-by-step trace (in units of TQ):** - t=0: P1 runs 0→2 (P1 remaining=5). Queue: P1(5), P2(4), P3(2), P4(1) [in arrival order]. - t=2: P2 runs 2→4 (P2 remaining=2). Queue: P1(5), P3(2), P4(1), P2(2). - t=4: P3 runs 4→6 (P3 remaining=0 → completes). Queue: P1(5), P4(1), P2(2). - t=6: P4 runs 6→7 (P4 remaining=0 → completes). Queue: P1(5), P2(2). - t=7: P1 runs 7→9 (P1 remaining=3). Queue: P2(2), P1(3). - t=9: P2 runs 9→11 (P2 remaining=0 → completes). Queue: P1(3). - t=11: P1 runs 11→14 (P1 remaining=0 → completes). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Time:} \quad & 0\;2\;4\;6\;7\;\;\;9\;\;\;\;\;11\;\;\;\;\;14 \\ \text{Process:} \quad & P1 \vert P2 \vert P3 \vert P4 \vert P1 \vert P2 \vert P1 \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | ST (first) | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|------------|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 7 | 0 | 14 | 14 | 7 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 11 | 10 | 6 | | P3 | 2 | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4 | 2 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | 6 | 7 | 4 | 3 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{14 + 10 + 4 + 4}{4} = \frac{32}{4} = 8.00 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{7 + 6 + 2 + 3}{4} = \frac{18}{4} = 4.50 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **RR Performance Note**: $\overline{WT}$ is between SJF (4.75) and FCFS (6.25). RR's strength is **Response Time**, not WT. **Compute Average Response Time (RT):** $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{RT} &= \frac{RT_1 + RT_2 + RT_3 + RT_4}{4} = \frac{(0-0) + (2-1) + (4-2) + (6-3)}{4} = \frac{0+1+2+3}{4} = 1.50 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ This is **excellent for interactive systems**. --- ## 3.7 Algorithm Comparison Matrix (Master Reference Table) | Algorithm | Preemptive? | Average WT | Response Time | Starvation? | Best Use Case | |-----------|-------------|------------|---------------|-------------|---------------| | **FCFS** | No | High (6.25) | Poor (for short jobs behind long) | No | Batch, simple systems | | **SJF** | No | **Optimal (4.75)** | Good | Yes (long jobs) | Batch with known BTs | | **SRTF** | Yes | **Optimal (2.75)** | Excellent | Yes (long jobs) | Batch, real-time capable | | **Priority** | Optional | Good | Good | **Yes (low-priority)** | Real-time, multi-user | | **RR** | Yes (TQ) | Moderate (4.50) | **Best (1.50)** | No | Time-sharing, interactive | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CPU Scheduling Decision Workflow (High-Level Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef trigger fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef queue fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cpu fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff A[Scheduling Trigger
Event Detected]:::trigger B[Update Ready Queue

A non-preemptive algorithm (e.g., FCFS) **removes the Running→Ready transition** from its allowed set. ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern OS Scheduler ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef in fill:#118ab2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef mid fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef out fill:#ef476f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef store fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000,color:#000 U[User Processes
AT, BT, Priority]:::in K[Kernel Scheduler Core]:::mid RQ[Ready Queue
FIFO + Heap]:::store PCB[PCB Pool
Process Control Blocks]:::store CPU[CPU Execution Unit]:::out IO[I/O Subsystem
Waiting Queue]:::out LOG[Performance Log
WT, TAT, RT]:::out U --> K K <--> RQ K <--> PCB K --> CPU CPU --> IO IO --> RQ K --> LOG CPU --> LOG ``` **Annotations of each block:** | Block | Role in Scheduling | |-------|-------------------| | **User Processes** | Submit $(AT, BT, \text{Priority})$ to kernel. | | **Kernel Scheduler Core** | Houses the scheduling algorithm + dispatcher. | | **Ready Queue** | Data structure holding runnable processes (FIFO for FCFS/RR, min-heap for SJF, priority-queue for Priority). | | **PCB Pool** | Stores saved contexts (registers, PC, stack pointer) for context switching. | | **CPU Execution Unit** | Executes the dispatched process. | | **I/O Subsystem** | Issues I/O; the process moves to the Waiting Queue. | | **Performance Log** | Logs WT, TAT, RT for analysis (KTU-style evaluation). | # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define CPU scheduling. Explain the role of the short-term scheduler and dispatcher with the help of a neat diagram.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **CPU Scheduling** is the OS activity of selecting one of the processes in the Ready Queue and allocating the CPU to it for execution. It is performed by the **Short-Term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler)**, which must decide *which* ready process runs next. > > The **Dispatcher** is the module that actually gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler. Its functions are: > 1. **Context Switching** — Saving the state of the previous process into its PCB and loading the saved state of the new process. > 2. **Switching to User Mode** — Setting the CPU to execute in user mode for the new process. > 3. **Jumping to the proper location** — Restoring the Program Counter to resume execution of the new process. > > The combined time taken by the dispatcher to perform these actions is called **Dispatch Latency**, which should be kept as small as possible to maintain system responsiveness. > > *(Block-level functional architecture from Section 4.4 may be referenced for the diagram.)* **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** - Definition: 1 mark - Short-term scheduler role: 1 mark - Dispatcher role + dispatch latency: 1 mark --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive | |----------|----------------|------------| | CPU release | Process voluntarily releases | OS forcibly removes process | | Decision points | Only 2 (Running→Wait, Termination) | All 4 possible state transitions | | Complexity | Low | High (context-switch overhead) | | Response time | Poor for short jobs | Excellent | | Example | **FCFS, SJF** | **SRTF, Round Robin, Preemptive Priority** | In non-preemptive scheduling, once the CPU is allocated to a process, it keeps it until it terminates or switches to Waiting state. In preemptive scheduling, the scheduler can interrupt a running process to give the CPU to a higher-priority or newly-arrived shorter process. **Mark Distribution:** - 2 differences: 2 marks - Examples: 1 mark --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE Part B for Module 1 has an **internal choice**. You must answer either Question A *or* Question B in full. --- ### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — FCFS + SJF + SRTF Mixed Mastery **`(a)`** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` Consider the following set of processes, with the length of the CPU-burst time given in milliseconds: | Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time | |---------|--------------|------------| | P1 | 0 | 8 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | | P3 | 2 | 9 | | P4 | 3 | 5 | **Draw Gantt charts** illustrating the execution of these processes using **FCFS, Non-Preemptive SJF, and Preemptive SJF (SRTF)**. Compute the **waiting time** and **turnaround time** for each algorithm. Determine which algorithm gives the **minimum average waiting time**, and justify your answer. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **A.1 — FCFS Schedule:** Execution order by arrival: P1 → P2 → P3 → P4. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gantt:}\quad & \vert\;\; P1\;(0-8) \;\vert\;\; P2\;(8-12) \;\vert\;\; P3\;(12-21) \;\vert\;\; P4\;(21-26) \;\;\vert \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT−AT | WT = TAT−BT | |---------|----|----|----|-------------|-------------| | P1 | 0 | 8 | 8 | 8 | 0 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 12 | 11 | 7 | | P3 | 2 | 9 | 21 | 19 | 10 | | P4 | 3 | 5 | 26 | 23 | 18 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{8+11+19+23}{4} = 15.25 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{0+7+10+18}{4} = 8.75 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **A.2 — Non-Preemptive SJF:** At t=0 only P1 → run P1 (0→8). At t=8, ready = {P2(4), P3(9), P4(5)}. Shortest = P2. At t=12, ready = {P3(9), P4(5)}. Shortest = P4. At t=17, ready = {P3}. Run P3 (17→26). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gantt:}\quad & \vert\;\; P1\;(0-8) \;\vert\;\; P2\;(8-12) \;\vert\;\; P4\;(12-17) \;\vert\;\; P3\;(17-26) \;\;\vert \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 8 | 8 | 8 | 0 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 12 | 11 | 7 | | P3 | 2 | 9 | 26 | 24 | 15 | | P4 | 3 | 5 | 17 | 14 | 9 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{8+11+24+14}{4} = 14.25 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{0+7+15+9}{4} = 7.75 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **A.3 — SRTF (Preemptive SJF):** - t=0: P1 (rem=8) runs. - t=1: P2 arrives (BT=4). P1 rem=7 vs P2=4. P2 preempts. P2 (rem=4) runs. - t=2: P3 arrives (BT=9). P2 rem=3 < P3=9 < P1=7. P2 continues. - t=3: P4 arrives (BT=5). P2 rem=2 is shortest. P2 continues. - t=4: P2 completes. Ready rems: P1=7, P3=9, P4=5. P4 (rem=5) shortest. - t=4–9: P4 runs to completion. - t=9: Ready: P1=7, P3=9. P1 shorter. - t=9–16: P1 runs to completion. - t=16–25: P3 runs to completion. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gantt:}\quad & P1(0-1) \vert P2(1-4) \vert P4(4-9) \vert P1(9-16) \vert P3(16-25) \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 8 | 16 | 16 | 8 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 4 | 3 | -1 ⚠ | | P3 | 2 | 9 | 25 | 23 | 14 | | P4 | 3 | 5 | 9 | 6 | 1 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{16+3+23+6}{4} = 12.00 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{8 + (-1) + 14 + 1}{4} = 5.50 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall**: P2 has WT = -1 because it arrived at t=1 and was scheduled immediately (no wait). Negative WT is **physically valid** here (process did not wait at all) but students often mistakenly write WT = 0 for it. Either -1 or 0 is accepted; explain in your answer. **Conclusion:** SRTF gives the **minimum average waiting time = 5.50 ms**, confirming that SRTF is the **optimal algorithm** for minimizing average waiting time among all CPU scheduling algorithms. ✔ --- **`(b)`** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Explain the Round Robin scheduling algorithm with a suitable example. What happens when the time quantum is too large or too small? How does Round Robin differ from FCFS? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **B.1 — Algorithm Definition (2 marks):** Round Robin (RR) is a **preemptive, time-sharing** scheduling algorithm designed for fairness in interactive systems. Each process is allocated a fixed time unit called a **Time Quantum (TQ)**. If the process does not complete within the quantum, it is **preempted** and appended to the back of the Ready Queue, and the scheduler dispatches the next process in the queue. **B.2 — Numerical Example (3 marks):** Consider P1(BT=10), P2(BT=4), P3(BT=3), P4(BT=2) all arriving at t=0 with TQ=3. - t=0–3: P1 runs (rem=7). Queue: P2, P3, P4, P1. - t=3–6: P2 runs (rem=1). Queue: P3, P4, P1, P2. - t=6–9: P3 runs (rem=0 → completes). Queue: P4, P1, P2. - t=9–11: P4 runs (rem=0 → completes). Queue: P1, P2. - t=11–12: P2 runs (rem=0 → completes). Queue: P1. - t=12–15: P1 runs (rem=4). Queue: P1. - t=15–19: P1 runs (rem=0 → completes). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gantt:}\quad & P1(0-3) \vert P2(3-6) \vert P3(6-9) \vert P4(9-11) \vert P2(11-12) \vert P1(12-15) \vert P1(15-19) \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | ST | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 10 | 0 | 19 | 19 | 9 | | P2 | 0 | 4 | 3 | 12 | 12 | 8 | | P3 | 0 | 3 | 6 | 9 | 9 | 6 | | P4 | 0 | 2 | 9 | 11 | 11 | 9 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{19+12+9+11}{4} = 12.75 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{9+8+6+9}{4} = 8.00 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **B.3 — Time Quantum Trade-off (1.5 marks):** | Quantum Size | Effect | |--------------|--------| | **TQ → ∞** | RR degenerates into **FCFS** (no preemption effectively occurs). | | **TQ → 0** | Theoretically fastest response, but **excessive context-switching overhead**; throughput collapses to nearly zero. CPU spends all its time switching, not running. | | **TQ too small** | Too many context switches, poor throughput, high dispatcher overhead. | | **TQ too large** | Long waiting time for short jobs; degrades to FCFS. | | **Sweet spot** | TQ should be **slightly larger than the average CPU burst** (or ~80% of bursts should be shorter than TQ). Typical real value: 10–100 ms. | **B.4 — RR vs FCFS (0.5 marks):** | Aspect | FCFS | RR | |--------|------|-----| | Preemption | None | Yes (TQ-based) | | Response time | Poor for short jobs behind long | Excellent (no head-of-line blocking) | | Starvation | No | No | | Throughput | High (less overhead) | Lower (context switch overhead) | --- ### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Priority + Aging + Round Robin Alternative **`(a)`** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Consider the following processes. Smaller priority number = higher priority. | Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time | Priority | |---------|--------------|------------|----------| | P1 | 0 | 5 | 3 | | P2 | 1 | 3 | 1 | | P3 | 2 | 8 | 4 | | P4 | 3 | 6 | 2 | **i.** Draw the Gantt chart and compute average waiting time and average turnaround time using **non-preemptive priority scheduling**. **(3.5 marks)** **ii.** What is **starvation**? Show how **aging** prevents starvation in this case. **(2 marks)** **iii.** Repeat (i) for **preemptive priority scheduling**. Compare the two results. **(1.5 marks)** **Model Solution:** **i. Non-Preemptive Priority Schedule:** - t=0: only P1 → run P1 (0→5). - t=5: ready = {P2(prio 1), P3(prio 4), P4(prio 2)}. Pick P2 (highest priority). - t=5→8: P2 runs. - t=8: ready = {P3(4), P4(2)}. Pick P4. - t=8→14: P4 runs. - t=14: ready = {P3}. Run P3 (14→22). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gantt:}\quad & \vert P1(0-5) \vert P2(5-8) \vert P4(8-14) \vert P3(14-22) \vert \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 0 | | P2 | 1 | 3 | 8 | 7 | 4 | | P3 | 2 | 8 | 22 | 20 | 12 | | P4 | 3 | 6 | 14 | 11 | 5 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{5+7+20+11}{4} = 10.75 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{0+4+12+5}{4} = 5.25 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **ii. Starvation & Aging (2 marks):** > **Starvation (Indefinite Blocking)** is a scheduling problem in which a process waits indefinitely in the ready queue because processes of higher priority keep arriving. In the above schedule, P3 (priority 4) is the lowest-priority process. If a continuous stream of high-priority processes arrived (P5, P6, ...), P3 would never get the CPU. > > **Aging** is a solution that **gradually increases the priority of processes that wait in the ready queue for a long time**. For example: every 5 ms, increment the priority (decrease the priority number) of any waiting process by 1. After 20 ms of waiting, P3's priority would change from 4 → 3 → 2 → 1, eventually guaranteeing it the CPU. This prevents indefinite starvation. **iii. Preemptive Priority Schedule:** - t=0: only P1 (prio 3) → run P1. - t=1: P2 arrives (prio 1 < P1's prio 3). P2 preempts P1. P1 rem = 4. - t=2: P3 arrives (prio 4). P2 (prio 1) is still highest. P2 continues. - t=3: P4 arrives (prio 2). P2 (prio 1) still highest. P2 continues. - t=4: P2 completes. Ready: P1(rem 4, prio 3), P3(rem 8, prio 4), P4(rem 6, prio 2). Highest = P4. - t=4–10: P4 runs to completion. - t=10: Ready: P1(rem 4, prio 3), P3(rem 8, prio 4). Pick P1. - t=10–14: P1 runs to completion. - t=14–22: P3 runs to completion. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Gantt:}\quad & P1(0-1) \vert P2(1-4) \vert P4(4-10) \vert P1(10-14) \vert P3(14-22) \end{aligned} $$ | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---------|----|----|----|-----|----| | P1 | 0 | 5 | 14 | 14 | 9 | | P2 | 1 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 0 | | P3 | 2 | 8 | 22 | 20 | 12 | | P4 | 3 | 6 | 10 | 7 | 1 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{14+3+20+7}{4} = 11.00 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{9+0+12+1}{4} = 5.50 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison:** Preemptive gives slightly higher $\overline{WT}$ (5.50 vs 5.25) but **lower individual WT for the critical process P4 (1 vs 5)**. Preemptive is more responsive for high-priority arrivals. ✔ --- **`(b)`** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the following with one-line examples:** - **(i)** Throughput - **(ii)** Turnaround time - **(iii)** Waiting time - **(iv)** Response time - **(v)** CPU utilization - **(vi)** Dispatch latency **Explain why response time is the most important metric in a time-sharing system, and how Round Robin optimizes it. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Throughput:** The number of processes completed per unit time. *Example: If 10 processes complete in 5 seconds, throughput = 2 processes/second.* **(ii) Turnaround Time:** Total time elapsed from process submission to its completion. $TAT = CT - AT$. *Example: A process arriving at t=2 and completing at t=10 has TAT = 8 ms.* **(iii) Waiting Time:** Total time a process spent in the ready queue (not executing). $WT = TAT - BT$. *Example: A process with TAT=10 and BT=3 has WT=7 ms.* **(iv) Response Time:** Time from submission to the first CPU response. $RT = ST - AT$. *Example: A process arriving at t=0 and first scheduled at t=2 has RT=2 ms.* **(v) CPU Utilization:** Percentage of time the CPU is doing useful work. *Example: 80% utilization means CPU is idle only 20% of the time.* **(vi) Dispatch Latency:** Time taken by the dispatcher to switch the CPU from one process to another. *Example: Typical Linux dispatch latency ≈ 10 microseconds.* **Why Response Time is Paramount in Time-Sharing (3 marks):** In a time-sharing system (e.g., a multi-user UNIX server, a modern desktop), the user's perception of "speed" is governed by how quickly the system acknowledges their input. A long-running scientific batch job can have a TAT of minutes and the user is satisfied, but if typing a character in an editor takes 5 seconds to appear, the user will perceive the system as "frozen." > **Response time is the key indicator of system interactivity.** Low response time (sub-100 ms) creates the illusion of dedicated service. The OS must guarantee that **no process waits more than a few quanta** before getting its first CPU slice. **How Round Robin Optimizes Response Time (2 marks):** RR is the **canonical algorithm for minimizing response time** in time-sharing systems because: 1. **Every process gets the CPU within one time quantum** of its arrival. This bounds the worst-case response time to at most $(n-1) \times TQ$ where $n$ is the number of processes. 2. **By preempting after TQ**, RR prevents any single process from monopolizing the CPU and starving others — guaranteeing fair access. 3. **The choice of TQ** is the tunable knob: a small TQ (≈10–100 ms) keeps response time low for interactive jobs without excessive context-switching cost. 4. RR is **work-conserving** — the CPU is never idle when there is a runnable process, so throughput is also reasonably high. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Mistakes):** > 1. **Forgetting preemption in SRTF/Priority** — Examiners deduct **2 marks per process** if you do not check whether a new arrival should preempt the current process. > 2. **Using remaining time instead of original burst time** for TAT/WT calculations. Always use the **original (full) BT** in $WT = TAT - BT$. > 3. **Mis-ordering ties in SJF/Priority** — Convention is to break ties by **arrival time (FCFS as tie-breaker)**. If unspecified, mention this explicitly. > 4. **Confusing Response Time with Waiting Time** — Response Time uses the *first* start time, not total. RR's strength is in RT, not WT. > 5. **Forgetting the unit ms** — Always write "ms" in numerical answers; KTU examiners deduct partial marks for missing units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Re-read 30 minutes before the exam.** - ✅ **Process Scheduling** = selecting a process from the Ready Queue for the CPU; performed by the **Short-Term Scheduler**. - ✅ **Dispatcher** = module that performs the context switch. **Dispatch Latency** must be minimized. - ✅ **Four scheduling decision moments**: Running→Waiting, Running→Ready, Waiting→Ready, Termination. - ✅ **Five criteria**: CPU Utilization, Throughput (maximize); TAT, WT, RT (minimize). - ✅ **Key formulas**: $TAT = CT - AT$, $WT = TAT - BT$, $RT = ST - AT$. - ✅ **FCFS**: Non-preemptive, simple, suffers from **Convoy Effect**. - ✅ **SJF**: Non-preemptive, **optimal average WT** when all jobs at t=0; suffers **starvation**; needs burst-time prediction ($\tau_{n+1} = \alpha t_n + (1-\alpha)\tau_n$). - ✅ **SRTF**: Preemptive SJF; **provably optimal average WT** among all algorithms; ties broken by AT. - ✅ **Priority**: Smaller number = higher priority (KTU convention); can starve low-priority → **Aging** is the cure. - ✅ **Round Robin**: Preemptive, TQ-based, best for **time-sharing**; TQ trade-off (large→FCFS, small→overhead). - ✅ **Negative WT is allowed** in SRTF when a process is scheduled the instant it arrives (no waiting). - ✅ **Always state** the preemption mode of the algorithm in your answer. - ✅ **Always draw** the Gantt chart before computing metrics — partial marks are awarded for the chart alone. - ✅ **Performance order** (average WT, all arrivals=0): SRTF ≤ SJF ≤ RR (small TQ) ≤ RR (large TQ) ≤ FCFS. - ✅ **Linux CFS** is based on a fair-share weighted RR; **Windows** uses a 32-level priority queue with RR within each level; **RTOS** uses fixed priority + RR for deadlines.

topics.title: The Multilevel Feedback Queue: Basic Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Multilevel Feedback Queue: Basic Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** is a CPU scheduling algorithm that attempts to **optimize a system by separating processes based on their observed CPU-burst characteristics**, rather than relying on prior knowledge provided by the user. It is a *defining* example of a learning-based, adaptive scheduler that resolves the fundamental problem of unknown burst times by **inferring job behavior at run-time** and adjusting the process priority accordingly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAMT595 – Module 1):** MLFQ is studied as a multi-queue CPU scheduling algorithm that combines the principles of **FCFS, Round Robin, and Priority Scheduling** into a single, cohesive, adaptive framework. It is one of the most important exam topics in this module.

In the MLFQ model, the OS maintains a hierarchy of *N* distinct queues, each with a **decreasing order of priority** from top (highest) to bottom (lowest). Each queue is governed by its own **time quantum** and may use its own scheduling discipline (typically Round Robin for higher queues and FCFS for the lowest queue).

### 1.2 The Four Basic Rules (Silberschatz Formulation)

The classical MLFQ is governed by the following four operational rules. These rules constitute the *kernel* of every board exam question on this topic:

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 1 – Priority Rule:** *If Priority(A) > Priority(B), then A runs (and B does not).*
>
> **Rule 2 – Equal Priority Rule:** *If Priority(A) = Priority(B), then A and B run in Round Robin fashion using the time slice of that queue.*
>
> **Rule 3 – Admission Rule:** *When a job first enters the system, it is placed at the highest priority (the topmost queue).*
>
> **Rule 4 – Demotion/Promotion Rule:** *If a job uses up the time slice allotted at its current level (i.e., it is CPU-bound), its priority is reduced and it is moved down one queue. If a job relinquishes the CPU before the time slice expires (i.e., it is I/O-bound), it remains at the same priority level.*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Counter"

Imagine a **multi-specialty hospital outpatient department** with three counters:

- **Counter 1 (Highest Priority):** Quick tests (blood pressure, temperature) — patients here are *fast* and finish within minutes.
- **Counter 2 (Medium Priority):** Standard consultations — slightly longer procedures (~15 minutes).
- **Counter 3 (Lowest Priority):** Full diagnostic workups — patients needing extensive, lengthy tests.

**Rule 3 (Admission):** Every new patient first goes to **Counter 1**. The hospital assumes you might be a quick case.

**Rule 4 (Demotion):** If a patient doesn't finish at Counter 1 within the allotted 2 minutes, the nurse assumes they are a "long case" and sends them down to **Counter 2**. If they still don't finish at Counter 2 within 15 minutes, they go to **Counter 3**.

**Rules 1 & 2 (Priority):** A patient at Counter 1 is **always served before** any patient at Counter 2 or 3, regardless of arrival order — a true priority discipline. Within the same counter, patients are served in turn (Round Robin).

**Intuition takeaway:** MLFQ *learns* from behavior. Short, interactive jobs stay near the top and get excellent response time. Long, CPU-hungry jobs gracefully sink to the bottom where they don't interfere with system responsiveness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in MLFQ Analysis:**
> - **Turnaround Time ($T_{turnaround}$):** $T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$
> - **Response Time ($T_{response}$):** $T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$
> - **Time Quantum ($q$):** The maximum CPU burst allowed before demotion (measured in **milliseconds** in modern OS).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Goals of MLFQ

The MLFQ is designed to **simultaneously optimize three often-conflicting goals** in CPU scheduling:

1. **Minimize Turnaround Time (Efficiency):** Achieved by running shorter jobs to completion first.
2. **Minimize Response Time (Interactivity):** Achieved by giving high priority to interactive, I/O-bound jobs.
3. **Maximize CPU Utilization (Throughput):** Achieved by never leaving the CPU idle when runnable jobs exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Design Philosophy:** *We do not know whether a job will be a "short job" or a "long job" a priori. Therefore, we run it briefly at high priority, and if it does not finish, we assume it is a long job and demote it.*

### 2.2 Algorithmic Decision Flow — Step-by-Step

When a CPU scheduling decision is made, the MLFQ dispatcher executes the following logic:

- **Step 1 (Entry):** A newly arrived process $P_i$ is enqueued at the topmost queue ($Q_0$). Its allocated time quantum is $q_0$.
- **Step 2 (Selection):** The scheduler scans queues from $Q_0$ downward. The first **non-empty** queue is selected. This implements **Rule 1** (strict priority).
- **Step 3 (Intra-Queue):** If multiple jobs exist in the selected queue, they are scheduled in **Round Robin order** with the queue's specific quantum. This implements **Rule 2**.
- **Step 4 (Quantum Expiry):** If $P_i$ consumes the full quantum $q_k$ of queue $Q_k$ without blocking, $P_i$ is **demoted** to $Q_{k+1}$ with a new (typically larger) quantum $q_{k+1}$. This implements **Rule 4**.
- **Step 5 (Voluntary Yield):** If $P_i$ blocks for I/O before the quantum expires, it remains at queue $Q_k$ upon wakeup. This is the "reward" for being well-behaved and interactive.
- **Step 6 (Preemption):** If a higher-priority job arrives (e.g., a new job enters at $Q_0$), the currently running job is **immediately preempted** and returned to its queue's tail.

### 2.3 Why MLFQ Works — The "Why" Behind Each Rule

| Rule | Intended Behaviour | Problem It Solves |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Rule 1 (Priority) | Prevents CPU-bound jobs from monopolizing the CPU | Avoids the **convoy effect** seen in FCFS |
| Rule 2 (Round Robin) | Ensures fairness among jobs of equal priority | Prevents **starvation** within a queue |
| Rule 3 (High Initial Priority) | Gives every new job a chance to be interactive | Avoids initial misclassification |
| Rule 4 (Demotion) | Penalizes long CPU bursts | Pushes CPU-hungry jobs out of the high-priority region |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|----------|----------------|-------|-------|
| Turnaround Time | $T_{turnaround} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | **ms** or **s** | Lower is better |
| Response Time | $T_{response} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$ | **ms** or **s** | Critical for interactivity |
| Waiting Time | $T_{wait} = T_{turnaround} - T_{burst}$ | **ms** or **s** | Time spent in ready queue |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{T_{total}}$ | **jobs/sec** | Higher is better |
| Effective Quantum | $q_{eff} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} q_i$ for a job demoted $k$ times | **ms** | Total CPU time allowed before reaching bottom |
| CPU Utilization | $U_{CPU} = 1 - I_{idle}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le U_{CPU} \le 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The MLFQ philosophy is the direct intellectual ancestor of the **Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)**, the **FreeBSD ULE scheduler**, and the classical **Windows NT scheduler** (up to Windows XP). Modern OSs use *weighted* or *decay-based* variants, but the multilevel feedback principle is universal.

### 2.5 Tunable Parameters of MLFQ

The behaviour of an MLFQ is fully characterized by **five parameters** (a high-yield exam point):

1. **Number of Queues ($N$):** Typically 3 to 5 in practical systems.
2. **Scheduling Algorithm per Queue:** Usually Round Robin (upper) and FCFS (lowest).
3. **Method to Raise Priority:** Only in advanced variants (avoids starvation).
4. **Method to Lower Priority:** Time-slice expiration (Rule 4).
5. **Initial Queue Placement Strategy:** Always at the topmost queue (Rule 3) in the basic form.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since the MLFQ is a *behavioural* scheduling algorithm rather than a closed-form mathematical one, the "derivations" in this section consist of **exhaustive step-by-step trace analyses** through a workload, constructing the **Gantt chart** and computing standard scheduling metrics. This is exactly the methodology followed by KTU examiners.

### 3.1 Worked Example: Trace Analysis of an MLFQ System

**System Configuration:**
- **Number of Queues:** 3 (namely $Q_0$, $Q_1$, $Q_2$)
- **Quantums:** $q_0 = 4$ **ms**, $q_1 = 8$ **ms**, $q_2 = \text{FCFS}$ (run-to-completion)
- **Queue disciplines:** $Q_0$ → Round Robin, $Q_1$ → Round Robin, $Q_2$ → FCFS

**Workload:**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Total CPU Burst (ms) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| $P_1$   | 0                 | 12                   |
| $P_2$   | 0                 | 6                    |
| $P_3$   | 0                 | 4                    |

All three jobs arrive at $t = 0$. By **Rule 3**, all are admitted to the topmost queue $Q_0$.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Gantt Chart Construction

**Step 1 — Initial State at $t=0$:** All three jobs are in $Q_0$. The Round Robin schedule begins with $P_1$ (chosen by FCFS tie-breaker). It gets the full quantum of **4 ms**.

$$[0 \to 4] : P_1 \text{ in } Q_0 \text{ (consumes full 4 ms of quantum)}$$

**Step 2 — Demotion of $P_1$ at $t=4$:** Since $P_1$ used up the entire quantum of $Q_0$, it is **demoted to $Q_1$** (Rule 4). Remaining burst of $P_1$ is $12 - 4 = 8$ **ms**. The scheduler now picks the next job in $Q_0$, which is $P_2$.

$$[4 \to 8] : P_2 \text{ in } Q_0 \text{ (consumes full 4 ms of quantum)}$$

**Step 3 — Demotion of $P_2$ at $t=8$:** $P_2$ used the entire quantum. Remaining burst: $6 - 4 = 2$ **ms**. $P_2$ is demoted to $Q_1$. $Q_0$ now contains only $P_3$.

$$[8 \to 12] : P_3 \text{ in } Q_0 \text{ (consumes full 4 ms of quantum)}$$

**Step 4 — Demotion of $P_3$ at $t=12$:** $P_3$ used the entire quantum. Remaining burst: $4 - 4 = 0$ **ms**. **$P_3$ completes.** However, since its burst is fully consumed, demotion is irrelevant.

**Step 5 — $Q_0$ is empty at $t=12$.** Scheduler scans down and selects the highest non-empty queue, which is now $Q_1$ containing $P_1$ (8 ms remaining) and $P_2$ (2 ms remaining). $P_1$ runs first (FCFS tie-breaker).

$$[12 \to 20] : P_1 \text{ in } Q_1 \text{ (quantum = 8 ms, completes its remaining 8 ms)}$$

**Step 6 — $P_1$ completes at $t=20$.** $Q_1$ still contains $P_2$ with 2 ms remaining. The quantum of $Q_1$ is 8 ms, but $P_2$ only needs 2 ms.

$$[20 \to 22] : P_2 \text{ in } Q_1 \text{ (finishes its remaining 2 ms; $P_2$ completes)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $P_2$ did **not** use up the full 8 ms quantum of $Q_1$, but it is *terminating*, so the issue of "voluntary yield vs. quantum expiry" is moot. $P_2$ completes at $t=22$.

---

### 3.3 Consolidated Gantt Chart

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart} \; : \quad & \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert P_3 \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert \\
& \vert 0 \;\;\; 4 \;\;\; 8 \;\;\; 12 \;\;\; 20 \;\;\; 22 \vert \quad \text{(all in ms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the staircase pattern: $P_1$ and $P_2$ traverse *two* queues, while $P_3$ terminates within $Q_0$. This visualizes the "feedback" principle — long jobs are penalized (demoted), short jobs finish at the top.

---

### 3.4 Metric Computation Matrix

We now compute the three standard scheduling metrics for each process.

**For $P_1$:** Arrival $= 0$, First Run $= 0$, Completion $= 20$, Burst $= 12$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{response}(P_1) &= T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival} = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround}(P_1) &= T_{completion} - T_{arrival} = 20 - 0 = 20 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait}(P_1) &= T_{turnaround} - T_{burst} = 20 - 12 = 8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $P_2$:** Arrival $= 0$, First Run $= 4$, Completion $= 22$, Burst $= 6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{response}(P_2) &= T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival} = 4 - 0 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround}(P_2) &= T_{completion} - T_{arrival} = 22 - 0 = 22 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait}(P_2) &= T_{turnaround} - T_{burst} = 22 - 6 = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $P_3$:** Arrival $= 0$, First Run $= 8$, Completion $= 12$, Burst $= 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{response}(P_3) &= T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival} = 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
T_{turnaround}(P_3) &= T_{completion} - T_{arrival} = 12 - 0 = 12 \text{ ms} \\
T_{wait}(P_3) &= T_{turnaround} - T_{burst} = 12 - 4 = 8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Aggregate System Performance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{T_{turnaround}} &= \dfrac{20 + 22 + 12}{3} = \dfrac{54}{3} = 18 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{T_{response}} &= \dfrac{0 + 4 + 8}{3} = \dfrac{12}{3} = 4 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{T_{wait}} &= \dfrac{8 + 16 + 8}{3} = \dfrac{32}{3} \approx 10.67 \text{ ms} \\
\text{Throughput} &= \dfrac{3 \text{ jobs}}{22 \text{ ms}} \approx 0.136 \text{ jobs/ms} = 136 \text{ jobs/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix (MLFQ vs. Other Algorithms)

| Metric | FCFS | SJF (Non-Preemptive) | Round Robin (q=4) | **MLFQ (This Example)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\overline{T_{turnaround}}$ | 14.67 ms | 11.33 ms | 18.0 ms | **18.0 ms** |
| $\overline{T_{response}}$ | 0.0 ms | 0.0 ms | 5.33 ms | **4.0 ms** |
| $\overline{T_{wait}}$ | 8.67 ms | 5.33 ms | 14.0 ms | **10.67 ms** |
| Pre-emption Required | No | No | Yes | **Yes** |
| Requires Burst Knowledge | No | Yes | No | **No** (Learns!) |

> [!NOTE]
> The comparison reveals an important insight: MLFQ achieves a **response time (4 ms)** that is **better than plain Round Robin (5.33 ms)** while being **completely agnostic of future burst lengths** — a clear demonstration of its adaptive intelligence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MLFQ Architecture and Job Lifecycle

The following block diagram illustrates the **queue hierarchy**, the **direction of demotion**, and the **scheduling decision flow** of the Multilevel Feedback Queue.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([New Job Arrives]) --> admitQ0[Place in Q0 - Highest Priority - Quantum 4ms]
    admitQ0 --> check0{Does it use full quantum of Q0?}
    check0 -- Yes, burst remains --> moveQ1[Demote to Q1 - Medium Priority - Quantum 8ms]
    check0 -- No, burst finishes --> finishA([Job Terminates])
    moveQ1 --> check1{Does it use full quantum of Q1?}
    check1 -- Yes, burst remains --> moveQ2[Demote to Q2 - Lowest Priority - FCFS]
    check1 -- No, burst finishes --> finishA
    moveQ2 --> runQ2[Run to completion using FCFS]
    runQ2 --> finishA
    schedPick[Scheduler picks highest non-empty queue] --> rrQ0[Round Robin in Q0]
    rrQ0 --> schedPick
    schedPick2[If Q0 empty, scheduler picks Q1] --> rrQ1[Round Robin in Q1]
    rrQ1 --> schedPick2
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Block Diagram — MLFQ Dispatcher Internals

The following diagram expands the **internal architecture of the MLFQ dispatcher**, showing the interaction between the **timer interrupt hardware**, the **queue manager**, and the **CPU**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputA[Admission Path]
        newJob([New Process]) --> admitHook[Admission Controller]
        admitHook --> topQ[Q0 - Quantum 4ms - RR]
    end
    subgraph demotePath[Demotion Logic]
        timer[Tick Interrupt from Timer Hardware] --> quantCheck{Quantum Exhausted?}
        quantCheck -- Yes --> demoteHook[Demote to Lower Queue]
        quantCheck -- No --> stayHook[Keep in Current Queue]
    end
    subgraph dispatchPath[Dispatch Path]
        readyScan[Scan Queues Top to Bottom] --> selectQ[Select Highest Non-Empty Queue]
        selectQ --> loadProc[Load Process PCB onto CPU]
        loadProc --> cpurun([CPU Executes])
    end
    topQ --> readyScan
    stayHook --> readyScan
    demoteHook --> midQ[Q1 - Quantum 8ms - RR]
    midQ --> readyScan
    midQ --> lowQ[Q2 - FCFS - No Quantum]
    lowQ --> readyScan
    cpurun --> timer
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic — State Transition Table

| Current Event | Current Queue | Action | New Queue | Rule Applied |
|---------------|---------------|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Process Arrives | (none) | Admit | $Q_0$ | Rule 3 |
| Quantum Expired, Burst Remains | $Q_k$ | Demote | $Q_{k+1}$ | Rule 4 |
| Process Blocks for I/O | $Q_k$ | Keep on Wakeup | $Q_k$ | Rule 4 (yield case) |
| Higher Priority Job Arrives | $Q_k$ | Preempt | $Q_k$ (tail) | Rule 1 |
| Same Queue, Quantum Expired | $Q_k$ | Rotate within | $Q_k$ | Rule 2 |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define the Multilevel Feedback Queue scheduling algorithm and list its four basic rules. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** is an adaptive CPU scheduling algorithm that uses multiple ready queues of differing priorities to schedule processes. It attempts to **minimize turnaround and response time simultaneously** by learning the CPU-burst behaviour of processes at run-time and adjusting their priorities accordingly.

**The four basic rules are:**

1. **Rule 1:** If $\text{Priority}(A) > \text{Priority}(B)$, then process $A$ is scheduled to run, and $B$ is not.
2. **Rule 2:** If $\text{Priority}(A) = \text{Priority}(B)$, processes $A$ and $B$ are scheduled in **Round Robin** fashion.
3. **Rule 3:** When a process enters the system, it is placed in the **highest-priority queue**.
4. **Rule 4:** When a process exhausts its time quantum at its current queue, it is **demoted** to a lower-priority queue. If it yields the CPU voluntarily (I/O), it remains at the same priority.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A complete answer must list *all four* rules verbatim and add at least one sentence on the *purpose* (learning burst behaviour). Partial marks are awarded even if one rule is paraphrased incorrectly, so the *order* of demotion and admission must be crystal clear.

---

#### Question 2: Why is the MLFQ considered an "adaptive" scheduler, and what problem does Rule 3 attempt to solve? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

MLFQ is considered adaptive because it **dynamically changes the priority of a process at run-time** based on its observed CPU-burst behaviour, rather than relying on a fixed priority assigned at submission. This allows the system to *learn* whether a process is interactive (short bursts) or CPU-bound (long bursts).

**Rule 3 — Admission at highest priority — solves the following problem:** Without a-priori knowledge of a process's burst length, the OS must make an assumption. **Assuming every new process is interactive** gives it the best possible response time. If the assumption is wrong, Rule 4 corrects it by demoting the process. This initial optimism is the foundation of the MLFQ's responsiveness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners look for two distinct ideas: (i) *adaptivity = changing priority at run-time*, and (ii) *Rule 3 is an "optimistic admission" heuristic that relies on Rule 4 as a safety net*. Conflating these two ideas results in a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Sub-Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Consider an MLFQ scheduler with 3 queues. The time quantum for $Q_0$ is 4 ms, for $Q_1$ is 8 ms, and $Q_2$ uses FCFS. Draw the Gantt chart and compute the average turnaround time and average response time for the following workload.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Total CPU Burst (ms) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| $P_1$   | 0                 | 18                   |
| $P_2$   | 1                 | 6                    |
| $P_3$   | 2                 | 10                   |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Admit all jobs at $t=0$ to $Q_0$.** At $t=0$, only $P_1$ has arrived. It begins executing in $Q_0$.

- $[0 \to 4]$: $P_1$ runs in $Q_0$, exhausts quantum, demoted to $Q_1$ (remaining burst: $14$ ms).

**Step 2: At $t=4$, $P_2$ has already arrived at $t=1$.** $Q_0$ contains $P_2$ and $P_3$ (the latter arrived at $t=2$).

- $[4 \to 8]$: $P_2$ runs in $Q_0$, exhausts quantum, demoted to $Q_1$ (remaining burst: $2$ ms).
- $[8 \to 12]$: $P_3$ runs in $Q_0$, exhausts quantum, demoted to $Q_1$ (remaining burst: $6$ ms).

**Step 3: $Q_0$ empty at $t=12$.** Move to $Q_1$ which now holds $P_1$ (14 ms), $P_2$ (2 ms), $P_3$ (6 ms). Round Robin in $Q_1$ with quantum 8 ms.

- $[12 \to 20]$: $P_1$ runs in $Q_1$, uses 8 ms of its 8 ms quantum (since its remaining burst 14 ms > quantum 8 ms), demoted to $Q_2$ (remaining burst: $6$ ms).
- $[20 \to 22]$: $P_2$ runs in $Q_1$, only needs 2 ms. **Terminates at $t=22$.** (Did not exhaust quantum — terminates.)
- $[22 \to 28]$: $P_3$ runs in $Q_1$, uses 6 ms of its 8 ms quantum (remaining 0 ms, but quantum not exhausted — actually $P_3$ terminates at $t=28$ since its burst becomes 0). **Terminates at $t=28$.**

**Step 4: $Q_1$ empty at $t=28$.** $Q_2$ holds $P_1$ with 6 ms remaining (FCFS).

- $[28 \to 34]$: $P_1$ runs in $Q_2$ under FCFS to completion. **Terminates at $t=34$.**

**Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt} \; : \; & \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert P_3 \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert P_3 \vert P_1 \vert \\
& \vert 0 \;\;\; 4 \;\;\; 8 \;\;\; 12 \;\;\; 20 \;\;\; 22 \;\;\; 28 \;\;\; 34 \vert \quad \text{(ms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Metrics.**

**For $P_1$:** Arrival $= 0$, First Run $= 0$, Completion $= 34$, Burst $= 18$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{turnaround}(P_1) &= 34 - 0 = 34 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response}(P_1) &= 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $P_2$:** Arrival $= 1$, First Run $= 4$, Completion $= 22$, Burst $= 6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{turnaround}(P_2) &= 22 - 1 = 21 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response}(P_2) &= 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $P_3$:** Arrival $= 2$, First Run $= 8$, Completion $= 28$, Burst $= 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{turnaround}(P_3) &= 28 - 2 = 26 \text{ ms} \\
T_{response}(P_3) &= 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Aggregate Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{T_{turnaround}} &= \dfrac{34 + 21 + 26}{3} = \dfrac{81}{3} = 27 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{T_{response}} &= \dfrac{0 + 3 + 6}{3} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often forget that $P_2$ and $P_3$ do **not** get demoted when they *terminate* within a quantum — demotion is only relevant when burst remains after quantum expiry. Termination is a separate path in the state machine.

---

#### Question A — Sub-Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Explain the major problems that arise in the basic MLFQ scheme and how the advanced rules (5 and 6) attempt to solve them.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The basic MLFQ (Rules 1–4) suffers from **two well-known pathological problems**:

**Problem 1: Starvation of Long Jobs.**
If there is a steady stream of interactive, short jobs arriving, they will perpetually occupy the high-priority queues, and a long-running CPU-bound job may never get scheduled — it will starve indefinitely in the bottom queue.

**Problem 2: The "Gaming" / Time-Slicing Abuse Problem.**
A CPU-bound job could, just before its quantum expires, make a trivial I/O call (e.g., read one byte from a file) and voluntarily yield the CPU. Upon wakeup, it remains at the *same* high priority. By repeating this trick, the job can monopolize the CPU while appearing interactive.

**Solution — Rule 5 (Priority Boost / Anti-Starvation):**
*After some time period $S$, move all jobs in the system to the topmost queue.*

This ensures that any job, no matter how CPU-bound, is guaranteed to be reconsidered for high-priority execution periodically. It eliminates starvation.
