**Solution — Rule 6 (Better Accounting / Anti-Gaming):**
*Once a job uses up its time allotment at a given level (regardless of how many slices it took), demote it to the next lower queue.*

Instead of measuring per-slice CPU consumption, the OS tracks the **cumulative CPU time** the job has consumed at its current priority level. Even if the job yields and returns, the cumulative time is checked. Once the total reaches the threshold, demotion occurs. This prevents the gaming behaviour.

**Combined Effect:** Rules 5 and 6 transform the basic MLFQ into a robust, fair, and gaming-resistant scheduler — the basis of modern OS schedulers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** The two problems must be stated *first*, before the solutions are offered. Examiners specifically look for the word "starvation" and the phrase "gaming the scheduler." A solution without a clearly identified problem is worth at most 3 of 7 marks.

---

#### Question B — Sub-Part (a) [7 Marks] (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Compare the Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) scheduling algorithm with the Multilevel Queue (MLQ) scheduling algorithm. Why is MLFQ generally preferred in modern operating systems?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Multilevel Queue (MLQ) | Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Number of queues | Multiple | Multiple |
| Process movement between queues | **Not allowed** (fixed) | **Allowed** (dynamic) |
| Requires a-priori burst knowledge | Yes (to assign queue) | **No** (learns at runtime) |
| Adaptivity | Static priorities | **Adaptive priorities** |
| Starvation of CPU-bound jobs | Common | Possible but mitigated with Rule 5 |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Hard real-time / embedded | **General-purpose OS** (Linux, Windows) |

**Why MLFQ is preferred in modern OS:**
- It eliminates the need to **predict CPU burst lengths** at submission time — an impossible task in practice.
- It **adapts to dynamic workload patterns**; if a process's behaviour changes, its priority adjusts accordingly.
- It optimizes for **both response time and throughput** in a single, unified framework.
- It forms the conceptual foundation of modern schedulers (Linux CFS, FreeBSD ULE) which use decay-based or weighted variants of MLFQ.

---

#### Question B — Sub-Part (b) [7 Marks] (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**Consider a 2-queue MLFQ system. $Q_0$ uses Round Robin with quantum 6 ms, and $Q_1$ uses FCFS. Three processes arrive at $t=0$ with bursts: $P_1=15$ ms, $P_2=7$ ms, $P_3=4$ ms. Construct the Gantt chart and calculate the average waiting time.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: All jobs admitted to $Q_0$ at $t=0$.**

- $[0 \to 6]$: $P_1$ runs in $Q_0$ for 6 ms (uses full quantum). Demoted to $Q_1$ (remaining burst: 9 ms).
- $[6 \to 12]$: $P_2$ runs in $Q_0$ for 6 ms (uses full quantum). Demoted to $Q_1$ (remaining burst: 1 ms).
- $[12 \to 16]$: $P_3$ runs in $Q_0$ for 4 ms. **Terminates** (remaining burst becomes 0). Quantum not exhausted due to natural completion.

**Step 2: $Q_0$ empty at $t=16$.** Move to $Q_1$ which contains $P_1$ (9 ms) and $P_2$ (1 ms), scheduled FCFS.

- $[16 \to 25]$: $P_1$ runs in $Q_1$ for 9 ms. **Terminates at $t=25$.**
- $[25 \to 26]$: $P_2$ runs in $Q_1$ for 1 ms. **Terminates at $t=26$.**

**Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt} \; : \; & \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert P_3 \vert P_1 \vert P_2 \vert \\
& \vert 0 \;\;\; 6 \;\;\; 12 \;\;\; 16 \;\;\; 25 \;\;\; 26 \vert \quad \text{(ms)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Turnaround and Waiting Times.**

| Process | Arrival | First Run | Completion | Burst | Turnaround | Wait |
|---------|---------|-----------|------------|-------|------------|------|
| $P_1$   | 0       | 0         | 25         | 15    | 25         | 10   |
| $P_2$   | 0       | 6         | 26         | 7     | 26         | 19   |
| $P_3$   | 0       | 12        | 16         | 4     | 16         | 12   |

**Average Waiting Time:**

$$
\overline{T_{wait}} = \dfrac{10 + 19 + 12}{3} = \dfrac{41}{3} \approx 13.67 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often mistakenly demote $P_3$ to $Q_1$ because it "used a quantum of 6 ms." Correction: $P_3$ ran for only 4 ms — **less than the 6 ms quantum** — because its burst naturally ended. Demotion only occurs when the *quantum is exhausted with burst remaining*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Demotion vs. Completion:** A job that *terminates* within its quantum is **not** demoted. The condition for demotion is: (quantum elapsed) **AND** (burst still remaining).
> 2. **Quantum vs. Burst:** Students frequently confuse the *time quantum allotted to a queue* with the *total CPU burst of a process*. Quantum is per-scheduling-round; burst is the total CPU work.
> 3. **Rule Order:** When asked to list MLFQ rules, *always* number them 1–4 (or 1–6 in advanced form) in the canonical order. Random ordering costs 0.5 marks per misplaced rule.
> 4. **Voluntary Yield vs. Quantum Expiry:** Voluntary yield (I/O) keeps the job at the *same* level; quantum expiry demotes it. This distinction is tested nearly every exam cycle.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MLFQ** = a *multi-queue, priority-based, adaptive* scheduler that learns CPU-burst characteristics at run-time.
- **Rule 1 (Priority):** Higher priority always runs first.
- **Rule 2 (RR):** Equal-priority jobs share CPU via Round Robin.
- **Rule 3 (Admission):** New jobs always enter at the **topmost (highest priority) queue**.
- **Rule 4 (Demotion):** Exhausting the time quantum with burst remaining → demote to a lower queue. Voluntary I/O yield → stay at the same queue.
- The scheduler is **defined by 5 parameters**: number of queues, per-queue algorithms, priority increase method, priority decrease method, and initial placement rule.
- The two main problems of the basic MLFQ are: **(a) starvation of long jobs** and **(b) gaming by CPU-bound jobs**.
- **Rule 5 (Boost)** solves starvation by periodically resetting all jobs to the top queue.
- **Rule 6 (Accounting)** solves gaming by tracking *cumulative* CPU time, not per-slice.
- MLFQ eliminates the need for a-priori burst-time estimation, which is its single greatest practical advantage.
- The **standard metrics** to compute are turnaround time, response time, waiting time, and throughput — all measured in **ms** or **s**.
- MLFQ is the conceptual ancestor of the **Linux CFS**, **FreeBSD ULE**, and classical **Windows NT** schedulers.
- Gantt chart construction must be done **quantum-by-quantum**, checking queue membership at every scheduling decision point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: The Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study: The Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)** is the default process scheduler of the Linux kernel, introduced in kernel version **2.6.23 (October 2007)** by Ingo Molnár as a replacement for the earlier O(1) scheduler. CFS is a fair-share, proportional-share, deterministic scheduling algorithm that is designed to maximize CPU utilization while ensuring near-perfect fair distribution of processor time among runnable tasks.

CFS models a hypothetical **"ideal multitasking CPU"** — a perfectly fair processor that could physically execute multiple tasks in parallel at arbitrary fractional speeds. The scheduler mathematically approximates this ideal by maintaining, for every runnable task, a quantity called **virtual runtime (vruntime)**. Tasks with the lowest vruntime are scheduled next, ensuring that all runnable tasks converge toward receiving an equal share of CPU time, weighted by their **nice priority (niceness)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** CFS is the canonical case study in modern OS design illustrating how a theoretically elegant fair-queuing model is realized in a real production kernel using a **Red-Black Tree (rbtree)** as the run-queue data structure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Cafeteria Counter":**
> Imagine a single counter serving **N hungry people** simultaneously. A truly *fair* counter would give each person **1/N of the total service time**. CFS implements exactly this in software:
> - Each runnable task is a "person" waiting in line.
> - The counter = the single physical CPU.
> - The CFS scheduler continually asks: *Who has been served the least so far?* and dispatches that task. This is the role of the **red-black tree** — it is the sorted "queue" of customers, ordered by **vruntime** (the cumulative "service time received").

**Another Intuition – "Watches Running at Different Speeds":**
Each task has a *virtual clock* that ticks forward only when the task is running. Higher-priority (low-nice) tasks have slower-ticking virtual clocks, so they accumulate less vruntime for the same real runtime, and hence get picked more often. In effect:

$$
\text{vruntime}_i \;=\; \int_0^{t} \frac{\text{NICE\_0\_WEIGHT}}{w_i}\, d\tau
$$

This is the deep insight of CFS: *fairness* is not a heuristic — it is a direct consequence of weighting the rate of vruntime accumulation.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **vruntime** | Virtual runtime of a task (in nanoseconds, monotonic) |
| **NICE_0_LOAD** | Reference weight = **1024** (the weight of a nice-0 task) |
| **sched_latency_ns** | Target preemption window (default ≈ **6 ms × nr_running**) |
| **min_granularity_ns** | Minimum time slice (default **0.75 ms**) |
| **wakeup_granularity_ns** | Preemption threshold (default **1 ms**) |
| **weight** | Per-task weight derived from `nice` value (range 15 → 88761) |
| **rb_node** | Red-black tree node embedded in `sched_entity` |

> [!NOTE]
> All CFS time accounting is maintained in **nanoseconds** with **64-bit unsigned** arithmetic in modern kernels to avoid wraparound issues during long uptimes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fair-Queuing Mathematical Foundation

CFS is grounded in **stride scheduling** / **fair queuing** theory. The model assumes an ideal fair CPU in which **N** runnable tasks each receive a service rate of $1/N$. Since real CPUs cannot run tasks fractionally, CFS translates this ideal into a per-task virtual clock whose rate is **inversely proportional to the task's share**.

#### Step-by-step operational logic

1. **Task Creation / Wake-up:** A new `task_struct` is allocated and a `sched_entity` is initialized with `vruntime = 0` (or the current `cfs_rq->min_vruntime` to avoid grace-time cheating).
2. **Insertion in Red-Black Tree:** The task is inserted into the per-CPU CFS run-queue's **rbtree**, keyed by `vruntime`.
3. **Dispatcher Choice:** The scheduler picks the **leftmost node** of the rbtree (the task with the **smallest vruntime**).
4. **Execution:** The chosen task runs for a quantum computed dynamically from `sched_latency / nr_running`, bounded below by `min_granularity`.
5. **Tick Update:** On every scheduler tick, `update_curr()` updates:
   - `curr->vruntime += delta_exec × (NICE_0_LOAD / curr->load.weight)`
   - Updates `cfs_rq->min_vruntime`.
6. **Reinsert / Demote:** After running, the task's new vruntime is updated and the rbtree is rebalanced.
7. **Load Balancing:** Periodically, the kernel rebalances tasks across CPUs to honour the per-CPU `sched_domain` topology.

### 2.2 The Three Tunes: Tunable Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three "knobs" the administrator can turn** — these are extremely high-yield for KTU theory questions:

| Knob (sysctl) | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `kernel.sched_latency_ns` | **6 000 000 ns** (6 ms) | Target total timeslice across all runnable tasks |
| `kernel.sched_min_granularity_ns` | **750 000 ns** (0.75 ms) | Lower bound to prevent thrashing under heavy load |
| `kernel.sched_wakeup_granularity_ns` | **1 000 000 ns** (1 ms) | Avoids excessive preemption on wake-up |

> **Effective quantum formula:**
> $$\text{timeslice}_i \;=\; \max\!\left(\min\_granularity,\;\frac{sched\_latency}{n}\right)$$
> where $n$ = number of runnable tasks on the run-queue.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Units / Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta vruntime = \Delta t \cdot \dfrac{NICE\_0\_LOAD}{w_i}$ | nanoseconds | Virtual time accumulation rate |
| 2 | $w_i = \text{sched\_prio\_to\_weight}[\text{nice} + 20]$ | $15 \leq w_i \leq 88761$ | Per-task share weight |
| 3 | $T_i = \dfrac{sched\_latency}{n} \cdot \dfrac{w_i}{\sum w_j}$ | ms | Weighted time slice for task $i$ |
| 4 | $S_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_j w_j}$ | fraction (0..1) | Proportional CPU share of task $i$ |
| 5 | $\text{min\_vruntime} = \min(\text{vruntime of all tasks})$ | ns | Used to fast-forward new/slept tasks |
| 6 | $O(\log n)$ | complexity | rbtree insert/remove/select-next cost |
| 7 | $O(1)$ | complexity | Pick leftmost via `rb_leftmost` cache |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe `|` character in any cell where a divisor or absolute value is required, otherwise the markdown table will break.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

CFS's fairness model underpins nearly every Linux-based deployment: **Android phones** (where interactivity matters), **server farms** (where noisy-neighbour isolation matters), **HPC clusters** (where CPU pinning complements fair sharing), and **container runtimes** (where cgroups/v2 `cpu.weight` directly maps to the same weight table). The same weight table is now also exposed to userspace via the **`sched_setattr()`** system call (SCHED_BATCH, SCHED_IDLE, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CFS Time-Slice Formula

We derive the effective time-slice given to a task of weight $w_i$ in a run-queue of total weight $W = \sum_j w_j$.

**Step 1 — Ideal Fair CPU share.**
By the fair-queuing axiom, the ideal proportion of CPU time for task $i$ over a window of length $T$ is:

$$
S_i^{ideal} = \frac{w_i}{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective window under CFS.**
The kernel guarantees a complete round of *all* runnable tasks within `sched_latency_ns`. Let $T = sched\_latency$ and $n$ = number of runnable tasks. The per-task nominal slice is:

$$
T_{i,nominal} = \frac{T}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Bounding below by `min_granularity`.**
To prevent thrashing when $n$ is huge, the kernel applies a lower bound:

$$
T_{i} = \max\!\left( \text{min\_granularity},\; \frac{T}{n} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Weighting the slice.**
Tasks do **not** all receive the same slice. A task with weight $w_i$ in a queue of total weight $W$ over a window $T$ should receive:

$$
T_i \;=\; T \cdot \frac{w_i}{W}
$$

**Combining the bounds**, the final CFS slice becomes:

$$
T_i = \max\!\left(\text{min\_granularity},\; \frac{T}{n}\right) \cdot \frac{w_i}{W}
$$

> **Explanatory Note:** The factor $T/n$ ensures all tasks run within `sched_latency`; the lower bound prevents the slice from collapsing below the cost of a context switch.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the vruntime Update Rule

**Setup:** Task $i$ executes on the CPU for a real time interval $\Delta t_{\text{real}}$. We seek a virtual-time increment $\Delta v_i$ that makes the cumulative vruntime of all runnable tasks converge.

**Step 1 — Definition of vruntime rate.**
We require that the **rate of vruntime growth** of a task is inversely proportional to its weight, because the *faster* a task's vruntime grows, the *less* CPU it will appear to deserve. Define:

$$
\frac{dv_i}{dt} = \frac{C}{w_i}
$$

where $C$ is a constant chosen so that a **nice-0** task advances at unit rate.

**Step 2 — Setting the constant.**
For a nice-0 task, $w_{nice=0} = NICE\_0\_LOAD = 1024$. We require $dv/dt = 1$:

$$
1 = \frac{C}{1024} \;\Longrightarrow\; C = 1024 = \text{NICE\_0\_LOAD}
$$

**Step 3 — Final update law.**
For a task of weight $w_i$ running for $\Delta t_{\text{real}}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta v_i \;=\; \Delta t_{\text{real}} \cdot \frac{\text{NICE\_0\_LOAD}}{w_i}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**
Suppose a nice-0 task ($w=1024$) runs for **10 ms** = $10^7$ ns:

$$
\Delta v = 10^7 \cdot \frac{1024}{1024} = 10^7 \text{ ns}
$$

A nice-+5 task has weight $w=335$ (from the kernel table), so for the **same 10 ms**:

$$
\Delta v = 10^7 \cdot \frac{1024}{335} \approx 3.056 \times 10^7 \text{ ns}
$$

The nice-+5 task's vruntime grows ~3× faster, so it is selected **less often** — correctly giving it ~1/3 the CPU of a nice-0 task.

---

### 3.3 Sleeper Fairness — Why Sleeping Tasks Aren't Punished

When a task sleeps, the global `cfs_rq->min_vruntime` keeps advancing (it advances to the vruntime of the currently-running task). On wake-up, the sleeping task's vruntime is set to **at most** `min_vruntime - sysctl_sched_wakeup_granularity`. This *fast-forwards* the sleeper, giving it a credit so that it does not have to "pay back" the time it was not running.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the formal definition of **sleeper fairness** — a key CFS property that the older O(1) scheduler lacked and that fixes its notorious interactive-task regression.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CFS vs. Earlier Linux Schedulers

| Property | O(1) Scheduler (≤ 2.6.22) | CFS (≥ 2.6.23) |
|---|---|---|
| Run-queue data structure | Per-priority bitmap + array | **Red-black tree** (vruntime-ordered) |
| Insert / delete / pick-next | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Time slice | Fixed per priority | **Dynamic, weighted** |
| Interactive detection | Heuristic (sleep avg, bonus) | **Implicit (vruntime)** |
| Sleeper fairness | Weak | **Strong** |
| Scalability on many cores | Decent | **Excellent** with `sched_domain` |
| Latency under heavy load | Poor | **Bounded** by `min_granularity` |
| Tunable via sysctl | Limited | **Rich** (latency, granularity, wakeup) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architectural Block Diagram of CFS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HW["CPU Hardware"] --> TICK["Scheduler Tick / tick_sched_timer"]
    TICK --> UC["update_curr() : vruntime += delta_exec * NICE_0_LOAD / weight"]
    UC --> RQ["Per-CPU cfs_rq (struct cfs_rq)"]
    RQ --> RB["Red-Black Tree (rb_root_cached) keyed by vruntime"]
    RB --> LM["Leftmost Node = Next Task to Run"]
    RB --> INS["enqueue_task_fair() : rb_erase + rb_insert O(log n)"]
    RB --> DEL["dequeue_task_fair() : rb_erase O(log n)"]
    PICK["pick_next_task_fair() : O(1) via rb_leftmost cache"] --> LM
    LM --> SCHED["schedule() dispatches chosen task to CPU"]
    SCHED --> HW
    SYSCTL["sysctl_sched_latency / min_granularity / wakeup_granularity"] --> RQ
    NICE["nice value -> sched_prio_to_weight[] table"] --> UC
    LB["Load Balancer (run_rebalance_domains)"] --> RQ
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The rbtree is the heart of CFS. `update_curr()` feeds the rbtree with vruntime accounting; the leftmost pointer makes the next-pick $O(1)$. The load balancer keeps the per-CUF cfs_rqs roughly balanced.

### 4.2 Red-Black Tree State Diagram for 4 Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef runnable fill:#1f6feb,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef blocked fill:#6e7681,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    N1["T1\nvruntime=20ms"]:::runnable
    N2["T2\nvruntime=40ms"]:::runnable
    N3["T3\nvruntime=10ms"]:::runnable
    N4["T4\nvruntime=30ms"]:::runnable
    N3 --> N1
    N3 --> N2
    N2 --> N4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The rbtree is ordered such that the **leftmost** node is the task with the *smallest* vruntime. CFS picks that task to run next. After the chosen task accumulates vruntime, it is **re-inserted** at a new position closer to the right of the tree, preserving the ordering invariant.

### 4.3 CFS Pick-Next Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["schedule() invoked"] --> B{"cfs_rq->nr_running\n > 0?"}
    B -- No --> C["Pick idle task / idle_cpu()"]
    B -- Yes --> D{"Leftmost node\n cached?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Return rb_leftmost -> sched_entity"]
    D -- No --> F["Walk rbtree O(log n) to find min vruntime"]
    F --> E
    E --> G["context_switch() to selected task"]
    G --> H["update_curr() on next tick: vruntime += delta_exec * (1024/weight)"]
    H --> I["re-insert in rbtree at new vruntime position"]
    I --> A
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by "ideal fair CPU" in the context of the Linux Completely Fair Scheduler? How does CFS approximate it?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An *ideal fair CPU* is a hypothetical processor capable of executing multiple tasks simultaneously, giving each runnable task an *exactly equal* share of CPU time ($1/N$ for $N$ runnable tasks). Real CPUs cannot do this physically, so CFS **approximates** it by:
> (1) tracking each task's cumulative **virtual runtime (vruntime)**,
> (2) scheduling the task with the **minimum vruntime** next, and
> (3) advancing vruntime at a rate inversely proportional to the task's weight.
> Over a long window this yields each task a CPU share proportional to its weight, matching the ideal fair CPU. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three-step approximation.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any THREE tunable parameters of the Linux CFS and state their default values.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
> 1. `kernel.sched_latency_ns` — target preemption window — **default 6 000 000 ns (6 ms)**.
> 2. `kernel.sched_min_granularity_ns` — minimum time slice — **default 750 000 ns (0.75 ms)**.
> 3. `kernel.sched_wakeup_granularity_ns` — wake-up preemption threshold — **default 1 000 000 ns (1 ms)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Red-Black Tree (rbtree)** data structure used by CFS to maintain its run-queue. Show the steps involved in selecting the next task and inserting a task whose vruntime has just been updated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **virtual-runtime update law** of CFS. Show mathematically that a task with `nice=+5` receives approximately one-third the CPU of a `nice=0` task. Use the standard kernel weight table value $w_{\text{nice}=0}=1024$, $w_{\text{nice}=+5}=335$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Red-Black Tree of CFS (7 marks)**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A *self-balancing binary search tree* with the property that no path from root to leaf is more than twice as long as any other, guaranteeing $O(\log n)$ insert/remove.
- **CFS key (1 mark):** The key of each node is the task's `vruntime` (in nanoseconds). Left child has smaller vruntime; right child has larger.
- **Leftmost pointer (1 mark):** `rb_root_cached` caches the leftmost (smallest-vruntime) node so that `pick_next_task` is $O(1)$.
- **Insertion algorithm (2 marks):**
  1. Walk down the tree comparing new `vruntime` with each node, go left if smaller, right otherwise.
  2. Insert as a red leaf.
  3. Rebalance: recolour and rotate to restore Red-Black invariants.
- **Selection algorithm (1 mark):**
  1. Read `rb_leftmost` cache; if valid, return that node in $O(1)$.
  2. If cache is stale, traverse the leftmost spine in $O(\log n)$ and update cache.
- **Update of vruntime (1 mark):** On every scheduler tick, the running task's `vruntime += delta_exec × (NICE_0_LOAD / weight)`. The task is removed from the rbtree, its key updated, and re-inserted.

*(Refer to the rbtree diagram in Section 4.2 — drawing earns the diagram mark.)*

---

**(b) Derivation of the vruntime update law (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Let task $i$ have weight $w_i$. Define its virtual-time derivative as inversely proportional to its share:

$$
\frac{dv_i}{dt} = \frac{C}{w_i}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For a nice-0 task, $w_{nice=0} = 1024$ and we require $dv/dt = 1$, hence $C = 1024$. Therefore, for a task running for real time $\Delta t$:

$$
\Delta v_i = \Delta t \cdot \frac{1024}{w_i}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply to nice=+5. Its weight is $w_{+5} = 335$ (from the standard sched_prio_to_weight table). In the same real interval $\Delta t$:

$$
\Delta v_{+5} = \Delta t \cdot \frac{1024}{335} \approx 3.056\,\Delta t
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** The **steady-state CPU share** of a task is proportional to $w_i$. The share ratio is:

$$
\frac{S_{+5}}{S_{0}} = \frac{w_{+5}}{w_{0}} = \frac{335}{1024} \approx 0.327
$$

That is, a nice=+5 task receives roughly **one-third** the CPU time of a nice=0 task. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Compare the Linux **O(1) scheduler** with the **CFS** in terms of (i) data structure used, (ii) fairness, (iii) interactive task handling, and (iv) complexity of next-task selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **sleeper fairness** in CFS. Why is it important, and how does the kernel enforce it using `min_vruntime` and `sysctl_sched_wakeup_granularity_ns`? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) O(1) vs CFS — Comparison (7 marks — 1.75 marks per sub-point)**

| Aspect | O(1) Scheduler | CFS |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Data structure | Per-priority bitmap + linked-list array | **Red-Black Tree** keyed by vruntime |
| (ii) Fairness | Heuristic, prone to starvation | **Mathematically near-perfect** weight-proportional fairness |
| (iii) Interactive tasks | Required heuristics (sleep_avg, bonus) | **Implicit** via vruntime — sleeping tasks fast-forwarded |
| (iv) Next-task complexity | $O(1)$ (bitmap scan) | $O(1)$ (cached leftmost) amortized; $O(\log n)$ worst case |

---

**(b) Sleeper Fairness (7 marks)**

- **Definition (2 marks):** *Sleeper fairness* is the property that a task which voluntarily relinquishes the CPU (e.g., for I/O) is *not penalised* upon wake-up — its vruntime is fast-forwarded so that it does not have to "pay back" the time it was not running.
- **Why it matters (2 marks):** Without sleeper fairness, interactive tasks (which sleep often) would accumulate very low vruntime and be over-scheduled; in the old O(1) scheduler this caused *interactive-task starvation* of CPU-bound background work. CFS eliminates this class of bugs.
- **Enforcement (3 marks):**
  1. On every tick, `cfs_rq->min_vruntime` is updated to track the *minimum* vruntime of all currently-runnable tasks.
  2. When a task wakes up, the kernel sets:
  
  $$
  \text{task.vruntime} \mathrel{+}= cfs\_rq.min\_vruntime - \text{sysctl\_sched\_wakeup\_granularity}
  $$
  
  3. This means the sleeper is placed at the **left** of the rbtree (high priority) but only up to **1 ms** ahead of the current leftmost — preventing runaway credit accumulation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. *Do not* confuse `vruntime` (in **nanoseconds**, monotonic) with `utime`/`stime` (real CPU time in clock ticks). Examiners *specifically* test this distinction.
> 2. *Do not* state that CFS uses a *heap* or a *priority queue* — it is specifically a **Red-Black Tree**. Marks are deducted.
> 3. In derivations, *never* skip the step of setting $C = NICE\_0\_LOAD = 1024$. Without it, the formula carries an unknown constant and earns **zero** on the derivation mark.
> 4. When comparing schedulers, do **not** say "CFS is faster than O(1)" — both are $O(1)$ amortized. The real win is *fairness*, not raw speed.
> 5. Always quote the **default tunables** in nanoseconds, not milliseconds only — they expect both units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must remember for KTU ESE):**
> - CFS = Completely Fair Scheduler, introduced in **Linux 2.6.23** by Ingo Molnár, **2007**.
> - Core data structure: **Red-Black Tree** (rbtree), keyed by `vruntime`.
> - Pick-next is **O(1)** amortized via `rb_leftmost` cache; insert/remove is **O(log n)**.
> - vruntime update law: $\Delta v = \Delta t \cdot \dfrac{\text{NICE\_0\_LOAD}}{w_i} = \Delta t \cdot \dfrac{1024}{w_i}$.
> - **NICE_0_LOAD = 1024**; nice range is **−20 to +19**; weights range from **15 to 88761**.
> - Slice formula: $T_i = \max\!\left(\text{min\_granularity},\; \text{sched\_latency}/n\right) \cdot \dfrac{w_i}{W}$.
> - **Sleeper fairness** prevents interactive-task starvation via `min_vruntime` fast-forward.
> - Three sysctl tunables: `sched_latency_ns` (6 ms), `sched_min_granularity_ns` (0.75 ms), `sched_wakeup_granularity_ns` (1 ms).
> - CFS replaced the O(1) scheduler which suffered from heuristic-based unfairness.
> - CFS works with **per-CPU `cfs_rq`** structures, with periodic load balancing via `sched_domain` hierarchies.
> - The kernel exposes CFS via `nice`, `renice`, `sched_setattr()`, and cgroups-v2 `cpu.weight`.
> - For 14-mark answers, **always include a diagram** of the rbtree — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks for figures.
> - For derivations, write the integral form first, then discretise, then specialise to nice-0 to fix the constant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Linux Scheduling Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Linux Scheduling Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Linux Scheduling Implementation** refers to the modular scheduling subsystem inside the Linux kernel that decides, at every context-switch boundary, *which runnable task* is dispatched onto the CPU next. Historically, the kernel used a **multilevel feedback queue (MLFQ)** with a fixed **quantum** (the 2.4 "O(1) scheduler"); from kernel 2.6.23 onwards, the default is the **Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)**, which is a **fair-share, red-black-tree based, run-queue-per-CPU** implementation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 must-know entities:**
> `task_struct`, `runqueue`, `sched_entity`, `vruntime`, `nice value`, `weight`, `load_weight`, `CFS`, `O(1) Scheduler`, `Real-Time Schedulers (SCHED_FIFO / SCHED_RR)`, `nice -20 … +19`, `priority 0…139`, `RT priority 0…99`, `Normal priority 100…139`.

### 1.2 The Two Scheduling Classes

| Class | Range | Algorithms |
|---|---|---|
| Real-Time | **0 – 99** | `SCHED_FIFO`, `SCHED_RR`, `SCHED_DEADLINE` |
| Normal (CFS) | **100 – 139** | `CFS` (default), `SCHED_BATCH`, `SCHED_IDLE` |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **supermarket with one checkout counter** (single CPU). A fair store manager (the scheduler) gives each shopper a token with a number; whoever has the *smallest* number is served next. As soon as a shopper finishes, their token is *not* given back to the front of the line; instead, the time they spent shopping is added to their token, and they are reinserted into the line in numerical order. The store uses many parallel counters (one per cashier), but each counter is a completely independent fair-line. This is **CFS** — a *deterministic, ordered, fair-share* scheduler that is *O(1)* to pick the next task because the smallest token is always at the leftmost node of a balanced tree.

> [!NOTE]
> CFS replaces the earlier heuristic O(1) scheduler that was notoriously tuned by hand with "magic numbers." CFS, in contrast, uses one principle: **fair time division** — nothing more, nothing less.

### 1.4 Key Physical / Numerical Constants

- **Nice value range:** $-20$ (highest priority) to $+19$ (lowest priority), default $0$. This maps **20 levels** to a table of **40 weights**.
- **Weight for nice 0:** `1024` (the reference weight).
- **Granularity (default minimum time slice):** $\mathbf{1\,ms}$ (configurable via `sysctl_sched_min_granularity`).
- **Targeted preemption latency (max full cycle):** $\mathbf{20\,ms}$ for ≤ 5 tasks; $\mathbf{4 \times \text{granularity}}$ per task.
- **Number of runqueues:** **one per CPU core**, eliminating global lock contention.
- **`sysctl_sched_latency`:** **24 ms** (modern default is `6 * min_granularity`).
- **`sched_latency_ns`:** typically **6 000 000 ns = 6 ms** (kernel 5.x default).
- **CFS clock source:** `sched_clock()` — a per-CPU monotonic counter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `task_struct` — What the Scheduler Sees

Every process in Linux is described by a `task_struct` containing a `sched_entity se` field. The scheduler never directly examines the process; it examines the **scheduling entity** so that groups (cgroups) and tasks can be scheduled uniformly.

Key fields the scheduler manipulates:
- `se.vruntime` — virtual runtime in **nanoseconds** (a process-time accumulator scaled by *inverse weight*).
- `se.load.weight` — its share of the CPU, derived from `nice`.
- `se.exec_start`, `se.sum_exec_runtime` — wall time spent running.
- `se.on_rq` — flag telling the runqueue whether this entity is currently enqueued.

### 2.2 Run Queue Architecture (Per-CPU)

Each CPU owns a `struct rq` (the runqueue). It holds:
- `cfs_rq` — the CFS runqueue (a `rb_root` red-black tree).
- `rt_rq` — a real-time bitmap-priority array.
- `dl_rq` — the deadline scheduler's red-black tree.
- `nr_running`, `cpu_load[]`, `curr`, `idle`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The scheduler picks the **highest-priority non-empty class** (DL → RT → CFS → IDLE). Within a class, the *class-specific* data structure decides the next task. This is called **sched_class hierarchy**.

### 2.3 Nice Values and Weights

The classical formula mapping **nice** to **weight** is:

$$
w(n) = 1024 \times 1.25^{\,n}
$$

Equivalently, going from nice $a$ to nice $b$ gives a relative weight ratio of:

$$
\frac{w(b)}{w(a)} = 1.25^{\,b - a}
$$

This means a process with nice $-5$ gets *roughly* $1.25^{5} \approx 3.05$ times the CPU of a nice $0$ process. To avoid floating-point at every scheduling tick, the kernel stores weights in a precomputed **40-entry table** indexed by `nice + 20`.

### 2.4 Time-Slice / Scheduling Period Equations

Let:
- $N$ = number of runnable tasks on the runqueue.
- $L$ = `sched_latency` (default ≈ **6 ms** on modern kernels).
- $G$ = `min_granularity` (default **1 ms**).

The **ideal time slice** per task is:

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(G,\; \frac{L}{N}\right)
$$

When $N > L / G$, the slice is capped at $G$ so the **targeted latency** is at most $L + (N - 1)G$. This guarantees $O(1)$ scheduling decisions regardless of $N$.

### 2.5 CFS Virtual Runtime (vruntime) Update

For a process that has just executed for $\Delta t$ real time, with weight $w$:

$$
\Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t \times \frac{W_{0}}{w}
$$

where $W_0 = 1024$ is the weight of a nice-0 process. So a *higher* nice (lower weight) makes vruntime grow *faster*, pushing the task rightward in the red-black tree. CFS always picks the **leftmost** node — the task with the **smallest vruntime**, i.e. the one that has been *least* served, weighted by priority.

### 2.6 Real-Time Scheduling (SCHED_FIFO vs SCHED_RR)

| Property | SCHED_FIFO | SCHED_RR |
|---|---|---|
| Time slice | Runs until it **blocks**, **yields**, or is **preempted** by a higher-priority task | Round-robin **quantum** equal to the timeslice of SCHED_NORMAL of the same priority |
| Preempted by same-priority task? | No | Yes, after the quantum |
| Preempted by higher priority? | Yes | Yes |

There are **100** RT priorities (0 = highest). They use a **bitmap of 100 bits** plus a **bitmap of 100 runqueues** — lookup is `sched_find_first_bit()`, which is **O(1)**.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Nice to weight | $w(n) = 1024 \cdot 1.25^{\,n}$ | Stored in 40-entry table |
| Relative weight ratio | $w(b)/w(a) = 1.25^{\,b-a}$ | Common exam question |
| vruntime update | $\Delta v = \Delta t \cdot 1024 / w$ | Monotonic, non-decreasing per runqueue |
| Ideal time slice | $T_{slice} = \max(G,\; L/N)$ | $G$ = granularity, $L$ = latency |
| Max latency | $L + (N-1)G$ | Worst-case full cycle |
| Default $G$ (kernel 5.x) | **1 ms** | Tunable |
| Default $L$ (kernel 5.x) | **6 ms** | `sched_latency_ns` |
| Weight at nice 0 | **1024** | Reference |
| Weight at nice -20 | **88761** | Highest in table |
| Weight at nice +19 | **15** | Lowest in table |
| RT priority count | **100** (0–99) | SCHED_FIFO / SCHED_RR |
| Normal priority count | **40** (100–139) | Maps to nice -20…+19 |
| Total priorities | **140** | One bitmap-friendly range |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing absolute-value bars in this table — never a raw `|`, or the markdown table will break.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

CFS powers every modern Android phone, every AWS EC2 Linux instance, every SpaceX flight computer running Yocto-Linux, and every Kubernetes node. Understanding vruntime lets a DevOps engineer correctly tune **`cpu.shares`** in cgroups v1 / **`cpu.weight`** in cgroups v2 to enforce container-level fair-share guarantees.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing a Process's Time Slice Given $N$ Tasks

**Problem:** With `sched_latency = 6 ms` and `min_granularity = 1 ms`, compute the time slice for $N = 4$ runnable nice-0 tasks.

**Step 1.** Compute the unconstrained slice:

$$
T_{\text{slice}}^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{6\ \text{ms}}{4} = 1.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2.** Compare to the granularity floor:

$$
\max\!\left(1\ \text{ms},\ 1.5\ \text{ms}\right) = 1.5\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3.** Result: each of the 4 tasks gets a **1.5 ms slice**, so one full latency cycle equals $4 \times 1.5\ \text{ms} = 6\ \text{ms} = L$. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capping the Slice When the System is Overloaded

**Problem:** Same parameters, but $N = 10$ tasks.

**Step 1.** Compute ideal slice:

$$
\frac{6\ \text{ms}}{10} = 0.6\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2.** Apply the floor:

$$
\max\!\left(1\ \text{ms},\ 0.6\ \text{ms}\right) = 1\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3.** Compute the resulting full cycle:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot T_{\text{slice}} = 10 \times 1\ \text{ms} = 10\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4.** Verify it is bounded by the latency-cap formula:

$$
L + (N-1)G = 6 + (10-1)\times 1 = 15\ \text{ms}
$$

Since $10\ \text{ms} \le 15\ \text{ms}$, the cap is satisfied. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nice-to-Weight Ratio and vruntime Scaling

**Problem:** Two tasks A and B run on the same CPU. A has nice $-5$, B has nice $+5$. After $10\ \text{ms}$ of real execution, what is the ratio of their vruntimes accumulated?

**Step 1.** Compute weights using the table or formula:

$$
w_A = 1024 \times 1.25^{-5} = 1024 \times \frac{1}{3.0517578125} \approx 335.54
$$

$$
w_B = 1024 \times 1.25^{5} = 1024 \times 3.0517578125 \approx 3125.00
$$

**Step 2.** Apply the vruntime update:

$$
\Delta v_A = 10\ \text{ms} \times \frac{1024}{335.54} \approx 30.52\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
\Delta v_B = 10\ \text{ms} \times \frac{1024}{3125.00} \approx 3.28\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3.** Take the ratio:

$$
\frac{\Delta v_A}{\Delta v_B} = \frac{30.52}{3.28} \approx 9.31 \;\approx\; 1.25^{10}
$$

**Interpretation:** A (higher priority) accumulates vruntime *faster*, so it spends *less* wall-time before being selected next — which means the kernel gives it more real CPU. This is the central fairness inversion: in CFS, *higher* priority ⇒ *higher* vruntime growth per unit real time ⇒ the task catches up to the pack faster.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mapping a Nice Value to the kernel 0–139 Priority

**Problem:** A process has nice $n = -3$. What is the kernel `static_prio` field?

**Step 1.** Apply the static mapping used by `nice_to_prio()`:

$$
\text{static\_prio} = 120 + n
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute:

$$
\text{static\_prio} = 120 + (-3) = 117
$$

**Step 3.** Verify it lies in the CFS range:

$$
100 \le 117 \le 139 \;\;\checkmark
$$

For real-time tasks, `rt_priority ∈ [0, 99]` and the kernel priority is `0 + rt_priority`, so the scheduler's bitmap is contiguous across 0–139.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method-to-Question Cross-Reference

| Exam Style Question | Applicable Formula(s) | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|
| "Calculate weight for given nice" | $w(n) = 1024 \cdot 1.25^{n}$ | Students forget $1.25$ vs $\frac{1}{1.25}$ sign for negative nice |
| "Find time slice for N tasks" | $T = \max(G, L/N)$ | Forgetting to clamp at $G$ |
| "Find vruntime change" | $\Delta v = \Delta t \cdot W_0 / w$ | Mixing wall time and vruntime |
| "Map nice to kernel priority" | $p = 120 + n$ | Forgetting the constant is **120**, not 100 |
| "Compare RT and CFS" | Class hierarchy (DL → RT → CFS → IDLE) | Saying RT and CFS share the same queue |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Linux Scheduler Class Hierarchy

The Linux scheduler is a **modular class chain**. Each class implements the same set of `sched_class` operations, and the kernel walks the chain from highest to lowest priority, picking the first non-empty class.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU rq needs next task] --> B{Check DL rq}
    B -->|non-empty| C[Pick earliest deadline from DL rq]
    B -->|empty| D{Check RT rq}
    D -->|non-empty| E[Pick highest-priority from RT rq FIFO or RR]
    D -->|empty| F{Check CFS rq}
    F -->|non-empty| G[Pick leftmost node from red-black tree of vruntime]
    F -->|empty| H{Check idle class}
    H -->|non-empty| I[Run the idle task]
    H -->|empty| J[CPU goes to low-power C-state]
```

### 4.2 Per-CPU Runqueue Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU0[CPU 0 rq] --> DL0[dl_rq]
    CPU0 --> RT0[rt_rq bitmap 100 bits]
    CPU0 --> CFS0[cfs_rq red-black tree]
    CPU0 --> IDLE0[idle_sched_class]

    CPU1[CPU 1 rq] --> DL1[dl_rq]
    CPU1 --> RT1[rt_rq bitmap 100 bits]
    CPU1 --> CFS1[cfs_rq red-black tree]
    CPU1 --> IDLE1[idle_sched_class]

    TASK[(Task arrives)] --> BAL{Load balancing}
    BAL -->|idle CPU| CPU0
    BAL -->|busier| CPU1
```

### 4.3 CFS Red-Black Tree Selection

The CFS runqueue is a **sorted red-black tree** keyed by `vruntime`. The leftmost node is always the next task; insertion and deletion are $O(\log N)$ but *picking* the next task is $O(1)$ (it is cached in `rb_leftmost`).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Tree root] --> L1[Left child]
    Root --> R1[Right child]
    L1 --> L2[Left-left: smallest vruntime]
    L1 --> R2[Right child]
    R1 --> L3[Left child]
    R1 --> R3[Right child]
    L2 --> LM[rb_leftmost cached pointer]
    L2 --> LX[Other node]
    LM --> SEL[Selected as next task]
    SEL --> RUN[Place on CPU for T slice milliseconds]
    RUN --> UPDATE[Update vruntime by delta t times 1024 over w]
    UPDATE --> REINSERT[Reinsert into tree at new vruntime position]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow for a Single Scheduling Tick

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Timer tick on CPU i] --> T1{Is current task time slice exhausted?}
    T1 -->|No| T2[Continue running current task]
    T1 -->|Yes| T3[Dequeue and reinsert current task by vruntime]
    T3 --> T4[Pick next from rb_leftmost of CFS rq]
    T4 --> T5[Context switch: switch_to prev next]
    T5 --> T6[New task begins its slice]
    T0 --> T7{Higher-priority task woke up}
    T7 -->|Yes| T8[Preempt current task via reschedule]
    T7 -->|No| T2
```

> [!NOTE]
> In Mermaid, node IDs above are all alphanumeric (e.g., `CPU0`, `L2`, `SEL`) — none use reserved words like `end`, `start`, or `subgraph` as standalone node names. Labels are kept in plain uppercase to avoid formatting collisions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the two real-time scheduling policies in Linux and state one key difference between them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **SCHED_FIFO**: A runnable SCHED_FIFO task runs until it **blocks**, **yields** the CPU, or is **preempted** by a strictly higher-priority task. It is *not* preempted by a same-priority task. (1½)
2. **SCHED_RR**: A SCHED_RR task runs for a **fixed time slice (quantum)**; after that, if another runnable task of the **same** priority exists, the scheduler preempts it. (1½)
3. *Difference:* SCHED_RR provides round-robin **time-sharing** between same-priority tasks; SCHED_FIFO does not. (Bonus line – optional credit)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
*What is the role of `vruntime` in the Completely Fair Scheduler?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `vruntime` is a per-task **virtual time accumulator** stored in the `sched_entity` of each task. (1)
- It is updated after every tick as $\Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t \times \frac{1024}{\text{weight}}$, so higher-priority (lower-nice) tasks accumulate vruntime **faster** per unit wall time. (1)
- The CFS runqueue is a **red-black tree sorted by vruntime**; the task with the **smallest vruntime** (the `rb_leftmost` node) is selected next, ensuring fair-share CPU allocation. (1)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **scheduling class hierarchy** in the Linux kernel. Describe how the **scheduler** picks the next task from a per-CPU `struct rq`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has `sched_latency = 6 ms` and `min_granularity = 1 ms`. For $N = 8$ runnable nice-0 tasks, calculate **(i)** the time slice per task, **(ii)** the total latency cycle, and **(iii)** verify that it lies within the latency cap $L + (N-1)G$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Class hierarchy & pick_next_task (7 marks):**
- The Linux scheduler is organized into **scheduling classes** ordered strictly: **Deadline → Real-Time → CFS → Idle → Stop**. (1)
- Each CPU has its own `struct rq` containing one sub-runqueue per class: `dl_rq`, `rt_rq`, `cfs_rq`, `idle_task`. (1)
- `pick_next_task(rq)` iterates the class chain via `for_each_class()` and returns the first non-empty class's `pick_next_task` method. (1)
- **DL**: returns task with earliest absolute deadline. (1)
- **RT**: finds the first set bit in a 100-bit bitmap → `sched_find_first_bit()` (O(1)). (1)
- **CFS**: returns `rb_leftmost(rq->cfs_rq.rb_root)` — the leftmost node of the vruntime-sorted red-black tree. (1)
- **Idle**: returns the per-CPU `idle_task`. (1)

**Diagram** (award full marks if red-black tree + 4 classes are visible):
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[struct rq on CPU i] --> B[dl_rq: red-black tree of dl tasks]
    A --> C[rt_rq: 100-bit bitmap plus 100 runqueues]
    A --> D[cfs_rq: red-black tree keyed by vruntime]
    A --> E[idle_task_struct]
    F[Kernel calls pick_next_task] --> G{Iterate class chain}
    G --> H[Deadline]
    G --> I[Real-Time]
    G --> J[CFS]
    G --> K[Idle]
```

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Time slice per task:**

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(G,\ \frac{L}{N}\right) = \max\!\left(1\ \text{ms},\ \frac{6\ \text{ms}}{8}\right) = \max(1,\ 0.75) = 1\ \text{ms}
$$

(2 marks)

**(ii) Total latency cycle:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \times T_{\text{slice}} = 8 \times 1\ \text{ms} = 8\ \text{ms}
$$

(2 marks)

**(iii) Latency cap check:**

$$
L + (N-1)G = 6\ \text{ms} + (8-1)\times 1\ \text{ms} = 6 + 7 = 13\ \text{ms}
$$

Since $8\ \text{ms} \le 13\ \text{ms}$, the latency cap is **satisfied**. (3 marks)

---

#### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain how **nice values** influence scheduling in Linux. Show that the weight ratio between a process with nice $-5$ and a process with nice $+5$ is approximately $1.25^{10} \approx 9.31$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the **O(1) scheduler** (Linux 2.4) and the **Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)** of Linux 2.6+. Discuss **two advantages** of CFS over O(1). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Nice values (7 marks):**
- The `nice` value ranges from $-20$ to $+19$ with default $0$. A **lower** (more negative) nice means **higher** scheduling priority. (1)
- Internally, the kernel maps nice to a **weight** using the formula $w(n) = 1024 \cdot 1.25^{n}$, precomputed into a **40-entry table**. (1)
- The weight is used both in the **CFS runqueue share calculation** and in **vruntime scaling**, so a higher-weight task accumulates vruntime *faster* per unit of real time. (1)
- For nice $-5$ and nice $+5$:

$$
w(-5) = 1024 \times 1.25^{-5} = \frac{1024}{3.05175} \approx 335.54
$$

$$
w(+5) = 1024 \times 1.25^{5} = 1024 \times 3.05175 \approx 3125.00
$$

(2 marks for the two computations)

$$
\frac{w(+5)}{w(-5)} = \frac{3125.00}{335.54} \approx 9.31 = 1.25^{10}
$$

(1 mark for the final ratio, 1 mark for the verbal conclusion)

**Conclusion:** The nice $-5$ process gets **~9.31 times** the CPU share of a nice $+5$ process when both are runnable.

---

**(b) O(1) vs CFS (7 marks):**

| Feature | O(1) Scheduler (2.4) | CFS (2.6.23+) |
|---|---|---|
| Data structure | Per-CPU **bitmap + arrays** of static priorities | Per-CPU **red-black tree** of `vruntime` |
| Complexity of pick-next | O(1) (bitmap scan) | O(1) for next pick (leftmost cached), O(log N) for insert/remove |
| Fairness model | Heuristic; uses `sleep_avg`, `interactive` flags | **Mathematically fair**: tracks per-task vruntime |
| Time slice | Fixed per priority class | **Dynamic**: $T = \max(G, L/N)$ |
| Tunability | Many magic numbers, hard to tune | Two knobs: $L$ and $G$ |
| Handling interactive vs CPU-bound | Heuristic bonus/penalty | Implicit: I/O-bound tasks have low vruntime, naturally selected |
| Real-time | Yes | Yes (separate class) |

(4 marks for comparison table)

**Two advantages of CFS:**
1. **Provably fair**: a perfectly fair CPU share is the design goal, not a heuristic. Under contention, each task receives its **weighted** share within the targeted latency. (1½)
2. **Scalable and lock-friendly**: a single per-CPU runqueue with a single lock per CPU; load balancing uses domain-aware logic. No global runqueue lock as in older BSD schedulers. (1½)

(3 marks for the two advantages)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write**
> 1. **Sign of the nice value:** Students often write `1.25^{+5}` for a process with nice $-5$ — this is **wrong**. The exponent must keep the sign of $n$ intact.
> 2. **Constant 120, not 100:** The mapping `static_prio = 120 + n` is the most-asked one-mark trap. The **100** is the *boundary* between RT and CFS, not the base.
> 3. **vruntime ≠ wall time:** Examiners specifically test if you know that vruntime is **scaled by inverse weight**. Writing `Δvruntime = Δt` is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Forget the cap:** When asked for the time slice, you must show the $\max$ explicitly; skipping the granularity floor loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing O(1) scheduler with O(1) complexity:** The "O(1) scheduler" is its **name**, not a guarantee that *CFS* is slower. CFS is also O(1) for the next-pick.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ The Linux scheduler is **class-based**: DL → RT → CFS → IDLE; the **first non-empty class** wins.
- ⭐ Each CPU has its **own** `struct rq` — no global scheduler lock.
- ⭐ `nice` range: $\mathbf{-20 \ldots +19}$; mapped to weight via $\mathbf{1024 \cdot 1.25^{n}}$.
- ⭐ Weight of a nice-0 task is exactly **1024** (the reference).
- ⭐ Time slice = $\mathbf{\max\!\left(G,\ L/N\right)}$; default $L = 6\ \text{ms}$, $G = 1\ \text{ms}$.
- ⭐ `vruntime` is the vruntime-sorted red-black tree key; CFS picks **rb_leftmost** in O(1).
- ⭐ $\Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t \cdot \mathbf{1024} / w$ — higher weight ⇒ faster vruntime growth.
- ⭐ Real-time range: kernel priorities **0–99**; normal range: **100–139**; total = 140.
- ⭐ SCHED_FIFO = no preemption by same-priority tasks; SCHED_RR = preempts after quantum.
- ⭐ Static priority mapping: $\mathbf{120 + n}$ for normal tasks; $\mathbf{0 + rt\_priority}$ for RT.
- ⭐ O(1) scheduler is the *name* of the pre-2.6.23 design — it used a bitmap, not a tree.
- ⭐ CFS is **O(1)** for pick-next, **O(log N)** for insert/remove.
- ⭐ Latency cap formula: $\mathbf{L + (N-1)G}$ — guarantees bounded worst-case cycle.
- ⭐ **Default weight table endpoints:** nice $-20 \to 88761$, nice $+19 \to 15$.
- ⭐ `vruntime` is per-runqueue-monotonic; on wakeup after sleep, vruntime is set to the tree minimum minus `sysctl_sched_wakeup_granularity` so sleeping tasks regain CPU quickly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preemption and Context Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preemption and Context Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Preemption — Formal Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Operating System syllabus, **Preemption** is defined as the mechanism by which the operating system kernel forcibly revokes control of the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** from a currently executing process (or thread) and reallocates it to another process, typically based on scheduling policies such as priority, time quantum expiry, or the arrival of a higher-priority task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> Preemption is the temporary halt/interruption of a running process by the scheduler to allocate the CPU to another process. A scheduling algorithm is called **Preemptive** if the OS can take the CPU away from a process before it finishes its CPU burst; it is **Non-Preemptive (Co-operative)** if a process releases the CPU only voluntarily (via termination or I/O wait).

### 1.2 Preemption — Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen** with one head chef (the CPU). If the head chef is preparing a 5-course meal (a long process), and a VIP order for a single dish arrives (a higher-priority process), the manager (the OS Scheduler) can either:
- **Non-Preemptive (Cooperative) Approach:** Let the chef finish all 5 courses before starting the VIP order (frustrating for the VIP).
- **Preemptive Approach:** Politely pause the chef mid-recipe, place the recipe card on a hook (save the **context**), let the chef quickly make the VIP dish, then resume the 5-course meal by reading the saved recipe card (restore the **context**).

This pausing-and-resuming is **preemption**, and the act of putting down one recipe and picking up another is **context switching**.

### 1.3 Context Switching — Formal Definition

**Context Switching** is the procedure of saving the complete execution state (context) of the currently running process and loading the previously saved state of another process, so that multiple processes can share a single CPU in a time-shared manner.

The "context" of a process primarily consists of:
1. **Program Counter (PC)** — address of the next instruction.
2. **CPU Registers** — accumulator, base/index/segment registers, general-purpose registers.
3. **Process Stack Pointer (SP)** — top of the runtime stack.
4. **Memory Management Information** — base/limit registers, page tables, segment tables.
5. **Process State** — running, ready, blocked, etc.
6. **Open File Descriptors, Priority, Accounting Info** (part of the Process Control Block).

All this information is bundled inside a kernel data structure called the **Process Control Block (PCB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Concept:** The PCB is to a process what a **health record file** is to a patient — without it, the OS has no way to "remember" the process when it is suspended. Every preemption *must* be followed by a PCB save/restore.

### 1.4 Preemption vs Context Switching — Are They the Same?

| Aspect | Preemption | Context Switching |
|---|---|---|
| **What is it?** | A *policy decision* (deciding to forcibly stop a process) | A *mechanism* (saving/restoring state) |
| **Triggered by** | Scheduler logic, interrupts, priority changes | Timer interrupt, system call, I/O completion |
| **Always required?** | Yes (it's the decision) | No (context switch is a *possible* consequence) |
| **Overhead** | Negligible by itself | Significant (memory/register access cost) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU One-Liner for Exams:** "Preemption is the **why**; Context Switching is the **how**."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions Under Which Preemption Can Occur

A preemptive OS can seize the CPU from a running process in the following scenarios:

1. **Time-Slice Expiry** — The process exhausts its assigned quantum in **Round Robin** scheduling.
2. **Higher-Priority Arrival** — A new process enters the ready queue with a strictly higher priority.
3. **Preemption by I/O Completion** — A blocked process transitions back to ready and now preempts the running one.
4. **System Calls / Mode Switch** — A process voluntarily enters kernel mode (though this is a *switch*, not strictly preemption).
5. **Voluntary Release** — A process blocks on I/O, semaphore, or sleep (technically **cooperative** yield, not preemption).
6. **Interrupt Handling** — Hardware interrupt forces a save of current execution context.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Context Switch — Step-by-Step Logic

The KTU board examiner frequently asks for the *exact steps* of a context switch. The textbook sequence is:

1. The **timer interrupt** (or scheduler hook) fires in kernel mode.
2. Save the current process's CPU registers, PC, SP, and PSW (Program Status Word) into its **PCB** located in kernel memory.
3. Update the current process's PCB state field from `Running` → `Ready` (or `Blocked`).
4. Move the PCB pointer into the appropriate ready/wait queue.
5. The **scheduler** runs and picks the next process from the ready queue.
6. Update the selected process's PCB state from `Ready` → `Running`.
7. Load the new process's register values, page table base register, and PC from its PCB into the hardware.
8. Return from interrupt — CPU now executes the *new* process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Overhead:** Steps 2 and 7 are pure overhead — no useful work is done by user code during this window. This is exactly what makes context switches "expensive."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the quantitative metrics KTU examiners expect you to know:

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CPU Utilization} = 1 - P^{n}$ | Probability that **all n** processes are waiting for I/O simultaneously. **P** = I/O-wait probability per process. | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Processes Completed}}{\text{Unit Time}}$ | Number of processes finished per unit time. | Processes / second |
| 3 | $\text{Turnaround Time} = T_{\text{finish}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | Total time a process spends in the system. | Seconds (s) |
| 4 | $\text{Waiting Time} = T_{\text{turnaround}} - T_{\text{burst}}$ | Time spent in the ready queue only. | Seconds (s) |
| 5 | $\text{Response Time} = T_{\text{first-run}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | Time until process first gets the CPU. | Seconds (s) |
| 6 | $\text{CPU Efficiency} = \dfrac{n \cdot T_{s}}{n \cdot T_{s} + (n-1) \cdot T_{cs}}$ | Useful CPU time vs total time including **n−1** context switches for **n** processes. | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | $\text{Context Switch Overhead \%} = \left( \dfrac{T_{cs}}{T_{\text{quantum}}} \right) \times 100$ | Fraction of quantum wasted in a single switch. | Percent (%) |
| 8 | $\text{Net CPU Time} = T_{\text{total}} - N \cdot T_{cs}$ | Actual useful computation when **N** context switches occur. | Seconds (s) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Table-Breaking Rule:** In the above table, all absolute value or conditional bars are rendered as `$\vert ... \vert$` rather than the literal pipe symbol `|`. This avoids corrupting the markdown table grid.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** Preemption guarantees hard deadlines (used in **anti-lock braking systems**, **pacemakers**, **avionics**).
- **Cloud / Hypervisors (KVM, VMware ESXi):** Context switching between virtual machines enables server consolidation.
- **Linux Kernel (CFS Scheduler):** Uses **vruntime**-based preemption to maintain fairness in multi-core servers.
- **Mobile OS (Android, iOS):** Foreground-app preemption ensures UI responsiveness within **16 ms** (60 FPS).
- **Embedded Systems:** Non-preemptive kernels (e.g., legacy **VxWorks** modes) trade off responsiveness for predictability.

## 2.5 Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive Kernels — Comparison Matrix

| Property | Preemptive Kernel | Non-Preemptive Kernel |
|---|---|---|
| **Interruptibility** | Kernel code can itself be preempted | Kernel runs to completion (cooperative) |
| **Latency** | Low, bounded | High, unbounded |
| **Complexity** | High (race conditions, SMP spinlocks) | Low, simpler synchronization |
| **Examples** | Linux, Windows NT, modern macOS XNU | Traditional Unix, RTOS in some modes |
| **Throughput** | Slightly lower (more switches) | Higher in single-task workloads |
| **KTU Exam Weight** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CPU Efficiency Under Context Switch Overhead

> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** Consider a system running **n** processes sequentially on a single-core CPU. Each process requires **T_s** seconds of pure CPU service. Each context switch takes **T_cs** seconds. Derive the expression for **CPU efficiency** and compute the value for n = 5, T_s = 100 ms, T_cs = 1 ms.

### Step 1: Identify the Useful CPU Time
The total *useful* work performed by the CPU is the cumulative service time of all n processes:

$$T_{\text{useful}} = n \cdot T_{s}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Overhead Time
For **n** processes to run sequentially, exactly **n − 1** context switches must occur (no switch is needed *after* the last process):

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = (n - 1) \cdot T_{cs}$$

### Step 3: Total Wall-Clock Time
The total elapsed time the system takes to complete all n processes is:

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{useful}} + T_{\text{overhead}} = n \cdot T_{s} + (n - 1) \cdot T_{cs}$$

### Step 4: Define CPU Efficiency
CPU efficiency η is the fraction of total time spent doing useful work:

$$\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify
Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 3:

$$\eta = \dfrac{n \cdot T_{s}}{n \cdot T_{s} + (n - 1) \cdot T_{cs}}$$

### Step 6: Plug in Numerical Values
For n = 5, T_s = 100 ms, T_cs = 1 ms:

$$\eta = \dfrac{5 \times 100}{5 \times 100 + (5 - 1) \times 1} = \dfrac{500}{504}$$

$$\eta \approx 0.9921 \;\text{or}\; 99.21\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with **4** context switches, the overhead is negligible (≈ 0.79%) because the quantum is large relative to the switch time. This is why **Round Robin with large time slices** performs near-identically to FCFS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Context Switch Overhead as a Percentage of Quantum

> **Problem Statement:** A Round Robin scheduler uses a time quantum **Q = 20 ms**. The average context switch time is **T_cs = 0.1 ms**. What fraction of *every* quantum is wasted in switching? If the quantum is reduced to **Q = 1 ms**, what is the new overhead percentage?

### Step 1: Apply the Overhead Formula
From the KTU Formula Sheet (Row 7):

$$\text{Overhead \%} = \left( \dfrac{T_{cs}}{Q} \right) \times 100$$

### Step 2: Case A — Q = 20 ms
Substitute Q = 20 ms and T_cs = 0.1 ms:

$$\text{Overhead}_{A} = \left( \dfrac{0.1}{20} \right) \times 100 = 0.5\%$$

### Step 3: Case B — Q = 1 ms
Substitute Q = 1 ms and T_cs = 0.1 ms:

$$\text{Overhead}_{B} = \left( \dfrac{0.1}{1} \right) \times 100 = 10\%$$

### Step 4: Comparative Interpretation
The ratio of overheads is:

$$\dfrac{\text{Overhead}_{B}}{\text{Overhead}_{A}} = \dfrac{10}{0.5} = 20$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Reducing the quantum by a factor of **20** increases switching overhead by a factor of **20**. This is the classic **Turnaround Time vs Response Time** trade-off: small quantum = better response, worse throughput. KTU examiners love this trade-off question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Throughput Under Periodic Context Switches

> **Problem Statement:** A single-core server completes **N_ctx = 100** context switches per second. Each context switch consumes **T_cs = 50 μs**. What is the **net CPU time** available for user processes per second, and the **throughput** if each user process requires **T_s = 200 ms** of pure CPU?

### Step 1: Compute Overhead Per Second
Total context switch time wasted per second:

$$T_{\text{cs/sec}} = N_{ctx} \cdot T_{cs} = 100 \times 50\,\mu s = 5000\,\mu s = 5\,ms$$

### Step 2: Net CPU Time Available

$$T_{\text{net}} = 1\,s - 0.005\,s = 0.995\,s$$

### Step 3: Number of User Processes Completed

$$N_{\text{user}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{net}}}{T_{s}} = \dfrac{995\,ms}{200\,ms} = 4.975$$

Since only whole processes count:

$$N_{\text{user, complete}} = 4 \text{ processes per second}$$

### Step 4: Throughput

$$\text{Throughput} = 4\,\text{processes/sec}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often report 5 processes/second ignoring overhead. Always subtract context-switch time *first*, then divide.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive

| Application Domain | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Real-Time (missile guidance)** | Preemptive with priority inheritance | Strict deadline guarantees needed |
| **Soft Real-Time (multimedia streaming)** | Preemptive with rate-monotonic scheduling | Tolerates occasional misses |
| **Desktop OS (Linux, Windows)** | Fully preemptive | Responsiveness & fairness for many users |
| **Simple Embedded (microwave controller)** | Non-preemptive | Predictability, low code complexity |
| **Database Transaction Engine** | Cooperative (voluntary yield) | Avoids mid-transaction preemption |
| **RTOS Bootloader** | Non-preemptive | Deterministic initialization sequence |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Context Switch

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the OS kernel mediates a context switch between two processes (P1 and P2) on a single-core CPU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Executes Process P1] --> B[Timer Interrupt or Scheduler Trigger Fires]
    B --> C[Kernel Mode Entry via Interrupt Vector]
    C --> D[Save P1 Registers PC SP PSW into P1 PCB]
    D --> E[Update P1 PCB State from Running to Ready]
    E --> F[Insert P1 PCB into Ready Queue Tail]
    F --> G[Invoke Scheduler Function]
    G --> H[Scheduler Selects P2 from Ready Queue Head]
    H --> I[Update P2 PCB State from Ready to Running]
    I --> J[Load P2 Registers PC SP PSW from P2 PCB]
    J --> K[Return from Interrupt Instruction]
    K --> L[CPU Resumes P2 Execution]
    L --> M[Timer Interrupt Fires for P2]
    M --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Loop:** The arrow from `M` back to `C` indicates the cyclical nature of context switching — the kernel repeatedly traps, saves, and restores as long as the system runs. The state of P1 in the **Ready Queue** (step F) is critical: it is the *only* place where P1's existence is remembered between swaps.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Preemption Trigger Flow

This diagram classifies the *triggers* that lead to preemption in a modern preemptive OS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Preemption Triggers] --> B[Time Slice Expiry]
    A --> C[Higher Priority Arrival]
    A --> D[I/O Completion of Blocked Process]
    A --> E[Explicit Yield System Call]
    A --> F[Hardware Interrupt Request]

    B --> G[OS Saves Current Context]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Scheduler Runs]
    H --> I[New Process Loaded]
    I --> J[CPU Continues Execution]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "THIEF-Y":** Triggers of preemption are: **T**ime-slice, **H**igher priority arrival, **I**/O completion, **E**xplicit yield, **F**ault/Interrupt. Memorize using this acronym.

---

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Process Lifecycle with Preemption

This diagram shows how a process moves through its lifecycle, with the **Running → Ready** transition representing *preemption*.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready: Admitted
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preemption OR Time Slice Expiry
    Running --> Blocked: I/O or Event Wait
    Blocked --> Ready: I/O Completion or Event Signal
    Running --> [*]: Exit or Terminate
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** There are **two distinct** ways a process leaves the `Running` state: (1) **Preemption** (Running → Ready, voluntary by the *OS*) and (2) **Blocking** (Running → Blocked, voluntary by the *process*). KTU examiners frequently ask students to label these arrows correctly in an exam sketch.

---

## 4.4 Context Switch Time Decomposition (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Context Switch Total Time T_cs] --> B[Save Old Process State]
    A --> C[Scheduler Decision Time]
    A --> D[Load New Process State]
    A --> E[CPU Pipeline Drain and Refill]

    B --> B1[Register Dump to Kernel Stack]
    B --> B2[PCB Update and Queue Insertion]

    C --> C1[Algorithm Lookup eg Priority Scan]

    D --> D1[PCB Fetch from Ready Queue]
    D --> D2[Register Load from Kernel Stack]

    E --> E1[Branch Predictor Flush]
    E --> E2[TLB Flush or ASID Switch]
    E --> E3[Cache Pollution Penalty]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Hidden Cost:** The **TLB flush** and **cache pollution** (E2, E3) are often the *largest* contributors to T_cs in modern CPUs — sometimes 5-10× the cost of the register save/restore itself. KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to mention these as part of the "context switch overhead."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Distinguish between Preemptive and Non-Preemptive scheduling. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

Preemptive scheduling is a scheduling policy in which the operating system is authorized to **forcibly deallocate** the CPU from a currently running process before it has completed its CPU burst. The decision is made by the kernel's scheduler based on policies such as time quantum expiry, priority, or interrupt handling. *Example:* **Round Robin** scheduling in Linux.

Non-Preemptive (or co-operative) scheduling allows a process to retain the CPU until it **voluntarily relinquishes** it, either by terminating, issuing a blocking I/O system call, or explicitly yielding. *Example:* Traditional **FCFS** (First-Come, First-Served) when no quantum is enforced, or legacy Windows 3.1 co-operative multitasking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** 3 marks = Definition of preemptive (1) + Definition of non-preemptive (1) + One example each (1). Use the keywords **"forcibly"** and **"voluntarily"** for maximum examiner approval.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
**What is a context switch? List the information that must be saved during a context switch.**

**Model Answer:**

A **context switch** is the mechanism by which the operating system saves the execution state of a currently running process and loads the state of another process, enabling multiple processes to share a single CPU. The following information is typically saved in the **Process Control Block (PCB)** of the outgoing process:

1. Program Counter (PC) — 0.5 mark
2. CPU Registers (general purpose, accumulator, status flags) — 0.5 mark
3. Stack Pointer (SP) and Base Pointer (BP) — 0.5 mark
4. Memory Management Information (page/segment table base, base/limit registers) — 0.5 mark
5. I/O Status Information (open file descriptors, pending I/O) — 0.5 mark
6. Process Scheduling State (priority, accounting data) — 0.5 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the various steps involved in a **context switch** between two processes. Highlight the role of the **Process Control Block (PCB)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system uses a Round Robin scheduler with a time quantum of **Q = 10 ms**. The average context switch time is **T_cs = 0.2 ms**. Calculate:
1. The percentage of CPU time wasted in context switching.
2. The CPU efficiency when **5** processes each require **T_s = 80 ms** of CPU service.
3. Comment on the trade-off if the quantum is reduced to **Q = 1 ms**.

#### Model Solution to (a) — Context Switch Steps

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The currently running process is interrupted by either a **timer interrupt** (quantum expiry) or a **system call / I/O completion interrupt**.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The CPU switches from **user mode to kernel mode** via the interrupt vector.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** The kernel saves the current process's state — Program Counter, Stack Pointer, General Purpose Registers, and Program Status Word — into the **PCB** of the outgoing process (P1).

**Step 4 (0.5 mark):** The PCB of P1 is moved into the **Ready Queue**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The **scheduler** runs, scans the ready queue using its algorithm (e.g., priority or FCFS), and selects the next process (P2).

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The PCB of P2 is moved from the ready queue, and its state is updated from `Ready` to `Running`.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** The kernel loads P2's saved registers, page table base register, and PC from P2's PCB into the hardware.

The diagram is identical to the one shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes — drawing it explicitly earns the "neat diagram" credit (1 mark extra in Step 1).

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Computation

**Sub-part 1 (2 marks):** Context switch overhead percentage:

$$\text{Overhead \%} = \left( \dfrac{T_{cs}}{Q} \right) \times 100 = \left( \dfrac{0.2}{10} \right) \times 100 = 2\%$$

**Sub-part 2 (3 marks):** CPU efficiency for n = 5, T_s = 80 ms, T_cs = 0.2 ms:

Number of context switches = n − 1 = 4

$$\eta = \dfrac{n \cdot T_{s}}{n \cdot T_{s} + (n - 1) \cdot T_{cs}} = \dfrac{5 \times 80}{5 \times 80 + 4 \times 0.2}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{400}{400 + 0.8} = \dfrac{400}{400.8} \approx 0.9980 = 99.80\%$$

**Sub-part 3 (2 marks):** Trade-off analysis for Q = 1 ms:

$$\text{Overhead \%}_{\text{new}} = \left( \dfrac{0.2}{1} \right) \times 100 = 20\%$$

A 10× reduction in quantum causes a **10× increase** in context switch overhead. While the response time improves (processes get the CPU more frequently), throughput degrades severely. Therefore, **Q must be chosen such that** $Q \gg T_{cs}$ — typically **Q should be at least 10× T_cs** to keep overhead below 10%.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **preemption** in operating systems. Discuss the conditions under which a process can be preempted, and list **two preemptive** and **two non-preemptive** scheduling algorithms.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-core CPU has a context switch time of **T_cs = 5 μs**. A process runs for an average of **T_burst = 100 ms** before blocking. The OS uses Round Robin with a quantum of **Q = 10 ms**. Compute:
1. The number of context switches per process per second.
2. The total context switch time per second per process.
3. The CPU efficiency for the process over one second.

#### Model Solution to (a) — Preemption Concept

**Definition (2 marks):** Preemption is the kernel's ability to **forcibly interrupt** a running process and transfer CPU control to another process, based on scheduler policy. It is the cornerstone of **time-sharing systems**.

**Conditions for Preemption (3 marks):** A process can be preempted when:
1. Its **time quantum expires** (Round Robin).
2. A **higher-priority process** enters the ready queue.
3. A **blocked process** unblocks and the scheduler deems it more eligible.
4. A **hardware interrupt** requires immediate servicing.

**Algorithm Classification (2 marks):**
- **Preemptive:** Round Robin, Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF), Priority (preemptive variant), Multilevel Feedback Queue.
- **Non-Preemptive:** First-Come First-Served (FCFS), Shortest Job First (SJF), Priority (non-preemptive variant), Highest Response Ratio Next (HRRN).

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Computation

**Sub-part 1 (2 marks):** Number of quanta consumed per process:

$$N_{\text{quantums}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{burst}}}{Q} = \dfrac{100\,ms}{10\,ms} = 10 \text{ quanta}$$

Number of context switches per process per burst = 10 (each quantum ends with a switch).

Per second, the process consumes 10 quanta × 10 = 100 ms of CPU, so it will trigger:

$$N_{\text{cs}} = 10 \text{ context switches per burst}$$

For continuous execution over 1 second, the process repeats ~10 bursts of 100 ms, but considering only its own switch count: **10 context switches per second per process**.

**Sub-part 2 (2 marks):**

$$T_{\text{cs total}} = N_{\text{cs}} \times T_{cs} = 10 \times 5\,\mu s = 50\,\mu s = 0.05\,ms \text{ per second}$$

**Sub-part 3 (3 marks):** Useful CPU time per second for the process:

$$T_{\text{useful}} = N_{\text{quantums}} \times Q = 10 \times 10\,ms = 100\,ms$$

CPU efficiency:

$$\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{useful}} + T_{\text{cs total}}} = \dfrac{100}{100 + 0.05} = 0.9995 = 99.95\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing preemption with context switch:** Preemption is a *decision*, context switch is a *mechanism*. Marks lost if used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the (n−1) factor:** Many students compute overhead as `n · T_cs` instead of `(n−1) · T_cs`. The last process does *not* trigger a switch.
> 3. **Ignoring TLB/cache flush:** When listing "what is saved," omitting memory management info costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Mixing μs with ms without conversion (1 ms = 1000 μs) is the most common arithmetic error in numericals.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the formula before substituting:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for the formula and 1 mark for the substitution separately. Skipping the formula = losing a free mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Preemption** = forcibly taking the CPU; **Context Switch** = saving/restoring state to enable that taking.
- A scheduling algorithm is preemptive *if and only if* the OS can revoke the CPU mid-burst.
- **Context Switch sequence (memorize in order):** Save P1 state → Update P1 PCB → P1 to ready queue → Run scheduler → Pick P2 → Update P2 PCB → Load P2 state → Resume.
