| **Implementation Complexity** | Moderate (retry loop) | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Typical Use Case** | Lock-free counters, queues, stacks | Simple spinlock | Long critical sections | Resource pool access |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Lock-Free Counter Increment

**Problem:** Design a thread-safe counter using CAS (no locks).

**Step 1 — Initialize**

$$
\text{counter} = 0 \quad \text{(shared, in main memory)}
$$

**Step 2 — Thread $T_i$ Reads the Current Value**

Each thread performs a non-atomic load:

$$
\text{old}_i = \text{counter}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the New Value Locally**

$$
\text{new}_i = \text{old}_i + 1
$$

> **Note:** The computation above is performed in the thread's **private register** — no shared state is touched.

**Step 4 — Attempt Atomic Commit via CAS**

The thread attempts:

$$
\text{CAS}(\text{counter},\ \text{old}_i,\ \text{new}_i)
$$

**Case 1: Success** (no other thread incremented meanwhile)

$$
\text{counter}: \text{old}_i \rightarrow \text{new}_i = \text{old}_i + 1
$$

The thread is done.

**Case 2: Failure** (another thread incremented concurrently)

The CAS returns a value $\text{old}'_i \neq \text{old}_i$. The thread **restarts** from Step 2 with the updated $\text{old}_i \leftarrow \text{old}'_i$.

**Step 5 — Pseudocode Assembly**

```
do {
    old = atomic_load(counter);
    new = old + 1;
} while (CAS(counter, old, new) != old);  // Retry until success
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical pattern for **optimistic concurrency control**: read optimistically, compute locally, commit conditionally, retry on failure.

## 3.4 The ABA Problem — Derivation and Resolution

**The Scenario:**

A shared pointer `P` points to a node `A` in a lock-free stack. Two threads operate:

- **Thread $T_1$:** Reads `P = A`, prepares to pop `A` by CAS.
- **Thread $T_2$:** Pops `A` (stack now points to `B`), then pushes `A` back (stack now points to `A` again).

**The Race:**

After $T_2$'s pop-push cycle, the pointer value is **bitwise identical** to what $T_1$ originally read (both are the address of `A`). However, the **logical state** of the stack has changed — `A`'s `next` pointer is now different.

**$T_1$'s CAS executes:**

$$
\text{CAS}(P,\ A_{\text{address}},\ B_{\text{address}})
$$

CAS sees the *address* matches and **succeeds incorrectly**, corrupting the stack.

### Resolution Strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Tagged Pointers** | Pack a version counter into unused pointer bits: `$\text{ptr} \vert \text{counter}$` | Limited by pointer alignment bits |
| **Double-Width CAS (DCAS)** | CAS on an adjacent `(ptr, version)` pair: `CMPXCHG16B` (x86) | Requires 128-bit atomicity |
| **Hazard Pointers** | Threads advertise pointers they are using; reclamation defers until safe | Adds runtime overhead |
| **Epoch-Based Reclamation** | Threads declare read-side critical sections with epochs | Simpler but less fine-grained |
| **RCU (Read-Copy-Update)** | Readers are wait-free; updaters defer reclamation | Linux kernel standard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: CAS Operational Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start CAS Operation] --> B[Step 1: Read value at memory_address]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compare loaded value with expected_value]
    C --> D{Values Match?}
    D -- YES --> E[Step 3a: Write new_value to memory_address]
    E --> F[Return new_value as success indicator]
    D -- NO --> G[Step 3b: Leave memory_address unchanged]
    G --> H[Return current actual value as failure indicator]
    F --> I[End]
    H --> I[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart captures the **decision diamond** central to CAS. In hardware, this decision is made by the CPU's **arithmetic logic unit (ALU)** in a single clock cycle, with the memory bus held locked throughout.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Lock Acquisition Using CAS (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THREAD_A["Thread A"]
        T1[Read lock variable]
        T2[Expected = 0]
        T3[CAS lock 0 to 1]
    end
    subgraph SHARED_MEM["Shared Memory - Lock Variable"]
        L0[Initial: Lock = 0]
        L1[After Thread A CAS: Lock = 1]
    end
    subgraph THREAD_B["Thread B"]
        U1[Read lock variable]
        U2[Expected = 0 but actual = 1]
        U3[CAS fails - retry]
    end
    L0 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> L1
    L1 --> U1
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> U3
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: ABA Problem Timeline (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Thread 1
    participant STK as Shared Stack Pointer
    participant T2 as Thread 2
    T1->>STK: Read pointer P = NodeA
    T2->>STK: Pop NodeA - P = NodeB
    T2->>STK: Pop NodeB - P = NodeC
    T2->>STK: Push NodeA back - P = NodeA
    Note over T1,STK: Pointer value unchanged but logical state changed
    T1->>STK: CAS P from NodeA to Next - INCORRECT SUCCESS
    Note over STK: Stack corrupted
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Lock-Free Counter Retry Loop (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LoadValue
    LoadValue: Load current counter value into local variable OLD
    LoadValue --> ComputeNew: OLD loaded successfully
    ComputeNew: Compute NEW = OLD plus 1 in private register
    ComputeNew --> AttemptCAS
    AttemptCAS: Execute CAS counter OLD to NEW
    AttemptCAS --> Success: CAS returned OLD match confirmed
    AttemptCAS --> LoadValue: CAS returned different value - retry
    Success --> [*]: Increment complete
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state diagram emphasizes the **retry loop** that characterizes all CAS-based lock-free algorithms. The thread *spins* (busy-waits) in the retry cycle, but unlike a blocking mutex, it never relinquishes the CPU to the kernel scheduler.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Compare and Swap (CAS) instruction. List its three operands and explain why it is considered atomic.**

**Model Answer:**

> Compare and Swap (CAS) is an atomic hardware instruction used for synchronization in multiprocessor systems. It operates on three operands: a **memory address**, an **expected value**, and a **new value**. The instruction atomically reads the current value at the memory address, compares it with the expected value, and if they match, replaces it with the new value. It is considered atomic because the entire read-compare-write sequence executes as a **single, indivisible CPU instruction**, typically enforced by a hardware bus lock, preventing any other thread from observing or modifying the memory location during the operation.

*(Examiner's note: A diagram earns bonus credit. Mention the return value mechanism.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the ABA problem in the context of Compare and Swap? How is it resolved?**

**Model Answer:**

> The ABA problem occurs when a memory location's value changes from **A to B and back to A** between a thread's initial read and its subsequent CAS. The CAS instruction, which only checks bitwise value equality, **incorrectly succeeds** because the value matches, even though the underlying logical state has changed. This commonly occurs in lock-free data structures such as stacks and queues. It is resolved using techniques such as **tagged pointers** (packing a version counter into unused pointer bits), **double-width CAS** (atomic CAS on a pointer-version pair using instructions like `CMPXCHG16B`), or **safe memory reclamation** schemes such as hazard pointers and epoch-based reclamation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions for this module offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both are provided below with complete model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Compare and Swap (CAS) instruction in detail. Describe its three-phase execution with suitable pseudocode and discuss the hardware mechanisms used to guarantee atomicity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Compare and Swap instruction, design a **lock-free counter increment** algorithm. Explain how it handles concurrent updates and discuss its advantages over a traditional mutex-based counter. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Compare and Swap (CAS)** instruction is an atomic hardware primitive with the signature:

$$
\text{CAS}(A,\ E,\ N)
$$

where $A$ is the memory address, $E$ is the expected value, and $N$ is the new value.

**Three-Phase Execution:**

**Phase 1 — Read:** The hardware reads the current value at address $A$ into an internal register.

**Phase 2 — Compare:** The loaded value is compared with $E$ using a hardware comparator.

**Phase 3 — Conditional Swap:** If and only if the values match, $N$ is written to $A$.

The pseudocode semantics (abstract, not implementation) are:

```
CAS(A, E, N):
    atomically:
        old = *A
        if old == E:
            *A = N
        return old
```

**Hardware Atomicity Mechanisms:**

| Mechanism | Description | Architectures |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Bus Locking** | CPU asserts `LOCK#` signal, blocking other cores from accessing the bus | x86 (`LOCK CMPXCHG`) |
| **Cache Locking** | Locks the cache line containing the address | Modern x86 with cache coherence |
| **LR/SC Pair** | `LL` marks the address; `SC` succeeds only if no other core modified it | MIPS, ARM, RISC-V, PowerPC |
| **Transactional Memory** | Executes optimistically; aborts and retries on conflict | Intel TSX, IBM Power TM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Atomicity** means the entire CAS executes as a single instruction from the perspective of *all* other threads in the system. No partial state is ever visible.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Lock-Free Counter Design Using CAS:**

```
initialize: counter = 0

increment(counter):
    do {
        old = counter;              // Non-atomic load
        new = old + 1;              // Local computation
    } while (CAS(counter, old, new) != old);
```

**Concurrency Handling:**

Multiple threads may simultaneously read the same `old` value. However, **only one thread's CAS will succeed** — the winner updates `counter` from `old` to `new`. All losers receive the updated value as the return and **retry** with the fresh snapshot.

**Advantages Over Mutex-Based Counter:**

| Aspect | CAS Counter | Mutex Counter |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Kernel Transitions** | Zero (user-space spin) | Two (enter/exit kernel) |
| **Deadlock** | Impossible | Possible |
| **Priority Inversion** | None | Common |
| **Scalability** | High (no global lock) | Limited by lock contention |
| **Power Efficiency** | Lower (busy-wait) | Higher (thread parks) |

The CAS-based design is preferred when **contention is low to moderate** and the critical section is **extremely short** (a single variable update).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Compare and Swap (CAS)** instruction with the **Test-and-Set (TAS)** instruction. Show how CAS can be used to implement an **atomic lock** and prove its mutual exclusion property. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ABA problem** in detail. Illustrate it with a lock-free stack example and propose two methods to mitigate it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison: CAS vs. TAS**

| Feature | Compare and Swap | Test and Set |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| **Operands** | 3 (address, expected, new) | 1 (address) |
| **Return Value** | Actual value at address | Old value (0 or 1) |
| **Conditional Write** | Yes — only if match | No — always writes 1 |
| **Expressive Power** | Can implement TAS, fetch-add, etc. | Cannot implement CAS |
| **Starvation** | No (lock-free) | Yes (unfair spin) |
| **ABA Vulnerability** | Yes | No (only 0/1 values) |

**CAS-Based Lock Implementation:**

```
initialize: lock = 0

acquire(lock):
    do {
    } while (CAS(lock, 0, 1) != 0);

release(lock):
    lock = 0;     // Simple store (safe because only the owner writes 1→0)
```

**Proof of Mutual Exclusion (by contradiction):**

Suppose two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ simultaneously hold the lock.

For $T_1$ to hold the lock, its CAS must have succeeded, meaning $L$ transitioned from $0$ to $1$ atomically.

For $T_2$ to *also* hold the lock, its CAS must also have succeeded, meaning it observed $L = 0$ at the instant of comparison.

By the **atomicity of CAS**, the $0 \to 1$ transition happens in a single indivisible step. If $T_1$'s CAS observed $L = 0$ and wrote $L = 1$, then *no other thread can observe $L = 0$ afterward* until $T_1$ releases it. Hence $T_2$'s CAS must observe $L = 1 \neq 0$ and fail. Contradiction. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The ABA Problem:**

The ABA problem arises when a CAS-based algorithm reads a value $A$ from a shared location, but before the CAS executes, **another thread changes the value to $B$ and then back to $A$**. The CAS, comparing only the bit pattern, **incorrectly succeeds**, even though the intermediate state was logically different.

**Lock-Free Stack Illustration:**

Consider a lock-free singly-linked stack with a top pointer `T`.

| Step | Thread 1 | Thread 2 | Stack Top `T` |
|------|----------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Reads `T = ptr(A)`, `A.next = ptr(B)` | — | A → B |
| 2 | — | Pops A: `T = ptr(B)` | B |
| 3 | — | Pops B: `T = NULL` | ∅ |
| 4 | — | Pushes A back: `T = ptr(A)` | A |
| 5 | CAS(T, ptr(A), ptr(B)) — **succeeds** | — | B (incorrect!) |

Thread 1's CAS succeeds because `T` still equals `ptr(A)`, but logically the stack has been completely restructured. The subsequent state is corrupted.

**Mitigation Methods:**

**Method 1 — Tagged Pointers (Version Counters):**

Pack an incrementing version number into the high bits of the pointer (relying on natural alignment). The CAS then operates on the combined `(pointer, version)` pair, so even if the pointer address repeats, the version will differ and the CAS will correctly fail.

```
CAS(&top, (ptr(A), ver=5), (ptr(B), ver=6))
```

**Method 2 — Double-Width CAS (DCAS):**

On 64-bit x86, use the `CMPXCHG16B` instruction to atomically compare and swap a 128-bit quantity comprising the 64-bit pointer and a 64-bit version counter. This provides a true atomic update of the pointer-version pair in a single hardware step.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Treating CAS as a software function:** Students often write CAS as `if-else` pseudocode and forget to emphasize that it is a **hardware atomic instruction**. Marks are deducted for not stating "enforced by hardware atomicity / bus lock / LOCK prefix."
> 2. **Forgetting the return value:** CAS does NOT return a boolean. It returns the **actual value found at the address**. Students who claim it returns `true/false` lose marks.
> 3. **Confusing CAS with TAS:** Several students describe CAS as having a "single operand" — it has **three** operands.
> 4. **Ignoring the ABA problem:** Any CAS answer that does not mention the ABA problem (even briefly) is considered incomplete for a 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Writing code without comments:** KTU expects students to explain *why* each line of pseudocode is necessary, not just list the code.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for CAS (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **CAS = Compare and Swap** — an **atomic hardware instruction** with three operands: `(address, expected, new)`.
- The instruction is **atomic**: the read-compare-write cycle executes as a single, indivisible CPU step.
- **Return value** is the **actual value found at the address**, NOT a boolean. Success is inferred by comparing the return value with `expected`.
- If $*addr = \text{expected}$, CAS writes `new` and returns `new`. If $*addr \neq \text{expected}$, no write occurs and CAS returns the current value.
- CAS is the foundation of **lock-free** and **wait-free** synchronization, avoiding kernel transitions and deadlocks.
- CAS **can implement TAS** (`CAS(addr, 0, 1)` mimics `TAS(addr)`), but **TAS cannot implement CAS** (CAS is more powerful).
- Hardware implementations: x86 `CMPXCHG` (with `LOCK` prefix), ARM `CASA`, RISC-V `LR/SC` pair.
- **Lock-free counter pattern:** spin-retry loop with `do-while CAS(...)`.
- **ABA Problem:** value cycles A→B→A; CAS incorrectly succeeds. Resolved via **version counters, tagged pointers, or DCAS**.
- CAS provides **sequential consistency** for the target address (stronger than relaxed memory ordering).
- **Advantages over mutexes:** no deadlock, no kernel overhead, better scalability on multicore.
- **Disadvantages:** busy-waiting wastes CPU cycles; vulnerable to ABA; complex to implement correctly.
- **Real-world uses:** Java `AtomicInteger`, C++ `std::atomic::compare_exchange`, Linux kernel `cmpxchg`, lock-free queues.
- **KTU expects:** formal definition, three-phase explanation, pseudocode with comments, one real-world example, and at least one comparison table.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Queues: Sleeping Instead of Spinning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using Queues: Sleeping Instead of Spinning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of process/thread synchronization, **"Using Queues: Sleeping Instead of Spinning"** refers to the fundamental operating system mechanism in which a thread that cannot acquire a required resource (such as a lock, a data structure, or an I/O channel) is **removed from the CPU's run queue (descheduled) and placed into a per-resource wait queue**, where it remains in a **blocked (SLEEPING) state** until another thread explicitly wakes it up. The thread consumes **zero CPU cycles** while it sleeps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard OS Terminology):**
> Sleeping (also called **blocking** or **deferred re-scheduling**) is the act of voluntarily relinquishing the CPU by transitioning a thread from the **RUNNING** state to the **BLOCKED/WAITING** state, and inserting its descriptor (PCB/TCB) into a **wait queue** associated with a synchronization primitive. The complementary operation, **wakeup**, transitions the thread back to the **READY** state and re-inserts it into the run queue.

The opposite — **spinning (busy-waiting)** — keeps the thread in the RUNNING state while it repeatedly checks a condition variable, wasting CPU cycles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Shop Queue

Imagine you walk into a small café and the cashier tells you, *"Your table is not ready, please wait."* You have two ways to wait:

| Strategy | Real-World Equivalent | OS Equivalent |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Spinning (Busy-Waiting)** | You stand at the counter, staring at the cashier, asking *"Is it ready? … Is it ready? … Is it ready?"* every second. You block other customers from approaching. | The thread repeatedly executes a tight loop (`while (!ready) ;`), monopolizing the CPU. |
| **Sleeping (Blocking)** | The cashier hands you a **buzzer/pager** and tells you, *"Go sit down; I will buzz you when the table is free."* You rest, check your phone, and only react when the buzzer rings. | The thread calls `sleep()` / `wait()`, moves to a wait queue, and is woken only when another thread calls `wakeup()` / `signal()`. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **buzzer (signal/wakeup)** is the magical hardware/software abstraction that solves the busy-wait problem. Without it, you would have no choice but to keep asking!

## 1.3 Why Queues Are Mandatory

If multiple threads are waiting for the **same resource** (e.g., 5 threads waiting for a single lock), the OS must remember *all* of them. This is why every synchronization primitive maintains a **wait queue (a FIFO list of descriptors)**. The queue ensures:

1. **Fairness** — Threads are woken roughly in the order they fell asleep (FIFO discipline).
2. **No Lost Threads** — Every sleeping thread is accounted for; none are forgotten.
3. **Efficient CPU Usage** — Sleeping threads are not scheduled, freeing the CPU for useful work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The textbook treatment of this topic comes from the classic *Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces* (OSTEP) Chapter on Concurrency, where the descriptor is **def** (a thread/process control structure) and the wait queue is implemented as a linked list of `def` pointers.

## 1.4 The States a Thread Passes Through

A thread transitioning through the sleep-wakeup protocol traverses four states:

1. **RUNNING** → executes the lock-acquisition / condition-check code.
2. **Transition to BLOCKED** → `sleep()` is invoked; descriptor is enqueued onto the wait queue.
3. **BLOCKED** → removed from the run queue; CPU is free for other threads.
4. **READY** → after `wakeup()` is called by another thread; descriptor is enqueued onto the run queue, awaiting the CPU.

The **state-transition diagram** is rendered in Section 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Sleep and Wakeup

The mechanism relies on two complementary kernel primitives:

### 2.1.1 The `sleep()` Primitive (Block)
When a thread determines that it cannot make progress (e.g., the lock it needs is held, or the buffer it must read from is empty), it:

1. **Acquires the OS's internal queue lock** to protect the wait queue.
2. **Inserts its own `def` (descriptor) onto the tail** of the per-resource wait queue.
3. **Sets its state to SLEEPING / BLOCKED**.
4. **Calls the scheduler's `switch()` function**, which performs a **context switch** to another runnable thread.
5. The current thread's execution is **suspended** — it no longer consumes CPU.
6. **Releases the user-level lock** it was holding (in many designs, the lock is released *atomically* with the sleep — this avoids deadlock).

### 2.1.2 The `wakeup()` Primitive (Unblock)
When a thread that holds a resource is ready to relinquish it, it:

1. **Acquires the OS's internal queue lock** to protect the wait queue.
2. **Removes a `def` from the head** of the wait queue (or wakes all waiters, depending on semantics).
3. **Changes that thread's state from SLEEPING to READY**.
4. **Re-inserts the descriptor** onto the run queue.
5. The awakened thread is **not** immediately run — it must wait for the scheduler to select it.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Sleeping — Engineering Trade-Offs

| Criterion | Spinning (Busy-Wait) | Sleeping (Blocking) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **CPU usage while waiting** | Wasted (100% on one core) | Near 0% |
| **Energy consumption** | High | Low |
| **Wakeup latency** | Very low (immediate check) | Higher (one context switch ≈ 1–10 µs) |
| **Throughput on multi-core** | OK if critical section is short | Better if critical section is long |
| **Implementation complexity** | Trivial (a `while` loop) | Requires wait queues, atomics, race-condition care |
| **Risk** | Starvation of co-runners | Lost-wakeup bug (see §3) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - Use **spinlocks** when the critical section is *shorter than* a context switch (typically < 1 µs).
> - Use **blocking (sleeping) locks** when the critical section is *longer than* a context switch.

## 2.3 The Producer–Consumer Context (Canonical Example)

In a bounded buffer of size `N`, the canonical use of sleep/wakeup is:

- **Consumer** behavior: If buffer is empty → `sleep(&empty)`; otherwise consume; if buffer was full before this consume → `wakeup(&full)`.
- **Producer** behavior: If buffer is full → `sleep(&full)`; otherwise produce; if buffer was empty before this produce → `wakeup(&empty)`.

Two wait queues are used: `full` and `empty`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations govern the *efficiency* and *correctness* considerations of the sleep-vs-spin design. These are the high-yield equations for KTU numerical/analytical questions.

> [!NOTE]
> Throughout the table below, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ is rendered as `\vert` so that markdown table syntax is never broken.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $U_{\text{eff}} = 1 - P_{\text{busy-wait}}$ | Effective CPU utilization = fraction of time not spent spinning | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $T_{\text{context-switch}} = T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{scheduler}}$ | Cost of one context switch between threads | µs (1–10 µs typical) |
| 3 | $T_{\text{turnaround}} = T_{\text{arrival}} + T_{\text{wait-queue}} + T_{\text{execution}}$ | Total time a thread spends in the system | ms |
| 4 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{\text{completed}}}{T_{\text{wall-clock}}}$ | Jobs completed per second | jobs/s |
| 5 | $P_{\text{spin-better}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{cs}}}{T_{\text{ctx-sw}}}$ | Spin wins when $P_{\text{spin-better}} < 1$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{\text{wakeup-latency}} = T_{\text{ctx-sw}} + T_{\text{ready-queue-residence}}$ | Delay between `wakeup()` and actual re-execution | µs |
| 7 | $\text{Queue size}_{\text{max}} = N_{\text{cores}} \cdot \lceil \tfrac{T_{\text{burst}}}{T_{\text{quantum}}} \rceil$ | Worst-case wait-queue length (Little's Law applied) | threads |
| 8 | $T_{\text{avg-wait}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{sync}}}{1 - \rho}$ | Average synchronization wait time (M/M/1-inspired) where $\rho$ is utilization | s |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux kernel** uses `wait_event()` / `wake_up()` for blocking I/O and the futex subsystem for hybrid fast-path spin + slow-path sleep.
- **Java** uses `Object.wait()` and `Object.notify()`.
- **POSIX threads (pthreads)** use `pthread_cond_wait()` and `pthread_cond_signal()`.
- **Database engines** (e.g., InnoDB) use sleep/wakeup for row-level lock waits.
- **Embedded / RTOS** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) use `xQueueReceive()` with block-time instead of spin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The technique you learn here is the conceptual ancestor of **all modern condition variables and semaphore implementations**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Naïve Sleep/Wakeup Protocol (Pseudocode)

The simplest version, used as a teaching baseline in KTU textbooks, is:

```
// consumer
int consume(void) {
    if (count == 0)        // check if buffer empty
        sleep(&empty);     // go to sleep on "empty" wait-queue
    int item = remove_item();
    count = count - 1;
    if (count == N-1)      // if we just freed a slot
        wakeup(&full);     // wake a sleeping producer
    return item;
}

// producer
void produce(int item) {
    if (count == N)        // check if buffer full
        sleep(&full);      // go to sleep on "full" wait-queue
    insert_item(item);
    count = count + 1;
    if (count == 1)        // if we just added the first item
        wakeup(&empty);    // wake a sleeping consumer
}
```

## 3.2 The Lost-Wakeup Problem (The Famous Race Condition)

### 3.2.1 Statement of the Bug

> [!WARNING]
> The naïve code above has a **race condition** that causes **lost wakeups** — wakeup signals that are sent *before* a thread has gone to sleep, and are therefore never received. This is one of the most heavily tested KTU topics.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Race Walk-Through

Assume **one producer thread P** and **one consumer thread C**, and a buffer of size $N = 1$. Initial state: $count = 0$.

**Time Step 1 — Interleaving 1:**

| Step | P executes | C executes | State |
|------|------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | `if (count == N)` → `0 == 1`? No | — | count=0, P proceeds |
| 2 | (about to call `insert_item`) | `if (count == 0)` → `0 == 0`? Yes | count=0, C enters sleep path |
| 3 | (preempted) | **interrupted before `sleep()`** | — |
| 4 | `insert_item(item)` | — | count=1 |
| 5 | `count = count + 1` | — | count=1 |
| 6 | `if (count == 1)` → Yes | — | — |
| 7 | `wakeup(&empty)` | — | **Wakeup fired, but C is NOT yet on the wait queue!** |
| 8 | — | `sleep(&empty)` | **C goes to sleep FOREVER — wakeup was lost** |

### 3.2.3 The Heart of the Problem — Derivation of the Race Window

The window of vulnerability is the interval between the **condition check** (`if (count == 0)`) and the **actual sleep** (`sleep(&empty)`). During this window, the thread is *committed to sleeping* but is not yet *on the wait queue*.

Mathematically, the probability of the lost-wakeup bug increases as:

$$P_{\text{lost-wakeup}} \;\propto\; \frac{T_{\text{check-to-sleep}}}{T_{\text{quantum}}}$$

where $T_{\text{check-to-sleep}}$ is the time elapsed between the condition test and the completion of the `sleep()` call, and $T_{\text{quantum}}$ is the OS scheduling quantum. As multi-core systems and faster CPUs shrink the relative window, the bug becomes both rarer and harder to reproduce — but never zero.

## 3.3 The Canonical Solution — Atomic Sleep & Wakeup

### 3.3.1 Solution 1: Inverted Check Order + Atomic Sleep

The classic OSTEP solution: **swap the order** so that the wakeup test happens *after* the thread is on the wait queue, and the wait/sleep is made atomic with respect to wakeup.

```
// consumer
int consume(void) {
    lock(&mutex);
    while (count == 0)               // LOOP, not if — see §3.4
        cond_wait(&empty, &mutex);   // atomic: release mutex + add to wait-queue + sleep
    int item = remove_item();
    count = count - 1;
    cond_signal(&full);
    unlock(&mutex);
    return item;
}
```

`cond_wait()` performs three steps **atomically** (with respect to other threads):
1. Release the user-level mutex.
2. Insert the thread onto the wait queue.
3. Deschedule (sleep).

`cond_signal()` performs two steps atomically:
1. Remove a thread from the wait queue.
2. Wake it up (move to ready queue).

### 3.3.2 Solution 2: Memory Barriers & Per-Thread Flags

Used in low-level kernels (e.g., Linux `wait_queue`):

```
// consumer
int my_flag = 0;
sleep_on(&q);          // check flag under barrier-protected region
// inside sleep_on: memory barrier ensures flag read after queue insertion
```

A **memory barrier (fence)** is a hardware instruction (`mfence` on x86, `dmb` on ARM) that prevents the CPU and compiler from reordering the queue-insertion before the condition-check.

## 3.4 The Mesa vs. Hoare Semantics Distinction (Analytical Table)

| Property | Hoare Semantics | Mesa Semantics (used in Java, Linux, pthreads) |
|----------|----------------|------------------------------------------------|
| After `signal()`, who runs? | Signaler suspends; waiter runs immediately | Signaler continues; waiter re-enters ready queue |
| Spurious wakeups possible? | No | **Yes** — waiters must re-check condition in a `while` loop |
| Re-check idiom | `if (cond)` | `while (cond)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The reason we use `while` (not `if`) around the wait is precisely to defend against Mesa-semantics spurious wakeups and the re-acquisition race on the lock.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree

```
                        Is critical section < 1 µs?
                                  │
                ┌─────────────────┴─────────────────┐
               YES                                   NO
                │                                     │
        Use SPINLOCK                          Use MUTEX (sleep)
        (e.g., test-and-set,                   (cond_wait / wakeup)
         compare-and-swap)                            │
                                                      │
                                          Is the wait bounded?
                                                  │
                                  ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
                                 YES                              NO
                                  │                                │
                          Simple semaphore              Need priority inheritance
                          or cond_var                       (priority inversion
                                                           problem — see Mars
                                                           Pathfinder bug)
```

## 3.6 Derivation of the "Better to Sleep" Threshold

The crossover point where sleeping becomes more efficient than spinning is found by equating their CPU costs.

- Cost of **spinning** for a wait of $T_w$ seconds: $C_{\text{spin}} = T_w$ (one CPU fully busy).
- Cost of **sleeping** for the same wait: $C_{\text{sleep}} = 2 \cdot T_{\text{ctx-sw}}$ (context-switch out + context-switch in).

Sleeping wins when:

$$C_{\text{sleep}} < C_{\text{spin}}$$

$$2 \cdot T_{\text{ctx-sw}} < T_w$$

$$T_w > 2 \cdot T_{\text{ctx-sw}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Hook:** If $T_{\text{ctx-sw}} = 5 \, \mu s$, then any wait longer than **10 µs** should use the sleep mechanism, not spinning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Sleep and Wakeup Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Thread RUNNING]) --> B{Condition satisfied?}
    B -- YES --> C[Acquire resource]
    C --> D[Do work]
    D --> E[Release resource]
    E --> F[Set state to READY for waiters]
    F --> G([Thread RUNNING again])
    B -- NO --> H[Lock wait-queue mutex]
    H --> I[Insert self into wait queue]
    I --> J[Set state to BLOCKED]
    J --> K[Call scheduler switch]
    K --> L([Thread SLEEPING - 0% CPU])
    L -- wakeup signal received --> M[Set state to READY]
    M --> N[Insert into run queue]
    N -- scheduler picks --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Producer/Consumer Race

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Producer
    participant Q as Wait Queue empty
    participant C as Consumer
    participant R as Run Queue
    Note over C: Step 1: count = 0
    C->>C: Check count == 0 -> TRUE
    Note over C: Step 2: about to call sleep
    P-->>P: insert_item and increment count to 1
    P->>Q: wakeup(empty)  -- LOST WAKEUP!
    Note over Q: Wakeup fires but C not yet enqueued
    C->>Q: sleep(empty)  -- C sleeps forever
    Note over C: BUG: Lost wakeup
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — OS Wait Queue Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[CPU Core]
        CORE[Core 0]
    end
    subgraph RQ[Run Queue]
        R1[Thread T1]
        R2[Thread T2]
        R3[Thread T3]
    end
    subgraph WQ[Wait Queue for Lock L]
        W1[Thread T4]
        W2[Thread T5]
        W3[Thread T6]
    end
    subgraph BLOCKED[SLEEPING Threads]
        B1[Thread T7]
        B2[Thread T8]
    end
    CORE -.schedules.-> RQ
    RQ -- lock unavailable --> WQ
    WQ -- I O or event not ready --> BLOCKED
    BLOCKED -- wakeup called --> WQ
    WQ -- lock released --> RQ
    RQ -.context switch.-> CORE
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Machine — Thread Lifecycle with Sleep

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> READY
    READY --> RUNNING: scheduler dispatch
    RUNNING --> READY: quantum expired
    RUNNING --> BLOCKED: sleep or wait
    BLOCKED --> READY: wakeup or signal
    RUNNING --> [*]: exit
    READY --> [*]: exit
    note right of BLOCKED
        Thread is in wait queue.
        Uses 0 percent CPU.
        Awaiting wakeup event.
    end note
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use purely alphanumeric node identifiers (e.g., `RQ`, `WQ`, `P`, `C`, `B1`) and contain no markdown formatting inside their labels, in compliance with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between busy-waiting (spinning) and blocking (sleeping) in the context of process synchronization. State one scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Spinning (Busy-Wait) | Sleeping (Blocking) |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| CPU consumption while waiting | **100% on one core** | **0%** |
| Mechanism | Tight loop checking a condition | `sleep()` → wait queue → `wakeup()` |
| Wakeup latency | Immediate (next loop iteration) | Delayed (one context switch) |
| Energy | High | Low |
| Preferred scenario | Lock held for < 1 µs, e.g., **spinlocks in kernel** | I/O or lock held for > 10 µs, e.g., **producer-consumer buffer** |

(1 mark per row, plus 1 mark for one preferred-scenario example.) **Total: 3 marks.**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the "lost-wakeup" problem in the sleep/wakeup protocol? How does the use of condition variables solve it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The lost-wakeup problem is a race condition in which a producer thread calls `wakeup()` *before* the consumer thread has executed `sleep()` and joined the wait queue. The signal is therefore sent into an empty queue and is never received, causing the consumer to sleep forever.
- **Cause (1 mark):** The window between *checking* the condition and *actually sleeping* is non-atomic.
- **Solution via condition variables (1 mark):** `cond_wait()` is an **atomic** primitive that simultaneously (a) releases the mutex, (b) inserts the thread onto the wait queue, and (c) deschedules the thread. Because the queue insertion and the state change to BLOCKED happen as one indivisible step, any concurrent `signal()` will either see the thread in the queue (and wake it) or will be queued for the next wait. A memory barrier (fence) and a `while` loop re-check defend against the re-acquisition race.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the sleep and wakeup mechanism used in inter-process communication. Using a producer-consumer problem with a shared buffer of size $N$, show how the naïve sleep/wakeup code leads to the lost-wakeup race condition. Provide an interleaving trace.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Describe how condition variables solve the lost-wakeup problem. Compare Hoare and Mesa semantics, and justify why a `while` loop (not an `if`) is used to re-check the condition after `cond_wait()` returns.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A, Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Mechanism explanation (2 marks):**

The sleep/wakeup mechanism is a synchronization primitive in which a thread that cannot proceed (because a condition is unmet) is **removed from the run queue** and placed onto a **per-condition wait queue**. The thread enters the **BLOCKED** state and **stops consuming CPU**. Another thread, upon making the condition true, calls `wakeup()`, which moves one or more sleeping threads from the wait queue back to the run queue and changes their state to **READY**. The awakened thread is later dispatched by the scheduler.

**Step 2 — Naïve code (1 mark):**

```c
// Consumer
int consume() {
    if (count == 0) sleep(&empty);
    int item = remove_item(); count--;
    if (count == N-1) wakeup(&full);
    return item;
}
// Producer
void produce(int item) {
    if (count == N) sleep(&full);
    insert_item(item); count++;
    if (count == 1) wakeup(&empty);
}
```

**Step 3 — Interleaving trace (3 marks):**

Let $N = 1$, initial state `count = 0`, with Producer P and Consumer C.

| # | Thread | Statement | count | C state | Wait queue `empty` |
|---|--------|-----------|-------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | C | `if (count == 0)` → TRUE | 0 | about to sleep | empty |
| 2 | (preempted before `sleep`) | — | 0 | in transition | empty |
| 3 | P | `if (count == N)` → FALSE | 0 | — | empty |
| 4 | P | `insert_item(...)` | 1 | — | empty |
| 5 | P | `count++` | 1 | — | empty |
| 6 | P | `if (count == 1)` → TRUE | 1 | — | empty |
| 7 | P | `wakeup(&empty)` | 1 | — | **wakeup fired into empty queue** |
| 8 | C | `sleep(&empty)` | 1 | **BLOCKED** | C now enqueued |
| — | — | P exits | 1 | C sleeps forever | C still sleeping |

**Result (1 mark):** The wakeup at step 7 was lost because C was not yet on the queue. C will sleep until the next unrelated `wakeup(&empty)` — or forever if none occurs. This is the **lost-wakeup race condition**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A, Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Condition variable solution (2 marks):**

```c
// Consumer
lock(&mutex);
while (count == 0) cond_wait(&empty, &mutex);
int item = remove_item(); count--;
cond_signal(&full);
unlock(&mutex);

// Producer
lock(&mutex);
while (count == N) cond_wait(&full, &mutex);
insert_item(item); count++;
cond_signal(&empty);
unlock(&mutex);
```

`cond_wait()` performs **atomically**: (i) release the mutex, (ii) add the calling thread to the wait queue, (iii) deschedule. `cond_signal()` removes one waiter and re-awakens it.

**Step 2 — Why the race is closed (2 marks):**

Because the queue insertion in step (ii) of `cond_wait()` happens *before* the thread releases the CPU in step (iii), any concurrent `cond_signal()` is guaranteed to either:
- Find the thread on the queue (and wake it), or
- Be delivered to a future wait (since the signal is held in a flag or remembered state, depending on the implementation). In a properly designed condition variable, a signal sent with no waiters is **not lost** — it is stored or simply re-evaluated on the next `while` iteration by the next arrival.

**Step 3 — Hoare vs. Mesa semantics (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Hoare | Mesa |
|--------|-------|------|
| After signal, who runs? | Waiter runs **immediately**; signaler is suspended | Signaler continues; waiter moves to ready queue |
| Spurious wakeups | Impossible | **Possible** (due to cancellation, timeout, multi-cpu races) |
| Re-check needed? | `if` is sufficient | **`while` is required** |

**Step 4 — Why `while` and not `if` (1 mark):**

Because Mesa semantics permits the signaled thread to be re-preempted before it actually runs, and because a spurious wakeup may fire even when the condition is false, the awakened thread **must re-verify the condition** under the held mutex. Using `if` would allow the thread to proceed even when the buffer is empty (or full), re-introducing the original race.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a state-transition diagram, explain how a thread moves from RUNNING to BLOCKED state during a `sleep()` call and returns to READY state on a `wakeup()` call. Mention the role of the wait queue.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Discuss the trade-offs between spinlocks and blocking mutexes. Derive the threshold wait-time beyond which sleeping strictly dominates spinning in CPU-cycle cost.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B, Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to §4.4 Mermaid State Machine in Section 4.)

The thread lifecycle is **NEW → READY → RUNNING → BLOCKED → READY → RUNNING → EXIT**.

**Step 2 — `sleep()` transition (2 marks):**

When a thread executes `sleep(condition)`:
1. The OS acquires the internal wait-queue spinlock to make the operation atomic.
2. The thread's `def` (descriptor) is **appended to the tail** of the per-condition wait queue.
3. The thread's state is set to `BLOCKED`.
4. The scheduler is invoked; a context switch removes the thread from the CPU.
5. The wait-queue lock is released.

**Step 3 — `wakeup()` transition (2 marks):**

When another thread calls `wakeup(condition)`:
1. The OS acquires the wait-queue lock.
2. A `def` is **removed from the head** of the wait queue (FIFO fairness).
3. The removed thread's state is set to `READY`.
4. The thread is **appended to the run queue** of its CPU.
5. The wait-queue lock is released; the scheduler is invoked on the next scheduling decision.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B, Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Trade-off table (3 marks):**

| Property | Spinlock | Blocking Mutex |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| CPU waste while waiting | Yes (100% on one core) | No (0%) |
| Context switch cost | None | Two (block + wake) |
| Best for | Very short critical sections | Long waits, I/O, kernel blocking |
| Implementation | Test-and-set / CAS | Wait queue + scheduler |
| Risk | Starvation, priority inversion | Lost-wakeup if not careful |

**Step 2 — Cost-equality derivation (3 marks):**

Let $T_w$ be the wait time (seconds) the thread must wait for the resource.

- **Spin cost:** $C_{\text{spin}} = T_w$ CPU-seconds wasted on the spinning core.
- **Sleep cost:** $C_{\text{sleep}} = 2 \cdot T_{\text{ctx-sw}}$ (one switch out, one switch in).

Setting $C_{\text{sleep}} = C_{\text{spin}}$:

$$2 \cdot T_{\text{ctx-sw}} = T_w^*$$

$$T_w^* = 2 \cdot T_{\text{ctx-sw}}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

If $T_w > 2 T_{\text{ctx-sw}}$, sleeping strictly dominates spinning. For $T_{\text{ctx-sw}} = 5 \, \mu s$, the threshold is $T_w^* = 10 \, \mu s$. This is the "better-to-sleep" threshold, used in hybrid futex designs in Linux.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using `if` instead of `while`** around `cond_wait` — loses 1–2 marks even if the rest is correct. This is the *single most common mistake* in KTU answers.
> 2. **Forgetting to release the user-level mutex atomically with the sleep** — causes deadlock. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing "spurious wakeup" with "lost wakeup"** — they are different. Spurious wakeup = wakeup with no apparent cause (defensive loop helps). Lost wakeup = wakeup with no receiver (atomic wait+queue insert fixes it).
> 4. **Not mentioning the memory barrier / atomicity** — modern examiners expect you to say *how* the race is closed, not just *that* it is closed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Night Before the Exam:**

- **Sleeping ≠ Spinning:** Sleeping = BLOCKED state, 0% CPU, uses wait queues. Spinning = RUNNING state, wastes CPU in a loop.
- **Two primitives only:** `sleep(condition)` and `wakeup(condition)`. The condition is the address of a wait queue.
- **Wait queue is mandatory:** It stores the descriptors of all sleeping threads awaiting the same event.
- **Lost-wakeup bug** = the textbook race; caused by non-atomicity between the *condition check* and the *sleep call*.
- **Solution = atomicity:** Use `cond_wait()` (which fuses mutex-release + queue-insert + sleep) or memory barriers.
- **Always use `while`, not `if`:** Defends against Mesa semantics, spurious wakeups, and the re-acquisition race.
- **Better-to-sleep threshold:** $T_w > 2 \cdot T_{\text{ctx-sw}}$.
- **Real-world APIs:** POSIX `pthread_cond_wait/signal`, Java `Object.wait/notify`, Linux kernel `wait_event/wake_up`.
- **Spinlock use case:** Critical sections under ~1 µs; especially in OS kernels and on multi-core systems.
- **Mutex use case:** Anything longer than a few µs, I/O waits, inter-process coordination.
- **Spurious wakeup vs. lost wakeup:** Spurious = defensive `while` loop; lost = atomic `cond_wait`.
- **State transitions:** NEW → READY → RUNNING → BLOCKED → READY → RUNNING → EXIT. (Refer to the Mermaid state machine in §4.4.)
- **The Producer/Consumer model** is the canonical illustration — expect it on the exam.

**End of Module 2 — Topic: Using Queues: Sleeping Instead of Spinning.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semaphores - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semaphores - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — SEMAPHORES: DEFINITION

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semaphore (Dijkstra, 1965):** A *semaphore* **S** is a non-negative integer variable that is accessed **only through two standard atomic (indivisible) operations**, traditionally named **P (Proberen / Wait / Test)** and **V (Verhogen / Signal / Increment)**. The integer value of the semaphore represents the number of available resources, and the atomicity of the P and V operations is the *fundamental guarantee* that makes semaphores a valid process-synchronization primitive.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (Outcome-Based Education under NEP 2020), the definition must be stated in three parts:

| Component | Formal Statement |
|---|---|
| **Data Part** | An integer variable `S` initialized to a non-negative value |
| **Wait Operation (P)** | Atomic block — decrements S; if resulting S < 0, the calling process is blocked |
| **Signal Operation (V)** | Atomic block — increments S; if resulting S ≤ 0, one blocked process is awakened |

The atomicity guarantee ensures that once a process starts a P or V operation, no other process can access the semaphore until the operation is fully completed. This is the cornerstone of semaphores for **mutual exclusion** and **process synchronization**.

## 1.2 The Two Fundamental Types of Semaphores

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 1 — Binary Semaphore (also called Mutex Lock):** Its value can only be **0 or 1**. Used for solving the *critical section problem* (mutual exclusion between processes). Init: `S = 1`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type 2 — Counting Semaphore:** Its value can range over an **unrestricted non-negative integer domain** (0, 1, 2, …, N). Used to control access to a finite pool of identical resources (e.g., N buffers, N database connections, N printer slots). Init: `S = N`.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### Analogy A: The Single Restroom Key
Imagine a public restroom with a single key hanging on the door:

- The **integer value of the semaphore** = the number of keys currently available at the reception desk.
- **P (Wait)** operation: A person goes to the desk, *atomically* takes a key. If a key exists, the count drops by 1 and they enter. If **no key exists** (count = 0), they must **wait in a queue** outside until someone returns one.
- **V (Signal)** operation: A person finishes, returns the key to the desk, the count rises by 1, and the next person in the waiting queue is *handed the key and allowed in*.

The "atomicity" here is enforced by the **single receptionist** — only one person interacts with the key pile at a time, exactly like the OS kernel disabling interrupts or using a hardware lock around the P/V routines.

### Analogy B: The Toll Plaza with N Booths
A toll plaza with `N = 4` open booths:
- Semaphore `S = 4` (four available booths).
- A car arriving performs **P(S)** — it waits (joins queue) if all 4 booths are occupied.
- A car leaving performs **V(S)** — it frees a booth and the first car in the queue proceeds.
- This is a perfect **counting semaphore** example with bounded resources.

## 1.4 Why Semaphores are Needed in KTU Scope

Without semaphores:
- Race conditions corrupt shared data
- Critical sections overlap, producing non-deterministic output
- Processes need to *co-operate* (e.g., producer/consumer, readers/writers) but no safe signaling mechanism exists

With semaphores:
- A small, well-defined integer **replaces** complex busy-wait loops
- The OS kernel handles the **blocked queue** automatically
- The programmer writes *only the synchronization intent* — not the low-level hardware interlocks
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Two Atomic Primitives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complete formal definition of a semaphore includes the **state of the semaphore** AND the **state of the blocked-process queue** attached to it. The full data structure is therefore a **tuple** (S, Q), where `Q` is the FIFO queue of sleeping processes.

### 2.1.1 The **P (Wait / Proberen / Test)** Operation

The P-operation, when invoked on semaphore `S`, performs the following *atomic* sequence:

- **Step 1 (Read):** Inspect the current value of `S`.
- **Step 2 (Decrement):** Compute `S ← S − 1`. This read-modify-write is performed as a single uninterruptible step.
- **Step 3 (Decide):**
  - If the new value `S ≥ 0` ⟹ the requesting process **proceeds** and enters the critical section.
  - If the new value `S < 0` ⟹ the process is **blocked (suspended)** and appended to the queue `Q` associated with `S`.

The classical pseudocode form of `wait(S)` is:

```
wait(S):
    S = S - 1
    if (S < 0):
        block(this process)   // add to Q and sleep
```

### 2.1.2 The **V (Signal / Verhogen / Increment)** Operation

The V-operation, when invoked on semaphore `S`, performs the following *atomic* sequence:

- **Step 1 (Increment):** Compute `S ← S + 1`. This read-modify-write is performed as a single uninterruptible step.
- **Step 2 (Decide):**
  - If the new value `S > 0` ⟹ no blocked process existed; simply continue.
  - If the new value `S ≤ 0` ⟹ at least one process is blocked in queue `Q`; **wake up (unblock)** exactly one process from `Q` and place it on the ready queue.

The classical pseudocode form of `signal(S)` is:

```
signal(S):
    S = S + 1
    if (S <= 0):
        wakeup(one process from Q)
```

## 2.2 Why the Decrement/Increment Order is Crucial (The 'How')

The order **decrement-first-then-test** in P (and **increment-first-then-test** in V) is what gives the semaphore its blocking semantics:

- If we tested *first* and decremented *later*, two processes could both see `S = 1`, both decide to proceed, and then both decrement to `0` — destroying mutual exclusion.
- By **decrementing atomically with the test**, only one process can ever see the value transition from `1 → 0`; the second one sees `0 → −1` and is forced to block.

## 2.3 Binary vs Counting — The Formal Distinction

| Property | Binary Semaphore (Mutex) | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| **Range of S** | {0, 1} only | {0, 1, 2, …, N} |
| **Initial Value** | `S = 1` (one resource) | `S = N` (N resources) |
| **Primary Use** | Mutual exclusion (only one process in critical section) | Bounded resource pool access |
| **Implementation** | Atomic compare-and-swap (CAS) or interrupt-disable | Built on top of binary semaphore + shared integer counter |
| **Can implement the other?** | A counting semaphore can be implemented using **N** binary semaphores; a binary semaphore is a counting semaphore with `N = 1` | |
| **Wake-up policy** | Strong semaphore: FIFO wake-up; Weak semaphore: undefined wake-up order | Same distinction applies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong Semaphore** — Uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue** for blocked processes ⟹ guarantees freedom from **starvation**.
> **Weak Semaphore** — No defined wake-up order ⟹ may suffer from starvation under certain scheduling policies.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Condition | Meaning | KTU Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Semaphore value after `k` successful P-ops | $S_{final} = S_{initial} - k$ | If $S_{final} \ge 0$: all `k` proceeded | 2–3 |
| 2 | Number of processes currently inside critical section | $\vert CS \vert = S_{initial} - S_{current}$ | For binary mutex with $S_{init} = 1$ | 3 |
| 3 | Condition for blocking in P | $S - 1 < 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; S \le 0 \text{ before the P}$ | Process is forced to sleep | 2 |
| 4 | Condition for wake-up in V | $S + 1 \le 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; S \le -1 \text{ before the V}$ | A process was blocked; wake one | 2 |
| 5 | Mutual exclusion invariant | At any instant, $\vert CS \vert \le 1$ when $S_{init} = 1$ | Guaranteed by atomicity | 3 |
| 6 | Counting semaphore for N identical resources | $S_{init} = N$ | N processes can proceed concurrently | 2 |
| 7 | Producer–Consumer invariant | $0 \le \text{items in buffer} \le N$ | Enforced by two semaphores | 5–7 |
| 8 | Dining philosophers — deadlock-free condition | Resource ordering: acquire lower-numbered fork first | Breaks circular wait | 7 |
| 9 | Atomicity enforcement | Either disable interrupts (uniprocessor) or use `test_and_set`/`compare_and_swap` (multiprocessor) | Guarantees indivisibility of P/V | 3 |
| 10 | Starvation-freedom | Use **strong** semaphores (FIFO queue) | No process waits forever | 2 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Semaphores

- **Operating system kernels** — Used internally to protect the ready queue, the file-system buffer cache, and the device-driver tables (Linux kernel uses `struct semaphore` historically; now `rwsem` and `mutex`).
- **Database engines** — Counting semaphores limit concurrent connections to a connection pool (e.g., 100 DB connections out of 1000 sessions).
- **Embedded & RTOS systems** — FreeRTOS, VxWorks use binary and counting semaphores for task synchronization and ISR-to-task signaling.
- **Distributed systems** — Generalized to **distributed semaphores** using consensus protocols (e.g., Redis Redlock, ZooKeeper recipes) for cross-host resource coordination.
- **Multimedia & networking** — Bounded buffer semaphores regulate the producer (packet-capture thread) → consumer (protocol-stack thread) pipeline.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proof of Mutual Exclusion Using a Binary Semaphore

**Given:**
- A binary semaphore `mutex` initialized to `mutex = 1`.
- Two processes `P₁` and `P₂` that both want to enter the same critical section.

**Claim:** At any instant of time, **at most one** of `P₁` and `P₂` is executing the critical section.

**Proof by contradiction (atomicity of P and V):**

Consider the four-execution sequence below for the two processes around the critical section:

```
Process P₁            Process P₂            mutex value
-----------           -----------           -----------
...                                    Initial:  mutex = 1
wait(mutex)  [S=0]                       mutex = 0
                       wait(mutex)       mutex = -1
                                            P₂ is blocked (queue)
   < P₁ in critical section >
   < P₂ sleeping in queue Q >
...                                    mutex = -1
signal(mutex) [S=0, wake P₂]             mutex = 0
                                            P₁ leaves, P₂ woken
                       < P₂ in critical section >
signal(mutex)                            mutex = 1
```

Formal mathematical argument:

**Step 1 — Assume** both `P₁` and `P₂` are inside the critical section simultaneously.

**Step 2 — To enter**, both must have successfully completed `wait(mutex)`, which means both must have decremented `mutex` atomically.

**Step 3 — Since `mutex` started at 1**, the maximum number of *successful* (non-blocking) decrements before `mutex` becomes `−1` is **exactly one** (the first one takes `1 → 0`, allowed; the second one takes `0 → −1`, *blocked*).

**Step 4 — Contradiction:** Two processes cannot both have decremented past `0` without one of them being blocked. Therefore our assumption is false.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Mutual exclusion holds. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Semantics of the Negative Counter

**Given:** After `k` P-operations and `j` V-operations on a semaphore `S` initialized to `N`.

**Step 1 — Net change:**

$$
S_{final} = S_{initial} - k + j
$$

**Step 2 — Number of currently-blocked processes:**

$$
\text{blocked} = \max(0,\; -S_{final}) = \max(0,\; k - j - N)
$$

**Step 3 — Number of processes currently inside the critical section (for mutex, `N = 1`):**

$$
\vert CS \vert = S_{init} - S_{final} = 1 - S_{final} = 1 - (1 - k + j) = k - j
$$

This explains the elegant interpretation: a negative value of `S` is *literally* the number of processes sleeping on the semaphore.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Operation on S and on Q

| Operation | Pre-condition | Action on `S` | Action on Queue `Q` | Resulting State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `wait(S)` when `S > 0` | At least one resource free | `S ← S − 1` (new S ≥ 0) | No change | Process **proceeds**; resources available = `S` |
| `wait(S)` when `S = 0` | No resource free | `S ← S − 1` (new S = −1) | Process **appended** to `Q` | Process **sleeps** |
| `wait(S)` when `S < 0` | `|S|` processes already blocked | `S ← S − 1` (more negative) | Process **appended** to `Q` | Process **sleeps** |
| `signal(S)` when `S ≥ 0` (no one blocked) | All previously decrements were "successful" | `S ← S + 1` (new S ≥ 0) | No change | One more resource now available |
| `signal(S)` when `S < 0` | At least one process blocked in `Q` | `S ← S + 1` (still ≤ 0) | **One** process removed and woken | That process re-runs `wait(S)` again? **No** — it proceeds directly to critical section |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the woken-up process does NOT re-execute wait(S):** The convention is that the increment in V *compensates* for the blocking decrement in P. The wake-up "transfers" the resource from the count to the process. This is called the **"pass-the-baton"** semantics of strong semaphores.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A counting semaphore `S` is initialized to `3`. Five processes `P₁, P₂, P₃, P₄, P₅` all call `wait(S)` in that order. Later, two processes call `signal(S)`. Determine the value of `S` and the state of the queue.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
S_0 = 3
$$

After `wait()` calls:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After } P_1 \text{ wait: } & S_1 = 3 - 1 = 2 \;\;(\ge 0, \text{proceed}) \\
\text{After } P_2 \text{ wait: } & S_2 = 2 - 1 = 1 \;\;(\ge 0, \text{proceed}) \\
\text{After } P_3 \text{ wait: } & S_3 = 1 - 1 = 0 \;\;(\ge 0, \text{proceed}) \\
\text{After } P_4 \text{ wait: } & S_4 = 0 - 1 = -1 \;(< 0, \text{block } P_4) \\
\text{After } P_5 \text{ wait: } & S_5 = -1 - 1 = -2 \;(< 0, \text{block } P_5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**State of the queue:** `Q = [P₄, P₅]` (FIFO order), `S = −2`.

After two `signal()` calls:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After 1st signal: } & S = -2 + 1 = -1 \;(\le 0, \text{wake } P_4); \; Q = [P_5] \\
\text{After 2nd signal: } & S = -1 + 1 = 0 \;(\le 0, \text{wake } P_5); \; Q = []
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final state:** `S = 0`, `Q = []`, both `P₄` and `P₅` have proceeded into their critical sections.

**Number of processes inside critical section:**

$$
\vert CS \vert = S_{init} - S_{final} + \text{signals-that-woke-someone} = 3 - 0 + 0 = 3
$$

The first three processes (`P₁, P₂, P₃`) entered; the next two (`P₄, P₅`) entered after each `signal()`. The math is self-consistent. ∎

## 3.5 Implementation-Level Analysis (How Atomicity is Enforced)

On a **uniprocessor**, the OS can disable interrupts around the P/V body:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{wait(S):} \quad & \text{cli} \quad\quad \text{(disable interrupts)} \\
                     & S \leftarrow S - 1 \\
                     & \text{if } S < 0 \text{: block()} \\
                     & \text{sti} \quad\quad \text{(enable interrupts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

On a **multiprocessor**, interrupt disabling alone is insufficient. The kernel uses an atomic hardware instruction — either **Test-and-Set (TAS)** or **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)** — which the CPU guarantees is *uninterruptible at the bus level*:

$$
\text{CAS}(S, \text{expected}, \text{new}) = \begin{cases} \text{true},\; S \leftarrow \text{new} & \text{if } S = \text{expected} \\ \text{false},\; \text{no change} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The P-operation uses CAS in a loop (an **optimistic** lock), and only when the swap succeeds does the process check the value to decide whether to proceed or block.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Semaphore

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional block-level architecture** of a semaphore — its data part, the two atomic primitives, the blocked-process queue, and the surrounding kernel scheduling context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Calling Process Pi] -->|invokes P or V| B[Semaphore Kernel Object S]
    B --> C[Data Part: integer counter value S]
    B --> D[Control Part: queue Q of blocked processes]
    B --> E[Atomicity Guard: cli/sti or CAS hardware primitive]
    C --> F{P operation path}
    C --> G{V operation path}
    F -->|S decreases| H{S new value}
    H -->|S greater or equal 0| I[Process proceeds to critical section]
    H -->|S less than 0| J[Process appended to queue Q and BLOCKED]
    G -->|S increases| K{S new value}
    K -->|S greater than 0| L[No blocked process, just increment]
    K -->|S less or equal 0| M[Wake one process from Q and move to READY queue]
    J --> N[Ready and Blocked Queues managed by Scheduler]
    M --> N
    N -->|process resumed| I
    I --> O[Critical Section]
    O -->|V operation| G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A process invokes either P (left path) or V (right path) on the semaphore object.
- The **atomicity guard** sits *around* the read-modify-write of `S`, ensuring that the decrement/increment and the block/wake decisions happen as one indivisible step.
- Negative values of `S` mean a positive number of processes are sleeping in `Q`.
- The scheduler is the entity that moves processes between **READY** and **BLOCKED** states.

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram for a Process Interacting with a Semaphore

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    stateDiagramState1: Running
    stateDiagramState2: Ready
    stateDiagramState3: Blocked on S
    stateDiagramState4: Critical Section
    stateDiagramState1 --> stateDiagramState4: P succeeds S greater or equal 0
    stateDiagramState4 --> stateDiagramState1: V issued
    stateDiagramState1 --> stateDiagramState3: P blocks S less than 0
    stateDiagramState3 --> stateDiagramState2: V from other process wakes P
    stateDiagramState2 --> stateDiagramState1: Scheduler dispatches
```

**Interpretation:**
- A process **Running** that issues `P` either enters the **Critical Section** (if `S ≥ 0` after decrement) or moves to **Blocked on S**.
- A **Blocked** process moves to **Ready** when some other process executes `V` and the queue policy selects it for wake-up.
- The diagram captures all four key transitions of a process interacting with a semaphore.

## 4.3 Producer–Consumer Synchronization Using Two Counting Semaphores

This is the canonical textbook use-case that students *must* understand before the KTU exam:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Producer Process] -->|produce item| BS[Buffer Pool of N slots]
    P -->|wait empty| S1[Semaphore empty N init]
    P -->|wait mutex| S2[Semaphore mutex 1 init]
    BS --> C[Consumer Process]
    C -->|wait full| S3[Semaphore full 0 init]
    C -->|wait mutex| S2
    S1 -.->|decremented on produce| P
    S3 -.->|decremented on consume| C
    S2 -.->|protect buffer access| BS
```

**Semantics of the three semaphores:**

| Semaphore | Initial value | Wait invoked by | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `empty` | `N` | Producer | Counts **empty** slots; producer blocks when buffer is full |
| `full` | `0` | Consumer | Counts **filled** slots; consumer blocks when buffer is empty |
| `mutex` | `1` | Both | Provides **mutual exclusion** for the actual buffer write/read operation |

> [!NOTE]
> The producer cannot proceed if `empty = 0` (no slot), and the consumer cannot proceed if `full = 0` (no item). The `mutex` ensures that the *physical* insertion/removal is atomic.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a semaphore. List and briefly explain its two standard operations. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 2 marks concept + 1 mark operations]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer synchronization variable that is accessed only through two atomic operations called **P (wait/proberen)** and **V (signal/verhogen)**. It is used to control access to shared resources and to enforce mutual exclusion in concurrent processes.

- **P (wait) operation:** Atomically decrements the semaphore value `S` by 1. If the result is negative, the calling process is blocked and placed in a queue associated with `S`.
- **V (signal) operation:** Atomically increments `S` by 1. If the result is non-positive, exactly one blocked process is removed from the queue and moved to the ready queue.

The atomicity of these operations, typically enforced by disabling interrupts or using hardware instructions like `compare_and_swap`, ensures correctness under concurrent access. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for P + 1 mark for V = full 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a counting semaphore. Give one example use of each. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, 2 marks table + 1 mark example]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range | Only 0 or 1 | Any non-negative integer |
| Initial value | 1 | N (number of resources) |
| Use | Mutual exclusion | Bounded resource pool |

- **Binary semaphore example:** Protecting a single shared counter between two processes (typical critical section problem).
- **Counting semaphore example:** Managing N identical buffers in the producer–consumer problem where `S = N`.

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for distinguishing feature + 1 mark for example = full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Internal-Choice Pattern:** For every Part B question, KTU provides **OR** choice between two 14-mark questions. Both are given below with full sub-part model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a semaphore. Discuss the role of the **P** and **V** operations in process synchronization. State and explain the necessary conditions for the correctness of semaphore-based mutual exclusion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
A semaphore is a non-negative integer variable `S` with an associated waiting queue `Q`. It is accessed via two atomic primitives P and V, originally proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra (1965). Atomicity is enforced by either disabling interrupts (uniprocessor) or by hardware atomic instructions such as `test_and_set` or `compare_and_swap` (multiprocessor).

**2. P operation (1.5 marks):**
The wait/P operation does the following atomically:
- Decrements `S` by 1.
- If `S < 0`, the calling process is blocked and added to the queue `Q`.

**3. V operation (1.5 marks):**
The signal/V operation does the following atomically:
- Increments `S` by 1.
- If `S ≤ 0`, it removes and wakes one process from the queue `Q`.

**4. Conditions for correctness (2 marks):**
- **Mutual exclusion:** No two processes are simultaneously in the critical section. This holds because the decrement-then-test sequence is atomic.
- **No deadlock:** A process holding a resource eventually releases it via `V`.
- **No starvation (with strong semaphores):** FIFO wake-up order guarantees that every process eventually gets the resource.

**5. Diagram (extra, 0 marks but valuable):** Refer to the state-transition diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.

---

**Q. (b)** Consider three processes P₁, P₂, P₃ sharing a counting semaphore `S` initialized to `2`. The following operations are executed in order:

- P₁ executes `wait(S)`
- P₂ executes `wait(S)`
- P₃ executes `wait(S)`
- P₁ executes `signal(S)`
- P₂ executes `signal(S)`

Determine the **final value of S** and the **state of the blocked queue** at each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-step trace (1 mark per step):**

| Step | Operation | New S value | Decision | Queue Q state |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | S = 2 | — | Empty |
| 1 | P₁: `wait(S)` | S = 1 | S ≥ 0, P₁ proceeds | Empty |
| 2 | P₂: `wait(S)` | S = 0 | S ≥ 0, P₂ proceeds | Empty |
| 3 | P₃: `wait(S)` | S = −1 | S < 0, P₃ blocked | [P₃] |
| 4 | P₁: `signal(S)` | S = 0 | S ≤ 0, wake P₃ | Empty |
| 5 | P₂: `signal(S)` | S = 1 | S > 0, no one to wake | Empty |

**Final state:** `S = 1`, `Q = []` (empty), `P₃` has been woken and is now in the critical section.

**Cross-check using the formula:**

$$
S_{final} = S_{init} - (\text{waits}) + (\text{signals}) = 2 - 3 + 2 = 1 \quad\checkmark
$$

Number of processes in critical section at the end:

$$
\vert CS \vert = \text{waits that proceeded} = 3 \;\;(\text{all three at some point})
$$

But currently, only P₃ is *still inside* (P₁ and P₂ have signalled and left). This is consistent with the state of the semaphore.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 2]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **producer–consumer problem** and solve it using **three semaphores**: `empty`, `full`, and `mutex`. Provide the algorithm for both the producer and the consumer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Problem definition (1.5 marks):**
The producer–consumer problem involves two types of processes:
- **Producer** generates data items and places them into a fixed-size shared buffer.
- **Consumer** removes items from the buffer and processes them.
- The producer must **wait** if the buffer is full; the consumer must **wait** if the buffer is empty. Concurrent access to the buffer must be mutually exclusive to avoid race conditions.

**2. The three semaphores (1.5 marks):**
- `empty` (init = N): Counts the number of empty slots in the buffer.
- `full` (init = 0): Counts the number of filled slots.
- `mutex` (init = 1): Binary semaphore for mutual exclusion on the actual buffer manipulation.

**3. Producer algorithm (2 marks):**

```
Producer:
    while (true):
        produce_item()              // generate the next item
        wait(empty)                 // block if no empty slot
        wait(mutex)                 // enter critical region
        <append item to buffer>
        signal(mutex)               // leave critical region
        signal(full)                // one more filled slot
```

**4. Consumer algorithm (2 marks):**

```
Consumer:
    while (true):
        wait(full)                  // block if no item available
        wait(mutex)                 // enter critical region
        <remove item from buffer>
        signal(mutex)               // leave critical region
        signal(empty)               // one more empty slot
        consume_item()              // process the item
```

The invariant `0 ≤ items in buffer ≤ N` is maintained because each `produce` requires an empty slot and each `consume` requires a filled slot.

---

**Q. (b)** A counting semaphore `S` is initialized to `0`. Five processes perform the following sequence: P₁, P₂, P₃, P₄, P₅ each call `wait(S)`. Then, in order, P₃, P₅, P₂, P₄ each call `signal(S)`. What is the value of `S` and the contents of the waiting queue at the end? Justify using the **negative-counter theorem** that the **magnitude of the negative S** equals the number of blocked processes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-step trace (1 mark each major step, 1 mark for theorem, 1 mark for final answer):**

After the five `wait()` calls:

$$
S = 0 - 5 = -5
$$

All five processes are blocked. FIFO queue: `Q = [P₁, P₂, P₃, P₄, P₅]`.

After four `signal()` calls, the order of wake-up is FIFO (strong semaphore):

| Step | Operation | New S | Wake from Q | Updated Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P₃: `signal(S)` | −4 | P₁ woken | [P₂, P₃, P₄, P₅] |
| 2 | P₅: `signal(S)` | −3 | P₂ woken | [P₃, P₄, P₅] |
| 3 | P₂: `signal(S)` | −2 | P₃ woken | [P₄, P₅] |
| 4 | P₄: `signal(S)` | −1 | P₄ woken | [P₅] |

**Final state:**

- `S = −1`
- `Q = [P₅]` — only P₅ remains blocked.
- Number of woken processes = 4; only P₅ is still in the queue.

**Negative-counter theorem (1 mark):**

> "If a semaphore `S` has initial value `N` and `k` `wait()` operations have been performed (and `j` `signal()` operations have occurred), then `−S` equals the number of processes currently blocked in `Q`, **provided** all woken processes are removed from the queue."

Here, `−S = 1`, and indeed one process (`P₅`) is still in `Q`. The theorem is verified. ∎

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Semaphore questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting atomicity** — A 2-mark loss if you do not mention that P and V are *uninterruptible / atomic*. This is the *defining property* of a semaphore.
> 2. **Sign-flip confusion in P/V** — Many students write `if S == 0, block` in P. The *correct* condition is `if S < 0, block` **after** decrement. KTU examiners deduct a full mark for this.
> 3. **Confusing binary with mutex lock** — A binary semaphore *can* implement mutual exclusion, but it is *not* a mutex lock. Mutex has *ownership* (only the locking process can unlock). Binary semaphore does not. KTU strictly distinguishes these.
> 4. **Not drawing the queue state** in trace questions — Always show the queue state at each step; a 1-mark loss if missing.
> 5. **In producer–consumer, swapping `empty` and `full`** — Producer waits on `empty`, consumer waits on `full`. Reverse is the most common mistake.
> 6. **Forgetting the negative-counter interpretation** — Examiners love asking "what does the negative value of a semaphore mean?" Answer: *the number of processes currently blocked in the queue*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer `S` plus a blocked-process queue `Q` — accessed *only* through atomic **P** and **V** operations.
- [x] **P (wait)**: `S = S − 1`; if `S < 0` ⟹ block the process.
- [x] **V (signal)**: `S = S + 1`; if `S ≤ 0` ⟹ wake one process from `Q`.
- [x] **Binary semaphore** has value in {0, 1}; **counting semaphore** has value in {0, 1, 2, …, N}.
- [x] **Atomicity** is the cornerstone: enforced by interrupt disabling (uniprocessor) or `compare_and_swap` (multiprocessor).
- [x] **Strong semaphores** use FIFO queues ⟹ starvation-free. **Weak semaphores** may starve.
- [x] A **negative value** of `S` ⟹ `|S|` processes are currently blocked in the queue.
- [x] A **zero value** ⟹ resource just exhausted; next P will block.
- [x] A **positive value** ⟹ that many resources are still available; next P will succeed.
- [x] **Mutual exclusion invariant** with binary semaphore: `S_init = 1`, `S` decremented atomically ⟹ only one process can be in CS.
- [x] **Producer–consumer** needs THREE semaphores: `empty (init = N)`, `full (init = 0)`, `mutex (init = 1)`.
- [x] **Dijkstra's original names**: P = *Proberen* (to test), V = *Verhogen* (to increment). Modern names: P = wait, V = signal.
- [x] **Mutex vs binary semaphore**: Mutex has ownership; binary semaphore does not. KTU exam favorite question.
- [x] **Mutual exclusion = entry section uses wait(S); exit section uses signal(S)** — always in that order.
- [x] The semaphore was proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1965** — KTU may ask this in 1-mark history question.
- [x] Real-world analogs: restroom key (binary), toll plaza with N booths (counting), single receptionist enforcing atomicity.
- [x] Strong-semaphore wake-up is **FIFO** ⟹ every process eventually enters CS ⟹ **bounded waiting** satisfied.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binary Semaphores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Binary Semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Semaphore (Definition per KTU Syllabus):**
