> A **binary semaphore** is a synchronization primitive that can take only two discrete values, namely **0** (locked / unavailable) and **1** (unlocked / available). It is formally defined as a protected integer variable `S ∈ {0, 1}` accessed exclusively through two atomic, indivisible operations: `wait(S)` (also called `P(S)` or `down(S)`) which decrements the semaphore, and `signal(S)` (also called `V(S)` or `up(S)`) which increments the semaphore.

Mathematically, a binary semaphore is the tuple:

$$
B = \langle S, W \rangle
$$

where:
- $S \in \{0, 1\}$ is the current value
- $W$ is the set of processes currently blocked (waiting) on $B$

The atomicity of the two primitives guarantees **mutual exclusion** in the access of the shared resource that the semaphore protects.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Bathroom Lock)

Imagine a single-stall public restroom in a crowded railway station. The door has a single bolt lock:

- When the bolt is **free (unlocked)**, the indicator reads `GREEN (1)`. Any one person can enter, slide the bolt, and the indicator flips to `RED (0)`.
- When someone is **inside (locked)**, no one else can enter; newcomers must wait in the queue (`blocked queue W`).
- When the person inside **exits**, they slide the bolt back, the indicator returns to `GREEN (1)`, and the first person in the queue is allowed in (one process is woken up).

This is *exactly* a binary semaphore: a **single-slot** resource whose availability toggles between `1` and `0`, never more.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A binary semaphore is **not** the same as a *mutex*. A mutex is a *locking mechanism* (ownership-restricted), while a binary semaphore is a *signaling mechanism*. With a mutex, only the process that locked it can unlock it. With a binary semaphore, any process can `signal()`, even one that did not perform the `wait()`. This distinction is a frequent KTU 2024 exam question.

### 1.3 Key Properties

- **Two-valued state:** $S$ is strictly constrained to $\in \{0, 1\}$.
- **Atomic operations:** `wait` and `signal` are uninterruptible; they execute as a single indivisible step.
- **Blocking semantics:** If `wait` finds $S = 0$, the calling process is suspended and added to the waiting queue $W$.
- **Wake-up semantics:** `signal` resumes (if any) one blocked process from $W$; otherwise it simply sets $S = 1$.

### 1.4 Why Binary Semaphores?

> [!NOTE]
> The binary semaphore was introduced by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** (1965) to solve the **Critical Section Problem** without the limitations of software-only solutions such as Dekker's or Peterson's algorithms. It also avoids the overhead of hardware-only solutions (interrupt disabling) by providing a clean, kernel-mediated abstraction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Two Primitives

The textbook definition of binary semaphore operations (Silberschatz / Galvin / Gagne convention used by KTU) is as follows.

#### 2.1.1 The `wait(S)` Operation (also called `P(S)` / `down(S)`)

The pseudo-code of `wait` enforces the binary constraint that $S$ can never go below $0$:

$$
\text{wait}(S):\quad
\begin{cases}
S \leftarrow S - 1 \\[2pt]
\text{if } S < 0 \Rightarrow \text{block the calling process and add it to } W \\[2pt]
\text{if } S \ge 0 \Rightarrow \text{process enters its critical section}
\end{cases}
$$

The *Why:* The decrement-then-test pattern ensures that if multiple processes call `wait` concurrently, **only one** observes $S \ge 0$ and proceeds; the rest are blocked.

#### 2.1.2 The `signal(S)` Operation (also called `V(S)` / `up(S)`)

$$
\text{signal}(S):\quad
\begin{cases}
S \leftarrow S + 1 \\[2pt]
\text{if } S \le 0 \Rightarrow \text{wake up exactly one process from } W \\[2pt]
\text{if } S > 0 \Rightarrow \text{no process was waiting; value restored to } 1
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial insight:** The sign of $(S+1)$ after `signal` is what dictates whether to wake a process. Because $S$ is *binary*, $S+1$ can be at most $1$. If after incrementing we still have $S \le 0$, it means at least one process was previously blocked, so we wake one. Otherwise, the value is simply set to $1$.

### 2.2 State Transition Analysis

The internal state of a binary semaphore has exactly four possible configurations, and `wait` / `signal` drive transitions among them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State 0:} \quad & S = 1,\; W = \emptyset \quad \text{(free, no waiters)} \\
\text{State 1:} \quad & S = 0,\; W = \emptyset \quad \text{(held, no waiters)} \\
\text{State 2:} \quad & S = 0,\; W = \{P_1\} \quad \text{(held, one waiter)} \\
\text{State 3:} \quad & S = 0,\; W = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\} \quad \text{(held, multiple waiters)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Valid transitions:
- `wait` from State 0 → State 1
- `wait` from States 1, 2, 3 → same state with $W$ growing
- `signal` from State 1 → State 0
- `signal` from States 2, 3 → same state with $W$ shrinking by one

### 2.3 Binary Semaphore vs. Counting Semaphore (High-Yield Comparison)

This is one of the **most-asked KTU questions** for Module 2.

| Property | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range $S$ | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | $S \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ (unbounded) |
| Number of resources | Exactly **1** instance | $N$ instances (initial value = $N$) |
| `wait` decrements to | At most $0$ | May go arbitrarily negative |
| Implementation | Can be built from a counting semaphore with max=1 | Can be built from $N$ binary semaphores |
| Typical use | Mutual exclusion (lock) | Resource allocation pools |
| Process ownership | None (any process can signal) | None |
| FIFO fairness | Optional (depends on queue policy) | Optional |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Constraint / Boundary | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Semaphore value | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | $0 \le S \le 1$ | Resource is either free or busy |
| `wait` decrement | $S \leftarrow S - 1$ | $S \ge 0$ enforced | Decrement is logical only |
| `signal` increment | $S \leftarrow S + 1$ | $S \le 1$ enforced | Increment is logical only |
| Number of processes in CS | $\le 1$ | Mutual exclusion invariant | Safety property |
| Waiting queue size | $\vert W \vert = 1 - S$ | When $S=0$ | Tracks blocked count |
| No. of wakeups per `signal` | Exactly **1** | $S$ increments by 1 | One process released |
| Total processes served | $\sum \text{signal()} = \sum \text{wait()}$ | Over any execution | Conservation law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in real engineering:** Binary semaphores underpin every modern OS's lock primitive (`pthread_mutex_t` in POSIX, `CRITICAL_SECTION` in Windows, `java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock`). They also form the basis of producer-consumer buffers, reader-writer synchronization, and the classical Dining-Philosophers Problem. In embedded systems (FreeRTOS, VxWorks), binary semaphores are used for ISR-to-task synchronization — the ISR performs `signal` to release a waiting task.

### 2.5 Three Implementation Strategies for Atomicity

For a binary semaphore to be correct, `wait` and `signal` must be **atomic**. Three approaches exist:

1. **Hardware-based: Test-and-Set (TSL / atomic CAS)**
2. **Software-based: Disable interrupts** (kernel mode only)
3. **Hybrid: OS scheduler support** (block/wakeup primitives)

The first is most common in user-space libraries; the second is used inside the kernel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Atomic Implementation Using Test-and-Set (Hardware)

The Test-and-Set instruction is the standard hardware primitive that the OS uses to enforce atomicity. Let us derive the implementation from first principles.

#### Step 1: Define the Test-and-Set primitive

$$
\text{test\_and\_set}(target):\quad
\begin{aligned}
\text{tmp} & \leftarrow \text{target} \\
\text{target} & \leftarrow \text{TRUE} \\
\text{return} & \;\text{tmp}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is executed atomically by the CPU in a single bus cycle (or via cache-lock on modern x86).

#### Step 2: Construct the lock flag

Declare a shared boolean `lock` initialized to `FALSE` ($0$). This flag guards the entry into the critical section of the semaphore code itself.

#### Step 3: Wrap the body of `wait` and `signal` with TSL

The kernel-level implementation of binary semaphore `S` becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{wait}(S):\quad & \\
1.\quad & \text{while (test\_and\_set}(\&\text{lock}) == \text{TRUE}) \\
2.\quad & \qquad \text{;} \;\; // \text{busy-wait until we acquire the spinlock} \\
3.\quad & S.value \leftarrow S.value - 1 \\
4.\quad & \text{if } S.value < 0 \text{ then} \\
5.\quad & \qquad \text{add current process to } W \\
6.\quad & \qquad \text{block the process (schedule another)} \\
7.\quad & \text{else} \\
8.\quad & \qquad \text{// process has the resource; may proceed} \\
9.\quad & \text{end if} \\
10.\quad & \text{lock} \leftarrow \text{FALSE} \;\; // \text{release the spinlock} \\
\\
\text{signal}(S):\quad & \\
1.\quad & \text{while (test\_and\_set}(\&\text{lock}) == \text{TRUE}) \\
2.\quad & \qquad \text{;} \\
3.\quad & S.value \leftarrow S.value + 1 \\
4.\quad & \text{if } S.value \le 0 \text{ then} \\
5.\quad & \qquad \text{remove one process } P_j \text{ from } W \\
6.\quad & \qquad \text{wakeup}(P_j) \\
7.\quad & \text{else} \\
8.\quad & \qquad S.value \leftarrow 1 \;\; // \text{clamp to binary max} \\
9.\quad & \text{end if} \\
10.\quad & \text{lock} \leftarrow \text{FALSE}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explanation of Line 8 of `signal`:** Even though $S$ is binary, the algebraic expression $S \leftarrow S + 1$ is a *logical* operation. If no process was waiting, $S$ would formally become $2$. The clamping `$S.value \leftarrow 1$` reinstates the binary invariant. This is a common pitfall in KTU exams.

#### Step 4: Verify the Binary Invariant

We claim that at all times $S \in \{0, 1\}$. Proof by induction on the number of operations.

- **Base case:** Initially $S = 1$ ✓
- **Inductive step:** Suppose $S \in \{0, 1\}$ before an operation.
  - `wait`: $S$ becomes $S - 1 \in \{-1, 0\}$. If $-1$, process blocks and the *effective* value (number of available resources) is still $0$. Logical invariant maintained.
  - `signal`: $S$ becomes $S + 1 \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. If $2$, the clamp on Line 8 reduces it to $1$. If $\le 0$, a process is woken, restoring the count of available resources to exactly $1$ for that process. Invariant maintained. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Walk-Through: Producer–Consumer With a Binary Semaphore

Consider two cooperating processes $P_{\text{prod}}$ and $P_{\text{cons}}$ sharing a single shared variable `buffer`. We synchronize them with a binary semaphore `mutex` initialized to $1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{prod}}: \quad & \text{produce item} \\
& \text{wait}(\text{mutex}) \\
& \text{write item to buffer} \\
& \text{signal}(\text{mutex}) \\
\\
P_{\text{cons}}: \quad & \text{wait}(\text{mutex}) \\
& \text{read item from buffer} \\
& \text{consume item} \\
& \text{signal}(\text{mutex})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Trace Table (Interleaved Execution)

Assume $\text{mutex} = 1$, $W = \emptyset$.

| Step | Operation | mutex (before) | mutex (after) | $W$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `wait(mutex)` by $P_{\text{prod}}$ | 1 | 0 | $\emptyset$ | Producer enters CS |
| 2 | `signal(mutex)` by $P_{\text{prod}}$ | 0 | 1 | $\emptyset$ | Resource released |
| 3 | `wait(mutex)` by $P_{\text{cons}}$ | 1 | 0 | $\emptyset$ | Consumer enters CS |
| 4 | `signal(mutex)` by $P_{\text{cons}}$ | 0 | 1 | $\emptyset$ | Resource released |

Now suppose both call `wait` *concurrently*:

| Step | Operation | mutex (before) | mutex (after) | $W$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `wait(mutex)` by $P_{\text{prod}}$ | 1 | 0 | $\emptyset$ | $P_{\text{prod}}$ enters CS |
| 2 | `wait(mutex)` by $P_{\text{cons}}$ | 0 | -1 | $\{P_{\text{cons}}\}$ | $P_{\text{cons}}$ blocked |
| 3 | `signal(mutex)` by $P_{\text{prod}}$ | -1 | 0 | $\emptyset$ | $P_{\text{cons}}$ woken, enters CS |

This proves that **at most one process is in the critical section at any time** (mutual exclusion).

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Binary Semaphore | Mutex Lock | Spinlock | Monitor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ownership | None | Strict (owner unlocks) | None | Per object |
| Busy-wait | Depends on impl | No (blocking) | Yes | No |
| Suitable for single-processor | Yes (with kernel support) | Yes | No (wastes CPU) | Yes |
| Suitable for multiprocessor | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Re-entrant | Yes (separate instance needed) | No (unless explicit) | No | N/A |
| Priority inversion risk | Yes (unless PI) | Yes (unless PI) | Yes | Language-level handling |
| KTU typical marks | 7-8 | 5-6 | 3-4 | 3-4 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Machine: Binary Semaphore Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateFree : initialize S = 1
    StateFree : S = 1, W = empty
    StateFree --> StateHeld : wait(S)
    StateHeld : S = 0, W = empty
    StateHeld --> StateWaiting : wait(S)
    StateWaiting : S = 0, W non empty
    StateWaiting --> StateHeld : signal(S)
    StateHeld --> StateFree : signal(S)
    StateWaiting --> [*] : process exit
    StateHeld --> [*] : process exit
    StateFree --> [*] : process exit
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: wait(S) Operational Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process calls wait S] --> B[S value equals S value minus 1]
    B --> C{Is S value less than 0}
    C -- Yes --> D[Add process to waiting queue W]
    D --> E[Block the calling process]
    E --> F[Scheduler picks next ready process]
    C -- No --> G[Process enters critical section]
    G --> H[Execute critical section code]
    H --> I[Call signal S to release]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: signal(S) Operational Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process calls signal S] --> B[S value equals S value plus 1]
    B --> C{Is S value less than or equal to 0}
    C -- Yes --> D[Remove one process Pj from W]
    D --> E[Wakeup Pj]
    E --> F[Pj resumes and enters critical section]
    C -- No --> G[Clamp S value to 1]
    G --> H[No process was waiting; semaphore now free]
    F --> I[Continue execution]
    H --> I[Continue execution]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hardware-Supported Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Process P] --> B[System Call wait S]
    B --> C[Kernel Space]
    C --> D[Acquire Spinlock via Test and Set]
    D --> E[Decrement S]
    E --> F{Is S less than 0}
    F -- Yes --> G[Add P to Waiting Queue W]
    G --> H[Block P in Scheduler]
    F -- No --> I[Grant Critical Section Access]
    I --> J[Release Spinlock]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Return to User Space]
    L[Another Process Q] --> M[System Call signal S]
    M --> N[Acquire Spinlock]
    N --> O[Increment S]
    O --> P{Any process in W}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Wak eup blocked process from W]
    P -- No --> R[Set S to 1]
    Q --> S[Release Spinlock]
    R --> S
    S --> T[Return to User Space]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Architecture: Producer–Consumer With Binary Semaphore

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Prod as Producer Process
    participant KS as Kernel Semaphore
    participant Cons as Consumer Process
    Note over KS: mutex = 1, W = empty
    Prod->>KS: wait mutex
    KS-->>Prod: mutex = 0, access granted
    Note over Prod: Inside critical section
    Prod->>KS: signal mutex
    KS-->>Prod: mutex = 1, released
    Cons->>KS: wait mutex
    KS-->>Cons: mutex = 0, access granted
    Note over Cons: Inside critical section
    Cons->>KS: signal mutex
    KS-->>Cons: mutex = 1, released
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`, `flowX`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels use only plain uppercase text, no bold, italics, or HTML.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Define a binary semaphore. Why is it called "binary"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A binary semaphore is a synchronization tool that can take only the values $0$ or $1$. It is called "binary" because the semaphore variable $S$ has exactly two legal states: $S = 1$ (resource free / unlocked) and $S = 0$ (resource busy / locked). It is used to provide mutual exclusion to a single shared resource. The two atomic operations `wait(S)` and `signal(S)` toggle the semaphore between these two states. **(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for use case)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**
*Distinguish between a binary semaphore and a counting semaphore.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | $S \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N\}$ |
| Resource count | Exactly 1 | Up to $N$ |
| Typical use | Mutual exclusion | Resource pool |

**(3 marks: 1 mark per distinguishing point, full table → 3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the implementation of `wait(S)` and `signal(S)` operations on a binary semaphore. Show how mutual exclusion is guaranteed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the use of binary semaphores to solve the producer–consumer problem for a shared single-slot buffer. Show a trace table for a sample interleaved execution. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

The implementation of a binary semaphore requires that `wait` and `signal` execute *atomically*. This is achieved using a hardware `test-and-set` instruction.

**Step 1 — Test-and-Set primitive** (atomic):
```
boolean test_and_set(boolean *target) {
    boolean tmp = *target;
    *target = TRUE;
    return tmp;
}
```

**Step 2 — `wait(S)` implementation:**
```
wait(binary_semaphore *S) {
    while (test_and_set(&S->lock) == TRUE)  // acquire spinlock
        ;                                   // busy-wait
    S->value = S->value - 1;
    if (S->value < 0) {
        add_to_queue(S->wait_queue, current_process);
        block(current_process);
    }
    S->lock = FALSE;                        // release spinlock
}
```

**Step 3 — `signal(S)` implementation:**
```
signal(binary_semaphore *S) {
    while (test_and_set(&S->lock) == TRUE)
        ;
    S->value = S->value + 1;
    if (S->value <= 0) {
        process P = remove_from_queue(S->wait_queue);
        wakeup(P);
    } else {
        S->value = 1;    // clamp to binary
    }
    S->lock = FALSE;
}
```

**Step 4 — Proof of mutual exclusion:**

Consider $n$ processes $P_1, \ldots, P_n$ calling `wait(S)` concurrently. The atomic test-and-set guarantees that only one process at a time can pass the spinlock and modify `S->value`. Suppose $P_i$ is the first to acquire the spinlock:

- $S$ was initially $1$. $P_i$ decrements to $0$, then releases the spinlock. $P_i$ now has the resource.
- Any other $P_j$ arriving acquires the spinlock later, finds $S = 0$, decrements to $-1$, and is added to the waiting queue and blocked.
- Only when $P_i$ (or some other holder) calls `signal` does the value rise to $0$, triggering a wakeup of exactly one $P_j$.

Thus at any moment $\le 1$ process is in the critical section. The three required conditions (mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting) are all satisfied. **(7 marks: 2 marks for code, 2 marks for atomicity argument, 3 marks for proof)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Problem statement:** Two processes, a producer $P$ and a consumer $C$, share a single-slot buffer `buf`. They must be synchronized so that $P$ writes only when the buffer is empty, and $C$ reads only when the buffer is full.

**Semaphores used:**
- `mutex` (binary) initialized to $1$ → protects `buf` from simultaneous access
- `full` (binary) initialized to $0$ → signals buffer has data
- `empty` (binary) initialized to $1$ → signals buffer is free

**Code:**
```
Producer:                          Consumer:
while (TRUE) {                     while (TRUE) {
    produce_item(&item);               wait(full);
    wait(empty);                       wait(mutex);
    wait(mutex);                       read_item(&item, buf);
    write_item(buf, item);             signal(mutex);
    signal(mutex);                     signal(empty);
    signal(full);                      consume_item(&item);
}                                   }
```

**Trace Table (interleaved execution):**

| Step | Action | empty | full | mutex | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | 1 | 0 | 1 | — |
| 1 | P: `wait(empty)` | 0 | 0 | 1 | OK |
| 2 | P: `wait(mutex)` | 0 | 0 | 0 | P writes to buf |
| 3 | P: `signal(mutex)` | 0 | 0 | 1 | Released |
| 4 | P: `signal(full)` | 0 | 1 | 1 | Buffer now full |
| 5 | C: `wait(full)` | 0 | 0 | 1 | OK |
| 6 | C: `wait(mutex)` | 0 | 0 | 0 | C reads from buf |
| 7 | C: `signal(mutex)` | 0 | 0 | 1 | Released |
| 8 | C: `signal(empty)` | 1 | 0 | 1 | Buffer empty again |

The trace confirms: producer and consumer never access `buf` simultaneously (mutual exclusion via `mutex`), and synchronization is enforced via `full` and `empty`. **(7 marks: 2 marks for code, 2 marks for semaphore choice, 3 marks for trace table)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of binary semaphores. What problems can arise if `wait` and `signal` are not implemented atomically? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the priority inversion problem in the context of binary semaphores and discuss any one solution to it. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Limitations of Binary Semaphores:**

1. **No ownership:** Any process can `signal` a semaphore even if it never performed a `wait`. This can lead to logical errors in code.
2. **Deadlock potential:** If a process performs `wait` and forgets to `signal`, the resource is permanently locked, and the system hangs.
3. **Lost wakeup problem:** A `signal` executed when no process is waiting has no effect (value simply increments to $1$); a `wait` from a different process then proceeds even though no synchronization is intended.
4. **Busy-waiting overhead:** If implemented with test-and-set spinlocks, the kernel burns CPU cycles.
5. **Starvation:** A low-priority process may be perpetually denied access if higher-priority processes keep taking the semaphore.

**Consequence of non-atomic `wait`/`signal`:**

If `wait` and `signal` are not atomic, two processes can interleave their decrements. For example, suppose $S = 1$ and $P_1, P_2$ both execute `wait` non-atomically:

- $P_1$ reads $S = 1$, then is preempted before writing $S \leftarrow 0$.
- $P_2$ reads $S = 1$, then writes $S \leftarrow 0$ and enters the critical section.
- $P_1$ resumes and writes $S \leftarrow 0$ and *also* enters the critical section.

Now both processes are in the critical section simultaneously — **mutual exclusion is violated**. This is precisely why test-and-set or interrupt disabling is mandatory. **(7 marks: 4 marks for limitations, 3 marks for non-atomicity consequence with example)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Priority Inversion:**
Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority process $H$ is blocked waiting for a resource held by a low-priority process $L$, while a medium-priority process $M$ preempts $L$, preventing $L$ from finishing and releasing the resource. $H$ is thus effectively delayed by $M$, even though $H$ has higher priority than $M$.

**Classic example:** Mars Pathfinder (1997). A high-priority meteorological task was starved by a low-priority bus-management task, causing system resets.

**Trace with binary semaphore `S`:**

| Time | H | M | L | S | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | ready | ready | ready | 1 | — |
| 1 | `wait(S)` → in CS | — | — | 0 | H holds S |
| 2 | preempted by M | runs | — | 0 | M doesn't need S |
| 3 | blocked | runs | — | 0 | M preempts L too (wait, L hasn't run yet) |
| 4 | blocked | runs | preempted (no, L runs) | 0 | M yields, L runs |
| 5 | blocked | ready | `signal(S)` then H resumes | 1 | H finally gets S |

**Solution: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

When a high-priority process $H$ blocks on a semaphore held by a low-priority process $L$, the OS *temporarily boosts* $L$'s priority to that of $H$. This prevents any medium-priority process from preempting $L$, ensuring $L$ finishes its critical section quickly and releases the semaphore. After $L$ releases the semaphore, its priority is restored to its original value.

**Variants:** Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) assigns each semaphore a ceiling priority equal to the highest priority of any process that may lock it. **(7 marks: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for trace, 3 marks for PIP/PCP solution)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Conflating mutex with binary semaphore** — a binary semaphore is *not* ownership-bound. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the clamp `$S \leftarrow 1$` in `signal`** when no process is waiting. Many students stop at `$S \leftarrow S + 1$` and fail to enforce the binary invariant. **−1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Not showing the spinlock acquisition in the TSL implementation** — examiners specifically test whether students understand that even `wait`/`signal` themselves must be guarded. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Drawing the trace without the waiting queue column $W$** — in a binary-semaphore trace, showing the *logical* value of $S$ is not enough; the waiting set $W$ must also be shown. **−1 mark**.
> 5. **In producer–consumer, using only one semaphore** — for a single-slot buffer, you need at least three (or two) semaphores. Using just `mutex` does not synchronize fullness/emptiness. **−2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **binary semaphore** is a two-valued synchronization primitive: $S \in \{0, 1\}$.
- It exposes two atomic operations: **`wait(S)`** (decrement / block) and **`signal(S)`** (increment / wakeup).
- It is **not** a mutex — semaphores are signaling tools with no ownership concept.
- It is most commonly used to enforce **mutual exclusion** on a single shared resource.
- Atomicity of `wait`/`signal` is enforced via **Test-and-Set** (user space) or **interrupt disabling** (kernel).
- The implementation must **clamp** $S$ to $1$ in `signal` when no process is waiting, to preserve the binary invariant.
- Trace tables for binary semaphores **must include the waiting queue $W$**, not just the value of $S$.
- In the producer–consumer problem, a binary semaphore is used alongside other semaphores (e.g., `full`, `empty`) to synchronize fullness/emptiness, not just access.
- **Priority inversion** is a real-world hazard; **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** is the standard remedy.
- The `wait` operation always decrements $S$ first, then tests; the `signal` operation always increments first, then tests. Reversing this order breaks correctness.
- The number of wakeups per `signal` is **exactly 1**, never more.
- In KTU 2024 Scheme, expect direct definitions (3 marks), comparison questions (7 marks), and full implementation + trace questions (14 marks).

> **End of Module 2 — Binary Semaphores Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Producer/Consumer (Bounded Buffer) Problem and Its Solution Using Semaphores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Producer/Consumer (Bounded Buffer) Problem and Its Solution Using Semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Producer/Consumer Problem** (also called the **Bounded-Buffer Problem**) is a classic synchronization problem in operating systems where two types of processes — **producers** and **consumers** — share a common, fixed-size buffer of size **N**. The producer generates data items and places them into the buffer, while the consumer removes data items from the buffer and uses them. The problem is to ensure that:

1. The producer must not overwrite a buffer slot that has not yet been consumed (i.e., the buffer is **full**).
2. The consumer must not read from a buffer slot that is empty or has already been read (i.e., the buffer is **empty**).
3. Only **one** process may access the shared buffer at a time (**mutual exclusion**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 — Process Synchronization):**
> The bounded-buffer problem is the foundational case study for the use of **counting semaphores** combined with a **binary (mutex) semaphore**. Examiners expect students to identify the three semaphores, justify their initial values, and explain the order of `wait()` and `signal()` operations.

The standard solution uses **three semaphores**:
- `mutex` — a **binary semaphore** initialized to **1**, ensuring mutual exclusion when accessing the buffer.
- `empty` — a **counting semaphore** initialized to **N**, counting the number of empty slots.
- `full` — a **counting semaphore** initialized to **0**, counting the number of occupied (full) slots.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Window:**
> Imagine a restaurant where the **chef (producer)** cooks dishes and places them on a serving counter that has a fixed number of slots, say **5**. The **waiter (consumer)** picks up dishes and delivers them to customers.
>
> - The chef **cannot** place a new dish on the counter if **all 5 slots are full** (counter full → chef waits).
> - The waiter **cannot** pick up a dish if **the counter is empty** (no dish → waiter waits).
> - The chef and waiter **cannot** touch the same dish at the same instant (mutual exclusion).
>
> The `empty` counter tracks free slots, the `full` counter tracks ready dishes, and `mutex` prevents the two hands from colliding. This is precisely the Bounded-Buffer Problem.

This problem is a direct, generalized, real-world manifestation of the **race-condition** problem, and its solution is the cornerstone of all process-synchronization questions in KTU examinations.

### 1.3 Why This Problem Matters in KTU

- It is a **Module 2 high-weightage topic** in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination).
- It tests understanding of **semaphore primitives** (`wait` / `signal` or `P` / `V`).
- It is the simplest illustrative case of the **Readers–Writers** and **Dining Philosophers** problems that follow.
- It bridges theory (semaphores) with practice (inter-process communication in Unix/Linux using POSIX semaphores).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Semaphores — Logic Breakdown

Let the shared bounded buffer be an array `buffer[N]` where `N` is the maximum capacity. Two index pointers are used:

- `in`  — points to the next **free** position where the producer will insert.
- `out` — points to the next **full** position from which the consumer will remove.

Both `in` and `out` are initialized to **0** and are maintained modulo **N** (i.e., they wrap around: `in = (in + 1) mod N`).

#### Step-by-step logical breakdown of the **Producer** process

1. **Wait** for at least one empty slot → `wait(empty)`.
   - *Why?* If the buffer is full, the producer must block; this is the safety condition.
2. **Wait** for exclusive access to the buffer → `wait(mutex)`.
   - *Why?* The producer and consumer must never simultaneously modify the shared index `in` or the buffer contents.
3. **Insert** the produced item into `buffer[in]` and increment `in` modulo N.
4. **Signal** the mutex to release exclusive access → `signal(mutex)`.
5. **Signal** that a new item is available → `signal(full)`.
   - *Why?* The consumer may have been blocked because `full = 0`; this wakes it up.

#### Step-by-step logical breakdown of the **Consumer** process

1. **Wait** for at least one full slot → `wait(full)`.
   - *Why?* If the buffer is empty, the consumer must block.
2. **Wait** for exclusive access to the buffer → `wait(mutex)`.
3. **Remove** the item from `buffer[out]` and increment `out` modulo N.
4. **Signal** the mutex to release exclusive access → `signal(mutex)`.
5. **Signal** that a new empty slot is available → `signal(empty)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Point — The `wait`/`signal` Order:**
> The `wait(mutex)` is performed **after** `wait(empty)` (or `wait(full)`) and **before** the critical section. The corresponding `signal(mutex)` is performed **after** the critical section. Reversing the order of `wait(empty)` and `wait(mutex)` can lead to **deadlock** — a frequent pitfall in KTU answers.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Variable | Initial Value | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Buffer Capacity | $N$ | Given in problem | Constant | Total number of slots |
| Mutual Exclusion Semaphore | `mutex` | $\textbf{1}$ | Binary Semaphore | Ensures only one process enters the critical section |
| Empty Slots Counter | `empty` | $N$ | Counting Semaphore | Counts available empty positions |
| Full Slots Counter | `full` | $\textbf{0}$ | Counting Semaphore | Counts items ready to be consumed |
| Producer Insert Index | `in` | $\textbf{0}$ | Shared Integer | Next free position (updated mod $N$) |
| Consumer Remove Index | `out` | $\textbf{0}$ | Shared Integer | Next full position (updated mod $N$) |
| Invariant | `in` = `out` | Always | Condition | Holds when buffer is **empty** (after `in = out` wrap) or full |
| Wrap-around formula | Position update | $\vert(in+1)\vert \bmod N$ | Modular Arithmetic | Implements circular buffer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Used in **Linux kernel I/O buffering** (`/proc/sys/vm/dirty_*` and ring buffers).
> - Implemented in **pipe() / FIFO** inter-process communication in Unix.
> - Forms the basis of **circular queue (ring buffer) data structures** in embedded firmware, network packet processing, and audio/video streaming pipelines.
> - In **Java** and **C#**, the `BlockingQueue<E>` (e.g., `ArrayBlockingQueue(N)`) is a direct industrial implementation of the bounded-buffer abstraction.

### 2.3 Initial Value Justification (Why $N$, $0$, $1$?)

- `empty = N` : At start, **all** `N` slots are empty, so the count of empty slots is `N`. A producer can proceed immediately.
- `full = 0`  : At start, **no** slot contains data, so the count of full slots is `0`. A consumer must wait.
- `mutex = 1` : Only **one** process is allowed in the critical section at any time. `1` represents the "lock is free" state.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Canonical Pseudocode Solution

Below is the textbook solution (Silberschatz / Galvin / Gagne style) — written in **standard OS pseudocode**, not in any specific programming language.

```
/* Shared data */
semaphore mutex = 1;     /* Binary semaphore for mutual exclusion   */
semaphore empty = N;     /* Counting semaphore: number of empty slots */
semaphore full  = 0;     /* Counting semaphore: number of full slots  */

item  buffer[N];         /* The bounded buffer (circular)              */
int   in  = 0;           /* Producer insert index                      */
int   out = 0;           /* Consumer remove index                      */

/* -------- PRODUCER PROCESS -------- */
while (true) {
    /* 1. Produce an item (outside critical section) */
    item nextProduced = produce_item();

    /* 2. Wait until there is an empty slot */
    wait(empty);

    /* 3. Acquire exclusive access to the buffer */
    wait(mutex);

    /* ----- Critical Section: Begin ----- */
    buffer[in] = nextProduced;
    in = (in + 1) mod N;
    /* ----- Critical Section: End   ----- */

    /* 4. Release exclusive access */
    signal(mutex);

    /* 5. Inform that one more slot is now full */
    signal(full);
}

/* -------- CONSUMER PROCESS -------- */
while (true) {
    /* 1. Wait until there is at least one full slot */
    wait(full);

    /* 2. Acquire exclusive access to the buffer */
    wait(mutex);

    /* ----- Critical Section: Begin ----- */
    item nextConsumed = buffer[out];
    out = (out + 1) mod N;
    /* ----- Critical Section: End   ----- */

    /* 3. Release exclusive access */
    signal(mutex);

    /* 4. Inform that one more slot is now empty */
    signal(empty);

    /* 5. Consume the item (outside critical section) */
    consume_item(nextConsumed);
}
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Trace for $N = 3$

Let $N = 3$ (buffer of size 3). Initial state: `mutex = 1`, `empty = 3`, `full = 0`, `in = 0`, `out = 0`.

| Step | Action | `empty` before | `full` before | `mutex` before | `in` after | `out` after | `empty` after | `full` after | Buffer State (`[a,b,c]`) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Producer executes `wait(empty)` | 3 | 0 | 1 | – | – | 2 | 0 | `[ , , ]` |
| 2 | Producer executes `wait(mutex)` | 2 | 0 | 1 | – | – | 2 | 0 | `[ , , ]` |
| 3 | Producer inserts item `A`; `in=(0+1) mod 3 = 1` | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 0 | `[A, , ]` |
| 4 | Producer `signal(mutex)` | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 0 | `[A, , ]` |
| 5 | Producer `signal(full)` | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 | `[A, , ]` |
| 6 | Producer inserts `B` and `C` (similar) | 0 | 2 | 1 | 3→0 | 0 | 0 | 2 | `[A,B,C]` |
| 7 | Producer attempts `wait(empty)` → **blocks** (empty=0) | 0 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2 | `[A,B,C]` |
| 8 | Consumer `wait(full)` → full becomes 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | `[A,B,C]` |
| 9 | Consumer `wait(mutex)`; consumes `A`; `out = 1` | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | `[ ,B,C]` |
| 10 | Consumer `signal(mutex)`; `signal(empty)` → empty=1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | `[ ,B,C]` |
| 11 | Unblocked producer now inserts `D` into slot 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0→1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | `[D,B,C]` |

> [!NOTE]
> The invariant `empty + full == N` is **always preserved** (after every operation, $empty + full = N = 3$ in this trace). This is the algebraic correctness proof expected by KTU examiners.

### 3.3 Algebraic Proof of Correctness

The three correctness conditions for a synchronization solution are **mutual exclusion**, **progress**, and **bounded waiting**. For the bounded-buffer problem, the additional condition is that **no item is lost or duplicated**.

$$
\text{Invariant:}\quad \text{empty} + \text{full} = N
$$

#### Proof of Invariant (Producer side)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{empty}_{\text{new}} &= \text{empty}_{\text{old}} - 1 \quad &\text{(from wait(empty))} \\
\text{full}_{\text{new}}  &= \text{full}_{\text{old}} + 1 \quad  &\text{(from signal(full))} \\
\therefore\ \text{empty}_{\text{new}} + \text{full}_{\text{new}} &= (\text{empty}_{\text{old}} - 1) + (\text{full}_{\text{old}} + 1) \\
&= \text{empty}_{\text{old}} + \text{full}_{\text{old}} \\
&= N \quad &\text{(by induction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Proof of Invariant (Consumer side)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{full}_{\text{new}} &= \text{full}_{\text{old}} - 1 \quad &\text{(from wait(full))} \\
\text{empty}_{\text{new}} &= \text{empty}_{\text{old}} + 1 \quad &\text{(from signal(empty))} \\
\therefore\ \text{empty}_{\text{new}} + \text{full}_{\text{new}} &= (\text{empty}_{\text{old}} + 1) + (\text{full}_{\text{old}} - 1) \\
&= \text{empty}_{\text{old}} + \text{full}_{\text{old}} \\
&= N
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the invariant `empty + full = N` is **preserved after every operation**, ensuring the buffer can never be over-filled or over-drained. The base case holds because initially `empty = N` and `full = 0`, so `N + 0 = N`. By induction, the invariant holds for all $k \ge 0$ operations.

### 3.4 Deadlock and Starvation Analysis

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall — Deadlock from `wait` Order:**
> If a producer executes `wait(mutex)` **before** `wait(empty)`, and the buffer is full, the producer holds the `mutex` while waiting. Any consumer trying to enter will block on `wait(mutex)`, but no consumer can free an empty slot (because the consumer must first acquire `mutex`). **Result:** deadlock.

The current solution is **deadlock-free** because:
1. The buffer state can never simultaneously satisfy `empty = 0` and `full = 0` (since `empty + full = N \ge 1$).
2. At least one of `wait(empty)` or `wait(full)` will succeed at any given time.
3. The producer releases `mutex` before potentially blocking, and vice versa.

**Starvation:** With strict FIFO queueing of blocked processes (a standard assumption in KTU textbook solutions), neither the producer nor the consumer starves.

### 3.5 Time-Complexity and Performance

Let $T_{cs}$ be the time spent inside the critical section. The throughput of the system is:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{1}{T_{cs} + T_{ctx}} \quad \text{items per second}
$$

where $T_{ctx}$ is the average context-switch overhead. This shows that **minimizing the critical section** is the key to high performance — a guideline KTU students must mention in design questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRODUCER_BLOCK["PRODUCER PROCESS"]
        direction TB
        P1["Produce Item nextProduced"] --> P2["wait empty"]
        P2 --> P3["wait mutex"]
        P3 --> P4["buffer in equals nextProduced"]
        P4 --> P5["in equals in plus 1 mod N"]
        P5 --> P6["signal mutex"]
        P6 --> P7["signal full"]
        P7 --> P1
    end

    subgraph SHARED_RESOURCES["SHARED CIRCULAR BUFFER"]
        direction TB
        S0["Slot 0"]
        S1["Slot 1"]
        S2["Slot 2"]
        SDOTS["..."]
        SN["Slot N minus 1"]
        S0 --- S1
        S1 --- S2
        S2 --- SDOTS
        SDOTS --- SN
    end

    subgraph CONSUMER_BLOCK["CONSUMER PROCESS"]
        direction TB
        C1["wait full"] --> C2["wait mutex"]
        C2 --> C3["nextConsumed equals buffer out"]
        C3 --> C4["out equals out plus 1 mod N"]
        C4 --> C5["signal mutex"]
        C5 --> C6["signal empty"]
        C6 --> C7["Consume nextConsumed"]
        C7 --> C1
    end

    P4 -. writes to .-> S0
    C3 -. reads from .-> S0
    P7 -. increments .-> SEMFULL["semaphore full"]
    P2 -. decrements .-> SEMEMPTY["semaphore empty"]
    C1 -. decrements .-> SEMFULL
    C6 -. increments .-> SEMEMPTY
    P3 -. acquires .-> SEMMUTEX["semaphore mutex equals 1"]
    C2 -. acquires .-> SEMMUTEX
    P6 -. releases .-> SEMMUTEX
    C5 -. releases .-> SEMMUTEX
```

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram of the Bounded Buffer

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EmptyState: empty equals N full equals 0
    EmptyState --> OneItem: Producer executes wait empty then signal full
    OneItem --> TwoItems: Producer adds second item
    TwoItems --> FullState: Producer adds Nth item
    FullState --> TwoItems: Consumer executes wait full then signal empty
    TwoItems --> OneItem: Consumer removes second item
    OneItem --> EmptyState: Consumer removes last item
    FullState --> FullState: Producer BLOCKED on wait empty
    EmptyState --> EmptyState: Consumer BLOCKED on wait full
```

### 4.3 Sequence of Operations (Time-Ordered)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PR as Producer
    participant SE as Sem empty
    participant SM as Sem mutex
    participant BUF as Shared Buffer
    participant SF as Sem full
    participant CO as Consumer

    Note over PR,CO: Initial: empty equals N, full equals 0, mutex equals 1

    PR->>SE: wait empty
    SE-->>PR: empty becomes N minus 1
    PR->>SM: wait mutex
    SM-->>PR: mutex becomes 0
    PR->>BUF: buffer in equals item
    PR->>BUF: in equals in plus 1 mod N
    PR->>SM: signal mutex
    SM-->>PR: mutex becomes 1
    PR->>SF: signal full
    SF-->>PR: full becomes 1

    CO->>SF: wait full
    SF-->>CO: full becomes 0
    CO->>SM: wait mutex
    SM-->>CO: mutex becomes 0
    CO->>BUF: item equals buffer out
    CO->>BUF: out equals out plus 1 mod N
    CO->>SM: signal mutex
    SM-->>CO: mutex becomes 1
    CO->>SE: signal empty
    SE-->>CO: empty becomes N
```

### 4.4 Bounded-Buffer Layout (Circular Queue)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Slot 0\nA"] --> B["Slot 1\nB"] --> C["Slot 2\nC"] --> D["Slot 3\nempty"] --> E["Slot 4\nempty"]
    E --> F["Slot 0\nA"]
    IN["in pointer = 1"] -. points to .-> B
    OUT["out pointer = 0"] -. points to .-> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**What is the producer–consumer problem? State the role of the three semaphores (`mutex`, `empty`, `full`) used in its solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The producer–consumer problem is a classic synchronization problem in which producer processes generate data items and place them into a fixed-size shared buffer, while consumer processes remove and use those items. The challenge is to prevent the producer from overwriting a full buffer and the consumer from reading an empty buffer, while ensuring that only one process accesses the buffer at a time.

The three semaphores used are:

| Semaphore | Initial Value | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `mutex` | **1** | Binary semaphore providing **mutual exclusion** to the critical section. |
| `empty` | **N** | Counting semaphore tracking the number of **empty slots** in the buffer. |
| `full` | **0** | Counting semaphore tracking the number of **occupied (full) slots** in the buffer. |

*(1 mark — definition; 1.5 marks — three semaphore roles; 0.5 mark — initial values.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Why is the order of `wait(empty)` and `wait(mutex)` important in the producer process? What happens if the order is reversed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The standard order is **`wait(empty)` first, then `wait(mutex)`**. This ensures that the producer blocks **before** acquiring the lock if the buffer is full. If the order is reversed — i.e., `wait(mutex)` is called first — and the buffer is full, the producer holds the `mutex` while attempting `wait(empty)`. Since the consumer must acquire `mutex` to consume and free an empty slot, both processes will be blocked indefinitely. This results in **deadlock**, where neither the producer nor the consumer can proceed.

*(1 mark — correct order; 1 mark — consequence of reversal; 1 mark — deadlock explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern — Module 2:**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and **must include an internal choice** (either-or). Sub-parts are typically `(a)` for **7 marks** and `(b)` for **7 marks**, often involving pseudocode writing and analytical explanation.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Write the complete pseudocode for the bounded-buffer solution using semaphores. Clearly identify the **initial values** of all shared variables and the **wait/signal** operations of both producer and consumer.

**(b) [7 marks]** Trace the algorithm for a buffer of size **N = 3**, showing the values of `empty`, `full`, `mutex`, `in`, and `out` after each operation, for the sequence: Producer inserts 2 items, Consumer removes 1 item, Producer inserts 1 more item.

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*(Full 1 mark for shared data declaration, 2 marks for producer pseudocode, 2 marks for consumer pseudocode, 1 mark for initial values, 1 mark for clear labeling of critical section.)*

```
/* ====== Shared data (declared outside both processes) ====== */
#define N  /* given buffer size */
item  buffer[N];
int   in   = 0;             /* Producer index   */
int   out  = 0;             /* Consumer index   */

semaphore mutex = 1;        /* Mutual exclusion  */
semaphore empty = N;        /* Number of empty slots */
semaphore full  = 0;        /* Number of full slots  */

/* ============== PRODUCER PROCESS ============== */
process Producer {
    while (true) {
        item nextProduced = produce_item();   /* Non-critical */

        wait(empty);                          /* Block if buffer full */
        wait(mutex);                          /* Enter CS            */

        /* ----- Critical Section ----- */
        buffer[in] = nextProduced;
        in = (in + 1) mod N;
        /* ----- End Critical Section ----- */

        signal(mutex);                        /* Exit CS             */
        signal(full);                         /* Inform consumer     */
    }
}

/* ============== CONSUMER PROCESS ============== */
process Consumer {
    while (true) {
        wait(full);                           /* Block if buffer empty */
        wait(mutex);                          /* Enter CS              */

        /* ----- Critical Section ----- */
        item nextConsumed = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) mod N;
        /* ----- End Critical Section ----- */

        signal(mutex);                        /* Exit CS               */
        signal(empty);                        /* Inform producer       */

        consume_item(nextConsumed);            /* Non-critical          */
    }
}
```

**Initial values summary (1 mark):**
- `mutex = 1` (lock free)
- `empty = N` (all slots empty)
- `full  = 0` (no item available)
- `in = 0`, `out = 0`

**Part (b) — 7 marks:** Trace Table for $N = 3$

Initial: `empty = 3, full = 0, mutex = 1, in = 0, out = 0`.

| Step | Operation | `wait/signal` sequence | `empty` | `full` | `mutex` | `in` | `out` | Buffer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial state | — | 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | `[ , , ]` |
| 1 | P produces `X` | `wait(empty)`→`wait(mutex)`→insert→`signal(mutex)`→`signal(full)` | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | `[X, , ]` |
| 2 | P produces `Y` | `wait(empty)`→`wait(mutex)`→insert→`signal(mutex)`→`signal(full)` | 1 | 2 | 1 | 2 | 0 | `[X,Y, ]` |
| 3 | C consumes `X` | `wait(full)`→`wait(mutex)`→remove→`signal(mutex)`→`signal(empty)` | 2 | 1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | `[ ,Y, ]` |
| 4 | P produces `Z` | `wait(empty)`→`wait(mutex)`→insert→`signal(mutex)`→`signal(full)` | 1 | 2 | 1 | 0 (wrap) | 1 | `[ ,Y,Z]` |

> **Invariant Check:** At every step, `empty + full = 3 = N` ✓

---

#### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **bounded-buffer problem** in detail. Discuss why simple integer variables cannot be used to solve it and how semaphores provide an effective solution.

**(b) [7 marks]** With suitable examples, show that the bounded-buffer solution is **deadlock-free** and **starvation-free** under the assumption of FIFO semaphore queues. What happens if the assumption is dropped?

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The bounded-buffer problem arises when a producer and consumer share a common buffer of size `N`. The producer cannot insert if the buffer is full; the consumer cannot remove if the buffer is empty. Concurrent access must be serialized.

**Why simple integer variables fail (4 marks):**

If we use integer variables like `count` to track buffer occupancy, updates like `count = count + 1` and `count = count - 1` are **non-atomic**. Consider this incorrect code:

```
Producer:                       Consumer:
buffer[in] = nextProduced;      item nextConsumed = buffer[out];
in = (in + 1) mod N;            out = (out + 1) mod N;
```

If both run concurrently, the read–modify–write of `in` and `out` can interleave, leading to **race conditions**, data loss, or duplicate consumption. The hardware-level test-and-set is too low-level and not portable.

**How semaphores solve it (3 marks):**

Semaphores provide **atomic** `wait()` and `signal()` operations, implemented in the OS kernel. The two counting semaphores (`empty`, `full`) act as automatic counters that block processes when their count is zero, and the binary semaphore (`mutex`) ensures that the critical section (modifying `in` and `out`) is entered by only one process at a time. Thus:

- The producer waits on `empty` if buffer is full — no overwriting possible.
- The consumer waits on `full` if buffer is empty — no under-reading possible.
- The buffer indices `in` and `out` are protected by `mutex` — no race condition.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Deadlock-freeness (3 marks):**

Assume, for contradiction, that the system reaches a deadlock. Then both producer and consumer are blocked. This means:
- The producer is blocked on `wait(empty)` (so `empty = 0`).
- The consumer is blocked on `wait(full)` (so `full = 0`).
- Both are blocked on `wait(mutex)` OR one of them holds `mutex`.

But the invariant `empty + full = N` must hold. If both `empty = 0` and `full = 0`, then $N = 0$, which contradicts the given $N \ge 1$. Also, since a process releases `mutex` before blocking on the next wait, the held `mutex` situation cannot persist. **Hence, deadlock is impossible.**

**Starvation-freeness under FIFO (2 marks):**

With strict FIFO ordering of the blocked-process queue, every process that calls `wait(S)` on semaphore `S` will eventually reach the head of the queue. Once at the head, if `S > 0`, it proceeds; if `S = 0`, some other process must perform `signal(S)` to unblock the queue. Since the system is deadlock-free and progress is guaranteed, **no process starves**.

**Without FIFO assumption (2 marks):**

If the queue is not FIFO, a process could be perpetually overtaken by newly arriving processes. For example, an aggressive producer might repeatedly fill and signal `full`, while the consumer remains blocked at the back of the queue. The consumer would experience **starvation**. This is why semaphore implementations in real OS kernels (e.g., POSIX `sem_wait`) typically use FIFO or priority-based ordering to prevent starvation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention initial values** of semaphores — **−1 mark** in KTU scripts.
> 2. **Writing the wrong `wait/signal` order** (e.g., `wait(mutex)` before `wait(empty)`) — KTU examiners treat this as a **conceptual error**, deducting up to **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Using Python or any specific language** — The KTU syllabus asks for **pseudocode in OS textbook style**. Writing Python/C code in a theory paper is allowed but loses clarity marks; abstract pseudocode is preferred.
> 4. **Failing to state the invariant** `empty + full = N` — A complete solution **must** mention this for full marks.
> 5. **Not addressing deadlock/starvation** in 14-mark questions — A full-mark answer **must** include a paragraph on correctness.
> 6. **Confusing `in`/`out` with `empty`/`full`** — These are entirely different variables; mixing them up is a common 14-mark question killer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Bounded-Buffer Problem is the synchronization of producers and consumers sharing a buffer of fixed size $N$.
- **Three Semaphores:** `mutex = 1` (binary), `empty = N` (counting), `full = 0` (counting).
- **Wait/Signal Order in Producer:** `wait(empty)` → `wait(mutex)` → CS → `signal(mutex)` → `signal(full)`.
- **Wait/Signal Order in Consumer:** `wait(full)` → `wait(mutex)` → CS → `signal(mutex)` → `signal(empty)`.
- **Invariant (Mandatory for full marks):** `empty + full = N` always.
- **Indices:** `in` and `out` are updated as `(in + 1) mod N` and `(out + 1) mod N` respectively, implementing a **circular buffer**.
- **Correctness Conditions Satisfied:** Mutual exclusion ✓, Progress ✓, Bounded waiting ✓, No lost/duplicated items ✓.
- **Deadlock-Free:** Proven by contradiction using the invariant.
- **Starvation-Free:** Only under FIFO queueing assumption.
- **Real-world uses:** Pipes in Unix, ring buffers in kernel I/O, `BlockingQueue` in Java, audio/video streaming buffers, network packet queues.
- **Critical Section must be minimal:** Only buffer index updates and item transfer belong inside `wait(mutex)` ... `signal(mutex)`. Item production/consumption is **outside** the CS.
- **Trap to avoid:** Reversing `wait(empty)` and `wait(mutex)` causes deadlock; never do this in a KTU answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reader-Writer Locks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Reader-Writer Locks # 📘 Module 2 — Reader-Writer Locks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reader-Writer Lock (RW-Lock)** is a synchronization primitive that allows **concurrent read access** to a shared resource by multiple threads (readers), but enforces **exclusive write access** by only one thread (writer) at a time, while blocking all other readers and writers. A **Reader-Writer Lock** maintains an internal counter and state flag that tracks the number of active readers and the presence of an active writer. It is formally classified under the family of *non-binary semaphores* and *conditional mutual exclusion constructs* in classical synchronization theory (Dijkstra, 1965; Courtois et al., 1971). The lock can be in one of the following logical states at any instant: 1. **Free State** — No thread currently holds the lock. 2. **Read-Locked State** — One or more readers currently hold shared (read) access. 3. **Write-Locked State** — Exactly one writer currently holds exclusive (write) access. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Reading Room Imagine a **university library reading hall** that contains a single shared document (say, the *Master Attendance Register*): - **Scenario A (Readers):** When students (readers) want to *view* the register, multiple students can stand around the desk and read it simultaneously — reading does not modify the register. - **Scenario B (Writer):** When the *Attendance Officer* (writer) needs to *update* the register with new entries, **everyone must leave the desk**. The officer must work alone to prevent anyone from reading half-updated data. This is precisely the contract enforced by a Reader-Writer Lock. The "magic switch" at the entrance of the hall automatically changes its rules: - If the hall is empty → anyone (reader or writer) can enter. - If at least one reader is inside → other readers may enter, but a writer must **wait**. - If a writer is inside → everyone else (readers and writers) must **wait** until the writer finishes. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** Reader-Writer Locks are specifically tested in Module 2 under *Process Synchronization*. The expected answer must always specify (a) the *type* of lock (reader-preference / writer-preference / fair), (b) the *shared variables* used, and (c) a *proof of mutual exclusion*. ### 1.3 Classification of Reader-Writer Solutions | Category | Description | Starvation Risk | |----------|-------------|-----------------| | **Reader-Preference (First)** | If a reader is waiting, new readers bypass waiting writers | Writers may starve | | **Writer-Preference (Second)** | Once a writer arrives, subsequent readers are blocked | Readers may starve | | **Fair (Third / FIFO)** | Requests are served in strict arrival order | No starvation | ### 1.4 POSIX API (For Reference) In **POSIX Pthreads** (IEEE Std 1003.1), the RW-lock API is: ```c pthread_rwlock_t rwlock = PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER; int pthread_rwlock_rdlock(pthread_rwlock_t *rwlock); // acquire read lock int pthread_rwlock_wrlock(pthread_rwlock_t *rwlock); // acquire write lock int pthread_rwlock_unlock(pthread_rwlock_t *rwlock); // release lock ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept A Reader-Writer solution requires **four shared variables** (classical formulation) plus two **binary semaphores / mutexes** for atomic control. ### 2.2 Shared Variables (Reader-Preference) | Variable | Type | Purpose | |----------|------|---------| | `readcount` | `int` (init = 0) | Counts active readers inside the critical section | | `mutex` | `semaphore` (init = 1) | Protects `readcount` from race conditions | | `wrt` | `semaphore` (init = 1) | Enforces mutual exclusion between writers and the *first* reader | ### 2.3 Reader-Preference Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic **Reader Process:** 1. `wait(mutex)` — gain exclusive access to `readcount`. 2. `readcount = readcount + 1`. 3. **If** `readcount == 1` → `wait(wrt)` (first reader locks out writers). 4. `signal(mutex)` — release `readcount`. 5. ⟶ **CRITICAL SECTION** (read shared data). 6. `wait(mutex)`. 7. `readcount = readcount - 1`. 8. **If** `readcount == 0` → `signal(wrt)` (last reader releases writer lock). 9. `signal(mutex)`. **Writer Process:** 1. `wait(wrt)` — gain exclusive access. 2. ⟶ **CRITICAL SECTION** (write shared data). 3. `signal(wrt)`. ### 2.4 Writer-Preference Algorithm — Additional Variables | Variable | Type | Purpose | |----------|------|---------| | `readcount` | `int` (init = 0) | Active readers | | `writecount` | `int` (init = 0) | Waiting writers | | `mutex` | `semaphore` (init = 1) | Protects `readcount` | | `wrt` | `semaphore` (init = 1) | Mutual exclusion | | `readTry` | `semaphore` (init = 1) | Blocks new readers when a writer is waiting | | `rmutex` | `semaphore` (init = 1) | Protects `readcount` and `readTry` interaction | ### 2.5 Why "Preference" Matters — The "Why" Behind Each Step > [!NOTE] > **The Core Dilemma:** When a writer is waiting and new readers keep arriving, the writer can be **starved indefinitely** in reader-preference. The fix in writer-preference is the `readTry` semaphore — readers must check (and pass through) this gate, and a waiting writer can close the gate by `wait(readTry)`. - **Step 3 (Reader-Pref):** Only the *first* reader calls `wait(wrt)`. This is the key insight — subsequent readers piggyback on the first reader's lock. - **Step 8:** Only the *last* reader calls `signal(wrt)`. This guarantees writers see `readcount == 0` before they are unblocked. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Condition Sheet | # | Property | Mathematical / Logical Condition | Units / Type | |---|----------|--------------------------------|--------------| | 1 | Mutual Exclusion (Writers) | At most 1 writer in CS: $\#W \leq 1$ | Integer count | | 2 | Mutual Exclusion (W vs R) | Writer in CS $\Rightarrow$ `readcount = 0` and no reader in CS | Boolean | | 3 | Mutual Exclusion (Readers concurrent) | Many readers allowed: $\#R \geq 0$ unrestricted while $\#W = 0$ | Integer count | | 4 | Progress | If no process is in CS and some want to enter, selection cannot be postponed indefinitely | Logical | | 5 | Bounded Waiting | After a process requests entry, a finite number of processes may enter before it | Bounded integer $N$ | | 6 | Starvation (Reader-Pref) | Writers may wait while readers keep arriving: $W_{wait} = \infty$ possible | Risk indicator | | 7 | Lock State Invariant | $\text{State} = \text{Free} \oplus \text{ReadLocked} \oplus \text{WriteLocked}$ | Enumerated | | 8 | Reader Count Range | $0 \leq \text{readcount} \leq N_{max}$ | Integer | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Reader-Writer Locks are extensively deployed in: - **Databases (InnoDB, PostgreSQL):** MVCC implementations use RW-locks on index pages. - **Linux Kernel:** `rwlock_t`, `seqlock_t`, and `RCU` mechanisms. - **File Systems:** Allowing concurrent reads but serializing writes (e.g., `/proc` files). - **In-Memory Caches:** Memcached, Redis use RW-locks for hash table buckets. - **Compiler Symbol Tables:** Multiple phases read, only one writes. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Pseudocode Derivations (Reader-Preference) ### 3.1.1 Reader Process — Exhaustive Step Walkthrough $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Process } \text{Reader}() \{ \\ &\quad P_1:\ \text{wait}(\textit{mutex}); \quad &&\text{// Atomically acquire readcount lock.} \\ &\quad P_2:\ \textit{readcount} \leftarrow \textit{readcount} + 1; \quad &&\text{// Increment active reader count.} \\ &\quad P_3:\ \text{if } (\textit{readcount} == 1) \text{ then } \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}); \quad &&\text{// First reader blocks writers.} \\ &\quad P_4:\ \text{signal}(\textit{mutex}); \quad &&\text{// Release readcount lock.} \\ &\quad P_5:\ \quad && \\ &\quad P_6:\ \quad \textcolor{blue}{\text{// CRITICAL SECTION (READ)}} \\ &\quad P_7:\ \quad \text{/* perform read on shared data */} \\ &\quad P_8:\ \quad && \\ &\quad P_9:\ \text{wait}(\textit{mutex}); \quad &&\text{// Re-acquire readcount lock for decrement.} \\ &\quad P_{10}:\ \textit{readcount} \leftarrow \textit{readcount} - 1; \quad &&\text{// Decrement active reader count.} \\ &\quad P_{11}:\ \text{if } (\textit{readcount} == 0) \text{ then } \text{signal}(\textit{wrt}); \quad &&\text{// Last reader unblocks a writer.} \\ &\quad P_{12}:\ \text{signal}(\textit{mutex}); \quad &&\text{// Release readcount lock.} \\ &\quad P_{13}:\ \text{while (true) } \{\ \text{read}... \} \\ &\} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.2 Writer Process — Exhaustive Step Walkthrough $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Process } \text{Writer}() \{ \\ &\quad W_1:\ \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}); \quad &&\text{// Acquire exclusive write access.} \\ &\quad W_2:\ \quad && \\ &\quad W_3:\ \quad \textcolor{blue}{\text{// CRITICAL SECTION (WRITE)}} \\ &\quad W_4:\ \quad \text{/* perform write on shared data */} \\ &\quad W_5:\ \quad && \\ &\quad W_6:\ \text{signal}(\textit{wrt}); \quad &&\text{// Release exclusive write access.} \\ &\quad W_7:\ \text{while (true) } \{\ \text{write}... \} \\ &\} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Proof of Mutual Exclusion — Step-by-Step Analysis **Claim:** At most one writer can be in the critical section at any time. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Proof (by contradiction):} \\ &\text{Assume two writers } W_a \text{ and } W_b \text{ are simultaneously in CS.} \\ &\Rightarrow W_a \text{ has executed } W_1: \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}) \text{ and is past it.} \\ &\Rightarrow W_b \text{ has executed } W_1: \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}) \text{ and is past it.} \\ &\Rightarrow \textit{wrt} \text{ has been decremented twice without an intervening signal.} \\ &\text{But } \textit{wrt} \text{ is a binary semaphore, initial value } = 1. \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Second } \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}) \text{ must block } W_b. \quad \text{Contradiction.} \\ &\blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ **Claim:** No reader can be in CS when a writer is in CS. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Proof:} \\ &\text{A reader } R \text{ enters CS only after the first reader executes } \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}). \\ &\text{Once } \textit{wrt} = 0, \text{ no writer can pass } W_1. \\ &\text{Writer enters CS } \iff \textit{wrt} = 1 \text{ and is then set to } 0. \\ &\text{Writer can only acquire } \textit{wrt} = 1 \text{ when last reader executes } \text{signal}(\textit{wrt}). \\ &\text{This requires } \textit{readcount} = 0 \text{ at the moment of signal.} \\ &\text{Therefore, when writer is in CS, } \textit{readcount} = 0. \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Starvation Analysis (Reader-Preference) $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Scenario: } \textit{readcount} = 0,\ \textit{wrt} = 1. \\ &\text{Order of arrival: } W_1, R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_n. \\ &\text{Timeline:} \\ &\quad t_0:\ W_1 \text{ arrives, executes } \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}) \Rightarrow \textit{wrt} = 0. \quad W_1 \text{ enters CS}. \\ &\quad t_1:\ W_1 \text{ finishes, executes } \text{signal}(\textit{wrt}) \Rightarrow \textit{wrt} = 1. \\ &\quad t_2:\ R_1 \text{ arrives, executes } \text{wait}(\textit{mutex}) \Rightarrow \textit{mutex} = 0. \\ &\quad t_3:\ R_1 \text{ increments readcount } \Rightarrow \textit{readcount} = 1. \\ &\quad t_4:\ R_1 \text{ executes } \text{wait}(\textit{wrt}) \Rightarrow \textit{wrt} = 0. \\ &\quad t_5:\ R_1 \text{ executes } \text{signal}(\textit{mutex}). \quad R_1 \text{ enters CS}. \\ &\quad t_6:\ R_2, R_3, \dots, R_n \text{ all enter CS without needing } \textit{wrt}. \\ &\text{If new readers } R_{n+1}, R_{n+2}, \dots \text{ keep arriving, the writer } W_2 \text{ waits forever.} \\ &\text{Hence, } W_2 \text{ is starved.} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Solutions | Criterion | Reader-Preference | Writer-Preference | Fair (FIFO) | |-----------|------------------|-------------------|-------------| | Mutual Exclusion | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Progress | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Bounded Waiting (Readers) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (may starve) | ✅ Yes | | Bounded Waiting (Writers) | ❌ No (may starve) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Throughput (Read-heavy) | High | Lower | Moderate | | Throughput (Write-heavy) | Lower | High | Moderate | | Implementation Complexity | Low | Medium | High | | Semaphores Required | 2 | 5 | 4+ | ## 3.5 State Transition Analysis $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Let } S = (\textit{readcount},\ \textit{wrt},\ \text{State}). \\ &S_{\text{initial}} = (0,\ 1,\ \text{Free}) \\ &S_{\text{read}} = (n \geq 1,\ 0,\ \text{ReadLocked}) \\ &S_{\text{write}} = (0,\ 0,\ \text{WriteLocked}) \\ &\text{Transitions:} \\ &\quad S_{\text{Free}} \xrightarrow{\text{wait(wrt)}} S_{\text{write}} \\ &\quad S_{\text{Free}} \xrightarrow{\text{first reader}} S_{\text{read}} \\ &\quad S_{\text{read}} \xrightarrow{\text{new reader}} S_{\text{read}} \quad (\textit{readcount} \uparrow) \\ &\quad S_{\text{read}} \xrightarrow{\text{last reader exits}} S_{\text{Free}} \\ &\quad S_{\text{write}} \xrightarrow{\text{writer exits}} S_{\text{Free}} \end{aligned} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Reader Process (Reader-Preference) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Reader wants to read] --> B[wait mutex] B --> C[readcount = readcount + 1] C --> D{readcount == 1?} D -- Yes --> E[wait wrt] D -- No --> F[signal mutex] E --> F F --> G[CRITICAL SECTION: Read Data] G --> H[wait mutex] H --> I[readcount = readcount - 1] I --> J{readcount == 0?} J -- Yes --> K[signal wrt] J -- No --> L[signal mutex] K --> L L --> M[End: Reader done] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Writer Process (Reader-Preference) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Writer wants to write] --> B[wait wrt] B --> C[CRITICAL SECTION: Write Data] C --> D[signal wrt] D --> E[End: Writer done] ``` ## 4.3 Lock State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Free Free --> ReadLocked : first reader arrives
wait wrt Free --> WriteLocked : writer arrives
wait wrt ReadLocked --> ReadLocked : more readers arrive
readcount increases ReadLocked --> Free : last reader exits
signal wrt WriteLocked --> Free : writer exits
signal wrt Free --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Writer-Preference Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Reader Arrives] --> R2[wait readTry] R2 --> R3[wait rmutex] R3 --> R4[readcount = readcount + 1] R4 --> R5{readcount == 1?} R5 -- Yes --> R6[wait wrt] R5 -- No --> R7[signal rmutex] R6 --> R7 R7 --> R8[signal readTry] R8 --> R9[CRITICAL SECTION: Read] R9 --> R10[wait rmutex] R10 --> R11[readcount = readcount - 1] R11 --> R12{readcount == 0?} R12 -- Yes --> R13[signal wrt] R12 -- No --> R14[signal rmutex] R13 --> R14 R14 --> R15[Reader Done] W1[Writer Arrives] --> W2[wait wmutex] W2 --> W3[writecount = writecount + 1] W3 --> W4{writecount == 1?} W4 -- Yes --> W5[wait readTry] W4 -- No --> W6[signal wmutex] W5 --> W6 W6 --> W7[wait wrt] W7 --> W8[CRITICAL SECTION: Write] W8 --> W9[signal wrt] W9 --> W10[wait wmutex] W10 --> W11[writecount = writecount - 1] W11 --> W12{writecount == 0?} W12 -- Yes --> W13[signal readTry] W12 -- No --> W14[signal wmutex] W13 --> W14 W14 --> W15[Writer Done] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define a Reader-Writer problem. Why is it different from the classical Bounded-Buffer problem? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The Reader-Writer problem is a synchronization problem where multiple concurrent *reader* processes require simultaneous read-only access to a shared data object, while *writer* processes require exclusive write access. It differs from the Bounded-Buffer problem in two key ways: 1. In Bounded-Buffer, producers and consumers are **symmetric and complementary** (one produces, one consumes, fixed buffer slots). In Reader-Writer, readers and writers have **asymmetric access requirements** — readers can coexist, but writers must be alone. 2. The critical resource in Bounded-Buffer is the **buffer slot count**; in Reader-Writer it is the **shared data object itself**, with the access type (read vs. write) determining the locking policy. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **List any three differences between Reader-Preference and Writer-Preference solutions. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | # | Reader-Preference | Writer-Preference | |---|------------------|-------------------| | 1 | New readers bypass waiting writers | New readers are blocked if a writer is waiting | | 2 | Writers may starve | Readers may starve | | 3 | Uses 2 semaphores (`mutex`, `wrt`) | Uses 5 semaphores (`mutex`, `wrt`, `readTry`, `wmutex`, `rmutex`) | | 4 | Lower overhead, higher read throughput | Higher overhead, fairer under write load | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Reader-Preference Solution** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Write the complete Reader-Preference solution to the Reader-Writer problem using semaphores. Clearly define all shared variables. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Prove that the solution ensures mutual exclusion. Show whether the bounded-waiting condition holds for both readers and writers. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Shared Variables:** | Variable | Type | Initial Value | |----------|------|--------------| | `readcount` | integer | 0 | | `mutex` | semaphore | 1 | | `wrt` | semaphore | 1 | **Reader Process:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{wait}(\textit{mutex}); \\ &\textit{readcount} \leftarrow \textit{readcount} + 1; \\ &\text{if } (\textit{readcount} == 1) \text{ then wait}(\textit{wrt}); \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{mutex}); \\ &\text{// CRITICAL SECTION: read shared data} \\ &\text{wait}(\textit{mutex}); \\ &\textit{readcount} \leftarrow \textit{readcount} - 1; \\ &\text{if } (\textit{readcount} == 0) \text{ then signal}(\textit{wrt}); \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{mutex}); \end{aligned} $$ **Writer Process:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{wait}(\textit{wrt}); \\ &\text{// CRITICAL SECTION: write shared data} \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{wrt}); \end{aligned} $$ **Mark Split:** Variables (2M), Reader logic (3M), Writer logic (1M), Final entry (1M). #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Mutual Exclusion Proof:** - *Between writers:* The `wrt` semaphore is binary (initial value 1). Two writers cannot simultaneously pass `wait(wrt)` because the second `wait` will block. ✅ - *Between writer and reader:* A writer passes `wait(wrt)` only when `wrt = 1`. This happens only when a reader executes `signal(wrt)`, which requires `readcount = 0`. So no reader is in CS. ✅ - *Between readers:* Multiple readers allowed by design — this is intentional, not a violation. ✅ **Bounded Waiting:** - *For readers:* ✅ A waiting reader will be admitted once the current readers finish (readcount drops to 0 and the last one signals `wrt`). - *For writers:* ❌ **Bounded waiting FAILS.** In a continuous stream of arriving readers, the writer may starve indefinitely. Hence, reader-preference satisfies mutual exclusion and progress but **not** bounded waiting for writers. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Writer-Preference Solution** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Write the Writer-Preference solution to the Reader-Writer problem using semaphores. Define all shared variables clearly. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Prove mutual exclusion and explain how writer starvation is prevented. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Shared Variables:** | Variable | Type | Initial Value | |----------|------|--------------| | `readcount` | integer | 0 | | `writecount` | integer | 0 | | `mutex` | semaphore | 1 | | `wrt` | semaphore | 1 | | `readTry` | semaphore | 1 | | `rmutex` | semaphore | 1 | **Reader Process:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{wait}(\textit{readTry}); \\ &\text{wait}(\textit{rmutex}); \\ &\textit{readcount} \leftarrow \textit{readcount} + 1; \\ &\text{if } (\textit{readcount} == 1) \text{ then wait}(\textit{wrt}); \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{rmutex}); \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{readTry}); \\ &\text{// CRITICAL SECTION: read} \\ &\text{wait}(\textit{rmutex}); \\ &\textit{readcount} \leftarrow \textit{readcount} - 1; \\ &\text{if } (\textit{readcount} == 0) \text{ then signal}(\textit{wrt}); \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{rmutex}); \end{aligned} $$ **Writer Process:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{wait}(\textit{wmutex}); \\ &\textit{writecount} \leftarrow \textit{writecount} + 1; \\ &\text{if } (\textit{writecount} == 1) \text{ then wait}(\textit{readTry}); \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{wmutex}); \\ &\text{wait}(\textit{wrt}); \\ &\text{// CRITICAL SECTION: write} \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{wrt}); \\ &\text{wait}(\textit{wmutex}); \\ &\textit{writecount} \leftarrow \textit{writecount} - 1; \\ &\text{if } (\textit{writecount} == 0) \text{ then signal}(\textit{readTry}); \\ &\text{signal}(\textit{wmutex}); \end{aligned} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Mutual Exclusion:** Identical to Reader-Preference argument — `wrt` is binary; the first reader locks it, the last reader unlocks it. Writer and reader CS are mutually exclusive. **Writer Starvation Prevention:** - When the *first* waiting writer arrives, it executes `wait(readTry)`, setting `readTry = 0`. - This blocks **all subsequent new readers** at their very first `wait(readTry)`. - Only readers already inside the CS (those who passed `readTry` before the writer arrived) can finish. - Once the last of these finishes (`readcount = 0`), it signals `wrt`, allowing the writer to enter. - Therefore, the writer's wait is bounded by the number of *currently executing* readers — not by an unbounded stream of new arrivals. ✅ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to define initial values** of semaphores and counters (−1 to −2 marks). > 2. **Using only `wait(wrt)` without `readcount` checks** — this becomes the simple binary semaphore solution, not the Reader-Writer solution (−3 marks). > 3. **Confusing `readcount == 1` (first reader) with `readcount == 0` (last reader)** — wrong placement of `wait(wrt)` and `signal(wrt)` breaks mutual exclusion (−2 to −4 marks). > 4. **Omitting the bounded-waiting analysis** in Part (b) — the answer must explicitly state that reader-preference *fails* bounded waiting for writers (−2 marks). > 5. **Not explaining the role of `readTry`** in writer-preference — examiners expect the starvation-prevention argument to be written out, not just asserted (−2 marks). > 6. **Writing answer in plain prose without algorithm/pseudocode block** — KTU expects a clean, structured algorithm presentation (−1 to −2 marks on presentation). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Reader-Writer Lock** is a synchronization primitive allowing concurrent reads but exclusive writes. - **Four canonical solutions exist:** (1) Reader-Preference, (2) Writer-Preference, (3) Fair/FIFO, (4) POSIX Pthread `pthread_rwlock_t`. -
