**Key invariant:** Writer in CS $\iff$ `readcount = 0` AND `wrt = 0`. - **First reader** locks the writer out by calling `wait(wrt)`; **last reader** unlocks it via `signal(wrt)`. - **Reader-Preference** uses 2 semaphores; **Writer-Preference** uses 5 semaphores (adds `readTry`, `wmutex`, `rmutex`). - **Mutual exclusion** holds in both solutions; **Progress** holds in both. - **Bounded waiting** for **writers** holds only in Writer-Preference / Fair solutions — **not** in Reader-Preference. - **State machine** has 3 states: Free, ReadLocked, WriteLocked — transitions are atomic and driven by semaphore operations. - **Real-world use:** Databases (row/page locks), Linux kernel (`rwlock_t`, `seqlock`), in-memory caches, file systems. - **POSIX API:** `pthread_rwlock_rdlock`, `pthread_rwlock_wrlock`, `pthread_rwlock_unlock`. - **For KTU answers:** always (1) define variables with types and initial values, (2) provide clean pseudocode, (3) state which property is satisfied/violated, (4) justify with a brief proof. - **Memory aid (KTU-style):** "*First reader locks, last reader unlocks, writer waits alone.*"

topics.title: Case Study: Linux Kernel Synchronization Methods - Spin Locks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Linux Kernel Synchronization Methods - Spin Locks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Spin Lock** is a low-level, busy-wait synchronization primitive used inside the Linux kernel to protect critical sections from concurrent access by multiple threads or CPUs. Unlike mutexes, a spin lock does not put the contending thread/process to sleep; instead, it repeatedly executes a tight atomic loop ("spinning") until the lock becomes available. Spin locks are intended exclusively for **non-preemptible kernel contexts** and are used when the lock is expected to be held for very short durations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this case study maps to the application of classical synchronization techniques (Peterson's, Test-and-Set, Compare-and-Swap) to a real, production-grade operating system kernel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Spin locks are the **canonical real-world example** of Compare-and-Swap (CAS) and Test-and-Set (TAS) atomic instructions. Any exam question on this topic expects you to map Linux internals back to the classical algorithms taught in Module 2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a single-stall bathroom in a busy office.

- A **mutex** is like a key with a number system. If the bathroom is occupied, you leave your name at the door and go back to your desk to sleep. The receptionist wakes you when the key is free.
- A **spin lock** is like the same single-stall bathroom, but instead of leaving and going back to work, you stand at the door, **continuously knocking and peeking through the crack** every few microseconds, refusing to leave, until the person inside finally unlocks the door.

> [!NOTE]
> Standing and knocking wastes your own energy (CPU cycles), but it saves the **overhead of context switching**, which is far more expensive in kernel mode. Linux uses this trade-off aggressively.

## 1.3 Core Properties of Linux Spin Locks

| Property | Value/Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Lock Holder State** | **Non-preemptible** while holding the lock |
| **Waiting Behavior** | **Busy-wait** (spins) — does NOT sleep |
| **Allowed Holders** | Only **one** thread at a time |
| **Interrupt Safety** | Variants exist for IRQ context (`spin_lock_irqsave`) |
| **Context** | **Process context** (SMP-aware) and **interrupt context** |
| **Typical Hold Time** | **< few microseconds** |
| **Underlying Primitive** | **Atomic** compare-and-swap / test-and-set |

## 1.4 Why Spin Locks Exist in Linux

Linux, as an SMP (Symmetric Multi-Processing) kernel, must guarantee that data structures shared across CPUs are accessed atomically. Disabling interrupts alone is not enough on multi-core systems because another CPU can still access the data. Spin locks solve this with a combination of:

1. **Hardware atomic instructions** (provided by x86, ARM, etc.)
2. **Compiler and CPU memory barriers**
3. **Preemption disabling** on the holding CPU

> [!NOTE]
> On **uni-processor (UP) kernels**, spin locks compile down to nothing more than preemption disabling because no other CPU can race. On **SMP kernels**, the atomic spin loop becomes essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Atomic Primitives

Linux spin locks are built on top of the hardware-supported atomic instructions covered earlier in Module 2. The kernel abstracts these behind architecture-independent interfaces.

### 2.1.1 Test-and-Set (TAS)

A single, indivisible operation that:
1. Reads the current value of a memory location.
2. Writes a new value to that location.
3. Returns the original value.

Mathematically, for lock variable $L$ and desired new value $1$:

$$\text{TAS}(L): \quad \{ \text{old} \leftarrow L;\ L \leftarrow 1;\ \text{return old} \}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On the lock itself**, $L = 0$ means "free" and $L = 1$ means "held". The caller knows it acquired the lock only when TAS returns the value $0$.

### 2.1.2 Compare-and-Swap (CAS)

A more powerful atomic instruction:

$$\text{CAS}(L, \text{expected}, \text{desired}): \quad \text{if } L = \text{expected, then } L \leftarrow \text{desired, return true; else return false}$$

CAS is the **foundation of modern ticket, queued, and MCS spin locks** in Linux.

## 2.2 Classic Test-and-Set Spin Lock (Naive)

The simplest spin lock implementation that students must master:

$$\text{acquire}(L): \quad \text{while } \text{TAS}(L) = 1 \text{ do } \{ \text{nop} \}$$

$$\text{release}(L): \quad L \leftarrow 0$$

```text
// PSEUDOCODE — NOT actual C source — purely algorithmic
acquire(L):
    while test_and_set(L) == 1:
        spin  // burn CPU cycles

release(L):
    L = 0   // store must be a normal write
```

> [!WARNING]
> The naive TAS spin lock suffers from **unbounded starvation** and **cache-line bouncing** (discussed in Section 2.5). Real Linux kernels use much more sophisticated variants.

## 2.3 Linux Ticket Lock (Pre-2.6.25 → improved version)

Linux used a **fair, FIFO ticket spin lock** (also called the "Anderson queue" or "two-counter ticket lock"). Two counters are used:

- $L.\text{owner}$ — the ticket number currently holding the lock
- $L.\text{next}$  — the next ticket to be issued

$$\text{acquire}(L): \quad \text{myTicket} \leftarrow \text{FAA}(L.\text{next}, 1);\ \text{while } L.\text{owner} \neq \text{myTicket} \text{ do } \{ \text{spin} \}$$

$$\text{release}(L): \quad L.\text{owner} \leftarrow L.\text{owner} + 1$$

where $\text{FAA}$ denotes Fetch-And-Add.

> [!NOTE]
> Ticket locks guarantee **bounded waiting** — every waiter eventually gets a turn — which satisfies KTU's discussion of fair synchronization.

## 2.4 Linux Queued / MCS Spin Lock (Modern, kernel ≥ 4.x)

Modern Linux uses a **per-CPU queued spin lock** derived from the **MCS (Mellor-Crummey, Scott) lock** to eliminate cache-line bouncing.

Each waiting CPU enqueues its own node in a per-CPU linked list. The lock holder only spins on its **own local** node, not on a globally-shared lock word.

$$\text{enqueue}(L, \text{node}): \quad \text{node.next} \leftarrow \text{NULL};\ \text{prev} \leftarrow \text{XCHG}(L.\text{tail}, \text{node})$$

$$\text{if prev} = \text{NULL}: \quad \text{node.locked} \leftarrow \text{false} \quad \text{(caller became owner directly)}$$

$$\text{else}: \quad \text{prev.next} \leftarrow \text{node};\ \text{while node.locked = true do } \{ \text{spin} \}$$

$$\text{release}(L, \text{node}): \quad \text{if node.next} = \text{NULL}: \quad \text{if CAS}(L.\text{tail}, \text{node}, \text{NULL}) = \text{true} \quad \text{return}; \quad \text{while node.next = NULL do } \{ \text{spin} \}$$

$$\text{node.next.locked} \leftarrow \text{false}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MCS locks are the state-of-the-art** and are heavily tested in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions for 14-mark problems.

## 2.5 The Cache-Line Bouncing Problem

On multi-core systems, every CPU writing to the lock variable $L$ invalidates the corresponding **cache line** on all other CPUs (MESI / MOESI protocol). The cost of this coherence traffic is:

$$T_{\text{acquire}} \approx T_{\text{TAS}} + (N_{\text{waiters}} \times T_{\text{cache-miss}})$$

MCS locks convert this to:

$$T_{\text{acquire}}^{\text{MCS}} \approx T_{\text{TAS}} + T_{\text{local-cache-check}}$$

yielding a complexity reduction from $O(N)$ to $O(1)$ for cache traffic per lock acquisition.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Test-and-Set** | $\text{old} \leftarrow L;\ L \leftarrow 1$ | Returns old value atomically |
| **Compare-and-Swap** | $\text{if } L = e,\ L \leftarrow d$ | Returns success boolean |
| **Fetch-and-Add** | $\text{old} \leftarrow L;\ L \leftarrow L + \Delta$ | Used in ticket locks |
| **Atomic Exchange** | $\text{XCHG}(a, b) \Leftrightarrow a \leftrightarrow b$ | Used in MCS enqueue |
| **Naive lock complexity** | $O(N)$ cache traffic | $N$ = number of waiters |
| **MCS lock complexity** | $O(1)$ cache traffic | Spins on local node |
| **TAS fairness** | Unbounded starvation possible | Favourable CPU may always win |
| **Ticket lock fairness** | Bounded, FIFO | One counter-pair |
| **Preemption cost vs spin cost** | $\text{spin wins if } T_{\text{hold}} \ll T_{\text{context-switch}}$ | Linux heuristic |
| **Memory barrier types** | rmb, wmb, mb, smp_mb, smp_rmb, smp_wmb | Enforced inside spin_lock/unlock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Almost every shared structure in the Linux kernel — scheduler runqueues, inode tables, slab allocators, network stack queues — is protected by a spin lock or one of its reader-writer / IRQ variants. Understanding spin locks is essential for any kernel developer, embedded systems engineer, or HPC programmer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Correctness Argument for Ticket Spin Locks

We will formally prove that the ticket lock satisfies **mutual exclusion** and **bounded waiting**.

### Step 1 — Define the invariant

Let $L = (L.\text{owner}, L.\text{next})$. The invariant is:

$$L.\text{owner} \leq \text{tickets issued} < L.\text{next}$$

At quiescence (no in-flight acquire), this becomes $L.\text{owner} = L.\text{next}$ (the lock is free).

### Step 2 — Acquire step (Fetch-and-Add)

A thread entering $\text{acquire}$ performs:

$$\text{myTicket} \leftarrow L.\text{next};\quad L.\text{next} \leftarrow L.\text{next} + 1$$

Because FAA is atomic, exactly one thread gets each integer value, and the sequence of returned tickets is strictly increasing and **globally ordered**.

### Step 3 — Exit condition

The thread exits the spin loop when $L.\text{owner} = \text{myTicket}$. Until that moment, it spins. Only one ticket number equals the current owner at any time (because $L.\text{owner}$ is updated monotonically by exactly one thread at a time, the releaser).

### Step 4 — Mutual exclusion

If two threads $A$ and $B$ both believed they were inside the critical section, their tickets would both equal $L.\text{owner}$, which is impossible because the relaser increments $L.\text{owner}$ by exactly $1$ per release. $\blacksquare$

### Step 5 — Bounded waiting

Let $N$ be the number of threads currently spinning. The owner of the lock will release it after finite time, incrementing $L.\text{owner}$ by $1$. Within at most $N$ such releases, any specific thread's ticket will be reached. Hence waiting is **bounded by $N$** (FIFO order). $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Naive TAS Starvation Bound

A single TAS loop can be starved if the lock is released and re-acquired faster on a sibling CPU than the spinning CPU's coherence-fetch latency.

Let $T_{\text{coh}}$ be the time to fetch the updated cache line, and let $T_{\text{hold}}$ be the critical section duration. The probability that any given spinning CPU wins the race is:

$$P_{\text{win}} = \frac{T_{\text{coh}}}{T_{\text{coh}} + T_{\text{hold}}}$$

For $N$ spinners in a probabilistic worst case, the expected wait time can be unbounded as $P_{\text{win}} \to 0$ (i.e. when $T_{\text{coh}} \ll T_{\text{hold}}$), demonstrating the **theoretical starvation** of the naive TAS lock.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**. Examiners love it because it connects hardware cost models to algorithmic fairness.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Spin Lock Variants vs. Properties

| Property | Naive TAS | Ticket Lock | MCS / Queued Lock |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mutual Exclusion** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Bounded Waiting / Fairness** | ❌ No (unbounded starvation) | ✅ Yes (FIFO) | ✅ Yes (FIFO) |
| **Cache-Line Bouncing** | Severe (every CPU writes $L$) | Reduced (only $L.\text{owner}$ writer) | Eliminated (local spin) |
| **Cache Traffic per Acquire** | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ reads, $O(1)$ writes | $O(1)$ |
| **Memory Footprint** | One word | Two words | One word + per-CPU node |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Trivial | Moderate | High |
| **Used in Modern Linux** | No (educational) | Historical (early 2.6) | ✅ Yes (current) |
| **Preemption-Safe?** | Requires `preempt_disable()` | Yes | Yes |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A quad-core system runs a workload where $4$ worker threads contend for a spin lock holding time $T_{\text{hold}} = 200\ \text{ns}$. The cache-coherence round-trip is $T_{\text{coh}} = 40\ \text{ns}$. Estimate the throughput of the naive TAS lock vs. the MCS lock.

### Solution — Naive TAS

Using the analytical formula:

$$P_{\text{win}} = \frac{40}{40 + 200} = \frac{40}{240} = \frac{1}{6}$$

With $N = 4$ contenders, expected acquisitions per second per thread is approximately:

$$R_{\text{naive}} \approx \frac{P_{\text{win}}}{T_{\text{hold}} \cdot N} = \frac{1/6}{200 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 4} \approx 2.08 \times 10^{5}\ \text{acquisitions/s/thread}$$

### Solution — MCS

Each thread's spin is purely local; the global traffic is constant:

$$R_{\text{MCS}} \approx \frac{1}{T_{\text{hold}} \cdot N_{\text{serialized}}} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 4} = 1.25 \times 10^{6}\ \text{acquisitions/s}$$

### Comparison

$$\frac{R_{\text{MCS}}}{R_{\text{naive}}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{6}}{2.08 \times 10^{5}} \approx 6.0\times$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MCS lock delivers roughly a **6× throughput improvement** for this workload, illustrating why the Linux kernel invested in queuing spin locks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords are used as node names. Labels contain clean uppercase text only.

## 4.2 High-Level Architecture of Linux Spin Lock Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread Enters Critical Section] --> B[preempt_disable called]
    B --> C[Acquire atomic lock word via CAS or XCHG]
    C --> D{Lock acquired successfully}
    D -- YES --> E[Execute critical section code]
    D -- NO  --> F[Spin on local MCS node or shared lock word]
    F --> G[Check lock status repeatedly]
    G --> D
    E --> H[Release lock with atomic store]
    H --> I[Memory barrier smp_mb]
    I --> J[preempt_enable called]
    J --> K[Thread exits critical section]
```

## 4.3 Linux Spin Lock Variant Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPIN[Linux Spin Lock Family]
    SPIN --> BAS[Basic spin_lock]
    SPIN --> IRQ[IRQ Variants]
    SPIN --> BH[BH Variants]
    SPIN --> RW[Reader Writer Variants]
    SPIN --> DBG[Debug Variant]

    BAS --> BASE_BH[spin_lock_bh]
    IRQ --> IRQ_SAVE[spin_lock_irqsave]
    IRQ --> IRQ_DIS[spin_lock_irq]
    BH --> BH_LOCK[spin_lock_bh]
    RW --> RD[read_lock]
    RW --> WR[write_lock]
    DBG --> DBG_LOCK[debug_spin_lock]
```

## 4.4 State Diagram of a Linux Ticket Spin Lock

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Free : lock initialized
    Free --> Held : FAA returns ticket i
    Held --> Held : next thread spins waiting for owner
    Held --> Held : owner still inside critical section
    Held --> Free : owner writes owner equals owner plus 1
    Free --> Held : next FAA issued ticket j where j equals owner
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram, the state transition "Held → Free" corresponds to the **release** path, and the "Free → Held" transition corresponds to the **acquire** path. All other "Held → Held" self-loops represent contending threads **spinning**.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — MCS Lock Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU1[CPU 1 Caller] --> ENQ1[Enqueue local MCS node]
    CPU2[CPU 2 Caller] --> ENQ2[Enqueue local MCS node]
    CPU3[CPU 3 Caller] --> ENQ3[Enqueue local MCS node]
    ENQ1 --> TAIL[Atomic XCHG with global tail pointer]
    ENQ2 --> TAIL
    ENQ3 --> TAIL
    TAIL --> OWN[First caller becomes lock owner]
    OWN --> CRIT[Execute critical section]
    CRIT --> REL[Release path: unlock next waiter]
    REL --> NEXT[Set successor node locked flag to false]
    NEXT --> NEXTWAKE[Successor exits local spin loop]
    NEXTWAKE --> CRIT2[Successor executes critical section]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The crucial insight is that each CPU spins on **its own private MCS node** stored in local memory, so the global cache line is touched only at enqueue and dequeue. This is the engineering reason behind the **6× throughput** improvement we derived in Section 3.4.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define a **spin lock**. Why is it preferred over a **mutex** in certain Linux kernel contexts?

**Model Answer:**

A **spin lock** is a synchronization primitive where a thread attempting to acquire an already-held lock **busy-waits (spins)** in a tight loop, repeatedly checking the lock's status, instead of being descheduled.

It is preferred over a mutex in kernel contexts where:
1. The lock is held for **very short durations** (typically less than the cost of a context switch).
2. The context is **non-sleepable** (e.g., interrupt handlers, scheduler code, softirqs).
3. Preemption must remain **disabled** while the lock is held.

Spin locks avoid the high overhead of context switches and wake-up latency, at the cost of burning CPU cycles during contention.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is **cache-line bouncing**? How does the **MCS lock** mitigate it?

**Model Answer:**

**Cache-line bouncing** is the performance penalty that occurs on SMP systems when multiple CPUs repeatedly write to the same memory location (e.g., a shared lock word). Each write invalidates the cache line on all other CPUs, forcing expensive cache-coherence traffic.

The **MCS (Mellor-Crummey, Scott) lock** mitigates this by giving each waiting CPU its **own private node** in a linked list. Spinning occurs on the **local node's `locked` flag**, so the global lock word is touched only once at enqueue and once at dequeue, reducing coherence traffic from $O(N)$ to $O(1)$ per acquire.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** **(a)** With a clear diagram, explain the **Linux ticket spin lock** algorithm. Prove that it guarantees **mutual exclusion** and **bounded (FIFO) waiting**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **naive Test-and-Set (TAS) spin lock** with the **MCS queued spin lock** in terms of:
- Fairness / starvation
- Cache-line bouncing behavior
- Memory footprint
- Implementation complexity

Include the **time complexity per acquire** for both variants. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The ticket lock maintains a pair of counters $(L.\text{owner}, L.\text{next})$ in a single atomic word. The algorithm is:

```text
// PSEUDOCODE — algorithmic description only
acquire(L):
    myTicket = fetch_and_add(L.next, 1)   // atomic
    while L.owner != myTicket:
        spin

release(L):
    L.owner = L.owner + 1                 // atomic store
```

**Mutual Exclusion Proof:**

We assert the invariant that at any time, exactly one thread satisfies the condition $L.\text{owner} = \text{myTicket}$. Suppose two threads $A$ and $B$ both believed they held the lock. Then $A.\text{myTicket} = L.\text{owner} = B.\text{myTicket}$, which contradicts the fact that FAA returns strictly increasing unique values to each caller. Hence, at most one thread can be inside the critical section.

**Bounded Waiting Proof:**

A thread $T$ that received ticket $k$ must wait for the owner counter to advance from its current value to $k$. The owner counter advances by exactly $1$ per release, and there are finitely many threads ahead of $T$ in the FIFO order. Hence $T$ will be admitted within at most $(k - L.\text{owner})$ releases, which is finite. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

| Criterion | Naive TAS Lock | MCS Queued Lock |
|---|---|---|
| Fairness | **Unbounded starvation** possible | **FIFO / bounded waiting** guaranteed |
| Cache-Line Bouncing | Severe; all waiters write global $L$ | Eliminated; each spins on local node |
| Time Complexity (cache traffic per acquire) | $O(N)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Memory Footprint | One word | One global word + per-CPU node |
| Implementation Complexity | Trivial | High (linked list, atomic enqueue) |

Conclusion: MCS locks are the preferred choice in modern Linux kernels because they eliminate cache-line bouncing and provide guaranteed fairness at the cost of higher implementation complexity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** **(a)** Describe the **Linux queued spin lock** (MCS) algorithm. Show the **enqueue**, **spin**, and **release** paths with a functional block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quad-core system runs $4$ contending worker threads on a shared counter protected by a spin lock. The cache-coherence latency is $T_{\text{coh}} = 50\ \text{ns}$ and the critical section holds the lock for $T_{\text{hold}} = 250\ \text{ns}$.
- Compute the **per-thread acquisition probability** for the naive TAS lock.
- Compute the **per-thread throughput** for the naive TAS lock and the MCS lock.
- Show that the MCS lock achieves a higher throughput and explain why. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The MCS lock maintains a **per-CPU node** and a **global tail pointer**. Each node has a `locked` flag and a `next` pointer.

**Enqueue path:** The caller atomically swaps its node into the global tail:

```text
// PSEUDOCODE — algorithmic description only
node.next = NULL
prev = XCHG(L.tail, node)
```

If `prev` is NULL, the caller is the **first acquirer** and is granted the lock immediately. Otherwise, the caller sets `prev.next = node` and spins on its own `node.locked` flag.

**Spin path:** The caller polls `node.locked` from its local memory; no global cache line is touched.

**Release path:** The owner inspects `node.next`. If a successor exists, it sets `successor.locked = false`, releasing the next waiter. If no successor exists, the owner attempts to CAS the tail pointer to NULL, claiming the lock is now free.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread calls acquire] --> B[Initialize local MCS node]
    B --> C[Atomic XCHG with global tail]
    C --> D{prev NULL}
    D -- YES --> E[Lock acquired, no spin needed]
    D -- NO  --> F[Link prev.next to my node]
    F --> G[Spin on local node.locked]
    G --> H{node.locked FALSE}
    H -- NO  --> G
    H -- YES --> I[Lock acquired via handoff]
    I --> J[Execute critical section]
    J --> K[Release path: check node.next]
    K --> L{Successor exists}
    L -- YES --> M[Set successor.locked to FALSE]
    L -- NO  --> N[CAS tail to NULL]
    M --> O[Done]
    N --> O
```

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Acquisition probability for naive TAS:**

$$P_{\text{win}} = \frac{T_{\text{coh}}}{T_{\text{coh}} + T_{\text{hold}}} = \frac{50}{50 + 250} = \frac{50}{300} = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 2 — Per-thread throughput for naive TAS:**

$$R_{\text{naive}} = \frac{P_{\text{win}}}{N \cdot T_{\text{hold}}} = \frac{1/6}{4 \times 250 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{6 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{5}\ \text{ops/s/thread}$$

**Step 3 — Per-thread throughput for MCS:**

The MCS lock serializes the global lock word accesses:

$$R_{\text{MCS}} = \frac{1}{N \cdot T_{\text{hold}}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 250 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.0 \times 10^{6}\ \text{ops/s/thread}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup:**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{R_{\text{MCS}}}{R_{\text{naive}}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{6}}{1.67 \times 10^{5}} = 6.0\times$$

**Explanation:** The MCS lock achieves higher throughput because it confines all spinning to **local CPU cache lines**, eliminating the expensive cache-coherence traffic that dominates naive TAS lock performance. The global lock word is touched only at enqueue and dequeue, which is an $O(1)$ cost regardless of the number of waiters.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "spin lock" with "mutex":** Many students wrongly state that spin locks "block" the process. They do **not** — they busy-wait. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Ignoring the preemption-disable step:** A correct Linux spin lock answer must mention that `preempt_disable()` is paired with `preempt_enable()` around the critical section.
> 3. **Forgetting memory barriers:** The `smp_mb()` (or architecture-specific `dmb`, `mfence`) is required inside the unlock path. Losing **1 mark** if omitted.
> 4. **Mixing up MCS with Ticket:** Ticket locks still have $O(N)$ cache reads because the owner field is shared. Only MCS is $O(1)$ in coherence traffic.
> 5. **No C code allowed in non-CS theory questions:** For KTU theory papers, write the algorithm in **pseudocode or English**, not in full C syntax.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Spin lock = busy-wait** synchronization; do **not** sleep, do **not** schedule.
- Always paired with **`preempt_disable()` / `preempt_enable()`**.
- Cannot be held while calling **anything that may sleep** (e.g., `kmalloc(GFP_KERNEL)`, `mutex_lock`, `schedule`).
- **TAS** = Test-and-Set (atomic read-modify-write).
- **CAS** = Compare-and-Swap (atomic conditional write).
- **Naive TAS lock:** simple, but **unfair** and **cache-line-bouncing-prone**.
- **Ticket lock:** fair, FIFO, but still $O(N)$ coherence traffic.
- **MCS / Queued lock:** **state of the art** in Linux; $O(1)$ cache traffic per acquire; spins on local node.
- **IRQ-safe variants:** `spin_lock_irqsave()` disables IRQs on local CPU to prevent deadlock with interrupt handlers.
- **Reader-writer spin lock (`rwlock_t`):** allows concurrent readers but exclusive writers.
- **Memory barriers:** `smp_mb()`, `smp_rmb()`, `smp_wmb()` are mandatory to prevent compiler/CPU reordering inside the lock region.
- **Real-world hold time:** < few microseconds, else context-switch overhead of a mutex becomes cheaper.
- **On UP kernels:** spin lock = preemption disable (atomic spin is a no-op).
- **Linux source location:** `include/linux/spinlock.h`, `kernel/locking/spinlock.c`.
- **Starvation proof:** naive TAS has unbounded starvation; ticket and MCS both have bounded waiting.
- **Throughput formula for naive TAS:** $R \approx P_{\text{win}} / (N \cdot T_{\text{hold}})$.
- **Throughput formula for MCS:** $R \approx 1 / (N \cdot T_{\text{hold}})$.
- **KTU favourite 14-mark question:** "Compare ticket lock vs MCS lock" with derivation of fairness and cache traffic.
- **Mermaid nodes:** always alphanumeric, prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`).
- **Latex:** always one blank line before and after every `$$` display block.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semaphores
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semaphore (Dijkstra, 1965):** A semaphore is a **non-negative integer synchronization variable** accessed only through two standard atomic (indivisible) operations traditionally called **P (Proberen / Wait / Down)** and **V (Verhogen / Signal / Up)**. It is a fundamental OS kernel construct used to control concurrent access to shared resources and to coordinate the execution of cooperating processes or threads.

A semaphore **S** is fundamentally a protected integer variable that supports exactly two operations. Its value can never become negative under correct kernel implementation. The term "atomic" means the test-and-modify sequence cannot be interrupted by any other process — this is guaranteed either by hardware atomic instructions (e.g., `test_and_set`, `compare_and_swap`) or by kernel-level disabling of interrupts.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Bathroom Key Holder

Imagine a public washroom with only **3 keys hanging on a board** outside the door. The rule is simple:

- **To enter:** A person must take a key. If the board has 0 keys, they must **wait** in a queue.
- **To exit:** The person **returns** the key to the board. The next person in the queue (if any) immediately wakes up and takes it.

Here:
- The **number of keys** = value of the semaphore.
- **Taking a key** = `wait(S)` operation (decrement).
- **Returning a key** = `signal(S)` operation (increment).
- **Queue outside the door** = the OS wait/blocked queue.

This analogy beautifully captures the two flavors of semaphores:

| Real-World Analogy | OS Semaphore Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| A washroom with **1 key** (toilet lock) | **Binary Semaphore (S ∈ {0, 1})** | Mutual exclusion, like a mutex |
| A parking lot with **N slots** | **Counting Semaphore (S ≥ 0)** | Resource count, allows up to N concurrent users |

## 1.3 Why Semaphores Exist

> [!NOTE]
> In a multiprogramming environment, multiple processes often share resources (printers, files, memory buffers). Without synchronization, **race conditions** occur, where outcome depends on the uncontrollable interleaving of process execution. Semaphores were introduced by **Edsger W. Dijkstra (1965)** as a tractable, systematic solution to the **Critical Section Problem** that scales beyond just two processes.

## 1.4 Types of Semaphores (KTU 2024 Syllabus High-Yield)

1. **Binary Semaphore (Mutex Semaphore):** Value can be either `0` or `1`. Used for **mutual exclusion**.
2. **Counting Semaphore:** Value can range over an unrestricted non-negative integer domain. Used for **resource counting** (e.g., 10 database connections).
3. **Strong Semaphore:** Defines process selection order from the blocked queue (typically **FIFO** — fair).
4. **Weak Semaphore:** Does not guarantee order (selection is undefined).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Two Atomic Operations

The two fundamental semaphore operations are the **P (wait)** and **V (signal)** primitives. KTU examiners expect students to be able to reproduce these in pseudo-code or describe them precisely.

### 2.1.1 The `wait(S)` / `P(S)` Operation

This operation attempts to **decrement** the semaphore value by 1. If the result would be negative, the calling process is **blocked** (added to the semaphore's associated wait queue).

**Atomic Logic Steps:**

1. Test: Check if the value of S is greater than 0.
2. If **Yes**: Decrement S by 1 atomically and continue execution (process enters critical section).
3. If **No**: Block the calling process; place it in the semaphore's wait queue. The process remains blocked until some other process performs a `signal(S)`.

### 2.1.2 The `signal(S)` / `V(S)` Operation

This operation **increments** the semaphore value by 1. If there are processes blocked on S, one of them is **unblocked** and made ready to run.

**Atomic Logic Steps:**

1. Increment S by 1 atomically.
2. If there exists at least one process blocked in S's wait queue, remove one process (typically the head — FIFO order) and move it from the blocked state to the **ready queue**.
3. The calling process continues execution.

## 2.2 Canonical Pseudo-Code for Semaphore Primitives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** Examiners award full marks only if `wait()` and `signal()` are shown as **indivisible (atomic)** operations. Mention that the test and modification must occur without interruption.

```
wait(S) {
    while (S <= 0)        // busy-wait (spinlock version)
        ;                 // do nothing
    S--;
}

signal(S) {
    S++;
}
```

The above is the **busy-waiting / spinlock** version, which wastes CPU. In a real OS kernel, a **blocking implementation** is used:

```
wait(S) {
    S--;                                   // 1. atomic decrement
    if (S < 0) {                           // 2. check for blocking
        block(this process);               // 3. suspend & add to queue
    }
}

signal(S) {
    S++;                                   // 1. atomic increment
    if (S <= 0) {                          // 2. someone is waiting
        wakeup(one process from queue);    // 3. unblock
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> The decrement and the test in step 1–2 of `wait()` must be performed **atomically**. On a uniprocessor, this is achieved by **disabling interrupts**. On a multiprocessor, hardware instructions such as `compare_and_swap` (CAS) or `test_and_set` are required.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept / Property | Formula / Condition | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Initial value of binary semaphore `mutex` | `mutex = 1` | Unlocked state; first process may enter CS |
| Initial value of counting semaphore `S` | `S = N` | N identical instances of a resource available |
| Invariant (counting) | `S ∈ ℤ, S ≥ 0` | Semaphore value never goes negative |
| Critical section usage pattern | `wait(mutex); CS; signal(mutex);` | Enforce mutual exclusion |
| Number of processes in CS simultaneously | `≤ ⌊S_initial⌋` | Bound by initial semaphore value |
| Blocked-process condition (blocking impl.) | `S < 0 ⇒ |S| = #blocked processes` | Magnitude of negative value = queue size |
| Total processes accounted for | `S + |S| = S_initial` | Conservation law when no re-init |
| Producer–Consumer buffer slots | `empty = N, full = 0` | Initial buffer state |
| Resource utilization check | `Utilization = (Active Processes) / S_initial` | Efficiency metric ∈ [0, 1] |
| Fairness guarantee | **FIFO wake-up** | Required for **strong** semaphores |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution on notation:** To avoid table formatting issues, the absolute value of a negative semaphore value is written as `\vert S \vert` (e.g., `\vert -3 \vert = 3`) when used in mathematical derivations below.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Database Connection Pools:** Web servers use a counting semaphore initialized to the pool size.
- **Operating System Kernels:** Linux `sema_init()`, FreeBSD `sem_init()` and POSIX `sem_t` implement these.
- **Real-Time Systems:** Semaphores coordinate I/O device access in RTOS (e.g., VxWorks, FreeRTOS).
- **Multithreaded Applications:** Java's `Semaphore` class, Python's `threading.Semaphore`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains exhaustive derivations and worked analyses that are core to KTU 2024 Part B questions.

## 3.1 The Producer–Consumer Problem (Bounded Buffer) — Full Solution

The producer–consumer problem (also called the **Bounded Buffer Problem**) is a classic synchronization problem used in KTU exams every year. It has **two counting semaphores** and **one binary semaphore**.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
- A **producer** process generates data items and places them into a shared buffer of size `N`.
- A **consumer** process removes items from the buffer and processes them.
- The buffer is **circular** and shared — concurrent access must be safe.
- **Constraints:** No overflow (buffer full) and no underflow (buffer empty).

### 3.1.2 Semaphore Definitions

Let us define three semaphores:

- `empty` — counting semaphore counting the number of empty slots. Initial value = `N`.
- `full` — counting semaphore counting the number of filled slots. Initial value = `0`.
- `mutex` — binary semaphore enforcing mutual exclusion on buffer access. Initial value = `1`.

### 3.1.3 The Producer Process (Step-by-Step Derivation)

The producer must (i) wait for an empty slot, (ii) lock the buffer, (iii) deposit item, (iv) unlock the buffer, (v) announce a new full slot.

```
Producer:
    while (true) {
        // 1. Produce an item
        item = produce();
        // 2. Decrement empty count (block if buffer is full)
        wait(empty);
        // 3. Acquire exclusive access to buffer
        wait(mutex);
        // 4. Critical section: place item in buffer
        buffer[in]  = item;
        in = (in + 1) % N;
        // 5. Release buffer
        signal(mutex);
        // 6. Increment full count
        signal(full);
    }
```

### 3.1.4 The Consumer Process (Step-by-Step Derivation)

The consumer must (i) wait for a full slot, (ii) lock the buffer, (iii) remove item, (iv) unlock the buffer, (v) announce a new empty slot.

```
Consumer:
    while (true) {
        // 1. Decrement full count (block if buffer is empty)
        wait(full);
        // 2. Acquire exclusive access to buffer
        wait(mutex);
        // 3. Critical section: remove item from buffer
        item         = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) % N;
        // 4. Release buffer
        signal(mutex);
        // 5. Increment empty count
        signal(empty);
        // 6. Consume the item
        consume(item);
    }
```

### 3.1.5 Invariant Analysis

At any point in time, the following invariant holds:

$$
\text{empty} + \text{full} = N
$$

**Proof (by state inspection):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state:} \quad & \text{empty} = N, \ \text{full} = 0 \\
& \implies \text{empty} + \text{full} = N + 0 = N \ \checkmark \\
\text{After producer's } wait(empty): \quad & \text{empty} \to \text{empty} - 1 \\
\text{After producer's } signal(full): \quad & \text{full} \to \text{full} + 1 \\
\text{Net change:} \quad & (\text{empty} - 1) + (\text{full} + 1) = \text{empty} + \text{full} = N
\end{aligned}
$$

By symmetric reasoning, every consumer action changes `empty` by `+1` and `full` by `-1`, again preserving the sum. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 The Dining Philosophers Problem — Full Solution

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
- 5 philosophers sit around a circular table.
- Between every pair is **one chopstick** (5 chopsticks total).
- A philosopher **thinks**, gets **hungry**, then must pick up **both** the left and right chopsticks to **eat**, then puts them down.
- **Goal:** Design a synchronization scheme avoiding **deadlock** and **starvation**.

### 3.2.2 Naive (Deadlock-Prone) Solution

```
Philosopher i:
    wait(chopstick[i]);              // pick left
    wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);      // pick right
    // ... eat ...
    signal(chopstick[i]);
    signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
    // ... think ...
```

**Why it deadlocks:** If all 5 philosophers simultaneously pick up their left chopstick, all chopsticks are taken, and each waits forever for a right chopstick. **Circular wait** condition of Coffman is met.

### 3.2.3 Solution: A "Dining Court" Counting Semaphore (TANENBAUM'S SOLUTION)

Introduce a counting semaphore `S` initialized to **4**, limiting the number of philosophers simultaneously attempting to sit at the table:

```
semaphore chopstick[5] = {1, 1, 1, 1, 1};
semaphore S = 4;       // at most 4 philosophers may compete

Philosopher i:
    think();
    wait(S);                          // try to be seated
    wait(chopstick[i]);
    wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
    eat();
    signal(chopstick[i]);
    signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
    signal(S);                        // leave the table
    think();
```

**Deadlock Avoidance Proof:**

If at most 4 philosophers are seated, by the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least one chopstick among the 5 on the table will remain unused (since 4 philosophers need 8 picks, but there are only 4 seated → 4 left picks → 1 chopstick untouched). That chopstick is free for the philosopher whose neighbor doesn't need it, breaking the circular wait. **Deadlock impossible.**

## 3.3 The Readers–Writers Problem — Full Solution

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
- A shared database/file.
- Multiple **readers** may read concurrently (no conflict among readers).
- A **writer** needs **exclusive** access.
- **Goal:** Allow maximum concurrent reading while ensuring writer consistency.

### 3.3.2 Semaphores and Variable

- `mutex` (binary, init = 1) — protects the shared `readcount` variable.
- `wrt` (binary, init = 1) — provides exclusive access to the resource for writers.
- `readcount` (integer, init = 0) — number of active readers.

### 3.3.3 Code (First Reader–Writers Solution — reader preference)

```
Reader:
    wait(mutex);
    readcount++;
    if (readcount == 1)        // first reader
        wait(wrt);
    signal(mutex);
    // ... perform reading ...
    wait(mutex);
    readcount--;
    if (readcount == 0)        // last reader
        signal(wrt);
    signal(mutex);

Writer:
    wait(wrt);
    // ... perform writing ...
    signal(wrt);
```

**Key Insight (step-by-step):**

- The **first** reader to arrive acquires `wrt` → blocks writers.
- Subsequent readers increment `readcount` only — they do **not** lock `wrt`.
- The **last** reader to leave releases `wrt` → unblocks a pending writer.
- This gives **readers priority**; writers may starve (a known limitation).

**Correctness invariant:**

$$
\text{At any instant,} \quad \#\text{active readers} \geq 0 \quad \wedge \quad (\#\text{active writers} \in \{0, 1\}) \quad \wedge \quad (\#\text{active writers} = 0) \Leftarrow (\#\text{active readers} \geq 1)
$$

In words: at most one writer, and writers and readers are mutually exclusive.

## 3.4 Deadlock and Starvation Analysis (Matrix)

| Property | Producer–Consumer | Dining Philosophers (Naive) | Dining Philosophers (Tanenbaum) | Readers–Writers (1st) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mutual Exclusion | ✅ (via `mutex`) | ❌ violated at pickup | ✅ | ✅ |
| Progress | ✅ | ❌ (deadlock) | ✅ | ✅ |
| Bounded Wait | ✅ (FIFO) | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ (writer starvation possible) |
| Deadlock-Free | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (proven) | ✅ |
| No Starvation | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** A common pitfall is forgetting the **Pigeonhole Principle** justification for Tanenbaum's solution. Always include the 1-line proof in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Semaphore State Machine (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Process calls wait S]) --> B{S greater than 0}
    B -- Yes --> C[S decreases by 1]
    C --> D[Process enters Critical Section]
    D --> E[Process executes CS code]
    E --> F([Process calls signal S])
    F --> G[S increases by 1]
    G --> H{Any blocked process on S}
    H -- Yes --> I[Wake up one process from queue]
    I --> J([Process moves to Ready Queue])
    H -- No --> K([Semaphore state updated])
    B -- No --> L[Process is added to S wait queue]
    L --> M[Process enters Blocked state]
    M --> N{A signal S occurs}
    N -- Yes --> I
    N -- No --> O([Process remains blocked])
```

## 4.2 Producer–Consumer Synchronization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Producer
        P1[produce item] --> P2[wait empty]
        P2 --> P3[wait mutex]
        P3 --> P4[buffer in equals item]
        P4 --> P5[signal mutex]
        P5 --> P6[signal full]
        P6 --> P1
    end

    subgraph SharedBuffer["Shared Bounded Buffer N slots"]
        B0[Slot 0]
        B1[Slot 1]
        BN[Slot N-1]
    end

    subgraph Consumer
        C1[wait full] --> C2[wait mutex]
        C2 --> C3[item equals buffer out]
        C3 --> C4[signal mutex]
        C4 --> C5[signal empty]
        C5 --> C6[consume item]
        C6 --> C1
    end

    P4 -. deposits .-> B0
    C3 -. extracts .-> B1
    P4 -. deposits .-> BN
```

## 4.3 Dining Philosophers — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Table["Dining Table - 5 Chopsticks"]
        C0[Chopstick 0]
        C1[Chopstick 1]
        C2[Chopstick 2]
        C3[Chopstick 3]
        C4[Chopstick 4]
    end

    subgraph Seating["Semaphore S equals 4 - Seating Capacity"]
        CAP[Seating limit enforced]
    end

    subgraph Phils["5 Philosophers"]
        PH0[P0]
        PH1[P1]
        PH2[P2]
        PH3[P3]
        PH4[P4]
    end

    PH0 -->|wait S| CAP
    PH1 -->|wait S| CAP
    PH2 -->|wait S| CAP
    PH3 -->|wait S| CAP
    PH4 -->|wait S| CAP

    PH0 -->|wait C0 then C1| Table
    PH1 -->|wait C1 then C2| Table
    PH2 -->|wait C2 then C3| Table
    PH3 -->|wait C3 then C4| Table
    PH4 -->|wait C4 then C0| Table
```

## 4.4 Readers–Writers Synchronization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R1["Reader 1"]
        R1A[wait mutex]
        R1B[readcount increment]
        R1C{readcount equals 1}
        R1D[wait wrt]
        R1E[READ resource]
        R1F[readcount decrement]
        R1G{readcount equals 0}
        R1H[signal wrt]
        R1I[signal mutex]
        R1A --> R1B --> R1C -- yes --> R1D --> R1E
        R1E --> R1F --> R1G -- yes --> R1H --> R1I
        R1C -- no --> R1E
        R1G -- no --> R1I
    end

    subgraph WR["Writer"]
        WA[wait wrt]
        WB[WRITE resource exclusive]
        WC[signal wrt]
        WA --> WB --> WC
    end

    R1D -. blocks .-> WR
    R1H -. unblocks .-> WR
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define a semaphore. List and explain its two standard operations. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer synchronization variable used for coordinating access to shared resources in concurrent processes. It is accessed only through two atomic operations:

1. **wait(S) / P(S):** Decrements the semaphore value. If the result would become negative, the calling process is **blocked** and added to the semaphore's queue.
2. **signal(S) / V(S):** Increments the semaphore value. If any process is blocked on the semaphore, one of them is **woken up** (unblocked) and moved to the ready queue.

Both operations must be **atomic (indivisible)** to prevent race conditions, typically guaranteed by disabling interrupts or using hardware atomic instructions.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. Differentiate between binary semaphore and counting semaphore. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range | `0` or `1` | Any non-negative integer |
| Initial value | `0` or `1` | `N` (number of resources) |
| Primary use | Mutual exclusion | Resource counting |
| Equivalent to | Mutex (mostly) | Pool / capacity control |
| Multiple instances | No (only 1 at a time) | Yes (up to N concurrent accesses) |
| Example | Locking a file | `N` DB connections in a pool |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Model Paper Adaptation]**

### Question A: 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the Producer–Consumer (Bounded Buffer) problem in detail. Design a synchronization solution using semaphores and write the producer and consumer code. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the Dining Philosophers Problem? Show how the naive solution leads to deadlock, and explain Tanenbaum's solution with proof of deadlock-freedom. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Producer–Consumer (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define semaphores:**

- `empty` = counting semaphore, init `N` (number of buffer slots).
- `full` = counting semaphore, init `0` (no filled slots at start).
- `mutex` = binary semaphore, init `1` (buffer initially free).

**Step 2 — Producer code:**

```
Producer:
    while (true) {
        item = produce();
        wait(empty);          // block if buffer full
        wait(mutex);          // enter CS
        buffer[in] = item;
        in = (in + 1) mod N;
        signal(mutex);        // exit CS
        signal(full);         // one more filled slot
    }
```

**Step 3 — Consumer code:**

```
Consumer:
    while (true) {
        wait(full);           // block if buffer empty
        wait(mutex);          // enter CS
        item = buffer[out];
        out = (out + 1) mod N;
        signal(mutex);        // exit CS
        signal(empty);        // one more empty slot
        consume(item);
    }
```

**Step 4 — Correctness argument:**

- `empty` ensures producer blocks when buffer is full — no overflow.
- `full` ensures consumer blocks when buffer is empty — no underflow.
- `mutex` ensures only one process manipulates buffer indices at a time — no race.
- Invariant `empty + full = N` is always preserved.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Dining Philosophers (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Problem statement (1 mark):** 5 philosophers, 5 chopsticks, alternate think/eat, deadlock-prone.

**Step 2 — Naive solution (2 marks):** Each philosopher picks left then right chopstick. If all 5 pick left simultaneously, circular wait → deadlock.

**Step 3 — Tanenbaum's Solution (3 marks):** Add a counting semaphore `S = 4`:

```
semaphore S = 4;
semaphore chopstick[5] = {1,1,1,1,1};

Philosopher i:
    think();
    wait(S);
    wait(chopstick[i]);
    wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
    eat();
    signal(chopstick[i]);
    signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);
    signal(S);
    think();
```

**Step 4 — Proof of deadlock-freedom (1 mark):** At most 4 philosophers may sit at the table. By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, at least one chopstick remains unclaimed among 5 chopsticks when 4 philosophers are competing (since 4 philosophers need at most 4 left picks, but 5 chopsticks exist → at least 1 free). That free chopstick is reachable by at least one hungry philosopher, breaking the circular wait.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the Readers–Writers problem. Write a semaphore-based solution with reader preference and discuss its drawbacks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any **two classical synchronization problems** other than producer–consumer. For each, write the semaphore-based solution and explain the correctness. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Readers–Writers (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Problem (1 mark):** Multiple readers may read simultaneously; writers need exclusive access.

**Step 2 — Semaphores & variables (1 mark):**

- `mutex = 1` (binary) — protects `readcount`.
- `wrt = 1` (binary) — exclusive access for writers.
- `readcount = 0` (integer) — number of active readers.

**Step 3 — Code (3 marks):**

```
Reader:
    wait(mutex);
    readcount++;
    if (readcount == 1) wait(wrt);
    signal(mutex);
    /* read shared data */
    wait(mutex);
    readcount--;
    if (readcount == 0) signal(wrt);
    signal(mutex);

Writer:
    wait(wrt);
    /* write shared data */
    signal(wrt);
```

**Step 4 — Drawback (2 marks):** This is the **first readers–writers problem** (reader preference). If readers arrive continuously, the writer may **starve** — it can never acquire `wrt` because as long as one reader is active, `wrt` remains held, and new readers keep arriving. The **second readers–writers problem** (writer preference) and the **third** (fair) solutions fix this.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Two Synchronization Problems (7 Marks)

### Problem 1: The Bounded Buffer (Producer–Consumer) — Re-state (3 Marks)

> Already covered in detail in Q.A(a). State the three semaphores (`empty`, `full`, `mutex`), the invariant `empty + full = N`, and the logic that prevents overflow and underflow.

### Problem 2: The Sleeping Barber Problem (4 Marks)

**Setup:** A barber shop has 1 barber, 1 barber chair, and `N` waiting chairs. Customers arrive; if any waiting chair is free they sit; otherwise they leave. The barber sleeps when no customers are present.

**Semaphores:**

- `customers = 0` (counting) — number of waiting customers.
- `barbers = 0` (counting) — whether barber is ready.
- `mutex = 1` (binary) — protects `waiting` count.
- `waiting = 0` (int) — waiting chair occupancy.

**Barber process:**

```
Barber:
    while (true) {
        wait(customers);     // sleep if no customers
        wait(mutex);
        waiting--;
        signal(barbers);     // barber ready
        signal(mutex);
        cut_hair();
    }
```

**Customer process:**

```
Customer:
    wait(mutex);
    if (waiting < N) {
        waiting++;
        signal(customers);   // announce arrival
        signal(mutex);
        wait(barbers);       // wait until barber is free
        get_haircut();
    } else {
        signal(mutex);       // leave (no chair)
    }
```

**Correctness:** `customers` ensures barber sleeps on zero count and wakes on first arrival. `barbers` ensures customer blocks until the assigned barber is free. `waiting < N` ensures no customer is admitted beyond capacity; others leave (no starvation of admitted customers).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting atomicity:** Declaring `wait()` / `signal()` as a simple decrement/increment without emphasizing that the **test-and-modify must be atomic** (interrupts disabled or hardware CAS). — *Lose 1–2 marks.*
> 2. **Reversing `wait()` and `mutex` order:** Doing `wait(mutex); wait(empty);` causes **deadlock** (mutex held while waiting on full buffer; consumer may not free mutex). The correct order is **resource semaphores (`empty`, `full`) FIRST, then `mutex`**.
> 3. **Omitting the invariant proof:** Stating `empty + full = N` without a derivation. Examiners want a 2-line justification.
> 4. **Missing FIFO justification for strong semaphores:** In starvation-related questions, students forget to state that strong semaphores use FIFO queues.
> 5. **Confusing semaphore with mutex:** A mutex has an **ownership** constraint (only the locking thread can unlock); a semaphore has no such ownership. Examiners check this distinction.
> 6. **In Dining Philosophers (Tanenbaum), forgetting the Pigeonhole Principle line.** The 1-line proof is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read Before Exam**

- **Dijkstra (1965)** invented semaphores; `P` = Proberen (Dutch: to test), `V` = Verhogen (to increment).
- Semaphore value is a **non-negative integer**; only `wait()` and `signal()` may modify it.
- `wait(S)` ≡ `P(S)` ≡ `down(S)` ≡ `decrement(S)`. `signal(S)` ≡ `V(S)` ≡ `up(S)` ≡ `increment(S)`.
- **Binary semaphore:** value ∈ {0, 1}; used for mutual exclusion.
- **Counting semaphore:** value ≥ 0; used for resource pooling.
- `wait()` blocks **the calling process** if `S ≤ 0`; `signal()` wakes **one waiting process** if any exist.
- **Atomicity** is non-negotiable: achieved via interrupt disable (uniprocessor) or atomic instructions (multiprocessor).
- **Strong semaphores** use FIFO wake-up → bounded waiting; **weak** semaphores may starve processes.
- **Producer–Consumer invariant:** `empty + full = N` (always true after initialization).
- **Order of semaphore calls (critical rule):** Resource semaphores (`empty`, `full`) BEFORE `mutex`; else deadlock.
- **Dining Philosophers naive solution → deadlock**; Tanenbaum's fix uses `S = 4` + Pigeonhole argument.
- **Readers–Writers 1st solution = reader preference**; writers can starve; 2nd solution = writer preference; 3rd = fair (uses FIFO or a `turnstile` semaphore).
- **Mutex vs Semaphore:** Mutex has ownership; semaphore does not. Mutex typically only allows the holder to release; semaphore's `signal()` may be called by any process.
- **Deadlock conditions (Coffman):** mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait. Semaphore solutions must break at least one.
- **Spinlock vs blocking semaphores:** spinlock wastes CPU but has zero context-switch overhead; blocking semaphores conserve CPU but pay context-switch cost.
- **POSIX semaphores:** `sem_init`, `sem_wait`, `sem_post`, `sem_destroy`. `sem_t` type.
- **Linux kernel:** uses `struct semaphore { raw_spinlock_t lock; unsigned int count; struct list_head wait_list; }`.
- **Java equivalent:** `java.util.concurrent.Semaphore` with `acquire()` and `release()`.
- **Inverse relationship rule:** A **negative** semaphore value (in the blocking implementation) indicates the number of blocked processes.
- **Conservation law:** If no process is performing a new `wait()` or `signal()` initially, `S + (#blocked) = S_initial` at all times.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mutexes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mutexes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mutex** (short for **Mutual Exclusion Lock**) is a fundamental synchronization primitive provided by an operating system (or a threading library such as POSIX `pthread`) that enforces *mutual exclusion* on a critical section of code. Only **one thread at a time** is permitted to acquire (lock) a given mutex; all other competing threads are blocked from entering the protected region until the owning thread releases (unlocks) the mutex.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A mutex is a *lockable object* that a thread acquires before entering a critical section and releases after leaving it. Acquisition of an already-locked mutex causes the calling thread to **block (sleep)** on the kernel's wait queue until the holder releases it.

### Core Properties of a Mutex

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Binary State** | A mutex is always in one of two states: `UNLOCKED` (free) or `LOCKED` (held by exactly one thread). |
| **Ownership** | A mutex has a strict *owner* — the thread that successfully locked it. Only the owner is allowed to unlock it. |
| **Atomicity** | The test-and-set operation that flips the state is guaranteed to be *atomic* (indivisible) at the hardware level. |
| **Blocking Semantics** | Attempting to lock a held mutex causes the calling thread to be descheduled and placed in the mutex's *wait queue*. |
| **Bounded Waiting** | When the holder releases the mutex, exactly one waiting thread is awakened (kernel picks from wait queue). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mutex vs. Binary Semaphore — A KTU Favourite Question!**
> While a binary semaphore can be signaled by *any* thread, a **mutex can only be released by the thread that locked it**. This single rule is the most-tested distinction in KTU ESE Module 2 questions.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Washroom Key

Imagine a small office with **one washroom and one key hanging on the wall outside the door**.

- The washroom is the **critical section** (shared resource — the toilet).
- The key is the **mutex**.
- The employees are the **threads**.

**What happens in real life?**

1. An employee arrives, takes the key from the hook, and enters the washroom. *(The thread acquires the lock and enters the critical section.)*
2. Another employee arrives. The key is gone, so they must **wait outside**, perhaps doing other work or standing in a queue. *(The thread is blocked and placed on the wait queue.)*
3. The first employee finishes, unlocks the door, and **hangs the key back on the hook**. *(The thread releases the lock.)*
4. The kernel (or "washroom manager") hands the key to **one** waiting employee, who then proceeds in. *(One waiting thread is woken up.)*

> [!TIP]
> **Why the key must be returned only by the person who took it:** Imagine if any random passerby could return the key while the first person was still inside — the first person might get locked in! This is precisely why mutexes enforce *ownership*: only the locking thread can unlock. A semaphore would have no such rule.

---

## 1.3 Why Mutexes Are Necessary — The Race Condition Problem

Without a mutex, two threads accessing shared data (say, a counter variable `x = 0`) may interleave their operations:

- **Thread A**: reads `x` (gets `0`), computes `x + 1`, writes `x = 1`.
- **Thread B**: reads `x` (gets `0` because A hasn't written yet), computes `x + 1`, writes `x = 1`.

**Result**: After both threads complete, `x = 1` instead of the expected `2`. The increment was **lost**. This is a classic **race condition**, and the mutex is the cure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A mutex does **not** prevent race conditions by itself — it only provides the *mechanism* to serialize access. The programmer must still remember to lock *every* access path to the shared data. Forgetting even one unlock leads to either a **deadlock** or **lost updates**.

---

## 1.4 Mutex in the KTU 2024 CO Mapping

| Course Outcome | How Mutex Maps |
|---|---|
| **CO2** – Apply process synchronization techniques | Mutex is the primary tool for solving critical-section problems. |
| **CO3** – Analyze deadlock conditions | Mutex-induced wait-for cycles lead to deadlocks (circular wait condition). |
| **CO4** – Implement OS primitives using APIs | `pthread_mutex_init/lock/unlock` is a KTU-mandated lab/API topic. |

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics for Mutex Performance

When discussing mutex implementations, the following **standard metrics** are evaluated in KTU numerical questions:

- **Lock Acquisition Latency (L)**: Time taken for an uncontended lock/unlock cycle — typically **a few hundred nanoseconds** for a non-blocking mutex and **microseconds** for a kernel-blocking mutex.
- **Context-Switch Cost (C)**: Time spent saving and restoring thread state — usually **1–10 microseconds** on modern Linux kernels.
- **Throughput (T)**: Number of lock/unlock operations per second — often **millions/sec** for spinlock mutexes and **hundreds of thousands/sec** for blocking mutexes under contention.

> [!NOTE]
> These three metrics — **L, C, T** — form the basis of the standard formula: **T = 1 / (L + p·C)**, where `p` is the probability of contention. This formula is examinable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Steps of a Mutex Operation

A mutex abstraction logically consists of three sub-operations, each of which the OS/Kernel must guarantee to be **atomic** (non-interruptible by other threads).

### Step 1: `lock(mutex)` — Test-and-Set

The thread checks the current state of the mutex. If `UNLOCKED`, it atomically transitions the state to `LOCKED` and sets `owner = current_thread`. If `LOCKED`, the thread is added to the mutex's wait queue and the scheduler blocks it.

### Step 2: **Critical Section Execution**

The thread now safely executes the protected code, knowing that no other thread is inside. The CPU can perform reads, writes, and complex calculations on shared variables without interference.

### Step 3: `unlock(mutex)` — Release and Wake

The thread atomically transitions the mutex from `LOCKED` back to `UNLOCKED`, clears the `owner` field, and removes one (or more, depending on policy) waiting thread from the wait queue, marking it `RUNNABLE` so the scheduler can dispatch it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind each step:**
> - *Why atomic?* — Because if two threads could simultaneously read `UNLOCKED` and both set it to `LOCKED`, mutual exclusion would be violated. Atomicity is achieved via hardware instructions like **TAS**, **CAS**, or **fetch-and-add**.
> - *Why a wait queue?* — To enforce fairness and bounded waiting. A naive spin loop would waste CPU cycles.
> - *Why clear the owner?* — So the next acquirer is recorded correctly, and so that debugging tools can identify deadlocks.

---

## 2.2 Mutex Implementation Strategies

### A. Spinlock-Based Mutex (Busy-Wait)

The waiting thread executes a tight loop, repeatedly testing the lock variable until it becomes available.

**Advantages:** Zero context-switch overhead, ultra-low latency (sub-microsecond), ideal for short critical sections and multi-core systems.

**Disadvantages:** Wastes CPU cycles (100% core utilization while spinning), starvation possible, energy-inefficient, and on a single-core system it is *useless* (the holder can never release because the spinner holds the CPU).

### B. Blocking Mutex (Kernel-Sleeper)

When contention is detected, the thread executes a system call (`futex` on Linux, `KeWaitForSingleObject` on Windows) that **deschedules** the thread and places it in the mutex's wait queue. The CPU is freed for other work.

**Advantages:** No CPU wastage, fair scheduling by the kernel, lower power consumption, mandatory for long critical sections.

**Disadvantages:** Two context switches (block + wake) per contended acquire — significant overhead (microseconds).

### C. Adaptive Mutex (Hybrid)

The thread spins for a bounded number of iterations first; if the lock is still held, it falls back to blocking. This is the **POSIX default** for `pthread_mutex_t` on most modern systems (Linux uses the `futex` mechanism under the hood with adaptive spinning on multi-core).

---

## 2.3 The `pthread_mutex_t` API (POSIX Standard — KTU Mandatory)

```c
#include <pthread.h>

pthread_mutex_t mtx = PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER;   // Static init
int pthread_mutex_init(pthread_mutex_t *mtx, const pthread_mutexattr_t *attr);
int pthread_mutex_lock(pthread_mutex_t *mtx);       // Blocking acquire
int pthread_mutex_trylock(pthread_mutex_t *mtx);    // Non-blocking attempt
int pthread_mutex_timedlock(pthread_mutex_t *mtx, const struct timespec *abs);
int pthread_mutex_unlock(pthread_mutex_t *mtx);     // Release
int pthread_mutex_destroy(pthread_mutex_t *mtx);    // Cleanup
```

### Mutex Attributes (`pthread_mutexattr_t`)

| Attribute | Possible Values | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | `PTHREAD_MUTEX_NORMAL` | No deadlock detection; unlocking from non-owner = undefined behavior. |
| **Type** | `PTHREAD_MUTEX_ERRORCHECK` | Returns `EPERM` if non-owner tries to unlock. (Recommended for debugging.) |
| **Type** | `PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE` | Same thread can lock multiple times; requires equal unlocks. |
| **Type** | `PTHREAD_MUTEX_DEFAULT` | Implementation-defined (usually behaves like `NORMAL`). |
| **Protocol** | `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` | Enables **priority inheritance** to combat priority inversion. |
| **Protocol** | `PTHREAD_PRIO_PROTECT` | Uses **priority ceiling** protocol. |
| **Process-shared** | `PTHREAD_PROCESS_SHARED` | Allows mutex to be shared across processes (via shared memory). |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Atomic Test-And-Set** | TAS | `old = *lock; *lock = 1; return old;` | Hardware-level atomic op |
| **Compare-And-Swap** | CAS | `if (*p == expected) *p = desired;` | ABA problem possible |
| **Lock Latency** | $L$ | Uncontended lock+unlock time | **nanoseconds** |
| **Context-Switch Cost** | $C$ | Save/restore registers + cache flush | **microseconds** (typ. $\approx 1\text{–}10 \, \mu s$) |
| **Contention Probability** | $p$ | $p = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot t_h}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $t_h$ = hold time |
| **Effective Throughput** | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{1}{L + p \cdot C}$ | **ops/sec** |
| **Spinlock Wasted CPU** | $W$ | $W = N_{spinners} \cdot t_{wait} \cdot P_{core}$ | $P_{core}$ = core power, **Watts** |
| **Critical Section Condition** | — | $\text{Mutual Exclusion} \land \text{Progress} \land \text{Bounded Wait}$ | **All three required** by KTU |
| **Priority Inheritance Bound** | $T_{PI}$ | A high-priority task is blocked for at most **one** lower-priority critical section. | KTU Q favourite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse** a *mutex* with a *critical section*. The critical section is the **code region**; the mutex is the **mechanism** that protects it. KTU examiners frequently award partial credit for getting this distinction right.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Mutexes

Mutexes are the workhorse of nearly every concurrent software system you can name:

- **Databases** (MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL): Row-level locks are implemented as mutexes protecting buffer-pool pages.
- **Operating System Kernels** (Linux): The `inode` mutex, `mmap` semaphore, and Big Kernel Lock all evolved from earlier spinlock-based mutexes.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems** (Automotive ECU, avionics): Priority-inheritance mutexes are **mandatory** under the **OSEK/VDX** and **AUTOSAR** standards to bound priority inversion.
- **High-Performance Computing** (HPC, MPI runtimes): User-space mutexes built on `futex` provide near-spinlock latency with blocking fallback.
- **Gaming and Graphics Engines** (Unreal, Unity): Job-system workers use mutexes to protect scene-graph mutation.

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves a 7-mark question of the form: *"Explain how mutexes are used in [insert real OS / database]."* Memorize at least one engineering application.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Atomic Test-And-Set to a Working Mutex

We will now derive, **one logical step at a time**, how a hardware `TAS` (Test-And-Set) instruction can be lifted into a complete blocking mutex.

---

### **Stage 1: The Hardware Atomic Primitive**

Modern CPUs (x86 `LOCK BTS`, ARM `LDREX/STREX`, RISC-V `AMO`) provide an atomic instruction:

```
TAS(addr):      // Atomic — uninterruptible
    old_value = *addr
    *addr = 1
    return old_value
```

**Justification:** Without atomicity, two cores could both read `old_value = 0`, both write `*addr = 1`, and both "win" the lock — violating mutual exclusion. The `LOCK` prefix on x86 (or `aq`/`rl` qualifiers on RISC-V) flushes the store buffer and locks the memory bus, making the operation *sequentially consistent* with respect to other cores.

---

### **Stage 2: Naive Spinlock (First Attempt)**

Using `TAS`, we can write the simplest possible mutex:

```c
typedef int mutex_t;
#define UNLOCKED 0
#define LOCKED   1

void lock(mutex_t *m) {
    while ( TAS(m) == LOCKED )   // Spin while already locked
        ;   // <-- busy-wait loop
}

void unlock(mutex_t *m) {
    *m = UNLOCKED;
}
```

**Logical issue:** This is a *spinlock*. It works, but has three KTU-discussable problems:

1. **No fairness** — thread starvation is possible.
2. **Wastes CPU** — 100% utilization of the spinning core.
3. **Single-core uselessness** — on a 1-CPU machine, the holder cannot run to release the lock because the spinner owns the CPU.

We now *upgrade* the spinlock to address these.

---

### **Stage 3: Adding a Wait Queue (Blocking Mutex)**

The kernel maintains a *FIFO wait queue* per mutex. The `lock()` and `unlock()` become **system calls**.

```c
// Pseudocode (Kernel Space)

struct futex_mutex {
    atomic int state;     // 0 = UNLOCKED, 1 = LOCKED
    wait_queue_t waiters; // List of sleeping threads
};

void futex_lock(struct futex_mutex *m) {
    // Step A: Try fast-path (no syscall if uncontended)
    if (atomic_CAS(&m->state, 0, 1) == 0)        // CAS = Compare-And-Swap
        return;                                    // We got the lock — return to user space

    // Step B: Slow-path — we lost the race; enter kernel
    while (1) {
        // Atomically: if state == 0, set to 1 and return SUCCESS
        if (atomic_CAS(&m->state, 0, 1) == 0)
            return;
        // Otherwise, sleep on the wait queue
        current_thread->state = BLOCKED;
        enqueue(&m->waiters, current_thread);
        schedule();     // Context switch — CPU is freed
        // On wakeup, loop back and re-try CAS
    }
}

void futex_unlock(struct futex_mutex *m) {
    atomic_store(&m->state, 0);   // Release the lock
    if (!waitqueue_empty(&m->waiters))
        wake_one(&m->waiters);    // Wake exactly one waiter
}
```

**Step-by-step logic explanation:**

| Step | Action | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| A | `atomic_CAS(&m->state, 0, 1)` | This is the *fast path*. Uncontended locks never enter the kernel — they execute entirely in user space at hardware speed. |
| B | On contention, we set our thread to `BLOCKED` and **enqueue ourselves** *before* checking the state again. | This avoids a "lost wakeup" race: if we checked the state first, the holder could release between our check and our sleep, leaving us asleep forever. |
| C | `schedule()` yields the CPU | Allows the lock holder to run, finish, and call `unlock()`. |
| D | On wakeup, the **CAS is retried** in a loop | Just in case another thread stole the lock between wakeup and retry (rare but possible). |
| E | `wake_one()` on unlock | Wakes exactly **one** waiter — bounded waiting is satisfied. |

---

### **Stage 4: The futex Optimization (Linux's `futex` System Call)**

The full POSIX `pthread_mutex_lock` on Linux uses a **two-level** mechanism:

1. **User-space atomic op** (CAS) — handles uncontended case without a syscall.
2. **`futex()` syscall** — only used for blocking and waking.

This is the *fast user-space locking* (futex) design by Franke, Russell, and Kirkwood (IBM, 2002) and is the reason Linux mutexes scale so well.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Mutex Flavors

The following table is a **KTU high-yield summary** that you should be able to draw from memory.

| Property | Spinlock Mutex | Blocking Mutex | Recursive Mutex | Priority-Inherit Mutex |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Waits via** | Busy loop | Sleep on wait queue | Busy loop or sleep (counter-based) | Sleep + dynamic priority boost |
| **CPU usage while waiting** | **High (100%)** | **Zero** | Varies | Zero |
| **Context switches per contention** | **0** | **2** (block + wake) | 0 or 2 | 2 |
| **Latency (uncontended)** | $\approx 25\text{ ns}$ | $\approx 50\text{ ns}$ (fast path) | $\approx 30\text{ ns}$ | $\approx 60\text{ ns}$ |
| **Suitable for** | Short CS, multi-core | Long CS, single/multi-core | Code with re-entrant functions | Real-time, RTOS |
| **Risk** | Starvation, single-core deadlock | Convoy effect, thundering herd | Confusion on unlock count | Complex implementation |
| **Fairness** | None (LIFO usually) | FIFO (kernel queue) | None (single thread) | FIFO + PI |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Throughput Calculation

**Problem:** A mutex is used in a system where the lock acquisition latency is **$L = 200 \, ns$**, the context-switch cost is **$C = 5 \, \mu s$**, and the probability of contention is **$p = 0.4$** (40% of acquisitions contend). Calculate the effective throughput **$T$**.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$
L = 200 \, ns = 0.2 \, \mu s, \quad C = 5 \, \mu s, \quad p = 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the effective throughput formula.**

$$
T = \frac{1}{L + p \cdot C}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
T = \frac{1}{0.2 \, \mu s + (0.4)(5 \, \mu s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
0.2 + 2.0 = 2.2 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Take the reciprocal.**

$$
T = \frac{1}{2.2 \, \mu s} = \frac{1}{2.2 \times 10^{-6} \, s} \approx 454{,}545 \, \text{ops/sec}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare to the uncontended throughput (sanity check).**

$$
T_{\text{uncontended}} = \frac{1}{L} = \frac{1}{0.2 \, \mu s} = 5{,}000{,}000 \, \text{ops/sec}
$$

**Interpretation:** Contention at $p = 0.4$ reduces throughput by a factor of $\approx 11\times$. This is why **minimizing critical-section length** is a golden rule of concurrent programming — every microsecond inside the mutex is a microsecond the entire system is serialized.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: The Lost Wakeup Bug (Derivation of a Correctness Fix)

A naive blocking mutex (Version 1) has a subtle bug:

```c
// VERSION 1 — BUGGY
void lock(mutex *m) {
    if (atomic_get(&m->state) == 0)
        atomic_set(&m->state, 1);
    else {
        sleep_on(&m->waiters);    // (a) Sleep AFTER checking state
    }
}
void unlock(mutex *m) {
    atomic_set(&m->state, 0);
    wake_one(&m->waiters);
}
```

**Trace of the lost wakeup race:**

| Time | Thread A (holder) | Thread B (newcomer) |
|---|---|---|
| $t_0$ | Holds lock | Reads `state == 1` (sees locked) |
| $t_1$ | Calls `unlock()` | About to call `sleep_on()` |
| $t_2$ | Sets `state = 0` | — |
| $t_3$ | Calls `wake_one()` (no waiters yet) | — |
| $t_4$ | Returns from unlock | Calls `sleep_on()` — **sleeps forever** |

**The fix** is to atomically set our intention to sleep *before* checking the state, and to re-check the state *after* being put on the queue. This is why the correct version uses a `while` loop with `CAS` *inside* the loop (Stage 3 above).

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners specifically test whether students understand **why** the loop-and-CAS pattern is necessary. The phrase "to avoid lost wakeups" earns full credit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mutex State Machine — Lifecycle of a Mutex Object

The following Mermaid state diagram captures every legal transition a mutex can undergo during program execution.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STATE_INIT : pthread_mutex_init
    STATE_INIT : Mutex object allocated
    STATE_INIT : state UNLOCKED
    STATE_INIT : owner NULL
    STATE_INIT : waiters EMPTY

    STATE_INIT --> STATE_LOCKED : thread1 lock success
    STATE_INIT --> STATE_INIT : pthread_mutex_destroy

    STATE_LOCKED : Mutex held by owner
    STATE_LOCKED : state LOCKED
    STATE_LOCKED : owner thread1
    STATE_LOCKED : waiters optional

    STATE_LOCKED --> STATE_INIT : owner unlock and no waiters
    STATE_LOCKED --> STATE_CONTENDED : thread2 lock fails
    STATE_LOCKED --> STATE_LOCKED : recursive lock allowed (type RECURSIVE)

    STATE_CONTENDED : state LOCKED
    STATE_CONTENDED : owner thread1
    STATE_CONTENDED : waiters has thread2
    STATE_CONTENDED : thread2 is BLOCKED

    STATE_CONTENDED --> STATE_LOCKED : owner unlock and wake thread2
    STATE_CONTENDED --> STATE_CONTENDED : thread3 lock fails and joins queue
    STATE_CONTENDED --> STATE_INIT : owner unlock and queue empty

    STATE_INIT --> [*] : destroy
    STATE_LOCKED --> [*] : illegal destroy
    STATE_CONTENDED --> [*] : illegal destroy
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Rectangles are *states*, arrows are *events* (lock, unlock, destroy), and the colon-prefixed lines are *annotations* describing the data fields of the mutex in that state. The **double transition** from `STATE_CONTENDED → STATE_LOCKED` represents the moment the kernel hands the lock to one of the waiters.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The futex-Based Mutex Subsystem

The following diagram maps the *internal architecture* of a typical kernel-backed mutex, showing how the user-space fast path interacts with the kernel slow path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Thread calls pthread_mutex_lock] --> B{Atomic CAS<br/>state 0 to 1<br/>in user space}
    B -- CAS succeeded --> C[Return immediately<br/>Fast Path Latency approx 25 ns]
    B -- CAS failed --> D[Enter Kernel via futex syscall]
```
