```mermaid
    D --> E[Enqueue thread T on mutex wait queue]
    E --> F[Mark T as BLOCKED]
    F --> G[Invoke scheduler schedule]
    G --> H[CPU runs other ready threads]
    H --> I{Owner thread calls unlock}
    I --> J[Atomic store state 0]
    J --> K{Wait queue empty?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Return to user space]
    K -- No --> M[Wake one waiter from queue]
    M --> N[Waiter marked RUNNABLE]
    N --> O[Scheduler dispatches waiter]
    O --> P[Waiter retries atomic CAS]
    P -- CAS succeeded --> Q[Waiter returns from lock]
    P -- CAS failed --> E
```

**Reading guide:**

| Node | Meaning |
|---|---|
| A | The very first instruction executed by the user thread. |
| B | The *gatekeeper*. If the lock is free, we never enter the kernel — this is the **fast path**. |
| C | The happy uncontended case. Most locks in real workloads take this path. |
| D | The **slow path** begins here. We trap into kernel mode via a syscall. |
| E–F | The thread marks itself blocked *and* joins the queue. Order matters — see the lost-wakeup fix in Section 3.4. |
| G–H | The CPU is released; other threads (or the lock holder) get to run. |
| I–J | The owner releases the lock. |
| K | Decision: any waiters? |
| M–P | One waiter is woken; it must re-attempt the CAS to actually acquire the lock. |

---

## 4.3 Multi-Thread Contention Scenario (Time-Sequence View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Thread T1
    participant M as Mutex M
    participant WQ as Wait Queue WQ
    participant T2 as Thread T2
    participant T3 as Thread T3
    participant CPU as CPU Scheduler

    T1->>M: lock(M)  attempt 1
    M-->>T1: GRANTED  state LOCKED owner T1
    Note over T1,M: T1 inside critical section

    T2->>M: lock(M)  attempt 2
    M-->>T2: BLOCKED  enqueue T2
    M->>WQ: append T2
    T2->>CPU: schedule  T2 SLEEPING

    T3->>M: lock(M)  attempt 3
    M-->>T3: BLOCKED  enqueue T3
    M->>WQ: append T3
    T3->>CPU: schedule  T3 SLEEPING

    Note over WQ: WQ = T2 then T3  FIFO order

    T1->>M: unlock(M)
    M->>WQ: dequeue T2 and wake
    M-->>T1: state UNLOCKED
    T1->>CPU: yield  T1 continues post critical

    CPU->>T2: dispatch T2
    T2->>M: CAS 0 to 1
    M-->>T2: GRANTED  state LOCKED owner T2
    Note over T2,M: T2 inside critical section

    T2->>M: unlock(M)
    M->>WQ: dequeue T3 and wake
    CPU->>T3: dispatch T3
    T3->>M: CAS 0 to 1
    M-->>T3: GRANTED
```

> [!TIP]
> This sequence diagram is the **single most important visual** for KTU questions on mutex ordering and fairness. Practice drawing it freehand — examiners reward well-labelled diagrams.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Safeguard Compliance

For all diagrams above, the following KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards have been strictly observed:

- **Node Identifier Alpha Rule:** Every node ID begins with a letter (`A`, `B`, `T1`, `WQ`, etc.) and is purely alphanumeric. No reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` are used as node names.
- **Label Formatting Restriction:** No bold (`**`), italics (`*`), or HTML tags appear inside double-quoted labels. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text (e.g., `"Atomic CAS"`, `"Wait Queue WQ"`).
- **Block-Level Adaptation:** Where a topic required a complex physical drawing (e.g., a memory-layout view of the `pthread_mutex_t` struct), the diagram was adapted to a **functional block architecture** mapping user-space, kernel-space, and scheduler interactions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`

> **"What is a mutex? State any two differences between a mutex and a binary semaphore."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **mutex** (Mutual Exclusion Lock) is a synchronization primitive that allows only one thread at a time to access a shared resource. The thread that locks a mutex must be the one to unlock it.

**Two differences between a mutex and a binary semaphore:**

1. **Ownership:** A mutex has an *owner* — only the thread that acquired the lock can release it. A binary semaphore has *no ownership*; any thread can execute `signal()`/`V()` on it.
2. **Purpose:** A mutex is intended purely for *mutual exclusion* (locking a critical section). A binary semaphore is intended for *signaling* between threads (e.g., "task X has finished, wake task Y") and can also be used for mutual exclusion as a side effect.

*(Third valid difference: a mutex supports priority inheritance protocols on real-time systems, while a binary semaphore typically does not.)* **— 3 marks**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU 2024 Scheme Sample]`

> **"Explain priority inversion. How do priority-inheritance mutexes solve it?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly where a high-priority thread is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority thread, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities. It occurs when a low-priority thread holds a mutex that a high-priority thread needs; the high-priority thread blocks, and a medium-priority thread (which does not need the mutex) preempts the holder, further delaying the high-priority task.

**Example:** Thread H (high prio) → blocks on mutex held by L (low prio); M (medium prio) preempts L; H now waits for L *and* M to complete.

**Solution via Priority Inheritance:** When a high-priority thread blocks on a mutex held by a low-priority thread, the kernel **temporarily raises** the holder's priority to that of the highest waiter. This prevents medium-priority threads from preempting the holder, **bounding the inversion to at most one critical-section duration** and ensuring bounded waiting.

**— 3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU ESE – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice A]`

> **(a)** Explain the design and implementation of a **blocking mutex** using atomic Compare-And-Swap (CAS) and a kernel wait queue. Include the **lost-wakeup problem** and its solution. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare **spinlock-based** and **blocking mutexes** in terms of CPU utilization, latency, context-switch overhead, fairness, and suitability for single-core vs. multi-core systems. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A **blocking mutex** is a mutual-exclusion primitive where a contending thread is *descheduled* and placed on a kernel wait queue, freeing the CPU.

**Step 1 — Data structure (in the kernel):**

```c
struct blocking_mutex {
    atomic_t state;            // 0 = UNLOCKED, 1 = LOCKED
    struct wait_queue *wq;     // FIFO queue of sleeping threads
    pid_t owner;               // PID of holding thread (debugging)
};
```

**Step 2 — The `lock()` algorithm:**

```c
void block_lock(struct blocking_mutex *m) {
    while (1) {
        // Atomically test-and-claim
        if (atomic_CAS(&m->state, 0, 1) == 0)   // returns previous value
            return;                               // we got the lock

        // We lost the CAS; prepare to sleep
        prepare_to_block(&m->wq);                 // add to wait queue
        if (atomic_CAS(&m->state, 0, 1) == 0)     // re-check after enqueue
            return;                               // race won
        actually_block();                         // syscall: sleep
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — The `unlock()` algorithm:**

```c
void block_unlock(struct blocking_mutex *m) {
    atomic_store(&m->state, 0);            // release
    wake_up_one(&m->wq);                   // wake one waiter
}
```

**Step 4 — The Lost-Wakeup Problem and Its Fix:**

| Buggy version | Correct version |
|---|---|
| Check `state`; if locked, then `sleep()`. | First add to wait queue; then re-check `state` inside a `while` loop. |
| Race: holder releases *between* check and sleep → wakeup is sent to no one → new thread sleeps forever. | Fix: by joining the queue *before* the final state check, the wake-up cannot be lost — the holder's `wake_one()` will find us. |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact invariant is: **a thread is on the wait queue *if and only if* it has decided to sleep.** This invariant is what prevents the lost wakeup. **(7 marks — full marks breakdown: 2 for data structure, 2 for lock code, 1 for unlock code, 2 for lost-wakeup discussion.)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

| Criterion | Spinlock Mutex | Blocking Mutex |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU utilization while waiting** | **High (100%)** — wastes one core per contender | **Zero** — sleeping thread is unscheduled |
| **Uncontended latency** | $\approx 25 \, ns$ (one atomic op) | $\approx 50 \, ns$ (atomic op + minor kernel bookkeeping) |
| **Contended latency** | Depends on holder's CS length, no syscall | Adds 2 context switches ($\approx 5 \, \mu s$ each on Linux) |
| **Context switches per contention** | **0** | **2** (block + wake) |
| **Fairness** | Typically **LIFO / no guarantee** | Typically **FIFO** via kernel wait queue |
| **Single-core behavior** | **Useless / deadlock-prone** (spinner owns the CPU; holder cannot run) | **Works correctly** (sleep yields CPU to holder) |
| **Multi-core behavior** | **Excellent** for short CS | **Excellent** for long CS |
| **Power consumption** | High (spinning core draws full power) | Low (core can be parked) |
| **Risk** | Starvation, single-core deadlock | Thundering-herd, convoy effect |
| **KTU use case** | Kernel-internal short CS, e.g., `spin_lock_irqsave` | User-space thread CS > 1 $\mu s$ |

**Engineering rule of thumb:** Use a spinlock if the critical section is shorter than the cost of two context switches ($\approx 2 \times 5 \, \mu s = 10 \, \mu s$ on typical Linux). Otherwise, use a blocking mutex.

**(7 marks — full marks breakdown: 1 for each row's first column, 1 for the engineering rule, 1 for overall comparison paragraph.)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU ESE – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]`

> **(a)** With a suitable **POSIX `pthread` C program**, demonstrate how a mutex is used to protect a shared counter from race conditions. Show the expected output for `N = 100000` increments and `2` threads. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain **priority inheritance protocol** in detail, with a **priority-inversion scenario** and a numerical example. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Program — `counter_mutex.c`:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <pthread.h>

#define N 100000
long long counter = 0;                  // Shared resource
pthread_mutex_t mtx = PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER;

void* worker(void* arg) {
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        pthread_mutex_lock(&mtx);       // Enter critical section
        counter++;                       // Protected read-modify-write
        pthread_mutex_unlock(&mtx);     // Leave critical section
    }
    return NULL;
}

int main(void) {
    pthread_t t1, t2;
    pthread_create(&t1, NULL, worker, NULL);
    pthread_create(&t2, NULL, worker, NULL);
    pthread_join(t1, NULL);
    pthread_join(t2, NULL);
    printf("Final counter value = %lld (expected %d)\n",
           counter, 2 * N);
    pthread_mutex_destroy(&mtx);
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Final counter value = 200000 (expected 200000)
```

**Without the mutex** (i.e., naive `counter++` with no lock), the output would vary between runs and almost always be **less than 200000** due to lost updates — a classic race condition.

**Explanation:**

- The mutex `mtx` is statically initialized via `PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER`.
- Each thread locks `mtx` before incrementing `counter` and unlocks immediately after.
- `pthread_join()` in `main()` ensures both threads have finished before we print.
- The expected value is exactly `2N = 200000` because the mutex serializes every increment.

**Compile and run:**

```
gcc -O2 -pthread counter_mutex.c -o counter_mutex
./counter_mutex
```

**(7 marks — 2 for code structure, 2 for correctness of mutex API usage, 2 for explanation, 1 for expected output.)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** is a real-time synchronization technique designed to **bound** the duration of priority inversion.

**Detailed Mechanism:**

1. Each mutex has a *priority ceiling* attribute or maintains a *highest-priority waiter* record.
2. When a thread `T_h` (high priority) blocks on a mutex held by `T_l` (low priority), the kernel **dynamically raises** `T_l`'s effective priority to that of `T_h`.
3. This prevents any medium-priority thread `T_m` from preempting `T_l`, because `T_l` now has the highest priority among the runnable threads.
4. When `T_l` releases the mutex, its priority is **restored** to its original value.
5. The high-priority thread `T_h` then becomes runnable and immediately acquires the mutex.

**Numerical Example — Three Threads:**

| Time (ms) | Thread H (prio 7) | Thread M (prio 4) | Thread L (prio 1) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Running | Ready | Ready |
| 5 | Tries to lock M1 held by L → **blocks** | — | L starts running, locks M1 |
| 10 | — | M preempts? **No — L is now at prio 7** | L continues CS |
| 15 | — | — | L unlocks M1, prio restored to 1, **wakes H** |
| 16 | H acquires M1, runs | — | L preempted |

**Bounding property:** The high-priority thread `H` is blocked for at most **one** critical-section execution time of `L`. Without PIP, `H` could be blocked for an *unbounded* number of `M` task executions.

**Pthread configuration:**

```c
pthread_mutexattr_t attr;
pthread_mutexattr_init(&attr);
pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(&attr, PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT);
pthread_mutex_init(&mtx, &attr);
```

**(7 marks — 2 for PIP definition, 2 for the trace, 1 for the bounding property, 1 for the pthreads API, 1 for the diagram/table.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing binary semaphore with mutex** — KTU examiners *intentionally* mark you down here. Always state that a mutex has an *owner*.
> 2. **Forgetting the lost-wakeup fix** — simply writing `if(state==1) sleep()` will lose 2 marks immediately. Always use a `while` loop with `CAS` inside.
> 3. **Confusing "wait queue" with "spinning"** — these are *opposite* strategies. If your answer says "the thread waits by spinning on a wait queue" you have contradicted yourself.
> 4. **Missing the `pthread_join()` in the C program** — without it, `main` may exit before the threads finish, giving a wrong counter.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the throughput calculation — always say **microseconds, ops/sec, nanoseconds**. Unit-less numbers lose 0.5 marks each.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Must-Know Bullets for KTU Module 2 — Mutexes:**

- ☐ **Mutex = Mutual Exclusion Lock**, a binary lockable object that ensures only one thread is in the critical section.
- ☐ A mutex has an **owner**; only the owner can release it. This distinguishes it from a binary semaphore.
- ☐ The three **guarantees** a mutex provides: *Mutual Exclusion*, *Progress*, *Bounded Waiting* (all three are required by the KTU critical-section problem).
- ☐ The two **atomic hardware primitives** that underpin mutexes are `TAS` (Test-And-Set) and `CAS` (Compare-And-Swap).
- ☐ **Spinlock mutex** = busy-wait; zero context switches; ideal for *short* critical sections on *multi-core* systems.
- ☐ **Blocking mutex** = sleep on wait queue; two context switches per contention; ideal for *long* critical sections and *single-core* systems.
- ☐ **Linux futex** is a two-level mechanism: user-space CAS (fast path) + kernel `futex()` syscall (slow path).
- ☐ The **lost-wakeup bug** is fixed by enqueuing the thread on the wait queue *before* the final state check, and re-checking inside a `while` loop.
- ☐ **POSIX API**: `pthread_mutex_init`, `pthread_mutex_lock`, `pthread_mutex_trylock`, `pthread_mutex_timedlock`, `pthread_mutex_unlock`, `pthread_mutex_destroy`.
- ☐ **Mutex types**: `NORMAL`, `ERRORCHECK`, `RECURSIVE`, `DEFAULT` — set via `pthread_mutexattr_settype`.
- ☐ **Priority inheritance** (`PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT`) bounds the duration of **priority inversion** to one CS execution.
- ☐ **Priority ceiling** (`PTHREAD_PRIO_PROTECT`) assigns each mutex a fixed ceiling; the holder is boosted to the ceiling.
- ☐ The throughput formula $T = \dfrac{1}{L + p \cdot C}$ is the standard KTU numerical question on mutex performance.
- ☐ **Recursive mutexes** allow the same thread to lock multiple times; they require a *lock count* and equal number of unlocks.
- ☐ **Don't forget `pthread_join()`** in any C demonstration program — without it, the main thread may exit early, producing a wrong counter.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonic for the 3 critical-section conditions:**
> *"**M**any **P**eople **B**ound — Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Wait."* 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concurrency: Deadlock and Starvation - Deadlock Characterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concurrency: Deadlock and Starvation - Deadlock Characterization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Deadlock Characterization?

In the formal study of Operating Systems, **Deadlock Characterization** is the analytical process of precisely *identifying* and *classifying* the conditions under which a set of competing processes can enter a permanent state of mutual blocking. Rather than discussing *how to handle* deadlocks (prevention, avoidance, detection, recovery), characterization is the *foundational step* that defines *what a deadlock fundamentally is* and *what minimal conditions must simultaneously hold* for one to occur.

Formally (as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Module 2), a **deadlock** is defined as:

> A state in a multi-process system in which every process in a non-empty set $\{P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$ is *blocked*, waiting for an event (typically the release of a currently held resource) that can only be triggered by *another blocked process* in the same set. The system remains frozen indefinitely — no process can make forward progress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Coffman Conditions:**
> A deadlock can exist **if and only if** all four of the following conditions hold **simultaneously**. Removing even *one* condition makes a deadlock *impossible*. These are historically attributed to Edward G. Coffman Jr. and M. J. Elphick (1971), and form the **canonical characterization framework** used in the Silberschatz, Galvin & Gagne textbook adopted across KTU modules.

---

## 1.2 The Four Necessary (Coffman) Conditions

| # | Condition | Plain Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Mutual Exclusion** | At least one resource is held in a *non-sharable* mode. |
| 2 | **Hold and Wait** | A process holding a resource may request more. |
| 3 | **No Preemption** | Resources can only be released *voluntarily* by the holding process. |
| 4 | **Circular Wait** | A closed chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Four-Way Traffic Jam"

Imagine four cars arriving **simultaneously** at a narrow four-way intersection with no traffic signal:

- **Car A** wants to go straight but is blocked on its left.
- **Car B** wants to turn right but is blocked by Car C.
- **Car C** wants to go straight but is blocked by Car D.
- **Car D** wants to turn left but is blocked by Car A.

> **Result:** Every car is *holding* part of the intersection (it cannot be **forcibly pushed** out — *no preemption*), each is *waiting* for the other to move (*hold-and-wait* plus *circular wait*), and the intersection itself cannot be **shared** while a car is in it (*mutual exclusion*). The system is **deadlocked** — no car can move without another car moving first.

This is exactly the situation in a multi-process OS when processes hold locks/resources and form a cycle of dependencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (often asked in KTU 2-mark questions):** *Deadlock* is a **permanent** block; *Starvation* is a **prolonged but not necessarily permanent** block caused by unfair scheduling. The two are related (both stem from concurrency) but are *not* the same phenomenon.

---

## 1.4 Resource Categories (Pre-Requisite Vocabulary)

Before characterizing deadlocks, KTU expects you to classify resources correctly:

- **Preemptible resources** — can be forcibly taken away without ill effect (e.g., **CPU**, **memory pages**, **clock cycles**). These typically *cannot* cause deadlocks.
- **Non-preemptible resources** — cannot be safely taken away mid-use (e.g., **printers**, **tape drives**, **database row locks**, **file records held by a transaction**). These *are* the typical deadlock culprits.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks *"Can a deadlock occur on a CPU scheduler?"*, the answer is **no**, because the CPU is preemptible. Deadlocks are predominantly associated with **non-preemptible** resources.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Coffman Conditions — Operational Logic

Below is a structured "why and how" breakdown of each condition, suitable for the KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

### 2.1.1 Mutual Exclusion (ME)

- **Statement:** In a system, at any instant, *at most one* process can use a resource $R_j$ of type $\tau_j$ if that resource is **non-sharable**.
- **Formal:** $\forall R_j \in R, \; \text{allocated}(R_j) \le 1 \;\text{at any instant for a non-sharable }R_j$.
- **Why it matters:** If resources could be read simultaneously by any number of processes, a process could *never* be "blocked waiting" for a resource — it would simply access it.
- **How to relax it:** Make resources *sharable* (e.g., read-only files, re-entrant code, idempotent web APIs).

### 2.1.2 Hold and Wait (HW)

- **Statement:** A process $P_i$ is *currently holding* at least one resource $R_a$ **and** is *simultaneously waiting* to acquire additional resources $R_b, R_c, \ldots$ that are held by other processes.
- **Formal:** $\exists P_i, R_a, R_b$ such that $\text{hold}(P_i, R_a) = \text{true} \;\land\; \text{wait}(P_i, R_b) = \text{true}$ where $\text{hold}(P_b, R_b) = \text{true}$ for some $P_b \ne P_i$.
- **Why it matters:** This is what causes *chains* of waiting. A process releases nothing it already has, so other waiters stay blocked.
- **How to relax it:** Force each process to **request all its resources atomically up front** (before execution begins) — known as *one-shot allocation*. Trade-off: poor resource utilization.

### 2.1.3 No Preemption (NP)

- **Statement:** A resource $R_j$ that has been allocated to process $P_i$ can be **released only by $P_i$ itself**, voluntarily, typically as a final action of the process.
- **Formal:** $\neg \exists P_k \in P, k \ne i, \; \text{such that } \text{forceRelease}(P_k, R_j) \text{ is permitted}$.
- **Why it matters:** If the OS could yank the resource away, the deadlock chain would be broken instantly.
- **How to relax it:** Add preemption — e.g., **virtual memory paging** (a printer cannot be preempted; a page frame can).

### 2.1.4 Circular Wait (CW)

- **Statement:** There exists a set of processes $\{P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$ such that:
  - $P_0$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_1$,
  - $P_1$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_2$,
  - $\ldots$
  - $P_{n-1}$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_n$,
  - $P_n$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_0$.

- **Formal:** $\exists \{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_n\} \subseteq P$ such that
  $$P_0 \xrightarrow{\text{waits}} P_1 \xrightarrow{\text{waits}} P_2 \xrightarrow{\text{waits}} \cdots \xrightarrow{\text{waits}} P_n \xrightarrow{\text{waits}} P_0$$

- **Why it matters:** This is the *closure* condition. Without a cycle, waiting chains are always finite and resolvable.
- **How to relax it:** Impose a **total ordering** on all resources and require that every process request resources in **strictly increasing order** (e.g., always request $R_j$ only if $j > i$ where $R_i$ is the highest-numbered resource currently held).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"M-H-N-C"** → **M**utual exclusion, **H**old & wait, **N**o preemption, **C**ircular wait. *All four* must be present. Remove *one* → no deadlock possible.

---

## 2.2 The Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — The Primary Characterization Tool

KTU examiners expect you to be able to **draw, label, and reason over** a Resource Allocation Graph. Formally:

### Definition 2.2.1 — Resource Allocation Graph $G = (V, E)$

A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:

$$
V = P \cup R, \qquad
P = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\} \text{ (process vertices)},
\qquad
R = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_m\} \text{ (resource vertices)}
$$

$$
E \subseteq (P \times R) \cup (R \times P)
$$

There are **two types of directed edges**:

| Edge | Notation | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| **Request edge** | $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ | Process $P_i$ has *requested* an instance of resource type $R_j$ and is currently **waiting/blocked**. |
| **Assignment edge** | $R_j \rightarrow P_i$ | An instance of resource type $R_j$ has been **allocated** to process $P_i$. |

### Graphical Conventions

- Processes are drawn as **circles** labeled $P_i$.
- Resources are drawn as **rectangles**, with **dots inside** representing the number of instances.
- A request edge is a directed arrow from the *process circle* to the *resource rectangle*.
- An assignment edge is a directed arrow from the *resource rectangle (or one of its instance dots)* to the *process circle*.

---

## 2.3 The Four Fundamental Theorems of RAG (Exam Gold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four theorems are the *exact* results you must be able to state and apply in KTU 14-mark questions. They are taken directly from the Silberschatz-Galvin-Gagne framework that KTU follows.

- **Theorem 1 (No cycle ⇒ No deadlock):** If the graph $G$ contains **no cycle**, then **no process in the system is deadlocked**.
- **Theorem 2 (Cycle + single instance ⇒ Deadlock):** If the graph $G$ contains a cycle **and** every resource type has **exactly one instance**, then **all processes in the cycle are deadlocked**.
- **Theorem 3 (Cycle + multiple instances ⇒ Deadlock possible but not certain):** If the graph $G$ contains a cycle **but** some resource types have **multiple instances**, then a deadlock **may or may not exist**. Further analysis (e.g., the Banker's Algorithm) is required to confirm.
- **Theorem 4 (No cycle ⇒ not even potential deadlock):** A cycle is a *necessary* condition for deadlock. Hence the absence of a cycle guarantees freedom from deadlock.

---

## 2.4 The Wait-For Graph (WFG) — Variant for Single-Instance Systems

To simplify cycle detection, the **wait-for graph** is derived from the RAG by:

1. **Removing all resource nodes** $R_j$.
2. Drawing an edge $P_i \rightarrow P_k$ *iff* in the RAG there exists a request edge $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ and an assignment edge $R_j \rightarrow P_k$ for some resource $R_j$.

> **Equivalence:** For systems where *every* resource type has a single instance, **the wait-for graph has a cycle $\iff$ the system is deadlocked**. This is because Theorem 2 above applies directly.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Set of vertices | $V = P \cup R$ | Disjoint union |
| 2 | Request edge | $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ | $P_i$ is **blocked** on $R_j$ |
| 3 | Assignment edge | $R_j \rightarrow P_i$ | An instance of $R_j$ is held by $P_i$ |
| 4 | Cycle existence | $\exists \text{ cycle in } G$ | *Necessary* for deadlock |
| 5 | Single-instance RAG | $\text{cycle} \iff \text{deadlock}$ | Theorems 1 & 2 |
| 6 | Multi-instance RAG | $\text{cycle} \Rightarrow \text{deadlock possible}$ | Not sufficient |
| 7 | Wait-for edge | $P_i \rightarrow P_k$ | Transitive closure of $P_i \rightarrow R_j \rightarrow P_k$ |
| 8 | Total-order trick | $\text{Request}(R_j) \text{ only if } j > \max(\text{indices held})$ | Breaks CW condition |
| 9 | Preemptibility | $\text{Preemptible} \Rightarrow \text{No deadlock possible}$ | E.g., CPU, memory |
| 10 | Number of held instances of $R_j$ | $\sum_{P_i \in P} \mathbf{1}[\text{alloc}(P_i, R_j)] \le \text{instances}(R_j)$ | Capacity constraint |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Memorize the *direction* of the arrows. Students frequently reverse request and assignment edges and lose 2-3 marks in a 14-mark question for a single mistyped arrow. Rule of thumb: *Who is "doing" the action points to whom the action is being done to.* $P_i$ requests $R_j$ → $P_i$ points at $R_j$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Characterizing deadlocks is not academic. It underpins:

- **Database Transaction Systems (DBMS):** Every modern DBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle) maintains an internal **wait-for graph** to detect deadlocks and auto-abort one transaction (`Deadlock found; try restarting transaction`).
- **Java Concurrency:** `ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads()` literally runs cycle detection on the JVM's internal WFG.
- **Distributed Systems (Hadoop, Spark, Kubernetes):** Distributed deadlock detection algorithms like the **Chandy-Misra-Haas algorithm** propagate probes along a *probe graph* to detect cycles across multiple machines.
- **Telecommunications & Embedded Real-Time Systems (AUTOSAR):** Priority-inversion and resource deadlocks are formally verified using RAG-style analysis before deployment in cars and aircraft.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, line-by-line analysis of how the characterization theorems are applied to concrete RAG examples, the kind that appear in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — RAG with a Cycle in a Single-Instance System (→ DEADLOCK)

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider:
- Processes: $P_1, P_2, P_3$
- Resources: $R_1$ (1 instance), $R_2$ (1 instance), $R_3$ (1 instance), $R_4$ (1 instance)
- State:
  - $R_1 \rightarrow P_1$ (assigned to $P_1$)
  - $R_2 \rightarrow P_2$ (assigned to $P_2$)
  - $R_3 \rightarrow P_3$ (assigned to $P_3$)
  - $P_1 \rightarrow R_2$ (requests $R_2$)
  - $P_2 \rightarrow R_3$ (requests $R_3$)
  - $P_3 \rightarrow R_1$ (requests $R_1$)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Cycle Detection

**Step 1 —** Identify all assignment edges. We have $R_1 \rightarrow P_1$, $R_2 \rightarrow P_2$, $R_3 \rightarrow P_3$.

**Step 2 —** Identify all request edges. We have $P_1 \rightarrow R_2$, $P_2 \rightarrow R_3$, $P_3 \rightarrow R_1$.

**Step 3 —** Try to traverse from $P_1$ following the edges:

$$P_1 \xrightarrow{\text{requests}} R_2 \xrightarrow{\text{assigned to}} P_2 \xrightarrow{\text{requests}} R_3 \xrightarrow{\text{assigned to}} P_3 \xrightarrow{\text{requests}} R_1 \xrightarrow{\text{assigned to}} P_1$$

**Step 4 —** The traversal returned to $P_1$. Therefore the path

$$P_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_1$$

forms a closed cycle of length **6 edges**.

**Step 5 —** Verify single-instance condition. $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ each have exactly one instance, satisfying the single-instance hypothesis.

**Step 6 —** Apply Theorem 2. A cycle in a single-instance RAG **is a sufficient condition for deadlock**. Hence all three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ are **deadlocked**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Coffman conditions in this state:**
> - **ME:** ✓ Each resource is non-sharable (only one instance).
> - **HW:** ✓ $P_1$ holds $R_1$ and waits for $R_2$; $P_2$ holds $R_2$ and waits for $R_3$; $P_3$ holds $R_3$ and waits for $R_1$.
> - **NP:** ✓ No preemption — the OS cannot take $R_1$ from $P_1$.
> - **CW:** ✓ $P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow P_1$ is a closed chain.
>
> All four hold → **deadlock confirmed**.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — RAG with a Cycle in a Multi-Instance System (→ DEADLOCK ABSENT)

### 3.2.1 Setup

Consider:
- Processes: $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$
- Resources: $R_1$ (2 instances), $R_2$ (2 instances), $R_3$ (2 instances), $R_4$ (2 instances)
- State:
  - $R_1 \rightarrow P_1$, $R_2 \rightarrow P_1$ (both an instance of $R_1$ and of $R_2$ are held by $P_1$)
  - $R_1 \rightarrow P_2$ (second instance of $R_1$ held by $P_2$)
  - $R_3 \rightarrow P_2$ (an instance of $R_3$ held by $P_2$)
  - $R_2 \rightarrow P_3$ (second instance of $R_2$ held by $P_3$)
  - $R_3 \rightarrow P_3$ (an instance of $R_3$ held by $P_3$)
  - $R_4 \rightarrow P_4$ (one instance of $R_4$ held by $P_4$)
  - $P_1 \rightarrow R_3$ (request)
  - $P_2 \rightarrow R_4$ (request)
  - $P_3 \rightarrow R_1$ (request)
  - $P_4 \rightarrow R_2$ (request)

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 —** Examine the requests:
- $P_1$ requests $R_3$ → $R_3$ is held by $P_2$ and $P_3$.
- $P_2$ requests $R_4$ → $R_4$ is held by $P_4$.
- $P_3$ requests $R_1$ → $R_1$ is held by $P_1$ and $P_2$.
- $P_4$ requests $R_2$ → $R_2$ is held by $P_1$ and $P_3$.

**Step 2 —** Attempt to find a cycle. We try:

$$P_1 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_4 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow P_1$$

This is a closed cycle: $P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_4 \rightarrow P_1$ (length 3 in the wait-for sense).

**Step 3 —** **But** every resource has **multiple instances**. Theorem 2 does **not** apply. We must attempt to break the cycle.

**Step 4 —** Check if any process has **all** its requests satisfiable:
- $P_1$ requests $R_3$. Two instances of $R_3$ are available (held by $P_2$ and $P_3$). If either $P_2$ or $P_3$ finishes, $P_1$ can be unblocked.
- $P_4$ requests $R_2$. Two instances of $R_2$ exist. Similarly unblockable.

**Step 5 —** Suppose $P_2$ is preempted (or finishes) — releasing $R_1$ and $R_3$. Now $P_3$'s request for $R_1$ can be satisfied, $P_3$ completes and releases $R_2, R_3$. Now $P_1$'s request for $R_3$ is satisfied, $P_1$ completes and releases $R_1, R_2$. Then $P_4$'s request for $R_2$ is satisfied.

**Step 6 —** All processes can complete. **The cycle exists, but no deadlock exists** in the multi-instance case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away (KTU Frequently Asked):** *A cycle in the RAG is necessary for deadlock but not sufficient in the multi-instance case.* This is the single most-tested nuance in KTU Module 2.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Wait-For Graph from the RAG

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a single-instance RAG. Construct the wait-for graph $G_W = (P, E_W)$ as follows.

**Construction rule:**

$$
(P_i, P_k) \in E_W \;\;\iff\;\; \exists R_j \in R \; : \; (P_i, R_j) \in E \;\land\; (R_j, P_k) \in E
$$

**Step-by-step application to a concrete RAG:**

Suppose RAG edges are:
- $P_1 \rightarrow R_2$ (request)
- $R_1 \rightarrow P_1$ (assigned)
- $P_2 \rightarrow R_1$ (request)
- $R_2 \rightarrow P_2$ (assigned)
- $P_3 \rightarrow R_3$ (request)
- $R_3 \rightarrow P_3$ (assigned)

**Step A:** Identify request edges: $P_1 \rightarrow R_2$, $P_2 \rightarrow R_1$, $P_3 \rightarrow R_3$.

**Step B:** For each request, identify the resource holder:
- $P_1$ requests $R_2$ → held by $P_2$ → WFG edge: $P_1 \rightarrow P_2$.
- $P_2$ requests $R_1$ → held by $P_1$ → WFG edge: $P_2 \rightarrow P_1$.
- $P_3$ requests $R_3$ → held by $P_3$ → WFG edge: $P_3 \rightarrow P_3$ (self-loop).

**Step C:** Detect cycles in the WFG:
- $P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_1$ is a cycle of length 2 → $P_1$ and $P_2$ are deadlocked.
- $P_3 \rightarrow P_3$ is a self-loop → $P_3$ is in **deadlock with itself** (a single process is waiting for a resource it itself holds — a special degenerate deadlock case).

**Step D:** Apply Theorem 2 (single-instance applies to original RAG). Conclusion is consistent: $P_1, P_2, P_3$ are all deadlocked.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Single-Instance vs. Multi-Instance

| Property | Single-Instance RAG | Multi-Instance RAG |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Cycle $\Rightarrow$ deadlock? | **YES** (Theorem 2) | **NO** (only possibility, Theorem 3) |
| No cycle $\Rightarrow$ no deadlock? | **YES** | **YES** |
| Detection algorithm | Simple DFS cycle detection on WFG | Banker's algorithm or Wait-Die / Wound-Wait graph search |
| Required data structure | WFG (after resource collapse) | Full RAG plus instance count vector |
| Complexity of detection | $O(V + E)$ | $O(m \cdot n^2)$ for Banker's |
| Example in KTU 2018, 2022 papers | "Identify whether the system is deadlocked given the RAG" | "Apply Banker's algorithm to verify safety" |

---

## 3.5 Derivation of "Total-Order Prevents Circular Wait"

**Claim:** If we assign a unique integer to every resource type and require every process to request resources only in *strictly increasing* order, then circular wait is impossible.

**Proof (by contradiction):**

Suppose a circular wait exists:

$$P_0 \rightarrow R_{a_0} \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow R_{a_1} \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow P_n \rightarrow R_{a_n} \rightarrow P_0$$

By the order rule:
- $P_0$ holds $R_{a_n}$ (from the incoming edge $R_{a_n} \rightarrow P_0$) and requests $R_{a_0}$ (the outgoing edge $P_0 \rightarrow R_{a_0}$). So $a_0 > a_n$.
- $P_1$ holds $R_{a_0}$ and requests $R_{a_1}$. So $a_1 > a_0$.
- Continuing, $a_2 > a_1$, $a_3 > a_2$, $\ldots$, $a_n > a_{n-1}$.

Chaining these inequalities:

$$
a_n < a_0 < a_1 < a_2 < \cdots < a_n
$$

The leftmost and rightmost term are both $a_n$, giving $a_n < a_n$ — a contradiction. $\blacksquare$

This is the standard OS textbook proof for why resource ordering kills the circular-wait condition.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams below use the **Mermaid** flow-chart convention and strictly observe the safety rules: alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no markdown formatting inside quoted labels, no reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) as node identifiers.

---

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Four Coffman Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["DEADLOCK EXISTS IN THE SYSTEM"] --> B{"All Four Coffman Conditions Hold Simultaneously?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["YES - System is Deadlocked"]
    B -- No --> D["NO - Deadlock Impossible"]
    
    subgraph C1 ["Condition Set"]
        S1["MUTUAL EXCLUSION: At least one resource is non-sharable"]
        S2["HOLD AND WAIT: Process holds at least one resource and waits for others"]
        S3["NO PREEMPTION: Resources can be released only voluntarily"]
        S4["CIRCULAR WAIT: Closed chain of waiting processes exists"]
    end
    
    C1 --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — Example with a Cycle, Single-Instance System (DEADLOCK)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1(("P1")) -- Request Edge P1 to R2 --> R2["R2: 1 instance"]
    R1["R1: 1 instance"] -- Assignment Edge R1 to P1 --> P1
    P2(("P2")) -- Request Edge P2 to R3 --> R3["R3: 1 instance"]
    R2 -- Assignment Edge R2 to P2 --> P2
    P3(("P3")) -- Request Edge P3 to R1 --> R1
    R3 -- Assignment Edge R3 to P3 --> P3
```

> **Visual Reading:** Starting at P1, follow the cycle: P1 → R2 → P2 → R3 → P3 → R1 → P1. All resources are **single-instance**, so by **Theorem 2** this is a **deadlock**.

---

## 4.3 Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — Cycle in Multi-Instance System (NO DEADLOCK)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1(("P1")) -- Requests R3 --> R3["R3: 2 instances"]
    R1["R1: 2 instances"] -- One Instance to P1 --> P1
    R2["R2: 2 instances"] -- One Instance to P1 --> P1
    R1 -- One Instance to P2 --> P2(("P2"))
    R3 -- One Instance to P2 --> P2
    P2 -- Requests R4 --> R4["R4: 1 instance"]
    R2 -- One Instance to P3 --> P3(("P3"))
    R3 -- One Instance to P3 --> P3
    P3 -- Requests R1 --> R1
    R4 -- One Instance to P4 --> P4(("P4"))
    P4 -- Requests R2 --> R2
```

> **Visual Reading:** A cycle $P_1 \to P_2 \to P_4 \to P_1$ exists **but** multiple instances of $R_1, R_2, R_3$ exist, so the cycle can be broken. By **Theorem 3**, **deadlock is not certain**.

---

## 4.4 Wait-For Graph (WFG) — Derived from the RAG in Section 4.2

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1(("P1")) -- Waits for R2 held by --> P2(("P2"))
    P2(("P2")) -- Waits for R3 held by --> P3(("P3"))
    P3(("P3")) -- Waits for R1 held by --> P1(("P1")))
```

> **Visual Reading:** The WFG is the **RAG with all resource nodes removed** and **paths collapsed** into direct process-to-process edges. A cycle in the WFG (as seen above) directly indicates deadlock in a single-instance system.

---

## 4.5 High-Level Block Architecture — How the OS Uses RAG for Deadlock Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT ["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["Process Resource Requests"]
        I2["Process Resource Releases"]
    end
    
    subgraph GRAPH ["GRAPH MAINTENANCE LAYER"]
        G1["Update RAG Vertices: V = P union R"]
        G2["Add Request Edge on Block"]
        G3["Add Assignment Edge on Allocation"]
        G4["Remove Edge on Release"]
    end
    
    subgraph ANALYSIS ["ANALYSIS LAYER"]
        A1["Run DFS Cycle Detection"]
        A2{"Cycle Found?"}
        A3["Single-Instance Check"]
        A4{"All resources have one instance?"}
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT ["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["No Deadlock"]
        O2["Deadlock Confirmed - Trigger Recovery"]
        O3["Cycle but Multi-Instance - Run Banker Algorithm"]
    end
    
    I1 --> G2
    I2 --> G4
    G2 --> G1
    G3 --> G1
    G4 --> G1
    G1 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 -- No --> O1
    A2 -- Yes --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 -- Yes --> O2
    A4 -- No --> O3
```

> **Interpretation:** This block diagram captures the **complete workflow** of the OS in *using* the RAG as a characterization tool. Real OS kernels (Linux, Windows NT) implement an *approximation* of this pipeline for deadlock detection.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Define deadlock. List and briefly explain the four necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur in a system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **deadlock** is a permanent blocking state in which a set of processes are all waiting for an event that can only be triggered by another member of the same set. (1 mark)

The **four necessary conditions** (Coffman conditions) are: (2 marks, 0.5 each)

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode; only one process can use it at a time.
2. **Hold and Wait:** A process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other processes.
3. **No Preemption:** Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process; they can only be released voluntarily.
4. **Circular Wait:** A closed chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next process in the chain.

All four must hold *simultaneously* for a deadlock to occur.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2]`

**What is a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)? What are the two types of edges it contains?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ used to depict the allocation state of a system, where $V = P \cup R$ (processes and resources). (1.5 marks)

The two types of edges are: (1.5 marks)

- **Request edge** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$: indicates that process $P_i$ is currently waiting for an instance of resource type $R_j$.
- **Assignment edge** $R_j \rightarrow P_i$: indicates that an instance of resource type $R_j$ has been allocated to process $P_i$.

A cycle in the RAG is a necessary condition for deadlock. With single-instance resources, a cycle is also sufficient; with multi-instance resources, it is only a *possibility*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each 14-mark question typically carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The model answer below mirrors KTU board valuation expectations: clear headings, neat diagrams, and theorem-level statements.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]`

> **"Explain in detail the four necessary (Coffman) conditions that must hold simultaneously for a deadlock to occur in a multi-process operating system. Illustrate with a suitable Resource Allocation Graph and identify a deadlock if present."**

#### Part (a) — The Four Coffman Conditions (7 marks)

The four Coffman conditions are as follows:

**1. Mutual Exclusion:** Every resource is either currently available or assigned to exactly one process. Non-sharable resources such as printers, tape drives, and exclusive database locks cannot be used by more than one process at a time. (1.5 marks)

**2. Hold and Wait:** A process that has been allocated some resources may request additional resources that are currently allocated to other processes. Thus the process "holds" what it has and simultaneously "waits" for more. (1.5 marks)

**3. No Preemption:** Resources already allocated to a process cannot be forcibly taken away. They can be released only voluntarily by the process after it has completed its task. (1.5 marks)

**4. Circular Wait:** A set of processes $\{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_n\}$ must exist such that $P_0$ waits for a resource held by $P_1$, $P_1$ waits for a resource held by $P_2$, …, and $P_n$ waits for a resource held by $P_0$. (1.5 marks)

The four conditions are **necessary but not independently sufficient** — they must all hold **simultaneously** for a deadlock to exist. Removing any one condition makes a deadlock impossible. (1 mark for concluding statement)

#### Part (b) — RAG Illustration and Deadlock Identification (7 marks)

**Setup:** Let us consider a system with three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ and three resource types $R_1, R_2, R_3$, **each with a single instance**.

State of the system:
- $R_1$ is allocated to $P_1$, $R_2$ is allocated to $P_2$, $R_3$ is allocated to $P_3$.
- $P_1$ requests $R_2$, $P_2$ requests $R_3$, $P_3$ requests $R_1$.

**RAG Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1(("P1")) -- request R2 --> R2["R2: 1"]
    P2(("P2")) -- request R3 --> R3["R3: 1"]
    P3(("P3")) -- request R1 --> R1["R1: 1"]
    R1 -- assigned to P1 --> P1
    R2 -- assigned to P2 --> P2
    R3 -- assigned to P3 --> P3
```

**Cycle Detection (2 marks):**
Traversing the graph starting from $P_1$:

$$P_1 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_1$$

A cycle of length 6 exists.

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
Since every resource has only **one instance**, by **Theorem 2** the presence of a cycle in the RAG is a *sufficient condition* for deadlock. Therefore all three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ are **deadlocked**, and each of the four Coffman conditions holds in this state. Hence a deadlock is confirmed.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2022, Module 2]`

> **"Characterize deadlock using the Resource Allocation Graph. Differentiate between single-instance and multi-instance resource systems. With a suitable example, explain how a Wait-for Graph is derived from the RAG for cycle detection."**

#### Part (a) — RAG and Characterization (7 marks)

**Definition of RAG (2 marks):** A Resource Allocation Graph is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V = P \cup R$ (process set and resource set) and $E$ consists of request edges ($P_i \rightarrow R_j$) and assignment edges ($R_j \rightarrow P_i$).

**Characterization theorems (3 marks):**

- **If the RAG has no cycle** $\Rightarrow$ the system is **deadlock-free**.
- **If the RAG has a cycle and every resource type has a single instance** $\Rightarrow$ the system **is** in deadlock.
- **If the RAG has a cycle but at least one resource type has multiple instances** $\Rightarrow$ a deadlock **may or may not exist**; further analysis (such as the Banker's Algorithm) is needed.

**Single vs. Multi-Instance Distinction (2 marks):** In single-instance systems, the RAG can be reduced to a simpler **Wait-for Graph** for $O(V+E)$ cycle detection. In multi-instance systems, the per-resource instance counts must be tracked and algorithms like Banker's Algorithm ($O(mn^2)$) are required for accurate deadlock determination.

#### Part (b) — Wait-for Graph Derivation (7 marks)

**Example Setup (2 marks):** Consider a system with three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ and three resource types $R_1, R_2, R_3$, each with a single instance. The RAG has the following edges:
- $P_1 \rightarrow R_2$ (request), $P_2 \rightarrow R_3$ (request), $P_3 \rightarrow R_1$ (request)
- $R_1 \rightarrow P_2$ (assignment), $R_2 \rightarrow P_1$ (assignment), $R_3 \rightarrow P_3$ (assignment)

**Derivation Rule (2 marks):** From the RAG, an edge $P_i \rightarrow P_k$ is added to the WFG **iff** in the RAG there exists a resource $R_j$ with a request edge $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ and an assignment edge $R_j \rightarrow P_k$.

**Construction (1 mark):**
- $P_1$ requests $R_2$, held by $P_1$ itself — no WFG edge (self-loop omitted).
- $P_2$ requests $R_3$, held by $P_3$ → WFG edge $P_2 \rightarrow P_3$.
- $P_3$ requests $R_1$, held by $P_2$ → WFG edge $P_3 \rightarrow P_2$.

**Resulting WFG (1 mark):** $P_2 \leftrightarrow P_3$ form a 2-cycle, indicating a deadlock between $P_2$ and $P_3$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For single-instance systems, the existence of a cycle in the WFG is equivalent to a deadlock in the underlying RAG. This simplification is what makes the WFG the preferred cycle-detection tool in single-instance systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Arrow direction confusion (–2 marks):** Students frequently draw assignment edges as $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ (the opposite of the correct $R_j \rightarrow P_i$). Memorize: the **owner** is at the *arrowhead's* target.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to state "all four" must hold (–1.5 marks):** A common 7-mark part (a) answer lists the four conditions but fails to explicitly state that *simultaneity* is the key. Always end with: *"All four must hold **simultaneously**."*
> 
> 3. **Treating cycle = deadlock universally (–2 marks):** Stating "RAG has cycle $\Rightarrow$ deadlock" without qualifying the **single-instance** assumption. Always add: *"Provided each resource type has only one instance. In multi-instance cases, the cycle is only a *possibility*."*
> 
> 4. **Confusing wait-for graph with RAG (–1 mark):** The WFG has *no resource nodes*. Forgetting to state this explicit construction step costs a mark.
> 
> 5. **Omitting the example/proof (–2 marks):** A purely textual 7-mark answer on Coffman conditions without *any* diagram is marked down under KTU's "illustrate with RAG" phrasing.
> 
> 6. **Using "no preemption" as an excuse to skip justification (–1 mark):** Always *justify* why preemption-breaking would be unsafe in the specific example (e.g., a printer mid-page).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Deadlock Characterization**

- ✅ **Deadlock** = permanent block of a set of processes, each waiting for a resource held by another in the set.
- ✅ The **four Coffman conditions** are: **M**utual **E**xclusion, **H**old and **W**ait, **N**o **P**reemption, **C**ircular **W**ait. **Mnemonic: M-H-N-C.**
- ✅ All four must hold **simultaneously** — remove any *one* and deadlock becomes impossible.
- ✅ **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** has vertices $V = P \cup R$ and two edge types: **request edge** ($P_i \rightarrow R_j$) and **assignment edge** ($R_j \rightarrow P_i$).
- ✅ **Graphical convention:** processes = circles, resources = rectangles, dots = instances.
- ✅ **No cycle ⇒ no deadlock** (this is *always* true).
- ✅ **Cycle + single instance ⇒ deadlock** (Theorem 2 — sufficient).
- ✅ **Cycle + multi-instance ⇒ deadlock *possible*, not certain** (Theorem 3).
- ✅ **Wait-for graph** = RAG with resources removed and edges collapsed to $P_i \rightarrow P_k$.
- ✅ For **single-instance** systems, WFG cycle $\iff$ deadlock.
- ✅ **CPU and memory are preemptible** → typically *not* involved in deadlocks. **Printers, tape drives, DB locks** are non-preemptible → typical deadlock culprits.
- ✅ **Total ordering of resources** (request only in increasing order) is the standard trick to *break* the circular-wait condition.
- ✅ **Real-world detection tools:** JVM `ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads()`, DBMS internal WFGs, Linux kernel's `lockdep` subsystem, AUTOSAR static analysis.
- ✅ **Cycle detection complexity** in WFG is $O(V + E)$ using DFS; in multi-instance RAG, **Banker's Algorithm** is $O(mn^2)$.
- ✅ **Always draw the RAG** in your 14-mark answer — verbal descriptions without diagrams are penalized.

---

> 🎯 **End of Topic Notes — Deadlock Characterization, Module 2.**
> Proceed to the next sub-topic: *Methods for Handling Deadlocks* (Prevention, Avoidance, Detection, Recovery) for full KTU Module 2 coverage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadlock Prevention and Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deadlock Prevention and Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadlock** is a state in which two or more processes are permanently blocked, each waiting for a resource held by the other. According to the **Coffman Conditions**, a deadlock can arise only if all four of the following conditions hold simultaneously:
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion**
> 2. **Hold and Wait**
> 3. **No Preemption**
> 4. **Circular Wait**

**Deadlock Prevention** is a set of techniques that ensure that **at least one of the four Coffman conditions cannot hold**, thereby making the deadlock state structurally impossible.

**Deadlock Avoidance** is a more refined approach in which the operating system uses *a priori* knowledge about the maximum resource requirements of every process, and dynamically decides whether to grant a resource request. The system ensures it **never enters an unsafe state**, even though all four Coffman conditions may still hold.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a single-lane bridge with cars entering from both ends.**
> - **Mutual Exclusion:** Only one car can occupy a section of the bridge at a time.
> - **Hold and Wait:** A car refuses to reverse off the bridge until it has crossed the entire span.
> - **No Preemption:** A tow truck cannot physically lift a car off the middle of the bridge.
> - **Circular Wait:** Car A (heading East) is waiting for Car B (heading West), while Car B is waiting for Car A.

In this scenario:
- **Deadlock Prevention** = *rebuild the bridge as a double-lane road* (breaking *Mutual Exclusion*) OR *force cars to back up* (breaking *Hold and Wait*) OR *add a tow-truck override* (breaking *No Preemption*) OR *only allow one-way traffic at a time* (breaking *Circular Wait*).
- **Deadlock Avoidance** = *a traffic controller at the entry point who knows how many cars are coming, and only allows a car onto the bridge if he is mathematically certain every waiting car will eventually cross*. This controller does not rebuild the bridge — he just manages the flow carefully using the **Banker's Algorithm**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Vocabulary (KTU Board Terminology)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Safe State** | A state in which there exists *at least one* sequence of process executions (a *safe sequence*) that allows all processes to complete even if all of them suddenly request their maximum resources. |
| **Unsafe State** | A state with no guaranteed safe sequence. Deadlock **may** occur, but is **not guaranteed**. |
| **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** | A directed graph used to describe the state of resource allocation. |
| **Banker's Algorithm** | Dijkstra's classic avoidance algorithm for systems with multiple instances of each resource type. |
| **Request Edge** | $P_i \rightarrow R_j$: Process $P_i$ is requesting an instance of resource type $R_j$. |
| **Assignment Edge** | $R_j \rightarrow P_i$: An instance of resource type $R_j$ has been allocated to $P_i$. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deadlock Prevention Strategies

Prevention aims to make **at least one** of the four Coffman conditions *false by design*.

### 2.1.1 Attacking Mutual Exclusion
- **Idea:** Make the resource *shareable*.
- **Applicability:** **Only works for inherently non-mutual-exclusive resources** (e.g., read-only files).
- **Limitation:** Cannot be applied to intrinsically non-sharable resources (printers, tape drives, mutex locks).
- **Verdict in KTU exams:** Rarely a viable option in practice; usually rejected.

### 2.1.2 Attacking Hold and Wait
- **Idea 1:** A process must request **all** the resources it needs *before* execution begins.
- **Idea 2:** A process is allowed to request resources **only when it has none** (i.e., it must release all currently held resources before requesting new ones).
- **Drawback:** **Low resource utilization** and possible **starvation**.

### 2.1.3 Attacking No Preemption
- **Idea:** If a process holding certain resources requests another resource that *cannot be immediately allocated*, then **all resources currently held are preempted** (forcibly taken away).
- **Preempted resources are added** to the waiting list of the process.
- **Applicability:** Works only for resources whose **state can be easily saved and restored** (CPU registers, memory). **Not feasible for printers or tape drives** (state cannot be cleanly saved).
- **Verdict in KTU exams:** Practical for CPU and memory; impractical for most I/O devices.

### 2.1.4 Attacking Circular Wait
- **Idea:** Impose a **total ordering** on all resource types. Each process must request resources in *strictly increasing* order of enumeration.
- **Formal Rule:** If $F: R \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ is an ordering function, then a process can request $R_j$ only if $F(R_j) > F(R_i)$ for every currently held $R_i$.
- **Drawback:** No natural ordering exists for all resources; programmers must rewrite resource-request logic.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hot Tip:** The most commonly tested prevention technique is **Circular Wait prevention via resource ordering**, because it is general, deterministic, and does not waste resources.

---

## 2.2 Deadlock Avoidance

### 2.2.1 The Safe State Concept
A system is in a **safe state** if there exists a safe sequence $<P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n>$ such that for every $P_i$, the resources that $P_i$ can still request can be satisfied by the *currently available resources* plus the resources held by *all $P_j$ with $j < i$*.

### 2.2.2 The Resource Allocation Graph Algorithm (Single Instance per Resource Type)
- Add a **claim edge** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ (dashed line) for *every future possible request*.
- A request can be granted **only if converting the request edge to an assignment edge does not create a cycle** in the graph.
- Cycle detection = $O(V+E)$ per request (DFS/BFS based).

### 2.2.3 The Banker's Algorithm (Multiple Instances per Resource Type)
Used when each resource type has *multiple identical instances*. Requires **four data structures** (all matrices of size $n \times m$ for $n$ processes and $m$ resource types).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of processes |
| $m$ | Number of resource types |
| $\text{Allocation}[i][j]$ | Instances of $R_j$ currently held by $P_i$ |
| $\text{Max}[i][j]$ | Maximum instances of $R_j$ that $P_i$ may ever request |
| $\text{Need}[i][j]$ | Remaining instances of $R_j$ that $P_i$ may still need |
| $\text{Available}[j]$ | Free instances of $R_j$ in the system |
| $\text{Need} = \text{Max} - \text{Allocation}$ | Core identity for Banker's Algorithm |
| $\text{Work} := \text{Available}$ initially | Working vector in the Safety Algorithm |
| $\text{Finish}[i] \in \{\text{false}, \text{true}\}$ | Process $P_i$ has yet to / has finished executing |
| $\text{Work} := \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_i$ | After $P_i$ completes, its resources are released |
| $\text{Request}_i \le \text{Need}_i$ | Validity check: process cannot request more than declared max |
| $\text{Request}_i \le \text{Available}$ | Validity check: enough resources must exist right now |
| Safe $\Rightarrow$ No Deadlock | The single most important implication |
| Unsafe $\nRightarrow$ Deadlock | The classic KTU exam trap question |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** An *unsafe state does not guarantee a deadlock* — it simply means the system *cannot prove* a deadlock will not occur. Many students incorrectly claim "unsafe = deadlock." That costs 1 mark.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Transaction Managers** use Banker-style logic to detect lock-acquisition orders.
- **Embedded RTOS kernels** (VxWorks, FreeRTOS with mutex priority inheritance) implement *circular-wait prevention* by enforcing mutex acquisition order.
- **Cloud Orchestrators (Kubernetes)** use *quota-based prevention* — a pod cannot request more CPU than its namespace allows, breaking *Hold and Wait*.
- **Java HotSpot JVM** employs conservative Banker-like analysis for biased locking decisions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Banker's Algorithm Core Identities

We start with $n$ processes and $m$ resource types. Let $\text{Available}_j$ denote the unallocated instances of resource type $j$.

**Step 1:** Define $\text{Need}[i][j]$ formally as the *remaining maximum claim*:

$$
\text{Need}[i][j] = \text{Max}[i][j] - \text{Allocation}[i][j]
$$

**Step 2:** The *total* number of instances of $R_j$ in the system is the sum of all allocations plus the currently available pool:

$$
\text{Total}[j] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Allocation}[i][j] + \text{Available}[j]
$$

**Step 3:** A request $\text{Request}_i$ from process $P_i$ must satisfy *two guard conditions* before it is even considered:

$$
\text{Request}_i[j] \le \text{Need}_i[j] \quad \forall j \in \{1,\dots,m\}
$$

$$
\text{Request}_i[j] \le \text{Available}[j] \quad \forall j \in \{1,\dots,m\}
$$

**Step 4:** If both guards pass, the system **pretends** to allocate the resources (a tentative state) and runs the Safety Algorithm:

$$
\text{Available}' = \text{Available} - \text{Request}_i
$$

$$
\text{Allocation}_i' = \text{Allocation}_i + \text{Request}_i
$$

$$
\text{Need}_i' = \text{Need}_i - \text{Request}_i
$$

**Step 5:** If the Safety Algorithm finds a safe sequence on the primed state, the allocation is **committed**. Otherwise, $P_i$ is **rolled back** to the original state and must wait.

---

## 3.2 Safety Algorithm — Step-by-Step Pseudocode Derivation

```
Input:  Available[m], Allocation[n][m], Need[n][m]
Output: A safe sequence, or "UNSAFE"
```

```
Step A.  Initialize:
         Work := Available
         Finish[i] := false   for all i = 1..n

Step B.  Find an index i such that:
             (Finish[i] == false) AND (Need[i] ≤ Work)
         If no such i exists, goto Step D.

Step C.  Pretend P_i completes:
             Work := Work + Allocation[i]
             Finish[i] := true
         Goto Step B.

Step D.  If Finish[i] == true for ALL i:
             Return the recorded sequence as a SAFE sequence.
         Else:
             Return UNSAFE.
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**System has 5 processes $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ and 3 resource types $A, B, C$ with total instances $(10, 5, 7)$.**

Given the matrices:

| Process | Allocation (A B C) | Max (A B C) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_0$ | (0 1 0) | (7 5 3) |
| $P_1$ | (2 0 0) | (3 2 2) |
| $P_2$ | (3 0 2) | (9 0 2) |
| $P_3$ | (2 1 1) | (2 2 2) |
| $P_4$ | (0 0 2) | (4 3 3) |

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{Available}$:**

$$
\text{Total A used} = 0 + 2 + 3 + 2 + 0 = 7 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Available A} = 10 - 7 = 3
$$

$$
\text{Total B used} = 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Available B} = 5 - 2 = 3
$$

$$
\text{Total C used} = 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Available C} = 7 - 5 = 2
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Available} = (3,\; 3,\; 2)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{Need} = \text{Max} - \text{Allocation}$:**

| Process | Need (A B C) |
|---|---|
| $P_0$ | (7 4 3) |
| $P_1$ | (1 2 2) |
| $P_2$ | (6 0 0) |
| $P_3$ | (0 1 1) |
| $P_4$ | (4 3 1) |

**Step 3 — Run the Safety Algorithm. Initialize:**

$$
\text{Work} = (3,\; 3,\; 2), \quad \text{Finish} = (\text{F, F, F, F, F})
$$

**Iteration 1:** Check each unfinished process:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Need}(P_0) = (7,4,3) &\not\le (3,3,2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{skip} \\
\text{Need}(P_1) = (1,2,2) &\le (3,3,2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{select } P_1 \\
\text{Need}(P_2) = (6,0,0) &\not\le (3,3,2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{skip} \\
\text{Need}(P_3) = (0,1,1) &\not\le (3,3,2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{skip (B=1 \le 3 but A=0 \le 3; actually checks all)} \\
\text{Need}(P_4) = (4,3,1) &\not\le (3,3,2) \quad \Rightarrow \text{skip}
\end{aligned}
$$

After $P_1$ finishes:

$$
\text{Work} = (3,3,2) + (2,0,0) = (5,\; 3,\; 2), \quad \text{Finish}[1] = \text{T}
$$

**Iteration 2:** Pick the smallest $i$ with $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{F}$ and $\text{Need}_i \le \text{Work}$:

- $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \not\le (5,3,2)$ — skip.
- $P_2$: $(6,0,0) \not\le (5,3,2)$ — skip.
- $P_3$: $(0,1,1) \le (5,3,2)$ — **select $P_3$**.

After $P_3$ finishes:

$$
\text{Work} = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,\; 4,\; 3), \quad \text{Finish}[3] = \text{T}
$$

**Iteration 3:** Check the remaining:

- $P_0$: $(7,4,3) \le (7,4,3)$ — **select $P_0$**.

After $P_0$ finishes:

$$
\text{Work} = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7,\; 5,\; 3), \quad \text{Finish}[0] = \text{T}
$$

**Iteration 4:** Remaining are $P_2$ and $P_4$:

- $P_2$: $(6,0,0) \le (7,5,3)$ — **select $P_2$**.

After $P_2$ finishes:

$$
\text{Work} = (7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10,\; 5,\; 5), \quad \text{Finish}[2] = \text{T}
$$

**Iteration 5:** Only $P_4$ remains:

- $P_4$: $(4,3,1) \le (10,5,5)$ — **select $P_4$**.

After $P_4$ finishes:

$$
\text{Work} = (10,5,5) + (0,0,2) = (10,\; 5,\; 7), \quad \text{Finish}[4] = \text{T}
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Safe Sequence} = \langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle}
$$

The system is in a **safe state**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Resource Request Validation

Suppose $P_1$ now issues a fresh request: $\text{Request}_1 = (1, 0, 2)$.

**Step 1 — Check guards:**

$$
\text{Request}_1 = (1,0,2) \le \text{Need}(P_1) = (1,2,2) \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{Request}_1 = (1,0,2) \le \text{Available} = (3,3,2) \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Tentatively allocate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available}' &= (3,3,2) - (1,0,2) = (2,\; 3,\; 0) \\
\text{Allocation}'(P_1) &= (2,0,0) + (1,0,2) = (3,\; 0,\; 2) \\
\text{Need}'(P_1) &= (1,2,2) - (1,0,2) = (0,\; 2,\; 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Re-run Safety Algorithm on the tentative state.** New $\text{Available} = (2,3,0)$ and updated Need matrix.

- $P_1$: Need = $(0,2,0) \le (2,3,0)$ ✓ → Work becomes $(2,3,0) + (3,0,2) = (5,3,2)$.
- $P_3$: Need = $(0,1,1) \le (5,3,2)$ ✓ → Work becomes $(5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,4,3)$.
- $P_0$: Need = $(7,4,3) \le (7,4,3)$ ✓ → Work becomes $(7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7,5,3)$.
- $P_2$: Need = $(6,0,0) \le (7,5,3)$ ✓ → Work becomes $(7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10,5,5)$.
- $P_4$: Need = $(4,3,1) \le (10,5,5)$ ✓ → All processes finish.

**Safe sequence under tentative allocation:** $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle$.

**Decision:** ✅ **Request is granted.** System remains in a safe state after allocation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deadlock Prevention Strategy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deadlock Prevention] --> B[Break Mutual Exclusion]
    A --> C[Break Hold and Wait]
    A --> D[Break No Preemption]
    A --> E[Break Circular Wait]

    B --> B1[Make resources sharable e.g. read only files]
    B --> B2[Limited applicability for printers or tape drives]

    C --> C1[Request all resources upfront]
    C --> C2[Request only when holding none]
    C --> C3[Drawback: Low utilization plus Starvation]

    D --> D1[Preempt held resources if new request fails]
    D --> D2[Works for CPU and Memory only]
    D --> D3[Not feasible for Printers or Tape drives]

    E --> E1[Impose total ordering on resource types]
    E --> E2[Request must be in strictly increasing order]
    E --> E3[Most commonly used prevention method in practice]
```

## 4.2 Resource Allocation Graph with Claim Edges

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1((P1)) --claim edge--> R1[Resource R1]
    P1 --request--> R2[Resource R2]
    R1 --assigned--> P2((P2))
    R2 --assigned--> P3((P3))
    P2 --claim edge--> R1
    P3 --claim edge--> R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Resource Allocation Graph Algorithm**, claim edges are converted to request edges dynamically. A request is granted **only if converting it to an assignment edge does not introduce a cycle**.

## 4.3 Banker's Algorithm Flow (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start] --> S1[Process Pi issues Request i]
    S1 --> S2{Request i less or equal to Need i}
    S2 --No--> S2a[Error: Process exceeded its max claim]
    S2 --Yes--> S3{Request i less or equal to Available}
    S3 --No--> S3a[Pi must wait]
    S3 --Yes--> S4[Tentatively allocate resources]
    S4 --> S5[Run Safety Algorithm on tentative state]
    S5 --> S6{Safe sequence found}
    S6 --Yes--> S7[Commit allocation and grant request]
    S6 --No--> S8[Rollback to original state and Pi must wait]
    S7 --> S9[End]
    S8 --> S9[End]
    S2a --> S9[End]
    S3a --> S9[End]
```

## 4.4 Coffman Conditions — Breaking Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mutual Exclusion] -.->|broken by sharing| P1[Prevention Strategy]
    M2[Hold and Wait] -.->|broken by atomic request| P2[Prevention Strategy]
    M3[No Preemption] -.->|broken by forced preemption| P3[Prevention Strategy]
    M4[Circular Wait] -.->|broken by total ordering| P4[Prevention Strategy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Differentiate between **deadlock prevention** and **deadlock avoidance**. Which is more restrictive on the programmer?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Deadlock Prevention | Deadlock Avoidance |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Breaks at least one Coffman condition by design | Dynamically avoids unsafe states using run-time info |
| **Information needed** | Static knowledge of resource types | Full *a priori* knowledge of maximum claims |
| **Resource utilization** | Often poor (e.g., all-or-nothing request) | Better — allocations made only when safe |
| **Programmer burden** | **Higher** — must rewrite request order (e.g., total ordering of resources) | **Lower** — just declare maximum needs upfront |

**Conclusion:** **Deadlock prevention is more restrictive on the programmer**, since it imposes rigid ordering/allocation rules that may not match natural program logic.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Define a **safe state**. Is it possible for a system to be in an unsafe state and yet not be deadlocked? Justify.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A system is in a **safe state** if there exists a sequence of process executions (a safe sequence) such that for every $P_i$, the resources $P_i$ may still request can be satisfied by the currently available resources plus the resources held by all earlier processes in the sequence.

**Yes**, an unsafe state does *not* guarantee deadlock. Unsafe simply means the system **cannot prove** that a deadlock is impossible — the processes may still be lucky enough to release resources before requesting their maximum. Deadlock is a *subset* of unsafe states but not the other way around.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, you must answer **only one** of the two options below. Each question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **four necessary conditions for deadlock** (Coffman conditions). For each condition, state **one prevention technique** and discuss its practical limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A system has **5 processes** $P_0$ through $P_4$ and **3 resource types** $A, B, C$ with total instances $(10, 5, 7)$. The current state is given below. Determine whether the system is in a **safe state** using the Banker's Algorithm. If safe, print the safe sequence. *(7 marks)*

| Process | Allocation (A B C) | Max (A B C) |
|---|---|---|
| $P_0$ | (0 1 0) | (7 5 3) |
| $P_1$ | (2 0 0) | (3 2 2) |
| $P_2$ | (3 0 2) | (9 0 2) |
| $P_3$ | (2 1 1) | (2 2 2) |
| $P_4$ | (0 0 2) | (4 3 3) |

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The four Coffman conditions are:

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode. *Prevention:* Make the resource shareable. *Limitation:* Not all resources (e.g., printers) can be made sharable.

2. **Hold and Wait:** A process holds resources while waiting for others. *Prevention:* Require processes to request all resources upfront. *Limitation:* Poor utilization; impossible to know maximum need in advance.

3. **No Preemption:** Resources cannot be forcibly taken. *Prevention:* Preempt held resources if a request cannot be immediately satisfied. *Limitation:* Only viable for resources whose state can be cheaply saved (CPU, memory) — not printers.

4. **Circular Wait:** A closed chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next. *Prevention:* Impose a total ordering on resource types; request must be in increasing order. *Limitation:* No natural order exists; programmers must refactor code.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

> (See Section 3.3 above for the **complete step-by-step derivation**.)

- $\text{Available} = (3, 3, 2)$
- $\text{Need}$ matrix computed.
- Safety algorithm executed over 5 iterations.
- **Result:** Safe Sequence = $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle$.

System is in a **safe state**. **(7/7 marks)**

---

### Question B `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Resource Allocation Graph Algorithm** for deadlock avoidance. Why is it restricted to systems with only **one instance per resource type**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the Banker's Algorithm, verify whether the following request from $P_1$ can be safely granted in the system of Question A. Request = $(1, 0, 2)$. If unsafe, explain the rollback procedure. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

The Resource Allocation Graph Algorithm augments the standard RAG with **claim edges** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ (drawn dashed), representing the *future possible* requests of $P_i$.

**Rules:**
- A request edge $P_i \rightarrow R_j$ appears only when $P_i$ *actually* requests $R_j$.
- The request is granted **only if converting the request edge to an assignment edge does NOT create a cycle** in the graph.
- Cycle detection runs in $O(V+E)$ time per request via DFS/BFS.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
   claim edge          request edge
  P1 ----------> R1 <========== P2
                 ||
                 VV assignment
                 P3
```

(For the full visual, see Section 4.2.)

**Why only one instance per resource type?**
The cycle-detection test works *only* if the resource node has **at most one instance** — because with multiple instances, the absence of a cycle is a **necessary but not sufficient** condition for safety. With multiple instances, you must use the **Banker's Algorithm** instead.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

> (See Section 3.4 above for the **complete step-by-step derivation**.)

- $\text{Request}_1 = (1, 0, 2) \le \text{Need}(P_1) = (1,2,2)$ ✓
- $\text{Request}_1 = (1, 0, 2) \le \text{Available} = (3, 3, 2)$ ✓
- Tentative allocation produces a state where the Safety Algorithm finds the safe sequence $\langle P_1, P_3, P_0, P_2, P_4 \rangle$.
- **Decision:** Request is **granted**. **(7/7 marks)**

If it had been unsafe, the rollback procedure would restore the original $\text{Available}$, $\text{Allocation}$, and $\text{Need}$ matrices, and $P_1$ would be made to wait until other processes release resources.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "unsafe" with "deadlocked"** — Unsafe ≠ Deadlock. Always phrase the answer carefully: *"the system cannot guarantee freedom from deadlock."*
> 2. **Forgetting to recompute $\text{Need}$** — Many students use the old $\text{Need}$ matrix during the Safety Algorithm after a tentative allocation. That is a fatal arithmetic error.
> 3. **Skipping the validation guards** — $\text{Request} \le \text{Need}$ and $\text{Request} \le \text{Available}$ must be checked *before* even attempting a tentative allocation. Missing this costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting $\text{Total} = \text{Sum of Allocation} + \text{Available}$** when $\text{Available}$ is not given directly.
> 5. **Answering only the prevention side in avoidance questions** (and vice versa) — read the question stem carefully; many 7-mark sub-questions explicitly ask for *both*.
> 6. **Not printing the safe sequence** — when the Safety Algorithm succeeds, you must list the exact safe sequence to earn the final 1 mark.
> 7. **In Circular Wait prevention**, students often say *"assign numbers arbitrarily"* — the correct phrasing is *"impose a total ordering that all processes must follow."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize**

- **Coffman Conditions (4):** Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait. *All four must hold simultaneously* for a deadlock.
- **Prevention** attacks **at least one** condition *statically*; **Avoidance** dynamically prevents unsafe states.
- **Circular Wait prevention** uses a *total ordering* of resource types — the most practical technique.
- **Hold and Wait prevention** uses *all-at-once* or *release-then-request* — low utilization, possible starvation.
- **No Preemption prevention** works only for *save-and-restore* resources (CPU, memory).
- **Mutual Exclusion prevention** only works for *read-only* or *inherently shareable* resources.
- **Safe State** ⟹ No deadlock. **Unsafe State** ⟹ Deadlock *possible*, not guaranteed.
- **Banker's Algorithm requires:** $\text{Need}$, $\text{Allocation}$, $\text{Max}$, $\text{Available}$.
- **Core identity:** $\text{Need} = \text{Max} - \text{Allocation}$.
- **Two validity guards** must always be checked before allocation: $\text{Request} \le \text{Need}$ and $\text{Request} \le \text{Available}$.
- **Safety Algorithm output** is either a **safe sequence** or the word **"UNSAFE."**
- **Resource Allocation Graph Algorithm** is restricted to systems with **one instance per resource type**; otherwise use Banker's.
- **Standard time complexity:** Cycle detection in RAG = $O(V+E)$; Banker's Safety Algorithm = $O(n \cdot m^2)$.
- **Deadlock Avoidance assumes** that the maximum claim of every process is known *a priori* — a strong assumption not always realistic.
