- **Avoidance vs Detection:** Avoidance prevents entering unsafe states; Detection allows unsafe states but uses a recovery mechanism (kill/rollback).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadlock Detection and Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deadlock Detection and Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Deadlock Detection and Recovery** refers to a non-preventive strategy in operating systems where the system permits deadlocks to occur, dynamically identifies their occurrence through algorithmic inspection of resource allocation and request states, and subsequently restores computational progress by forcibly reclaiming resources or terminating processes.

The detection mechanism operates by constructing a **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** in systems with single instances of each resource type, or by executing a **Banker's-style matrix algorithm** in systems with multiple instances. Recovery, the second phase, resolves the detected deadlock by either aborting deadlocked processes or preempting resources from them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike **Prevention** (which structurally removes one of the four Coffman conditions) or **Avoidance** (which uses the Banker's Algorithm to pre-validate safety), **Detection and Recovery** accepts that deadlocks *may* form and handles them *after* the fact. This trades off lower runtime overhead for higher recovery cost.

The system formally tracks two matrices at every scheduling checkpoint:
- **Allocation Matrix $\mathbf{A}$** — the current holdings of each process.
- **Request Matrix $\mathbf{R}$** — the outstanding demands of each process.

A deadlock is declared if and only if no sequence exists in which all processes can complete, i.e., the system is **not in a safe state** in the detection sense.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Four-Way Intersection

Imagine a roundabout (traffic circle) with four entry roads. Each road is a one-way street with a queue of cars waiting. The cars on the North road want to exit South, but South is blocked by cars wanting to exit East, which is blocked by cars wanting to exit North. No single car can move because each is holding its place on the roundabout and simultaneously demanding space held by another.

- **Detection** is the traffic controller scanning the roundabout and declaring, "This is a jam, no car can move."
- **Recovery** is the controller dispatching a tow truck (process termination) to remove one car, OR temporarily rerouting traffic (resource preemption) to break the cycle.

In computing terms, the cars are **processes**, the roundabout slots are **resources**, and the controller is the **OS kernel's deadlock detector**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just prevent it?** Prevention and avoidance are conservative — they restrict resource requests even when no deadlock would actually form. Detection allows maximal concurrency and is preferred in systems (like databases and distributed systems) where resources are abundant but predictability of request patterns is low.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Detection Systems

- **Detection Cost** $\mathbf{D_c}$: The CPU overhead of running the detection algorithm. Typically **$O(m \times n^2)$** where $m$ = resource types and $n$ = processes.
- **Recovery Cost** $\mathbf{R_c}$: The amortized loss from terminating processes or rolling back checkpoints.
- **Detection Frequency** $\mathbf{f_d}$: How often the detector is invoked. Common choices are:
  - On every request (high overhead, fast detection)
  - Periodically (balanced)
  - When CPU utilization drops below threshold (lazy, low overhead)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deadlock Detection — Two Algorithmic Regimes

The detection algorithm is bifurcated by the resource multiplicity:

#### Regime A: Single Instance of Each Resource Type

When every resource type has exactly one instance, a **Wait-For Graph (WFG)** is sufficient. The WFG is derived from the RAG by collapsing all resource nodes into edges between processes:

- An edge $P_i \rightarrow P_j$ exists iff $P_i$ is waiting for a resource currently held by $P_j$.
- **Deadlock exists $\iff$ the WFG contains a cycle.**

Cycle detection can be performed via Depth-First Search (DFS) in **$O(V + E)$** time, where $V = n$ processes and $E$ is the number of wait edges.

#### Regime B: Multiple Instances of Each Resource Type

Here, the WFG is insufficient because a cycle is *necessary but not sufficient* for deadlock. We employ a **matrix-based detection algorithm** that mimics the Banker's safety algorithm:

**Data Structures Used:**
- **Available $[m \times 1]$** vector: number of available instances of each resource.
- **Allocation $[n \times m]$** matrix: currently allocated instances.
- **Request $[n \times m]$** matrix: outstanding requests.

### 2.2 The Detection Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Initialize **Work = Available** and **Finish $[i]$ = false** for all $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$.
2. Find an index $i$ such that:
   - $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$
   - $\text{Request}_i \leq \text{Work}$ (component-wise, i.e., for every resource type $k$, $R_{i,k} \leq \text{Work}[k]$)
3. If such $i$ exists:
   - Set $\text{Work} = \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_i$
   - Set $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{true}$
   - Go to Step 2.
4. If no such $i$ exists, terminate.
5. **If any $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ for $i = 1 \dots n$, then process $P_i$ is deadlocked.**

> [!NOTE]
> **The logical equivalence:** A process $P_i$ can "finish" in the simulation only if its outstanding request can be granted with currently available resources, after accounting for what it would release upon completion. This is the same safety logic as the Banker's Algorithm.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation / Condition** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Deadlock Existence (Single Instance) | $\exists$ cycle in WFG | Cycle is necessary and sufficient |
| Deadlock Existence (Multi Instance) | $\exists i: \text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ after algorithm | A safe sequence cannot be found |
| Component-wise $\leq$ check | $R_{i,k} \leq \text{Work}[k] \quad \forall k \in \{1, \dots, m\}$ | A request must not exceed availability |
| Work Update | $\text{Work} \leftarrow \text{Work} + \text{Allocation}_i$ | Simulate $P_i$ releasing its resources |
| Algorithm Complexity | $O(m \times n^2)$ | $m$ = resource types, $n$ = processes |
| WFG Cycle Detection | $O(V + E) = O(n + n^2)$ | DFS-based, Tarjan's SCC variant |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction from Banker's:** The Banker's Algorithm requires a *maximum claim* matrix $C$ and uses $C_i - \text{Allocation}_i$ as the need. The Detection Algorithm uses the **actual current Request matrix** $R$ directly — no future claim is needed because detection is reactive, not predictive.

### 2.4 Recovery Strategies

Once a deadlock is confirmed, two families of recovery are available:

#### A. Process Termination (Abrupt Recovery)
- **Abort all deadlocked processes:** Maximum cost, but breaks all cycles immediately.
- **Abort one process at a time:** Iteratively terminate a process, re-run detection, repeat until no deadlock remains. Allows selective termination based on priority, cost, or rollback feasibility.

**Selection Heuristics for "which process to kill":**
- Lowest-priority process
- Process with least CPU time consumed (least work lost)
- Process with least allocated resources (easiest to preempt)
- Process that is not currently in the middle of I/O burst

#### B. Resource Preemption (Gradual Recovery)
- **Select a victim process** (similar heuristics as above).
- **Rollback:** The victim is rolled back to a safe checkpoint; if no safe checkpoint exists, the process must be aborted.
- **Starvation Prevention:** Ensure that a process is not repeatedly selected as victim. Common remedy: include the number of rollbacks in the cost function so the OS eventually favors less-picked victims.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Management Systems (DBMS):** Use detection and recovery heavily because lock-based concurrency control regularly forms wait cycles, and the cost of deadlocks is far less than the cost of pre-validated avoidance.
- **Distributed Systems (Hadoop, Spark):** Implement distributed deadlock detection via **edge-chasing** or **centralized coordinator** algorithms (e.g., Chandy-Misra-Haas algorithm).
- **Embedded and Real-Time Systems:** Rarely use detection because of hard timing constraints; prevention is preferred. Detection is reserved for soft real-time contexts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Multi-Instance Deadlock Detection

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**

Consider a system with **$n = 5$ processes** $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ and **$m = 4$ resource types** $A, B, C, D$. The current state is given below:

$$
\text{Available} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B & C & D \\ \hline 1 & 5 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{Allocation} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D \\
\hline
P_0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
P_1 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
P_2 & 3 & 0 & 3 & 4 \\
P_3 & 2 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
P_4 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 4
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{Request} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D \\
\hline
P_0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
P_1 & 2 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\
P_2 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
P_3 & 1 & 0 & 3 & 0 \\
P_4 & 0 & 0 & 2 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Determine whether the system is in a deadlock. If yes, identify the deadlocked processes.**

---

#### Step 1: Initialize State

The detector sets:

$$
\text{Work} = \text{Available} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{Finish} = \begin{bmatrix} F & F & F & F & F \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{for } P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4
$$

**Logic:** Work is initialized to current free resources. Finish marks processes that have been simulated to completion. Initially, no process has completed.

---

#### Step 2: Scan for an Index $i$ Where Finish[i] = false AND Request$_i$ $\leq$ Work

**Try $P_0$:** $\text{Request}_0 = [0, 0, 1, 2]$ and $\text{Work} = [1, 5, 2, 0]$.

Component-wise: $0 \leq 1$ ✓, $0 \leq 5$ ✓, $1 \leq 2$ ✓, $2 \leq 0$ ✗.

**Result:** $P_0$ **cannot** finish. Move to next.

**Try $P_1$:** $\text{Request}_1 = [2, 0, 2, 0]$ and $\text{Work} = [1, 5, 2, 0]$.

Component-wise: $2 \leq 1$ ✗. **$P_1$ cannot finish.**

**Try $P_2$:** $\text{Request}_2 = [0, 0, 1, 2]$ and $\text{Work} = [1, 5, 2, 0]$. Same as $P_0$: $2 \leq 0$ ✗. **$P_2$ cannot finish.**

**Try $P_3$:** $\text{Request}_3 = [1, 0, 3, 0]$ and $\text{Work} = [1, 5, 2, 0]$.

Component-wise: $1 \leq 1$ ✓, $0 \leq 5$ ✓, $3 \leq 2$ ✗. **$P_3$ cannot finish.**

**Try $P_4$:** $\text{Request}_4 = [0, 0, 2, 2]$ and $\text{Work} = [1, 5, 2, 0]$. $2 \leq 0$ ✗. **$P_4$ cannot finish.**

---

#### Step 3: Termination

No index $i$ satisfied the conditions. The algorithm terminates.

**Final state:**

$$
\text{Finish} = \begin{bmatrix} F & F & F & F & F \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Conclusion:** All five processes $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ are deadlocked because no process can be granted its outstanding request given the current available resources, and consequently no process can release its allocations to break the deadlock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Resource $D$ is the bottleneck. Available $D = 0$, but the total outstanding demand for $D$ across all processes is $2 + 0 + 2 + 0 + 2 = 6$. Until at least one process can be "fast-forwarded" past its request for $D$, no progress is possible. However, no process can finish without $D$, and no process can release $D$ without finishing — a perfect deadlock cycle.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Recovery via Selective Termination

Continuing the same system state above, suppose we must recover. Apply the **"abort one at a time"** strategy.

**Strategy:** Abort $P_0$ first because it has the **lowest total allocation** (least work to lose).

#### Step 1: Terminate $P_0$

$P_0$'s allocated resources are released back to Available:

$$
\text{Available}_{\text{new}} = \text{Available}_{\text{old}} + \text{Allocation}_0
$$

$$
\text{Available}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Re-run detection with new Available:** Now $\text{Request}_0$ is irrelevant. We check $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$.

**Try $P_1$:** $\text{Request}_1 = [2, 0, 2, 0] \leq [1, 5, 3, 2]$? → $2 \leq 1$ ✗. Fails.

**Try $P_2$:** $\text{Request}_2 = [0, 0, 1, 2] \leq [1, 5, 3, 2]$? → All ✓. **$P_2$ can finish!**

Update Work:

$$
\text{Work} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + \text{Allocation}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 & 6 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Mark $P_2$ as finished.

**Try $P_3$:** $\text{Request}_3 = [1, 0, 3, 0] \leq [4, 5, 6, 6]$? → All ✓. **$P_3$ finishes.**

Update Work:

$$
\text{Work} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 & 6 & 6 \end{bmatrix} + \text{Allocation}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 5 & 6 & 6 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 & 7 & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Try $P_4$:** $\text{Request}_4 = [0, 0, 2, 2] \leq [6, 6, 7, 6]$? → All ✓. **$P_4$ finishes.**

Update Work:

$$
\text{Work} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 & 7 & 6 \end{bmatrix} + \text{Allocation}_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 & 7 & 6 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 6 & 8 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Try $P_1$ again:** $\text{Request}_1 = [2, 0, 2, 0] \leq [6, 6, 8, 10]$? → All ✓. **$P_1$ finishes.**

$$
\text{Finish} = \begin{bmatrix} T & T & T & T & T \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Result after aborting $P_0$:** The system is now deadlock-free. **One process termination broke the cycle.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Component-Wise Inequality

The condition $\text{Request}_i \leq \text{Work}$ is a vector inequality. For resource type $k$:

$$
R_{i,k} \leq W_k \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sum_{j \neq i} A_{j,k} + W_k \geq R_{i,k} + A_{i,k}
$$

The left side is the total number of instances of type $k$ present in the system *excluding* $P_i$'s holdings. The right side is what $P_i$ would need to hold to complete (its current allocation + outstanding request). The inequality expresses the **physical constraint** that the system has enough instances to "simulate" granting $P_i$'s request without violating any other process's share.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Detection Algorithm Flow (Multi-Instance Regime)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start Detection]) --> B[Initialize Work = Available]
    B --> C[Set Finish i = false for all i]
    C --> D{Exists i such that Finish i = false AND Request i less than or equal to Work}
    D -- Yes --> E[Work = Work plus Allocation i]
    E --> F[Set Finish i = true]
    F --> D
    D -- No --> G{Any Finish i = false remaining}
    G -- Yes --> H[Process Pi is Deadlocked]
    G -- No --> I([System is Deadlock Free])
    H --> J([Initiate Recovery])
    I --> K([Continue Normal Operation])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The algorithm is a fixed-point iteration. Each pass tries to "fast-forward" a process whose request is satisfiable. When no such process exists, the residual $\text{Finish}[i] = \text{false}$ set identifies the deadlocked cohort.

### 4.2 Recovery Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Deadlock Detected]) --> B{Choose Recovery Method}
    B -- Option 1 --> C[Process Termination]
    B -- Option 2 --> D[Resource Preemption]
    C --> E{All at once or one at a time}
    E -- All --> F[Abort all deadlocked processes]
    E -- Selective --> G[Apply victim selection heuristic]
    G --> H[Re-run detection]
    H --> I{Deadlock resolved}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J([System Recovered])
    F --> J
    D --> K[Select victim process]
    K --> L[Rollback to safe checkpoint]
    L --> M[Preempt resources]
    M --> H
    H2{Deadlock resolved} --> J
    L --> H2
    M --> H2
```

### 4.3 Wait-For Graph Transformation (Single Instance Regime)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAG[Resource Allocation Graph]
        P1RAG[Process P1] -->|holds| R1RAG[Resource R1]
        R1RAG -->|requested by| P2RAG[Process P2]
        P2RAG -->|holds| R2RAG[Resource R2]
        R2RAG -->|requested by| P3RAG[Process P3]
        P3RAG -->|holds| R3RAG[Resource R3]
        R3RAG -->|requested by| P1RAG
    end
    subgraph WFG[Wait For Graph Collapsed]
        P1WFG[P1] -->|waits for| P2WFG[P2]
        P2WFG -->|waits for| P3WFG[P3]
        P3WFG -->|waits for| P1WFG
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The transformation rule:** Resource nodes are *deleted* from the RAG. The two edges that touched the resource node (one from the holder, one to the requester) are merged into a single directed edge from holder to requester. The resulting WFG has only process nodes. A cycle in the WFG is the deadlock signature.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*Differentiate between Deadlock Prevention and Deadlock Detection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Deadlock Prevention** | **Deadlock Detection** |
|---|---|
| A *proactive* strategy that pre-emptively negates at least one of the four Coffman conditions (Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait) so that a deadlock *cannot form*. | A *reactive* strategy that allows deadlocks to form, identifies them via the Wait-For Graph or matrix algorithm, and triggers recovery. |
| High runtime overhead — restricts valid resource requests. | Low runtime overhead — only invokes detection periodically or on triggers. |
| Used in safety-critical / hard real-time systems (e.g., avionics). | Used in databases, general-purpose OS, distributed systems. |
| Guarantees no deadlock *by construction*. | Guarantees no deadlock *by recovery*. |

*(Full 3 marks for a crisp 4-point contrast with one example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

*What is a Wait-For Graph? How is it derived from a Resource Allocation Graph?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Wait-For Graph (WFG)** is a directed graph used in deadlock detection for systems with a **single instance of each resource type**. Nodes represent processes only; an edge $P_i \rightarrow P_j$ indicates that $P_i$ is waiting for a resource currently held by $P_j$.

**Derivation from RAG:**
1. Remove all resource nodes from the RAG.
2. For every resource instance, merge the two incident edges (the "allocated-to" edge from the holder process and the "requested-by" edge from the requester process) into a single edge $P_{\text{holder}} \rightarrow P_{\text{requester}}$.
3. The result is a process-only directed graph.

**Deadlock criterion:** A deadlock exists **if and only if** the WFG contains a cycle.

*(1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for derivation steps, 0.5 marks for the cycle criterion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the algorithm for deadlock detection in a system with multiple instances of each resource type. Use suitable data structures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a system with 5 processes $P_0$ to $P_4$ and 3 resource types $A$ (10 instances), $B$ (5 instances), $C$ (7 instances). The snapshot at time $T_0$ is:

$$
\text{Available} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B & C \\ \hline 3 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{Allocation} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C \\
\hline
P_0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P_1 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\
P_2 & 3 & 0 & 2 \\
P_3 & 2 & 1 & 1 \\
P_4 & 0 & 0 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{Request} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C \\
\hline
P_0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
P_1 & 2 & 0 & 2 \\
P_2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
P_3 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
P_4 & 0 & 0 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Run the deadlock detection algorithm and determine whether the system is deadlocked. If yes, list the deadlocked processes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Algorithm (7 marks):**

*Data Structures (2 marks):*
- **Available** vector of length $m$: $m$ = number of resource types. Available[j] = number of free instances of type $j$.
- **Allocation** matrix of size $n \times m$: Allocation[i][j] = number of instances of type $j$ held by process $P_i$.
- **Request** matrix of size $n \times m$: Request[i][j] = number of instances of type $j$ that $P_i$ is currently waiting for.

*Algorithm Steps (4 marks):*
1. Initialize **Work = Available**. For all $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$, set **Finish[i] = false**.
2. Find an index $i$ such that both:
   - **Finish[i] == false**
   - **Request$_i$ $\leq$ Work** (component-wise: for all resource types $k$, Request[i][k] $\leq$ Work[k])
3. If such $i$ exists, perform:
   - **Work = Work + Allocation$_i$** (simulate $P_i$ finishing and releasing its resources)
   - **Finish[i] = true**
   - Go back to Step 2.
4. If no such $i$ exists, the algorithm terminates.
5. If there exists an $i$ with **Finish[i] == false**, then process $P_i$ is deadlocked.

*Complexity (1 mark): $O(m \times n^2)$.*

---

**(b) Worked Example (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Initialize.

$$
\text{Work} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{Finish} = \begin{bmatrix} F & F & F & F & F \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Scan each process.

- $P_0$: Request = [0, 0, 0] $\leq$ [3, 3, 2] ✓ → **$P_0$ can finish.**
  - Work = [3, 3, 2] + [0, 1, 0] = [3, 4, 2]. Finish[$P_0$] = T.
- $P_1$: Request = [2, 0, 2] $\leq$ [3, 4, 2] ✓ → **$P_1$ can finish.**
  - Work = [3, 4, 2] + [2, 0, 0] = [5, 4, 2]. Finish[$P_1$] = T.
- $P_2$: Request = [0, 0, 0] $\leq$ [5, 4, 2] ✓ → **$P_2$ can finish.**
  - Work = [5, 4, 2] + [3, 0, 2] = [8, 4, 4]. Finish[$P_2$] = T.
- $P_3$: Request = [1, 0, 0] $\leq$ [8, 4, 4] ✓ → **$P_3$ can finish.**
  - Work = [8, 4, 4] + [2, 1, 1] = [10, 5, 5]. Finish[$P_3$] = T.
- $P_4$: Request = [0, 0, 2] $\leq$ [10, 5, 5] ✓ → **$P_4$ can finish.**
  - Work = [10, 5, 5] + [0, 0, 2] = [10, 5, 7]. Finish[$P_4$] = T.

**Step 3:** Termination.

$$
\text{Finish} = \begin{bmatrix} T & T & T & T & T \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Conclusion:** All processes can finish. **The system is NOT deadlocked.** The deadlock-free sequence is $\langle P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4 \rangle$.

*(1 mark per process evaluation + 1 mark for state updates + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the various techniques used for **deadlock recovery** after a deadlock has been detected. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has detected a deadlock involving 4 processes $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$. The current state is:

$$
\text{Available} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad (A, B, C)
$$

$$
\text{Allocation} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C \\
\hline
P_1 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\
P_2 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
P_3 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P_4 & 1 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{Request} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C \\
\hline
P_1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
P_2 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P_3 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
P_4 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply the **selective process termination** recovery strategy. Choose an appropriate victim process, terminate it, and re-run the detection algorithm. Show that the system is recovered. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Deadlock Recovery Techniques (7 marks):**

**Method 1: Abort all deadlocked processes (2 marks)**
- **Procedure:** The OS forcibly terminates all processes in the deadlocked set.
- **Advantage:** Simple; guarantees deadlock resolution immediately.
- **Disadvantage:** High cost — all in-flight work is discarded; starvation of critical processes.

**Method 2: Abort one process at a time (2 marks)**
- **Procedure:** Select a "victim" process, terminate it, release its resources, re-run the detection algorithm. Repeat until no deadlock remains.
- **Victim Selection Criteria:**
  - Process with lowest priority
  - Process with least CPU time consumed
  - Process with least allocated resources
  - Process that is not in the middle of a critical I/O operation
- **Advantage:** Granular, cost-controlled recovery.
- **Disadvantage:** High overhead due to repeated detection invocations; suboptimal victim choice may prolong recovery.

**Method 3: Resource Preemption (2 marks)**
- **Procedure:** Select a victim, roll it back to a previous safe checkpoint, and forcibly take its held resources. The victim is restarted later.
- **Advantage:** Avoids total process loss; preserves partial work.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires checkpointing infrastructure; risk of starvation if a process is repeatedly victimized.

**Starvation Prevention (1 mark):** Include the number of times a process has been a victim in the cost function, and roll back the process with the *smallest* rollback count to ensure fairness.

---

**(b) Worked Recovery Example (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Verify the deadlock.**

Work = [1, 0, 1], Finish = [F, F, F, F].

- $P_1$: Request = [0, 0, 0] $\leq$ [1, 0, 1] ✓ → $P_1$ can finish.
  - Work = [1, 0, 1] + [1, 1, 0] = [2, 1, 1]. Finish[$P_1$] = T.
- $P_2$: Request = [0, 1, 0] $\leq$ [2, 1, 1] ✓ → $P_2$ can finish.
  - Work = [2, 1, 1] + [1, 0, 1] = [3, 1, 2]. Finish[$P_2$] = T.
- $P_3$: Request = [1, 0, 0] $\leq$ [3, 1, 2] ✓ → $P_3$ can finish.
  - Work = [3, 1, 2] + [0, 1, 0] = [3, 2, 2]. Finish[$P_3$] = T.
- $P_4$: Request = [0, 0, 0] $\leq$ [3, 2, 2] ✓ → $P_4$ can finish.

Hmm — actually the system is *not* in deadlock in the example as stated. The proper KTU recovery question would require a true deadlock snapshot. **For pedagogical completeness, assume a modified Request matrix** that creates a true deadlock, e.g.:

$$
\text{Request}_{\text{modified}} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C \\
\hline
P_1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
P_2 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
P_3 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
P_4 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

In that case, **select $P_4$ as victim** (smallest total allocation = [1, 0, 1], cost of loss = 2 instances). Terminate $P_4$.

**Step 2: Release $P_4$'s allocation.**

$$
\text{Available}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Re-run detection.**

- $P_1$: Request = [0, 1, 0] $\leq$ [2, 0, 2]? → $1 \leq 0$ ✗. Fails.
- $P_2$: Request = [1, 0, 0] $\leq$ [2, 0, 2] ✓ → $P_2$ can finish.
  - Work = [2, 0, 2] + [1, 0, 1] = [3, 0, 3]. Finish[$P_2$] = T.
- $P_3$: Request = [0, 0, 1] $\leq$ [3, 0, 3] ✓ → $P_3$ can finish.
  - Work = [3, 0, 3] + [0, 1, 0] = [3, 1, 3]. Finish[$P_3$] = T.
- $P_1$: Request = [0, 1, 0] $\leq$ [3, 1, 3] ✓ → $P_1$ can finish.

**Result:** All Finish flags = T. **System recovered by terminating $P_4$.**

*(3 marks for victim selection rationale, 2 marks for resource release, 2 marks for re-detection and conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the Detection Algorithm with the Banker's Algorithm.** Detection uses the **current** Request matrix $R$. Banker's uses the **Need** matrix $C_i - A_i$. Examiners explicitly deduct marks if the wrong matrix is used.
> 2. **Forgetting to update Work after every process finish.** The whole algorithm hinges on accumulating released resources. A common error is to set Finish[i] = true without updating Work.
> 3. **Component-wise comparison is mandatory.** Writing $R_i \leq W$ without emphasizing "component-wise" loses 1 mark. Always state the comparison holds for all resource types $k$.
> 4. **Cycle in WFG is NOT sufficient for deadlock in multi-instance systems.** Many students incorrectly invoke the WFG cycle test for multi-instance problems. The matrix algorithm is required.
> 5. **Process termination cost is rarely considered in student answers.** Always justify *which* process to terminate using at least one selection heuristic (priority, work done, resource count).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Detection + Recovery accepts deadlocks, finds them, and fixes them after the fact.
- [ ] **Single Instance → Wait-For Graph → Cycle detection via DFS, $O(V+E)$.**
- [ ] **Multi Instance → Matrix algorithm with Available, Allocation, Request.**
- [ ] **Algorithm steps:** Init Work, Find $i$ with $R_i \leq W$, Update $W = W + A_i$, Mark Finish[i] = T. Repeat.
- [ ] **Deadlock $\iff$** some Finish[i] = F at termination.
- [ ] **Recovery Method 1:** Abort all deadlocked processes (simple, expensive).
- [ ] **Recovery Method 2:** Abort one at a time (granular, repeated detection).
- [ ] **Recovery Method 3:** Resource preemption + checkpoint rollback.
- [ ] **Victim selection criteria:** priority, work done, allocation size, I/O state.
- [ ] **Starvation in preemption:** Use rollback count as tie-breaker.
- [ ] **Algorithm complexity:** $O(m \times n^2)$ for multi-instance.
- [ ] **KTU trap:** WFG cycle test is **not** valid for multiple instances.
- [ ] **Real-world use cases:** DBMS, distributed systems, soft real-time systems.
- [ ] **Always show Work vector and Finish vector at every iteration step** for full marks.
- [ ] **Detective vs Preventive:** Detection runs **reactively**; prevention/abstraction run **proactively**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dining Philosophers Problem and Its Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dining Philosophers Problem and Its Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dining Philosophers Problem (Dijkstra, 1965):** A classical synchronization problem in operating systems that illustrates the challenges of **deadlock** and **resource contention** in concurrent processes. The problem involves **N philosophers** seated around a circular table, where each philosopher alternates between **thinking** and **eating**. Between every two adjacent philosophers lies exactly **one chopstick (fork)**. A philosopher requires **both adjacent chopsticks** simultaneously to eat. The challenge is to design a protocol that ensures **mutual exclusion, no deadlock, no starvation, and maximum parallelism**.

The problem models the **contention for shared resources** (chopsticks) among **concurrent processes** (philosophers) and is universally used to teach:
- **Deadlock Prevention** (breaking one of Coffman's 4 conditions)
- **Deadlock Avoidance** (using semaphores/monitors)
- **Starvation Freedom**
- **Resource Hierarchy** techniques

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **🍝 The Pizza Parlour Analogy:** Imagine **5 friends** sitting around a round dining table. Between every two friends is **exactly one fork** (5 forks total). To eat pasta, each friend needs **two forks** — the one on their left AND the one on their right. A friend **thinks** (chats), gets **hungry**, waits for both forks, **eats**, and then puts the forks back. The challenge for the host (OS) is to make sure no one starves while no two friends fight over the same fork.

In the OS world:
- **Philosophers → Processes / Threads**
- **Chopsticks → Shared Resources (e.g., I/O devices, file handles, database locks)**
- **Eating → Critical Section execution**
- **Thinking → Non-critical computation**

### 1.3 The Four Key Requirements (KTU High-Yield)

Any valid solution **MUST** satisfy all four conditions:

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** No two philosophers can hold the same chopstick at the same time.
2. **No Deadlock:** The system must never reach a state where all philosophers are blocked forever, each waiting for a chopstick held by another.
3. **No Starvation (Fairness):** Every hungry philosopher must eventually eat (bounded waiting).
4. **Maximum Concurrency:** Philosophers should be allowed to eat simultaneously whenever possible (e.g., philosophers 0, 2, 4 can eat together if resources allow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Dining Philosophers problem is a **mandatory topic** in Module 2 under *Process Synchronization* and is frequently asked in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition and **Part B (14 marks)** for full solution design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Problem Statement (Rigorous)

Let there be **N philosophers** (typically **N = 5**) numbered $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{N-1}$ arranged in a circle. Between philosopher $P_i$ and $P_{(i+1) \mod N}$ lies a chopstick $C_i$. Each philosopher follows this **infinite loop**:

1. **Think** for some time (no resource usage).
2. **Become Hungry** (state transition: `THINKING → HUNGRY`).
3. **Acquire** chopstick $C_{(i-1) \mod N}$ (left) and $C_i$ (right).
4. **Eat** for some time.
5. **Release** both chopsticks (state transition: `EATING → THINKING`).

### 2.2 The Naive (Broken) Solution — Why It Fails

The most obvious solution uses a **binary semaphore per chopstick**:

$$C_i = 1 \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$$

The philosopher $P_i$ executes:
```
wait(C[i]);            // pick up left chopstick
wait(C[(i+1) % 5]);    // pick up right chopstick
... eat ...
signal(C[(i+1) % 5]);  // put down right
signal(C[i]);          // put down left
```

> [!WARNING]
> **This causes DEADLOCK!** If every philosopher picks up their **left** chopstick simultaneously, all 5 chopsticks are held. Each philosopher then waits forever for the right chopstick — a **circular wait** (one of Coffman's 4 deadlock conditions).

### 2.3 Coffman's Four Conditions (Recall for Analysis)

A deadlock occurs **iff ALL four** hold simultaneously:

| # | Condition | Meaning in Dining Philosophers |
|---|-----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Mutual Exclusion** | A chopstick can be held by only one philosopher at a time |
| 2 | **Hold and Wait** | A philosopher holds the left chopstick while waiting for the right |
| 3 | **No Preemption** | A chopstick cannot be forcibly taken from a philosopher |
| 4 | **Circular Wait** | $P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0$ (circular chain of waiting) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Standard Value / Formula | Description |
|----------|------------------------|-------------|
| Number of Philosophers | $N = 5$ | Standard KTU assumption |
| Number of Chopsticks | $N = 5$ | Equals number of philosophers |
| State of Philosopher $P_i$ | $S_i \in \{\text{THINKING, HUNGRY, EATING}\}$ | Three-state finite machine |
| Maximum Concurrent Eaters | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor = 2$ | For $N=5$, at most 2 eat simultaneously |
| Time Complexity (naive) | $O(1)$ per philosopher | But causes deadlock |
| Time Complexity (resource hierarchy) | $O(1)$ per philosopher | Deadlock-free |
| Starvation Bound (arbiter solution) | Finite but unbounded | One philosopher may wait arbitrarily long |
| Starvation Bound (fair solution) | $(N-1)$ eating cycles | Each philosopher guaranteed to eat within bounded time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This problem directly models **multi-threaded database transactions** (e.g., two transactions trying to update two related rows), **distributed lock managers** (e.g., Redis Redlock, ZooKeeper), and **hardware arbitration** in multi-core CPUs where multiple cores compete for shared cache lines or memory buses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Solutions & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Solution 1: Resource Hierarchy (Deadlock-Free, Recommended by KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idea:** Break the **Circular Wait** condition by imposing a **total ordering** on resources. Require every philosopher to pick up the **lower-numbered chopstick first**.

**Algorithm for $P_i$:**

For philosopher $P_i$, define the two chopsticks as:
- **First** (lower number) = $\min(i, (i+1) \bmod 5)$
- **Second** (higher number) = $\max(i, (i+1) \bmod 5)$

The rule: **Always acquire the lower-numbered chopstick first.**

**Step-by-step logic for each philosopher:**

- $P_0$: Pick up $C_0$ first, then $C_1$
- $P_1$: Pick up $C_1$ first, then $C_2$
- $P_2$: Pick up $C_2$ first, then $C_3$
- $P_3$: Pick up $C_3$ first, then $C_4$
- $P_4$: Pick up $C_0$ first, then $C_4$ ← (note: $C_0 < C_4$, so left chopstick $C_4$ becomes the "second" pick)

**Pseudocode:**
```
process P[i] {
    int first  = min(i, (i+1) % 5);
    int second = max(i, (i+1) % 5);
    while (true) {
        think();
        wait(C[first]);        // always lower-numbered first
        wait(C[second]);       // then higher-numbered
        eat();
        signal(C[second]);     // release in reverse order
        signal(C[first]);
    }
}
```

**Why this works (proof of deadlock freedom):**
- The lowest-numbered chopstick $C_0$ can only be picked up by **at most one philosopher** at a time (mutual exclusion via semaphore).
- Whichever philosopher grabs $C_0$ first can then proceed to grab $C_1$ or $C_4$ (its other chopstick). 
- No other philosopher can be waiting for $C_0$ while holding a higher-numbered chopstick, because the rule forces them to acquire the lower one first.
- Therefore, **circular wait is impossible** — deadlock cannot occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** This solution is **deadlock-free but does NOT guarantee starvation freedom**. In theory, a philosopher could be perpetually skipped. However, in practice with fair semaphore implementations, starvation is extremely rare.

### 3.2 Solution 2: Arbitrator / Mutex Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idea:** Introduce a **single global mutex** (binary semaphore) that protects the entire process of picking up chopsticks. This breaks the **Hold and Wait** condition by making the "pick up both chopsticks" step **atomic**.

**Global variables:**
- `semaphore mutex = 1;`         // protects state changes
- `semaphore C[5] = {0,0,0,0,0};` // one per chopstick, init to 0 (blocked)
- `enum state[5] = {THINKING};`  // current state of each philosopher

**Three helper procedures:**

```
void test(int i) {
    if (state[i] == HUNGRY &&
        state[(i+4) % 5] != EATING &&
        state[(i+1) % 5] != EATING) {
        state[i] = EATING;
        signal(C[i]);        // unblock philosopher i (if waiting)
    }
}

void pickup(int i) {
    wait(mutex);
    state[i] = HUNGRY;
    test(i);                 // try to acquire both chopsticks
    signal(mutex);
    wait(C[i]);              // block if test() failed
}

void putdown(int i) {
    wait(mutex);
    state[i] = THINKING;
    test((i+4) % 5);         // wake up left neighbor if possible
    test((i+1) % 5);         // wake up right neighbor if possible
    signal(mutex);
}
```

**Philosopher process:**
```
process P[i] {
    while (true) {
        think();
        pickup(i);
        eat();
        putdown(i);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

| Step | Action | $P_0$ | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $P_3$ | $P_4$ | C[0] | C[1] | C[2] | C[3] | C[4] |
|------|--------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|------|------|------|------|------|
| 1 | Init | T | T | T | T | T | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | $P_0$ calls pickup(0) | H | T | T | T | T | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | test(0) succeeds → EATING | E | T | T | T | T | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | $P_1$ calls pickup(1) | E | H | T | T | T | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | test(1) fails (P₀ EATING) | E | H | T | T | T | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | $P_1$ blocks on C[1] | E | B | T | T | T | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 7 | $P_2$ calls pickup(2), test fails, blocks | E | B | B | T | T | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 8 | $P_0$ finishes eating, putdown(0) | T | T | T | T | T | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 9 | test(1) succeeds → P₁ EATING | T | E | T | T | T | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

(T = Thinking, H = Hungry, E = Eating, B = Blocked)

### 3.3 Solution 3: Chandy-Misra (Token-Based, for Distributed Systems)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, focus on Solutions 1 and 2. Solution 3 is for advanced/distributed context.

Uses **dirty/clean forks** with tokens:
- Each fork is either **dirty** (available) or **clean** (in use).
- Hungry philosophers send **request messages** to neighbors.
- A philosopher with a **dirty, requested fork** cleans it and sends it to the requester.
- **Guarantees:** No deadlock, no starvation, and maximum concurrency.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix of Solutions

| Solution | Deadlock-Free | Starvation-Free | Max Concurrency | Complexity | KTU Preference |
|----------|:-------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|------------|:--------------:|
| **Naive (semaphores)** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ High | $O(1)$ | Mentioned as bad example |
| **Resource Hierarchy** | ✅ Yes | ⚠️ Mostly | ✅ High | $O(1)$ | ⭐ Most common KTU answer |
| **Arbitrator / Mutex** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Limited (serialized pickup) | $O(1)$ | ⭐⭐ Most rigorous KTU answer |
| **Chandy-Misra** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ High | $O(\text{messages})$ | Advanced / elective |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Layout of the Dining Philosophers

```mermaid
graph TB
    P0(["P0 Philosopher"]):::phil
    P1(["P1 Philosopher"]):::phil
    P2(["P2 Philosopher"]):::phil
    P3(["P3 Philosopher"]):::phil
    P4(["P4 Philosopher"]):::phil
    
    C0(["C0 Chopstick"]):::fork
    C1(["C1 Chopstick"]):::fork
    C2(["C2 Chopstick"]):::fork
    C3(["C3 Chopstick"]):::fork
    C4(["C4 Chopstick"]):::fork
    
    C0 --- P0
    P0 --- C1
    C1 --- P1
    P1 --- C2
    C2 --- P2
    P2 --- C3
    C3 --- P3
    P3 --- C4
    C4 --- P4
    P4 --- C0
    
    classDef phil fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fork fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 State Machine of a Single Philosopher

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> THINKING
    THINKING --> HUNGRY : Becomes hungry
    HUNGRY --> EATING : Acquires both chopsticks
    EATING --> THINKING : Releases chopsticks
    HUNGRY --> HUNGRY : Waits for chopstick
    EATING --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Deadlock Scenario (Naive Solution)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0n["P0 holds C0\nwaits for C1"]:::dead
    P1n["P1 holds C1\nwaits for C2"]:::dead
    P2n["P2 holds C2\nwaits for C3"]:::dead
    P3n["P3 holds C3\nwaits for C4"]:::dead
    P4n["P4 holds C4\nwaits for C0"]:::dead
    
    P0n ==> P1n
    P1n ==> P2n
    P2n ==> P3n
    P3n ==> P4n
    P4n ==> P0n
    
    classDef dead fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Resource Hierarchy Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Philosopher Pi wants to eat"]:::start
    B{"Is left chopstick < right chopstick?"}:::decision
    C["Wait on C[min left right]"]:::acquire

    E["Eating Critical Section"]:::eat
    F["Signal C[max left right]"]:::release
    G["Signal C[min left right]"]:::release
    H["Back to Thinking"]:::end2
    
    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> C
    B -- No --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    
    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px

    classDef release fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

```

### 4.5 Arbitrator Solution Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Global State"
        M["Mutex Semaphore = 1"]:::mutex
        S["State Array: 5 entries\nTHINKING HUNGRY EATING"]:::state
        CS["Chopstick Semaphores C0..C4 = 0"]:::cs
    end
    
    subgraph "Philosopher Pi"
        T["Think Loop"]:::think
        PK["pickup i"]:::pickup
        EAT["Eat"]:::eat
        PD["putdown i"]:::putdown
    end
    
    subgraph "Helper Routines"
        TST["test i:\nCheck if neighbors NOT EATING"]:::test
        WL["wait C i if cannot eat"]:::wait
        WK["signal C i to wake neighbor"]:::wk
    end
    
    T --> PK
    PK --> TST
    TST --> WL
    WL --> EAT
    EAT --> PD
    PD --> TST
    TST --> WK
    PK -.uses.-> M
    PK -.reads.-> S
    PK -.signals.-> CS
    PD -.uses.-> M
    PD -.reads.-> S
    PD -.signals.-> CS
```
