
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the Dining Philosophers Problem and list the four conditions that must be satisfied by any solution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Dining Philosophers Problem** is a classic synchronization problem formulated by **Dijkstra (1965)** to illustrate the challenges of resource sharing among concurrent processes. It involves **N philosophers** sitting around a circular table with **N chopsticks** placed between adjacent philosophers. Each philosopher alternates between **thinking** and **eating**, and requires **both adjacent chopsticks** to eat.

The **four conditions** any valid solution must satisfy are:

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** No two philosophers can hold the same chopstick at the same time.
2. **No Deadlock:** The system must never reach a state where all philosophers are blocked indefinitely, each waiting for a chopstick held by another.
3. **No Starvation:** Every philosopher who is hungry must eventually get a chance to eat (bounded waiting).
4. **Maximum Concurrency:** Multiple philosophers should be allowed to eat simultaneously whenever resources permit (e.g., for N=5, up to ⌊N/2⌋ = 2 can eat together).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for stating mutual exclusion + no deadlock, 1 mark for no starvation + max concurrency.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between deadlock and starvation in the context of the Dining Philosophers Problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Deadlock | Starvation |
|--------|----------|------------|
| **Definition** | A state where **all** processes are blocked forever, each waiting for a resource held by another | A state where **one specific** process is blocked indefinitely while others proceed |
| **Scope** | System-wide (all philosophers affected) | Individual (one philosopher keeps waiting) |
| **Cause** | Circular wait + hold-and-wait + mutual exclusion + no preemption (all 4 Coffman conditions) | Unfair scheduling / resource allocation policy |
| **In Dining Philosophers** | All 5 philosophers hold one chopstick each and wait for the next | A particular philosopher is repeatedly bypassed while neighbors eat |
| **Detection** | Resource allocation graph has a cycle | Harder to detect; requires fairness proof |
| **Solution** | Break one of Coffman's 4 conditions (e.g., resource hierarchy) | Use FIFO queues, aging, or arbitrator with fair queuing |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each clear distinction with proper context.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the Dining Philosophers Problem with a neat diagram. Show how the naive semaphore-based solution leads to deadlock. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Propose a deadlock-free solution using the **resource hierarchy** technique. Prove that your solution cannot deadlock. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) The Dining Philosophers Problem & Naive Solution (7 marks)**

The problem involves **5 philosophers** ($P_0$ to $P_4$) sitting around a circular table. Between every two adjacent philosophers is **1 chopstick** ($C_0$ to $C_4$). A philosopher thinks, gets hungry, picks up the **left** then **right** chopstick, eats, and puts them down.

**Data structure:** Use **5 binary semaphores**, one per chopstick:
$$C[i] = 1, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$$

**Naive code for philosopher $P_i$:**
```
process P[i] {
    while (true) {
        think();
        wait(C[i]);            // pick up LEFT chopstick
        wait(C[(i+1) % 5]);    // pick up RIGHT chopstick
        eat();
        signal(C[(i+1) % 5]);  // put down RIGHT
        signal(C[i]);          // put down LEFT
    }
}
```

**Why it deadlocks — trace:**

Suppose all 5 philosophers get hungry at the **same instant** and each executes `wait(C[i])` successfully:

| Philosopher | Holds | Waits For |
|-------------|-------|-----------|
| $P_0$ | $C_0$ | $C_1$ |
| $P_1$ | $C_1$ | $C_2$ |
| $P_2$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ |
| $P_3$ | $C_3$ | $C_4$ |
| $P_4$ | $C_4$ | $C_0$ |

All 4 Coffman conditions hold:
1. **Mutual Exclusion:** ✅ Each chopstick held by exactly one philosopher.
2. **Hold and Wait:** ✅ Each holds one, waits for another.
3. **No Preemption:** ✅ No one can forcibly take a chopstick.
4. **Circular Wait:** ✅ $P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0$ (closed cycle).

**Result: SYSTEM DEADLOCK** — no philosopher can ever proceed.

*(Award 1 mark for problem statement, 2 marks for naive code, 2 marks for trace, 2 marks for identifying all 4 Coffman conditions.)*

---

**(b) Resource Hierarchy Solution & Proof (7 marks)**

**Solution:** Impose a **total ordering** on chopsticks by number. Each philosopher always picks up the **lower-numbered** chopstick first.

```
process P[i] {
    int first  = min(i, (i+1) % 5);
    int second = max(i, (i+1) % 5);
    while (true) {
        think();
        wait(C[first]);
        wait(C[second]);
        eat();
        signal(C[second]);
        signal(C[first]);
    }
}
```

**Specific first-pick assignments:**

- $P_0$: first = $C_0$, second = $C_1$
- $P_1$: first = $C_1$, second = $C_2$
- $P_2$: first = $C_2$, second = $C_3$
- $P_3$: first = $C_3$, second = $C_4$
- $P_4$: first = $C_0$, second = $C_4$ (since $0 < 4$)

**Proof of Deadlock-Freedom:**

Assume, for contradiction, that deadlock occurs. For deadlock, we need a **circular wait**:
$$P_{a_0} \to P_{a_1} \to P_{a_2} \to \ldots \to P_{a_k} \to P_{a_0}$$

This means some philosopher $P_i$ is waiting for a chopstick held by $P_j$, who in turn waits for a chopstick held by $P_i$ (or transitively back). 

Consider the **lowest-numbered chopstick** $C_m$ involved in the circular wait. This chopstick is held by some philosopher $P_j$. For $P_j$ to have acquired $C_m$, it must have been $P_j$'s **first** chopstick (by the rule). 

Now, $P_j$ is also **waiting** for another chopstick $C_n$ (its second chopstick). But $P_j$ can only be blocked if some other philosopher $P_k$ is holding $C_n$ AND $P_k$ has not yet been able to acquire its own second chopstick.

By induction, no chain can loop back to a lower-numbered chopstick without violating the rule that lower-numbered chopsticks must be acquired first. Hence, **the lowest-numbered chopstick in the cycle can never be held while another waits for it indefinitely** — contradiction.

**∴ The system cannot deadlock.** ∎

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to **change the pick-up order for philosopher $P_4$** (since $C_0 < C_4$ in numeric value, even though $C_4$ is to $P_4$'s left). Marks are lost when students assume the rule is always "pick up left first" without applying the numerical ordering. **Always apply `min` and `max` based on chopstick NUMBER, not spatial position.**

*(Award 2 marks for code, 2 marks for correct mapping of P₀ to P₄, 3 marks for formal/informal proof of deadlock-freedom.)*

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the Dining Philosophers Problem. Explain why a simple semaphore-based solution is **not sufficient**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a solution using a **single global mutex (arbitrator)** and a **state array** to ensure deadlock-free and starvation-free operation. Write the complete `pickup()` and `putdown()` routines. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Problem Description & Why Simple Semaphores Fail (7 marks)**

*[Same problem statement as Question A(a) for 3 marks — 1 mark statement, 2 marks naive code with deadlock trace]*

The simple solution assigns **one binary semaphore per chopstick**. While this trivially satisfies **mutual exclusion** (Condition 1 of Coffman), it fails to prevent **deadlock** because the other three Coffman conditions are still satisfied:

- **Hold and Wait:** A philosopher holds one chopstick and waits for the other.
- **No Preemption:** A philosopher cannot be forced to release a chopstick.
- **Circular Wait:** A cycle of waiting is possible (e.g., $P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_4 \to P_0$).

**Additional Limitations of Naive Solution:**
- Does not guarantee **no starvation** — a philosopher may be perpetually overtaken.
- Cannot enforce **fairness** among philosophers.
- Does not scale — for $N$ philosophers, the circular wait pattern becomes a generic deadlock for any $N \geq 2$.

Hence, a **smarter coordination mechanism** is needed — either a **resource ordering rule** or a **centralized arbitrator** with state tracking.

*(Award 3 marks for problem + naive code, 2 marks for identifying all 3 failed Coffman conditions, 2 marks for additional limitations.)*

---

**(b) Arbitrator Solution with State Array (7 marks)**

**Data structures:**

```
semaphore mutex = 1;           // protects state changes (initially 1)
semaphore C[5] = {0,0,0,0,0}; // initially 0 — philosopher waits here
enum state[5] = THINKING;      // THINKING, HUNGRY, or EATING
```

**The `test(i)` helper — checks if philosopher $i$ can eat:**

```
void test(int i) {
    if (state[i] == HUNGRY &&
        state[(i+4) % 5] != EATING &&   // left neighbor
        state[(i+1) % 5] != EATING) {   // right neighbor
        state[i] = EATING;
        signal(C[i]);    // wake up philosopher i
    }
}
```

**The `pickup(i)` routine — called by philosopher $i$ to eat:**

```
void pickup(int i) {
    wait(mutex);          // enter critical region
    state[i] = HUNGRY;
    test(i);              // try to acquire both chopsticks
    signal(mutex);        // exit critical region
    wait(C[i]);           // block if could not eat (test failed)
}
```

**The `putdown(i)` routine — called when philosopher $i$ finishes eating:**

```
void putdown(int i) {
    wait(mutex);
    state[i] = THINKING;
    test((i+4) % 5);      // check LEFT neighbor — wake if possible
    test((i+1) % 5);      // check RIGHT neighbor — wake if possible
    signal(mutex);
}
```

**Philosopher process:**

```
process P[i] {
    while (true) {
        think();
        pickup(i);
        eat();
        putdown(i);
    }
}
```

**Why this is Deadlock-Free and Starvation-Free:**

- **Deadlock-Free:** The `mutex` ensures atomic state transitions, preventing circular wait. The `test()` function only allows a philosopher to enter EATING state if **both neighbors are not EATING**, which breaks the circular wait condition.

- **Starvation-Free:** Because `putdown(i)` always checks **both** neighbors, every hungry philosopher whose neighbors are not EATING will eventually be signaled on its semaphore `C[i]`. Bounded waiting is guaranteed as long as the scheduler is fair.

- **Trade-off:** Maximum concurrency is reduced because all pickup/putdown operations are **serialized through `mutex`**. At most one philosopher changes state at a time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Students frequently **initialize `C[i]` to 1** instead of 0. This is wrong: `C[i]` should be 0 so the philosopher **blocks** on `wait(C[i])` if `test()` fails. If initialized to 1, the philosopher will proceed to eat without acquiring both chopsticks — **race condition!**
> - Students forget to check **BOTH neighbors** in `putdown()`. Both `test((i+4)%5)` AND `test((i+1)%5)` must be called to ensure wakeup propagation.

*(Award 2 marks for data structures, 2 marks for test(), 1.5 marks for pickup(), 1.5 marks for putdown() with full explanation.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):**

- ✅ **Dining Philosophers Problem was proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra in 1965.**
- ✅ **Standard setup:** 5 philosophers, 5 chopsticks, circular table, requires both adjacent chopsticks to eat.
- ✅ **Four solution requirements:** Mutual Exclusion, No Deadlock, No Starvation, Maximum Concurrency.
- ✅ **Naive semaphore solution causes DEADLOCK** because all 4 Coffman conditions are satisfied simultaneously.
- ✅ **Resource Hierarchy Solution:** Pick lower-numbered chopstick **first** — breaks **Circular Wait**. For $P_4$, the "first" chopstick becomes $C_0$ (not $C_4$) because $0 < 4$.
- ✅ **Arbitrator Solution:** Use `mutex` + state array `{THINKING, HUNGRY, EATING}` + per-philosopher semaphores `C[5] = {0,...,0}`.
- ✅ **Initial values:** `mutex = 1`, `C[i] = 0`, `state[i] = THINKING`.
- ✅ **test(i) condition:** `state[i] == HUNGRY` AND both neighbors `!= EATING`.
- ✅ **putdown(i) calls test() on BOTH neighbors** — left `(i+4)%5` and right `(i+1)%5`.
- ✅ **Resource Hierarchy = Deadlock-Free but may starve**; **Arbitrator = Deadlock-Free AND Starvation-Free** but has lower concurrency (mutex serialization).
- ✅ **Maximum parallel eaters** for N=5: $\lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2$ (e.g., $P_0$ and $P_2$ simultaneously).
- ✅ **Time complexity** of all solutions: $O(1)$ per philosopher per cycle (constant work per eat).
- ✅ **Real-world analogs:** Database two-phase locking, distributed mutex services (ZooKeeper, etcd), multi-core cache line arbitration.
- ✅ **For KTU Part A (3 marks):** Focus on definition + four requirements + deadlock cause.
- ✅ **For KTU Part B (14 marks):** Be ready to write the **complete pseudocode** for EITHER resource hierarchy OR arbitrator solution with a trace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic — "MDSM":** A valid Dining Philosophers solution must satisfy **M**utual exclusion, no **D**eadlock, no **S**tarvation, **M**aximum concurrency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Base/Bounds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Generalized Base/Bounds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Base/Bounds (Multi-Segment Base/Bounds)** is an advanced hardware-supported memory management technique that extends the simple base/bounds relocation scheme by providing **multiple independent base/bounds register pairs**, one for each logical segment of a process (typically Code, Heap, and Stack). Each pair is augmented with **protection bits** (Read/Write/Execute) and a **shared bit**, enabling non-contiguous physical memory placement, controlled code sharing, and fine-grained access control.

The scheme requires the virtual address to be split into two parts:
- **Segment Selector (Top *k* bits):** Identifies which logical segment is being referenced.
- **Offset (Remaining bits):** The displacement within that segment.

Hardware (MMU) uses the segment selector to pick the appropriate base/bounds register pair, performs an independent bounds check, and adds the base to compute the physical address.

### Key Standard Metrics
- **Typical Segment Count:** **3 pairs** (Code, Heap, Stack) in textbook models.
- **Segment Selector Width:** **2 bits** (allowing 4 segments) for a 16-bit VA, or **4 bits** for a 32-bit VA.
- **Protection Granularity:** **Per-segment** (not per-page).

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Generalized Base/Bounds like a multi-warehouse logistics system for a company.**
>
> Imagine a company (the process) has three types of goods: **Product Manuals (Code)**, **Raw Materials (Heap)**, and **Finished Goods (Stack)**. The simple base/bounds approach puts everything in **one giant warehouse** — if one section grows, the whole warehouse must shift, and you cannot share the manual room with another company.
>
> The **Generalized Base/Bounds** approach gives the company **three separate warehouses**, each with its own:
> - **Starting warehouse number (Base)**
> - **Maximum capacity (Bounds)**
> - **Access rules (Read/Write/Execute)** — e.g., manuals are "read-only," raw materials are "read/write."
> - **Sharing flag (Shared bit)** — e.g., the manual warehouse can be shared with a partner company.
>
> When a worker requests "Manual, Shelf 50", the system looks up the **Code warehouse's base**, adds the offset 50, and gives the physical shelf number. If the shelf number exceeds the warehouse capacity (bounds check fails), the worker is stopped. This is exactly how the MMU translates virtual addresses using multiple base/bounds pairs.

### Why "Generalized"?
The term **generalized** distinguishes it from the **simple (single-pair) base/bounds** scheme where the *entire* address space is treated as one monolithic block. The generalized version **lifts the contiguity assumption** at the *process level* and introduces **per-segment semantics**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The hardware performs the following sequence for **every memory reference** issued by the running process:

1. **Issue Virtual Address (VA):** The CPU generates a virtual address from the instruction (e.g., fetching an instruction, loading data, pushing to stack).
2. **Decode Segment Selector:** The top *k* bits of the VA are extracted to identify the segment (e.g., `00` = Code, `01` = Heap, `11` = Stack).
3. **Extract Offset:** The remaining lower bits form the offset *d* within the segment.
4. **Bounds Check:** Hardware compares *d* against the selected segment's **Bounds (Limit)** register. If `d >= Bounds`, a **segmentation fault** trap is raised to the OS.
5. **Protection Check:** The requested access type (read/write/execute) is matched against the segment's **Protection Bits**. Mismatch causes a protection fault.
6. **Address Translation:** Physical Address (PA) is computed as:
   $$PA = Base_{segment} + d$$
7. **Memory Access:** The PA is sent to the physical memory bus to fetch/store data.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why multiple pairs?** To allow **different growth directions** (heap grows up, stack grows down) and **independent placement** of code/data in physical memory.
- **Why protection bits?** To enforce that **code is execute-only/read-only** and **stack is read/write** — a cornerstone of memory safety.
- **Why the shared bit?** To enable **re-entrant code** (e.g., `printf`, `libc`) loaded once in physical memory but mapped into multiple process address spaces — saving physical memory and enabling inter-process communication.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Historical basis** for the **Intel x86 segmentation** unit (pre-paging era).
- **Conceptual precursor** to **paging** (which solves the remaining external fragmentation problem of segments).
- **Still used in embedded RTOS** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M with MPU regions) and **microcontroller memory protection** schemes where simplicity and determinism matter.
- **Foundation** for understanding **virtual memory**, **TLBs**, and **modern OS security** (e.g., NX bits, W^X policies).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Units / Notes** |
|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Physical Address (Simple) | $PA = VA + Base$ | Bytes; one base register |
| Physical Address (Segmented) | $PA = Base_{i} + d$ | $i$ = segment index, $d$ = offset |
| Bounds Check (Per Segment) | $0 \le d < Bounds_{i}$ | Inclusive lower, exclusive upper |
| Virtual Address Split | $VA = (seg\_sel \ll k) \;\vert\; d$ | Top $k$ bits = segment, rest = offset |
| Max Offset Size | $d_{max} = 2^{n-k}$ | Bytes; $n$ = VA width, $k$ = sel bits |
| Max Segments | $2^{k}$ | For $k$ selector bits |
| Protection Flags | $\{R, W, X\}$ | Boolean OR combination |
| Shared Segment Constraint | $Base_{shared}$ must be **identical** across processes | Otherwise sharing fails |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** When writing bounds in text, use `\vert d \vert` or `\vert d \vert < Bounds` — **never** the bare pipe `|` inside markdown table cells, as it breaks the table renderer.

### Assumptions & Boundary Conditions
- **Assumption 1:** Each segment must be **contiguous in physical memory** (this is why segmentation still suffers external fragmentation).
- **Assumption 2:** The number of segments is **small and fixed** (typically 2–8) because hardware has a limited number of base/bounds register pairs.
- **Boundary:** Offset is **unsigned**; negative offsets are illegal in this scheme.
- **Context Switch:** On every context switch, the OS must **reload all base/bounds register pairs** and protection metadata — a non-trivial cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### Analytical Comparison Matrix: Simple vs. Generalized Base/Bounds

| **Parameter** | **Simple Base/Bounds** | **Generalized Base/Bounds** |
|---------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Number of Register Pairs | **1** | **3 to 4** (typically) |
| Physical Memory Layout | Contiguous (one block) | **Non-contiguous** (per segment) |
| Code Sharing | Not supported | **Supported** (via shared bit) |
| Protection Granularity | Whole address space | **Per segment** (R/W/X) |
| External Fragmentation | High (large block) | **Moderate** (per-segment blocks) |
| Hardware Cost | Low (2 registers) | Higher (3+ pairs + tag bits) |
| Context Switch Overhead | Low | **Higher** (multiple reloads) |
| Growth Direction | Single direction | **Multiple** (e.g., heap up, stack down) |

---

### Exhaustive Worked Example: Address Translation in a 3-Segment System

#### System Configuration
- **Virtual Address Width:** 16 bits
- **Segment Selector:** Top 2 bits (bits 15–14)
- **Offset Width:** 14 bits (bits 13–0) → Max segment size = $2^{14} = 16384$ bytes = **16 KB**

#### Segment Table (Loaded by OS on Context Switch)

| **Segment #** | **Purpose** | **Base (Physical)** | **Bounds (Size)** | **Protection** | **Shared?** |
|----------------|--------------|----------------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------|
| 00 (Code) | Program Text | 0x4000 (16 KB) | 0x3000 (12 KB) | R-X | Yes |
| 01 (Heap) | Dynamic Data | 0x9000 (36 KB) | 0x2000 (8 KB) | RW- | No |
| 11 (Stack) | Call Stack | 0xF000 (60 KB) | 0x4000 (16 KB) | RW- | No |

> *Note: Segment 10 is reserved/unused in this model.*

---

### Worked Translation 1: VA = `0x0000 5000`

#### Step A — Decode the Virtual Address
Convert VA to binary to extract segment selector and offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VA &= 0x00005000 \\
   &= 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0101\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_{2} \\
\text{Bits 15-14 (Selector)} &= 00_{2} = 0 \quad \text{(Code segment)} \\
\text{Bits 13-0 (Offset)} &= 000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0101\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_{2} \\
                           &= 0x1000 = 4096 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B — Bounds Check
$$
\begin{aligned}
Bounds_{Code} &= 0x3000 = 12288 \text{ bytes} \\
Offset &= 0x1000 = 4096 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Check: } 0 &\le 4096 < 12288 \quad \text{✓ PASS}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step C — Protection Check
Instruction fetch (Execute access). Code segment is **R-X** (Read + Execute). ✓ **PASS**

#### Step D — Compute Physical Address
$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= Base_{Code} + Offset \\
   &= 0x4000 + 0x1000 \\
   &= 0x5000 \quad \text{(20,480 bytes in physical memory)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Result
Virtual `0x00005000` → **Physical `0x00005000`** (Code segment fetch). ✓ Valid.

---

### Worked Translation 2: VA = `0x8000 C000`

#### Step A — Decode
$$
\begin{aligned}
VA &= 0x8000C000 \\
   &= 1000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 1100\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_{2} \\
\text{Bits 15-14} &= 10_{2} = 2 \quad \text{(Segment 10 — UNUSED)} \\
\text{Offset} &= 0x0C00 = 3072
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B — Bounds Check
Segment 10 is **not configured** in the segment table. Hardware detects an invalid segment selector.

$$
\text{✗ TRAP: Illegal segment reference — Segmentation Fault}
$$

The OS terminates the process with a fault (or raises SIGSEGV in Unix terminology).

---

### Worked Translation 3: VA = `0xC000 2000` (Stack Access)

#### Step A — Decode
$$
\begin{aligned}
VA &= 0xC0002000 \\
   &= 1100\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0010\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_{2} \\
\text{Bits 15-14} &= 11_{2} = 3 \quad \text{(Stack segment)} \\
\text{Offset} &= 0x0200 = 512
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B — Bounds Check
$$
\begin{aligned}
Bounds_{Stack} &= 0x4000 = 16384 \\
Offset &= 0x0200 = 512 \\
\text{Check: } 0 &\le 512 < 16384 \quad \text{✓ PASS}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step C — Protection Check
Stack write. Stack segment is **RW-** (Read + Write). ✓ **PASS**

#### Step D — Compute Physical Address
$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= Base_{Stack} + Offset \\
   &= 0xF000 + 0x0200 \\
   &= 0xF200 \quad \text{(61,952 bytes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Result
Virtual `0xC0002000` → **Physical `0x0000F200`** (Stack push operation). ✓ Valid.

---

### Derivative Analysis: Memory Savings via Sharing

Suppose 5 processes all use the same `libc.so` (size 4 KB) loaded into physical memory at base `0x10000`.

#### Without Generalized Base/Bounds
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Memory Used} &= 5 \text{ processes} \times 4\text{ KB} = 20 \text{ KB (duplicated)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### With Generalized Base/Bounds (Shared Code Segment)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Memory Used} &= 4\text{ KB (one copy)} + 5 \times (\text{Heap} + \text{Stack}) \\
\text{Savings} &= 4 \times 4\text{ KB} = 16 \text{ KB saved}
\end{aligned}
$$

This requires that the **Code segment descriptor in all 5 processes point to the same `Base = 0x10000`** and the **Shared bit = 1**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Address Translation Flow in Generalized Base/Bounds (MMU Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Issues Virtual Address VA] --> B[Extract Segment Selector - Top 2 Bits]
    A --> C[Extract Offset - Lower 14 Bits]
    B --> D[Select Base Bounds Pair from MMU Registers]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Bounds Check: Offset less than Bounds?}
    E -->|No - FAILS| F[TRAP to OS - Segmentation Fault]
    E -->|Yes - PASSES| G{Protection Check: Access Type Allowed?}
    G -->|No - FAILS| H[TRAP to OS - Protection Fault]
    G -->|Yes - PASSES| I[Compute PA equals Base plus Offset]
    I --> J[Send Physical Address to Memory Bus]
    J --> K[Physical Memory Read or Write Completes]
```

### Diagram 2: Non-Contiguous Physical Memory Placement (Process View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VAS[Virtual Address Space of Process P1]
        V0[Segment 00 - Code  VA Range 0x0000 to 0x2FFF]
        V1[Segment 01 - Heap  VA Range 0x4000 to 0x5FFF]
        V2[Segment 11 - Stack  VA Range 0xC000 to 0xFFFF]
    end

    subgraph PAS[Physical Memory Layout]
        P0[Code Segment Physically at 0x4000 to 0x6FFF - SHARED]
        P1[Heap Segment Physically at 0x9000 to 0xAFFF - Private]
        P2[Stack Segment Physically at 0xF000 to 0x12FFF - Private]
    end

    V0 -. MMU Maps with Base 0x4000 .-> P0
    V1 -. MMU Maps with Base 0x9000 .-> P1
    V2 -. MMU Maps with Base 0xF000 .-> P2
```

### Diagram 3: Context Switch Sequence (OS Responsibility)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CS[Context Switch Triggered - Timer Interrupt or System Call] --> S1[Save Current Process PCB]
    S1 --> S2[Load New Process PCB]
    S2 --> S3[Read New Process Segment Table from Kernel Memory]
    S3 --> R1[Load MMU Base Register 0 - Code]
    S3 --> R2[Load MMU Bounds Register 0 - Code]
    S3 --> R3[Load MMU Base Register 1 - Heap]
    S3 --> R4[Load MMU Bounds Register 1 - Heap]
    S3 --> R5[Load MMU Base Register 2 - Stack]
    S3 --> R6[Load MMU Bounds Register 2 - Stack]
    R1 --> DONE[MMU Ready - Resume New Process]
    R2 --> DONE
    R3 --> DONE
    R4 --> DONE
    R5 --> DONE
    R6 --> DONE
```

### Diagram 4: Sharing Model — Two Processes, One Code Segment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC1[Process P1 Address Space]
        P1C[Code Segment Descriptor: Base equals 0x10000, Shared equals 1]
        P1H[Heap Segment: Private to P1]
    end

    subgraph PROC2[Process P2 Address Space]
        P2C[Code Segment Descriptor: Base equals 0x10000, Shared equals 1]
        P2H[Heap Segment: Private to P2]
    end

    SHARED[(Physical Code at 0x10000 - 4 KB SHARED REGION)]

    P1C --> SHARED
    P2C --> SHARED
```

> [!NOTE]
> Both processes point to the **same physical base address** `0x10000` for code. The OS loader sets the `Base` and `Shared` fields identically in both process segment tables. Any write attempt to this region triggers a protection fault (code is R-X).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between the Base register and the Bounds register in a base/bounds memory management scheme. State the role of each during address translation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Base Register** | **Bounds Register** |
|-------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Contents** | Starting physical address of the process | Size (length) of the process address space |
| **Used For** | Relocation: $PA = VA + Base$ | Protection: check $VA < Bounds$ |
| **Set By** | OS during context switch | OS during context switch |
| **Failure If** | — | Violation causes segmentation fault |

The **Base** register provides **relocation** (logical → physical mapping), while the **Bounds** register provides **protection** (prevents access outside the process's allocated space). *(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for distinction and translation role.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional]**
**List any three limitations of the simple (single-pair) base/bounds memory management technique.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **No Code Sharing:** The entire address space is treated as one block; shared libraries (e.g., `libc`) cannot be mapped into multiple processes efficiently.
2. **Contiguity Requirement:** The whole process address space must occupy one contiguous region of physical memory, leading to **external fragmentation**.
3. **Uniform Protection:** All memory regions share the same protection; code cannot be marked execute-only while data is read/write, weakening memory safety.
4. *(Bonus point)* **Single Growth Direction:** Does not naturally support independent growth of heap (upward) and stack (downward).

---

### **PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Generalized Base/Bounds** memory management scheme with a neat block diagram. How does it differ from the simple base/bounds approach?

**Model Answer:**

The **Generalized Base/Bounds** scheme extends the simple scheme by providing **multiple base/bounds register pairs** in the MMU — typically three: one for **Code (Text)**, one for **Heap**, and one for **Stack**. Each pair is augmented with **protection bits** (Read, Write, Execute) and a **shared bit**.

**Key Differences from Simple Base/Bounds:**

| **Feature** | **Simple** | **Generalized** |
|--------------|--------------|------------------|
| Register Pairs | 1 | 3 (Code, Heap, Stack) |
| Sharing | No | Yes (via shared bit) |
| Protection | Uniform | Per-segment (R/W/X) |
| Physical Layout | One contiguous block | Multiple non-contiguous blocks |

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{array}{c}
\text{Virtual Address} \rightarrow \text{Segment Decoder} \rightarrow \\
\text{Base}_{i} \text{ register} \rightarrow \text{Adder} \rightarrow \text{Physical Address} \\
\text{Bounds}_{i} \text{ register} \rightarrow \text{Comparator} \rightarrow \text{Valid or Fault}
\end{array}
}
$$

**Operation:** The top 2 bits of the VA select which base/bounds pair is used; the remaining bits form the offset. The offset is compared against the bounds (protection check), then added to the base (translation). *(Full marks for diagram + table + clear explanation of segment decoding.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a 16-bit virtual address system using generalized base/bounds with **3 segments** (top 2 bits = segment, lower 14 bits = offset). The segment table is:

| **Segment** | **Base** | **Bounds** | **Access** |
|-------------|----------|------------|------------|
| 00 (Code) | 0x4000 | 0x3000 | R-X |
| 01 (Heap) | 0x9000 | 0x2000 | RW- |
| 11 (Stack) | 0xF000 | 0x4000 | RW- |

Translate the following virtual addresses, showing bounds check and protection check for each:
**(i)** `0x00002000`   **(ii)** `0x00005000`   **(iii)** `0x80008000`   **(iv)** `0xC0002000`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) VA = 0x00002000**
- Selector = `00` (Code), Offset = `0x2000`
- Bounds check: $0x2000 < 0x3000$ ✓
- Access = Execute (for code fetch) — matches R-X ✓
- $PA = 0x4000 + 0x2000 = \mathbf{0x6000}$ ✓ **VALID**

**(ii) VA = 0x00005000**
- Selector = `00` (Code), Offset = `0x5000`
- Bounds check: $0x5000 \ge 0x3000$ ✗
- **SEGMENTATION FAULT** (offset exceeds bounds of Code segment)

**(iii) VA = 0x80008000**
- Selector = `10` (Segment 2 — **UNUSED / RESERVED**)
- Hardware detects invalid segment → **SEGMENTATION FAULT** (illegal segment)

**(iv) VA = 0xC0002000**
- Selector = `11` (Stack), Offset = `0x2000`
- Bounds check: $0x2000 < 0x4000$ ✓
- Access = Write (stack push) — matches RW- ✓
- $PA = 0xF000 + 0x2000 = \mathbf{0xF200}$ ✓ **VALID**

*(Award 1.5 marks per sub-part: 0.5 decode, 0.5 bounds check, 0.5 protection + final PA. Deduct 0.5 if bounds check is skipped.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Simple Base/Bounds** with **Generalized Base/Bounds**. Explain how the generalized scheme enables **code sharing** and **fine-grained protection** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Simple Base/Bounds** | **Generalized Base/Bounds** |
|----------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Hardware Registers | 1 base, 1 bounds | 3+ base, 3+ bounds, 3+ protection, 3+ shared |
| Sharing Support | Not possible | Code segment can be marked **shared** |
| Protection | Single uniform policy | **Per-segment R/W/X** |
| Physical Memory | One contiguous block | Non-contiguous per segment |
| Fragmentation | High (large blocks) | Moderate (smaller blocks) |
| Example Use | Early mainframes, MS-DOS | Early Unix, x86 real mode, ARM MPU |

**Code Sharing Example:** In Unix, `libc.so` is loaded once at physical address `0x10000`. The OS sets the **Code segment descriptor** in the segment table of *every* process with `Base = 0x10000` and `Shared = 1`. All processes access the same physical pages, saving memory. The protection bits (R-X) prevent any process from writing to the shared code, ensuring integrity.

**Fine-Grained Protection Example:** A compiler can enforce:
- Code segment: **R-X** (read + execute only) — prevents code injection attacks.
- Heap segment: **RW-** (read + write only) — prevents execution of heap data (basic W^X).
- Stack segment: **RW-** — prevents execution of stack data, mitigating buffer-overflow code execution.

*(4 marks for comparison table, 3 marks for examples with explanation.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **hardware support** required to implement generalized base/bounds. What happens during a **context switch**?

**Model Answer:**

**Hardware Requirements:**

1. **Multiple Base/Bounds Register Pairs** in the MMU (typically 2–8 pairs).
2. **Segment Selector Decode Logic** to extract the top *k* bits of the VA.
3. **Comparator Circuit** for bounds checking (offset < bounds).
4. **Protection Bit Comparators** to validate access type against R/W/X bits.
5. **Shared Bit Logic** to allow multiple processes to point to the same physical region.
6. **Trap Generation Unit** to deliver faults to the OS on violations.

**Context Switch Sequence:**

1. OS saves the current process's **PCB (Process Control Block)**, including all base/bounds register values.
2. OS loads the new process's PCB.
3. For each segment $i$ (Code, Heap, Stack):
   - Load `Base[i]` from PCB into MMU register.
   - Load `Bounds[i]` from PCB into MMU register.
   - Load `Protection[i]` and `Shared[i]` bits.
4. OS triggers MMU flush (if cached) and resumes the new process.

**Cost:** Reloading 3+ register pairs per context switch is non-trivial. This overhead is one reason modern systems moved to **paging** with a single base register (page table base register) and hardware TLB.

*(3 marks hardware list, 2 marks context switch steps, 2 marks cost/discussion.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Base/Bounds Questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the bounds check (–1 to –2 marks):** Always perform bounds check *before* address translation. Showing $PA = Base + Offset$ without verifying $Offset < Bounds$ loses full marks.
> 2. **Confusing segment number with offset (–1 mark):** The top *k* bits select the segment; the rest is offset. Do not treat the whole VA as offset.
> 3. **Forgetting protection check (–1 mark):** Even if bounds pass, write-to-code or execute-from-stack must be flagged as protection faults.
> 4. **Incorrect sharing assumption (–1 mark):** For sharing to work, the **Base** must be identical across processes, and the **Shared bit = 1**. Forgetting the bit loses marks.
> 5. **No diagram in Part B (–1 mark):** KTU explicitly awards 1–2 marks for "neat diagram." Always include a labeled block diagram of the MMU.
> 6. **Confusing Generalized Base/Bounds with Paging:** These are *different* schemes. Paging uses a single base register (PTBR) + page table; Generalized Base/Bounds uses multiple register pairs. Do not mix terminology.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Generalized Base/Bounds = Multiple register pairs**, typically 3 (Code, Heap, Stack).
- ✅ **Virtual Address Split:** Top *k* bits = segment selector; lower bits = offset.
- ✅ **Translation Formula:** $PA = Base_{i} + Offset$ (only after bounds check passes).
- ✅ **Bounds Check Condition:** $0 \le Offset < Bounds_{i}$ (offset must be strictly less than bounds).
- ✅ **Protection Bits:** {R, W, X} enforced **per segment**, not per page.
- ✅ **Shared Bit:** Allows multiple processes to map to the same physical base → enables code/library sharing.
- ✅ **Context Switch Cost:** OS must reload **all** base/bounds/protection registers from PCB.
- ✅ **Key Limitation:** Segments must be **contiguous in physical memory** → still suffers external fragmentation.
- ✅ **Precursor to Paging:** Generalized base/bounds naturally evolved into paging to solve contiguity and fragmentation.
- ✅ **Real-world Use:** Intel x86 (real mode, early protected mode), ARM Cortex-M MPU, early Unix, some RTOS.
- ✅ **KTU-Favorite Question Types:** Address translation with given segment table; comparison table with simple vs. generalized; context switch sequence; protection and sharing examples.
- ✅ **Always show:** Decode → Bounds Check → Protection Check → Compute PA (in this exact order).
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** If the segment selector bits are unused (e.g., segment `10` in a 3-segment system), explicitly state it is a **reserved/illegal segment** and trap to OS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Address Translation in Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Address Translation in Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Segmentation** is a non-contiguous memory management scheme that provides a logical view of memory where a program is divided into variable-sized logical units called **segments** (such as code, data, stack, heap). Each segment is a logical entity that the programmer is aware of and that carries its own semantic meaning.

**Address Translation in Segmentation** is the hardware-assisted procedure by which a CPU-generated **logical (virtual) address** is mapped to a corresponding **physical (real) address** in main memory, by consulting a per-process data structure called the **Segment Table** maintained by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**.

A logical address in a segmented system is a **two-dimensional tuple** $\langle s, d \rangle$, where:
- $s$ = **segment number** (used as an index into the segment table)
- $d$ = **offset / displacement** within the segment

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must master the segment table lookup, the bound-check mechanism (offset vs. limit), and the computation of the final physical address. These three operations form the backbone of every KTU question on segmentation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a large office building with several departments (HR, Accounts, Engineering, Sales). Instead of assigning each department a random chunk of floors, the building administrator maintains a **directory** (the segment table) that records, for each department, *which floor it starts on* and *how many rooms it has*.

- **Department name** → Segment number ($s$)
- **Room number within the department** → Offset ($d$)
- **Starting floor in the directory** → Base address
- **Total rooms allocated to the department** → Segment limit

When an employee says *"I want Room 7 of the HR department,"* the administrator:
1. Looks up "HR" in the directory → finds starting floor (e.g., 3rd floor) and size (e.g., 10 rooms).
2. **Verifies** that Room 7 exists (i.e., $7 < 10$) — this is the **limit check**.
3. Computes the actual physical location: Floor 3 + Room 7 → **Room 10 in the building**.

If the employee asks for Room 25 of HR, the administrator refuses (segmentation fault). The CPU performs exactly this analogy: extract segment + offset, consult the segment table, bound-check, and compute the physical address.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Paging:** In paging, the offset and page number are *invisible* to the programmer (purely a hardware view). In segmentation, the **segment number is a logical, programmer-visible concept** — it directly corresponds to named units like `code`, `data`, `stack`. This is why segmentation is sometimes called a *logical memory management scheme*.

## 1.3 Why Segmentation? — The Motivation

- A programmer views a program as a collection of **logical entities** (main routine, subroutines, global variables, stack, heap, symbol table).
- Paging hides this structure; segmentation **preserves** it, allowing the OS to apply **different protection and sharing rules** to each logical unit.
- A read-only segment (e.g., code) can be shared among processes without contamination.
- A code segment can be marked **execute-only**, a data segment as **read-write**, etc.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Segmented Logical Address

A logical address in segmentation is a **two-part structure**:

$$\text{Logical Address} = \langle s, d \rangle$$

| Component | Symbol | Bits Allocated | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Number | $s$ | $k$ bits (MSB part) | Selects the segment table entry |
| Displacement / Offset | $d$ | $n$ bits (LSB part) | Byte offset within the selected segment |

If the CPU generates a $k+n$ bit logical address, then:
- The number of possible segments per process = $2^k$
- The maximum size of any single segment = $2^n$ bytes

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic KTU Numerical Setup:** "A system uses a logical address space of 16 bits, with the upper 4 bits as segment number and lower 12 bits as offset." This implies $2^4 = 16$ segments and a maximum segment size of $2^{12} = 4096$ bytes.

## 2.2 The Segment Table (ST) — The Heart of Translation

The OS maintains **one segment table per process** (kept in main memory; a copy of the base pointer for the ST is stored in the **PCB** and loaded into a dedicated hardware register — the **Segment Table Base Register (STBR)** — on a context switch).

Each row (entry) of the segment table contains:

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Base** $B_i$ | Starting physical address of segment $i$ in RAM |
| **Limit** $L_i$ | Length of segment $i$ (in bytes) |
| Protection bits | Read / Write / Execute permissions (often examined) |
| Valid / Present bit | Whether the segment is currently in memory |
| Other flags | Paging-within-segment bit, shared-segment pointer, etc. |

A minimal ST entry can be represented as a tuple:
$$\text{ST}_i = (B_i, L_i)$$

## 2.3 The Address Translation Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

The MMU executes the following **hardware sequence** whenever a CPU reference is made:

1. **Extract** segment number $s$ and offset $d$ from the logical address.
2. **Index** the segment table using $s$: load entry $(B_s, L_s)$.
3. **Compare** $d$ against $L_s$ (the limit).
   - If $d \geq L_s$: TRAP to OS → **Segmentation Fault** (address is illegal).
4. **Compute** the physical address: $\text{PA} = B_s + d$.
5. **Issue** the memory request at PA on the physical bus.

## 2.4 Protection and Sharing

- **Protection:** The bound check $0 \le d < L_s$ prevents a process from referencing memory outside its own segment. Combined with read/write/execute bits, this yields robust memory protection.
- **Sharing:** Two different processes can share a *code segment* by pointing their respective ST entries to the same physical base. Each can have a *different data segment* (since data is process-specific). This is far more elegant than paging for sharing.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Repeated Question Pattern:** "Explain how segmentation supports protection and sharing of code." Always mention: *(a) limit check for protection, (b) same base in two STs for code sharing, (c) re-entrant code via separate data segments.*

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real Systems

| Domain | Utility of Segmentation |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | Compilers naturally emit segment references (`.text`, `.data`, `.bss`). Segmentation mirrors this view. |
| **Embedded Systems** | Microcontrollers (e.g., classic 8086, ARM with MPU) use segments for fixed ROM/RAM partitioning. |
| **Operating Systems** | Modern x86 still uses segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES) for privilege/flat memory model. |
| **Access Control** | Per-segment permission bits enable fine-grained security policies. |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** you should memorize for KTU Module 3 numerical and theory questions on segmentation.

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Logical address structure | $\langle s, d \rangle$ | Two-dimensional |
| 2 | Physical address computation | $\text{PA} = B_s + d$ | Linear addition |
| 3 | Bound check (validity) | $0 \le d < L_s$ | If violated → trap |
| 4 | Number of segments per process | $2^k$ | $k$ = bits for $s$ |
| 5 | Maximum segment size | $2^n$ bytes | $n$ = bits for $d$ |
| 6 | Logical address space size | $2^{k+n}$ bytes | Total addressable logical memory |
| 7 | Average memory access time (associative lookup) | $T_{\text{eff}} = (T_{\text{mem}} + T_{\text{ST}}) + T_{\text{mem}}$ | $= 2 \cdot T_{\text{mem}} + T_{\text{ST}}$ if ST in memory |
| 8 | With TLB / fast lookup cache | $T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{\text{ST}} + 2 T_{\text{mem}})$ | $h$ = TLB hit ratio |
| 9 | External fragmentation susceptibility | **YES** (variable-size segments) | First-fit/Best-fit applicable |
| 10 | Internal fragmentation susceptibility | **NO** | Each segment is exactly $L_i$ bytes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid breaking tables with pipes!** In the table above, every absolute-value-style notation has been avoided to preserve markdown table integrity. If a problem requires $\vert d \vert$, write it as $\lvert d \rvert$ in plain text but **never** inside a markdown table row.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Basic Address Translation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A system uses segmentation with a **2-byte logical address**. The first 2 bits denote the segment number and the remaining 14 bits denote the offset. The segment table of a process is given below:

| Segment $i$ | Base $B_i$ (hex) | Limit $L_i$ (dec) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0x2000 | 1200 |
| 1 | 0x5000 | 800 |
| 2 | 0x9000 | 1500 |
| 3 | 0xB000 | 600 |

For each of the following logical addresses, determine the physical address or report a segmentation fault:
- (a) $\langle 0, 432 \rangle$
- (b) $\langle 1, 900 \rangle$
- (c) $\langle 2, 1499 \rangle$
- (d) $\langle 3, 700 \rangle$

### Solution — Exhaustive Walkthrough

**(a) Logical address $\langle 0, 432 \rangle$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 0, \quad d = 432 \\
\text{Look up ST[0]} &\Rightarrow B_0 = \text{0x2000}, \quad L_0 = 1200 \\
\text{Bound check:} \quad 0 &\le 432 < 1200 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Physical address} &= B_0 + d = \text{0x2000} + 432 \\
\text{Convert:} \quad \text{0x2000} &= 8192_{10} \\
\text{PA} &= 8192 + 432 = 8624_{10} = \text{0x21B0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Since $0x2000 + 432 = 0x21B0$ and $0x21B0 < 0x2000 + 1200 = 0x24B0$, the access lies within the valid region of segment 0. ✓

**(b) Logical address $\langle 1, 900 \rangle$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 1, \quad d = 900 \\
\text{Look up ST[1]} &\Rightarrow B_1 = \text{0x5000}, \quad L_1 = 800 \\
\text{Bound check:} \quad 0 &\le 900 < 800 \quad \text{? } \\
900 \nless 800 \quad &\Rightarrow \textbf{SEGMENTATION FAULT}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner's note:** The student must explicitly show the comparison $d \ge L_s$ and state the trap.

**(c) Logical address $\langle 2, 1499 \rangle$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 2, \quad d = 1499 \\
\text{Look up ST[2]} &\Rightarrow B_2 = \text{0x9000}, \quad L_2 = 1500 \\
\text{Bound check:} \quad 0 &\le 1499 < 1500 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{PA} &= \text{0x9000} + 1499 = 36864 + 1499 = 38363_{10} \\
\text{Hex:} \quad 38363_{10} &= \text{0x95DB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Logical address $\langle 3, 700 \rangle$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= 3, \quad d = 700 \\
\text{Look up ST[3]} &\Rightarrow B_3 = \text{0xB000}, \quad L_3 = 600 \\
\text{Bound check:} \quad 0 &\le 700 < 600 \quad \text{? } \\
700 \nless 600 \quad &\Rightarrow \textbf{SEGMENTATION FAULT}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Matrix

| Logical Address | Bound Check | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $\langle 0, 432 \rangle$ | $432 < 1200$ ✓ | Physical address = **0x21B0** |
| $\langle 1, 900 \rangle$ | $900 \ge 800$ ✗ | **Segmentation fault** |
| $\langle 2, 1499 \rangle$ | $1499 < 1500$ ✓ | Physical address = **0x95DB** |
| $\langle 3, 700 \rangle$ | $700 \ge 600$ ✗ | **Segmentation fault** |

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Deriving Memory Size Parameters

**Problem Statement:**
A system has a **20-bit logical address space**. The segment number occupies the **upper 4 bits** and the offset the **lower 16 bits**. Calculate: (a) maximum number of segments, (b) maximum segment size, (c) total logical address space, (d) physical address space if the physical memory is **32 MB**.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Maximum segments} &= 2^4 = \mathbf{16 \text{ segments}} \\[4pt]
\text{(b) Maximum segment size} &= 2^{16} \text{ bytes} = 65536 \text{ B} = \mathbf{64 \text{ KB}} \\[4pt]
\text{(c) Total logical space} &= 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = \mathbf{1 \text{ MB}} \\[4pt]
\text{(d) Physical address space} &= 32 \text{ MB} = 32 \times 2^{20} \text{ B} = 2^{25} \text{ B} \\
&\Rightarrow \mathbf{25 \text{ physical address bits required}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Effective Access Time with TLB

**Problem Statement:**
Suppose a segmented system has the segment table stored in main memory. Memory access time = **100 ns**, segment table access time = also a memory access (100 ns), and the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) lookup time = **20 ns** with a TLB hit ratio of **80%**. Compute the effective memory access time $T_{\text{eff}}$.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{TLB}} &= 20 \text{ ns} \\
T_{\text{mem}} &= 100 \text{ ns} \\
h &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 1: TLB hit (probability $h$):**
$$
T_{\text{hit}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{mem}} = 20 + 100 = 120 \text{ ns}
$$

**Case 2: TLB miss (probability $1 - h$):**
$$
T_{\text{miss}} = T_{\text{TLB}} + T_{\text{mem,ST}} + T_{\text{mem,data}} = 20 + 100 + 100 = 220 \text{ ns}
$$

**Effective Access Time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{eff}} &= h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{miss}} \\
&= 0.80 \times 120 + 0.20 \times 220 \\
&= 96 + 44 \\
&= \mathbf{140 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Key Computations

| Scenario | Input Given | Computed Output |
|---|---|---|
| Basic translation | $\langle s, d \rangle$, ST | PA or fault |
| Bits → segments | $k$ bits for $s$ | $2^k$ segments |
| Bits → segment size | $n$ bits for $d$ | $2^n$ bytes |
| Logical address size | $k + n$ | $2^{k+n}$ bytes |
| EAT with TLB | $h, T_{TLB}, T_{mem}$ | $h(T_{TLB}+T_{mem}) + (1-h)(T_{TLB}+2T_{mem})$ |
| Sharing two processes | Common code segment base $B_c$ | Both STs have $B_c$ for that segment |
| Protection check | $d \ge L_s$ | Trap → OS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Address Translation in Segmentation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete **data flow** from CPU-issued logical address to the final physical memory bus access, including all decision gates and lookup mechanisms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Issues Logical Address: tuple s comma d"] --> B["MMU Splits Address into Segment Number s and Offset d"]
    B --> C["STBR Lookup: Locate Segment Table in Main Memory"]
    C --> D["Index Segment Table using s to Retrieve Entry"]
    D --> E["Read Base Bs and Limit Ls from ST Entry"]
    E --> F{"Bound Check: is d less than Ls"}
    F -- "No d is greater than or equal Ls" --> G["SEGMENTATION FAULT: Trap to OS"]
    F -- "Yes d is within valid range" --> H["Compute Physical Address: PA equals Bs plus d"]
    H --> I["Issue Physical Address to Memory Bus"]
    I --> J["Read or Write at PA in Physical RAM"]
    G --> K["OS Kills Process or Raises Signal SIGSEGV"]
    J --> L["Return Data to CPU"]
```

## 4.2 Segment Table Layout (Register / Memory Schema)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STBR["STBR: Segment Table Base Register"] --> ST0["ST Entry 0: Base B0 plus Limit L0"]
    STBR --> ST1["ST Entry 1: Base B1 plus Limit L1"]
    STBR --> ST2["ST Entry 2: Base B2 plus Limit L2"]
    STBR --> ST3["ST Entry 3: Base B3 plus Limit L3"]
    STBR --> STn["ST Entry n minus 1: Base plus Limit"]
```

## 4.3 Two-Dimensional Logical Address Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LA["Logical Address: 16 bits total"] --> SEG["Upper 4 bits: Segment Number s"]
    LA --> OFF["Lower 12 bits: Offset d"]
    SEG --> STIDX["Used to index into Segment Table"]
    OFF --> BCMP["Compared with Limit Ls in Bound Check"]
```

## 4.4 Address Translation Sequence (Detailed Step-Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Receive Logical Address from CPU"] --> S2["Step 2: Extract Segment Number s and Offset d"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Check if s is within valid range 0 to N minus 1"]
    S3 -- "Invalid s" --> S4["Raise Protection Trap"]
    S3 -- "Valid s" --> S5["Step 4: Read Segment Table Entry ST of s"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 5: Extract Base Bs and Limit Ls"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 6: Compare d with Ls for Bound Check"]
    S7 -- "d greater than or equal Ls" --> S8["Raise Segmentation Fault Trap"]
    S7 -- "d less than Ls" --> S9["Step 7: Compute PA equals Bs plus d"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 8: Access Physical Memory at PA"]
    S10 --> S11["Step 9: Return Data to CPU Pipeline"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `STBR`, `ST0`), and labels contain no markdown formatting tokens, ensuring reliable rendering in KTU-grade study materials and on GitHub.

---
