<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define segmentation. What are the fields of a segment table?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Segmentation (1 mark):** Segmentation is a memory management technique that divides a program into variable-sized logical units called segments, each representing a logical entity such as code, data, stack, or heap. Each segment has a name (segment number) and a length.

**Fields of a segment table (2 marks):** A segment table contains one entry per segment, and each entry typically includes:
1. **Base address** — the starting physical address of the segment in main memory.
2. **Limit** — the length of the segment (used for bound checking).
3. **Access protection bits** — read, write, execute permissions.
4. (Optional) **Valid/Present bit** and **shared-segment indicator**.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a segmentation fault? When does it occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **segmentation fault (1 mark)** is a hardware trap (exception) raised by the Memory Management Unit when a program attempts to access memory in a way that violates the segment protection rules.

**It occurs in two cases (2 marks):**
1. **Offset out of range:** The displacement $d$ is greater than or equal to the segment's limit $L_s$ (i.e., $d \ge L_s$).
2. **Invalid segment number:** The segment number $s$ exceeds the number of valid segments in the segment table.

When a segmentation fault occurs, control transfers to the OS, which typically terminates the offending process (Unix `SIGSEGV`).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, every Part B question carries 14 marks and offers a choice between two alternatives. The structure below provides both **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks.

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the address translation mechanism in segmentation. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Consider a segmented memory system with an 8-bit logical address: the upper 2 bits denote the segment number and the lower 6 bits denote the offset. The segment table is:

| Segment | Base (dec) | Limit (dec) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 100 | 30 |
| 1 | 200 | 20 |
| 2 | 300 | 25 |
| 3 | 400 | 15 |

For the logical addresses $\langle 0, 25 \rangle$, $\langle 1, 30 \rangle$, $\langle 2, 24 \rangle$, and $\langle 3, 18 \rangle$, determine the physical addresses or report segmentation faults. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Address Translation in Segmentation — Explanation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Logical Address Structure:** A CPU generates a two-part logical address $\langle s, d \rangle$, where $s$ is the segment number and $d$ is the offset within the segment.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Segment Table:** The OS maintains a **segment table** per process. Each entry contains the **Base** (physical start address) and **Limit** (segment length). A hardware register, the **Segment Table Base Register (STBR)**, holds the starting address of the current process's segment table.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — MMU Translation Procedure:**
- The MMU extracts $s$ and $d$ from the logical address.
- It indexes the segment table using $s$ to obtain $(B_s, L_s)$.
- It performs a **bound check**: $0 \le d < L_s$. If violated → segmentation fault.
- Otherwise, it computes the physical address: $\text{PA} = B_s + d$.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Protection and Sharing:**
- The bound check provides **protection** against illegal memory accesses.
- A common segment (e.g., shared code) can be accessed by multiple processes by mapping the same physical base in their respective segment tables.
- The data segment remains process-specific, enabling safe sharing of re-entrant code.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Diagram (description):** The diagram (see Section 4.4 above) shows the CPU → MMU → Segment Table → Physical Memory flow with the bound check gate.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: 8-bit logical address, $k = 2$ (segment bits), $n = 6$ (offset bits).

- Number of segments = $2^2 = 4$ ✓
- Max segment size = $2^6 = 64$ bytes

**Address 1: $\langle 0, 25 \rangle$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST[0]} &\Rightarrow B_0 = 100, \quad L_0 = 30 \\
\text{Bound check:} &\quad 25 < 30 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{PA} &= 100 + 25 = \mathbf{125}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Address 2: $\langle 1, 30 \rangle$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST[1]} &\Rightarrow B_1 = 200, \quad L_1 = 20 \\
\text{Bound check:} &\quad 30 \ge 20 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{Segmentation Fault}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Address 3: $\langle 2, 24 \rangle$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST[2]} &\Rightarrow B_2 = 300, \quad L_2 = 25 \\
\text{Bound check:} &\quad 24 < 25 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{PA} &= 300 + 24 = \mathbf{324}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Address 4: $\langle 3, 18 \rangle$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST[3]} &\Rightarrow B_3 = 400, \quad L_3 = 15 \\
\text{Bound check:} &\quad 18 \ge 15 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{Segmentation Fault}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Logical Address | Bound Check | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $\langle 0, 25 \rangle$ | $25 < 30$ ✓ | PA = 125 |
| $\langle 1, 30 \rangle$ | $30 \ge 20$ ✗ | Segmentation fault |
| $\langle 2, 24 \rangle$ | $24 < 25$ ✓ | PA = 324 |
| $\langle 3, 18 \rangle$ | $18 \ge 15$ ✗ | Segmentation fault |

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Differentiate between paging and segmentation. How does segmentation support memory protection and sharing of code? (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A system uses a 20-bit logical address with 4 bits for segment number and 16 bits for offset. If a process has segments with bases and limits: (Seg 0: Base=2000, Limit=500), (Seg 1: Base=4000, Limit=300), (Seg 2: Base=6000, Limit=700). Compute the physical addresses for logical addresses $\langle 0, 450 \rangle$ and $\langle 1, 299 \rangle$. What happens for $\langle 2, 800 \rangle$? (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Paging vs Segmentation and Protection/Sharing (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Paging | Segmentation |
|---|---|---|
| Unit of division | Fixed-size **pages** | Variable-size **segments** |
| Programmer visibility | **Invisible** (hardware-controlled) | **Visible** (logical units like code, data) |
| Address structure | $\langle \text{page\#}, \text{offset} \rangle$ | $\langle \text{segment\#}, \text{offset} \rangle$ |
| Fragmentation | Internal (last page) | External (between segments) |
| Logical mapping | No inherent meaning | Direct correspondence to program structure |
| Data structure | **Page table** | **Segment table** |
| Translation | $PA = \text{Frame\#} \times \text{PageSize} + \text{Offset}$ | $PA = \text{Base}_s + \text{Offset}$ |

**Protection (1.5 marks):** Segmentation provides protection via the **bound check** mechanism — every memory reference is validated against the segment's limit $L_s$. Access rights (read/write/execute) are stored as protection bits in each ST entry, allowing per-segment policies (e.g., code is execute-only).

**Sharing (1.5 marks):** Two processes can share a **code segment** by including the same base in their respective segment tables. The data segment remains process-specific (each process has its own data ST entry), enabling correct execution of re-entrant code without interference.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: 20-bit logical address, $k = 4$ (segment), $n = 16$ (offset).
- Max segments = $2^4 = 16$, Max segment size = $2^{16}$ B = 64 KB.

**Address 1: $\langle 0, 450 \rangle$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST[0]} &\Rightarrow B_0 = 2000, \quad L_0 = 500 \\
\text{Bound check:} &\quad 450 < 500 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{PA} &= 2000 + 450 = \mathbf{2450}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Address 2: $\langle 1, 299 \rangle$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST[1]} &\Rightarrow B_1 = 4000, \quad L_1 = 300 \\
\text{Bound check:} &\quad 299 < 300 \quad \checkmark \\
\text{PA} &= 4000 + 299 = \mathbf{4299}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Address 3: $\langle 2, 800 \rangle$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ST[2]} &\Rightarrow B_2 = 6000, \quad L_2 = 700 \\
\text{Bound check:} &\quad 800 \ge 700 \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{Segmentation Fault}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers (1 mark for table):**

| Logical Address | Bound Check | Physical Address |
|---|---|---|
| $\langle 0, 450 \rangle$ | $450 < 500$ ✓ | 2450 |
| $\langle 1, 299 \rangle$ | $299 < 300$ ✓ | 4299 |
| $\langle 2, 800 \rangle$ | $800 \ge 700$ ✗ | Segmentation fault |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bound check (–2 marks):** Many students directly compute $PA = B_s + d$ without verifying $d < L_s$. This violates the syllabus mandate.
> 2. **Misinterpreting segment bits (–1 mark):** If the problem says "upper $k$ bits" or "lower $n$ bits", ensure you allocate bits to the *correct* half of the logical address. KTU often uses either convention.
> 3. **Confusing $L_s$ as inclusive vs. exclusive (–1 mark):** The limit $L_s$ is the **length** of the segment, not the last valid address. Valid offsets are $0, 1, \ldots, L_s - 1$.
> 4. **Skipping the unit conversion to hex (–0.5 mark):** KTU frequently uses hexadecimal bases and offsets; always convert properly to base 10 before arithmetic.
> 5. **Omitting protection/sharing explanation in 7-mark theory (–2 marks):** The protection/sharing discussion is the *core* of the segmentation concept — KTU expects a full paragraph, not a one-liner.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Address Translation in Segmentation:**

- **Logical address** in segmentation is **two-dimensional**: $\langle s, d \rangle$ — segment number and offset.
- **Physical address** computation: $\text{PA} = B_s + d$, where $B_s$ is the base from the segment table.
- **Bound check** is the *defining security feature*: $0 \le d < L_s$. Failure → **Segmentation Fault**.
- **Segment table** is a per-process structure; its base pointer is held in the **STBR** (Segment Table Base Register).
- Each ST entry contains: **Base**, **Limit**, and **protection/access bits** (R/W/X).
- **Number of segments per process** = $2^k$ where $k$ = segment-number bits.
- **Max segment size** = $2^n$ bytes where $n$ = offset bits.
- **Total logical address space** = $2^{k+n}$ bytes.
- Segmentation differs from paging: it preserves **logical/programmer-visible** structure and supports **fine-grained protection and sharing**.
- Segmentation suffers from **external fragmentation** (variable-size segments) but has **no internal fragmentation**.
- **Effective access time with TLB**: $T_{\text{eff}} = h(T_{TLB}+T_{mem}) + (1-h)(T_{TLB} + 2T_{mem})$.
- KTU numerical pattern: 8-bit, 16-bit, 20-bit logical addresses with given bit splits — always derive segment count, max size, and resolve each tuple.
- Examiner's mantra: **Always show the bound check explicitly**; never skip it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support for Sharing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Support for Sharing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of Operating System Concepts, **Support for Sharing** refers to the collection of mechanisms, primitives, and policies provided by the OS that enable multiple concurrent processes (or threads) to **safely and efficiently share both physical resources** (e.g., CPU, memory, I/O devices like printers and disks) **and logical resources** (e.g., files, data structures, buffers, message queues). The kernel offers this support through three pillars of concurrency control: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Support for Sharing encompasses the OS facilities for **Mutual Exclusion**, **Synchronization**, and **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)**. It guarantees that cooperating processes can access shared resources without race conditions, deadlocks, or starvation, while still preserving data consistency and the mutual exclusion property.* The three foundational requirements of sharing support are: 1. **Mutual Exclusion (Mutex):** Only one process can use a non-shareable resource (critical resource) at a given time. 2. **Synchronization / Coordination:** Processes can coordinate their execution order using signals (wait/signal, P/V, send/receive). 3. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC):** Processes can exchange information (via shared memory or message passing). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Shared Printing Room (Print Spooler)** > > Imagine a small office with **5 employees** (processes) but only **1 printer** (shared resource). If all 5 walk up at the same time and start printing, the documents get jumbled, paper jams occur, and jobs are lost. The office manager (the Operating System) solves this by: > 1. **Issuing a token** (semaphore) — only the holder may enter the print room. > 2. **Maintaining a queue** (buffer) — jobs are spooled to disk in FIFO order. > 3. **Signaling completion** — when the printer finishes, the manager notifies the next employee. > > This is precisely how the OS provides *support for sharing* — through tokens (semaphores), queues (buffers), and signals (synchronization primitives). Another classic analogy is the **single-stall restroom in a railway station**: a status board outside (a flag/condition variable) tells waiting passengers whether it is occupied or free, preventing two people from entering simultaneously — this is *mutual exclusion enforced by synchronization*. ## 1.3 Why is Sharing Support Needed? | Scenario | Without OS Sharing Support | With OS Sharing Support | |----------|---------------------------|--------------------------| | Two processes update a bank balance simultaneously | Lost update / inconsistent data | Atomic operations via mutex | | Producer fills buffer while Consumer reads it | Buffer overrun / underrun | Semaphores (`empty` & `full`) | | Multiple processes print simultaneously | Printer hardware conflict | Spooling + scheduling | | Parent waits for child to finish | Polling wastes CPU | `wait()` system call (synchronization) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The phrase *"Support for Sharing"* in KTU textbooks (Stallings / Silberschatz) directly maps to **Chapter 5/6/7: Concurrency, Mutual Exclusion, Semaphores, Monitors, and Message Passing**. Expect at least one 7-mark sub-question on this in Part B of the End Semester Exam. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Sharing Support Mechanisms The Operating System supports sharing via **three principal paradigms**, each suited to different application needs: ### A. Mutual Exclusion Primitives These ensure **only one process** can be inside a Critical Section at a time. - **Hardware Support:** - **Disabling Interrupts** (CLI / STI) — coarse, only for single-processor kernels. - **Atomic Test-and-Set Lock (TSL) instruction** — uses a hardware `LOCK` signal on the bus. - **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)** — modern alternative used in lock-free programming. - **Software Solutions (Dekker's, Peterson's Algorithm):** Pure algorithmic mutual exclusion; rarely used in practice due to complexity and lack of scalability. ### B. Higher-Level Synchronization Primitives - **Semaphores** (Dijkstra, 1965): An integer counter accessed only through two atomic operations, **P (wait / down)** and **V (signal / up)**. - *Binary Semaphore (Mutex):* value ∈ {0, 1}. - *Counting Semaphore:* value ∈ {0, 1, 2, …, N}. - **Monitors** (Hoare / Brinch Hansen): A high-level language construct that encapsulates shared data and the procedures that operate on it, with mutual exclusion guaranteed by the compiler/runtime. ### C. Inter-Process Communication (IPC) - **Shared Memory Model:** Processes map a common memory region; fastest but requires explicit synchronization. - **Message Passing Model:** Kernel-mediated `send()` and `receive()` primitives; slower but more structured. ## 2.2 Requirements for a Correct Mutual Exclusion Solution A solution to the Critical Section Problem (the heart of "Support for Sharing") must satisfy **four mandatory conditions**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four Required Conditions (KTP — frequently asked):** > 1. **Mutual Exclusion:** No two processes may be in their critical sections at the same time. > 2. **Progress:** If no process is in the critical section and some processes *want* to enter, only those not in their remainder section can compete; the selection cannot be postponed indefinitely. > 3. **Bounded Waiting (Starvation Freedom):** There exists a bound on the number of times other processes can enter the critical section after a process has requested entry and before that request is granted. > 4. **No Assumption about Relative Speed:** The solution must work for any CPU speed or scheduling order. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation & Units | |---|---------|----------------------|------------------| | 1 | Semaphore value update (P) | $S = S - 1$ | Atomic, non-blocking on $S>0$ | | 2 | Semaphore value update (V) | $S = S + 1$ | Atomic, wakes one waiting process | | 3 | Binary Semaphore range | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | 1 = free, 0 = locked | | 4 | Counting Semaphore range | $0 \le S \le N$ | $N$ = initial resource count | | 5 | Condition for deadlock | $\exists\, p_i : \text{wait}(S_i) \wedge S_i = 0 \wedge \text{all procs blocked}$ | Logical condition | | 6 | Throughput of critical section | $T_{cs} = \dfrac{N_{executions}}{T_{total}}$ | executions per second | | 7 | Priority Inversion cost (approx.) | $T_{inv} = T_{cs-high} \cdot (N_{low}-1)$ | High-priority wait time | | 8 | Monitor condition queue length | $L_q = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ | $n_i$ = waiting processes per condition variable | | 9 | Spool buffer size (bounded) | $B_{max} = S_{max} - S_{min}$ | in bytes / blocks | | 10 | Average message-passing latency | $L_{avg} = 2 \cdot T_{copy} + T_{ctx\text{-}switch}$ | seconds, for `send`+`receive` pair | > **Key Engineering Insight:** Modern OS kernels (Linux, Windows NT) implement sharing support as a **layered architecture**: > *Hardware atomic instructions* (TSL/CAS) → *Mutex locks in kernel space* (e.g., Linux `futex`) → *Higher-level abstractions* (POSIX semaphores, System V IPC) → *User-space monitors* (Java `synchronized`, C# `lock`). ## 2.4 Types of Resource Sharing The OS supports sharing for **two fundamental categories** of resources: **1. Physical (Hardware) Resources** - **Sharable:** Disk drives (read-only access), memory, network bandwidth. - **Non-Sharable (Exclusive):** Printers, tape drives, scanners, the CPU core itself in uniprocessor mode. - **Virtually Sharable:** Modern OSes virtualize non-sharable devices via spooling (e.g., printer spooler). **2. Logical (Software) Resources** - **Data Structures:** Linked lists, hash tables, kernel buffers. - **Files:** Database records, configuration files, log files. - **Code:** Re-entrant (pure) procedures shared in memory; saves RAM. > [!NOTE] > **Pure Code / Re-entrant Code:** A piece of code is *re-entrant* if it can be safely called by multiple processes simultaneously because it does not modify itself and uses only local variables or caller-provided parameters. This is a form of *implicit sharing* supported by the OS loader. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Working of a Counting Semaphore — Exhaustive Logical Walkthrough We analyze the **atomic execution of the P (wait) and V (signal) operations**, which form the kernel of OS sharing support. ### 3.1.1 Semaphore Data Structure A semaphore is internally represented by the OS as a struct (C-style pseudocode for clarity): ```text typedef struct { int value; // current count of available resources struct PCB *wait_list; // queue of blocked processes } semaphore; ``` ### 3.1.2 The P(wait) Operation — Step-by-Step **Definition:** The P operation (Dutch *proberen* = "to test") atomically decrements the semaphore and, if the result is negative, blocks the calling process. $$ P(S): \quad S \leftarrow S - 1 $$ $$ \text{If } S < 0, \text{ then block}(P_i) \text{ and place } P_i \text{ in } S.\text{wait\_list} $$ **Step-by-step logical derivation:** 1. The hardware disables interrupts (single-CPU) or uses an atomic instruction (multi-CPU) to guarantee atomicity. 2. The current value of $S$ is decremented by 1: $S_{new} = S_{old} - 1$. 3. **Decision branch:** - If $S_{new} \ge 0$, the resource is still available. The process proceeds into its critical section. - If $S_{new} < 0$, the resource count is exhausted. The process is moved to the semaphore's wait queue, and a context switch occurs. 4. When a V operation is later invoked by another process, the blocked process is unblocked and re-scheduled. ### 3.1.3 The V(signal) Operation — Step-by-Step **Definition:** The V operation (Dutch *verhogen* = "to increment") atomically increments the semaphore and, if there are waiting processes, wakes one of them. $$ V(S): \quad S \leftarrow S + 1 $$ $$ \text{If } S \le 0, \text{ then wakeup}(P_j) \text{ where } P_j \in S.\text{wait\_list} $$ **Step-by-step logical derivation:** 1. Atomicity is guaranteed by the same mechanism as in P. 2. The value of $S$ is incremented: $S_{new} = S_{old} + 1$. 3. **Decision branch:** - If $S_{new} > 0$, no process was waiting. The signal is "stored" in the semaphore counter. - If $S_{new} \le 0$, at least one process was waiting. One is removed from the queue and moved to the ready queue. 4. The awakened process will be scheduled on the next CPU dispatch. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Atomicity Guarantee:** The decrement-then-test sequence in P, and the increment-then-test sequence in V, must execute as a single, indivisible instruction. This is typically implemented in modern kernels using **compare-and-swap (CAS)** hardware instructions: > > ```text > CAS(address, expected, new_value): > atomically: > if (*address == expected): > *address = new_value > return TRUE > else: > return FALSE > ``` ## 3.2 The Producer–Consumer (Bounded-Buffer) Problem This is the **canonical KTU exam problem** to test a student's grasp of "Support for Sharing." We provide the exhaustive solution using three semaphores. **Problem Setup:** - A buffer of finite size $N$ holds items produced by one or more *producers* and consumed by one or more *consumers*. - The buffer is a *shared resource*; concurrent access must be **mutually exclusive**. - Producers must *wait* when the buffer is full; consumers must *wait* when it is empty. **Required Semaphores:** | Semaphore | Initial Value | Purpose | |-----------|---------------|---------| | `mutex` | 1 | Mutual exclusion on buffer access | | `empty` | N | Counts empty slots | | `full` | 0 | Counts filled slots | **The complete solution (annotated with OS-level reasoning):** ```text Producer Process: while (TRUE) { // 1. Produce an item (no shared access here) item = produce_item(); // 2. Acquire an empty slot (decrement empty; block if 0) P(empty); // 3. Enter critical section (mutex ensures only one process // manipulates the buffer at a time) P(mutex); // 4. Place item into buffer insert_item(item); // 5. Leave critical section V(mutex); // 6. Signal that a new item is available V(full); } ``` ```text Consumer Process: while (TRUE) { // 1. Wait for at least one filled slot P(full); // 2. Enter critical section P(mutex); // 3. Remove item from buffer remove_item(&item); // 4. Leave critical section V(mutex); // 5. Signal that a slot is now free V(empty); // 6. Consume the item consume_item(item); } ``` **Detailed correctness analysis:** 1. **Mutual exclusion on buffer:** The `P(mutex)` and `V(mutex)` bracket ensure that only one process (producer or consumer) executes the actual `insert_item` or `remove_item` at any instant. The value of `mutex` is binary. 2. **Blocking when buffer is full:** Each successful `insert_item` is preceded by `P(empty)`. When all $N$ slots are full, `empty = 0`, so any further producer will block on `P(empty)`. The next consumer will `V(empty)`, freeing one slot and awakening the producer. 3. **Blocking when buffer is empty:** Each successful `remove_item` is preceded by `P(full)`. When all $N$ slots are empty, `full = 0`, blocking the consumer. The next producer will `V(full)`, awakening it. 4. **Deadlock freedom:** A circular wait is impossible because producers wait only on `empty` and `mutex`, while consumers wait only on `full` and `mutex`. The order of P operations (`empty`/`full` *before* `mutex`) is critical to preventing deadlock. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Reversing the order of `P(empty)` and `P(mutex)` (or `P(full)` and `P(mutex)`) causes a **classic deadlock**. Always acquire the *counting* semaphore first, then the *binary* semaphore. ## 3.3 The Readers–Writers Problem (Logical Derivation) **Setup:** A shared database allows *readers* (concurrent read access is safe) and *writers* (exclusive access required). We want: - Multiple readers may be in the critical section simultaneously. - A writer must have **exclusive** access — no other readers or writers may be present. **Semaphores & variables used:** $$ \text{Let } rw\_mutex = 1, \quad mutex = 1, \quad read\_count = 0 $$ **Mutual exclusion condition:** $$ \text{Writer in CS} \implies (\text{read\_count} = 0 \land \text{no other writer present}) $$ **The first-reader–writers solution:** | Reader Process | Writer Process | |----------------|----------------| | `P(mutex)` | `P(rw_mutex)` | | `read_count = read_count + 1` | `write to database` | | `if (read_count == 1) P(rw_mutex)` | `V(rw_mutex)` | | `V(mutex)` | | | `read database` | | | `P(mutex)` | | | `read_count = read_count - 1` | | | `if (read_count == 0) V(rw_mutex)` | | | `V(mutex)` | | **Explanation of the first-reader trick:** - The **first reader** (the one that increments `read_count` from 0 to 1) acquires `rw_mutex`, blocking any new writer. - **Subsequent readers** do not acquire `rw_mutex`, allowing them to read in parallel. - The **last reader** to leave (the one that decrements `read_count` from 1 to 0) releases `rw_mutex`, allowing a waiting writer to proceed. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** The first-reader solution gives *readers* priority. Writers may starve if readers keep arriving. The *second-reader solution* (writers preferred) reverses the priority. KTU questions often ask you to *compare* both. ## 3.4 Mutual Exclusion with Hardware — Test-and-Set **Definition of the atomic TSL instruction:** $$ \text{TSL}(R, S): \quad R \leftarrow S, \quad S \leftarrow 1 \quad \text{(atomically)} $$ **Spinlock-based mutex implementation:** ```text acquire(lock): while (TSL(register, lock) == 1) ; // busy-wait (spin) until lock becomes free release(lock): lock = 0; ``` **Analytical proof of correctness (3 conditions):** 1. *Mutual exclusion:* `lock` is either 0 or 1. Only the process that observed `lock = 0` via TSL can enter the CS. Once inside, all others observe 1 and spin. 2. *Progress:* When the CS becomes empty, the lock is set to 0. The next TSL call from a waiting process succeeds, allowing entry. No process in the remainder section can prevent progress. 3. *Bounded waiting:* With an explicit FIFO wait queue (instead of pure spin), a process waits at most $n-1$ turns (where $n$ is the number of contending processes) before entering the CS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** TSL-based spinlocks are used inside the Linux kernel for **very short critical sections** (e.g., protecting a single flag). For longer sections, the kernel *switches* to a sleeping lock (`futex`) to avoid wasting CPU cycles. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of OS Sharing Support The diagram below illustrates the **layered architecture** by which an OS supports resource sharing, from hardware primitives up to user applications. ```mermaid flowchart TB hw1[Hardware Level
CPU Atomics, Interrupts, TSL, CAS] --> hw2[Kernel Primitives
Spinlocks, Futex, Disable Interrupts] hw2 --> hw3[Kernel Synchronization Objects
Semaphores, Mutex, Condition Variables] hw3 --> hw4[System Call Interface
POSIX sem wait sig, shmget, msgsnd, msgrcv] hw4 --> hw5[User Space Libraries
pthread mutex, Java synchronized, C sharp lock] hw5 --> hw6[Application Processes
Producer, Consumer, Reader, Writer, Bank Tx] style hw1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style hw2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00 style hw3 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style hw4 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style hw5 fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style hw6 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram for KTU exams:** Each layer *depends on* the layer below it. Hardware provides atomicity; the kernel builds blocking primitives; the syscall interface exposes them; user libraries wrap them; applications consume them. This is also the typical structure for an "Explain the support mechanisms for process synchronization" question. ## 4.2 State Transition Diagram of a Process Using a Semaphore ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Running Running --> CS: P mutex succeeds
S greater than 0 Running --> Blocked: P mutex blocks
S equals 0 Blocked --> Ready: V signal from another process
moved to ready queue Ready --> Running: Scheduler dispatches
CPU allocated CS --> Running: V mutex released
CS execution complete Running --> [*] note right of Blocked: Process is in
semaphore wait queue note right of CS: Only one process
at a time here ``` ## 4.3 Producer–Consumer Interaction Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant P as Producer Process participant S1 as Semaphore empty participant S2 as Semaphore mutex participant Buf as Shared Buffer participant S3 as Semaphore full participant C as Consumer Process P->>S1: P empty (acquire slot) S1-->>P: granted P->>S2: P mutex (enter CS) S2-->>P: granted P->>Buf: insert item P->>S2: V mutex (leave CS) P->>S3: V full (announce item) C->>S3: P full (wait for item) S3-->>C: granted C->>S2: P mutex (enter CS) S2-->>C: granted C->>Buf: remove item C->>S2: V mutex (leave CS) C->>S1: V empty (free slot) C->>C: consume item ``` ## 4.4 Comparison: Semaphore vs Monitor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Synchronization Tool] --> B[Semaphore] A --> C[Monitor] B --> B1[Integer counter S] B --> B2[Atomic P and V ops] B --> B3[Error prone
e.g. missed signal] B --> B4[OS or language level] C --> C1[Encapsulated data and procs] C --> C2[Compiler enforces mutex] C --> C3[Condition variables for sync] C --> C4[Language level only] style B fill:#ffe0b2 style C fill:#c8e6c9 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a counting semaphore. Give one example use case for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore | |--------|------------------|---------------------| | Value range | $\in \{0, 1\}$ | $\in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N\}$ | | Interpretation | Locked / unlocked | Count of available resources | | Use case | Mutex for a single shared resource (e.g., printer access) | Managing $N$ identical resources (e.g., 10 database connections in a pool) | A binary semaphore is essentially a counting semaphore initialized to 1, but unlike a counting semaphore it is **never decremented below 0**, since it is typically implemented with stronger invariants. **Example:** `binary_sem` for the printer spooler; `counting_sem` (initial = 5) for a pool of 5 tape drives. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is a critical section? State the three requirements that a correct solution to the critical section problem must satisfy.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **critical section** is a segment of code in which a process accesses a shared resource (shared variable, shared file, printer, etc.) and where the outcome of execution may be inconsistent if two or more processes execute their critical sections concurrently. The three mandatory requirements (often four, including bounded waiting) are: 1. **Mutual Exclusion:** No two processes can be in their critical sections at the same time with respect to the same shared resource. 2. **Progress:** If no process is currently in the critical section and there are some processes that wish to enter, only the processes not in their remainder section can participate in the decision of which one will enter next, and this decision cannot be postponed indefinitely. 3. **Bounded Waiting:** There exists a bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter their critical sections after a process has indicated its desire to enter and before that request is granted. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. Each sub-part is typically worth 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks): Semaphores and Bounded Buffer **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **Producer–Consumer problem** with a bounded buffer. Using semaphores, write the complete solution for one producer and one consumer, and explain how mutual exclusion and synchronization are achieved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **priority inversion**? Explain with a real-time example. How does the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solve it? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q-A(a) The Producer–Consumer problem (also called the **Bounded-Buffer problem**) is a classic synchronization problem: - A **producer** thread generates data items and places them into a shared buffer of fixed size $N$. - A **consumer** thread removes items and processes them. - The producer must **wait** when the buffer is full; the consumer must **wait** when the buffer is empty. - The buffer itself is a critical resource — simultaneous `insert` and `remove` would corrupt internal pointers. **Semaphores used:** | Semaphore | Initial Value | Role | |-----------|---------------|------| | `empty` | $N$ | counts empty slots | | `full` | 0 | counts filled slots | | `mutex` | 1 | ensures mutual exclusion on buffer access | **Solution code:** ```text /* Shared variables */ semaphore empty = N; semaphore full = 0; semaphore mutex = 1; item buffer[N]; Producer: while (TRUE) { item = produce(); // 1. create an item P(empty); // 2. wait for a free slot P(mutex); // 3. enter critical section buffer[in] = item; // 4. insert into buffer in = (in + 1) mod N; // 5. update index V(mutex); // 6. leave critical section V(full); // 7. signal a new item available } Consumer: while (TRUE) { P(full); // 1. wait for an item P(mutex); // 2. enter critical section item = buffer[out]; // 3. remove from buffer out = (out + 1) mod N; // 4. update index V(mutex); // 5. leave critical section V(empty); // 6. signal a free slot consume(item); // 7. process the item } ``` **How mutual exclusion is achieved:** - `mutex` is a binary semaphore; only the process that successfully executes `P(mutex)` (i.e., sees `mutex == 1`) enters the critical section. The other process is blocked until `V(mutex)` is executed. **How synchronization is achieved:** - `empty` and `full` coordinate the *temporal* behavior. A producer cannot proceed past `P(empty)` until a consumer has freed a slot. A consumer cannot proceed past `P(full)` until a producer has filled one. **Why no deadlock:** The order `P(empty) → P(mutex)` is consistent. The consumer uses `P(full) → P(mutex)`. There is no circular wait because `empty/full` and `mutex` are independent counters — a producer can never be holding `mutex` and waiting for `full`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write `P(mutex); P(empty);` instead of `P(empty); P(mutex);`. This **causes deadlock**: producer 1 acquires `mutex`, fills the buffer, then blocks on `P(empty)`; consumer blocks on `P(mutex)`. Always order counting semaphores **before** binary semaphores. (Lose 2 marks if you write the wrong order.) --- #### Model Solution for Q-A(b) **Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in which a *higher-priority* task is **indirectly preempted by a lower-priority* task, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of the two tasks. **Real-Time Example (the Mars Pathfinder Bug, 1997):** A low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus semaphore. A medium-priority communication task ran and *pre-empted* the low-priority task (without acquiring the semaphore). The high-priority bus management task then woke up, tried to acquire the semaphore, found it held, and was forced to block. The result: the high-priority task was **indefinitely delayed** by a medium-priority task that had nothing to do with the shared resource — a classic inversion. **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task, the OS **temporarily elevates the lower-priority task's priority** to that of the highest-priority task waiting for that resource. - The low-priority task thus "inherits" the high priority and is *not* pre-empted by medium-priority tasks. - It runs at the elevated priority until it releases the resource. - Then its priority is restored, and the high-priority task resumes. **Effect on the example:** The meteorological task would inherit the bus manager's high priority; communication tasks could not pre-empt it; the bus manager would be unblocked almost immediately. The inversion window is bounded by the duration of the critical section. **Other solutions to priority inversion:** - **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP):** Each resource is assigned a ceiling priority = max priority of any task that may lock it. A task acquiring the resource is raised to the ceiling priority, preventing intermediate-priority tasks from interfering. - **Disabling interrupts** during the critical section (only for very short CS in single-CPU systems). --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Monitors and Message Passing (ALTERNATIVE) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** **(a)** What is a **Monitor**? Explain its components. Compare monitors with semaphores. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **message-passing model** of inter-process communication. Compare it with the **shared-memory model**. State two situations where message passing is preferred over shared memory. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q-B(a) **Definition:** A **Monitor** is a high-level synchronization construct, introduced by Hoare (1974) and Brinch Hansen (1975), that encapsulates: - The **shared data** (variables) of a resource, and - The **procedures** (operations) that manipulate that data, into a single module or object. The compiler (or runtime) automatically enforces **mutual exclusion** so that only **one process** can be active inside the monitor at any time. **Components of a Monitor:** 1. **Shared Data (local variables):** Visible only inside the monitor. 2. **Procedures (entry points):** The only way other processes interact with the data. 3. **Initialization code:** Executed once when the monitor is created. 4. **Condition Variables:** Used for synchronization within the monitor. Two operations are allowed: - `wait(cv)`: Suspend the calling process and release the monitor lock; the process is placed on the queue for condition variable `cv`. - `signal(cv)`: Wake up one process waiting on `cv`. If no process is waiting, the signal is *lost* (unlike semaphores). **Monitor skeleton (conceptual):** ```text monitor ResourceManager { int available = N; // shared data condition not_empty; // condition variable void acquire() { if (available == 0) wait(not_empty); // block and release lock available = available - 1; } void release() { available = available + 1; signal(not_empty); // wake up one waiter } } ``` **Comparison: Monitors vs Semaphores** | Aspect | Semaphore | Monitor | |--------|-----------|---------| | Abstraction level | Low-level (integer + P/V) | High-level (data + procedures) | | Mutual exclusion | Programmer must enforce via P/V discipline | Enforced automatically by compiler | | Ease of use | Prone to errors (e.g., missed signal, wrong order) | Safer; structured programming style | | Blocking primitive | `P(S)` blocks and decrements | `wait(cv)` blocks without changing a counter | | Signal semantics | `V(S)` always increments (no loss) | `signal(cv)` may be lost if no waiter | | Language support | OS / library level | Language level (Java `synchronized`, C# `Monitor`) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall — Signal Loss:** In monitors, a `signal` is **not remembered**. If no process is currently waiting, the signal is *lost*. With semaphores, `V` *is* remembered (counter increments). If you write "signals are remembered in monitors" in the exam, you lose 1 mark. --- #### Model Solution for Q-B(b) **Message Passing Model:** In this model, processes communicate by **explicitly sending and receiving messages** through the kernel, using two primitives: - `send(destination, message)` — the sending process transmits a message to a destination (process or mailbox). - `receive(source, message)` — the receiving process waits for a message from a specified or any source. Synchronization is implicit: `receive` blocks if no message is available; `send` may block (synchronous) or not (asynchronous), depending on the implementation. **Design issues:** 1. **Direct vs Indirect communication:** - *Direct:* `send(P, msg)` and `receive(Q, msg)` — explicit naming of partners. - *Indirect:* Messages are sent to and received from *mailboxes* (also called ports). Decouples sender and receiver. 2. **Synchronous (blocking) vs Asynchronous (non-blocking):** - *Synchronous send:* Sender blocks until the receiver has accepted the message (rendezvous). - *Asynchronous send:* Sender continues immediately; the OS buffers the message. 3. **Buffering:** Mailboxes may have *zero capacity* (rendezvous), *bounded capacity* (finite queue), or *unbounded capacity* (unlimited queue). **Comparison: Message Passing vs Shared Memory** | Aspect | Shared Memory | Message Passing | |--------|---------------|-----------------| | Speed | Very fast (no kernel mediation) | Slower (system calls + context switches) | | Setup | Requires `shmget` / `mmap` and explicit sync | Requires only `send`/`receive` | | Synchronization | Must use semaphores / mutex explicitly | Implicit in `receive` blocking | | Use case | High-throughput same-host IPC | Distributed systems, heterogeneous processes | | Data protection | Responsibility of the programmer | Guaranteed by the OS | | Portability | Tied to a single address space | Naturally works across networked machines | **Two situations where message passing is preferred:** 1. **Distributed Systems / Networked Processes:** When the communicating processes run on different machines connected by a network, they do not share an address space. Message passing (sockets, RPC, MPI) is the only practical mechanism. 2. **Heterogeneous or Untrusted Processes:** When processes written in different languages or owned by different parties must communicate, message passing provides a clean, well-defined contract (message format) and prevents accidental memory corruption — a vital property in microservices and OS kernels themselves (e.g., microkernel IPC like L4, seL4, QNX). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Summary of Common Deductions:** > 1. **Wrong P/V order** in Producer–Consumer → −2 marks (deadlock). > 2. **Confusing `wait`/`signal` of monitor with semaphore** (signals are NOT remembered) → −1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting `mutex` in Producer–Consumer** (only `empty`/`full`) → −2 marks. > 4. **Stating "semaphores are higher level than monitors"** → −1 mark (it is the opposite). > 5. **Writing "monitors are OS-level primitives"** → −1 mark (monitors are language-level). > 6. **Omitting the bounded-waiting condition** when listing the 3 CS requirements → −1 mark (often 4 conditions are expected). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Support for Sharing** - **Definition:** OS mechanisms (mutex, semaphores, monitors, message passing) that allow multiple processes to safely share resources. - **Three Categories of Sharing Support:** Mutual Exclusion primitives → Synchronization primitives (semaphores) → Inter-Process Communication (shared memory / message passing). - **Critical Section (CS):** Code segment accessing a shared resource; only one process may execute it at a time. - **Four Requirements of a correct CS solution:** Mutual exclusion, Progress, Bounded waiting, No assumption about CPU speed. - **Semaphores (Dijkstra):** Atomic integer + atomic `P`/`V`. Binary (0/1) vs Counting (0 to N). - **Producer–Consumer solution uses THREE semaphores:** `empty = N`, `full = 0`, `mutex = 1`. **Order matters** — `P(empty) → P(mutex)`, never the reverse. - **Readers–Writers Problem:** First-reader solution gives readers priority; writers may starve. Second-reader gives writers priority; readers may starve. - **Monitor (Hoare):** Encapsulates shared data + procedures. Mutual exclusion is compiler-enforced. Condition variables support `wait` and `signal`. **Signals are NOT remembered.** - **Hardware atomicity:** Provided by interrupt disabling (single-CPU), atomic instructions like **TSL** and **CAS** (multi-CPU). - **Peterson's Algorithm:** Software-only 2-process CS solution; elegant but rarely used in production. - **Priority Inversion:** High-priority task blocked by a low-priority task holding a lock, with a medium-priority task in between. Solution = **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**. Famous example = **Mars Pathfinder (1997)**. - **Message Passing:** Two primitives `send` and `receive`; can be direct/indirect, sync/async, with zero/bounded/unbounded buffering. - **Shared Memory:** Fastest IPC, but requires explicit synchronization; cannot cross machines. - **P($\mu$) / P($\Sigma$):** In KTU math notation, remember that *both* `P` and `V` are indivisible (atomic) operations on the semaphore counter. - **Key formula:** After $k$ `P` operations and $j$ `V` operations starting from initial value $S_0$, the semaphore value is $S = S_0 - k + j$, **provided no process is blocked**. The actual physical counter can go negative; the magnitude indicates the queue length. - **Re-entrant (Pure) Code:** Implicit sharing; safe for concurrent execution because it doesn't modify itself and uses only local data. - **Kernel-level examples:** Linux `futex`, Windows `Dispatcher Object`, POSIX `pthread_mutex_t`, Java `synchronized` block, C# `lock` statement.

topics.title: Virtual Memory - Paging: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory - Paging: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Virtual Memory** is a memory management technique that creates an illusion for processes (user programs) of having a large, contiguous working memory by utilizing both the **physical RAM (Random Access Memory)** and a portion of the **secondary storage (Hard Disk / SSD)** as a single unified logical address space.

**Paging** is the most widely implemented memory management scheme that brings this virtual memory concept to life. It is a **non-contiguous memory allocation** technique that divides:

- The **logical address space (Virtual Address Space)** of a process into fixed-size blocks called **Pages**.
- The **physical address space (Main Memory / RAM)** into fixed-size blocks called **Frames (or Page Frames)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Core Distinction**
> The fundamental difference between *Segmentation* and *Paging* is that **Paging divides memory uniformly into fixed-size blocks**, whereas Segmentation is a *variable-size* logical division based on the program’s semantic structure (functions, arrays, stacks, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Paging is invisible to the programmer.** The programmer writes code as if the process owns one huge contiguous block of memory starting at address 0. The OS and the **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** silently perform all translations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Book System"

Imagine a massive public library with a single reading room that can only seat **100 readers** at a time, but the library owns **1,000,000 books** in total.

- The **reading room tables** = Physical Memory (RAM) — small and fast.
- The **book warehouse (basement)** = Secondary Storage (Hard Disk) — huge and slow.
- Each **book** = A *Page* of the process (e.g., 4 KB of instructions/data).
- The **tables in the reading room** = *Frames* in physical memory.
- The **librarian's index card file** = The *Page Table* (a translation directory).

When you (a process) need a specific book (page) to study, you submit an index card (logical address). The librarian checks the index, and:

1. If the book is **already on a table** in the reading room → instant access. *(Page Hit)*
2. If the book is in the **warehouse**, the librarian **swaps** an old book out and brings the new one in. *(Page Fault → Swap-In)*

You, the reader, never know — and never care — whether the book was upstairs or in the basement. The illusion is perfect. **This is Virtual Memory with Paging.**

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Checklist

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Virtual Address / Logical Address | VA | Address generated by the CPU during program execution |
| Physical Address | PA | Actual address in the RAM where data resides |
| Page | — | Fixed-size block of the **logical address space** |
| Frame / Page Frame | — | Fixed-size block of the **physical address space** |
| Page Table | PT | Per-process data structure that maps Page Number → Frame Number |
| MMU | — | **Memory Management Unit**, specialized hardware that performs address translation at runtime |
| Page Size | $P$ | Typically **4 KB**, though 2 MB / 1 GB *Huge Pages* also exist |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Page Size Assumption:** Unless explicitly stated in the question, the default **page size** for KTU problems is **4 KB = $2^{12}$ bytes = 4096 bytes**. Page size **always equals** frame size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Paging — Step by Step

Paging is governed by a strict **two-level address translation** process executed by the MMU on every single memory reference. Let us break it down into the "Why" and "How" of each phase.

### Phase 1 — Logical Address Generation (CPU Side)

- The program counter issues a **Virtual Address (VA)** in the form: `(Page Number | Page Offset)`.
- The **Page Number (p)** acts as an *index* into the page table.
- The **Page Offset (d)** specifies the byte's position *within* the page.

### Phase 2 — Address Translation (MMU Side)

- The MMU receives `(p, d)`.
- It fetches the corresponding **Page Table Entry (PTE)** for page `p`.
- The PTE contains the **Frame Number (f)** in physical RAM.
- The MMU constructs the **Physical Address (PA)** as `(f \vert d)` — i.e., it *replaces* the page number with the frame number while keeping the offset intact.

### Phase 3 — Physical Memory Access (RAM Side)

- The constructed PA is sent over the memory bus to DRAM.
- The data byte is returned to the CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Equal-Size Division:** Because pages and frames are *exactly the same size*, the offset `d` remains unchanged during translation. The page boundary aligns perfectly with the frame boundary, which is why no byte-shuffling of the offset is needed. This makes hardware translation extremely fast.

---

## 2.2 Structure of the Page Table Entry (PTE)

A typical PTE contains the following fields:

| Field | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| **Frame Number (f)** | The core mapping — where the page lives in RAM |
| **Valid/Invalid Bit (V/I)** | `1` = page is in RAM; `0` = page is on disk (causes Page Fault) |
| **Protection Bits (R/W/X)** | Read / Write / Execute permissions |
| **Dirty Bit (Modified)** | `1` = page has been altered in RAM; needs write-back to disk |
| **Reference Bit (Access)** | Set by hardware on every access — used by page replacement algorithms |
| **Caching Disabled Bit** | For memory-mapped I/O pages |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions frequently test the PTE bit positions and the concept of the **Valid/Invalid bit** in determining *Page Faults* vs *Page Hits*.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table cold.** It is the single most important numerical tool for paging problems in KTU exams.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Logical Address (LA)** | $LA = p \times P + d$ | $p$ = page number, $d$ = offset |
| 2 | **Page Number (p)** | $p = \lfloor LA / P \rfloor$ | Integer division |
| 3 | **Page Offset (d)** | $d = LA \mod P$ | Remainder |
| 4 | **Physical Address (PA)** | $PA = f \times P + d$ | $f$ = frame number |
| 5 | **Number of Pages in Process** | $N_p = \lceil \text{Process Size} / P \rceil$ | Round up |
| 6 | **Number of Frames in RAM** | $N_f = \text{RAM Size} / P$ | Usually exact integer |
| 7 | **Page Table Size (in bytes)** | $PTS = N_p \times \text{PTE\_size}$ | Where PTE\_size is usually 4 bytes |
| 8 | **Page Table Size (in entries)** | Equals $N_p$ | One entry per virtual page |
| 9 | **Internal Fragmentation** | $IF = P - (\text{Last Page Occupied Bytes})$ | $\leq P - 1$ bytes |
| 10 | **Page Size from Address Bits** | $P = 2^d$ | If offset uses `d` bits |
| 11 | **Page Number Bits** | $\text{Bits for } p = \text{Total bits} - d$ | For VA of $n$ bits |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Rule for KTU Sheets:** When writing absolute values, **never** use the keyboard `|` inside a markdown table. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead. This avoids accidentally creating broken tables in your answer sheets.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Paging

| Domain | Application of Paging Concept |
|--------|--------------------------------|
| **Database Systems (DBMS)** | Buffer pool managers (e.g., PostgreSQL) page data in/out of RAM in 8 KB blocks |
| **Virtualization (VMware, KVM)** | Guest OS uses virtual pages; Hypervisor provides nested page tables (EPT/NPT) |
| **Mobile OS (Android, iOS)** | Apps run in paged sandboxes; inactive pages are compressed in zRAM or swapped |
| **Compilers** | Use paging-style segmentation of memory regions (.text, .data, .heap, .stack) |
| **GPU Computing** | Unified Memory Architecture uses paging for CPU-GPU data transfer |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Address Translation — Exhaustive Derivation

Let us derive the complete address translation pipeline from a raw logical address. This is the **core KTU numerical pattern**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Setup (Sample KTU Problem Pattern):**
> Given: Virtual Address Space = **16 GB**, Physical RAM = **4 GB**, Page Size = **4 KB**. A process generates the logical address `0x0000ABCD` (hexadecimal). Find the Page Number, Page Offset, and the resulting Physical Address (assuming the page table maps the page to Frame #5).

### Step 1 — Compute the Number of Bits in Each Address

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Logical Address Bits} &= \log_2(16 \text{ GB}) \\
&= \log_2(16 \times 2^{30}) \\
&= \log_2(2^{34}) = 34 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address Bits} &= \log_2(4 \text{ GB}) \\
&= \log_2(4 \times 2^{30}) \\
&= \log_2(2^{32}) = 32 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $\log_2(\text{memory size})$ gives the number of unique addresses, which is the bit width.

### Step 2 — Compute the Offset Bits (from Page Size)

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \log_2(P) \\
&= \log_2(4 \text{ KB}) \\
&= \log_2(2^{12}) = 12 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A page size of $2^{12}$ bytes means we need 12 bits to address every byte within a page.

### Step 3 — Compute the Page Number Bits

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{bits}} &= \text{Logical Address Bits} - d \\
&= 34 - 12 = 22 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The remaining high-order bits of the logical address are used to identify the page.

### Step 4 — Decompose the Hexadecimal Address `0x0000ABCD`

First, convert to binary by writing each hex digit as 4 bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x0000ABCD &= 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 1010\ 1011\ 1100\ 1101
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The address `0x0000ABCD` is a 16-hex-digit (64-bit) representation, but only 34 bits are actually meaningful. We pad with leading zeros for clarity.

### Step 5 — Extract Page Number and Page Offset

We split the binary string into **lower 12 bits (offset)** and **upper 22 bits (page number)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary} &: \underbrace{0000000000000000000\,0010}_{\text{Page Number (22 bits)}}\,\underbrace{101\,011\,110\,011\,01}_{\text{Page Offset (12 bits)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The rightmost 12 bits represent the offset. The bits to the left of that represent the page number.

### Step 6 — Convert Page Number to Decimal

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= 0b0000000000000000000010 \\
  &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The bit at position 1 (counting from 0) is set, so the value is $2^1 = 2$.

### Step 7 — Convert Page Offset to Decimal

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 0b101011110011 \\
  &= (1 \cdot 2^{11}) + (0 \cdot 2^{10}) + (1 \cdot 2^9) + (0 \cdot 2^8) + (1 \cdot 2^7) + (1 \cdot 2^6) + (1 \cdot 2^5) + (1 \cdot 2^4) + (0 \cdot 2^3) + (0 \cdot 2^2) + (1 \cdot 2^1) + (1 \cdot 2^0) \\
  &= 2048 + 0 + 512 + 0 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 \\
  &= 2803
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $d$ tells us *which exact byte* inside page #2 is being requested.

### Step 8 — Construct the Physical Address (using Frame #5)

The MMU looks up Page #2 in the Page Table and finds **Frame #5**. The physical address is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= f \times P + d \\
   &= 5 \times 4096 + 2803 \\
   &= 20480 + 2803 \\
   &= 23283
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Frame #5 starts at byte $5 \times 4096 = 20480$. The offset is the same as in the logical address, so we add them together.

### Step 9 — Verification (Backward Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \lfloor LA / P \rfloor = \lfloor 43981 / 4096 \rfloor = 10 \text{ (decimal of 0xABCD is 43981)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A small inconsistency is expected if the hex string is interpreted as a smaller 16-bit value. In KTU problems, always follow the question's given bit width (32-bit or 64-bit) carefully.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Paging vs. Segmentation

| Parameter | Paging | Segmentation |
|-----------|--------|--------------|
| Division Basis | Fixed size (e.g., 4 KB) | Variable size (logical units) |
| Visible to Programmer? | **No** (transparent) | **Yes** (named segments) |
| Fragmentation | Internal only (last page) | External only |
| Address Format | `(page_num, offset)` | `(segment_num, offset)` |
| Number of Tables | 1 page table per process | 1 segment table per process |
| External Fragmentation? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Internal Fragmentation? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Hardware Support | MMU with page table | MMU with segment table |

---

## 3.3 Internal Fragmentation Worst-Case Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU question: *"What is the maximum internal fragmentation in a paging system?"*

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF_{\max} &= P - 1 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The last page of a process is, in the worst case, occupied by only **1 byte**. The remaining $P - 1$ bytes of that page are wasted. For $P = 4$ KB, the maximum waste per process is **4095 bytes ≈ 4 KB**.

The **average internal fragmentation** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{P}{2} \text{ bytes per process}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why large page sizes (e.g., 2 MB Huge Pages in Linux) are used in databases — to reduce the *relative* fragmentation overhead. A 2-byte file wastes 4 KB with 4 KB pages (50% waste) but only 0.1% with 2 MB pages.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Paging Address Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Generates Logical Address VA] --> B[Split VA into Page Number p and Offset d]
    B --> C[MMU Receives p as Index]
    C --> D[Look up Page Table PT]
    D --> E{Valid or Invalid Bit}
    E -->|V is 1 - Page in RAM| F[Read Frame Number f from PTE]
    E -->|V is 0 - Page Fault| G[Trap to OS - Page Fault Handler]
    G --> H[OS Finds Free Frame in RAM]
    H --> I[Swap Page In from Disk to Frame]
    I --> J[Update PTE with Frame Number and Set V to 1]
    J --> F
    F --> K[MMU Constructs Physical Address PA = f concat d]
    K --> L[Send PA to RAM via Memory Bus]
    L --> M[RAM Returns Data to CPU]
    M --> N[Execution Continues]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style N fill:#d4edda
    style E fill:#fff3cd
    style G fill:#f8d7da
    style F fill:#d1ecf1
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Virtual to Physical Memory Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VAS["Virtual Address Space of Process P"]
        P0[Virtual Page 0]

        P2[Virtual Page 2]

        PT2[Entry: Page 2 to Frame 1]
        PT3[Entry: Page 3 to Frame 4]
        PT4[Entry: Page 4 to Frame 7]
    end

    subgraph RAM["Physical Memory - RAM - 8 Frames Total"]
        F0[Frame 0 - OS Kernel]
        F1[Frame 1 - Belongs to Page 2]
        F2[Frame 2 - Belongs to Page 0]
        F3[Frame 3 - Free]
        F4[Frame 4 - Belongs to Page 3]
        F5[Frame 5 - Belongs to Page 1]
        F6[Frame 6 - Free]
        F7[Frame 7 - Belongs to Page 4]
    end

    subgraph DISK["Secondary Storage - Hard Disk"]
        D0[Page File - Pages not in RAM]
    end

    P0 -.Maps via.-> PT0
    P1 -.Maps via.-> PT1
    P2 -.Maps via.-> PT2
    P3 -.Maps via.-> PT3
    P4 -.Maps via.-> PT4

    PT0 --> F2
    PT1 --> F5
    PT2 --> F1
    PT3 --> F4
    PT4 --> F7

    style P0 fill:#cfe2ff
    style P1 fill:#cfe2ff
    style P2 fill:#cfe2ff
    style P3 fill:#cfe2ff
    style P4 fill:#cfe2ff
    style F1 fill:#d1ecf1
    style F2 fill:#d1ecf1
    style F4 fill:#d1ecf1
    style F5 fill:#d1ecf1
    style F7 fill:#d1ecf1
    style F0 fill:#f8d7da
    style F3 fill:#d4edda
    style F6 fill:#d4edda
    style D0 fill:#fff3cd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the virtual pages are **non-contiguous** in physical RAM — Page 0 is in Frame 2, Page 1 in Frame 5, etc. This is the essence of **non-contiguous allocation** that paging enables. Frames 0, 3, and 6 are free or occupied by the OS.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Page Fault Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant MMU as MMU Hardware
    participant PT as Page Table
    participant OS as OS Kernel
    participant DISK as Secondary Storage
    participant RAM as Physical RAM

    CPU->>MMU: Send Virtual Address VA
    MMU->>PT: Look up PTE for Page Number p
    PT-->>MMU: Return PTE (V bit = 0)
    MMU->>CPU: Raise Page Fault Trap
    CPU->>OS: Trap to Page Fault Handler
    OS->>RAM: Check for a free frame
    alt Free Frame Available
        OS->>DISK: Locate page on disk
        DISK-->>OS: Return page contents
        OS->>RAM: Copy page into free frame
    else No Free Frame - Eviction Needed
        OS->>RAM: Select victim frame (replacement algo)
        OS->>DISK: Write victim page to disk if dirty
        OS->>RAM: Free the frame
        OS->>DISK: Load desired page into freed frame
    end
    OS->>PT: Update PTE (Frame Number + V bit = 1)
    OS->>CPU: Restart the faulting instruction
    CPU->>MMU: Re-translate the same VA
    MMU->>RAM: Access memory successfully
    RAM-->>CPU: Return data
```
