**(b)** Describe how a **Mutex Lock** can be used to solve this race condition. Provide the pseudocode and explain the role of the `acquire()` and `release()` functions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Race Condition on Shared Array (7 Marks):**

A **race condition** occurs when two or more processes access a shared variable (array element) concurrently, and at least one of them performs a write operation. The result depends on the non-deterministic order of instruction execution.

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
   Time    Process P1              Process P2              balance[5]
   ----     ------------             ------------             ---------
    t0     LOAD R1, balance[5]       ---                      1000
    t1     ---                       LOAD R2, balance[5]      1000
    t2     ADD R1, R1, #500          ---                      1000
    t3     ---                       ADD R2, R2, #500         1000
    t4     STORE R1, balance[5]      ---                      1500
    t5     ---                       STORE R2, balance[5]      1500  ❌
```

**Analysis:**
- Initial value: `balance[5] = 1000`
- Both processes P1 and P2 intend to credit 500 to the same account.
- Both read the same initial value (1000) before either writes back.
- **Expected Final Value**: 1000 + 500 + 500 = **2000**
- **Actual Final Value**: **1500** (One credit is lost)
- The interleaving at t1 (where P2 reads before P1 stores) causes the lost update.

---

**(b) Solution Using Mutex Lock (7 Marks):**

A **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion) Lock** is a synchronization primitive that ensures only one process can access the shared resource (array element) at a time.

**Pseudocode:**

```
// Shared Variables
int balance[10];
mutex mtx;             // Mutex lock variable

// Process Code for P1 and P2
acquire(mtx);                      // Entry Section
    balance[5] = balance[5] + 500; // Critical Section
release(mtx);                      // Exit Section
    // Remainder Section (other work)
```

**Function Definitions:**

- **`acquire(mtx)`**: An **atomic** operation that checks the value of `mtx`. If `mtx = 1` (unlocked), it sets `mtx = 0` (locked) and the process proceeds. If `mtx = 0` (locked), the process is **blocked** and added to a wait queue.

- **`release(mtx)`**: An **atomic** operation that sets `mtx = 1` (unlocked) and wakes up one of the waiting processes from the queue (if any).

**Corrected Execution:**

| Step | Operation | Process | mtx | balance[5] |
|:----:|:----------|:-------:|:---:|:----------:|
| 1 | acquire(mtx) | P1 | **0** | 1000 |
| 2 | acquire(mtx) | P2 | 0 | 1000 |
| 3 | P2 blocked | — | 0 | 1000 |
| 4 | balance[5] += 500 | P1 | 0 | **1500** |
| 5 | release(mtx) | P1 | **1** | 1500 |
| 6 | P2 unblocked → acquire(mtx) | P2 | **0** | 1500 |
| 7 | balance[5] += 500 | P2 | 0 | **2000** ✅ |
| 8 | release(mtx) | P2 | **1** | 2000 |

**Final Result**: `balance[5] = 2000` ✅ (Correct!)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)** instruction as a hardware solution to the critical section problem for shared array access. Provide the algorithm and explain how it guarantees atomicity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Mutex Locks**, **Semaphores**, and **Hardware Atomic Instructions (CAS/TSL)** as synchronization solutions for shared array access. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instruction (7 Marks):**

The **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)** instruction is a hardware-supported atomic primitive used to implement synchronization without locks. It atomically compares the value of a memory location (array element) with an expected value and, only if they match, swaps it with a new value.

**CAS Instruction Definition:**

$$
\text{CAS}(\&A[i], \text{expected}, \text{new}) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{new} \rightarrow A[i] & \text{if } A[i] == \text{expected} \quad (\text{returns TRUE}) \\
\text{no change} & \text{if } A[i] \neq \text{expected} \quad (\text{returns FALSE})
\end{cases}
$$

**Algorithm for Atomic Array Increment Using CAS:**

```
// Atomic increment of A[i] using CAS
do {
    old_value = A[i];                    // Read current value
    new_value = old_value + 1;           // Compute new value
} while (CAS(&A[i], old_value, new_value) == FALSE);
// Retry if another process modified A[i] in between
```

**How CAS Guarantees Atomicity:**

1. CAS is implemented as a **single, indivisible instruction** at the hardware level (e.g., `CMPXCHG` on x86, `LDXR/STXR` on ARM).
2. The CPU asserts the **LOCK signal** on the memory bus during execution, preventing any other processor from accessing the same memory address.
3. The **compare** and **swap** operations happen together as a single atomic step, so no other process can read the old value and write to it concurrently.
4. If another process has modified `A[i]`, CAS returns FALSE, and the current process **retries** with the updated value.

**CAS ensures Mutual Exclusion** without requiring a kernel-level lock, making it a foundation for **lock-free programming**.

---

**(b) Comparison of Synchronization Techniques (7 Marks):**

| Technique | Mechanism | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|:----------|:----------|:----------|:-------------|
| **Mutex Lock** | Software lock with `acquire()`/`release()` | Simple to implement and understand; provides mutual exclusion guaranteed by OS kernel. | Can lead to **deadlock** if not used carefully; incurs **context switch overhead** during contention. |
| **Semaphore** | Integer-based counter with `wait()`/`signal()` | Can handle multiple instances of a resource; useful for producer-consumer and reader-writer problems. | More complex than Mutex; prone to **programming errors** (e.g., missed `signal()` leads to deadlock). |
| **Hardware Atomic (CAS/TSL)** | Single atomic CPU instruction | **Lock-free**, no context switch; high performance under low contention. | **Busy-waiting** wastes CPU cycles; suffers from **ABA problem** in some data structures. |

**Detailed Comparison:**

- **Mutex Lock**: A binary semaphore with ownership semantics. Only the process that locked the mutex can unlock it. Best for protecting shared data structures like arrays.

- **Semaphore**: A generalized counter-based mechanism. Supports both **binary** (mutual exclusion) and **counting** (resource management) variants. Ideal when multiple identical resources are accessed.

- **Hardware Atomic Instructions**: Use CPU-level atomicity (bus locking, cache line locking). Best for **high-performance, low-contention** scenarios like counter increments.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1**: Students often forget to identify that `array[i]++` is **3 separate instructions**, not 1. Always mention LOAD, ADD, STORE explicitly to earn full marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2**: Students confuse **Mutex** with **Semaphore**. Remember: Mutex has **ownership** (only the locker can unlock), while Semaphore does not.
> - **Common Mistake #3**: When drawing interleaving traces, students forget to label the **time axis** (t₀, t₁, ...). Always label steps clearly for full credit.
> - **Common Mistake #4**: For CAS-based solutions, students often forget to mention the **retry loop** (`while (!CAS(...))`). The retry mechanism is crucial to the algorithm's correctness.
> - **Common Mistake #5**: Students miss the fact that the three critical section requirements (Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting) apply to array access scenarios and must be explicitly stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Accessing an Array" Example**

### **Core Concepts**
- [x] A shared array accessed by multiple processes/threads is the **canonical example** of a critical section.
- [x] The operation `array[i]++` is **NOT atomic** — it consists of **3 machine instructions**: LOAD, ADD, STORE.
- [x] A **race condition** occurs when concurrent access leads to unpredictable results.

### **Key Terminology**
- [x] **Critical Section**: Code segment that accesses shared variables (e.g., `array[i]++`).
- [x] **Race Condition**: Outcome depends on the order of execution of concurrent processes.
- [x] **Lost Update**: A specific race condition where one process's update is overwritten by another.
- [x] **Atomic Operation**: An indivisible operation that cannot be interrupted.

### **The Three Requirements**
- [x] **Mutual Exclusion**: Only one process in the critical section at a time.
- [x] **Progress**: No indefinite postponement if critical section is free.
- [x] **Bounded Waiting**: Finite bound on the number of times other processes can enter before a waiting process.

### **Solutions**
- [x] **Software**: Mutex Locks, Semaphores (Binary & Counting), Peterson's Solution.
- [x] **Hardware**: Test-and-Set (TSL), Compare-and-Swap (CAS), Swap instruction.

### **Numerical Quick Facts**
- [x] Number of atomic operations in `array[i]++` = **3**.
- [x] Number of interleavings for 2 processes doing 3 ops each = **20**.
- [x] Probability of lost update for 2 processes = **90%** (18 out of 20 interleavings).
- [x] Number of conditions for valid critical section solution = **3**.

### **Exam Strategy**
- [x] Always **draw the interleaving table** with time steps.
- [x] Always **state the three machine instructions** (LOAD, ADD, STORE).
- [x] Always **mention Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting** when discussing solutions.
- [x] For CAS questions, always show the **retry loop**.
- [x] Use a **real-world analogy** (e.g., bank account, shared whiteboard) for easy marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** The "Accessing an Array" example is **guaranteed** to appear in some form in the KTU Module 3 examination. Master the interleaving traces, the three conditions, and at least two solution approaches (one software, one hardware) to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TLB Hits and Misses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TLB Hits and Misses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB):** A specialized, fully-associative hardware cache (a high-speed associative memory) maintained by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) that stores recently used **Page Table Entries (PTEs)** — specifically, mappings between **virtual page numbers (VPN)** and **physical frame numbers (PFN)** — to accelerate the virtual-to-physical address translation process.

In the **paging mechanism**, every memory reference issued by the CPU requires a two-step access:

1. Look up the **Page Table** in main memory to find the corresponding physical frame.
2. Access the actual physical memory location to fetch the instruction/data.

A naive paging system therefore requires **two memory accesses per logical reference**, which is prohibitively slow. The TLB solves this bottleneck.

> [!NOTE]
> **TLB Hit:** A TLB hit occurs when the virtual page number (VPN) of the requested logical address is found in the TLB, allowing the MMU to retrieve the corresponding physical frame number in a single, ultra-fast associative lookup.
>
> **TLB Miss:** A TLB miss occurs when the requested VPN is NOT present in the TLB. The system must then access the in-memory page table (or multi-level page table), retrieve the missing PTE, and load it into the TLB — possibly evicting an older entry — before the actual physical memory access can proceed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian in a massive library (the main memory), and a reader asks for a book.

- **Without a TLB:** Every time the reader names a book, you must first walk to the **central catalog room** (the page table in main memory) to find the shelf number, then walk to the shelf to fetch the book. **Two long walks per request.**

- **With a TLB:** You keep a small **sticky-note pad on your desk** (the TLB) that remembers the last few catalog lookups (e.g., "Book ID 47 → Shelf C-12"). If the reader asks for one of those recently looked-up books, you glance at the sticky note — a **TLB hit** — and walk straight to the shelf. Only one walk needed!

- **TLB Miss:** If the book isn't on your sticky notes, you have no choice but to walk all the way to the catalog room (**page table access**), find the shelf, bring the book, and also write the new lookup on your sticky note for next time (**TLB refill/eviction**).

The **TLB hit ratio** is therefore the probability that a sticky-note lookup succeeds, and the **TLB miss penalty** is the cost of that slow catalog-room trip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics used in KTU problems:**
> - **TLB lookup time** = $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) or $T_{TLB}$ (typically 1–20 ns)
> - **Main memory access time** = $M$ or $T_m$ (typically 100–200 ns)
> - **TLB hit ratio** = $h$ (fraction, e.g., 0.8 means 80%)
> - **TLB miss ratio** = $(1 - h)$
> - **Effective Access Time (EAT)** = Average time per memory reference, considering TLB behavior

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of TLB Lookup

When the CPU generates a logical address, the following sequence is executed by the hardware (MMU + TLB):

1. **Step 1 — Extract VPN:** The CPU's logical address is split into a **Virtual Page Number (VPN)** and a **page offset**. The offset is passed through unchanged.

2. **Step 2 — TLB Associative Search:** All TLB entries are searched **in parallel** (fully associative) to see if any entry's tag matches the current VPN.

3. **Step 3 — Outcome Branch:**
   - **Hit Path:** TLB returns the corresponding **Physical Frame Number (PFN)**. The MMU concatenates the PFN with the offset to form the physical address, and the CPU accesses physical memory **once**.
   - **Miss Path:** Hardware triggers a **TLB miss handler** (often microcode or OS trap).
     - The page table in main memory is consulted (1 memory access).
     - The fetched PTE is loaded into the TLB (replacing a victim entry using LRU, FIFO, or random policy).
     - The physical address is then constructed, and physical memory is accessed (1 more memory access).
     - **Total: 2 memory accesses on a TLB miss.**

4. **Step 4 — Instruction Execution:** The CPU uses the data fetched from physical memory.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **canonical KTU 2024 Scheme EAT formulas** for TLB-based systems. Memorize these — they appear in **every KTU University Exam**.

| **Scenario** | **Formula for Effective Access Time (EAT)** | **Assumptions** |
|---|---|---|
| TLB Hit time ≈ 0 (parallel/memory-included) | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot M + (1-h) \cdot 2M$ | TLB lookup overlapped with memory access |
| Simplified version of above | $\text{EAT} = (2 - h) \cdot M$ | Same as above, expanded |
| TLB lookup time is separate (sequential) | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + M) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2M)$ | TLB accessed before main memory |
| Simplified version of above | $\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M$ | Same as above, expanded |
| Multi-level page table (2 levels) on TLB miss | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot M + (1-h) \cdot (3M)$ | Two page table accesses + one memory access on miss |
| Memory access on TLB miss uses Cache | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot M + (1-h) \cdot (M + M)$ | Cache hit assumed after page table access |

**Where:**
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio (decimal fraction, $0 \le h \le 1$)
- $M$ = Main memory access time (in **ns** or **µs**)
- $T_{TLB}$ = TLB lookup time (in **ns**)
- $(1-h)$ = TLB miss ratio

### 2.3 Why the TLB is "Lookaside"

The term **"lookaside"** is a KTU board-favourite keyword. It signifies that the TLB is searched **in parallel with** (or before, but independently of) the main memory access. The CPU does not stall waiting for the page table — it consults the TLB *first* and *aside from* the regular memory pipeline. This architectural choice is what enables the TLB to amortize its access time across all memory references.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Modern CPUs (Intel x86, ARM Cortex-A series):** Include split L1 TLBs for instructions (iTLB) and data (dTLB), with a unified L2 TLB. Misses in L1 are handled by L2 hardware walk — no OS trap.
- **GPUs and AI Accelerators:** Use deep TLB hierarchies to manage large virtual address spaces for deep learning workloads.
- **Databases and OS Kernels:** Exploit TLB locality by keeping working sets small (e.g., `mmap`-based memory pools).
- **Embedded Systems:** Use software-managed TLBs (e.g., MIPS, older ARM) where the OS controls TLB refill — critical in **RTOS design** for deterministic performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EAT Formula (General Case)

We begin by expressing EAT as a **weighted average** of the access time under each possible outcome.

**Let:**
- $h$ = probability of a TLB hit
- $(1 - h)$ = probability of a TLB miss
- $T_{TLB}$ = TLB lookup time
- $M$ = main memory access time

**Step 1 — Time on a TLB Hit:**

When a hit occurs, the CPU performs:
- One TLB lookup: $T_{TLB}$
- One main memory access: $M$

$$
T_{\text{hit}} = T_{TLB} + M
$$

**Step 2 — Time on a TLB Miss:**

When a miss occurs, the CPU performs:
- One TLB lookup (which fails): $T_{TLB}$
- One page table access in main memory: $M$
- One actual data access in main memory: $M$

$$
T_{\text{miss}} = T_{TLB} + M + M = T_{TLB} + 2M
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Average (EAT):**

$$
\text{EAT} = h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{miss}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{EAT} = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + M) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2M)
$$

Expanding:

$$
\text{EAT} = h \cdot T_{TLB} + h \cdot M + (1 - h) \cdot T_{TLB} + (1 - h) \cdot 2M
$$

Group the $T_{TLB}$ terms:

$$
\text{EAT} = [h + (1 - h)] \cdot T_{TLB} + h \cdot M + 2(1 - h) \cdot M
$$

Since $h + (1 - h) = 1$:

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + h \cdot M + 2(1 - h) \cdot M
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + [h + 2(1 - h)] \cdot M
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + [h + 2 - 2h] \cdot M
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M
$$

**Final canonical form (KTU exam-ready):**

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M}
$$

**Special Case:** If the TLB lookup is overlapped with the memory access (i.e., $T_{TLB} \to 0$ or is included in $M$):

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = (2 - h) \cdot M}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Consider a paging system with a TLB. The TLB has a hit ratio of **80%**. The TLB access time is **20 ns**, and the main memory access time is **100 ns**. Calculate the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**.

**Given Data:**
- $h = 0.80$
- $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns
- $M = 100$ ns

**Step 1 — Identify the correct formula (TLB lookup is sequential):**

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\text{EAT} = 20 + (2 - 0.80) \cdot 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inner bracket:**

$$
(2 - 0.80) = 1.20
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by M:**

$$
1.20 \cdot 100 = 120 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the TLB lookup time:**

$$
\text{EAT} = 20 + 120 = 140 \text{ ns}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = 140 \text{ ns}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Extended Example — Multi-Level Page Table on TLB Miss

> **Problem Statement:**
> A system uses a **two-level page table**. TLB hit ratio = 90%, TLB access time = 10 ns, memory access time = 100 ns. On a TLB miss, both levels of the page table must be accessed before the actual data. Compute the EAT.

**Given Data:**
- $h = 0.90$
- $T_{TLB} = 10$ ns
- $M = 100$ ns
- TLB miss cost = 2 page table accesses + 1 data access = $3M$

**Step 1 — Time on TLB Hit:**

$$
T_{\text{hit}} = T_{TLB} + M = 10 + 100 = 110 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Time on TLB Miss:**

$$
T_{\text{miss}} = T_{TLB} + 3M = 10 + 300 = 310 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute EAT:**

$$
\text{EAT} = h \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{\text{miss}}
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 0.90 \cdot 110 + 0.10 \cdot 310
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = 99 + 31 = 130 \text{ ns}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = 130 \text{ ns}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Reverse-Engineering Example (Finding the Required Hit Ratio)

> **Problem Statement:**
> The desired EAT is **150 ns**, memory access time is **100 ns**, and TLB lookup time is **20 ns**. Find the required TLB hit ratio.

**Step 1 — Use the canonical formula:**

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $h$:**

$$
150 = 20 + (2 - h) \cdot 100
$$

$$
130 = (2 - h) \cdot 100
$$

$$
(2 - h) = \frac{130}{100} = 1.30
$$

$$
h = 2 - 1.30 = 0.70
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{h = 0.70 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 70\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A hit ratio of **70%** is required. Notice that an EAT of 150 ns is *greater* than a single memory access (100 ns), but *less* than the worst-case 200 ns (2 accesses on every miss). The formula naturally handles these boundary conditions.

---

### 3.5 Boundary Condition Sanity Checks

| **Condition** | **Expected EAT** | **Formula Result** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h = 1$ (100% hit) | $T_{TLB} + M$ | $T_{TLB} + (2 - 1) \cdot M = T_{TLB} + M$ | Only one memory access — ideal |
| $h = 0$ (0% hit, TLB useless) | $T_{TLB} + 2M$ | $T_{TLB} + (2 - 0) \cdot M = T_{TLB} + 2M$ | Always two memory accesses — overhead added |
| $h = 0.5, T_{TLB} = 0$ | $1.5M$ | $(2 - 0.5) \cdot M = 1.5M$ | Halfway between best and worst case |
| $h = 0$ and $T_{TLB} = 0$ | $2M$ | $(2 - 0) \cdot M = 2M$ | Paging without TLB — original two-access cost |

These checks confirm the formula is mathematically consistent and physically meaningful.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TLB Lookup Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the decision logic of the MMU + TLB subsystem during a single virtual address translation. This is the standard KTU-board diagram style.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Generates Logical Address] --> B[Extract VPN and Offset]
    B --> C[Send VPN to TLB for Associative Lookup]
    C --> D{TLB Lookup Result}
    D -- Hit Found --> E[Retrieve PFN from TLB Entry]
    D -- Miss Detected --> F[Trap to Page Table in Main Memory]
    F --> G[Access Outer Page Table Directory]
    G --> H[Access Inner Page Table Entry]
    H --> I[Fetch PTE and PFN]
    I --> J[Load PTE into TLB Evict LRU Entry if Full]
    J --> K[Construct Physical Address PFN plus Offset]
    E --> K
    K --> L[Access Physical Memory for Data]
    L --> M[Return Data to CPU]
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU Exam Tip):**

- The **diamond** (decision node `D`) is the only branching point — every translation must traverse it.
- The **left branch** (TLB Hit) is fast: 1 associative compare + 1 memory access.
- The **right branch** (TLB Miss) is slow: 1 TLB fail + 2 page table accesses + 1 data access + 1 TLB refill.
- The two paths **re-converge** at node `K`, where the physical address is finally formed.

---

### 4.2 TLB Hit vs. Miss — Comparative Time-Domain Diagram

This block diagram visualizes the **relative time penalties** of each path on a common timeline, making the EAT concept intuitive.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TLB_HIT_PATH["TLB HIT PATH Fast Path"]
        direction LR
        A1[CPU] --> A2[TLB Lookup t1]
        A2 --> A3[PFN Retrieved]
        A3 --> A4[Memory Access M]
        A4 --> A5[Data to CPU]
    end

    subgraph TLB_MISS_PATH["TLB MISS PATH Slow Path"]
        direction LR
        B1[CPU] --> B2[TLB Lookup Fails t1]
        B2 --> B3[Page Table Access M]
        B3 --> B4[PTE Fetched]
        B4 --> B5[TLB Refill]
        B5 --> B6[Memory Access M]
        B6 --> B7[Data to CPU]
    end

    TLB_HIT_PATH --- TLB_MISS_PATH
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **visual width** of each subgraph corresponds to the **time consumed**. A student should immediately notice that the miss path has **3 sequential memory-bound operations** vs. the hit path's **1 memory-bound operation**, which is the source of the "miss penalty."

---

### 4.3 TLB Structure — Associative Mapping Schematic

The TLB is a **fully-associative cache**: any entry can hold any VPN. The following block diagram shows its internal organization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU[CPU Issues Virtual Address] --> VPN[Extract VPN Tag]
    VPN --> CAM[Associative Search Across All TLB Entries]
    CAM --> CHECK{Any Tag Match}
    CHECK -- Yes HIT --> OUTPUT[Output Corresponding PFN]
    CHECK -- No MISS --> TRAP[Signal TLB Miss to OS Hardware]
    TRAP --> REFILL[Load PTE from Page Table]
    REFILL --> CAM

    subgraph TLB_ENTRIES["TLB Entry Array Fully Associative"]
        direction TB
        E1[Entry 1 Tag PFN Valid]
        E2[Entry 2 Tag PFN Valid]
        E3[Entry 3 Tag PFN Valid]
        E4[Entry N Tag PFN Valid]
    end

    CAM -.-> TLB_ENTRIES
```

**Key takeaways for KTU answers:**

1. The TLB uses **content-addressable memory (CAM)** — all entries are searched **in parallel** in a single cycle.
2. A **valid bit** is essential because the TLB is often larger than the working set, and stale entries must be marked.
3. The TLB is **small** (typically 16–512 entries) but **extremely fast** (1–2 ns) — this trade-off is what makes it effective.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB). What is meant by a TLB hit and a TLB miss?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB):** A TLB is a small, fast, fully-associative hardware cache maintained by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) that stores a subset of the page table entries — specifically, the mappings between virtual page numbers (VPN) and physical frame numbers (PFN).
>
> **TLB Hit:** A TLB hit occurs when the VPN of the currently referenced logical address is found in the TLB, allowing the MMU to obtain the PFN directly without consulting the page table in main memory.
>
> **TLB Miss:** A TLB miss occurs when the requested VPN is not present in the TLB, forcing the system to access the page table in main memory, retrieve the required PTE, and load it into the TLB (possibly evicting an older entry) before the memory access can proceed.

**(Mark split: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the significance of the TLB hit ratio in a paging system? How does it affect the Effective Access Time (EAT)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **TLB hit ratio** ($h$) is the fraction of memory references that are resolved using the TLB without needing to consult the in-memory page table. It is a direct measure of how effectively the TLB exploits **locality of reference**.
>
> The EAT depends on $h$ as: $\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M$
>
> - A **higher** $h$ reduces the average memory access time, approaching the ideal of $T_{TLB} + M$ (a single memory reference).
> - A **lower** $h$ increases the EAT, approaching the worst case of $T_{TLB} + 2M$ (two memory references per logical access).
>
> Thus, maximizing the TLB hit ratio is critical for virtual memory performance, and OS designers use techniques like larger page sizes and good working-set management to keep $h$ high.

**(Mark split: 1 for definition, 2 for impact on EAT)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse the *TLB lookup time* ($T_{TLB}$) with the *memory access time* ($M$) and add them incorrectly. Remember: $T_{TLB}$ is in **nanoseconds** and is **separate** from main memory access.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** On a TLB miss, students forget that **two** main memory accesses occur (one for the page table, one for the data). Using only one $M$ here is the most frequent mark-loss in KTU papers.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert percentages (e.g., 80%) into decimals (0.80) when substituting into the EAT formula. KTU evaluators explicitly deduct 1 mark for this.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Omitting the unit (ns / µs) in the final answer — KTU strictly requires it.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the role of the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) in a paging system with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between a TLB hit and a TLB miss. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider a paging system with TLB. The TLB access time is 10 ns and the main memory access time is 100 ns. If the TLB hit ratio is 90%, compute the Effective Access Time (EAT). What would be the EAT if the hit ratio drops to 70%? Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Role of TLB and TLB Hit vs. Miss (7 marks):**

> In a paging system, every logical address issued by the CPU must be translated to a physical address using the page table stored in main memory. This translation requires at least **one main memory access** in addition to the actual data fetch, effectively doubling the memory access cost.
>
> The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, high-speed, fully-associative hardware cache within the MMU that stores a subset of the page table entries (VPN → PFN mappings). It exploits the **principle of locality**: most programs access only a small subset of their pages at any given time, so the TLB can satisfy most translations without consulting the slow in-memory page table.

**Block Diagram (describe verbally, 2 marks):**

> [CPU] → [Logical Address: VPN | Offset] → [TLB Associative Search] → {Hit → [PFN | Offset] → [Physical Memory]} OR {Miss → [Page Table in Main Memory] → [PTE fetched, TLB updated] → [PFN | Offset] → [Physical Memory]}

**TLB Hit vs. TLB Miss (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **TLB Hit** | **TLB Miss** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | VPN found in TLB | VPN not found in TLB |
| Page table access | Not required | Required (1–2 accesses for multi-level) |
| Memory accesses for data | 1 | 1 (after page table) |
| Total memory accesses | 1 (plus TLB) | 2 (plus TLB) |
| Frequency | High (~80–99% in practice) | Low (~1–20%) |
| Performance impact | Fast path | Slow path (miss penalty) |

**(b) EAT Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_{TLB} = 10$ ns
- $M = 100$ ns
- $h_1 = 0.90$ (Case 1)
- $h_2 = 0.70$ (Case 2)

**Formula:**

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M
$$

**Case 1: $h = 0.90$**

$$
\text{EAT}_1 = 10 + (2 - 0.90) \cdot 100
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_1 = 10 + (1.10) \cdot 100 = 10 + 110 = 120 \text{ ns}
$$

**Case 2: $h = 0.70$**

$$
\text{EAT}_2 = 10 + (2 - 0.70) \cdot 100
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_2 = 10 + (1.30) \cdot 100 = 10 + 130 = 140 \text{ ns}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):**

> When the TLB hit ratio drops from 90% to 70%, the EAT increases from 120 ns to 140 ns — a **16.67% degradation** in average memory access performance. This demonstrates the critical importance of maintaining a high TLB hit ratio. Modern systems achieve this through larger TLBs, larger page sizes (which improve locality per entry), and prefetching techniques. The result also shows that even a 20% drop in hit ratio causes noticeable slowdown, justifying the substantial hardware investment in multi-level TLBs in modern CPUs.

**(Mark split for part b: 1 formula + 3 Case 1 + 3 Case 2 = 7 marks, plus 2 comments)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the working of the TLB in a virtual memory system. Why is the TLB called a "lookaside" buffer? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A system has a TLB hit ratio of 85%. The TLB lookup time is 15 ns, and the main memory access time is 120 ns. On a TLB miss, the page table is accessed, and on a TLB hit, the data is accessed directly.
>   (i) Calculate the EAT.
>   (ii) If the system uses a two-level page table and the TLB miss ratio becomes 20%, calculate the new EAT. Compare both results. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Working of TLB and "Lookaside" Naming (7 marks):**

> The TLB is a hardware cache integrated into the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**. When the CPU issues a logical address, the MMU splits it into a **Virtual Page Number (VPN)** and a **page offset**. The VPN is sent to the TLB for a parallel, fully-associative search against all stored tags.

**Working Steps (3 marks):**

1. The MMU receives the logical address and extracts the VPN.
2. The TLB performs a parallel comparison of the VPN against all stored tags.
3. **On a TLB hit:** the corresponding Physical Frame Number (PFN) is read from the matched TLB entry. The physical address (PFN + offset) is sent to the cache/main memory.
4. **On a TLB miss:** the MMU triggers a page table walk. The PTE is fetched from main memory, the TLB is updated (replacing a victim entry using LRU/FIFO/random policy), and then the physical address is formed and accessed.

**Why "Lookaside"? (2 marks):**

> The TLB is called a **"lookaside" buffer** because it is consulted **in parallel with** (or **alongside but not inline with**) the main memory access pipeline. Unlike a traditional cache lookup that is *on the critical path* of the data fetch, the TLB is *off to the side* — the CPU continues its pipelined operations while the TLB resolves the address translation independently. This parallel/aside architecture allows the TLB lookup to be **overlapped with other CPU operations**, reducing effective latency.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

> [CPU] → [MMU: VPN extracted] → [TLB lookup in parallel] → (Hit: PFN) OR (Miss: Page Table access) → [Physical Address] → [Main Memory] → [Data to CPU]

---

**(b) EAT Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_{TLB} = 15$ ns
- $M = 120$ ns
- Case (i): $h = 0.85$, single-level page table
- Case (ii): TLB miss ratio = 20% → $h = 0.80$, two-level page table

**Formula (general):**

$$
\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (k + 1 - h) \cdot M
$$

where $k$ = number of page table accesses on a miss.

**Case (i): $k = 1$ (single-level), $h = 0.85$ (1 mark for setup):**

$$
\text{EAT}_i = 15 + (1 + 1 - 0.85) \cdot 120
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_i = 15 + (1.15) \cdot 120
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_i = 15 + 138 = 153 \text{ ns}
$$

**Case (ii): $k = 2$ (two-level), $h = 0.80$ (1 mark for setup):**

$$
\text{EAT}_{ii} = 15 + (2 + 1 - 0.80) \cdot 120
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_{ii} = 15 + (3 - 0.80) \cdot 120
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_{ii} = 15 + (2.20) \cdot 120
$$

$$
\text{EAT}_{ii} = 15 + 264 = 279 \text{ ns}
$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

> The EAT increased from **153 ns to 279 ns** — an **82.35% increase**. This is because:
> 1. The hit ratio dropped by 5% (from 85% to 80%), and
> 2. The miss penalty doubled (from $1M$ to $2M$ due to the two-level page table walk).
>
> Both factors compound to severely degrade performance. This shows why **single-level page tables** are preferred when feasible, and why the **TLB hit ratio must be kept high** in systems with multi-level page tables (e.g., x86-64 uses 4-level page tables, making TLB performance *critical*).

**(Total marks: 1 setup (i) + 2 calc (i) + 1 setup (ii) + 2 calc (ii) + 1 comparison = 7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Your Exam):**

- **TLB = Translation Lookaside Buffer**, a small, fast, **fully-associative** hardware cache storing VPN→PFN mappings. *(1 mark for definition, KTU favourite.)*

- **TLB Hit** → MMU gets PFN from TLB → **1 memory access** for data. **TLB Miss** → MMU walks page table → **2 (or more) memory accesses** + TLB refill.

- **Canonical EAT formula (MOST TESTED):**
  $$
  \text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot M
  $$
  (Or simply $\text{EAT} = (2 - h) \cdot M$ if $T_{TLB}$ is included in $M$.)

- **Generalized EAT for $k$-level page table:**
  $$
  \text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (k + 1 - h) \cdot M
  $$

- **Boundary checks to remember:**
  - $h = 1$ → $\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + M$ (best case)
  - $h = 0$ → $\text{EAT} = T_{TLB} + (k+1) \cdot M$ (worst case)

- **Common substitutions to memorize:**
  - 80% → 0.80
  - 90% → 0.90
  - 70% → 0.70

- **Always include units** (ns or µs) in the final answer — KTU deducts marks otherwise.

- **TLB refill policies** (theoretical): LRU, FIFO, Random. LRU is most common in hardware.

- **The TLB is "lookaside"** because it is searched **in parallel with** (not inline with) the main memory access pipeline.

- **Real-world performance:** Modern CPUs target TLB hit ratios of **>99%**. Even a 1% drop can cause measurable slowdowns in memory-intensive applications.

- **Exam traps to avoid:**
  1. Using $h$ as a percentage (e.g., 80 instead of 0.80).
  2. Forgetting the $+1$ memory access for the actual data on a hit.
  3. Mixing up the TLB lookup time ($T_{TLB}$) and memory access time ($M$).
  4. Omitting units in the final EAT value.
  5. Forgetting to add $T_{TLB}$ when it is given separately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling TLB Misses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling TLB Misses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, fully-associative hardware cache residing inside the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** that stores recently used **virtual-to-physical address translations** in the form of *(page number → frame number)* pairs.

A **TLB Miss** is the hardware event that occurs when the MMU searches the TLB for the virtual page number (VPN) of a memory reference issued by the CPU, but the corresponding translation entry is **not present** in the TLB. The system must then consult the **page table in main memory** to obtain the required frame number — an operation that incurs additional memory access latency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** *Virtual Memory → Page Tables → TLB → Handling of TLB Misses.* This topic is a **direct continuation of Paging and Virtual Memory** and is frequently combined with Effective Access Time (EAT) problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Librarian's Notebook

Imagine you are a librarian and a student asks for book *X*.

* **TLB = Your Pocket Notebook** containing the *last 50 books* you helped locate and their exact shelf numbers.
* **Page Table = The Master Card Catalogue** in the basement with *every* book in the library.

* **TLB Hit:** You glance at your notebook — the shelf is listed — you walk directly to the shelf. *Fast.*
* **TLB Miss:** The book is not in your notebook. You must trudge down to the basement, search the card catalogue, find the shelf number, walk back, *then* retrieve the book. *Slow.*

> [!NOTE]
> The TLB exploits **temporal locality** and **spatial locality** of memory references. A small cache with high hit ratio (typically **> 95%**) dramatically reduces the *effective* memory access time despite page tables being stored in slower main memory.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Standard Notation | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| TLB Hit | — | Required VPN-to-PFN translation found in TLB. |
| TLB Miss | — | Required translation absent from TLB. |
| TLB Hit Ratio | $h$ | Probability that a given reference finds the entry in TLB. |
| TLB Miss Ratio | $1-h$ | Probability of having to walk the page table. |
| TLB Access Time | $t$ | Time (ns) to query the TLB hardware. |
| Memory Access Time | $m$ | Time (ns) to access one word from main memory. |
| Page Table Walk | — | Sequential memory accesses to fetch translation. |
| ASID | — | Address Space Identifier — process tag in TLB entries. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Sequence on Every Memory Reference

Modern processors issue the following micro-operations for *every* load/store instruction:

1. **CPU** issues a virtual address $\langle VPN, Offset \rangle$.
2. **MMU** presents the VPN to the TLB for parallel lookup.
3. **Decision Branch**:
   * **TLB Hit (probability $h$):** The TLB returns the Page Frame Number (PFN) immediately. The MMU concatenates PFN + Offset to form the physical address, and the CPU proceeds to access main memory.
   * **TLB Miss (probability $1-h$):** The TLB signals a miss. The translation must be fetched from the page table stored in main memory.
4. **Page Table Walk:** One or more additional memory accesses retrieve the required PTE.
5. **TLB Update:** The fetched translation is inserted into the TLB (possibly evicting an older entry).
6. **Retry:** The CPU re-issues the original memory access using the now-available physical address.

### 2.2 Two Strategies for Handling a TLB Miss

#### A. Hardware-Managed TLB (Used in CISC architectures — e.g., Intel x86, Motorola 68xxx)

* The hardware MMU contains dedicated circuitry to **automatically walk the page table** on a TLB miss.
* On a miss, the hardware issues successive memory reads to traverse multi-level page tables until it locates the PTE.
* The PTE is loaded into the TLB by the hardware itself.
* The original memory access is then transparently retried.
* **Advantage:** OS code is simpler and faster (no trap overhead).
* **Disadvantage:** Hardware complexity is high; page table structure is **rigid** and fixed by the silicon design.

#### B. Software-Managed TLB (Used in RISC architectures — e.g., MIPS, SPARC, Alpha, PA-RISC)

* On a TLB miss, the hardware **raises a TLB exception / trap** and transfers control to a privileged OS handler.
* The OS handler is given the faulting VPN in a control register.
* The OS executes machine instructions (e.g., `TLBP`, `TLBR`, `TLBWI` in MIPS) to:
  1. Walk its own (arbitrarily structured) page table in software.
  2. Locate the correct PTE.
  3. **Write the translation explicitly into the TLB** using a privileged instruction.
  4. Return from exception; the hardware retries the faulting instruction.
* **Advantage:** OS is free to use *any* page table data structure (hash tables, inverted page tables, etc.); hardware is simpler.
* **Disadvantage:** TLB miss becomes a true *exception* — slightly higher latency due to trap save/restore overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In both models, the **TLB is searched in parallel** with the cache in modern designs, but logically the translation must be resolved *before* the physical cache lookup completes.

### 2.3 Role of ASID (Address Space Identifier)

When the OS performs a **context switch** between two processes, the TLB still holds translations belonging to the *old* process. Two solutions exist:

* **Flush the entire TLB** — simple but expensive (cold start after every switch).
* **Tag each TLB entry with an ASID** — a small unique process ID. The TLB compares the ASID alongside the VPN, allowing *co-existence* of translations from multiple processes. This is the standard modern approach.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | TLB Hit Time | $T_{hit} = t + m$ | TLB lookup + one memory access |
| 2 | TLB Miss Time (1-level PT) | $T_{miss} = t + m + m = t + 2m$ | Extra page table access |
| 3 | TLB Miss Time (2-level PT) | $T_{miss} = t + 2m + m + m = t + 4m$ | Outer + Inner + Data |
| 4 | TLB Miss Time (k-level PT) | $T_{miss} = t + (k+1) \cdot m$ | Generalised form |
| 5 | Effective Access Time (1-level) | $EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+2m)$ | Most common KTU problem |
| 6 | Effective Access Time (2-level) | $EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+4m)$ | Two-level paging |
| 7 | Memory Access w/o TLB | $T_{noTLB} = t + 2m$ | Always consult page table |
| 8 | Speedup Factor | $S = \dfrac{T_{noTLB}}{EAT}$ | Use to quantify TLB benefit |
| 9 | ASID width constraint | $2^{ASID\,bits} \geq N_{processes}$ | Avoid ASID exhaustion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical convention for KTU board answers:** Always state *both* quantities — TLB access time ($t$) **and** memory access time ($m$) — explicitly before substituting into the EAT equation. Examiners penalise answers that only mention "cache access time" ambiguously.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TLB miss handler is foundational to:

* **Demand Paging in General-Purpose OS** (Windows, Linux, macOS).
* **Embedded systems** with software-managed TLBs (MIPS/ARM cores).
* **Virtualization** (VMMs add a *second-level* TLB / tagged TLB, called a *V-TLB*, with nested page walks — the notorious "nested page table walk" cost).
* **Database engines** that use *huge pages* (2 MB / 1 GB) to reduce TLB miss rate and improve translation coverage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Access Time (EAT) — Single-Level Page Table

We model the memory access as a **probabilistic decision tree**.

**Case 1 — TLB Hit (Probability = $h$):**
The TLB provides the PFN directly. The total time incurred is the TLB access time *plus* the actual memory reference:

$$T_{hit} = t + m$$

**Case 2 — TLB Miss (Probability = $1-h$):**
The TLB access still occurs, but the entry is absent. The system must then access the page table in main memory (one extra memory access) before finally accessing the requested data:

$$T_{miss} = t + m + m = t + 2m$$

**Expected (Effective) Access Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= h \cdot T_{hit} + (1-h) \cdot T_{miss} \\[4pt]
    &= h \cdot (t + m) + (1-h) \cdot (t + 2m) \\[4pt]
    &= h \cdot t + h \cdot m + (1-h) \cdot t + 2(1-h) \cdot m \\[4pt]
    &= t \cdot [h + (1-h)] + m \cdot [h + 2(1-h)] \\[4pt]
    &= t + m \cdot [h + 2 - 2h] \\[4pt]
    &= t + m \cdot (2 - h)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU board form** for EAT with a single-level page table.

### 3.2 Extension to Multi-Level Page Tables

If the page table is *k*-levels deep, a single TLB miss requires $k$ extra memory accesses to traverse the page table hierarchy, plus 1 access for the actual data:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{miss}^{(k)} &= t + (k+1) \cdot m \\[4pt]
EAT^{(k)} &= h(t+m) + (1-h)\bigl(t + (k+1)m\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **two-level page table** ($k=2$):

$$
EAT^{(2)} = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+4m)
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Standard EAT Calculation

**Given:**
* TLB access time $t = 20$ ns
* Memory access time $m = 100$ ns
* TLB hit ratio $h = 0.95$ (i.e., 95%)

**Step 1:** Write the EAT equation for single-level paging:

$$EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+2m)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.95 \cdot (20 + 100) + (1 - 0.95) \cdot (20 + 2 \times 100) \\[4pt]
    &= 0.95 \cdot 120 + 0.05 \cdot 220 \\[4pt]
    &= 114 + 11 \\[4pt]
    &= 125 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with no-TLB baseline:

$$T_{noTLB} = t + 2m = 20 + 200 = 220 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the speedup:

$$
S = \frac{220}{125} \approx 1.76
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even a *modest* 95% hit ratio reduces memory access time by **~43%** — illustrating why TLB design is critical in modern CPU architectures.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Effect of Low Hit Ratio

**Given:** Same $t, m$, but $h = 0.70$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.70 \cdot 120 + 0.30 \cdot 220 \\[4pt]
    &= 84 + 66 \\[4pt]
    &= 150 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 25-percentage-point drop in hit ratio (95% → 70%) inflates EAT by **25 ns (+20%)**. This is why OS-level **TLB shootdown** on context switch and **huge-page support** are not optional luxuries but performance necessities.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Two-Level Page Table

**Given:** $t = 10$ ns, $m = 80$ ns, $h = 0.90$, page table has 2 levels.

**Step 1:** Apply the 2-level EAT formula:

$$EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+4m)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.90 \cdot (10 + 80) + 0.10 \cdot (10 + 4 \times 80) \\[4pt]
    &= 0.90 \cdot 90 + 0.10 \cdot 330 \\[4pt]
    &= 81 + 33 \\[4pt]
    &= 114 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Without TLB, even a 2-level walk alone costs:

$$T_{noTLB, 2L} = t + 4m = 10 + 320 = 330 \text{ ns}$$

The TLB cuts effective time to roughly **one-third** of the unassisted 2-level walk.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

| Page Table Type | Hit Ratio $h$ | $t$ (ns) | $m$ (ns) | EAT (ns) | Speedup |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| No TLB (1-level) | 0.00 | 20 | 100 | 220 | 1.00× |
| With TLB (1-level) | 0.80 | 20 | 100 | 140 | 1.57× |
| With TLB (1-level) | 0.95 | 20 | 100 | 125 | 1.76× |
| With TLB (1-level) | 0.99 | 20 | 100 | 121 | 1.82× |
| With TLB (2-level) | 0.90 | 20 | 100 | 180 | 1.22× |
| With TLB (2-level) | 0.99 | 20 | 100 | 128 | 1.72× |

> [!NOTE]
> Observe that with a 2-level page table, even a 90% hit ratio (very good) only just *matches* the unassisted 1-level case. **Higher hit ratio is mandatory for multi-level paging to remain a net win.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TLB Miss Handling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU issues virtual address VPN Offset"] --> B{"TLB Lookup using VPN and ASID"}
    B -- "TLB HIT" --> C["Read PFN from TLB entry"]
    C --> D["Form physical address PFN Offset"]
    D --> E["Access main memory at physical address"]
    E --> F["Return data to CPU"]
    B -- "TLB MISS" --> G{"TLB Management Type"}
    G -- "Hardware Managed" --> H["MMU walks page table in hardware"]
    G -- "Software Managed" --> I["MMU raises TLB exception"]
    I --> J["OS handler reads faulting VPN from control register"]
    J --> K["OS walks page table in software"]
    K --> L["OS loads PTE into TLB via privileged instruction"]
    H --> M["Hardware loads PTE into TLB"]
    L --> N["Return from exception to faulting instruction"]
    M --> N
    N --> B
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Software-Managed TLB Path (RISC Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Core"] -- "VA = VPN + Offset" --> MMU["MMU with TLB"]
    MMU -- "TLB Hit" --> PHY["Physical Address Bus"]
    MMU -- "TLB Miss" --> EXC["TLB Exception Vector"]
    EXC --> OSH["OS TLB Miss Handler"]
    OSH -- "Read PTE from RAM" --> RAM["Main Memory Page Table"]
    RAM -- "PTE Returned" --> OSH
    OSH -- "TLBWI instruction" --> MMU
    MMU -- "Retry original access" --> PHY
    PHY --> RAM
    RAM -- "Data" --> CPU
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Hardware vs Software TLB

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HARD["HARDWARE MANAGED TLB e.g. x86"]
        H1["TLB Miss Signal"] --> H2["MMU Page Walker FSM"]
        H2 --> H3["Read PTE from RAM"]
        H3 --> H4["Write PTE to TLB"]
        H4 --> H5["Retry Memory Access"]
    end
    subgraph SOFT["SOFTWARE MANAGED TLB e.g. MIPS SPARC"]
        S1["TLB Miss Signal"] --> S2["Trap to OS"]
        S2 --> S3["Save Context"]
        S3 --> S4["OS Walks Page Table"]
        S4 --> S5["OS Executes TLB Write Instruction"]
        S5 --> S6["Restore Context"]
        S6 --> S7["Retry Memory Access"]
    end
```

### 4.4 TLB Entry Structure (Schematic)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    block:ent["TLB ENTRY FORMAT"] columns 5
        V["Valid Bit 1b"] AS["ASID 8b"] VPN_T["VPN Tag 20b"] PFN["Page Frame Number 24b"] FL["Flags R W X 3b"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagram illustrates the canonical TLB entry. The **VPN Tag** is the value being compared during lookup; on a match *and* matching ASID *and* valid bit set, a TLB hit is declared and the PFN is forwarded to the physical address composer.

### 4.5 ASCII Reference — Address Decomposition

```
+----------------------+----------+      Virtual Address
|    Virtual Page #    |  Offset  |  =  (VPN)         (d)
|       (p - d)        |  (d)     |
+----------------------+----------+
         |
         |   TLB Lookup
         v
+----------------------+----------+      Physical Address
|  Page Frame Number   |  Offset  |  =  (PFN)         (d)
|       (p - d)        |  (d)     |
+----------------------+----------+
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*What is a TLB miss? Explain how it is handled in a system that uses a software-managed TLB.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *TLB miss* occurs when the Memory Management Unit searches the Translation Lookaside Buffer for a virtual-to-physical address translation and does not find a matching entry. In a **software-managed TLB** (used in RISC processors like MIPS), the hardware raises a *TLB exception* on a miss and transfers control to a privileged OS handler. The handler reads the faulting virtual page number from a control register, walks the OS-maintained page table in main memory to find the corresponding page-table entry, and then loads the translation into the TLB using a privileged instruction such as `TLBWI`. Finally, the OS returns from the exception and the hardware retries the faulting memory reference. (3 marks)

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Define Effective Access Time. Why is the TLB said to "amplify" the memory hierarchy?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *Effective Access Time* (EAT) is the *average* time taken to complete a memory reference, accounting for both TLB hits and misses. It is given by the standard KTU equation $EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+2m)$ for a single-level page table. The TLB is said to amplify the memory hierarchy because, although it is itself a small and fast hardware cache, it effectively gives the illusion that *all* page-table lookups are as fast as TLB lookups. A well-designed TLB with a hit ratio above 95% brings EAT close to the bare memory access time $m$, hiding the otherwise expensive page-table walk from the program's critical path. (3 marks)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Direct / Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the two approaches for handling TLB misses in modern operating systems. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

A *TLB miss* is resolved by one of two architectural strategies:

1. **Hardware-Managed TLB** (CISC: x86, 68xxx):
   * On a miss, dedicated MMU hardware state-machines walk the page table automatically.
   * The hardware performs successive memory reads to traverse multi-level page tables.
   * On locating the PTE, the hardware loads it into the TLB and retries the access.
   * **Advantages:** Faster miss handling, OS is simpler, no context-switch trap overhead.
   * **Disadvantages:** Rigid page-table format fixed by silicon, complex hardware.

2. **Software-Managed TLB** (RISC: MIPS, SPARC, Alpha):
   * On a miss, the hardware raises a TLB exception (trap).
   * The OS handler reads the faulting VPN from a control register.
   * The OS walks its own (arbitrarily designed) page table in software.
   * The OS uses a privileged instruction (e.g., `TLBWI`, `TLBWR`) to insert the translation into the TLB.
   * The handler returns; hardware retries the original instruction.
   * **Advantages:** OS freedom in page-table design, simpler hardware.
   * **Disadvantages:** Slightly higher latency due to trap save/restore.

(7 marks — full marks require a labelled block diagram, a comparison table, and explicit identification of the *trigger* and *handler* in each case.)

**(b)** In a paging system, the TLB access time is **15 ns** and the main memory access time is **120 ns**. If the TLB hit ratio is **90%**, calculate the *Effective Access Time* (EAT). Also compute the speedup obtained over a system without a TLB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $t = 15$ ns, $m = 120$ ns, $h = 0.90$.

**Step 1:** Standard EAT formula for single-level page table:

$$EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+2m)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.90 \times (15 + 120) + 0.10 \times (15 + 2 \times 120) \\[4pt]
    &= 0.90 \times 135 + 0.10 \times 255 \\[4pt]
    &= 121.5 + 25.5 \\[4pt]
    &= 147 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** EAT without a TLB (always walk the page table):

$$T_{noTLB} = t + 2m = 15 + 240 = 255 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4:** Speedup factor:

$$
S = \frac{T_{noTLB}}{EAT} = \frac{255}{147} \approx 1.73
$$

**Result:** $EAT = 147$ ns; Speedup $\approx 1.73\times$. (7 marks)

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled address translation diagram, describe how a *TLB hit* and a *TLB miss* are processed by the MMU. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

On every memory reference, the CPU issues a virtual address $\langle VPN, Offset \rangle$.

* **TLB Hit:** The MMU presents the VPN (and current process ASID) to the TLB. A matching entry is found, returning the PFN. The MMU concatenates PFN + Offset to form the physical address. Only **one** memory access is then needed to fetch the data. Total time $\approx t + m$.

* **TLB Miss:** The TLB lookup fails. The system must consult the page table in main memory. **One extra memory access** retrieves the PTE containing the PFN. The TLB is updated with this new translation. The CPU then re-issues the original memory reference. Total time $\approx t + 2m$ (for a single-level page table). The TLB update policy is typically *LRU* or *random replacement*.

The labelled diagram (already shown in SECTION 4) should depict: CPU → MMU/TLB → [Hit path: Physical Address Bus → Memory] and [Miss path: Trap/Page-Walk → RAM Page Table → TLB Update → Retry].

(7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between **hardware-managed** and **software-managed** TLB approaches. In which type of processor family is each typically used? Give one example instruction in a software-managed TLB that loads a translation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Hardware-Managed TLB | Software-Managed TLB |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Miss Handler** | MMU state machine in hardware | OS exception handler |
| **Page-Table Format** | Fixed by hardware design | Arbitrary (OS-defined) |
| **Latency on Miss** | Lower (no trap overhead) | Higher (trap save/restore) |
| **Hardware Complexity** | High | Low |
| **OS Complexity** | Low | High |
| **Processor Family** | CISC (Complex Instruction Set) | RISC (Reduced Instruction Set) |
| **Example Architecture** | Intel x86, Motorola 68xxx | MIPS, SPARC, Alpha, PA-RISC |
| **TLB Update Mechanism** | Implicit, by hardware | Explicit instruction, e.g. `TLBWI`, `TLBWR` in MIPS |

**Example software-managed TLB instruction:** In the MIPS architecture, the instruction **`TLBWI`** (TLB Write Indexed) — used together with the `Index` and `EntryHi`/`EntryLo` CP0 registers — explicitly inserts a virtual-to-physical translation into a TLB slot chosen by the OS.

(7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add the TLB access time $t$.** Many students write $EAT = h(m) + (1-h)(2m)$ — this is **wrong**. The TLB itself is queried on *every* reference (hit *or* miss).
> 2. **Misapplying multi-level formulas.** A two-level page table requires $t + 4m$ on a miss, **not** $t + 2m$. The number of memory accesses equals *(number of levels) + 1* on a miss.
> 3. **Confusing "no TLB" with "TLB miss".** A system with no TLB has hit ratio $h = 0$ — that is, *every* access walks the page table. Do not confuse this with a system that has a TLB but suffers 100% misses.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams.** A 7-mark question on TLB miss handling **explicitly** requires a labelled block diagram. Skipping the diagram typically forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Wrong unit notation.** Always express times in **ns** (nanoseconds) consistently throughout the calculation; mixing $\mu s$ and ns without conversion is a common error.
> 6. **Ignoring ASID during context switch.** When asked about *context-switch cost*, mention either TLB flush or ASID tagging — never leave the question partially answered.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **TLB** = small, fully-associative hardware cache inside the MMU storing *recent virtual-to-physical translations*.
* **TLB Miss** = the required translation is *not* in the TLB; the page table in main memory must be consulted.
* **Two miss-handling strategies:**
  * **Hardware-managed** (CISC, e.g. x86) — MMU walks the page table automatically.
  * **Software-managed** (RISC, e.g. MIPS, SPARC) — OS handler walks the page table, loads TLB via `TLBWI`-like instruction.
* **Canonical EAT equation (single-level):** $EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+2m)$.
* **Multi-level EAT (k-level):** $EAT = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+(k+1)m)$.
* **ASID** tags each TLB entry with a process ID, avoiding TLB flush on every context switch.
* **TLB exploits temporal and spatial locality** — a 95% hit ratio typically cuts memory access time by **~40–50%**.
* **Huge pages** (2 MB / 1 GB) reduce TLB miss rate by increasing per-entry translation coverage — a key optimization in databases and HPC.
* **In virtualised systems**, TLB misses may trigger *nested page table walks*, dramatically increasing the cost of a single TLB miss.
* **Always include $t$ (TLB access time) in EAT calculations** — it is incurred on every reference.
* **Speedup factor** $S = T_{noTLB} / EAT$ quantifies the TLB's contribution to system performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TLB Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TLB Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, high-speed **fully-associative hardware cache**, implemented inside the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**, that stores a subset of recently used **Virtual Page Number (VPN) → Physical Frame Number (PFN)** translations to accelerate the address-translation process during virtual memory access.

The TLB is sometimes referred to as an **Address Translation Cache (ATC)** or **Translation Cache**. It acts as a hardware-level shortcut, eliminating the need to access the (slower) in-memory page table for every memory reference.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Book Analogy:** Imagine a massive library with millions of books (main memory). Each book has a *catalogue number* (physical frame) and a *topic-based call number* (virtual address). Normally, to find a book, the librarian must consult a giant index file (the page table) which itself takes time to search. The librarian keeps a **small index card on the desk** (the TLB) containing the **most recently searched books**. When a request comes in, the librarian glances at the desk-card first; if found (a *hit*), she instantly hands over the book. If not found (a *miss*), she consults the giant catalogue, hands over the book, AND writes the new entry onto the desk-card for future requests.

In short: **TLB = fast cache for page-table translations**, just as CPU L1 cache is a fast cache for main-memory data.

### 1.3 Core Properties & Standard Metrics

The following engineering constants and ranges are **industry-typical** values used in KTU numerical problems and GATE/OS textbooks:

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| TLB size | **16 – 512 entries** |
| TLB lookup latency | **1 – 2 ns** |
| TLB associativity | Fully / N-way set-associative |
| TLB miss penalty | Page-table walk + memory access |
| TLB replacement policy | LRU, Pseudo-LRU, or Random |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** TLB operates **transparently** to the operating system at the hardware level, but the OS is responsible for **flushing or maintaining consistency** of the TLB during context switches (especially when ASID is unavailable).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 TLB Entry Structure (Hardware Layout)

Each TLB entry (row) typically consists of the following fields. The exact field set depends on the architecture, but a generic, KTU-style entry contains:

1. **Tag field** – stores the high-order bits of the VPN used for associative matching.
2. **Frame Number (PFN)** – the physical frame number where the page resides in RAM.
3. **Valid Bit (V)** – indicates whether the TLB slot contains a valid translation (`1 = valid`, `0 = invalid`).
4. **Dirty / Modified Bit (D)** – indicates whether the page has been written to.
5. **Reference / Use Bit (R)** – indicates whether the page has been recently accessed (used by page-replacement policies).
6. **Protection Bits** – Read / Write / Execute (R/W/X) permissions for the page.
7. **ASID (Address Space Identifier)** – optional field that uniquely identifies a process, allowing multiple processes to share the TLB without flushing on every context switch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ASID matters:** Without ASID, every context switch would require a **full TLB flush**, which is catastrophically expensive. Modern MMUs (ARM, x86 with PCID, MIPS) support ASID/PCID to avoid this flush.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step TLB Operation

The operational flow of TLB during a single memory reference is as follows:

- **Step 1 – Address Generation:** The CPU generates a logical (virtual) address consisting of **VPN + Offset**.
- **Step 2 – Parallel Lookup:** The VPN portion is simultaneously broadcast across **all TLB tags** (associative search). This is the hardware's killer feature — lookup is **O(1)** regardless of TLB size.
- **Step 3 – Compare:** The TLB compares the incoming VPN against all stored tags in one clock cycle.
- **Step 4 – TLB Hit:** If a tag matches AND the valid bit is set → the corresponding **PFN** is read out → physical address = PFN + Offset → memory access proceeds.
- **Step 5 – TLB Miss:** If no tag matches → the hardware initiates a **page-table walk** through main memory to fetch the translation. On retrieval, the new translation may be **inserted into the TLB** (replacing an old entry per the replacement policy).
- **Step 6 – Continue Execution:** The CPU uses the recovered PFN to access the actual physical memory.

### 2.3 Why TLB is Necessary — The "Why" Behind the Concept

Every memory access in a paged system would otherwise require **at least two physical memory accesses**: one for the page table and one for the actual data. Without a TLB, this **doubles the memory access time**, devastating performance.

By keeping the **working set's translations** in a fast hardware cache, the TLB reduces the effective number of memory references per logical access from **2 → 1** for the common case (TLB hit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 80/20 Rule applies:** Empirically, programs exhibit high **temporal locality** and **spatial locality** in page references. Typical TLB hit ratios in real workloads are **95% – 99%**, which makes the TLB one of the most cost-effective hardware components in any computer.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every** formula and definition required for KTU 2024 scheme ESE and module-level examinations on this topic.

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| TLB Hit Ratio | $h = \dfrac{\text{TLB Hits}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le h \le 1$ |
| TLB Miss Ratio | $1 - h$ | Dimensionless |
| TLB Access Time | $t_{\text{TLB}}$ | Nanoseconds (typically **1–2 ns**) |
| Memory Access Time | $t_{M}$ | Nanoseconds (typically **100 ns**) |
| **Effective Access Time (TLB Hit & Miss)** | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot (t_{\text{TLB}} + t_{M}) + (1 - h) \cdot (t_{\text{TLB}} + 2 t_{M})$ | Nanoseconds |
| **Simplified EAT** | $\text{EAT} = t_{\text{TLB}} + (2 - h) \cdot t_{M}$ | Nanoseconds |
| **Access Time with only Page Table** | $2 \cdot t_{M}$ | No TLB case |
| **Speedup Factor** | $\dfrac{2 t_{M}}{\text{EAT}}$ | Times faster than no-TLB |
| TLB Reach | $\text{TLB Size} \times \text{Page Size}$ | Bytes of memory accessible without a miss |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** In EAT problems, do **NOT** forget that the TLB must be consulted **every** memory reference (hit or miss). The $t_{\text{TLB}}$ term appears in **both** the hit-case and the miss-case terms of the EAT formula.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hardware Design:** CPU architects use TLB reach as a primary metric for cache hierarchy design. AMD and Intel publish TLB reach numbers in their architecture manuals.
- **Operating Systems:** OS developers use the TLB miss rate to tune page sizes (huge pages in Linux increase TLB reach dramatically).
- **Database Systems:** Databases like Oracle and PostgreSQL configure huge pages to maximize TLB coverage, since OLTP workloads have large working sets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Effective Access Time (EAT)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU ESE on this topic. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly below.

#### Step 0: Notation Setup

We define the following variables:

$$
h \;=\; \text{TLB Hit Ratio} \quad , \quad (1 - h) \;=\; \text{TLB Miss Ratio}
$$

$$
t_{\text{TLB}} \;=\; \text{Time to access TLB (lookup latency)}
$$

$$
t_{M} \;=\; \text{Time to access main memory (one reference)}
$$

> **Reasoning:** We need a memory access time benchmark because the page table resides in main memory itself, and so does the data the CPU ultimately needs. The miss case requires an extra page-table access — hence the factor of 2.

#### Step 1: Cost of a TLB Hit

When the VPN is found in the TLB:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{hit}} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; t_{M}
$$

> **Reasoning:** First we must consult the TLB (1 access, time $t_{\text{TLB}}$). After obtaining the PFN, we access the actual physical memory once (time $t_{M}$). No page-table walk is required.

#### Step 2: Cost of a TLB Miss

When the VPN is NOT found in the TLB:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{miss}} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; t_{M} \;+\; t_{M} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; 2 \cdot t_{M}
$$

> **Reasoning:** We consult the TLB (1 access, $t_{\text{TLB}}$), discover the entry is missing, walk the page table in main memory (1 access, $t_{M}$), retrieve the PFN, and then access the actual data word in main memory (1 more access, $t_{M}$). Total: **three** accesses — but only **two** of them are to main memory, which is why the standard formula uses $2 \cdot t_{M}$ for the extra cost beyond the TLB hit.

#### Step 3: Weighted Average Cost (EAT)

The effective access time is the probability-weighted average of both cases:

$$
\text{EAT} \;=\; h \cdot (\text{Cost}_{\text{hit}}) \;+\; (1 - h) \cdot (\text{Cost}_{\text{miss}})
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; h \cdot (t_{\text{TLB}} + t_{M}) \;+\; (1 - h) \cdot (t_{\text{TLB}} + 2 t_{M})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Algebraic Expansion

Distributing the coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; h \cdot t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; h \cdot t_{M} \;+\; (1 - h) \cdot t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; (1 - h) \cdot 2 t_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Group Like Terms

Grouping the $t_{\text{TLB}}$ terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; \big[\, h \cdot t_{\text{TLB}} + (1 - h) \cdot t_{\text{TLB}} \,\big] \;+\; h \cdot t_{M} \;+\; 2(1 - h) \cdot t_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

Factor out $t_{\text{TLB}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \cdot \big[\, h + (1 - h) \,\big] \;+\; h \cdot t_{M} \;+\; 2(1 - h) \cdot t_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $h + (1 - h) = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \cdot (1) \;+\; h \cdot t_{M} \;+\; 2(1 - h) \cdot t_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Final Simplified Form

Combine the $t_{M}$ terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; \big[\, h + 2(1 - h) \,\big] \cdot t_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; \big[\, h + 2 - 2h \,\big] \cdot t_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; (2 - h) \cdot t_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final EAT Formula:**
> $$\text{EAT} \;=\; t_{\text{TLB}} \;+\; (2 - h) \cdot t_{M}$$
> This is the canonical form used in **all** KTU textbook problems.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> **Problem Statement:** Consider a paged system with a TLB access time of **20 ns** and main memory access time of **100 ns**. Compute the EAT and speedup when the TLB hit ratio is (i) 80%, (ii) 95%, (iii) 99%.

Given: $t_{\text{TLB}} = 20$ ns, $t_{M} = 100$ ns.

**Case (i): $h = 0.80$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 20 + (2 - 0.80) \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + (1.20) \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + 120 \\
&= 140 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Without TLB: $2 \cdot t_{M} = 200$ ns. Speedup = $200 / 140 = 1.43 \times$.

**Case (ii): $h = 0.95$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 20 + (2 - 0.95) \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + (1.05) \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + 105 \\
&= 125 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Speedup = $200 / 125 = 1.60 \times$.

**Case (iii): $h = 0.99$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 20 + (2 - 0.99) \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + (1.01) \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + 101 \\
&= 121 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Speedup = $200 / 121 \approx 1.65 \times$.

#### Analytical Matrix Summary Table

| Hit Ratio $h$ | EAT (ns) | Speedup vs. No-TLB |
|---|---|---|
| 0.80 | 140 | 1.43 $\times$ |
| 0.90 | 130 | 1.54 $\times$ |
| 0.95 | 125 | 1.60 $\times$ |
| 0.99 | 121 | 1.65 $\times$ |
| 1.00 | 120 | 1.67 $\times$ |

> **Key Insight:** Even at a 100% hit ratio, the TLB cannot make EAT smaller than $t_{\text{TLB}} + t_{M} = 120$ ns, because the TLB lookup itself is unavoidable. This lower bound is the **asymptotic limit** of TLB performance.

### 3.3 Derivation of TLB Reach

TLB Reach defines the maximum amount of memory that can be accessed without inducing a TLB miss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TLB Reach} \;=\; (\text{Number of TLB entries}) \;\times\; (\text{Page Size})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If TLB has 64 entries and page size is 4 KB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TLB Reach} = 64 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 256 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means a working set larger than 256 KB will frequently cause TLB misses, even if all those pages are in memory. This is why **huge pages** (2 MB, 1 GB) are used in modern servers to increase reach.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TLB Lookup Process — Functional Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **complete runtime decision flow** during a single virtual memory access, from CPU-generated address to final data fetch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([CPU Generates Virtual Address]) --> split1{Extract VPN and Offset}
    split1 --> vpn1[VPN sent to TLB for associative lookup]
    vpn1 --> comp1{Compare VPN against all TLB tags in parallel}
    comp1 -- Match Found and Valid equals 1 --> hit1[TLB HIT Retrieve PFN from TLB]
    comp1 -- No Match OR Valid equals 0 --> miss1[TLB MISS Trigger page table walk in main memory]
    miss1 --> walk1[Access Page Table Entry in RAM]
    walk1 --> fetchpt[Fetch PFN from page table entry]
    fetchpt --> updatetlb[Insert new VPN to PFN mapping into TLB using LRU policy]
    updatetlb --> hit1
    hit1 --> combine1[Form Physical Address equals PFN plus Offset]
    combine1 --> mem1[Access Main Memory at Physical Address]
    mem1 --> data1([Return Data to CPU])
```

### 4.2 TLB Entry Structure — Block-Level Architecture

The following diagram shows the **internal fields of a single TLB entry** and the parallel-comparison hardware structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuA([CPU]) --> vpnBus[VPN Bus]
    vpnBus --> camA[Associative Tag Comparator]
    camA --> matchSig{Match Signal}
    matchSig -- Hit --> pfOut[PFN Output to MMU]
    matchSig -- Miss --> ptWalk[Page Table Walk Engine]
    subgraph entryBlock[TLB Entry Array Hardware]
        direction TB
        tagField[Tag Field Stores VPN]
        pfnField[PFN Field Stores Frame Number]
        validField[Valid Bit Field]
        dirtyField[Dirty Bit Field]
        refField[Reference Bit Field]
        protField[Protection Bits Field RWX]
        asidField[ASID Field Optional Process ID]
    end
    camA -. compares against .-> tagField
    matchSig -. selects .-> pfnField
    ptWalk --> memLookup[Memory Lookup Unit]
    memLookup --> memA([Main Memory])
```

### 4.3 TLB Organization Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1([TLB Organizations]) --> fa[Fully Associative TLB]
    root1 --> sa[Set Associative TLB]
    root1 --> dm[Direct Mapped TLB]
    fa --> faDesc[Each entry holds any VPN. Search all entries in parallel. Most flexible.]
    sa --> saDesc[Entries grouped into sets. VPN selects set then searched within. Balance of speed and complexity.]
    dm --> dmDesc[Each VPN maps to exactly one slot. Fastest but highest conflict misses.]
    faDesc --> use1[Used in high performance CPUs e.g. recent Intel and AMD cores]
    saDesc --> use2[Most common design point in modern desktop CPUs]
    dmDesc --> use3[Rarely used for TLB due to high miss rate]
```

### 4.4 Memory Access Timeline Comparison

```mermaid
gantt
    title TLB Hit vs TLB Miss Memory Access Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section TLB Hit Path
    TLB Lookup     :a1, 0, 1
    Memory Access :a2, after a1, 1
    section TLB Miss Path
    TLB Lookup     :b1, 0, 1
    Page Table Access :b2, after b1, 1
    Memory Access for Data :b3, after b2, 1
```

> **Reading the Timeline:** The hit path takes 2 access units. The miss path takes 3 access units (the extra unit being the page-table walk in main memory). This is the **visual proof** of why high TLB hit ratios are critical for performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB). Why is it required in a paged memory management system?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) is a small, fast, fully-associative hardware cache residing inside the Memory Management Unit (MMU) that stores recently used virtual-to-physical page number translations, namely VPN to PFN mappings.
>
> **Why Required (1.5 Marks):** Without TLB, every memory access requires an additional reference to the in-memory page table to translate the virtual address, which effectively doubles the memory access time. TLB accelerates this translation by serving most lookups from hardware, drastically improving system performance and reducing the effective memory access time.

---
