> A **File Descriptor (FD)** is a non-negative integer index (typically a small positive integer such as **0**, **1**, **2**, ...) maintained by the Operating System kernel inside the **Process Control Block (PCB)** of every running process. It acts as an opaque handle that uniquely identifies an **open file** (or socket, pipe, or device) for that process and is used as a reference key by all subsequent I/O system calls such as `read()`, `write()`, `lseek()`, and `close()`.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, the file descriptor is the **lowest-level abstraction** through which a user process interacts with the underlying file system. It is the *index* into three interrelated kernel data structures:

1. **Per-Process File Descriptor Table**
2. **System-Wide Open File Table**
3. **In-Memory Inode (v-node / i-node) Table**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Standard File Descriptors (POSIX Reserved)**
> - **FD 0 → `stdin`** (Standard Input) — connected to the keyboard
> - **FD 1 → `stdout`** (Standard Output) — connected to the terminal
> - **FD 2 → `stderr`** (Standard Error) — connected to the terminal
>
> These three are **automatically opened** by the kernel for every new process (by `fork()` and inherited via `exec()`).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Hotel Room Key Analogy**
>
> Imagine you walk into a luxury hotel (the **Operating System**). The hotel has many rooms (the **files on disk**). You go to the reception desk and ask for a room. The receptionist does *not* hand you the room itself — that would be absurd! Instead, the receptionist gives you a **plastic keycard with a unique room number** printed on it.
>
> This keycard is your **File Descriptor**.
> - The **room number printed on the card** = the integer value of the FD (e.g., 3, 4, 5).
> - The **physical room itself** = the actual file data on the disk.
> - The **hotel registry** = the kernel's Open File Table.
> - **Swiping the card** = invoking a system call (`read`/`write`).
> - **Returning the card at checkout** = calling `close(fd)`.
>
> You can have **many keycards** (multiple FDs) for the **same room** (same file opened twice) or for **different rooms** (different files). The hotel always knows who is holding which key.

A more precise CS analogy:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Library Borrowing Slip Analogy**
> A library (the OS) has millions of books (files on disk). When you borrow a book, the librarian gives you a **borrowing slip with a ticket number** (the FD). You use this ticket number every time you want to **read, extend your loan, or return** the book. The librarian maintains a master register (Open File Table) mapping ticket numbers to actual book identities. The ticket itself is meaningless without the librarian's register.

---

## 1.3 Why File Descriptors Exist — The Design Rationale

The kernel introduces file descriptors to solve **three fundamental engineering problems**:

| Engineering Problem | How FD Solves It |
|---|---|
| **Process Isolation** | A process cannot directly access another process's open files. FDs are scoped per-process inside the PCB. |
| **Abstraction / Hiding Disk Geometry** | The user sees only an integer; the kernel handles cylinders, sectors, blocks, and caching transparently. |
| **Uniform Interface** | The same `read(fd, buf, n)` call works for regular files, pipes, sockets, terminals, and devices. This is the **UNIX philosophy: everything is a file.** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**
> Students often confuse a **File Descriptor** with a **File Pointer (FILE \*)**. In C, `FILE *fp` is a **library-level (glibc) abstraction** that internally wraps a file descriptor. The FD is a *kernel concept*; `FILE *` is a *user-space library concept*. KTU exam questions frequently test this distinction.

---

## 1.4 Relationship Between FD, File Pointer, and Inode

A clear hierarchy exists in UNIX-like systems (which is what KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes):

```
User Program (C / Python)
        │
        │  uses
        ▼
   FILE *  (glibc stream — buffered I/O, e.g., fopen, fprintf)
        │
        │  internally contains
        ▼
   File Descriptor (small integer — unbuffered I/O, e.g., open, read)
        │
        │  indexes into
        ▼
   Open File Description (kernel struct file)
        │
        │  points to
        ▼
   Inode (on-disk metadata: permissions, size, block pointers)
```

This three-tier architecture is what gives UNIX its famed **uniformity and extensibility**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Kernel Data Structure

When a process opens a file via the `open()` system call, the kernel creates/updates **three distinct tables**. Understanding the interaction between these tables is a **favourite KTU exam topic** and appears frequently in Module 4 questions.

### Layer 1: Per-Process File Descriptor Table

- Located **inside the Process Control Block (PCB)** of each process.
- Each entry contains:
  - A set of **file descriptor flags** (`close-on-exec`, non-blocking, etc.)
  - A **pointer** to an entry in the system-wide Open File Table.
- Array index = the file descriptor number.
- Size is **process-dependent**; the soft limit is usually **1024**, hard limit is **1048576** (Linux).

### Layer 2: System-Wide Open File Table

- **One global table** for the entire kernel.
- Each entry is a structure often called `struct file` in Linux.
- Contains:
  - **Current file offset** (the read/write pointer position) — this is the most important field.
  - **Status flags** (`O_RDONLY`, `O_WRONLY`, `O_RDWR`, `O_APPEND`, `O_NONBLOCK`).
  - **Reference count** (how many FDs across all processes point to this entry).
  - Pointer to the **inode table entry**.

### Layer 3: In-Memory Inode (v-node) Table

- Also **global** to the kernel.
- Each entry is a copy/extension of the on-disk inode, loaded into RAM when the file is first accessed.
- Contains:
  - **File type** (regular, directory, character device, block device, FIFO, socket, symlink).
  - **Permissions** (rwx for owner / group / others).
  - **File size**, **timestamps** (atime, mtime, ctime).
  - **Pointers to data blocks** on disk.
- Reference count tracks how many Open File Table entries point to it.

---

## 2.2 The Complete Lifecycle of a File Descriptor

### Step-by-Step Operation

1. **`open(pathname, flags, mode)`** System Call
   - Kernel allocates the **lowest-numbered unused** integer in the FD table.
   - Creates a new entry in the **Open File Table** with `offset = 0`, `refcount = 1`.
   - Loads/caches the **inode** from disk.
   - Returns the integer FD to user space.

2. **`read(fd, buffer, count)`** System Call
   - Kernel uses `fd` as index into the **per-process FD table**.
   - Follows pointer to the **Open File Table entry** to find the **current offset**.
   - Follows inode pointer to find the **data blocks**.
   - Copies `count` bytes from disk (or page cache) into `buffer`.
   - **Advances the offset** by the number of bytes actually read.
   - Returns the number of bytes read.

3. **`write(fd, buffer, count)`** System Call
   - Similar to `read`, but writes data and advances the offset forward.
   - If `O_APPEND` flag is set, the kernel first sets the offset to the end-of-file.

4. **`lseek(fd, offset, whence)`** System Call
   - Manipulates the **current offset** in the Open File Table entry **without** doing any I/O.
   - `whence` can be `SEEK_SET`, `SEEK_CUR`, or `SEEK_END`.

5. **`dup(fd)` / `dup2(oldfd, newfd)`** System Call
   - Creates a **new FD** that points to the **same Open File Table entry**.
   - Both FDs **share the same file offset** (because they share the Open File entry).
   - Reference count of the Open File entry is incremented.

6. **`fork()`** System Call
   - The child process gets a **copy of the parent's FD table**.
   - Both parent and child FDs point to the **same Open File Table entries**.
   - Reference count of those entries is incremented.
   - Both processes share the **same file offset**.

7. **`close(fd)`** System Call
   - Removes the entry from the per-process FD table.
   - Decrements the reference count of the Open File Table entry.
   - If the reference count reaches **zero**, the Open File Table entry is freed.
   - If the inode's link count also reaches zero, the file may be deleted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of FD Reference Counting**
> The actual on-disk file is only released (data blocks freed) when **both** the Open File Table reference count AND the directory link count reach zero. This is why files remain on disk after `close()` if other hard links exist, and why open file descriptors survive even after the file is `unlink()`-ed from the directory.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the high-yield formulas, rules, and constants for File Descriptor problems. **Memorize this for ESE.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Default FD limit (soft)** | `RLIMIT_NOFILE` = **1024** | FDs per process (Linux default) |
| **Default FD limit (hard)** | `RLIMIT_NOFILE` = **1048576** | Max FDs (Linux ≥ 2.6.x) |
| **Reserved FDs** | `0, 1, 2` | `stdin, stdout, stderr` |
| **First user FD** | **3** | Smallest value returned by `open()` |
| **Total kernel state for one open file** | 1 FD table entry + 1 Open File entry + 1 Inode entry | Memory cost in kernel |
| **Offset after `read(fd, buf, n)`** | `offset_{new} = offset_{old} + n` | Bytes (signed) |
| **Offset after `lseek(fd, k, SEEK_SET)`** | `offset = k` | Absolute |
| **Offset after `lseek(fd, k, SEEK_CUR)`** | `offset = offset + k` | Relative |
| **Offset after `lseek(fd, k, SEEK_END)`** | `offset = file\_size + k` | Relative to EOF |
| **Number of FDs after `dup(fd)`** | `N_{new} = N_{old} + 1` | Same offset, same inode |
| **Reference count after `fork()`** | `refcount_{new} = refcount_{old} + 1` | For every open FD |
| **Minimum FDs a process must have** | **≥ 3** | Because of `stdin, stdout, stderr` |
| **`STDIN_FILENO` macro** | `0` | Defined in `<unistd.h>` |
| **`STDOUT_FILENO` macro** | `1` | Defined in `<unistd.h>` |
| **`STDERR_FILENO` macro** | `2` | Defined in `<unistd.h>` |

> [!TIP]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember**
> - `read()` returns **0** at **End Of File (EOF)**.
> - `read()` returns **-1** on **error** (and sets `errno`).
> - `open()` returns **-1** on failure.
> - An FD value of **-1** is **never valid** and is conventionally used to mean "no FD" (e.g., after `close()`).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | How File Descriptors Are Used |
|---|---|
| **Web Servers (Nginx, Apache)** | Each client socket connection gets a unique FD. A single server may handle 10,000+ concurrent FDs using `epoll()`. |
| **Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL)** | Tablespace files, WAL logs, and socket connections are all managed via FDs. `ulimit -n` tuning is critical. |
| **Container Runtimes (Docker)** | `/proc/<pid>/fd/` reveals the FDs of containerized processes. Seccomp and capability rules restrict FD-related syscalls. |
| **Logging Frameworks** | Log files are kept open as FDs and held even when the file is `unlink`-ed and recreated (the inode is preserved). |
| **IPC Mechanisms** | Pipes, FIFOs, and Unix domain sockets are all exposed as FDs. |
| **Profiling & Debugging** | Tools like `lsof`, `strace`, `/proc/<pid>/fd` are essential for diagnosing "too many open files" errors. |
| **Embedded Systems** | Limited FDs force careful resource management; some RTOSes limit FDs to **8 or 16**. |

---

## 2.5 Comparison Table: File Descriptor vs File Pointer vs Filename

| Property | Filename | File Descriptor (FD) | File Pointer (`FILE *`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | String (e.g., `"/etc/passwd"`) | Non-negative integer (e.g., `3`) | Pointer to a `FILE` struct |
| **Layer** | Human / path namespace | Kernel | User-space C library (glibc) |
| **Created by** | User/programmer | `open()`, `socket()`, `pipe()` | `fopen()` |
| **Used by** | Shells, scripts | System calls: `read, write, lseek` | Library calls: `fread, fwrite, fprintf` |
| **Buffered?** | N/A | **No** (unbuffered I/O) | **Yes** (has internal buffer) |
| **Closed by** | N/A | `close(fd)` | `fclose(fp)` |
| **Inheritable on `fork`?** | N/A | **Yes** (inherited) | Yes (inherited) |
| **Persistent across `exec`?** | N/A | Only if `O_CLOEXEC` is **not** set | Only if `freopen` is configured |
| **Visible in `/proc/<pid>/fd/`** | No | **Yes** | No (lives in user space) |
| **Used in KTU exams?** | As a path argument | **As the central concept** | As a distractor / comparison |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Tracing FD Allocation

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A C program executes the following sequence of operations. What are the file descriptor values returned at each step, assuming no errors occur and the standard descriptors `0, 1, 2` are already open?

```c
int fd1, fd2, fd3, fd4;
fd1 = open("a.txt", O_RDONLY);          // Step 1
fd2 = open("b.txt", O_RDWR);            // Step 2
fd3 = open("c.txt", O_WRONLY | O_CREAT, 0644);  // Step 3
close(fd1);                             // Step 4
fd4 = open("d.txt", O_RDONLY);          // Step 5
```

### Step-by-Step Analytical Trace

Let `FD_TABLE(p)` denote the set of currently open file descriptors in the per-process table for process `p`.

**Step 0 (Initial State):**
The kernel has automatically opened the three standard streams during process creation.

$$
FD\_TABLE(p) = \{0, 1, 2\}
$$

Where:
- $0$ → `stdin`
- $1$ → `stdout`
- $2$ → `stderr`

**Step 1: `fd1 = open("a.txt", O_RDONLY)`**

The kernel scans the FD table and finds that the **lowest unused** integer is **3**. A new Open File Table entry is created (call it `OFT[1]`) with `offset = 0`, `refcount = 1`, and a fresh inode for `a.txt` is loaded into the Inode Table (call it `INODE[a]`).

$$
FD\_TABLE(p) = \{0, 1, 2, 3\}
$$
$$
\boxed{fd1 = 3}
$$

**Step 2: `fd2 = open("b.txt", O_RDWR)`**

The lowest unused integer is now **4**. A new Open File Table entry `OFT[2]` is created and linked to a new inode `INODE[b]`.

$$
FD\_TABLE(p) = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}
$$
$$
\boxed{fd2 = 4}
$$

**Step 3: `fd3 = open("c.txt", O_WRONLY | O_CREAT, 0644)`**

The lowest unused integer is **5**. The `O_CREAT` flag tells the kernel to create `c.txt` on disk if it does not exist. Mode `0644` = `rw-r--r--`. New `OFT[3]` and `INODE[c]` are allocated.

$$
FD\_TABLE(p) = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$
$$
\boxed{fd3 = 5}
$$

**Step 4: `close(fd1)` → `close(3)`**

The kernel removes entry `3` from `FD_TABLE(p)`, decrements the reference count of `OFT[1]`. Since the reference count becomes **0**, `OFT[1]` is freed. However, `INODE[a]` may still exist in the inode cache (for re-opening later).

$$
FD\_TABLE(p) = \{0, 1, 2, 4, 5\}
$$

**Step 5: `fd4 = open("d.txt", O_RDONLY)`**

The lowest **unused** integer is now **3** (the slot freed in Step 4). A new Open File Table entry and inode are allocated.

$$
FD\_TABLE(p) = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}
$$
$$
\boxed{fd4 = 3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway (KTU favorite question):**
> `fd1` and `fd4` are **both equal to 3** because `open()` always returns the **lowest-numbered unused** descriptor. The previous FD 3 was freed by `close()` in between.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Offset Sharing After `dup()` and `fork()`

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A process opens a file and obtains `fd = 3`. It then performs 10 bytes of `read()` and 5 bytes of `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_CUR)`. After this, the process calls `dup(3)` to obtain `fd_new`, and the child (after `fork()`) reads 20 bytes using `fd_new`. **What is the final offset visible to the parent?**

### Step-by-Step Analytical Trace

Let $O_x$ denote the current file offset in the Open File Table entry that all these FDs point to.

**Initial state (after `open` returns 3):**

$$
O_x = 0
$$

**After `read(fd=3, buf, 10)` returns 10:**

$$
O_x = O_x + n_{read} = 0 + 10 = 10
$$

**After `lseek(fd=3, 0, SEEK_CUR)`:**

The `SEEK_CUR` whence means "relative to current offset". So:

$$
O_x = O_x + k = 10 + 0 = 10
$$

(Effectively, this is a no-op query that returns 10, which can be confirmed via the return value of `lseek`.)

**After `dup(3)` → returns `fd_new` (say 4):**

The kernel creates a new entry in the per-process FD table, both `fd=3` and `fd=4` now point to the **same** Open File Table entry. The reference count of that entry becomes **2**, but the **offset is shared**.

$$
O_x = 10, \quad \text{refcount} = 2
$$

**After `fork()`:**

The child process gets a **copy** of the parent's FD table, but both FDs in parent and child point to the **same Open File Table entry**. The reference count becomes **3**.

$$
O_x = 10, \quad \text{refcount} = 3
$$

**After child performs `read(fd=4, buf, 20)`:**

The offset in the shared Open File Table entry advances by 20:

$$
O_x = O_x + 20 = 10 + 20 = 30
$$

**The parent observes the new offset as 30** if it queries via `lseek(fd=3, 0, SEEK_CUR)`, because **all FDs sharing the same Open File entry see the same offset**.

> [!WARNING]
> **This is a classic KTU trap.** Many students think the parent and child have *independent* offsets. They do not — they share the Open File Table entry, and therefore share the offset. **Independent offsets only occur if `O_APPEND` is set or if separate `open()` calls are made.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: The "File Still Exists After `rm`" Mystery

> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A process opens a file `log.txt` and obtains `fd = 3`. While the file is still open, another process (or the same shell) executes `rm log.txt`. Later, the process writes 100 bytes via `write(3, buf, 100)`. **Will the write succeed? What happens?**

### Step-by-Step Analytical Trace

**State after `open("log.txt")`:**
- Per-process FD table: contains `3 → OFT[1]`
- System Open File Table: `OFT[1]` has `refcount = 1`, points to `INODE[log.txt]`
- Inode: `link_count = 1` (one directory entry, `log.txt`)

**`rm log.txt` is invoked:**
- The `unlink()` system call removes the directory entry for `log.txt`.
- The kernel **decrements the inode's link_count** → `link_count = 0`.
- However, `OFT[1]` still references the inode, and its `refcount` is still **1**.

$$
\text{File data is preserved while refcount}_{OFT} > 0 \text{ OR link\_count} > 0
$$

**Result:** The directory entry is gone (`ls` will not show it), but the inode and its data blocks are **kept alive in memory** because `OFT[1]` still points to them.

**After `write(3, buf, 100)`:**
- The write proceeds normally. The 100 bytes are written into the inode's data blocks.
- The file size is updated.
- **The data is safe on disk** even though no directory entry exists.

**After the process finally calls `close(3)`:**
- `refcount` of `OFT[1]` drops to **0**.
- The Open File Table entry is freed.
- The inode's link_count is already 0 and the OFT refcount is 0 → **the kernel reclaims the inode and its data blocks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is precisely why log-rotation utilities (like `logrotate`) work safely.** They rename or delete the open log file, then send a `SIGHUP` to the daemon, which re-opens the file. The daemon can keep writing to the old (now unlinked) FD without losing data, because the data blocks survive until `close()`.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common System Calls and Their FD Impact

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **rapid reference for KTU 2024 ESE problems** on File Descriptors. Print this in your mind.

| System Call | FD Table | Open File Table | Inode Table | Offset | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `open(path, flags)` | New entry | New entry (refcount = 1) | Loaded/cached | Set to **0** | Returns lowest free FD |
| `read(fd, buf, n)` | Unchanged | Unchanged | Unchanged | `+= n_returned` | Returns bytes read, or 0 at EOF |
| `write(fd, buf, n)` | Unchanged | Unchanged | Updated (size, mtime) | `+= n` | May grow file |
| `lseek(fd, k, S)` | Unchanged | Offset changed | Unchanged | Set per whence | Returns new offset |
| `close(fd)` | Entry removed | `refcount -= 1` | Freed if refcount=0 AND link=0 | — | FD number becomes reusable |
| `dup(fd)` | New entry → same OFT | `refcount += 1` | Unchanged | **Shared** | Atomic operation |
| `dup2(old, new)` | `new` points to same OFT | `refcount += 1` (and old `new` closed) | Unchanged | Shared | Used in shell redirection |
| `fork()` | **Copied** to child | `refcount += N` (one per FD) | Unchanged | **Shared** | Parent & child see same offset |
| `exec()` | Unchanged (unless `O_CLOEXEC`) | Unchanged | Unchanged | Unchanged | Standard FDs 0,1,2 preserved |
| `pipe(fds[2])` | Two new FDs | Two new OFT entries | Special pipe inode | 0 each | `fds[0]` = read end, `fds[1]` = write end |
| `socket()` | New FD | New OFT entry | Socket inode | 0 | For network communication |
| `unlink(path)` | Unchanged | Unchanged | `link_count -= 1` | Unchanged | File may persist if FD open |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Layer Kernel Data Structure (Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Description:** This diagram illustrates how a **single file descriptor (e.g., FD 3)** in a process's PCB links through the kernel's three-table architecture to the actual on-disk file. This is the single most important diagram for KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PCB["PROCESS P CONTROL BLOCK"]
        FDTable["PER PROCESS FILE DESCRIPTOR TABLE\nIndex | Pointer to OFT\n0   ->  OFT STDIN\n1   ->  OFT STDOUT\n2   ->  OFT STDERR\n3   ->  PTR TO OFT 7\n4   ->  NULL\n5   ->  NULL"]
    end

    subgraph OFT["SYSTEM WIDE OPEN FILE TABLE"]
        OFTstdin["OFT STDIN\nOffset 0\nFlags O RDONLY\nRefcount 1"]
        OFTstdout["OFT STDOUT\nOffset 0\nFlags O WRONLY\nRefcount 1"]
        OFTstderr["OFT STDERR\nOffset 0\nFlags O WRONLY\nRefcount 1"]
        OFT7["OFT 7\nOffset 0\nFlags O RDWR\nRefcount 2\nTHIS SHARED BY FD 3 IN P AND FD 4 IN Q"]
    end

    subgraph IT["IN MEMORY INODE TABLE"]
        IODev["INODE DEV TTY\nType Character Device\nMajor 4 Minor 1"]
        IOFile["INODE FILE LOG TXT\nType Regular File\nSize 1024 bytes\nMode 0644\nLink Count 1\nBlock Ptrs 1024 2048 4096"]
    end

    subgraph DISK["ON DISK STORAGE"]
        DISKLog["DATA BLOCKS OF LOG TXT\nOn sectors 1024 2048 4096"]
    end

    FDTable -- "index 3 dereferences" --> OFT7
    FDTable -- "index 0" --> OFTstdin
    FDTable -- "index 1" --> OFTstdout
    FDTable -- "index 2" --> OFTstderr
    OFT7 -- "vnode pointer" --> IOFile
    OFTstdin -- "vnode pointer" --> IODev
    OFTstdout -- "vnode pointer" --> IODev
    OFTstderr -- "vnode pointer" --> IODev
    IOFile -- "block pointers" --> DISKLog
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram in your KTU exam answer:**
> 1. Start at the **per-process FD table** (top).
> 2. Note the **index = the FD value**.
> 3. Follow the **pointer** to the **Open File Table entry** (middle).
> 4. Follow the **v-node pointer** to the **Inode** (lower middle).
> 5. The inode's **block pointers** finally reach the **disk data**.

---

## 4.2 Lifecycle State Machine of a File Descriptor

> [!NOTE]
> **Description:** A state machine showing how an FD transitions through its lifecycle within a single process. Useful for explaining `open`, `read`, `write`, `lseek`, and `close` interactions in viva and ESE.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FreeSlot: Process starts with FDs 0, 1, 2 open

    FreeSlot --> Allocated: open or dup or pipe or socket returns integer
    Allocated --> Allocated: read or write or lseek or fcntl
    Allocated --> Allocated: dup creates new FD pointing to same OFT
    Allocated --> Allocated: fork copies FD to child process

    Allocated --> FreeSlot: close fd is called
    Allocated --> FreeSlot: process exits and kernel auto closes all FDs
    Allocated --> Allocated: exec reinitializes if O CLOEXEC is set

    FreeSlot --> Allocated: next open or dup reuses this slot

    state Allocated {
        [*] --> PositionZero
        PositionZero --> PositionN: read or write advances offset
        PositionN --> PositionN: more I O
        PositionN --> PositionK: lseek with SEEK SET or SEEK CUR
        PositionK --> PositionZero: lseek with SEEK SET 0
    }
```

---

## 4.3 FD Behavior Across `fork()` and `dup()` (Comparison Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Description:** A side-by-side comparison of how file descriptors behave after `fork()` vs `dup()`. Both share the Open File Table entry, but in different ways.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE OPERATION"]
        P1["Process P\nFD Table:\n3 -> OFT A\nOFT A:\nOffset 10\nRefcount 1\nInode log txt"]
    end

    subgraph AFTERDUP["AFTER dup 3 IN PROCESS P"]
        P2["Process P\nFD Table:\n3 -> OFT A\n4 -> OFT A\nOFT A:\nOffset 10\nRefcount 2\nInode log txt"]
    end

    subgraph AFTERFORK["AFTER fork IN PROCESS P"]
        P3["Parent P\nFD Table:\n3 -> OFT A\nOFT A:\nOffset 10\nRefcount 2"]
        Q3["Child Q\nFD Table:\n3 -> OFT A\nOFT A SHARED\nOffset 10\nRefcount 2"]
    end

    BEFORE -- "dup 3 call" --> AFTERDUP
    BEFORE -- "fork call" --> AFTERFORK

    P3 -.- Q3
```

---

## 4.4 Shell Redirection and FD Plumbing

> [!NOTE]
> **Description:** A diagram showing how the shell uses `dup2()` to implement I/O redirection such as `command > file.txt`. This is a KTU viva favorite.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHELL["SHELL EXECUTES: ls > output.txt"]
        SH1["Step 1: open output.txt returns FD 3\nFD 3 -> OFT for output.txt (writable)"]
        SH2["Step 2: dup2 3 1\nFD 1 in child now points to OFT for output.txt"]
        SH3["Step 3: close 3\nFD 3 freed"]
        SH4["Step 4: exec ls\nls process inherits FDs 0 1 2\nBut FD 1 now writes to output.txt"]
        SH1 --> SH2 --> SH3 --> SH4
    end

    subgraph BEFORE_REDIR["BEFORE REDIRECTION"]
        BR1["Child FD Table:\n0 -> keyboard\n1 -> terminal\n2 -> terminal"]
    end

    subgraph AFTER_REDIR["AFTER REDIRECTION"]
        AR1["Child FD Table:\n0 -> keyboard\n1 -> output.txt (was terminal)\n2 -> terminal"]
    end

    BEFORE_REDIR -- "after dup2 3 1" --> AFTER_REDIR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is precisely how the UNIX shell implements I/O redirection.** The shell uses `dup2()` to *re-point* the existing standard descriptors (0, 1, 2) to new files before calling `exec()`. The child process never knows its `stdout` has been redirected.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Define a file descriptor. Why is it preferred over using the file's path name for I/O operations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **file descriptor (FD)** is a non-negative integer that the kernel uses as an index into the per-process file descriptor table to identify an open file. Every time a file is opened via `open()`, the kernel returns the lowest unused integer (starting from 3, since 0, 1, 2 are reserved for `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr`).

It is preferred over the file's path name for I/O operations for **three reasons**:

1. **Performance:** Path name lookup requires resolving directories and traversing the file system tree on every access. An FD is a direct integer index — an O(1) operation.
2. **State preservation:** The FD maintains the current file offset and access flags in the kernel, so the user does not need to specify them repeatedly.
3. **Security and abstraction:** The process only sees an integer; the kernel hides the underlying inode, disk location, and file system internals. It also prevents the process from accessing files it has not explicitly opened.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: Differentiate between a file descriptor and a `FILE *` pointer in C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | File Descriptor (FD) | `FILE *` Pointer |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer** | Kernel-level abstraction | User-space C library (glibc) abstraction |
| **Type** | `int` (small non-negative integer) | Pointer to a `FILE` struct |
| **System calls** | `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `close()` | `fopen()`, `fread()`, `fwrite()`, `fclose()` |
| **Buffering** | **Unbuffered** — every call goes to the kernel | **Buffered** — data is staged in a user-space buffer |
| **Performance** | Slower for small I/O due to syscall overhead | Faster for small I/O due to buffering |
| **Visibility** | Visible in `/proc/<pid>/fd/` | Not visible in proc filesystem |

The `FILE *` pointer **wraps** a file descriptor internally; you can extract the FD from a `FILE *` using `fileno(fp)`.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for layer distinction, 1 mark for type, 1 mark for buffering difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. **Answer BOTH sub-parts.**

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain the three kernel data structures involved in representing an open file in UNIX: the per-process file descriptor table, the system-wide open file table, and the in-memory inode table. How do they interact when a file is opened, read, and closed?
>
> **(b)** A process performs the following sequence of operations. Trace the file descriptor values returned and explain the state of the FD table and open file table at each step. Assume no errors occur.

```c
int a, b, c, d, e;
a = open("alpha", O_RDONLY);                    // Line 1
b = open("beta",  O_RDWR);                       // Line 2
c = dup(a);                                       // Line 3
d = open("gamma", O_WRONLY | O_CREAT, 0644);     // Line 4
close(b);                                         // Line 5
e = open("delta", O_RDONLY);                     // Line 6
```

#### Model Solution to (a) [7 marks]

The UNIX kernel uses **three interlinked tables** to manage open files:

**1. Per-Process File Descriptor Table** (1.5 marks)
- Located inside the **Process Control Block (PCB)** of each process.
- It is essentially an array where the **index is the file descriptor number**.
- Each entry contains:
  - A set of **file descriptor flags** (e.g., `FD_CLOEXEC`).
  - A **pointer** to an entry in the system-wide open file table.
- This table is **private to the process** — different processes have different tables, and one process cannot directly access another's FDs.
- The kernel automatically opens entries `0`, `1`, and `2` for every new process (for `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr`).

**2. System-Wide Open File Table** (2 marks)
- A **single global table** maintained by the kernel.
- Each entry (often called `struct file` in Linux) represents one **instance of an open file**.
- It contains:
  - The **current file offset** — this is the read/write pointer.
  - The **status flags** used to open the file (`O_RDONLY`, `O_WRONLY`, `O_RDWR`, `O_APPEND`, `O_NONBLOCK`).
  - The **reference count** — how many file descriptors (across all processes) point to this entry.
  - A **pointer to the in-memory inode** entry.
- Crucially, **multiple FDs can share a single Open File Table entry** (e.g., after `dup` or `fork`).

**3. In-Memory Inode (v-node) Table** (1.5 marks)
- Also a **global** kernel table.
- Each entry is a copy/extension of an on-disk inode, loaded into RAM on demand.
- Contains **file metadata**: file type, permissions, size, timestamps, link count, and pointers to data blocks on disk.
- Has its own reference count tracking how many Open File Table entries point to it.

**Interaction during the file lifecycle** (2 marks):

- **`open()`**: The kernel allocates the lowest unused FD, creates a new Open File Table entry with `offset = 0` and `refcount = 1`, loads the inode from disk, and returns the FD.
- **`read()`**: The kernel uses the FD to look up the Open File Table entry, follows the offset, fetches data via the inode's block pointers, copies it to user space, and advances the offset.
- **`close()`**: The FD slot is freed, the Open File Table entry's refcount is decremented. If it reaches 0, the entry is freed, and the inode's link count is checked; if both are 0, the file's data blocks are reclaimed.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) [7 marks]

Let `FD(p)` be the set of open FDs in process `p`, and let `OFT[i].refcount` denote the reference count of Open File Table entry `i`.

| Line | Operation | FD(p) After Operation | Notes / OFT State |
|---|---|---|---|
| (Start) | Initial | `{0, 1, 2}` | `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr` |
| **1** | `a = open("alpha", O_RDONLY)` | `{0, 1, 2, 3}` | `a = 3`. New `OFT[α]`, refcount = 1 |
| **2** | `b = open("beta", O_RDWR)` | `{0, 1, 2, 3, 4}` | `b = 4`. New `OFT[β]`, refcount = 1 |
| **3** | `c = dup(a)` → `c = dup(3)` | `{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}` | `c = 5`. **No new OFT entry**; FD 5 points to same `OFT[α]`, refcount becomes **2** |
| **4** | `d = open("gamma", O_WRONLY \| O_CREAT, 0644)` | `{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}` | `d = 6`. New `OFT[γ]`, refcount = 1 |
| **5** | `close(b)` → `close(4)` | `{0, 1, 2, 3, 5, 6}` | `OFT[β].refcount` becomes 0 → `OFT[β]` freed |
| **6** | `e = open("delta", O_RDONLY)` | `{0, 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 4}` | `e = 4` (lowest unused, since `b`'s slot was freed). New `OFT[δ]`, refcount = 1 |

**Final answers:** $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $c = 5$, $d = 6$, $e = 4$.

**Key insights for full marks (1 mark):**
- `dup()` does **not** create a new Open File Table entry — both FDs share the same offset.
- `e` reuses the slot freed by `close(b)`, hence the value 4 appears twice.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain how the `fork()` and `dup()` system calls affect file descriptors, open file entries, and the file offset. Use a clear diagram in your explanation. What is the practical significance of the shared offset in a parent-child process scenario?
>
> **(b)** Consider a process that opens a file `data.bin` and obtains `fd = 3`. It then performs a `fork()`. The parent writes 50 bytes starting at the current position. The child then seeks 10 bytes forward and writes 30 bytes. **What is the final file size, and what is the offset visible to the parent after the child writes?** Explain your answer with reference to the kernel data structures.

#### Model Solution to (a) [7 marks]

**`fork()` and File Descriptors** (3.5 marks)

When a process calls `fork()`:
1. The kernel creates a **new PCB** for the child.
2. The **entire per-process File Descriptor Table of the parent is duplicated** (shallow copy — only the array of pointers is copied, not the underlying Open File entries).
3. For **every open FD** in the parent, the **reference count of the corresponding Open File Table entry is incremented by 1**.
4. The **file offset is shared** because both parent and child FDs point to the same Open File Table entry.

**`dup()` and File Descriptors** (2 marks)

`dup(fd)` creates a **new FD in the same process** that points to the **same** Open File Table entry. The reference count is incremented, and the offset is again shared. If `dup2(oldfd, newfd)` is used, the kernel first closes `newfd` (if open) and then makes `newfd` point to the same OFT entry as `oldfd`.

**Comparison Table for clarity** (1 mark):

| Aspect | `fork()` | `dup()` |
|---|---|---|
| Number of processes | 2 (parent + child) | 1 (same process) |
| New FD created? | Yes, in child | Yes, in same process |
| New OFT entry? | No (shared) | No (shared) |
| Reference count | Increased by N (one per FD) | Increased by 1 |
| Offset shared? | **Yes** | **Yes** |

**Practical Significance** (0.5 marks):
- The shared offset is exploited by **parent-child pipes** and **preload libraries**.
- It enables the classic UNIX pattern where a parent sets up a pipe and then forks; both ends can read/write without confusion.
- It also means I/O is **not independent** between parent and child unless they use separate `open()` calls or `O_APPEND` mode.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) [7 marks]

**Initial state (after `open("data.bin")`):**

- `FD(p) = {0, 1, 2, 3}` where `fd = 3`.
- `OFT[data.bin].offset = 0`, `OFT[data.bin].refcount = 1`.
- The inode for `data.bin` is loaded; assume initial file size = **0 bytes** (or whatever the problem states).

**After `fork()`:**
- Child gets a copy of the FD table: `FD(c) = {0, 1, 2, 3}`.
- Both parent and child FD 3 point to the **same OFT entry**.
- `OFT[data.bin].refcount = 2`.
- The **offset is shared**: `OFT[data.bin].offset = 0` for both.

**Parent performs `write(3, buf, 50)`:**
- The kernel reads the current offset from `OFT[data.bin]`: `offset = 0`.
- Writes 50 bytes starting at byte 0.
- Advances the shared offset: `OFT[data.bin].offset = 50`.
- File size on disk: **50 bytes** (if it was 0 initially).
- The child observes the same offset (50) if it queries via `lseek`.

**Child performs `lseek(3, 10, SEEK_CUR)`:**
- `SEEK_CUR` = relative to current.
- New shared offset: `OFT[data.bin].offset = 50 + 10 = 60`.

**Child performs `write(3, buf, 30)`:**
- Writes 30 bytes starting at the current shared offset 60.
- These bytes occupy file positions **60, 61, ..., 89** (a total of 30 bytes).
- Advances the shared offset: `OFT[data.bin].offset = 60 + 30 = 90`.
- **File size on disk: max(50, 89+1) = 90 bytes**.
- Note: positions 50–59 remain as **null bytes / sparse hole** in the file (this is a sparse file).

**Final answers (2 marks):**
- **Final file size = 90 bytes.**
- **Offset visible to the parent = 90** (because offset is shared in the OFT entry).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Where students lose marks on this question type:**
> 1. **Treating parent and child as having independent offsets.** They do not — they share the OFT entry. Award 0 if the student says "child writes don't affect parent".
> 2. **Ignoring sparse-file behavior.** When the child writes at offset 60 after the parent's 50-byte write, the gap from 50 to 59 is a sparse hole. Some students incorrectly say "the file size is 50 + 30 = 80" (wrong) or "the write overwrites the parent's data" (also wrong).
> 3. **Confusing `O_APPEND`.** If `O_APPEND` were set, every write would atomically seek to EOF before writing, preventing the hole. The problem does not specify `O_APPEND`, so the sparse hole scenario applies.
> 4. **Forgetting to update reference count after `fork()`.** Always mention the refcount change to get full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram.** A KTU 14-mark question **demands a diagram** of the FD/OFT/Inode linkage. Even a rough hand-drawn diagram earns 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the KTU ESE.**

- [x] A **File Descriptor (FD)** is a **non-negative integer** that indexes into the per-process FD table.
- [x] FDs **0, 1, 2** are reserved for `stdin`, `stdout`, `stderr` respectively.
- [x] The **lowest unused** integer is returned by `open()`.
- [x] **Three kernel data structures** are involved: **Per-Process FD Table**, **System Open File Table**, and **In-Memory Inode Table**.
- [x] The **Open File Table** holds the **current file offset**; the **Inode Table** holds the **file metadata**.
- [x] `dup()` and `fork()` both **share the same OFT entry**, hence the **same offset** and **same inode**.
- [x] `close()` decrements the OFT reference count; when it reaches 0, the OFT entry is freed.
- [x] The file's **data blocks are freed** only when both the **OFT refcount** and the **inode link count** are 0.
- [x] `rm` (i.e., `unlink`) removes the **directory entry** and decrements the **link count**, but the file persists while a process holds it open.
- [x] `lseek()` **does not** perform I/O; it only modifies the offset in the OFT.
- [x] `O_APPEND` flag causes every write to atomically seek to EOF.
- [x] `O_CLOEXEC` flag causes the FD to be closed automatically on `exec()`.
- [x] The default **soft limit** on FDs per process in Linux is **1024**; the hard limit is **1048576**.
- [x] `read()` returns **0 at EOF** and **-1 on error**.
- [x] `open()` returns **-1 on failure**.
- [x] `FILE *` (glibc) is a **user-space abstraction** that **wraps** an FD; they are **not** the same thing.
- [x] Shell redirection is implemented using **`dup2()`** to re-point FDs 0, 1, 2 before `exec()`.
- [x] The **/proc/<pid>/fd/** directory shows all FDs open by a process.
- [x] **Seek operations are not allowed on pipes, sockets, or FIFOs** — only on regular files (KTU favourite viva question).
- [x] A **File Descriptor Flag** (per-FD) is different from a **File Status Flag** (per-OFT-entry). `FD_CLOEXEC` is the former; `O_APPEND` is the latter.
- [x] `dup2(oldfd, newfd)` is **atomic**; equivalent `close(newfd) + dup(oldfd)` is **not** atomic (race condition).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw a small FD-table diagram in your answer, even if not explicitly asked. Examiners award easy 1–2 marks for clear diagrams that show FD index, OFT pointer, and inode linkage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading and Writing Files (Sequential and Random Access)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reading and Writing Files (Sequential and Random Access)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of File Access Modes

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Operating System Concepts** syllabus, file access refers to the methodology employed by an operating system to read from and write to files stored in secondary memory. The two principal paradigms are:

### 🔹 Sequential File Access
**Sequential Access** is a file access methodology in which data is read or written in a linear, contiguous, forward-only order, beginning from a default starting position (the **Beginning-of-File**, BoF) or from the current file pointer position, and proceeding byte-by-byte (or record-by-record) until an **End-of-File (EOF)** condition is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Sequential access is the *default* access mode in operating systems like UNIX and Windows. The file pointer advances automatically by the number of bytes read or written — the OS kernel manages this offset transparently.

### 🔹 Random (Direct) File Access
**Random Access** (also called **Direct Access**) is a file access methodology in which the file pointer can be repositioned to any arbitrary byte offset within the file, allowing non-linear, out-of-order reading or writing of data blocks. This is achieved through dedicated system calls such as `lseek()` (POSIX) or `SetFilePointer()` (Win32).

| Parameter | Sequential Access | Random Access |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Default Pointer Movement | Forward (auto-increment) | Bidirectional (manual control) |
| Typical Use Case | Log files, streaming, tapes | Databases, indexing systems, ISAM |
| Read/Write Granularity | Byte/Record stream | Block/Offset-driven |
| Performance with Large Files | $O(n)$ linear scan | $O(1)$ direct seek |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Bookshelf vs. The Cassette

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition — Making it Stick!**

Imagine two completely different media for storing information:

🎞️ **Sequential Access = A Cassette Tape / VHS**
To reach your favorite song on a cassette tape, you must **fast-forward** (or rewind) through every preceding song. There is no "skip to track 7" button. The read head has a *current position*, and you can only move forward (or rewind fully to start). The order is fixed and linear.

📀 **Random Access = A CD / DVD / Vinyl Record**
On a CD player, you press **"Track 7"** and the laser jumps *instantly* to that exact physical location. The order of listening does not matter. Each track has an **addressable offset** (e.g., 2 minutes 14 seconds), and you can navigate to any offset in roughly equal time — regardless of file size.

**In OS terminology:** The cassette tape = `fgetc()` loop reading character by character until EOF. The CD = `fseek()` + `fread()` jumping to byte offset 4096 directly.

## 1.3 File Pointer Abstraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — The File Pointer (FP)**
> A **file pointer** is a kernel-managed integer variable (offset in bytes) associated with every open file descriptor. It denotes the **current read/write cursor position** within the file. Every successful `read()` advances the pointer by the number of bytes transferred; every successful `write()` advances it by the number of bytes written.

**Standard C Library Stdio Mapping:**

| Stdio Function (C) | Underlying System Call (POSIX) | Access Mode |
|--------------------|--------------------------------|-------------|
| `fopen()` | `open()` | Initialization |
| `fread()` / `fscanf()` | `read()` | Sequential read |
| `fwrite()` / `fprintf()` | `write()` | Sequential write |
| `fseek()` | `lseek()` | Random positioning |
| `ftell()` | `lseek(SEEK_CUR, 0)` | Query position |
| `feof()` | EOF flag check | End detection |
| `fclose()` | `close()` | Deallocation |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Standard Block Size** in UNIX file systems (ext4, UFS): **4096 bytes (4 KiB)**.
- **Maximum file size** on 32-bit systems: $2^{32} - 1 = \textbf{4,294,967,295 bytes} \approx \textbf{4 GiB}$.
- **Maximum file size** on 64-bit systems: $2^{63} - 1$ bytes (theoretically $\approx 9.22 \times 10^{18}$ bytes).
- **Standard PIPE buffer size** (Linux): **65,536 bytes (64 KiB)**.
- **Average HDD seek time**: ~**8–10 ms** (mechanical latency) — this is why random access is *still* more expensive than sequential on HDDs.
- **SSD random access latency**: ~**0.1 ms** — random access becomes nearly as fast as sequential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The UNIX File Descriptor Model

When a process issues `open()`, the kernel creates an entry in the **per-process file descriptor table** pointing to a **system-wide open file table** entry, which in turn points to an **inode** (the on-disk file metadata). The file pointer is stored within the open file table entry.

### 🔸 System Call Breakdown

#### 1️⃣ `open()` — File Initialization
```c
#include <fcntl.h>
int open(const char *pathname, int flags, mode_t mode);
```
- `O_RDONLY` = 0, `O_WRONLY` = 1, `O_RDWR` = 2
- `O_CREAT`, `O_TRUNC`, `O_APPEND`, `O_RANDOM`, `O_SEQUENTIAL` (Windows-specific hint)

#### 2️⃣ `read()` — Sequential Byte Stream Read
```c
ssize_t read(int fd, void *buf, size_t count);
```
Returns the number of bytes **actually** read (may be < `count` on EOF or signal interruption). The file pointer auto-advances.

#### 3️⃣ `write()` — Sequential Byte Stream Write
```c
ssize_t write(int fd, const void *buf, size_t count);
```
Returns the number of bytes written. May be < `count` if disk is full or interrupted.

#### 4️⃣ `lseek()` — Random Access Repositioning
```c
off_t lseek(int fd, off_t offset, int whence);
```
- `SEEK_SET` (0): Set pointer to `offset` bytes from beginning.
- `SEEK_CUR` (1): Set pointer to current position + `offset`.
- `SEEK_END` (2): Set pointer to `offset` bytes from end of file.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** `lseek()` does **not** read or write any data — it is a *purely metadata* operation that updates only the file pointer stored in the kernel's open file table. It is the only standard system call that enables true random access in UNIX.

## 2.2 The Whence Logic — KTU Critical Concept

The `whence` parameter defines the **reference point** for the new file pointer position. Let:
- $P_{\text{new}}$ = New file pointer position
- $P_{\text{cur}}$ = Current file pointer position
- $L$ = Total length of the file (in bytes)
- $\delta$ = The `offset` argument passed to `lseek()`

The three valid combinations are governed by these equations:

$$
P_{\text{new}} = \begin{cases} \delta & \text{if whence = SEEK_SET} \\ P_{\text{cur}} + \delta & \text{if whence = SEEK_CUR} \\ L + \delta & \text{if whence = SEEK_END} \end{cases}
$$

**Boundary Conditions (KTU Frequently Asked):**
- $\delta \geq 0$ for `SEEK_SET` and `SEEK_END` (negative values cause `EINVAL`).
- $|P_{\text{new}}|$ must not exceed the maximum file size limit (`ulimit -f`).
- Seeking **past end-of-file** with subsequent `write()` creates a **"hole"** (sparse file) — the OS allocates zero-filled blocks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Function | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|-------------------|------------|----------------------|
| $f_p$ | File pointer (current offset) | Bytes $[0, L]$ |
| $L$ | File length | Bytes |
| $\delta$ | Offset argument to `lseek()` | Bytes (signed) |
| `SEEK_SET` | Reference = BoF | $\delta \in [0, L]$ |
| `SEEK_CUR` | Reference = current FP | $\delta \in [-f_p, L - f_p]$ |
| `SEEK_END` | Reference = EOF | $\delta \in [-L, 0]$ typically |
| $n_b$ | Bytes transferred per `read/write` call | $\leq L$ |
| $T_{\text{seq}}$ | Sequential read time | $\frac{L}{R_{\text{transfer}}}$ |
| $T_{\text{rand}}$ | Random seek time | $T_{\text{seek}} + \frac{n_b}{R_{\text{transfer}}}$ |
| $R_{\text{transfer}}$ | Disk transfer rate | ~100–500 MB/s (SSD) |
| $T_{\text{seek}}$ | Average seek latency | **~8 ms (HDD)**, **~0.1 ms (SSD)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students write `lseek(fd, offset, SEEK_SET)` assuming it is zero-indexed **inclusive** — it is actually zero-indexed, meaning `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_SET)` puts the pointer at the **first byte** (index 0), not before it.

## 2.4 Sequential vs. Random: When to Use What?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**

- **Sequential access** dominates in:
  - **Log files** (systemd journals, Apache access logs) — append-only.
  - **Streaming media** (audio/video playback) — forward-only decoding.
  - **Data pipeline processing** (Hadoop MapReduce input phase).
  - **Backup utilities** (`tar`, `dd`) — entire-file copy.

- **Random access** is essential in:
  - **Database engines** (B-tree indexes, hash-based record retrieval).
  - **Virtual memory paging** (page-in/page-out at arbitrary virtual addresses).
  - **Compilers and linkers** (object file symbol tables — seek to specific offset).
  - **Video editing software** (timeline scrubbing).
  - **Filesystem journaling** (recovery seeks to specific journal entries).

**Performance Trade-off Equation (Time to Read a Block):**

$$
T_{\text{seq}} = \frac{n_b}{R_{\text{transfer}}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{rand}} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rotational}} + \frac{n_b}{R_{\text{transfer}}}
$$

For HDDs, $T_{\text{rand}} \gg T_{\text{seq}}$ due to mechanical seek. For SSDs, $T_{\text{rand}} \approx T_{\text{seq}}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Deriving the New File Pointer Position

**Problem Statement (Model KTU 14-Mark Style):**
A file of length $L = 1500$ bytes is opened. The initial file pointer is at position $f_p = 200$ bytes. The following sequence of operations is executed:

1. `read(fd, buf, 100)` — reads 100 bytes
2. `lseek(fd, 50, SEEK_CUR)` — advances 50 bytes
3. `write(fd, data, 250)` — writes 250 bytes
4. `lseek(fd, -300, SEEK_END)` — seeks 300 bytes before EOF
5. `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_SET)` — resets to beginning

**Derive the file pointer position after each operation.**

---

### 🔸 Step 0: Initial State

$$
f_p^{(0)} = 200 \text{ bytes}, \quad L^{(0)} = 1500 \text{ bytes}
$$

> Initial pointer is at byte offset 200 (i.e., the 201st byte in 1-indexed terms).

---

### 🔸 Step 1: `read(fd, buf, 100)`

**Logic:** A `read()` of 100 bytes advances the file pointer by exactly 100 bytes (assuming all bytes are available and no EOF is reached).

$$
f_p^{(1)} = f_p^{(0)} + n_b^{\text{read}} = 200 + 100 = 300 \text{ bytes}
$$

**File length remains unchanged:**
$$
L^{(1)} = L^{(0)} = 1500 \text{ bytes}
$$

> ✅ **KTU Validation:** $f_p^{(1)} = 300 \leq L^{(1)} = 1500$ — pointer is **valid** (within file).

---

### 🔸 Step 2: `lseek(fd, 50, SEEK_CUR)`

**Logic:** Using the `SEEK_CUR` equation:
$$
P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{cur}} + \delta
$$
Substituting values:
$$
f_p^{(2)} = f_p^{(1)} + 50 = 300 + 50 = 350 \text{ bytes}
$$

> ✅ **Validation:** $f_p^{(2)} = 350 \leq 1500$ — still within bounds.

---

### 🔸 Step 3: `write(fd, data, 250)`

**Logic:** A `write()` of 250 bytes advances the file pointer by 250 bytes AND extends the file length by 250 bytes (since we are writing at the **end** of existing content in this case — note: position 350 + 250 = 600, which is still within the original file, so the length stays the same in this specific example; but if writing past EOF, length would grow).

Since $f_p^{(2)} + 250 = 350 + 250 = 600 \leq 1500$:
$$
f_p^{(3)} = f_p^{(2)} + 250 = 350 + 250 = 600 \text{ bytes}
$$
$$
L^{(3)} = L^{(1)} = 1500 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(no length change, as 600 < 1500)}
$$

> ⚠️ **KTU Note:** If the write had occurred past EOF (e.g., if $f_p^{(2)} = 1400$ and we wrote 250 bytes), the file length would grow to $L^{(3)} = 1400 + 250 = 1650$ bytes. This is a classic KTU 14-mark trap question.

---

### 🔸 Step 4: `lseek(fd, -300, SEEK_END)`

**Logic:** Using the `SEEK_END` equation:
$$
P_{\text{new}} = L + \delta
$$
Here, $\delta = -300$ (negative, indicating *before* EOF):
$$
f_p^{(4)} = L^{(3)} + (-300) = 1500 - 300 = 1200 \text{ bytes}
$$

> ✅ **Validation:** $f_p^{(4)} = 1200 \in [0, 1500]$ — within bounds.

---

### 🔸 Step 5: `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_SET)`

**Logic:** Using the `SEEK_SET` equation:
$$
P_{\text{new}} = \delta = 0
$$
$$
f_p^{(5)} = 0 \text{ bytes}
$$

> The pointer is now reset to the very beginning of the file (BoF).

---

## 3.2 Final Analytical Matrix

| Step | Operation | Equation Used | Resulting $f_p$ (bytes) | Resulting $L$ (bytes) |
|------|-----------|---------------|-------------------------|-----------------------|
| 0 | Initial state | — | 200 | 1500 |
| 1 | `read()` 100 bytes | $f_p' = f_p + n_b$ | **300** | 1500 |
| 2 | `lseek(50, CUR)` | $P = f_p + \delta$ | **350** | 1500 |
| 3 | `write()` 250 bytes | $f_p' = f_p + n_b$ | **600** | 1500 |
| 4 | `lseek(-300, END)` | $P = L + \delta$ | **1200** | 1500 |
| 5 | `lseek(0, SET)` | $P = \delta$ | **0** | 1500 |

---

## 3.3 Sparse File (Hole) Creation — KTU Advanced Derivative

**Problem:** After Step 3 above, suppose the file pointer was at $f_p = 1490$ and we executed `write(fd, data, 100)`. Derive the resulting file layout.

**Step-by-Step:**

1. Bytes 0 to 1489 (1490 bytes total) = **Existing file data**.
2. Bytes 1490 to 1589 (100 bytes) = **Newly written data**.
3. Total file length:
$$
L' = 1490 + 100 = 1590 \text{ bytes}
$$
4. Final file pointer:
$$
f_p' = 1490 + 100 = 1590 \text{ bytes (at new EOF)}
$$

**Sparse File Case (Hole Creation):** If we had done `lseek(fd, 2000, SEEK_SET)` and then `write(fd, data, 50)`, the file would extend to $L = 2050$ bytes, but the 460 bytes between 1500 and 2000 are **logically zero** (a "hole") and may **not consume any physical disk blocks** in modern filesystems like ext4 or NTFS.

$$
L_{\text{sparse}} = L_{\text{original}} + (P_{\text{seek}} - L_{\text{original}}) + n_b^{\text{written}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** Sparse files are a common **4-mark short answer** in Module 4 exams. Always mention that they save disk space because the OS uses **extent mapping** to track only non-zero regions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture: File Descriptor and Open File Table Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Process A File Descriptor Table] -->|fd 0| E1[Entry 0 stdin]
    P1 -->|fd 1| E2[Entry 1 stdout]
    P1 -->|fd 2| E3[Entry 2 stderr]
    P1 -->|fd 3| E4[Entry 3 user file]

    P2[Process B File Descriptor Table] -->|fd 0| E1
    P2 -->|fd 1| E2
    P2 -->|fd 2| E3
    P2 -->|fd 4| E4

    E1 --> OFT1[Kernel Open File Table Entry 1]
    E2 --> OFT2[Kernel Open File Table Entry 2]
    E3 --> OFT3[Kernel Open File Table Entry 3]
    E4 --> OFT4[Kernel Open File Table Entry 4]

    OFT1 --> INODE1[Inode 1024 file pointer 0 ref count 3]
    OFT2 --> INODE2[Inode 2048 tty ref count 3]
    OFT3 --> INODE3[Inode 4096 tty ref count 3]
    OFT4 --> INODE4[Inode 8192 file pointer 1500 ref count 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Process A and Process B can share the **same kernel open file table entry** (e.g., after `fork()`). The `file pointer` is stored in the kernel's open file table entry, NOT in the process table. This is why a `lseek()` in one process changes the position visible to the other (when shared).

---

## 4.2 Sequential vs. Random Access Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start open file]) --> OP[Open file using open or fopen]
    OP --> DECIDE{Choose access mode}

    DECIDE -->|Sequential| SEQ[Initialize file pointer to 0 or EOF for append]
    SEQ --> LOOP{More data to process}
    LOOP -->|Yes| READ[Call read or fread]
    READ --> ADV[File pointer auto advances by n bytes]
    ADV --> LOOP
    LOOP -->|No| CLOSE1[Close file]

    DECIDE -->|Random| RND[Initialize file pointer to 0]
    RND --> SEEK[Call lseek or fseek to target offset]
    SEEK --> ROP{Read or Write at offset}
    ROP -->|Read| RREAD[Call read at current position]
    ROP -->|Write| RWRITE[Call write at current position]
    RREAD --> RSEEK2{Need another position}
    RWRITE --> RSEEK2
    RSEEK2 -->|Yes| SEEK
    RSEEK2 -->|No| CLOSE2[Close file]

    CLOSE1 --> END([End])
    CLOSE2 --> END
```

---

## 4.3 State Diagram of File Pointer Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BoF: open file
    BoF: Beginning of File offset 0
    BoF --> Mid: read or write n bytes
    Mid: Middle offset f p
    Mid --> Mid: sequential read write
    Mid --> Mid: lseek SEEK CUR or SEEK SET
    Mid --> BoF: lseek 0 SEEK SET
    Mid --> EoF: lseek 0 SEEK END
    Mid --> EoF: sequential write past current end
    EoF: End of File offset L
    EoF --> Mid: lseek negative offset SEEK END
    EoF --> Beyond: lseek positive offset SEEK END creates sparse hole
    Beyond: Beyond EOF offset greater than L
    Beyond --> Mid: lseek SEEK SET to valid offset
    Beyond --> [*]: close file
    EoF --> [*]: close file
    Mid --> [*]: close file
    BoF --> [*]: close file
```

---

## 4.4 Disk Access Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SEQ[Sequential Access Pattern]
        S0[Block 0] --> S1[Block 1]
        S1 --> S2[Block 2]
        S2 --> S3[Block 3]
        S3 --> S4[Block 4]
    end

    subgraph RAND[Random Access Pattern]
        R0[Block 0] -.-> R7[Block 7]
        R7 -.-> R2[Block 2]
        R2 -.-> R9[Block 9]
        R9 -.-> R1[Block 1]
    end

    style SEQ fill:#90EE90
    style RAND fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Insight:** The green (sequential) path represents head movement in a smooth, contiguous sweep — minimizing **seek time**. The red (random) path shows scattered, jumping head movement — each jump incurs the costly **average seek latency** $T_{\text{seek}}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define sequential file access. How does the file pointer behave during sequential read/write operations?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Sequential File Access** is a file access method in which data records are accessed one after another in a linear order, starting from the current file pointer position and progressing forward (or backward only by rewinding completely) until the end-of-file is reached.

**Behavior of the file pointer:**
- The file pointer is **automatically advanced** by the OS kernel after every `read()` or `write()` system call.
- The advancement equals the number of bytes successfully transferred.
- The pointer is initialized to **0 (BoF)** for `O_RDONLY`/`O_WRONLY`/`O_RDWR` modes, and to **end-of-file** for `O_APPEND` mode.
- No explicit repositioning is performed by the user program — control is implicit in the kernel.

**Example code:**
```c
int fd = open("data.txt", O_RDONLY);
char buf[100];
read(fd, buf, 100);  // Pointer auto-advances from 0 to 100
read(fd, buf, 100);  // Pointer auto-advances from 100 to 200
```

---

### **Question 2: Distinguish between sequential and random file access. Give one example use case for each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Sequential Access | Random Access |
|-------|-------------------|---------------|
| Pointer Movement | Automatic, forward-only | Manual, bidirectional via `lseek()` |
| Time to Access Nth Record | $O(N)$ — must read all preceding | $O(1)$ — direct seek to offset |
| Underlying Hardware Analogy | Magnetic tape | Magnetic disk / SSD |
| Code Example | `fgetc()` loop | `fseek()` + `fread()` |

**Sequential example use case (1 mark):** Reading a system log file `/var/log/syslog` line-by-line for analysis — we always proceed forward.

**Random example use case (1 mark):** A database engine (e.g., MySQL InnoDB) accessing a specific row in a table — it computes the byte offset from the primary key index and seeks directly.

---

## 📚 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing `SEEK_CUR` with `SEEK_SET` — leads to off-by-one errors in pointer position.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that `lseek()` itself does **not** trigger I/O — students incorrectly claim that `lseek` reads data.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In sparse file derivations, failing to account for the **logical zero-fill** of the hole region.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** For `O_APPEND` mode, students forget the kernel *atomically* repositions the pointer to EOF before every `write()`.
> - **Mark Loss Pattern:** Students who skip the "boundary validation" step (checking $0 \leq f_p \leq L$) lose **2 marks** in 14-mark derivations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**Consider a file `records.dat` of length $L = 2000$ bytes. The file is opened in `O_RDWR` mode with initial file pointer $f_p = 0$. The following sequence of operations is performed:**

1. `read(fd, buf, 500)` — read 500 bytes
2. `lseek(fd, 300, SEEK_CUR)` — seek 300 forward
3. `write(fd, data1, 200)` — write 200 bytes
4. `lseek(fd, -400, SEEK_END)` — seek 400 bytes before EOF
5. `read(fd, buf, 100)` — read 100 bytes
6. `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_SET)` — reset to BoF
7. `write(fd, data2, 50)` — write 50 bytes
8. `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_END)` — query file size

**(a)** Derive the file pointer position and file length after **each** of the 8 operations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the difference between sequential and random file access. State the role of the `lseek()` system call in enabling random access. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for (a) — [7 marks]**

**Step 0: Initial state** [0.5 mark]
$$
f_p^{(0)} = 0, \quad L^{(0)} = 2000
$$

**Step 1: `read(fd, buf, 500)`** [0.5 mark]
Logic: `read()` advances $f_p$ by bytes read.
$$
f_p^{(1)} = 0 + 500 = 500, \quad L^{(1)} = 2000
$$
✅ Valid: $500 \leq 2000$.

**Step 2: `lseek(fd, 300, SEEK_CUR)`** [0.5 mark]
Logic: $P = f_p + \delta$
$$
f_p^{(2)} = 500 + 300 = 800, \quad L^{(2)} = 2000
$$
✅ Valid: $800 \leq 2000$.

**Step 3: `write(fd, data1, 200)`** [1 mark]
Logic: `write()` advances $f_p$ by bytes written. Since $800 + 200 = 1000 \leq 2000$, file length is **unchanged**.
$$
f_p^{(3)} = 800 + 200 = 1000, \quad L^{(3)} = 2000
$$
✅ Valid: $1000 \leq 2000$.

**Step 4: `lseek(fd, -400, SEEK_END)`** [1 mark]
Logic: $P = L + \delta = 2000 + (-400) = 1600$
$$
f_p^{(4)} = 1600, \quad L^{(4)} = 2000
$$
✅ Valid: $1600 \in [0, 2000]$.

**Step 5: `read(fd, buf, 100)`** [0.5 mark]
Logic: $f_p$ advances by 100.
$$
f_p^{(5)} = 1600 + 100 = 1700, \quad L^{(5)} = 2000
$$
✅ Valid.

**Step 6: `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_SET)`** [0.5 mark]
Logic: $P = \delta = 0$
$$
f_p^{(6)} = 0, \quad L^{(6)} = 2000
$$

**Step 7: `write(fd, data2, 50)`** [0.5 mark]
Logic: $f_p$ advances by 50; length unchanged (since $0 + 50 = 50 \leq 2000$).
$$
f_p^{(7)} = 0 + 50 = 50, \quad L^{(7)} = 2000
$$

**Step 8: `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_END)`** [0.5 mark]
Logic: $P = L + 0 = 2000$
$$
f_p^{(8)} = 2000, \quad L^{(8)} = 2000
$$
**Final Result:** The file size is exactly 2000 bytes; the file pointer is at EOF.

**Summary Table for (a):** [1.5 marks]

| Step | Op | $f_p$ (bytes) | $L$ (bytes) |
|------|----|---------------|-------------|
| 0 | init | 0 | 2000 |
| 1 | read 500 | 500 | 2000 |
| 2 | lseek +300 CUR | 800 | 2000 |
| 3 | write 200 | 1000 | 2000 |
| 4 | lseek -400 END | 1600 | 2000 |
| 5 | read 100 | 1700 | 2000 |
| 6 | lseek 0 SET | 0 | 2000 |
| 7 | write 50 | 50 | 2000 |
| 8 | lseek 0 END | 2000 | 2000 |

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for (b) — [7 marks]**

**Definition of Sequential Access [1.5 marks]:**
Sequential access is the method of accessing file data in a linear, forward-only order, where the file pointer auto-advances after each `read()` or `write()` call. Data is processed in the order it is stored, with no ability to skip ahead without reading intermediate data. Typical example: reading a text file character-by-character using `fgetc()` in a loop until `feof()` is true.

**Definition of Random Access [1.5 marks]:**
Random (direct) access is the method of accessing file data at arbitrary byte offsets, where the file pointer can be repositioned explicitly by the programmer using `lseek()` (POSIX) or `fseek()` (C stdio) before each read/write operation. This allows $O(1)$ access to any byte, regardless of file size. Typical example: a database engine reading a specific record by computing its byte offset from the row ID.

**Neat Comparison Diagram [2 marks]:**

```
    SEQUENTIAL ACCESS (Tape Model)         RANDOM ACCESS (Disk Model)
    ============================           ============================
    BoF                                    BoF
     |                                      |
     v                                      v
    [B0]->[B1]->[B2]->[B3]->[B4]->EOF      [B0] [B1] [B2] [B3] [B4]
     ^                                      ^              ^
     |                                      |              |
    Must traverse B0, B1, B2, B3          Direct seek     Direct seek
    to reach B4                            to B2            to B4
    Time: O(N)                             Time: O(1)
```

**Role of `lseek()` in Enabling Random Access [2 marks]:**
The `lseek()` system call is the **only** mechanism in the POSIX API that allows a process to **explicitly reposition** the file pointer to an arbitrary offset without performing any I/O. It accepts three "whence" modes:

- `SEEK_SET`: Set pointer absolutely from BoF.
- `SEEK_CUR`: Set pointer relative to current position.
- `SEEK_END`: Set pointer relative to EOF.

`lseek()` works by updating the `f_pos` field in the kernel's open file table entry. Once repositioned, subsequent `read()` or `write()` calls operate at the new offset, effectively giving the process the ability to perform out-of-order file access — the defining feature of random access. Without `lseek()`, every file I/O would be strictly sequential.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Answer any ONE of the following:**

**(a)** With a suitable C program, explain how **sequential file access** is implemented using `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, and `close()` system calls. Show the byte-by-byte flow of data and the file pointer behavior. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **random file access** mechanism using the `lseek()` system call. Write a C program that uses `lseek()` to **read the middle 100 bytes** of a file of size 1000 bytes, and **modify the last 50 bytes** in place. **[7 marks]**

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for (a) — [7 marks]**

**C Program for Sequential File Access** [4 marks]:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>

int main() {
    int fd;
    char write_buf[] = "Hello, KTU Operating Systems!";
    char read_buf[100];
    ssize_t bytes;

    // Step 1: Open file for read-write, create if doesn't exist
    fd = open("seqfile.txt", O_RDWR | O_CREAT | O_TRUNC, 0644);
    if (fd < 0) {
        perror("open failed");
        return 1;
    }

    // Step 2: Sequential WRITE
    bytes = write(fd, write_buf, strlen(write_buf));
    printf("Written %zd bytes, FP now at %ld\n", bytes, (long)lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_CUR));

    // Step 3: Reset to BoF for sequential READ
    lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_SET);

    // Step 4: Sequential READ in a loop
    while ((bytes = read(fd, read_buf, 10)) > 0) {
        printf("Read %zd bytes, FP at %ld: %.*s\n",
               bytes, (long)lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_CUR), (int)bytes, read_buf);
    }

    // Step 5: Close
    close(fd);
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Written 28 bytes, FP now at 28
Read 10 bytes, FP at 10: Hello, KTU
Read 10 bytes, FP at 20:  Operating
Read 8 bytes, FP at 28:  Systems!
```

**Explanation of byte-by-byte flow** [3 marks]:

1. **`open()`**: Creates/opens the file. Returns file descriptor `fd = 3` (after stdin=0, stdout=1, stderr=2). The kernel sets the file pointer to **0** (BoF).

2. **`write()`**: Writes 28 bytes from `write_buf` to the file. The OS kernel **automatically advances** the file pointer from 0 to 28. The file is now 28 bytes long.

3. **`lseek(0, SEEK_SET)`**: Resets pointer to 0 (BoF) — necessary because write left pointer at EOF.

4. **`read()` in a loop**: Each `read()` of 10 bytes returns the next chunk and advances the file pointer by 10. The loop terminates when `read()` returns **0** (EOF condition).

5. **`close()`**: Releases the file descriptor, decrements the inode's reference count, and flushes buffered data to disk.

**Key Point:** Notice how the file pointer **auto-advances** by exactly the number of bytes transferred — no manual pointer management is needed. This is the defining characteristic of sequential access.

---

#### ✅ **Model Solution for (b) — [7 marks]**

**C Program for Random File Access with `lseek()`** [5 marks]:

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <fcntl.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>

int main() {
    int fd;
    char middle_buf[101] = {0};
    char new_tail[] = "MODIFIED-BY-KTU-EXAM-2024-PAD-PADDING";

    // Open existing 1000-byte file
    fd = open("data.bin", O_RDWR);
    if (fd < 0) {
        perror("open");
        return 1;
    }

    // ---- TASK 1: Read middle 100 bytes ----
    // Middle of 1000-byte file starts at offset 450 (i.e., bytes 450-549)
    if (lseek(fd, 450, SEEK_SET) == (off_t)-1) {
        perror("lseek to middle");
        return 1;
    }

    ssize_t n = read(fd, middle_buf, 100);
    if (n < 0) {
        perror("read middle");
        return 1;
    }
    middle_buf[n] = '\0';
    printf("Middle 100 bytes (FP now at %ld):\n%.*s\n",
           (long)lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_CUR), (int)n, middle_buf);

    // ---- TASK 2: Modify last 50 bytes in place ----
    // Last 50 bytes = file size 1000, so start at offset 950
    if (lseek(fd, 950, SEEK_SET) == (off_t)-1) {
        perror("lseek to end");
        return 1;
    }

    ssize_t w = write(fd, new_tail, 50);
    if (w < 0) {
        perror("write tail");
        return 1;
    }
    printf("Wrote %zd bytes at tail. FP at %ld\n",
           w, (long)lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_CUR));

    close(fd);
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of the `lseek()` mechanism** [2 marks]:

1. **`lseek(fd, 450, SEEK_SET)`**: This sets the file pointer to byte offset 450. The kernel updates `f_pos = 450` in the open file table entry. No data is transferred; this is a *purely metadata* operation.

2. **`read(fd, middle_buf, 100)`**: Reads 100 bytes starting at offset 450. Returns bytes at positions 450, 451, ..., 549. The file pointer auto-advances to 550.

3. **`lseek(fd, 950, SEEK_SET)`**: Jumps directly to offset 950, **bypassing** bytes 550–949 entirely. This is the *defining* feature of random access.

4. **`write(fd, new_tail, 50)`**: Overwrites bytes 950–999 (the last 50 bytes) in place. File length remains 1000 (in-place modification).

**Comparison Highlight:** Had we used sequential access, we would have had to read and discard bytes 0–949 before we could write to position 950 — wasting I/O bandwidth. Random access with `lseek()` allows **direct targeting** of any offset in $O(1)$ time.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — File I/O)**

- ✅ **Sequential access** = pointer auto-advances; default mode; no manual positioning.
- ✅ **Random access** = pointer explicitly set via `lseek()`; supports `SEEK_SET`, `SEEK_CUR`, `SEEK_END`.
- ✅ **`lseek()` does NOT read/write data** — it only updates the kernel's file pointer.
- ✅ **Three `whence` modes**:
  - `SEEK_SET`: $P = \delta$ (absolute from BoF).
  - `SEEK_CUR`: $P = f_p + \delta$ (relative to current).
  - `SEEK_END`: $P = L + \delta$ (relative to EOF).
- ✅ **Boundary rule:** $0 \leq f_p \leq L$ for valid pointer; seeking past EOF creates a **sparse file (hole)**.
- ✅ **`read()` returns 0 at EOF** — this is the **only** reliable way to detect end-of-file (NOT the return of `-1`).
- ✅ **`write()` past current EOF extends the file** — file length grows to $f_p + n_b^{\text{written}}$.
- ✅ **`O_APPEND` flag** atomically sets pointer to EOF **before every write** — prevents race conditions in concurrent appends.
- ✅ **File descriptor table** is per-process; **Open file table** is kernel-wide; **Inode table** is filesystem-wide.
- ✅ **Time complexity:** $T_{\text{seq}} = \frac{n_b}{R}$ vs. $T_{\text{rand}} = T_{\text{seek}} + \frac{n_b}{R}$.
- ✅ **HDD seek cost ~8 ms; SSD seek cost ~0.1 ms** — random access is dramatically more efficient on SSDs.
- ✅ **Sparse files** save disk space via extent-based mapping — holes are logical zeros, not physical zeros.
- ✅ **C stdio `fseek()` uses `SEEK_SET/SEEK_CUR/SEEK_END`** with values 0, 1, 2 — same semantics as POSIX `lseek()`.
- ✅ **`ftell()` returns the current file pointer position** — equivalent to `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_CUR)`.
- ✅ **Buffering:** `fread()`/`fwrite()` use a user-space buffer (default 4–8 KB); `read()`/`write()` are unbuffered system calls.
- ✅ **Return values:** `read()` returns `ssize_t` (bytes read, 0 = EOF, -1 = error); `lseek()` returns the new offset or `(off_t)-1` on error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Removing Files - Hard Links and Symbolic Links
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Removing Files - Hard Links and Symbolic Links # 📘 Module 4 — Removing Files: Hard Links and Symbolic Links ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview In a Unix/Linux-style file system, **"removing" a file is technically called *unlinking***. The system never deletes data the moment a user types `rm`; it follows a strict, reference-counted protocol governed by the **inode (index node)** data structure. Understanding this distinction is the single most important conceptual step in Module 4. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** > A file is said to be *removed* when its **directory entry is deleted**. The actual disk blocks are reclaimed by the file system **only when the inode's link count drops to zero and no process holds it open**. Hard links and symbolic links are two fundamentally different mechanisms for creating multiple references to the same underlying data. ### 🔹 What is a Hard Link? A **hard link** is a *second (or third, or Nth) directory entry that points directly to the same inode* as the original file. Both filenames share **identical metadata, identical permissions, and identical data blocks** because they *are the same inode* — they simply have different names in the directory hierarchy. > [!NOTE] > **Formally:** A hard link is a directory entry of type *regular file* whose `inode` field equals the `inode` field of another directory entry. The kernel increments the inode's `i_nlink` (link count) field each time a hard link is created. ### 🔹 What is a Symbolic Link (Symlink)? A **symbolic link** (or *soft link*) is a *special file of type `S_IFLNK`* whose contents store a **pathname string** that the kernel must resolve every time the symlink is accessed. The symlink has **its own independent inode** and its own metadata. > [!NOTE] > **Formally:** A symbolic link is a file whose data is not user content but a *target pathname*. The kernel intercepts `read()`, `open()`, and `stat()` calls and transparently redirects them to the target file or directory. --- ### 🌟 Conceptual Analogy — The House and the Signboard Imagine your data blocks are a **physical house** sitting on a plot of land (the inode). The inode is the *land deed* — it uniquely identifies the house and counts how many different *mailboxes* point to it. | Mechanism | Real-World Analogy | |-----------|-------------------| | **Original filename** | A house with a *single mailbox* marked "Main House" | | **Hard link** | A *second, third, or Nth mailbox* at the same gate — they all open the *same* house. Removing one mailbox does NOT demolish the house. | | **Symbolic link** | A *separate, tiny signboard* somewhere in the town that says *"Go to Main House"*. The signboard has its own little deed (its own inode). If the original house is demolished, the signboard becomes a **dangling sign pointing to nothing** — that's a *dangling symlink*. | > [!TIP] > **Intuition Shortcut:** *Hard link = same house, multiple doors. Symbolic link = a paper note that says "go to the house on 5th Street".* ### 🔹 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 - It is a **direct syllabus item** in Module 4 (File Systems / File Management). - It is a **favourite 14-mark ESE question**, typically phrased as *"Explain hard links and symbolic links with diagrams. What happens when a file is removed?"* - It tests **CO (Course Outcome) mapping**: typically **CO3 / CO4** depending on the official course outcome sheet — *Apply file system concepts and link management*. - The cognitive level is **Apply / Analyze (L3 / L4)** on Bloom's Revised Taxonomy. ### 🔹 Key Metrics You Must Memorize - **Hard link count** of a fresh file: **1** (the original directory entry). - **Maximum hard links to a single inode**: Limited by the inode's link count storage — practically **65,000+** on modern ext4, but the *POSIX minimum* is **LINK_MAX ≥ 8**. - **Symlink target length limit**: **4096 bytes** on most Linux file systems (the symlink's data block can hold one pathname). - **Hard link restriction**: ❌ **Cannot** cross file system boundaries. ❌ **Cannot** point to a directory (in standard POSIX; *only the superuser can in some systems via `.` and `..` hacks*). - **Symbolic link flexibility**: ✅ Can cross file systems. ✅ Can point to directories. ✅ Can point to non-existent targets (becomes *dangling*). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Inode — The Heart of File Removal Every file in a Unix file system is described by an **inode (index node)**, a kernel-resident data structure containing *everything about the file except its name*. The directory itself only stores `` mappings.
