A simplified inode contains the following critical fields (relevant to our topic): | Inode Field | Purpose | Relevance to Link/Removal | |-------------|---------|----------------------------| | `i_mode` | File type + permissions | Identifies regular file, symlink, directory | | `i_nlink` | **Link count** | Number of hard links; data erased when this hits **0** | | `i_uid` / `i_gid` | Owner info | Determines permission to remove | | `i_size` | File size in bytes | Used for symlink target length validation | | `i_blocks` | Pointers to data on disk | The actual content blocks | | `i_atime` / `i_mtime` / `i_ctime` | Access / modify / change times | Updated on `rm` and link operations | | `i_count` (in-memory) | Open file descriptor count | Data blocks freed only when this AND `i_nlink` are both **0** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Rule of File Removal:** A file's data blocks are physically reclaimed by the file system **iff** $\text{i\_nlink} = 0 \;\;\textbf{AND}\;\; \text{i\_count} = 0$ (no process has it open). --- ## 2.2 What Actually Happens When You Type `rm filename`? The shell invokes the `unlink()` system call (or `rm` calls it in a loop). The kernel executes the following logical sequence: **Step 1 — Name Lookup (Directory Resolution)** - The kernel parses the current working directory and locates the directory entry matching `filename`. - It reads the associated **inode number** (say, `inode = 42`). **Step 2 — Permission Check** - The kernel verifies the process has **write + execute permission** on the *containing directory* (not on the file itself, surprisingly!). - The `sticky bit` on the directory is also checked if applicable. **Step 3 — Decrement Link Count** - The kernel atomically decrements `i_nlink` of inode 42: $$i\_nlink_{new} = i\_nlink_{old} - 1$$ - If `i_nlink` was 1 before the call, it becomes **0**. **Step 4 — Remove Directory Entry** - The directory entry for `filename` is physically removed from the directory's data blocks (the *dirent* is overwritten with a "deleted" marker on ext4). **Step 5 — Conditional Block Reclamation** - If `i_nlink == 0` AND `i_count == 0` (no open file descriptors): - The file system driver frees all data blocks listed in `i_blocks[]`. - The inode itself is marked **free** in the inode bitmap. - The inode's disk block is added to the free list. **Step 6 — Update mtime / ctime** - The parent directory's `i_mtime` and `i_ctime` are updated to reflect the deletion. > [!NOTE] > If a process has the file open (e.g., a text editor has it open) when you `rm` it, the *name disappears* but the **blocks survive in memory** until the process closes the file. The traditional Unix phrase: *"Unlink removes the name, not the file."* --- ## 2.3 Hard Link — Detailed Mechanics When you execute `ln source.txt newname.txt`: 1. The kernel reads the inode number of `source.txt` (say, `42`). 2. A new directory entry `newname.txt → inode 42` is created in the target directory. 3. The kernel **increments** `i_nlink` of inode 42: $$i\_nlink_{new} = i\_nlink_{old} + 1$$ 4. Both filenames now point to *the exact same inode* — same `i_mode`, same `i_size`, same `i_blocks`. **Removing a hard link:** - Only the *specific* directory entry is deleted. - `i_nlink` is decremented. - **Data is preserved** as long as `i_nlink > 0`. > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many students write *"Deleting one hard link deletes the file"*. This is **WRONG**. The file *exists* until the *last* hard link is removed. This is a 2-to-3 mark deduction in valuation. --- ## 2.4 Symbolic Link — Detailed Mechanics When you execute `ln -s target.txt mylink.txt`: 1. A **new inode** (say, `99`) is allocated with `i_mode = S_IFLNK | 0777` and a size equal to the length of `"target.txt"`. 2. The content of inode 99 is the **target pathname string** (e.g., ASCII bytes for `target.txt`). 3. A directory entry `mylink.txt → inode 99` is added. 4. `i_nlink` of the *target* (inode 42) is **NOT changed**. **Removing a symbolic link:** - Only the symlink's own inode (99) is unlinked. - The target's `i_nlink` is unaffected. - The target file's data is untouched. **Removing the target of a symbolic link:** - The target file's `i_nlink` decrements. - The symlink still **exists** but now points to a non-existent file — it becomes a **dangling symlink**. > [!TIP] > **How to detect a dangling symlink:** Use `ls -l` — the symlink's display colour changes (red on most distros) and `readlink` will still show the path, but `cat` will return `No such file or directory`. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following high-density table is your **single-page revision weapon** for any link-related question. ⭐ = KTU-favourite line. | # | Property | Hard Link (⭐) | Symbolic Link | |---|----------|----------------|---------------| | 1 | **Inode shared with target?** | ✅ Yes — *same* inode | ❌ No — *own* inode | | 2 | **Created via** | `ln source dest` | `ln -s target linkname` | | 3 | **Effect on `i_nlink` of target** | Increments by **1** | **No change** | | 4 | **Effect on `i_nlink` when removed** | Decrements; data survives until `i_nlink = 0` | The symlink's own inode is freed | | 5 | **Crosses file systems?** | ❌ No — must be on same FS | ✅ Yes — fully portable | | 6 | **Can point to a directory?** | ❌ No (POSIX) | ✅ Yes | | 7 | **Can point to a non-existent target?** | ❌ No (creation fails) | ✅ Yes — creates a *dangling* symlink | | 8 | **Independent permissions?** | ❌ Inherits target's | ✅ Has its own `lrwxrwxrwx` | | 9 | **Survives target deletion?** | ✅ Yes (the file IS the target) | ❌ No — becomes dangling | | 10 | **Data erased on last `rm`?** | ✅ Yes — when `i_nlink = 0` | ✅ Yes — its own blocks freed | | 11 | **POSIX standard?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | 12 | **KTU typical marks** | **7 marks** for full explainer | **7 marks** for full explainer | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Reclamation Rule (Mnemonic "L+O=0"):** Disk blocks are freed **iff** $\text{LinkCount} + \text{OpenFileDescriptors} = 0$. Remember this as **L + O = 0** for last-minute recall. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Removing a File with Multiple Hard Links Let us walk through a complete, exhaustive scenario that the examiner can test in a 14-mark question. ### 📋 Initial State Setup ``` $ echo "Hello KTU" > original.txt $ ln original.txt backup1.txt $ ln original.txt backup2.txt $ ls -li *.txt ``` **Predicted Output:** ``` 1042 -rw-r--r-- 3 user user 10 Jan 15 10:00 original.txt 1042 -rw-r--r-- 3 user user 10 Jan 15 10:00 backup1.txt 1042 -rw-r--r-- 3 user user 10 Jan 15 10:00 backup2.txt ``` > The first column `1042` is the **inode number** — identical for all three. The second field after permissions, `3`, is the **link count**. ### 📐 State Vector (Before Removal) Let $L$ denote the link count, $B$ denote the number of allocated data blocks, $C$ denote the open file descriptor count, and $D$ denote the directory entry count. For inode `1042` containing `"Hello KTU\n"` (10 bytes): $$ L_{initial} = 3, \quad D_{initial} = 3, \quad B_{initial} = 1, \quad C_{initial} = 0 $$ ### 🔍 Step-by-Step: `rm backup1.txt` **Step 1 — Resolve the path `backup1.txt` in the current directory.** The directory block yields: $$\text{dirent}(\texttt{backup1.txt}) \;\longmapsto\; \text{inode} = 1042$$ **Step 2 — Acquire the inode lock for inode 1042 (in-memory copy).** The kernel loads the on-disk inode into the in-core inode table. Let $L_{in-core}$ represent the live link count. **Step 3 — Permission check on the parent directory.** The current directory `.` (inode, say, `1000`) is consulted for `w` and `x` permissions on the *parent*, not on inode 1042. **Step 4 — Atomic decrement of link count.** $$ L_{new} = L_{in-core} - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2 $$ This is done with the inode's `i_lock` held to prevent race conditions with concurrent operations. **Step 5 — Mark the directory entry as deleted.** The `dirent` for `backup1.txt` in the directory's data block is overwritten with a *null* or *unused* marker. The actual bytes are not zeroed — the space is simply marked free for reuse. **Step 6 — Decision: should we free blocks?** Apply the data reclamation predicate: $$ \text{RECLAIM} = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } L_{new} = 0 \;\;\textbf{AND}\;\; C_{new} = 0 \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Substituting: $$ \text{RECLAIM} = (L_{new} = 0) \land (C_{new} = 0) = (2 = 0) \land (0 = 0) = \text{FALSE} $$ **Result:** `RECLAIM = FALSE` ⇒ **data blocks remain intact**. ✅ **Step 7 — Update parent directory mtime / ctime.** $$\text{mtime}(.) = \text{now}, \quad \text{ctime}(.) = \text{now}$$ **Step 8 — Flush in-core inode back to disk.** The updated $L_{in-core} = 2$ is written to the inode table on disk. ### 📐 State Vector (After `rm backup1.txt`) $$ L_{after} = 2, \quad D_{after} = 2, \quad B_{after} = 1, \quad C_{after} = 0 $$ The file `"Hello KTU"` is **still recoverable** via `original.txt` or `backup2.txt`. --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Removing the Last Hard Link Continuing the example: ### 🔍 `rm original.txt` $$ L_{new} = L_{in-core} - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1 $$ $\text{RECLAIM} = (1 = 0) \land (0 = 0) = \text{FALSE}$. Still survives via `backup2.txt`. ### 🔍 `rm backup2.txt` $$ L_{new} = L_{in-core} - 1 = 1 - 1 = 0 $$ $\text{RECLAIM} = (0 = 0) \land (0 = 0) = \text{TRUE}$. ✅ **Now the data blocks are freed!** **Step 9 — Block deallocation.** The data block pointer in `i_blocks[0]` is followed, and that block is marked free in the block bitmap. The inode 1042 is marked free in the inode bitmap. **Step 10 — Final state.** $$ L_{final} = 0, \quad D_{final} = 0, \quad B_{final} = 0, \quad C_{final} = 0 $$ > [!TIP] > **Exhaustive derivation check:** If the test says *"A file has 5 hard links and you `rm` 2 of them"*, the answer is mathematically $L = 5 - 2 = 3$ and the data is **not yet freed**. Students who write *"file deleted"* lose 1 mark. --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Removing a Symbolic Link and Its Target ### 📋 Initial Setup ``` $ echo "KTU Notes" > notes.txt $ ln -s notes.txt softnotes.txt $ ls -li ``` **Predicted Output:** ``` 1042 -rw-r--r-- 1 user user 9 Jan 15 10:30 notes.txt 1099 lrwxrwxrwx 1 user user 9 Jan 15 10:30 softnotes.txt -> notes.txt ``` Two **different inodes** (1042 and 1099). The `->` denotes the symlink's target. ### 📐 State Vector For **inode 1042** (the *target* `notes.txt`): $$ L_{1042} = 1, \quad B_{1042} = 1, \quad \text{type} = \text{regular file} $$ For **inode 1099** (the *symlink* `softnotes.txt`): $$ L_{1099} = 1, \quad B_{1099} = 1, \quad \text{type} = \text{symlink}, \quad \text{content} = \texttt{"notes.txt"} $$ ### 🔍 Case A: `rm softnotes.txt` **Step 1 — Path resolves to inode 1099 (the symlink itself).** **Step 2 — Check that 1099 is of type `S_IFLNK`.** **Step 3 — Decrement link count of 1099:** $$ L_{1099,new} = 1 - 1 = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Free the symlink's data block** (which held `"notes.txt"`): $$ \text{RECLAIM}_{1099} = (0 = 0) \land (0 = 0) = \text{TRUE} $$ **Step 5 — Inode 1099 returned to the inode bitmap.** **Effect on target (inode 1042):** ✅ **Untouched.** $$ L_{1042} = 1 \;\;(\text{unchanged}), \quad B_{1042} = 1 \;\;(\text{unchanged}) $$ ### 🔍 Case B: `rm notes.txt` (target removed, symlink survives) **Step 1 — Path resolves to inode 1042.** **Step 2 — Decrement link count of 1042:** $$ L_{1042,new} = 1 - 1 = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Free the data blocks of inode 1042:** $$ \text{RECLAIM}_{1042} = (0 = 0) \land (0 = 0) = \text{TRUE} $$ **Step 4 — Inode 1042 returned to the inode bitmap.** **Effect on symlink (inode 1099):** Still exists in the directory, still has its own data, but the path it points to is now **invalid**. **Verification:** ``` $ cat softnotes.txt cat: softnotes.txt: No such file or directory $ readlink softnotes.txt notes.txt ``` The symlink is now a **dangling symbolic link** (also called a *broken symlink* or *orphan symlink*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Key Sentence (worth 1 mark by itself):** > *A symbolic link becomes dangling when its target is removed because the symlink's inode continues to exist and store the target's pathname string, but the target's inode has been deallocated and its data blocks reclaimed.* --- ## 3.4 The Mathematical Model of Link Counting Let $F$ be a file with inode $i$. Define: - $L(F)$ = hard link count (also called the *reference count on disk*). - $O(F)$ = count of open file descriptors across all processes. - $H(F)$ = *True* iff the file is *alive* (data blocks still allocated). - $D(F)$ = directory entries referencing inode $i$. **Invariants maintained by the kernel:** $$ L(F) = D(F) \quad \text{(on a quiescent file system)} $$ $$ H(F) = \text{TRUE} \iff L(F) > 0 \;\;\textbf{OR}\;\; O(F) > 0 $$ Equivalently, the file is *dead* (reclaimable) iff: $$ L(F) = 0 \;\;\textbf{AND}\;\; O(F) = 0 $$ **Proof of correctness (sketch for an examiner who asks):** - ($\Rightarrow$) If $H(F) = \text{FALSE}$, the kernel has already executed the reclaim path, which by definition only runs when both conditions hold. - ($\Leftarrow$) If both conditions hold, no directory entry references $i$ and no process can resurrect it via `read()`. The kernel is free to deallocate. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners love this as a "justify your answer" follow-up. Memorize the *iff* direction; it differentiates a topper from an average student. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Inode Block Diagram (Block-Level Architecture) The following block diagram depicts the **on-disk layout of an inode** with emphasis on the fields used in link management and removal. ```mermaid flowchart TB inodeBlock["INODE STRUCTURE - INODE 1042"] inodeBlock --> iMode["i_mode: File Type + Permissions
S_IFREG 0o644"] inodeBlock --> iNlink["i_nlink: Link Count
INITIALLY = 1, incremented on hard link"] inodeBlock --> iUid["i_uid / i_gid: Owner = user / user"] inodeBlock --> iSize["i_size: File size in bytes"] inodeBlock --> iAtime["i_atime: Last ACCESS time"] inodeBlock --> iMtime["i_mtime: Last MODIFY time"] inodeBlock --> iCtime["i_ctime: Last CHANGE time incl metadata"] inodeBlock --> iBlocks["i_blocks: 15 Pointers
12 direct, 1 indirect, 1 double, 1 triple"] iBlocks --> dataPtr1["Direct Block Ptr 0 = Block 7821"] dataPtr1 --> diskData["DISK BLOCK 7821
Holds actual file content bytes"] iNlink -.-> noteLink["CRITICAL for rm:
File data persists while
i_nlink is greater than 0"] ``` ## 4.2 Hard Link vs Symbolic Link — Architecture Comparison This block diagram illustrates the **fundamental structural difference** between the two link types — a KTU-favourite visual for 14-mark answers. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DIR1["DIRECTORY /home/student/"] A1["original.txt"] A2["backup.txt"] A3["softlink.txt"] end subgraph INODE_LAYER["INODE TABLE"] I42["INODE 1042
i_nlink = 2
i_mode = REGULAR FILE
i_blocks points to disk block 7821"] I99["INODE 1099
i_nlink = 1
i_mode = SYMLINK
i_blocks holds string 'original.txt'"] end subgraph DISK_LAYER["DISK BLOCKS"] D7821["BLOCK 7821
Actual file content:
Hello KTU"] end A1 -- arrow showing hard link to inode 1042 --> I42 A2 -- arrow showing hard link to inode 1042 --> I42 I42 -- data block pointer --> D7821 A3 -- arrow showing symlink to its own inode 1099 --> I99 I99 -. dashed arrow: indirection .-> I42 I42 -- data block pointer --> D7821 noteHard["HARD LINK: two names, ONE inode, same blocks"] noteSoft["SYMBOLIC LINK: separate inode storing a pathname string"] ``` ## 4.3 State Transition Diagram: File Removal with Hard Links This state machine captures **how the kernel decides whether to reclaim data** when `rm` is invoked. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Alive: File created, i_nlink = 1 Alive --> Alive: ln source dest
i_nlink incremented
data still allocated Alive --> Alive: rm hardlink
i_nlink decremented
data still allocated if i_nlink greater than 0 Alive --> Unlinked: rm last hardlink
i_nlink becomes 0
and no open file descriptors Alive --> Zombie: rm last hardlink
i_nlink becomes 0
BUT process has it open Zombie --> Unlinked: All processes close file
i_count becomes 0
data blocks freed Unlinked --> [*]: Blocks returned to free list
inode returned to inode bitmap note right of Zombie ZOMBIE STATE: name gone, data alive in memory. Classic Unix behavior. end note note right of Unlinked UNLINKED STATE: name gone, data blocks freed on disk. end note ``` ## 4.4 Dangling Symlink Formation Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Start: target.txt exists, softlink.txt -> target.txt"] rmTarget["User executes: rm target.txt"] checkNlink["Kernel decrements i_nlink of target to 0"] freeTarget["Kernel frees target data blocks and deallocates inode"] symlinkAlive["symlink.txt inode STILL EXISTS, holds string 'target.txt'"] dangling["symlink.txt is now a DANGLING SYMLINK"] catAttempt["User executes: cat softlink.txt"] error["Kernel resolves softlink.txt -> target.txt path -> ENOENT error"] start --> rmTarget rmTarget --> checkNlink checkNlink --> freeTarget freeTarget --> symlinkAlive symlinkAlive --> dangling dangling --> catAttempt catAttempt --> error ``` > [!TIP] > **For the KTU answer sheet:** When drawing diagrams in pen, use **rectangles for inodes**, **arrows with inode numbers** for hard links, and **dashed arrows with a label like "stores path: …"** for symlinks. Examiners reward clarity of separation between *inode-level* and *directory-level* artefacts. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question A1: Define a hard link. How is it different from a copy of a file? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **hard link** is a directory entry that points to the **same inode** as the original file. Both names share identical `i_nlink`, `i_mode`, `i_size`, and data blocks; modifying the file via one name immediately reflects in the other. The link count of the inode is incremented when a hard link is created, and the data blocks are freed only when this count drops to zero. A **copy** (`cp`) creates an entirely **new inode** with its own independently allocated data blocks. Changes made to the original do NOT affect the copy, and vice versa. Hard links are thus more space-efficient and always synchronized, whereas copies are independent snapshots. > *Keywords expected by the examiner: same inode, link count incremented, new inode for copy, independent blocks.* ✅ --- ### ❓ Question A2: What is a dangling symbolic link? When does it occur? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **dangling symbolic link** (also called a *broken* or *orphan* symlink) is a symbolic link whose target file or directory has been **removed, renamed, or moved** to a location that no longer matches the stored pathname. The symlink's own inode and its directory entry continue to exist, and the target pathname string is still readable via `readlink()`, but any attempt to access the file through the symlink returns the error `ENOENT (No such file or directory)`. **When it occurs:** It occurs when the target of a symlink is deleted, or when the target is moved to a different directory without updating the symlink. > *Keywords expected: symlink's own inode survives, target deallocated, ENOENT on access.* ✅ --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** In Part B, you are typically given an *internal choice* within a module. We present TWO alternative 14-mark questions, both of which could appear in the same paper. Choose ONE in the exam. --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Hard Link & Removal Focus `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **inode structure** and the concept of **link count**. How is the link count updated during file creation, hard-link creation, and file removal? (7 marks) **(b)** A file `report.txt` has 3 hard links named `report.txt`, `report_backup.txt`, and `report_old.txt`. Show the step-by-step execution of `rm report_backup.txt` followed by `rm report.txt` and state the state of the underlying inode and data blocks after each operation. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): **Inode Structure:** An inode (index node) is a 256-byte (typical) on-disk data structure in ext4 that stores all metadata of a file except its name. The directory only stores `` pairs. The relevant fields are `i_mode`, `i_nlink`, `i_uid`, `i_gid`, `i_size`, `i_atime`, `i_mtime`, `i_ctime`, and `i_blocks[]` (an array of 15 pointers — 12 direct, 1 indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect). **Link Count Concept:** `i_nlink` is a 16-bit (or 32-bit on ext4) counter that records the number of hard links (directory entries) pointing to this inode. It is initialized to **1** when the file is created. **Update Sequence:** | Operation | Effect on `i_nlink` | Data Block Status | |-----------|---------------------|-------------------| | `creat()` / write | Set to **1** | Allocated | | `ln source dest` (hard link) | Incremented by **1** | Unchanged | | `rm hardlink` | Decremented by **1** | Unchanged if $L > 0$ | | `rm` last hard link | Decremented to **0** | **Freed** if no process holds it open | **Diagram:** (Draw the inode block diagram as shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.) **Model Solution for (b):** **Initial state** — inode 7777, link count = 3, 1 data block allocated, 0 open file descriptors. **Operation 1: `rm report_backup.txt`** Step 1: Resolve `report_backup.txt` → inode 7777. Step 2: Check write+execute permission on parent directory. Step 3: Decrement link count: $L_{new} = 3 - 1 = 2$. Step 4: Remove directory entry for `report_backup.txt`. Step 5: Check reclaim predicate: $\text{RECLAIM} = (2 = 0) \land (0 = 0) = \text{FALSE}$. Step 6: Update parent mtime. **State after Operation 1:** $L = 2$, data block **preserved**, file accessible via `report.txt` and `report_old.txt`. ✅ **Operation 2: `rm report.txt`** Step 1: Resolve `report.txt` → inode 7777. Step 2: Check write+execute permission on parent directory. Step 3: Decrement link count: $L_{new} = 2 - 1 = 1$. Step 4: Remove directory entry for `report.txt`. Step 5: Check reclaim predicate: $\text{RECLAIM} = (1 = 0) \land (0 = 0) = \text{FALSE}$. Step 6: Update parent mtime. **State after Operation 2:** $L = 1$, data block **still preserved**, file accessible via `report_old.txt`. ✅ > *Note: To demonstrate full reclamation, the examiner may add a third sub-part asking you to also remove `report_old.txt` — at that point $L = 0$ and the data block is freed.* --- ### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Symbolic Link & Comparison Focus `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the concept of a **symbolic link** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the steps involved in creating and removing a symbolic link, and explain what happens when the target of a symbolic link is removed. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare **hard links and symbolic links** in terms of at least six parameters. Why are hard links restricted to a single file system, and why can they not point to directories? (7 marks) #### Model Solution for (a): **Symbolic Link Definition:** A symbolic link (symlink) is a special file of type `S_IFLNK` whose contents are a **pathname string** that the kernel resolves at access time. It has its own independent inode and its own metadata. **Creation Steps (`ln -s target linkname`):** 1. Allocate a new inode (e.g., inode 2099) with `i_mode = S_IFLNK | 0777` and `i_size = length of "target"`. 2. Write the target pathname as the file's *content* (stored in a data block, or stored inline in the inode for short targets). 3. Create a new directory entry `linkname → inode 2099`. 4. Increment the parent directory's link count by 1. 5. The target's `i_nlink` is **NOT** modified. **Removal Steps (`rm linkname`):** 1. Resolve `linkname` → inode 2099. 2. Decrement inode 2099's `i_nlink` to 0. 3. Free the symlink's data block (containing the target string). 4. Free inode 2099. 5. The target's `i_nlink` remains unchanged. **What happens when the target is removed:** When `rm target` is executed, the target's `i_nlink` decrements to 0 and its data blocks are freed. The symlink's inode (2099) and data block (containing the target string) continue to exist. The symlink is now **dangling** — any access through it returns `ENOENT`. The symlink will be cleaned up only when it is explicitly `rm`'d. **Diagram:** (Draw the architecture diagram from **Section 4.2**, emphasizing the dashed indirection arrow and the separate inode 1099.) **Model Solution for (b):** **Six-Parameter Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Hard Link | Symbolic Link | |-----------|-----------|---------------| | **Inode sharing** | Shares target's inode | Has its own inode | | **Cross-file-system** | ❌ Restricted to same FS | ✅ Crosses file systems | | **Directory target** | ❌ Cannot link to directories | ✅ Can link to directories | | **Effect of target deletion** | Survives (other links still work) | Becomes dangling | | **Storage overhead** | Minimal (just a dirent) | One inode + pathname string | | **Independence of metadata** | Shares permissions, timestamps | Has independent `lrwxrwxrwx` | **Why hard links are restricted to a single file system:** Hard links work by storing the **raw inode number** in the directory entry. Inode numbers are only **locally unique** within a single file system — inode 42 on `/dev/sda1` is completely unrelated to inode 42 on `/dev/sda2`. If a hard link crossed file systems, the kernel would have no way to disambiguate. Symbolic links avoid this problem because they store a **pathname string** (logical, not physical), which the kernel resolves file-system by file-system using the standard VFS namei lookup. **Why hard links cannot point to directories:** Allowing hard links to directories would create **cycles** in the directory tree (e.g., `a/parent/b/parent/b/parent/...` infinitely). This would break the tree's hierarchical structure, confuse utilities like `find`, `du`, and `rm -r`, and risk filesystem corruption. POSIX therefore forbids it; only the superuser can use directory hard links in limited cases. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (read this carefully before the exam):** > 1. ❌ Writing *"`rm` deletes the file"* — it **unlinks** the name. The data blocks are freed only when the link count and open descriptor count both reach zero. (–1 to –2 marks) > 2. ❌ Confusing the *permission check* — it is on the **parent directory**, not on the file itself. (–1 mark) > 3. ❌ Saying *"hard link and symbolic link are the same"* — they differ in **inode sharing, cross-FS support, and target deletion behaviour**. (–2 marks) > 4. ❌ Forgetting to draw a **diagram** in a 14-mark answer — at least one diagram is **mandatory** to score full marks. (–2 to –3 marks) > 5. ❌ Not distinguishing `i_nlink` (on-disk link count) from `i_count` (in-memory open file count) in your answer. (–1 mark) > 6. ❌ Stating *"deleting one hard link deletes the file"* — this is FALSE; the file persists until the **last** hard link is removed. (–2 marks) > 7. ❌ Missing the explanation of *why* hard links cannot cross file systems — examiners expect the **inode number locality** argument. (–1 mark) > 8. ❌ Failing to mention **dangling symlinks** when discussing target deletion in symbolic links. (–1 mark) --- ## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) - 📌 **`rm` is an *unlink*, not a *delete*.** Data is freed only when the inode's `i_nlink` and in-memory `i_count` both reach zero. - 📌 **Hard link = same inode.** Multiple directory entries reference one inode; `i_nlink` reflects the count. - 📌 **Symbolic link = separate inode storing a pathname.** The target's `i_nlink` is unaffected by symlink creation or removal. - 📌 **Hard link restrictions:** same file system only, cannot point to directories, cannot point to non-existent targets. - 📌 **Symbolic link freedoms:** cross file systems, point to directories, point to non-existent targets (becomes *dangling*). - 📌 **Data reclamation rule (L + O = 0):** $\text{RECLAIM} \iff (i\_nlink = 0) \land (i\_count = 0)$. - 📌 **Permission check for `rm` is on the *parent directory*, not the file itself.** - 📌 **Hard link removal ≠ file removal:** the file survives as long as *any* hard link exists. - 📌 **Dangling symlink:** symlink whose target's inode has been deallocated; the symlink itself is still alive. - 📌 **Inode fields to remember:** `i_mode`, `i_nlink`, `i_uid`, `i_gid`, `i_size`, `i_atime`, `i_mtime`, `i_ctime`, `i_blocks[]`. - 📌 **KTU mnemonic "L + O = 0":** Learn, Open-fd, Zero — disk freed. - 📌 **Always draw a diagram** in a 14-mark answer — at minimum an inode block diagram and a hard-link vs symlink comparison. - 📌 **POSIX minimum** hard links per file: 8 (per `LINK_MAX`). - 📌 **`ls -li`** shows inode numbers; identical inode numbers confirm a hard link. - 📌 **`ls -l`** shows symlinks with `->` arrow notation. - 📌 **`readlink`** displays the raw target string of a symlink without resolving it. - 📌 **`stat`** system call returns `i_nlink` as the `nlink` field — useful in shell scripts (`stat -c %h file`). - 📌 **Zombie file state:** unlinked from directory, data blocks alive in memory due to open file descriptors; reclaimed only when all descriptors close. - 📌 **Sticky bit on directory** prevents non-owners from deleting or renaming files (relevant to `rm` permission semantics). - 📌 **Directory self-references (`.` and `..`)** are the only "hard link to directory" entries POSIX permits. - 📌 **Symbolic link target length limit** is typically 4096 bytes (one data block) on standard Linux FSes. ---

topics.title: Creating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Creating # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Operating System Concepts**, the term **"Creating"** in Module 4 primarily refers to **Process Creation** and **Thread Creation** — the foundational mechanisms by which an operating system instantiates new units of execution. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this encompasses the lifecycle initialization phase where a passive program (executable file on disk) is transformed into an active, schedulable entity managed by the kernel. **Process Creation** is formally defined as the OS procedure that: - Allocates a unique **Process Identifier (PID)**. - Loads the program binary from secondary storage into **primary memory (RAM)**. - Constructs a **Process Control Block (PCB)** in the kernel's process table. - Establishes the execution context (stack, heap, program counter, registers). - Transitions the process from the **New** state to the **Ready** state in the process state diagram. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Process creation is intrinsically linked to **Process Scheduling**, **Context Switching**, and **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)**. Mastery of creation semantics is mandatory before tackling synchronization and deadlock problems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" Model Imagine an automobile manufacturing plant: | OS Concept | Real-World Analogy | Mapping | |---|---|---| | Executable Program (.exe / ELF) | **Blueprint of a car** | Static design, no existence yet | | `fork()` System Call | **Cloning a half-built car** | Duplicate the existing structure | | `exec()` System Call | **Replacing the car's body** | Transform identity while keeping resources | | **PCB (Process Control Block)** | **Unique VIN + Dashboard** | Identity + current state tracking | | Process States (New/Ready/Running) | **Car on Assembly Line** | Position in workflow | | Parent/Child Processes | **Master car & its variants** | Hierarchical creation lineage | > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** Just as a factory cannot start producing cars without first stamping a unique Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) on the chassis, the OS cannot schedule a process without first assigning a PID and creating its PCB. The "blueprint" (program) is dormant until the OS "stamps" it into existence. ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Marked Definitions) - **Process:** A program in execution — a dynamic entity comprising the **program code (text segment)**, **current activity (program counter)**, **stack (temporary data)**, **heap (dynamic memory)**, and **data section (global variables)**. - **PID (Process Identifier):** A unique **non-negative integer** assigned by the OS to every process. Default max value in Linux: **32768** (configurable up to **4194304** in `/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max`). - **PCB (Process Control Block):** A kernel data structure (often called `task_struct` in Linux) storing all metadata about a process. - **Parent Process:** The process that invokes the creation mechanism. In Unix, every process (except `init`/`systemd` with **PID 1**) has exactly **one parent**. - **Child Process:** The newly created process that is a duplicate or transformed version of its parent. - **System Call:** A controlled entry point into the kernel, e.g., `fork()`, `vfork()`, `clone()`, `CreateProcess()` (Windows), `posix_spawn()`. - **Init Process:** The **ancestor of all user processes**, traditionally assigned **PID 1**. ## 1.4 Why Process Creation Matters in OS Design Process creation is the **gateway** to: 1. **Multiprogramming** — multiple processes share the CPU. 2. **Modularity** — complex tasks split into cooperating processes. 3. **Concurrency & Parallelism** — exploit multi-core architectures. 4. **Fault Isolation** — one crashing process does not kill the entire system. 5. **Resource Fairness** — kernel mediates access to hardware. > [!TIP] > **For KTU 2024 Exams:** Always justify *why* a particular creation mechanism (`fork` vs. `vfork` vs. `posix_spawn`) is chosen in design questions. Examiners reward conceptual clarity over rote definitions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Events That Trigger Process Creation According to Silberschatz, Galvin, and Gagne (standard KTU reference framework), process creation is triggered by **four canonical events**: 1. **System Bootstrapping (Initialization):** - During OS startup, the kernel spawns several **daemon processes** (e.g., `systemd`, `kthreadd`, `ksoftirqd`). - These are typically **kernel threads** that remain resident for the system's lifetime. 2. **User-Initiated Execution:** - A user types a command in a shell (e.g., `./myprogram` or `ls -l`). - The shell (a process itself) executes a `fork()` followed by an `exec()` of the target program. 3. **Process Creation System Call:** - A running process explicitly invokes `fork()`, `CreateProcess()`, or `posix_spawn()` to spawn a child. - Used in **client-server architectures**, **web servers** (e.g., Apache's prefork MPM), and **parallel computing**. 4. **Batch Job Submission:** - In mainframe/minicomputer environments, the OS initiates processes based on a queued batch schedule. ## 2.2 Detailed Steps in Process Creation (Unix/Linux `fork()` Model) The `fork()` system call performs the following kernel operations: **Step 1 — PID Assignment:** - The kernel scans its PID bitmap to find the lowest unused integer. - Assigns a new unique PID. **Step 2 — PCB Duplication:** - A new `task_struct` is allocated from the **slab allocator** (or `alloc_task_struct_node()`). - The parent's PCB is **shallow-copied** (most fields duplicated as-is). **Step 3 — Address Space Setup:** - Modern OSes use **Copy-on-Write (COW)** optimization. - Initially, parent and child share the **same physical memory pages** marked as read-only. - A page fault occurs only when either process attempts to **write** to a shared page, triggering a physical copy. **Step 4 — Resource Inheritance:** - Open file descriptors are duplicated (refcount incremented in the file table). - Signal handlers, environment variables, and working directory are inherited. - **Resource limits** (ulimit) and **nice values** are copied. **Step 5 — Scheduling Enlistment:** - The child is placed in the **Ready Queue** of the scheduler. - A return value differentiation: parent receives the **child's PID**, child receives **0**. **Step 6 — Context Establishment:** - The child's kernel stack is set up so that upon first schedule, it appears to "return" from `fork()` with a return value of 0. ## 2.3 The `fork()` + `exec()` Paradigm This two-step mechanism is the **cornerstone of Unix process creation**: $$\text{Parent} \xrightarrow{\text{fork()}} \text{Parent + Child (identical copy)} \xrightarrow{\text{exec() in child}} \text{Parent} + \text{Child (new program)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The two-step separation allows the parent to manipulate the child's environment (e.g., redirect I/O via `dup2()`, close file descriptors, set environment variables) **between** `fork()` and `exec()`. This is impossible in Windows' unified `CreateProcess()` API. ## 2.4 Thread Creation (Lightweight Alternative) Unlike process creation, **thread creation** shares the same address space and most OS resources: | Attribute | Process Creation | Thread Creation | |---|---|---| | Memory Space | **Separate** virtual address space | **Shared** within the process | | PCB | New PCB created | New **TCB (Thread Control Block)** only | | Creation Cost | **High** (mm_struct, page tables) | **Low** (shared mm_struct) | | Communication | IPC required | **Shared memory** (direct) | | POSIX API | `fork()`, `posix_spawn()` | `pthread_create()` | | Windows API | `CreateProcess()` | `CreateThread()` / `_beginthreadex()` | | Typical Time | ~1–10 ms | ~10–100 µs | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Maximum PIDs (Linux default) | $\text{pid\_max} = 32768$ | integer | Configurable via `/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max` | | Linux `pid_max` (modern) | $\text{pid\_max} = 4194304 = 2^{22}$ | integer | Kernel 2.6+ with `CONFIG_BASE_SMALL=0` | | Threads per process (Linux) | $\text{max\_threads} \approx 32768$ (RAM-dependent) | integer | Computed as $\min(\text{mempages} / 16, \text{FIX\_TASKS\_MAX})$ | | Context switch time | $T_{cs} \approx 1\text{–}10 \;\mu s$ | microseconds | Approximate, hardware-dependent | | PCB Size (Linux `task_struct`) | $\approx 4\text{–}8 \;\text{KiB}$ | kilobytes | Includes embedded kernel stack | | Stack size (default thread) | $\text{ulimit -s} = 8 \;\text{MiB}$ | megabytes | Configurable per-thread | | Copy-on-Write (COW) | $\text{Physical pages copied only on write}$ | n/a | Saves memory in `fork()` | | Address space overhead | $\text{Page table size} = \frac{2^{N}}{4096} \times 8$ bytes | bytes | $N$ = virtual address bits (e.g., 48 for x86-64) | | Process creation rate | $R = \frac{1}{T_{fork}}$ | processes/second | Practical limit: ~10,000/sec | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Pipe-Safety Note:** All absolute values and vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $P \mid Q$ denotes divisibility) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Web Servers (Nginx, Apache):** Use `fork()`-based prefork model to handle concurrent client requests — each child serves one request independently. - **Browsers (Chromium):** Use **multi-process architecture** — one process per tab for fault isolation. Creation via `posix_spawn` or `CloneProcess` (Windows). - **Databases (PostgreSQL):** Use `fork()` to spawn worker backends for each client connection. - **Container Runtimes (Docker, runc):** Use `clone()` with namespace flags to create Linux containers — a form of "lightweight process creation" with isolated views of PID, mount, network, and UTS namespaces. - **Parallel Computing (OpenMP, MPI):** Thread creation via `omp_get_thread_num()` or `MPI_Comm_spawn()` enables multi-core execution of scientific workloads. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of PIDs in Linux Modern Linux kernels (since 2.6) dynamically size the PID space based on compile-time configuration. Let us derive the **upper bound**: **Step 1 — Start with the default constant:** $$\text{PID\_MAX\_DEFAULT} = 0x8000 = 32768$$ This value is hardcoded in the kernel header ``. **Step 2 — When `CONFIG_BASE_SMALL = 0` (default for production kernels):** $$\text{PID\_MAX\_LIMIT} = 2^{22} = 4194304$$ This is the **architectural maximum** because Linux uses an **id-type** (an `unsigned int` with reserved bits for namespace tagging). **Step 3 — Effective runtime maximum:** $$\text{pid\_max} = \min\!\left(\text{PID\_MAX\_LIMIT},\; \text{system-defined tuning}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Verifying with the formula:** $$\text{pid\_max} = \min\!\left(2^{22},\; 4194304\right) = 4194304 \;\;\; \checkmark$$ This explains why Linux servers can support up to **~4.2 million concurrent processes** without recompilation. ## 3.2 Derivation: Page Table Memory Overhead for a Process When a new process is created, the kernel must set up a page table. For **x86-64 architecture** (4-level page table: PGD → PUD → PMD → PT): **Step 1 — Virtual address space size (canonical):** $$V = 2^{48} \;\text{bytes} = 256 \;\text{TiB}$$ The hardware ignores the upper 16 bits, ensuring canonical addresses in the range $\vert \text{addr} \vert \leq 2^{47}-1$. **Step 2 — Number of virtual pages (with 4 KiB pages):** $$N_{\text{pages}} = \frac{2^{48}}{2^{12}} = 2^{36} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}736$$ **Step 3 — Each Page Table (PT) entry is 8 bytes. A PT covers $2^9 = 512$ entries (since $2^9 \times 8 = 4096$ bytes per PT).** **Step 4 — Number of leaf-level Page Tables needed for the entire VA range:** $$N_{\text{PT,leaf}} = \frac{2^{36}}{2^{9}} = 2^{27} = 134{,}217{,}728$$ **Step 5 — Memory needed for leaf PTs alone:** $$M_{\text{leaf}} = 2^{27} \times 4096 \;\text{bytes} = 2^{39} \;\text{bytes} = 512 \;\text{GiB}$$ **Step 6 — With 2 MiB huge pages (PMD-mapped), the leaf PT count drops:** $$N_{\text{PT,leaf}}' = \frac{2^{36}}{2^{18}} = 2^{18} = 262{,}144$$ $$M_{\text{leaf}}' = 2^{18} \times 4096 = 1 \;\text{GiB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A fully populated 48-bit address space would require **512 GiB** of page tables — clearly impractical. This is why OSes use **demand paging** (lazy allocation): page tables are populated only when pages are actually accessed, and the OS only allocates the **top-level PGD** (4 KiB) at creation time. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process vs. Thread vs. Container Creation | Parameter | `fork()` Process | `pthread_create()` Thread | `clone(CLONE_NEWPID)` Container | |---|---|---|---| | Address Space | New (COW) | Shared | New (namespaced) | | PID allocated | Yes | No (shares TGID) | Yes (new PID namespace) | | File descriptor table | Copied | Shared | New (or shared) | | Mount namespace | Inherited | N/A | New | | Network namespace | Inherited | N/A | New (typically) | | Creation latency $T_c$ | $\approx 1\text{–}10 \;\text{ms}$ | $\approx 10\text{–}100 \;\mu s$ | $\approx 10\text{–}50 \;\text{ms}$ | | Memory overhead $M_o$ | $\approx 4 \;\text{MiB}$ (VMA + stack) | $\approx 8 \;\text{MiB}$ (stack) | $\approx 50 \;\text{MiB}$ (cgroups + ns) | | Isolation strength | **Strong** | **Weak** | **Medium** | | Parent–child relationship | Yes (PPID) | Implicit (shared TGID) | Yes (within namespace) | ## 3.4 Derivation: Theoretical Maximum Process Creation Rate **Given:** - Average fork latency: $T_{\text{fork}} = 5 \;\text{ms} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s}$ - Single-threaded creator process **Step 1 — Sequential creation rate:** $$R_{\text{seq}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{fork}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \;\text{processes/second}$$ **Step 2 — With $k$ parallel creator threads (Amdahl's law applied):** $$R_{\text{parallel}} = k \times R_{\text{seq}} \times \left(1 - f_{\text{serial}}\right) + R_{\text{seq}} \times f_{\text{serial}}$$ Assuming creation is **fully parallelizable** ($f_{\text{serial}} \approx 0$): $$R_{\text{parallel}} = k \times 200$$ **Step 3 — For $k = 64$ creator threads:** $$R_{\text{parallel}} = 64 \times 200 = 12{,}800 \;\text{processes/second}$$ This matches the empirical "**fork bomb**" theoretical limit on commodity Linux hardware. > [!WARNING] > **Note:** This analysis assumes unlimited RAM and PID availability. In practice, PID exhaustion at **4194304** processes would terminate the system or invoke PID wraparound (which modern Linux avoids via atomic id allocators). ## 3.5 Worked Example: Address Space Layout of a Created Process For a process with: - Text segment: **64 KiB** - Data segment: **128 KiB** - Heap: **2 MiB** (initial) - Stack: **8 MiB** (initial) - Kernel space (upper half, x86-64): **128 TiB** (canonical) **Step 1 — Calculate total user-space virtual memory:** $$M_{\text{user}} = 64 \;\text{KiB} + 128 \;\text{KiB} + 2 \;\text{MiB} + 8 \;\text{MiB} = 10.19 \;\text{MiB}$$ **Step 2 — Express in bytes:** $$M_{\text{user}} = 64 \times 1024 + 128 \times 1024 + 2 \times 2^{20} + 8 \times 2^{20} = 10{,}682{,}368 \;\text{bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Number of 4 KiB pages needed:** $$N_{\text{pages}} = \left\lceil \frac{10{,}682{,}368}{4096} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2608.09 \right\rceil = 2609 \;\text{pages}$$ **Step 4 — Physical memory used (assuming all pages resident):** $$M_{\text{phys}} = 2609 \times 4096 = 10{,}686{,}464 \;\text{bytes} \approx 10.19 \;\text{MiB} \;\;\; \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Tip:** Practice such memory-layout derivations. The KTU board examiner frequently asks to compute the number of page table entries or the memory consumed by a process with a given segment layout. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process State Transition Diagram (Creation Phase Focus) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> procNew : Program file on disk procNew --> procReady : fork() returns
PCB initialized
PID assigned procReady --> procRunning : Scheduler dispatch
CPU allocated procRunning --> procReady : Time quantum expires
Preemption procRunning --> procWaiting : I/O or event wait
System call blocks procWaiting --> procReady : I/O completes
Event signaled procRunning --> procTerminated : exit() called
Resource cleanup procTerminated --> [*] : PCB deallocated
Parent reaps via wait() ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Process Creation Flowchart (Linux `fork()` Internals) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User process invokes fork syscall] --> B[Save user registers on kernel stack] B --> C[Allocate new task_struct slab] C --> D[Assign unique PID via idr_alloc] D --> E[Copy parent task_struct fields] E --> F[Setup new kernel stack for child] F --> G[Copy or share mm_struct with COW flag] G --> H[Duplicate file descriptor table dup_fd] H --> I[Duplicate signal handlers and sigpending] I --> J[Copy credentials cred struct] J --> K[Set child state to TASK_RUNNING] K --> L[Place child on runqueue] L --> M[Wake up child kernel thread] M --> N[Return to userspace: child gets 0, parent gets child PID] N --> O[End: Both parent and child resume execution] ``` ## 4.3 Process Control Block (PCB) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph pcbHeader [PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK] pid[PID: Unique Identifier] state[State: New Ready Running Waiting Terminated] pc[Program Counter: Next instruction address] regs[CPU Registers: General purpose, Stack pointer, PSW] end subgraph pcbMem [MEMORY MANAGEMENT] mm[mm_struct pointer] ptbr[Page Table Base Register] limits[Memory limits: text data stack heap] end subgraph pcbIO [I/O STATUS] fds[Open file descriptors list] iostat[I/O request queue pointers] end subgraph pcbAcct [ACCOUNTING] cpuTime[CPU time used] userTime[User mode time] kernelTime[Kernel mode time] children[Child process list] end subgraph pcbSched [SCHEDULING] priority[Static priority nice value] policy[Scheduling policy SCHED_NORMAL FIFO RR] rtPri[Real-time priority] end pcbHeader --> pcbMem pcbMem --> pcbIO pcbIO --> pcbAcct pcbAcct --> pcbSched ``` ## 4.4 The `fork()` + `exec()` Two-Stage Paradigm ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Parent Process P] -->|fork syscall| B[Parent P continues] A -->|fork syscall| C[Child C: identical copy] C -->|exec syscall| D[Child C prime: new program loaded] B -->|wait syscall| E[Parent blocks for child] D -->|exit syscall| F[Child terminated status returned] F -->|wait returns| E E --> G[Parent resumes execution] ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Block Diagram: Process vs. Thread Creation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph procCreate [PROCESS CREATION] p1[New PCB] --> p2[New Virtual Address Space] p2 --> p3[New Kernel Resources] p3 --> p4[Independent Execution] end subgraph threadCreate [THREAD CREATION] t1[New TCB Thread Control Block] --> t2[Shared Address Space] t2 --> t3[Shared Kernel Resources] t3 --> t4[Parallel Execution in same process] end procCreate -.->|Higher overhead| overheadNote[Cost: 1 to 10 ms] threadCreate -.->|Lower overhead| overheadNote ``` ## 4.6 Container Creation via `clone()` System Call ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Parent process calls clone with CLONE flags] --> B[Kernel checks flags] B --> C[Create new task_struct] C --> D[Setup new namespaces PID NET MOUNT UTS IPC USER] D --> E[Create cgroup entry for resource limits] E --> F[Mount rootfs from image layer] F --> G[Set up seccomp BPF filter] G --> H[Set capabilities and no_new_privs] H --> I[exec the container entrypoint] I --> J[Container process running in isolated environment] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** > - All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `procNew`, `pcbHeader`). > - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph` only as block headers, not as nodes). > - All labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or HTML formatting. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between process creation and thread creation. Mention any two advantages of using threads over processes.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Process Creation | Thread Creation | |---|---|---| | Resource cost | Heavy (new address space, PCB) | Light (shared address space, TCB only) | | Communication | Requires IPC mechanisms | Direct via shared memory | | Creation time | **1–10 ms** | **10–100 µs** | | Isolation | Strong fault isolation | Weak; one thread crash can kill the process | **Two advantages of threads:** 1. **Faster creation and context switching** — measured in microseconds rather than milliseconds. 2. **Efficient communication** — threads of the same process share the heap and global variables, eliminating the need for pipes, message queues, or shared memory APIs. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Explain the role of the Process Control Block (PCB) during process creation. List any four fields stored in a PCB.** **Model Answer:** The **PCB** is the kernel's bookkeeping data structure for a process. During creation, the kernel allocates a new PCB and populates it with the state required for scheduling and management. **Four key PCB fields:** 1. **Process ID (PID)** — unique integer identifier. 2. **Process State** — New, Ready, Running, Waiting, or Terminated. 3. **Program Counter (PC)** — address of the next instruction to execute. 4. **CPU Registers** — general-purpose registers, stack pointer, and program status word (PSW). Additional fields include memory management info, open file descriptors, signal masks, accounting data, and scheduling parameters. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question 3A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the different states a process goes through from its creation to termination. Discuss the role of the Process Control Block (PCB) in each state transition. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare `fork()`, `vfork()`, and `posix_spawn()` system calls in Unix/Linux. Under what circumstances would you prefer `posix_spawn()` over `fork()+exec()`? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question 3A (Part a):** **Process State Diagram:** ``` +-----------+ | New | +-----+-----+ | (Admitted) v +-----------+ +-----------+ | Ready |<------>| Running | +-----+-----+ +-----+-----+ ^ | | (I/O or event) | (Interrupt or | | system call) | v +-----+-----+ +-----------+ | Waiting | | Terminated| +-----------+ +-----------+ ``` **Explanation of each state:** 1. **New:** Process is being created. The OS allocates a PID, builds the PCB, and loads the program into memory. This is the *creation* phase — directly relevant to Module 4. 2. **Ready:** Process is waiting in the ready queue to be assigned to the CPU. The PCB state field is set to `TASK_RUNNING` (Linux terminology). The process is fully prepared to execute but has not been scheduled yet. 3. **Running:** Instructions are being executed by the CPU. The PCB stores the current values of the program counter, registers, and stack pointer — enabling **context restoration** after a context switch. 4. **Waiting (Blocked):** Process cannot proceed until an event occurs (e.g., I/O completion, signal arrival). The PCB is moved from the runqueue to a **wait queue** associated with the event source. 5. **Terminated:** Process has finished execution. The PCB remains until the parent reaps the exit status via `wait()` or `waitpid()` (preventing **zombie processes**). **Role of the PCB in state transitions:** - The PCB is the **single source of truth** about a process. - On every state change, the kernel updates the `state` field in the PCB. - During context switches, the PCB holds the **saved register context**, which the scheduler restores on the next dispatch. - Memory management fields in the PCB (`mm_struct` pointer) ensure the correct address space is loaded into the MMU. --- ### **Model Solution for Question 3A (Part b):** **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | `fork()` | `vfork()` | `posix_spawn()` | |---|---|---|---| | POSIX Standard | POSIX.1-1990 | Legacy/Berkeley | POSIX.1-2001 | | Address space | COW copy | Shared with parent (no copy) | Configurable | | Parent execution | Concurrent | **Suspended** until child calls `exec` or `exit` | Concurrent | | Use case | General-purpose forking | Tight memory, immediate `exec` | Secure, async-safe, signal-safe | | Thread safety | MT-Safe with care | **Not MT-Safe** | **MT-Safe and async-signal-safe** | | File descriptor actions | Inherited | Inherited | `posix_spawn_file_actions_t` API | | Modern relevance | Ubiquitous | Mostly deprecated | Recommended for new code | **When to prefer `posix_spawn()` over `fork()+exec()`:** 1. **Signal-safety in multithreaded programs:** `fork()` alone is unsafe in multithreaded processes (only the calling thread survives in the child, leading to deadlocks on mutexes). `posix_spawn()` handles this safely. 2. **Security-sensitive contexts:** When the child must run with a different UID/GID or in a sandbox, `posix_spawnattr_setsigdefault()`, `posix_spawnattr_setflags()` (e.g., `POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID`) provide a cleaner API. 3. **Async-signal safety:** If you must spawn a child from within a **signal handler** (a rare but valid use case, e.g., for `SIGCHLD`-replacement patterns), `posix_spawn()` is the only standards-compliant choice. 4. **Portability across Unix variants:** `fork()` is universal, but `vfork()` semantics vary. `posix_spawn()` provides a **standardized portable interface**. 5. **Performance-critical servers:** Modern glibc's `posix_spawn()` can use `vfork()` internally, avoiding COW overhead when the child immediately execs. --- ### **Question 3B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]` **(a)** Describe the `fork()` and `exec()` system calls in Unix. Explain how they work together to create a new process running a different program. Include a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the different events that lead to process creation in an operating system? Discuss the role of the `init` process (PID 1) in the Unix process hierarchy. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question 3B (Part a):** **The `fork()` System Call:** The `fork()` system call creates a **new process that is an almost exact duplicate of the calling process**. The prototype is: ```c pid_t fork(void); ``` - Returns **child's PID** in the **parent**. - Returns **0** in the **child**. - Returns **-1** on error (e.g., PID exhaustion, resource limit). After `fork()`, both parent and child continue execution from the **same program counter** (the instruction after the `fork()` call). The key difference is the return value, which allows the program to branch behavior. **The `exec()` System Call Family:** The `exec()` family (`execl`, `execv`, `execle`, `execve`, `execlp`, `execvp`) **replaces the current process image with a new program**. The prototype for `execve` is: ```c int execve(const char *pathname, char *const argv[], char *const envp[]); ``` - Loads the binary from `pathname` into memory. - Sets up a new stack, heap, and data segment. - **Preserves** the PID, open file descriptors (unless marked `FD_CLOEXEC`), and credentials. - **Does not return** on success (transitions to the new program's entry point). **Working Together — Typical Shell Pattern:** ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Shell displays prompt] --> B[User types command ls -l] B --> C[Shell calls fork] C --> D[Parent shell waits via waitpid] C --> E[Child calls execve with path to ls] E --> F[Kernel loads ls binary into child] F --> G[ls executes and prints directory listing] G --> H[ls calls exit with status] H --> I[Kernel sends SIGCHLD to parent] I --> J[Parent waitpid returns child PID and status] J --> K[Shell displays next prompt] ``` **Why the Two-Step Design?** The separation of `fork()` and `exec()` allows the **parent to manipulate the child's environment** between the two calls. Common operations include: - **I/O redirection:** `dup2(fd, STDOUT_FILENO)` to redirect stdout to a file. - **Closing file descriptors:** `close(fd)` to prevent leaks. - **Setting up pipes:** Creating a pipe and assigning one end to the child's stdin. - **Changing environment variables:** Modifying `environ` before `execve`. This flexibility is a defining feature of Unix process management and is impossible to replicate with Windows' unified `CreateProcess()` API. --- ### **Model Solution for Question 3B (Part b):** **Events Leading to Process Creation:** 1. **System Initialization:** - During boot, the kernel creates several **kernel threads** for housekeeping (e.g., `kthreadd` for managing other kernel threads, `migration` for CPU-hotplug operations). - The first user-space process is traditionally `/sbin/init` (or `/lib/systemd/systemd` on modern systems), which is assigned **PID 1**. 2. **User Login and Shell Execution:** - When a user logs in (via terminal, SSH, or GUI), the login manager spawns a shell process. - The shell itself can spawn child processes for every command. 3. **Explicit Process Creation System Calls:** - A process invokes `fork()` / `vfork()` / `posix_spawn()` (Unix) or `CreateProcess()` (Windows). - Used by daemons, servers, and parallel programs. 4. **Batch Job Initiation:** - In mainframe environments (and modern HPC schedulers like SLURM), the OS launches processes based on queued jobs. - The batch scheduler may use `fork()+exec()` or specialized APIs to start worker processes. 5. **Service Manager Requests:** - `systemd` listens on D-Bus or socket activation and spawns services on demand. - This is a form of "lazy process creation" — services are only started when first needed. **Role of `init` (PID 1):** The `init` process is the **ancestor of all user-space processes** in Unix. Its special properties include: 1. **Universal Parent:** Every process (except kernel threads) traces its parent lineage back to `init`. If a parent process dies before its child, the child is **reparented** to `init`, which becomes its new PPID. 2. **Zombie Reaper:** `init` periodically calls `wait()` to reap orphaned zombie processes. Without `init`, zombies would accumulate indefinitely, eventually exhausting the PID table. 3. **System Configuration:** `init` (especially `systemd`) reads system configuration, mounts filesystems, starts network services, and brings the system to the desired runlevel/target. 4. **Signal Handling:** `init` is **immune to `SIGTERM` and `SIGKILL` from unprivileged processes** — only the kernel or a process with `CAP_KILL` can signal it. This prevents accidental system shutdown. 5. **Orphan Adoption:** As mentioned, `init` adopts orphaned processes, ensuring they are properly cleaned up. 6. **Session Leader:** `init` may also be the session leader for system-wide sessions, providing a stable ancestor for login shells and daemons. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Process Creation Questions:** > 1. **Confusing `fork()` with `exec()`:** Many students describe them as one operation. Always clarify that `fork()` *duplicates* and `exec()` *replaces*. (–2 marks penalty) > 2. **Forgetting Copy-on-Write:** Modern `fork()` does NOT immediately copy memory. Mentioning COW shows depth. (+1 mark bonus) > 3. **Omitting return values:** Always state that `fork()` returns the child's PID to the parent and 0 to the child. Examiners look for this. > 4. **Confusing PID with PPID:** PID is the process's own ID; PPID is the parent's ID. Distinguish clearly. > 5. **Ignoring `init`'s PID = 1:** Many questions ask "which process has no parent?" — the answer is `init` (or `systemd` in modern Linux). > 6. **PCB field omissions:** Forgetting CPU registers or program counter in the PCB loses marks. Always list at least 4–5 fields. > 7. **State diagram direction errors:** Drawing arrows incorrectly between Ready/Running/Waiting states is a common mark-loser. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Creating** ### **Core Concepts** - [x] A **process** is a program in execution; creation transforms a static program into a dynamic, schedulable entity. - [x] A **thread** is a lightweight unit of execution sharing its parent process's address space. - [x] The **PCB** (`task_struct` in Linux) is the kernel's per-process metadata structure. ### **Creation Mechanisms** - [x] **Unix/Linux:** `fork()` + `exec()` (two-step); `vfork()` (legacy, no COW); `posix_spawn()` (modern, async-safe). - [x] **Windows:** `CreateProcess()` (unified single-call API). - [x] **POSIX Threads:** `pthread_create()` for threads; `pthread_join()` for cleanup. ### **Key Distinctions** - [x] `fork()` returns child PID to parent, 0 to child. - [x] `exec()` replaces the process image; preserves PID and open FDs. - [x] **COW (Copy-on-Write)** defers physical memory duplication until a write occurs. - [x] Parent–child relationships form a **process tree** rooted at `init` (PID 1). ### **Numbers to Memorize** - [x] Default Linux `pid_max` = **32768**; modern maximum = **$2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304$**. - [x] x86-64 virtual address space = **48 bits** (256 TiB). - [x] Default thread stack size = **8 MiB**. - [x] PCB size ≈ **4–8 KiB** (includes kernel stack). ### **Process States (KTU Board Favorite)** - [x] **New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated** - [x] State transitions are managed by the **scheduler** and **interrupt handlers**. ### **Real-World Relevance** - [x] **Fork-exec** powers shells, daemons, and web servers. - [x] **Threads** enable parallelism in OpenMP, Java, and pthreads programs. - [x] **Containers** (`clone()` with namespace flags) extend process creation to isolated environments. - [x] **`init` (PID 1)** is the universal ancestor and zombie reaper. ### **Common Exam Traps** - [x] Always specify whether the question is about **process** or **thread** creation. - [x] Distinguish between **PID namespace** and **global PID**. - [x] Remember that `fork()` does **not** copy the kernel thread; only the calling thread survives. - [x] `posix_spawn()` is the **modern, recommended** API for signal-safe and thread-safe creation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** When a question asks "explain process creation," structure your answer in three layers: > 1. **Definition + Triggers** (2–3 marks) > 2. **Mechanism + PCB + fork/exec** (4–5 marks) > 3. **Diagram + Examples + Real-world usage** (3–4 marks) > > Always end with a **neatly labeled diagram** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for presentation clarity.

topics.title: Reading and Deleting Directories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reading and Deleting Directories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **directory** in an operating system is a specialized file system object that acts as a container, maintaining an ordered list of entries (files and subdirectories) along with their metadata (name, inode number, type, size, timestamps). **Reading a directory** refers to the systematic retrieval of these entries through controlled system calls, while **deleting a directory** refers to the safe removal of a directory entry from its parent and the deallocation of its associated storage resources.

According to the **POSIX.1-2017 standard (IEEE Std 1003.1)**, the canonical system calls governing these operations are:

| Operation | System Call | Header File | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|---------|
| Open Directory | `opendir()` | `<dirent.h>` | Acquires a stream handle to a directory |
| Read Directory | `readdir()` | `<dirent.h>` | Returns the next directory entry |
| Close Directory | `closedir()` | `<dirent.h>` | Releases the stream handle |
| Reset Position | `rewinddir()` | `<dirent.h>` | Resets read pointer to first entry |
| Delete Directory | `rmdir()` | `<unistd.h>` | Removes an empty directory |
| Remove File/Dir | `unlink()` / `remove()` | `<unistd.h>` / `<stdio.h>` | Removes a directory entry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 4 (File Systems), students must master the directory abstraction, the API level operations for traversing the directory hierarchy, and the safety constraints (especially the *empty-directory* invariant) enforced by the kernel before a delete operation can succeed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy — The Office Building Filing Cabinet**

Imagine a corporate office building as the **root file system** (`/`). Each **floor** is a directory (e.g., `HR/`, `Finance/`, `Engineering/`). Each floor contains **rooms** (files) and **staircases to other floors** (subdirectories).

- **Reading a Directory** is like a security guard walking through a floor with a clipboard. He opens the floor's door (`opendir`), then reads each room's nameplate one by one from a catalog (`readdir`), and finally closes the door behind him (`closedir`). He never writes anything; he only *observes* and *records*.
- **Deleting a Directory** is like demolishing a floor. The building supervisor (`kernel`) will only approve demolition if the floor is **completely empty** — no people, no furniture, no boxes. If even a single room has furniture (a file inside), the demolition is rejected with an error (`ENOTEMPTY`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Must Be Empty" Rule?** The kernel refuses to recursively delete a directory via `rmdir()` to prevent catastrophic, silent data loss. Recursive deletion is a deliberate, explicit user-space operation (`rm -r`), not a kernel-default behavior.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following key constants are defined in `<errno.h>` and are critical for board examination answers:

- **`ENOTEMPTY`** = **39** (Directory not empty) — the most common delete-failure cause.
- **`ENOENT`** = **2** (No such file or directory) — the path does not exist.
- **`EACCES`** = **13** (Permission denied) — insufficient privileges.
- **`EBADF`** = **9** (Bad file descriptor) — invalid `DIR*` handle.
- **`EFAULT`** = **14** (Bad address) — pointer argument is outside allocated space.

> [!NOTE]
> **DT_DIR** (Directory type flag) and **DT_REG** (Regular file flag) defined in `<dirent.h>` classify entries returned by `readdir()`. The `d_type` field of `struct dirent` carries these flags.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The `dirent` Structure — The Atomic Unit of Directory Reading

Every entry returned by `readdir()` is encapsulated in the **`struct dirent`** (directory entry) data structure. Understanding its layout is mandatory for KTU exams.

```c
struct dirent {
    ino_t          d_ino;       // Inode number (unique file system identifier)
    off_t          d_off;       // Offset to the next dirent in the actual directory
    unsigned short d_reclen;    // Length of this record
    unsigned char  d_type;      // Type of file (DT_DIR, DT_REG, DT_LNK, etc.)
    char           d_name[256]; // Null-terminated filename
};
```

### 2.2 Operational Flow — Reading a Directory

The high-level logic for reading a directory follows a strict four-phase protocol:

1. **Acquire Phase (opendir):** The kernel translates the path string to an inode, validates execute (`x`) permission on every ancestor component of the path, and returns a `DIR *` opaque handle backed by an underlying file descriptor.
2. **Iteration Phase (readdir):** Each call advances an internal cursor and copies the next `dirent` from the on-disk directory block(s) into a user-supplied or static buffer. When the cursor reaches the end, `NULL` is returned.
3. **Reset Phase (rewinddir):** Optional. Resets the cursor to the first entry without closing/reopening the stream.
4. **Release Phase (closedir):** Closes the underlying file descriptor and frees the `DIR` structure in user-space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concurrency Invariant:** The `DIR *` stream returned by `opendir()` is **not thread-safe** under POSIX. Two threads sharing the same handle will produce undefined, non-deterministic results.

### 2.3 Operational Flow — Deleting a Directory

The `rmdir()` operation is governed by the following decision tree:

1. **Path Resolution:** Kernel traverses path components, validating execute permissions on every ancestor.
2. **Final Component Check:** The target must itself be an empty directory (zero entries excluding `.` and `..`).
3. **Unlink Operation:** The directory's entry is removed from its parent's directory file.
4. **Inode Deallocation:** The directory's inode is freed (link count drops to zero); its data blocks are returned to the free-space bitmap.
5. **Atomicity Guarantee:** On most modern UNIX file systems (ext4, XFS, Btrfs), this operation is **atomic** — it either succeeds completely or fails without side-effects.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic Form | Description | Unit / Domain |
|---------|---------------|-------------|---------------|
| Directory Entries Count | $n = \vert \text{children} \vert$ | Number of user-visible entries (excluding `.` and `..`) | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| Read Time Complexity | $T_{\text{read}} = O(n)$ | Linear in number of entries | Operations |
| Delete Time Complexity | $T_{\text{del}} = O(1)$ | Constant, since empty-check is a single inode field read | Operations |
| Recursive Delete (depth $d$) | $T_{\text{rec}} = O\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{d} n_i\right)$ | Sum of entries across all sublevels | Operations |
| Space Released | $S_{\text{released}} = B_{\text{dir}} + B_{\text{inode}}$ | Directory block + inode block size | Bytes |
| Block Size Constraint | $B_{\text{dir}} \leq B_{\text{fs}}$ | Directory size capped by file-system block size | Bytes |
| Entries Per Block | $k = \left\lfloor B_{\text{fs}} \,/\, \text{sizeof(dirent)} \right\rfloor$ | Maximum entries in a single block | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| Directory Link Count | $L = 2 + s$ | 2 for parent entries (`.` and `..`) plus `s` subdirectories | Integer $\geq 2$ |
| Symbolic Constraint | $n_{\text{empty}} = 0$ | Required state for `rmdir()` to succeed | Boolean |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Build Systems (Make, Maven):** Recursively traverse source trees, reading every directory to locate `Makefile` or `pom.xml` files.
- **Backup Utilities (rsync, tar):** Walk the directory tree reading entries to construct the archive manifest; delete obsolete snapshots.
- **Cloud Storage (AWS S3 Sync):** Server-side directory listings are used to reconcile local-vs-remote state.
- **Container Runtimes (Docker):** Use `opendir`/`readdir` to enumerate `overlayfs` layers during image construction.
- **Antivirus & File Integrity Monitors:** Continuously read directory entries to detect new or modified files.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Entries-Per-Block Formula

The maximum number of directory entries that can fit in a single file-system block is a fundamental disk-layout calculation. Let us derive it rigorously.

**Given:**
- File-system block size $B_{\text{fs}}$ (in bytes), commonly **4096 bytes** on ext4.
- Size of one `dirent` record on Linux: $S_{\text{dirent}} = 19 + \text{strlen}(d\_name) + 1$ bytes (fixed prefix plus null-terminated name).

**Worst-Case Bound (filename length = 255 characters):**

$$
S_{\text{dirent}}^{\max} = 19 + 255 + 1 = 275 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 1:** Determine raw entries per block under maximum filename length.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{\min} &= \left\lfloor \frac{B_{\text{fs}}}{S_{\text{dirent}}^{\max}} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
k_{\min} &= \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{275} \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
k_{\min} &= \left\lfloor 14.895 \right\rfloor \\[6pt]
k_{\min} &= 14 \text{ entries per block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Determine raw entries per block under typical filename length (8.3-format, e.g., `report.txt` = 10 chars):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{dirent}}^{\text{typ}} &= 19 + 10 + 1 = 30 \text{ bytes} \\[6pt]
k_{\text{typ}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{4096}{30} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 136.53 \right\rfloor = 136 \text{ entries per block}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.  
On ext4 with 4 KiB blocks, a *small* directory fits in a single inode block, but as soon as the entry count exceeds $k_{\min}$ (or $k_{\text{typ}}$), the kernel transparently promotes the directory to use **htree (hash tree) indexing**, reducing the lookup complexity from $O(n)$ to $O(\log n)$.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Walk-Through of `readdir()` Iteration

Consider a directory `project/` with entries ordered on disk as: `Makefile`, `src/`, `docs/`, `README.md`.

**Step 1 — Open:**
The kernel resolves `project/`, verifies `x` permission, allocates a `DIR` struct, and binds an underlying file descriptor (e.g., fd = 7).

**Step 2 — First readdir():**
The kernel reads the first on-disk entry, copies it into the static `dirent`, and returns a pointer to it.

$$
\text{return value} = \&(\text{dirent}\{d\_name = \text{"Makefile"}, d\_type = \text{DT\_REG}, d\_ino = 10245\})
$$

**Step 3 — Second readdir():**
Cursor advances by $d\_off$ bytes; returns entry for `src/`.

**Step 4 — Continue** until the last entry returns `NULL`.

**Step 5 — Closedir:**
fd = 7 is closed; `DIR` struct is freed.

**Complexity Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{read}}(n) &= c_{\text{open}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{\text{read}} + c_{\text{close}} \\[6pt]
&= O(1) + n \cdot O(1) + O(1) \\[6pt]
&= O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c_{\text{open}}$, $c_{\text{read}}$, $c_{\text{close}}$ are constant system-call overheads.

---

### 3.3 Recursive Deletion Algorithm and Its Complexity

Because `rmdir()` only handles empty directories, user-space tools implement **recursive deletion** as follows:

**Algorithm `recursive_delete(path)`:**

```
1.  opendir(path)
2.  while (entry = readdir(dir)) != NULL:
3.      if entry.d_name == "." or "..": skip
4.      construct child_path = path + "/" + entry.d_name
5.      if entry.d_type == DT_DIR:
6.          recursive_delete(child_path)    # depth-first descent
7.      else:
8.          unlink(child_path)              # delete file
9.  closedir(dir)
10. rmdir(path)                             # now empty, succeeds
```

**Step 1:** Compute the worst-case recursion depth. In a perfectly balanced tree of branching factor $b$ and height $h$, the depth at the deepest leaf is:

$$
d_{\max} = h = \log_{b} N
$$

where $N$ is the total number of leaf nodes (files).

**Step 2:** Compute total operations. Each non-root node triggers exactly one `rmdir` or `unlink`, and each directory incurs one `opendir` + one `readdir` loop. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{rec}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{d_{\max}} \left( O(n_i) + O(1) \right) \\[6pt]
&= O\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{d_{\max}} n_i \right) = O(N)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $n_i$ is the entry count at depth $i$ and $N$ is the total file-system object count.

**Step 3:** Verify space complexity. The recursion stack holds one frame per directory along the current root-to-leaf path:

$$
S_{\text{stack}} = O(d_{\max}) = O(\log_{b} N)
$$

---

### 3.4 Failure-Mode Decision Matrix for `rmdir()`

| Pre-condition checked by kernel | Failure Code | Marking Guidance |
|---------------------------------|--------------|------------------|
| Path resolves to a valid inode | `ENOENT` | "No such file or directory" |
| Caller has write+execute on parent | `EACCES` | "Permission denied" |
| Target is a directory (not a file) | `ENOTDIR` | "Not a directory" |
| Target directory has zero user-entries | `ENOTEMPTY` | "Directory not empty" |
| File system is not read-only | `EROFS` | "Read-only file system" |
| Path component is on different mount point with policy block | `EXDEV` | "Cross-device link" |
