## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Directory Hierarchy Under Analysis

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["root fs"] --> B["home"]
    A --> C["etc"]
    A --> D["var"]
    B --> E["alice"]
    B --> F["bob"]
    E --> G["documents"]
    E --> H["projects"]
    G --> I["notes.txt"]
    G --> J["resume.pdf"]
    H --> K["Makefile"]
    H --> L["src"]
    L --> M["main.c"]
    L --> N["utils.c"]
    H --> O["README.md"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The figure depicts a typical Linux home directory tree. To delete `projects/`, the kernel requires that `Makefile`, `src/`, `README.md` (and the entire subtree of `src/`) be removed first.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Reading a Directory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    User["User Application"] --> CallOpen["opendir path"]
    CallOpen --> Kernel1["Kernel: Path resolution + permission check"]
    Kernel1 --> AllocDir["Allocate DIR struct and file descriptor"]
    AllocDir --> ReturnHandle["Return DIR handle to user"]

    User --> CallRead["readdir handle"]
    CallRead --> FetchBlock["Fetch next directory block from disk cache"]
    FetchBlock --> CopyEntry["Copy dirent to user buffer"]
    CopyEntry --> ReturnEntry["Return pointer or NULL if end"]

    User --> CallClose["closedir handle"]
    CallClose --> ReleaseFD["Release underlying file descriptor"]
    ReleaseFD --> FreeDir["Free DIR struct"]
```

---

### 4.3 Recursive Delete State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Opened: opendir path
    Opened --> Iterating: while entry = readdir
    Iterating --> Iterating: entry is file - unlink
    Iterating --> Iterating: entry is directory - recursive call
    Iterating --> Closed: readdir returns NULL
    Closed --> Deleted: rmdir path - success
    Closed --> Failed: rmdir path - ENOTEMPTY or EACCES
    Deleted --> [*]
    Failed --> [*]
```

---

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Race Condition Awareness

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Process
    participant K as Kernel
    participant FS as File System

    U->>K: opendir /home/alice/projects
    K->>FS: validate parent execute perms
    FS-->>K: OK
    K-->>U: DIR handle

    U->>K: readdir loop
    loop For each entry
        K->>FS: read next dirent block
        FS-->>K: dirent bytes
        K-->>U: struct dirent pointer
    end

    U->>K: rmdir /home/alice/projects
    K->>FS: check nlink and child count
    FS-->>K: ENOTEMPTY (children exist)
    K-->>U: -1, errno set
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kernel-vs-User-Space Boundary:** Notice that `opendir`/`readdir`/`closedir` ultimately delegate to `open(2)`, `getdents(2)`, and `close(2)` system calls. The `<dirent.h>` library is a thin POSIX wrapper over these raw syscalls.
