## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Mandate (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A carries **3 marks** per question (short answer, ~30–40 words). Part B carries **14 marks** with internal choice, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solutions below are modeled on actual KTU valuation keys.

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAMT595]**

*Define the `opendir()` and `readdir()` system calls. Mention the header file in which they are declared.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`opendir()` is a POSIX library function declared in `<dirent.h>` that opens a directory stream corresponding to the pathname argument and returns a pointer of type `DIR *`, or `NULL` on failure with `errno` set. `readdir()` reads the next directory entry from the open directory stream and returns a pointer to a `struct dirent`, or `NULL` when the end of the directory is reached or an error occurs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAMT595]**

*Why does the `rmdir()` system call fail if the target directory is not empty? What error code is returned?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`rmdir()` fails on a non-empty directory to enforce the **safety invariant** that prevents accidental, recursive data loss; only an explicit user-driven operation (e.g., `rm -r`) may remove a populated directory. The kernel returns `-1` and sets `errno` to **`ENOTEMPTY`** ("Directory not empty"). Two entries (`.` and `..`) are reserved and do not count as content.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the structure `struct dirent` used in POSIX directory operations. List any **five** fields with their data types and purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a C program snippet, illustrate the steps involved in **reading a directory** using `opendir()`, `readdir()`, and `closedir()`. Explain each call. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Structure of `struct dirent`* **(7 marks)**

The `struct dirent` is defined in the POSIX header `<dirent.h>` and represents a single directory entry returned by `readdir()`. Five important fields are:

| Field | Data Type | Purpose |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| `d_ino` | `ino_t` | Inode number — unique identifier of the file within the file system |
| `d_off` | `off_t` | Offset of the next `dirent` from the start of the directory file |
| `d_reclen` | `unsigned short` | Length (in bytes) of this record; used to step to the next entry |
| `d_type` | `unsigned char` | File type — values include `DT_REG`, `DT_DIR`, `DT_LNK`, `DT_UNKNOWN` |
| `d_name[]` | `char[256]` | Null-terminated file or subdirectory name (max 255 chars + NUL) |

The structure is used to abstract a directory's record layout so that user programs can iterate entries without knowing the on-disk encoding (e.g., ext4 linear or htree).

**(b)** *C Program to Read a Directory* **(7 marks)**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <dirent.h>
#include <errno.h>
#include <string.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    DIR *dirp;
    struct dirent *entry;

    if (argc != 2) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s <directory>\n", argv[0]);
        return 1;
    }

    dirp = opendir(argv[1]);           /* Step 1: Open directory */
    if (dirp == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "opendir failed: %s\n", strerror(errno));
        return 1;
    }

    errno = 0;
    while ((entry = readdir(dirp)) != NULL) {  /* Step 2: Iterate */
        printf("Inode=%lu  Name=%s  Type=%d\n",
               (unsigned long)entry->d_ino,
               entry->d_name,
               entry->d_type);
        errno = 0;
    }

    if (errno != 0)
        perror("readdir");

    closedir(dirp);                    /* Step 3: Close */
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of each call (7 marks):**

1. **`opendir(argv[1])`** — Opens the directory whose path is in `argv[1]`. Returns a `DIR *` handle. Fails (returns `NULL`) if the path doesn't exist, isn't a directory, or the process lacks execute permission on any path component.
2. **`readdir(dirp)`** — Returns a pointer to a `struct dirent` describing the next entry. Returns `NULL` on end-of-stream or error; differentiate via `errno`.
3. **`closedir(dirp)`** — Releases the underlying file descriptor and frees the `DIR` structure. After this call, `dirp` must not be reused.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe the conditions under which `rmdir()` succeeds and fails. List at least **four** distinct error conditions with their `errno` values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a recursive C function `delete_dir(const char *path)` that deletes a directory and all its contents. Explain the logic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Conditions for `rmdir()` Success/Failure* **(7 marks)**

`rmdir()` succeeds **only if** every one of the following preconditions is true:

1. The path resolves to a valid existing directory.
2. The process has write permission on the *parent* directory and execute permission on every path component.
3. The target directory contains **zero user entries** (only `.` and `..` are permitted).
4. The file system is mounted read-write.

**Failure conditions** (each sets `errno`):

| Pre-condition Violated | `errno` Value | Symbolic Name |
|------------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Path does not exist | **2** | `ENOENT` |
| Path component is not a directory | **20** | `ENOTDIR` |
| Target directory contains files/subdirectories | **39** | `ENOTEMPTY` |
| Process lacks write/execute permission | **13** | `EACCES` |
| File system is mounted read-only | **30** | `EROFS` |
| I/O error during inode update | **5** | `EIO` |

**(b)** *Recursive Directory Deletion* **(7 marks)**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <dirent.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/stat.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <errno.h>

int delete_dir(const char *path) {
    struct dirent *entry;
    DIR *dirp = opendir(path);
    if (dirp == NULL) {
        perror("opendir");
        return -1;
    }

    while ((entry = readdir(dirp)) != NULL) {
        /* Skip the "." and ".." pseudo-entries */
        if (strcmp(entry->d_name, ".") == 0 ||
            strcmp(entry->d_name, "..") == 0)
            continue;

        char child_path[4096];
        snprintf(child_path, sizeof(child_path),
                 "%s/%s", path, entry->d_name);

        if (entry->d_type == DT_DIR) {
            /* Recurse into subdirectory */
            if (delete_dir(child_path) != 0) {
                closedir(dirp);
                return -1;
            }
        } else {
            /* Leaf file - unlink directly */
            if (unlink(child_path) != 0) {
                perror("unlink");
                closedir(dirp);
                return -1;
            }
        }
    }

    closedir(dirp);

    /* Directory is now empty - rmdir will succeed */
    if (rmdir(path) != 0) {
        perror("rmdir");
        return -1;
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Logic Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Open the directory** at `path`. If opening fails (e.g., `ENOENT`, `EACCES`), abort.
2. **Iterate entries** using `readdir()` in a `while` loop.
3. **Filter special entries** `.` and `..` to prevent infinite recursion.
4. **Construct child path** by concatenating `path` and `entry->d_name`.
5. **Branch on type:** if `DT_DIR`, recurse (`delete_dir(child_path)`); if `DT_REG` or other, call `unlink()` to remove the file.
6. **Close the directory stream** with `closedir()` once iteration is complete.
7. **Invoke `rmdir(path)`** — by this point all descendants are gone, so the empty-directory invariant holds and the call succeeds.
8. **Time complexity** is $O(N)$ over total objects; **space complexity** is $O(d_{\max})$ recursion depth.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Confusing `rmdir()` with `rm -r`. `rmdir()` deletes *only empty* directories. Writing code that calls `rmdir` inside a loop without checking emptyness is wrong.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to handle `.` and `..` in recursive delete. This causes **infinite recursion** and stack overflow. Examiners specifically look for this check.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Using `remove()` interchangeably with `rmdir()`. `remove()` is a `<stdio.h>` function that maps to `unlink()` for files and `rmdir()` for directories, but on some systems it does *not* recurse.
> - **Common Mistake #4 (–1 mark):** Not setting `errno = 0` before the `readdir` loop. Since `readdir` returns `NULL` for both end-of-stream *and* error, you must check `errno` after a `NULL` to distinguish the two.
> - **Common Mistake #5 (–1 mark):** Omitting the `<dirent.h>` header in code listings. KTU examiners deduct for missing standard includes.
> - **Common Mistake #6 (–2 marks):** Failing to mention the **permission** requirement on parent directory. `rmdir` needs `w+x` on parent; only `x` on the target.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Directory Reading** is a four-phase operation: `opendir` → loop of `readdir` → optional `rewinddir` → `closedir`.
- ✅ The **`DIR *` handle is opaque** and **not thread-safe**.
- ✅ `readdir()` returns `NULL` at end-of-stream **and** on error — always check `errno` to distinguish.
- ✅ The `dirent` structure's key fields are `d_ino`, `d_off`, `d_reclen`, `d_type`, and `d_name`.
- ✅ `rmdir()` is **strictly restricted to empty directories** — non-empty returns `ENOTEMPTY` (errno 39).
- ✅ Recursive deletion is a **user-space** algorithm, not a kernel primitive.
- ✅ Always skip `.` and `..` to avoid infinite recursion.
- ✅ Block-size bound: a 4 KiB block holds ~14 entries (worst-case filename) to ~136 entries (short filenames).
- ✅ Directory lookup on ext4 is $O(\log n)$ due to htree indexing, but `readdir` iteration is still $O(n)$.
- ✅ Link count of a directory $L = 2 + s$ where $s$ is the number of immediate subdirectories.
- ✅ Time complexity: read $O(n)$, empty delete $O(1)$, recursive delete $O(N)$ time, $O(d)$ space.
- ✅ File-system **atomicity** for `rmdir` guarantees no partial state on crash.
- ✅ Real-world users: build systems, backup tools, cloud sync, container runtimes, antivirus monitors.
- ✅ KTU expects the **complete `dirent` definition** and **at least four `errno` codes** for full marks on delete questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permission Bits and Access Control Lists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Permission Bits and Access Control Lists # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Permission Bits (also called File Mode Bits)** are the fundamental, low-level security mechanism in traditional Unix/Linux file systems. They are a fixed-size set of **9 protection bits** stored in the file's **inode** (index node) metadata. These 9 bits dictate exactly what actions a user is permitted to perform on a file or directory, divided into three distinct classes: **Owner (User)**, **Group**, and **Others (World)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** are an *extended* and *granular* file protection mechanism that supplements the coarse 9-bit model. An ACL is a list of **Access Control Entries (ACEs)**, where each ACE maps a specific user or group identity to a specific set of permissions. ACLs break out of the rigid "owner/group/world" trio and allow per-user or per-group permission assignment. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats these as a **layered security model**: the classical UNIX permission bits form the base layer (always evaluated first), and ACLs act as a refinement layer (consulted when the base layer grants ambiguous or default privileges). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a Company Office Building:** > > - **Permission Bits** are like the **three-color security badge system** painted on every door: > - **Green badge** (Owner) → can enter any room anytime > - **Yellow badge** (Group) → can enter common areas and team rooms > - **Red badge** (Others) → can only enter the reception and washroom > Each door literally has three locks, each responding to one badge color. Simple, fast, but inflexible. > > - **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** are like a **personalized visitor register** kept at the reception. It says: *"Mr. Sharma from the Audit team can also enter Room 305 on Tuesdays"*, *"Ms. John, the intern, can only read files in the archive room but not print"*. This is more flexible, more detailed, but requires a database lookup each time. > > Just as the badge (permission bits) is checked first quickly, and only when a personalized rule applies does the receptionist consult the ACL register. ## 1.3 Core Identities and Constants A UNIX file is associated with exactly **3 numerical identifiers** in its inode: | Identifier | Standard Name | Length | Purpose | |------------|---------------|--------|---------| | **UID** | User Identifier | **32-bit** (typically) | Identifies the file owner uniquely | | **GID** | Group Identifier | **32-bit** (typically) | Identifies the primary owning group | | **Mode** | Permission/Mode bits | **16-bit** (12 used) | Contains the 9 permission bits + 3 special bits | The **9 permission bits** are divided into three triplets, each triplet encoding three primitive rights: **Read (r)**, **Write (w)**, and **Execute (x)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the 9 Permission Bits Every file and directory has its access control encoded as 9 bits arranged from most-significant to least-significant position in the `mode` field of the inode. ``` Bit Position : 8 7 6 | 5 4 3 | 2 1 0 Class : OWNER (U) | GROUP (G) | OTHERS (O) Permission : r w x r w x r w x Octal Digit : 4 2 1 ``` ### 2.1.1 Interpretation of Each Bit For a **regular file**: | Bit | Symbol | Numeric Value | Effect on File | |-----|--------|---------------|----------------| | Read | `r` | **4** | Allows viewing file contents (`cat`, `less`, `cp source`) | | Write | `w` | **2** | Allows modifying file contents (`echo > file`, `vi file`) | | Execute | `x` | **1** | Allows running file as a program/script | For a **directory**, the meaning shifts subtly: | Bit | Symbol | Effect on Directory | |-----|--------|---------------------| | Read | `r` | Allows listing filenames inside (`ls`) | | Write | `w` | Allows adding, renaming, or deleting entries inside | | Execute | `x` | Allows *traversing* into the directory (`cd`) and accessing file metadata | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** On a directory, `x` does **NOT** mean "execute the directory". It means **search/traverse** permission — the ability to use the directory as part of a path. ### 2.2 The 3 Special Bits (Beyond the 9) KTU examiners frequently test these, often lumping them into the "mode" question. They occupy the upper triplet (bits 9, 10, 11) of the 12-bit mode field: | Bit | Name | Octal | Purpose | |-----|------|-------|---------| | **setuid** | Set User ID | **4000** | When set on an *executable file*, the process runs with the **owner's UID**, not the invoker's. | | **setgid** | Set Group ID | **2000** | On an executable, process runs with **owning group's GID**. On a directory, new files inherit the directory's GID. | | **sticky** | Sticky Bit | **1000** | On a directory (e.g., `/tmp`), only the **file owner, directory owner, or root** can delete/rename files inside, even if others have `w` permission. | ### 2.3 Symbolic vs. Octal Notation **Symbolic (mnemonic):** ``` chmod u+rwx, g+rx, o=r file.txt chmod a-x file.txt (a = all) ``` **Octal (numeric):** Sum the digit values for each class: $$\text{Permission Triplet Value} = (r \times 4) + (w \times 2) + (x \times 1)$$ Example: `rwx` = 4+2+1 = **7**, `r-x` = 4+0+1 = **5**, `r--` = 4+0+0 = **4**. A full mode like `rwxr-x---` becomes the octal triple **750**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notation | Notes | |---------|----------------|-----------------|-------| | Permission digit | $P = 4r + 2w + 1x$ | dimensionless integer $\in [0,7]$ | One digit per class | | Full file mode | $M = (\text{special} \times 1000) + (\text{owner} \times 100) + (\text{group} \times 10) + (\text{others})$ | 4-digit octal | e.g., `4755` = setuid + 755 | | Access Decision | Requesting UID match? $\Rightarrow$ owner class. Else GID match? $\Rightarrow$ group class. Else $\Rightarrow$ others class. | boolean test | Evaluated in this **fixed order** | | Directory traversal | Needs `x` on **every** ancestor directory in the path | boolean | `r` on `/home/user` alone is useless without `x` | | ACL default ACE | Inherits to new child files/dirs unless explicitly overridden | boolean flag | Only meaningful on directories | | Effective rights | $\text{EffectiveMask} = \text{ACL\_MASK} \;\&\; \text{Named\_User\_ACE}$ | bitwise AND | The MASK acts as an upper bound on named-user and named-group entries | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility Permission bits and ACLs underpin **every secure multi-user system**: - **Web servers** rely on `setuid` binaries like `passwd` to let ordinary users change a file they don't own. - **Shared project directories** use **setgid** + ACLs so all collaborators can edit without `chmod 777`. - **Public drop-boxes** (university submission portals) use the **sticky bit** to prevent students from deleting each other's submissions. - **Cloud file systems** (AWS S3, NFSv4) emulate ACLs on top of object metadata to support enterprise-scale authorization. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Decoding a Symbolic Mode into Octal **Problem:** A file has the symbolic mode `-rwxr-xr--`. Compute its octal representation. **Step 1 — Identify each class triplet.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Owner triplet} &: rwx \\ \text{Group triplet} &: r-x \\ \text{Others triplet} &: r-- \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the formula $P = 4r + 2w + 1x$ to each triplet.** For **Owner (`rwx`)**: $$ P_{\text{owner}} = (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 $$ For **Group (`r-x`)**: $$ P_{\text{group}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5 $$ For **Others (`r--`)**: $$ P_{\text{others}} = (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 $$ **Step 3 — Concatenate into a single octal string.** $$ \text{Mode} = 754 $$ **Step 4 — Note the leading file-type character.** The full `ls -l` output `-rwxr-xr--` confirms this is a **regular file** (the leading `-`), not a directory (`d`), symlink (`l`), or device file (`c`/`b`). **Final Answer:** `0754` or simply `754`. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Setting Special Bits **Problem:** A system administrator wants an executable file `backup_agent` to: - Always run with the **owner**'s privileges (setuid) - Be readable and executable by **owner and group**, writable only by owner - Have **no access** for others **Step 1 — Build the base 3-digit mode.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Owner} &= rwx = 4+2+1 = 7 \\ \text{Group} &= r-x = 4+0+1 = 5 \\ \text{Others} &= --- = 0+0+0 = 0 \\ \text{Base mode} &= 750 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Add the setuid special bit (octal 4000).** $$ \begin{aligned} M &= 4000 + 750 \\ &= 4750 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Translate to `chmod` command.** ```bash chmod 4750 backup_agent chmod u+s,g=rx,o= backup_agent ``` **Step 4 — Verify with `ls -l`.** Expected display: ``` -rwsr-x--- 1 root backup 2048 Jan 15 10:30 backup_agent ``` Note the `s` in the owner's execute position — this is the visible confirmation of the setuid bit. If the owner lacked `x`, it would show as `S` (capital), indicating setuid is set but the file is not actually executable. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Access Control List (ACL) Evaluation **Problem:** File `report.txt` has the following ACL on a Linux system: | Entry Type | Principal | Permissions | |------------|-----------|-------------| | Owner | `alice` | `rwx` | | Named User | `bob` | `r--` | | Owning Group | `editors` | `rw-` | | Named Group | `reviewers` | `r--` | | Mask | (system) | `r--` | | Others | `*` | `---` | User `bob` (member of group `editors`) attempts to write to `report.txt`. What is the result? **Step 1 — Identify matching ACL entries.** Bob matches: - The **Named User** entry for `bob` with rights `r--` - The **Group** entry for `editors` (because Bob is a member) with rights `rw-` **Step 2 — Apply the MASK restriction.** The MASK entry acts as the **upper bound** for any Named User, Named Group, or Owning Group entry. Effective rights are computed via bitwise AND with the MASK. For the **Named User** entry: $$ E_{\text{user}} = \text{r--} \;\&\; \text{r--} = \text{r--} $$ For the **Owning Group** entry: $$ E_{\text{group}} = \text{rw-} \;\&\; \text{r--} = \text{r--} $$ **Step 3 — Union the effective rights of all matching entries.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{final}} &= E_{\text{user}} \;\vert\; E_{\text{group}} \\ &= \text{r--} \;\vert\; \text{r--} \\ &= \text{r--} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with requested operation.** Bob requests **Write**, but his effective rights are `r--` only. **Final Result:** **Permission Denied (EACCES).** Even though Bob belongs to `editors` which nominally has `rw-`, the MASK clips it down to `r--`, and the write operation fails. > [!NOTE] > **This is the most common KTU pitfall**: students forget that ACL MASK acts as a ceiling. They see "editors has rw-" and assume Bob can write. The MASK overrides this assumption. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common `chmod` Octal Modes | Octal | Symbolic | Meaning | Typical Use Case | |-------|----------|---------|------------------| | `777` | `rwxrwxrwx` | World-writable | **Never use** — severe security hole | | `755` | `rwxr-xr-x` | Owner full, others read+execute | Standard for shared scripts, web content | | `700` | `rwx------` | Owner only | Private scripts, sensitive executables | | `644` | `rw-r--r--` | Owner write, others read | Default for new files | | `600` | `rw-------` | Owner only read+write | Private config files (e.g., SSH keys) | | `4755` | `rwsr-xr-x` | setuid + 755 | Privilege-elevating programs (e.g., `passwd`, `ping`) | | `2755` | `rwxr-sr-x` | setgid + 755 | Group-shared executables | | `1777` | `rwxrwxrwt` | sticky + 777 | `/tmp` — anyone can create, only owner can delete | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Access Decision Flowchart The following Mermaid diagram captures the **exact** order in which the kernel evaluates file access when a process makes a system call such as `open()`, `read()`, or `write()`. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process requests file access] --> B{Is process UID equal
to file owner UID?} B -- Yes --> C[Apply OWNER permission bits] B -- No --> D{Is process GID equal
to file group GID
OR is process in any
supplementary group?} D -- Yes --> E[Apply GROUP permission bits] D -- No --> F[Apply OTHERS permission bits] C --> G{Permission sufficient
for requested operation?} E --> G F --> G G -- Yes --> H[GRANT access] G -- No --> I[DENY access EACCES] ``` > [!NOTE] > The decision is **strictly first-match-wins**. If the requesting UID matches the owner, the group and others bits are **never consulted**, even if owner permission is insufficient. ## 4.2 ACL Storage Layout in an Inode/Extended Attribute When a filesystem (e.g., `ext4`, `xfs`, `ntfs`) supports ACLs, the ACL is stored as an extended attribute associated with the inode. The structure is a sequence of ACE records. ```mermaid flowchart LR inode[Inode Metadata Block] --> uidField[UID field 32-bit] inode --> gidField[GID field 32-bit] inode --> modeField[Mode field 16-bit] inode --> xattr[Extended Attribute
system.posix_acl_access] xattr --> ace1[ACE 1 Owner User alice rwx] xattr --> ace2[ACE 2 Named User bob r--] xattr --> ace3[ACE 3 Owning Group editors rw-] xattr --> ace4[ACE 4 Named Group reviewers r--] xattr --> ace5[ACE 5 MASK r--] xattr --> ace6[ACE 6 Others star triple dash] ace5 -.limits.-> ace2 ace5 -.limits.-> ace3 ace5 -.limits.-> ace4 ``` ## 4.3 Permission Bit Functional Architecture This block diagram illustrates how the three permission classes relate to the underlying mode bits and how ACLs integrate with them. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inodeLayer[Inode Mode Field] specialBits[Special Bits
setuid setgid sticky] ownerBits[Owner Triplet
r w x] groupBits[Group Triplet
r w x] otherBits[Others Triplet
r w x] end inodeLayer --> classicalCheck{Classical UNIX Check} classicalCheck -- Match --> grantOwner[Use Owner Bits] classicalCheck -- No Match --> groupCheck{Process in Group?} groupCheck -- Yes --> grantGroup[Use Group Bits] groupCheck -- No --> grantOther[Use Other Bits] grantOwner --> aclCheck{ACL present
on file?} grantGroup --> aclCheck grantOther --> aclCheck aclCheck -- Yes --> refineACL[Apply ACL refinements
via MASK intersection] aclCheck -- No --> finalDecision[Final Access Verdict] refineACL --> finalDecision ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between the UNIX permission bit model and Access Control Lists. When is an ACL preferred over classical permission bits?** **Model Answer (3 marks — allocation: 1.5 each):** | Aspect | Permission Bits | Access Control Lists | |--------|-----------------|----------------------| | Granularity | Only 3 fixed classes (owner, group, others) | Arbitrary number of named users/groups | | Storage | 9 bits inside inode | Variable-length list in extended attribute | | Flexibility | Coarse — cannot grant `bob` special access if `bob` is not in the owning group | Fine-grained — can grant `bob` individually | | Performance | Faster (bit-mask check) | Slightly slower (list traversal + MASK compute) | ACLs are preferred when **multiple distinct users need different individual rights** on the same file, or when the rigid owner/group/world model is too coarse — typical in collaborative development, academic project repositories, and enterprise databases. *(Examiner note: 3 marks require at least 2 clear points of difference + 1 use-case justification.)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Explain the purpose of the "sticky bit" with an example directory.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **sticky bit** (octal **1000**) is a special mode bit applied to **directories**. When set, it restricts file deletion and renaming within that directory: even if a user has `w` (write) permission on the directory, they can only delete or rename files **they themselves own**. The directory owner and `root` are exempt from this restriction. **Classic example:** The `/tmp` directory is world-writable (`rwxrwxrwt`) so that any user can create temporary files. Without the sticky bit, user `alice` could delete user `bob`'s temp files because she has write access to the directory. With the sticky bit set, Alice can create her own files but cannot delete Bob's. The `t` in `rwxrwxrwt` is the visible confirmation; if execute is missing it appears as `T`. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 Choice 1]` **Q: (a)** Explain the UNIX file permission model in detail. Describe the meaning of read, write, and execute permissions for both **regular files** and **directories** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the **setuid**, **setgid**, and **sticky** bits? Explain their purpose with one real-world example for each, and demonstrate how they are represented in octal mode notation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **1. Permission Model Overview:** *(1 mark)* UNIX associates every file with exactly one **owner (UID)** and one **owning group (GID)**. The inode stores 9 permission bits grouped into three triplets: **owner (u)**, **group (g)**, and **others (o)**. Access decisions are made in this strict order — first owner match, then group match, then others. **2. Regular File Permissions:** *(2 marks)* | Bit | Symbol | Numerical Weight | Effect | |-----|--------|------------------|--------| | Read | `r` | **4** | Allows process to read file content (`cat file`, `less file`) | | Write | `w` | **2** | Allows modification of file content (`echo data > file`, `vi file`) | | Execute | `x` | **1** | Allows kernel to load the file as a program (`./script.sh`) | **Example:** A script `run.sh` with mode `-rwxr-x---` (octal `750`): - Owner can read, modify, and execute it. - Group members can read and execute, but cannot modify it. - Others have no access. **3. Directory Permissions:** *(2 marks)* | Bit | Effect on Directory | |-----|---------------------| | `r` | Allows listing file names (`ls directory`) | | `w` | Allows adding new files, renaming, or deleting entries inside | | `x` | Allows **traversing** into the directory (`cd directory`) and accessing inodes of its files | **Critical nuance:** To access a file at path `/home/alice/notes.txt`, a process needs `x` permission on **every** ancestor (`/`, `/home`, `/home/alice`). Lacking `x` on any one of them makes the file inaccessible even if its own permissions are `rwxrwxrwx`. **4. Access Decision Algorithm:** *(2 marks)* $$ \text{Decision}(U, F) = \begin{cases} \text{Apply } U \text{ class if } U.\text{uid} = F.\text{owner} \\ \text{Apply } G \text{ class if } U.\text{uid} \neq F.\text{owner} \;\text{and}\; (U.\text{gid} = F.\text{group} \;\vert\; U \in F.\text{group}) \\ \text{Apply } O \text{ class otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Only **one** class is consulted; the kernel does **not** OR the rights from multiple classes. #### Model Solution for (b): **1. The Three Special Bits:** *(2 marks)* These bits occupy the upper triplet of the 12-bit mode field (positions 9, 10, 11) and are independent of the standard 9 permission bits. | Bit | Octal Value | Symbol in `ls` | |-----|-------------|----------------| | **setuid** | **4000** | `s` in owner-execute position | | **setgid** | **2000** | `s` in group-execute position | | **sticky** | **1000** | `t` in other-execute position | **2. setuid — Real Example:** *(1.5 marks)* The `/usr/bin/passwd` command must modify `/etc/shadow`, a file owned by `root` and not world-writable. To allow ordinary users to change their own password, `passwd` is installed as a **setuid root** binary: `-rwsr-xr-x 1 root root ... passwd` (mode `4755`). When any user invokes it, the resulting process runs with effective UID = 0 (root), enabling the shadow-file write. After the write, the process drops privileges. **3. setgid — Real Example:** *(1.5 marks)* On a **directory**, setgid (`chmod g+s shared_dir`) causes all newly created files inside to automatically inherit the directory's GID rather than the creator's primary group. This is invaluable in shared project folders: a project directory `/proj` owned by group `devs` with setgid set ensures any file dropped into it belongs to `devs`, so all team members can collaborate via standard group permissions. **4. sticky — Real Example:** *(1 mark)* The `/tmp` directory is a world-writable shared space. Sticky bit (`1777`) ensures users cannot delete each other's files. Mathematically: a user $U$ can delete file $F$ in directory $D$ only if $$ U = F.\text{owner} \;\vert\; U = D.\text{owner} \;\vert\; U.\text{euid} = 0 $$ **5. Octal Representation Summary:** *(1 mark)* $$ M_{\text{full}} = S_{\text{setuid}} \cdot 4000 + S_{\text{setgid}} \cdot 2000 + S_{\text{sticky}} \cdot 1000 + P_{\text{owner}} \cdot 100 + P_{\text{group}} \cdot 10 + P_{\text{others}} $$ --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 Choice 2]` *(Alternative)* **Q: (a)** Describe the structure and components of an **Access Control List (ACL)**. Explain the role of the **MASK** entry with a worked numerical example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)** model with the **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** model. In which scenario would a UNIX system primarily using permission bits and ACLs be classified? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **1. ACL Structure:** *(2.5 marks)* A POSIX ACL is an **ordered list of Access Control Entries (ACEs)** stored as an extended attribute on the file. Each ACE has three fields: | Field | Description | |-------|-------------| | **Tag Type** | What kind of principal (owner, named user, owning group, named group, mask, others) | | **Qualifier** | The UID or GID for named entries (omitted for owner, mask, others) | | **Permission Set** | The r/w/x triplet for this entry | Standard ordering of ACEs in a POSIX ACL: 1. `ACL_USER_OBJ` — owner (e.g., `alice`) 2. `ACL_USER` — named users (e.g., `bob`, `carol`) 3. `ACL_GROUP_OBJ` — owning group (e.g., `editors`) 4. `ACL_GROUP` — named groups (e.g., `reviewers`) 5. `ACL_MASK` — upper bound for entries 2, 3, 4 6. `ACL_OTHER` — fallback for everyone else **2. The MASK Entry — Purpose:** *(2 marks)* The MASK was introduced when POSIX.1e extended the classical 3-class model. It defines the **maximum permissions** that any Named User, Owning Group, or Named Group entry can grant. Without it, modifying the owning group's rights could accidentally elevate or restrict other named entries. Effective rights computation: $$ E_{\text{entry}} = P_{\text{entry}} \;\text{bitwise-AND}\; P_{\text{MASK}} $$ **3. Worked Numerical Example:** *(2.5 marks)* File `report.txt` ACL: | Tag | Principal | Stated Permissions | Octal | |-----|-----------|--------------------|-------| | USER_OBJ | alice | rwx | 7 | | USER | bob | rwx | 7 | | GROUP_OBJ | editors | rwx | 7 | | GROUP | reviewers | rwx | 7 | | MASK | — | r-- | **4** | | OTHER | * | --- | 0 | User `bob` requests **write** access. - Stated rights for `bob`: `rwx` = **7** - MASK: `r--` = **4** - Bitwise AND: `rwx & r-- = r--` = **4** So Bob's **effective rights** = `r--`. The write bit is masked away. **Access denied.** Now suppose the administrator realizes this and runs `setfacl -m m:rw report.txt`. The MASK becomes `rw-` = **6**. Recomputing: `rwx & rw- = rw-` = **6**. Bob can now read **and write**. The MASK alone changed Bob's effective rights without altering any individual ACE — illustrating why MASK is the recommended control point for ACL modification. #### Model Solution for (b): **1. DAC vs MAC Comparison:** *(4 marks)* | Aspect | DAC (Discretionary) | MAC (Mandatory) | |--------|---------------------|------------------| | Policy authority | Resource **owner** decides who gets access | **System policy** (admin/security kernel) decides | | Flexibility | High — owner can `chmod` freely | Low — users cannot override policy | | Basis | Identity of user (UID/GID) | **Security labels / clearance levels** (e.g., Top Secret, Secret) | | Enforcement | Kernel checks UID/GID match | Kernel + security module (SELinux, AppArmor) enforces label hierarchy | | Typical use | Personal workstations, Unix/Linux defaults | Military, government, high-security enterprise | | Vulnerability | Trojan horse can inherit owner's rights | More resistant to Trojan attacks | | Example OS | Standard Linux, Windows NTFS | SELinux-enabled Linux, Trusted Solaris | **2. Classification of UNIX Permission Bits + ACLs:** *(3 marks)* A standard UNIX system using only **permission bits and POSIX ACLs** is firmly in the **DAC** category. **Justification:** - The file **owner** can `chmod` or `setfacl` to grant or revoke access at will — discretionary control. - ACLs, while more granular, still permit the owner to manipulate ACEs without system-level intervention. - There is **no concept of security labels** (Top Secret vs Secret) — only identity-based authorization. - The kernel does not enforce any global, non-overridable policy; the owner is the ultimate authority over their files. A UNIX system only becomes MAC when augmented with frameworks such as **SELinux**, **AppArmor**, **TOMOYO**, or **Smack**, which impose additional label-based mandatory rules on top of the DAC base. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing directory `x` with file `x`:** Directory `x` means *traverse/search*, not *execute directory*. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Ignoring the MASK in ACL questions:** Students compute effective rights using only the stated ACE, forgetting that MASK restricts them. Nearly **50%** of ACL questions in KTU papers hinge on MASK. > 3. **Treating permission bits as additive:** Many students wrongly assume the kernel OR's all three classes. It does not — it uses **first match only**. > 4. **Forgetting that `chmod` does not change ACL MASK in old kernels:** A common trap when comparing `chmod` vs `setfacl`. The MASK survives a `chmod` on the group bits in some implementations. > 5. **Misidentifying `S` vs `s` in `ls -l`:** Capital `S` means special bit set but no execute; lowercase `s` means both. Examiners reward students who notice this nuance. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Permission Bits & ACLs** - **9 permission bits = 3 classes × 3 rights.** Classes are `u` (owner), `g` (group), `o` (others). Rights are `r` (4), `w` (2), `x` (1). - **Octal formula:** $P_{\text{class}} = 4r + 2w + 1x$. Full mode is the 3-digit (or 4-digit with specials) concatenation. - **Access decision is first-match-wins**, not OR-of-all. Owner match overrides everything. - **Directory `x` = traverse**, not execute. Without `x` on every ancestor directory, even `chmod 777` on the file does not help. - **Three special bits:** setuid (`4000`), setgid (`2000`), sticky (`1000`). They occupy the 4th leading octal digit. - **setuid on file** → process runs with owner's UID. Example: `/usr/bin/passwd`. - **setgid on directory** → new files inherit directory's GID. Used for shared project folders. - **sticky on directory** → only owner/root can delete files inside. Used on `/tmp`. - **ACL = list of ACEs.** Stored as extended attribute; consulted after classical bits. - **Standard ACE order:** USER_OBJ, USER, GROUP_OBJ, GROUP, MASK, OTHER. - **MASK is the ceiling** for Named User, Owning Group, and Named Group entries. Effective rights = ACE AND MASK. - **Default ACLs** exist only on directories and propagate to newly created child files/directories unless overridden. - **DAC vs MAC:** Plain UNIX permission bits and ACLs implement **DAC** — owner has full discretion. MAC (SELinux) is a separate, mandatory, label-based layer added on top. - **Common safe defaults:** files `644`, scripts `755`, private keys `600`, shared dirs `2775`, public drop-boxes `1777`. - **Tools to remember:** `chmod`, `chown`, `chgrp`, `umask`, `getfacl`, `setfacl`, `ls -l`, `stat`. - **Pitfall to avoid:** The `umask` value subtracts from the *requested* mode at file creation, so `umask 022` on a script created with default `777` yields `755`.

topics.title: Mounting a File System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mounting a File System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**File System Mounting** is the operating system procedure by which the kernel makes a *logical file system* (residing on a physical or virtual storage device) accessible to the user by attaching its **root directory** to an existing **mount point** within the system’s unified directory hierarchy (the *namespace*).

According to the **KTU 2024 Operating System Concepts syllabus**, every newly initialized file system must be explicitly attached to the directory tree before its files and directories can be accessed. The mechanism that performs this attachment is implemented at the **Virtual File System (VFS) layer** of the kernel, allowing heterogeneous file systems (ext4, NTFS, FAT32, NFS) to be unified under a single namespace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *"Mounting is the act of associating a file system with a directory in the existing file hierarchy. The directory on which a file system is mounted is called the **mount point**, and the contents of the mounted file system replace the previous contents of that directory."*

The data structure that records all active mount associations is called the **Mount Table** (sometimes referred to as the *mount list* or *mount information table*).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Library Bookshelf" Model)

Imagine your computer's directory tree is a **large empty library building with numbered rooms** (rooms = directories). When you buy a **new bookshelf full of books** (a new hard disk partition containing a file system), the books are physically present, but **the library has no idea they exist**. You must:

1. **Carry the bookshelf into the library.**
2. **Place it in a specific empty room** (this room becomes the *mount point*).
3. **Paint a sign on the door** of that room indicating what bookshelf was placed inside (this sign is the *mount table entry*).

Now, anyone entering that room and its sub-rooms can browse the new books using the library's catalog system. This entire act of carrying the shelf in, placing it, and labeling the door is what we call **mounting**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-life OS equivalent:** When you insert a **USB pen drive** into your laptop, the OS automatically detects it and mounts it under `/media/username/USB_LABEL` (Linux) or `This PC > D:` (Windows). The instant you "safely eject" it, the OS **unmounts** the file system — you can no longer access files through that path.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mount Point** | The existing directory in the directory tree where the new file system is attached. |
| **Mount Table** | A kernel-resident data structure storing the device, mount point, and file system type. |
| **Root File System** | The file system mounted at `/` (Linux) or `C:\` (Windows) at boot time. |
| **Volume** | A logical storage unit (partition) that contains a file system. |
| **VFS (Virtual File System)** | A kernel abstraction layer enabling uniform access to multiple file system types. |
| **Unmounting** | The reverse operation — detaching a file system from its mount point. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** *"Mount = Map a storage device to a Directory"*. The 'M' in **M**ount stands for **M**apping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Mounting Required?

The kernel cannot simply read raw bytes off a disk sector. It needs a structured way to:

1. **Track open files** (file descriptors, file pointers).
2. **Enforce access permissions** (read/write/execute).
3. **Cache frequently used blocks** (buffer cache / page cache).
4. **Synchronize with disk state** (write-back policies).

Mounting resolves all four by:

- Verifying the **file system signature** (superblock magic number).
- Initializing the **in-core (memory) copy of the superblock**.
- Setting up the **VFS inode and dentry cache** for the new file system.
- Updating the **mount table** so `pathname resolution` knows which file system owns which subtree.

---

## 2.2 The Mounting Procedure — Operational Steps

The OS executes the following sequence when a `mount()` system call is invoked:

**Step 1 — Device Identification:**
The kernel locates the block device (e.g., `/dev/sda2`) requested by the user.

**Step 2 — File System Type Resolution:**
The VFS layer determines the file system driver (ext4, NTFS, FAT, NFS) to invoke. The user passes this as a parameter: `mount -t ext4 /dev/sda2 /mnt/data`.

**Step 3 — Superblock Validation:**
The on-disk superblock is read, and its **magic number** is checked. If valid, the file system driver parses its structure (block size, inode count, free block bitmap).

**Step 4 — In-Core Mount Structure Creation:**
A `struct vfsmount` (Linux) or equivalent kernel object is allocated in memory, holding pointers to the superblock, root inode, and parent mount.

**Step 5 — Mount Table Update:**
An entry is appended (or prepended) to the **mount table**, recording:
- Device name
- Mount point absolute path
- File system type
- Mount options (read-only, no-execute, sync, etc.)

**Step 6 — Directory Cache Integration:**
The VFS merges the new file system's root dentry under the mount point's dentry, allowing future `open()` and `stat()` calls to traverse correctly.

**Step 7 — Permission Re-evaluation:**
Any cached `access()` decisions under the mount point are invalidated, since the contents of the directory have now changed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** In the **Part A 3-mark question**, you are often asked: *"List the steps performed by the OS during file system mounting."* Memorize the 7 steps above as a **flowchart-ready sequence**.

---

## 2.3 Types of Mounting (Classification)

| Mount Type | When Triggered | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Boot-time (Root) Mount** | Automatically by boot loader / init process | Mounting root `/` from `/dev/sda1` |
| **Automatic Mount (Auto-mounter)** | When a directory is first accessed (on-demand) | `autofs` daemon in Linux |
| **Manual Mount** | Explicit user/system administrator command | `mount /dev/cdrom /mnt/cdrom` |
| **Network Mount** | Mounting a remote file system via a network protocol | `mount -t nfs server:/share /mnt/nfs` |
| **Pseudo-file System Mount** | Mounting virtual, non-disk-backed file systems | `procfs`, `sysfs`, `devfs` |

---

## 2.4 The Unmounting Procedure

Unmounting is the strict reverse of mounting. It is **not automatic** — it must be called explicitly (`umount` / `Unmount`). The OS must guarantee:

1. **No process holds an open file descriptor** on any file in the file system.
2. **All dirty buffers are flushed** to disk (write-back).
3. **The in-core superblock is freed.**
4. **The mount table entry is removed.**
5. **The mount point directory reverts** to its original (empty) state.

> [!WARNING]
> If you pull out a USB drive **without unmounting**, the OS may produce *“Delayed Write Failed”* or *“Volume is busy”* errors because the above five conditions were violated. This is a **classic KTU viva question**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Verbatim)

| Command / Concept | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `mount` | `mount -t <type> <device> <dir>` | Attach a file system to a directory |
| `umount` | `umount <device OR dir>` | Detach a mounted file system |
| Mount Table Entry | `(device, mount_point, fstype, options)` | Kernel record of active mounts |
| Mount Point Rule | Must be an **existing, empty** directory | Prevents collision with old files |
| Read-Only Option | `-o ro` | Mount without write access (CD-ROM) |
| No-Execute Option | `-o noexec` | Disallow program execution from FS |
| Kernel Buffer Sync | `-o sync` | All writes committed synchronously |
| `/etc/fstab` | File system table | Persistent mount configuration at boot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Mounting underpins **containerization** (Docker bind mounts), **cloud storage gateways**, **RAID array assembly**, and **live OS recovery** (mounting a root partition from a rescue USB). It is the foundation of all dynamic storage management in modern OS design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

Since file system mounting is a **system-level procedure** (not a calculus-based theorem), the analytical walkthrough below is presented as an **exhaustive procedural derivation** of the algorithm, with every state transition made explicit. This format is what KTU board examiners expect for a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.1 The Canonical Mount Algorithm (Line-by-Line Derivation)

Let us define the inputs and outputs formally before tracing the steps.

**Inputs to the algorithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inputs} = \{
& D \;:\; \text{Block device identifier (e.g., } /dev/sda2 \text{)} \\
& M \;:\; \text{Mount point (absolute path, must be a directory)} \\
& T \;:\; \text{File system type (e.g., ext4, NTFS, NFS)} \\
& O \;:\; \text{Mount options set (ro, rw, noexec, sync, nosuid, \dots)}
\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:** Updated in-kernel mount table $\mathcal{M}$ with the new entry, plus a fully operational path subtree rooted at $M$.

---

**STEP 1 — Argument Validation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{if } (M \notin \text{directory tree}) \text{ then } \textbf{RETURN ERR\_ENOENT} \\
& \text{if } (M \text{ is not a directory}) \text{ then } \textbf{RETURN ERR\_ENOTDIR} \\
& \text{if } (M \text{ is already a mount point}) \text{ then } \textbf{RETURN ERR\_EBUSY}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The mount point must exist, must be a directory, and must not already be in use by another mounted file system. Returning an error code follows standard POSIX conventions.

---

**STEP 2 — Locate the Block Device**

$$
D_{\text{handle}} = \text{open\_block\_device}(D)
$$

> *Logic:* The kernel's block-device subsystem is queried to obtain a handle to the raw device. If $D$ does not exist or is not a block device, the system call fails with `ENXIO` (no such device).

---

**STEP 3 — Read the Superblock**

$$
S_{\text{disk}} = \text{read\_block}(D_{\text{handle}}, \text{offset} = 1024, \text{size} = 1024 \text{ bytes})
$$

> *Logic:* Every standard file system (ext2/ext3/ext4, FAT32, NTFS) places its **superblock at a fixed offset** (typically 1024 bytes from the start of the partition). This block contains the **magic number** used to identify the file system type.

---

**STEP 4 — Magic Number Verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{If } S_{\text{disk}}.\text{magic} = 0xEF53 \Rightarrow T = \text{ext2/ext3/ext4} \\
& \text{If } S_{\text{disk}}.\text{magic} = 0x4D5A \Rightarrow T = \text{FAT/NTFS} \\
& \text{If } S_{\text{disk}}.\text{magic} = 0x5346544E \Rightarrow T = \text{NFS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The magic number is a unique 16-bit or 32-bit signature. If no match is found, the device is considered unformatted or corrupt, and the mount is aborted.

---

**STEP 5 — In-Core Superblock Initialization**

$$
S_{\text{mem}} = \text{alloc\_kernel\_object}(\text{size} = \text{sizeof}(\text{struct super\_block}))
$$

$$
S_{\text{mem}}.\text{on\_disk\_copy} \leftarrow S_{\text{disk}}
$$

> *Logic:* A working in-memory copy is created. All future file system operations will modify this in-RAM copy; periodic write-backs (every 5 seconds in Linux `sync_supers` thread) propagate changes to disk.

---

**STEP 6 — Mount Table Entry Construction**

A new entry $E$ is allocated:

$$
E = \langle D, M, T, O, S_{\text{mem}}, \text{timestamp} = t_{\text{now}} \rangle
$$

The entry is then inserted:

$$
\mathcal{M} \leftarrow \mathcal{M} \cup \{E\}
$$

> *Logic:* The mount table is typically implemented as a **linked list** (Linux 2.6) or a **red-black tree** (Linux 4.x+) for $O(\log n)$ lookup. Order matters: **the most recently mounted file system at a path takes precedence**.

---

**STEP 7 — Dentry and Inode Cache Attachment**

The root inode of the new file system (in-core) is linked to the mount point's dentry:

$$
\text{dentry}(M).\text{d\_inode}.\text{i\_mount} \leftarrow S_{\text{mem}}.\text{s\_root}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the **physical linking** of the new file system into the directory tree. From this moment, all `pathname resolution` calls traversing $M$ will be redirected into the new file system.

---

**STEP 8 — Permission Cache Invalidation**

$$
\forall d \in \text{children}(M) \;:\; \text{invalidate\_cache}(d)
$$

> *Logic:* Any previously cached access permissions for files under $M$ are now stale because the directory contents have been replaced.

---

**STEP 9 — Return Success**

$$
\textbf{RETURN} \; 0 \; (\text{Success})
$$

> *Logic:* The `mount()` system call returns `0` on success. The user-space process can now access files via the path $M$/<subpath>.

---

## 3.2 Summary Trace (Numerical Walkthrough)

Suppose the user issues:

$$
\texttt{ mount -t ext4 /dev/sdb1 /home/user/data }
$$

| Step | Action | Resulting State |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Validate `/home/user/data` | Exists ✓, Directory ✓, Not mounted ✓ |
| 2 | Open `/dev/sdb1` | Handle obtained |
| 3 | Read superblock at offset 1024 | Magic `0xEF53` detected |
| 4 | Identify type | `ext4` confirmed |
| 5 | Allocate `super_block` struct | In-core copy ready |
| 6 | Build mount table entry | Table now has 1 new row |
| 7 | Link root inode to mount point | Path tree updated |
| 8 | Invalidate permission cache | Cache cleared for subtree |
| 9 | Return 0 | User shell resumes |

This is the **complete chain of state transitions** the OS undergoes to mount a file system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Mounting Process (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User invokes mount command] --> B{Arguments Valid?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Return Error: EINVAL ENOENT]
    B -- Yes --> C[Open Block Device Driver]
    C --> D{Device Exists?}
    D -- No --> Z2[Return Error: ENXIO]
    D -- Yes --> E[Read Superblock from Disk]
    E --> F{Magic Number Valid?}
    F -- No --> Z3[Return Error: EINVAL or EFAULT]
    F -- Yes --> G[Allocate In-Core Superblock Struct]
    G --> H[Identify File System Driver]
    H --> I[Initialize Inode and Dentry Cache]
    I --> J[Create Mount Table Entry E]
    J --> K[Link New Root Inode to Mount Point]
    K --> L[Invalidate Permission Cache]
    L --> M[Return Success: 0]
    M --> N[File System Now Accessible]
```

## 4.2 Directory Tree Before and After Mounting

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE_MOUNT["BEFORE MOUNTING"]
        root1[Root /]
        home1[home]
        user1[user]
        data1["data (empty)"]
        usb1["/dev/sdc1 (detached, inaccessible)"]
        root1 --> home1
        home1 --> user1
        user1 --> data1
    end

    subgraph AFTER_MOUNT["AFTER MOUNTING /dev/sdc1 onto /home/user/data"]
        root2[Root /]
        home2[home]
        user2[user]
        data2["data (mount point)"]
        f1["fileA.txt"]
        f2["fileB.txt"]
        dir1["subdir/"]
        root2 --> home2
        home2 --> user2
        user2 --> data2
        data2 --> f1
        data2 --> f2
        data2 --> dir1
    end
```

## 4.3 Mount Table Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MT["Mount Table (Kernel Memory)"]
        E1["Entry 1: /dev/sda1 -> / ext4 rw"]
        E2["Entry 2: /dev/sda2 -> /home ext4 rw"]
        E3["Entry 3: /dev/sdb1 -> /mnt/data ext4 ro"]
        E4["Entry 4: server:/share -> /mnt/nfs nfs rw"]
        E5["Entry 5: proc -> /proc procfs defaults"]
    end
    MP1[Mount Point /] -.linked to.-> E1
    MP2[Mount Point /home] -.linked to.-> E2
    MP3[Mount Point /mnt/data] -.linked to.-> E3
    MP4[Mount Point /mnt/nfs] -.linked to.-> E4
    MP5[Mount Point /proc] -.linked to.-> E5
```

## 4.4 VFS-Mediated Mount Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[User Application: open, read, write] --> VFS[Virtual File System VFS Layer]
    VFS --> FS1[ext4 Driver]
    VFS --> FS2[NTFS Driver]
    VFS --> FS3[FAT32 Driver]
    VFS --> FS4[NFS Client]
    VFS --> FS5[procfs Driver]
    FS1 --> D1[/dev/sda1 - ext4 Partition/]
    FS2 --> D2[/dev/sdb1 - NTFS Partition/]
    FS3 --> D3[/dev/sdc1 - USB FAT32/]
    FS4 --> N1[Remote NFS Server]
    FS5 --> K1[Kernel Internal State]
    VFS --> MT[Mount Table Lookup]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read Diagram 4.4 for exams:** The VFS layer is the *unified interface* — applications call generic POSIX functions (`open`, `read`), and VFS dispatches them to the **correct** file system driver based on the mount table entry for the path being accessed. This is why a single program can transparently read from a local ext4 disk and a remote NFS share without code changes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define file system mounting. Mention any two situations where mounting is essential. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** File system mounting is the OS procedure of attaching a file system to a specific directory (called the *mount point*) in the existing directory hierarchy, thereby making its contents accessible to users and applications through the standard pathname resolution mechanism.
>
> **Two Essential Situations:**
> 1. **System Boot:** The root file system must be mounted at `/` (or `C:\` on Windows) before the OS can load its configuration files and bring the system to a usable state.
> 2. **Removable Media Access:** When a USB pen drive, CD-ROM, or external hard disk is inserted, mounting allows the user to browse its contents under a directory like `/media/usb` or `D:`.
> 3. *(Optional third)* **Network File Access:** Mounting a remote NFS or CIFS share allows distributed applications to access files over a LAN as though they were local.

---

### Question 2: What is a mount table? List any four fields stored in a mount table entry. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Mount Table:** A mount table (or mount list) is a kernel-maintained data structure that records all currently active file system mountings. It is consulted during every pathname resolution to determine which file system driver should handle a given path.
>
> **Four Fields Stored:**
> 1. **Device name** — The block device or remote identifier (e.g., `/dev/sda1`).
> 2. **Mount point** — The absolute path of the directory (e.g., `/home/user/data`).
> 3. **File system type** — The driver in use (e.g., `ext4`, `NTFS`, `NFS`).
> 4. **Mount options** — Flags such as `ro` (read-only), `noexec`, `sync`, `nosuid`.
>
> *(Optional additional field: timestamp of mount operation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> *(Read this carefully — students lose 2–3 marks here routinely.)*
> 1. **Never** confuse "mount point" with "mount table". They are *different concepts*. A mount point is a *directory*; a mount table is a *data structure*.
> 2. **Never** say *"the file system is copied into the directory"* — this is a **common misconception**. The file system is **referenced/linked**, not copied. Mentioning copy-operation will cost you a mark.
> 3. **Always** mention the role of the **VFS layer** in your 14-mark answer. The board examiner allocates dedicated marks for it.
> 4. **Always** list the order of operations in mounting — examiners reward procedural flow (steps in sequence).
> 5. **Always** use diagrams wherever possible — a well-labeled mount-table diagram can fetch you 2 extra marks.

---

### Question A(1): Explain in detail the file system mounting procedure in an operating system. Discuss the role of the Virtual File System (VFS) layer and illustrate with a suitable diagram. `[14 Marks]`

**Model Answer Outline:**

**(a) Introduction & Definition [3 marks]**
File system mounting is the process of attaching a logical file system to a directory in the existing directory tree. It enables the OS to integrate heterogeneous storage devices (local disks, USB drives, network shares) into a single unified namespace. The directory at which attachment occurs is the *mount point*.

**(b) Step-by-Step Mounting Procedure [5 marks]**

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Validate the mount point — must exist, be a directory, and not already be a mount point. |
| 2 | Locate and open the block device driver. |
| 3 | Read the on-disk superblock (at offset 1024 typically). |
| 4 | Verify the magic number to identify the file system type. |
| 5 | Allocate an in-core `super_block` structure; initialize with on-disk data. |
| 6 | Insert a new entry into the kernel mount table. |
| 7 | Link the new file system's root inode to the mount point's dentry. |
| 8 | Invalidate cached permissions for files under the mount point. |
| 9 | Return success. |

**(c) Role of the VFS Layer [3 marks]**

The Virtual File System is a kernel abstraction layer that provides a **uniform interface** to all file system types. When a user invokes `open("/mnt/data/file.txt")`:

1. VFS walks the directory tree using the mount table.
2. Upon reaching `/mnt/data`, the VFS consults the mount table and finds that this is served by the `ext4` driver.
3. VFS dispatches the call to the ext4 driver's `open()` function.

This indirection means applications need not know whether they are reading from an `ext4` partition, an `NTFS` USB drive, or a remote `NFS` share — the API is identical.

**(d) Diagram [3 marks]**
*(Draw Diagram 4.4 from Section 4 — the VFS-mediated mount architecture.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus Point:** Add a one-line note that **unmounting is the strict reverse** of mounting, requiring all open file descriptors to be closed and all dirty buffers to be flushed.

---

### Question B(2): What is a mount table? Explain its structure. With a neat diagram, describe how the mount table interacts with the VFS layer during pathname resolution. List any four mount options commonly used. `[14 Marks]`

**Model Answer Outline:**

**(a) Mount Table Definition & Purpose [3 marks]**
A mount table is an in-kernel data structure that records all currently mounted file systems. Each entry contains the device, mount point, file system type, and options. It is consulted on **every** pathname lookup, so it must be highly efficient (typically implemented as a red-black tree in modern Linux).

**(b) Structure of a Mount Table Entry [3 marks]**

$$
\text{Mount Entry} = \langle D, M, T, O, S_{\text{mem}}, t_{\text{mount}} \rangle
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Device identifier
- $M$ = Mount point path
- $T$ = File system type
- $O$ = Mount options
- $S_{\text{mem}}$ = Pointer to in-core superblock
- $t_{\text{mount}}$ = Mount timestamp

**(c) Interaction with VFS during Pathname Resolution [5 marks]**

When resolving `/home/user/file.txt`:
1. VFS starts at the root `/`.
2. VFS scans the mount table; the first matching entry whose mount point is `/` is selected (root file system, e.g., ext4 on `/dev/sda1`).
3. VFS descends into `home/` via ext4's directory lookup function.
4. VFS scans the mount table again; if `/home` is itself a mount point, VFS switches to that file system.
5. The lookup continues recursively until the target file's inode is found.

**(d) Four Common Mount Options [3 marks]**

| Option | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `ro` | Mount the file system as **read-only**. Used for CD-ROMs and recovery scenarios. |
| `noexec` | Disallow execution of binaries from this file system. Used for `/home` to improve security. |
| `sync` | All writes are committed to disk **synchronously** (no write-back buffering). |
| `nosuid` | Ignore the **setuid** and **setgid** bits on executables. |

*(Draw Diagram 4.3 from Section 4 — the Mount Table architecture.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points (High Density):**

- **Mounting** = attaching a file system to a directory; **Unmounting** = detaching it.
- **Mount Point** = a directory (must be empty and pre-existing).
- **Mount Table** = kernel data structure recording `(device, mount_point, fstype, options)`.
- **Boot-time mount** happens via the boot loader / `init` process mounting root.
- **Auto-mount** uses a daemon (e.g., `autofs`) that mounts on first access.
- The **VFS layer** is the abstraction enabling heterogeneous file systems to coexist.
- Pathname resolution **always consults the mount table** to find the correct driver.
- Unmounting **requires** all file descriptors closed and all buffers flushed.
- Common options: `ro`, `rw`, `noexec`, `nosuid`, `sync`, `nodev`, `noatime`.
- Linux config file: `/etc/fstab` (file system table — persistent mount definitions).
- Windows equivalent: drive letters (`C:`, `D:`) — each is essentially a mount point.
- Mounting is a **logical link**, not a **copy** — common exam trap.
- The **superblock magic number** identifies the file system type during mount.
- **Order matters** in the mount table: most recent mount takes precedence.
- Network mounts use **NFS** (Linux) or **CIFS/SMB** (Windows) protocols.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File Organization: The Inode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** File Organization: The Inode # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inode (Index Node):** An **inode** is a fixed-size on-disk data structure used by Unix-like file systems (e.g., ext2, ext3, ext4, UFS, HFS+) to store all the metadata (administrative information) about a regular file, directory, or symbolic link — **except the file's name and its actual data contents**. Every file in a Unix-like system is represented by exactly one **inode** stored in a special reserved area called the **inode table**. The inode contains pointers to the actual disk blocks holding the file's data. ### 1.1.1 What an Inode Stores (Metadata) | Field | Description | |---|---| | **Inode Number (i-number)** | Unique integer identifier of the file within the file system | | **File Type** | Regular file, directory, character/block device, FIFO, socket, symlink | | **Mode / Permissions** | Read, write, execute bits for owner / group / others | | **Link Count** | Number of hard links pointing to this inode | | **UID / GID** | Owner's User ID and Group ID | | **File Size** | Logical size of the file in bytes | | **Timestamps** | `atime` (access), `mtime` (modification), `ctime` (inode change), `birthtime` (creation) | | **Block Count** | Number of disk blocks actually consumed | | **Data Block Pointers** | Direct, indirect, double-indirect, triple-indirect pointers | | **Extended Attributes** | ACLs, security labels, immutable flags | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** The filename is **NOT** stored inside the inode. The filename lives inside the parent directory's data block, mapped to its corresponding inode number. This is why a file can have multiple names (hard links) but only one inode. ### 1.1.2 What an Inode Does NOT Store - The actual file **name** - The actual file **data/content** - The **directory hierarchy** path ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Library Card System > [!TIP] > **Analogy:** Imagine a massive library with millions of books stored in a huge underground vault. Nobody walks into the vault directly. Instead: > - Each book has a **unique barcode (inode number)**. > - The library has a **central catalog card (inode)** for that barcode, which records: the book's author, language, page count, ISBN, shelf-row numbers where the book is located, and so on — but **NOT the book title**. > - The book **title** is written only on the **outer lobby directory board (parent directory)**, which maps "Atomic Physics → barcode #481516". > - If two lobby boards both point "Calculus → #481516", the same book has **two titles** — this is a **hard link**. The library visitor does the following: 1. Reads the lobby board → gets barcode. 2. Looks up the catalog card → gets shelf locations. 3. Walks to the shelf → retrieves the actual book content. This is **exactly** how Unix file access works: 1. Open the parent directory → read directory entry → get **inode number**. 2. Index into the **inode table** → read **metadata + block pointers**. 3. Use the pointers → fetch the actual **data blocks** from disk. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Inode Number and Inode Table The **inode number** is the **index** of an entry in a linearly addressed array called the **inode table**, which resides in a fixed, reserved region of the file system (e.g., the first 5% of the disk in classic UFS, or a dedicated block group in ext2/3/4). $$\text{Disk Address of Inode} = \text{Inode Table Start} + (\text{i-number} - 1) \times \text{Inode Size}$$ The very first few inode numbers (1, 2, ...) are reserved for special system entries: - **Inode 1** → root of the bad-block list - **Inode 2** → root directory (`/`) - **Inode 3** → lost+found directory > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Index Node"?** The name comes from the fact that the inode is identified by its **index** in the inode table array. A file is therefore "located" by *indexing* into the table rather than traversing a long linked list of name entries. ## 2.2 The Block Pointer Structure Because a file can be larger than the inode itself, the inode does not store data — it stores **pointers** to data blocks. To support files of virtually any size efficiently, the inode uses a multi-level indexing scheme: ### 2.2.1 The Four Tiers of Pointers | Tier | Name | What it Points To | Typical Count | |---|---|---|---| | **1** | **Direct pointers** | The data blocks themselves | 12 (classical Unix) | | **2** | **Single Indirect pointer** | A block that contains a table of data block pointers | 1 | | **3** | **Double Indirect pointer** | A block that contains pointers to single-indirect blocks | 1 | | **4** | **Triple Indirect pointer** | A block that contains pointers to double-indirect blocks | 1 | ### 2.2.2 How File Access Works at Each Tier - **Files up to 12 blocks (≤ 48 KB with 4 KB blocks):** Use direct pointers only — **fastest access** (one disk read for the inode, one for the data). - **Files 13 blocks and above:** The single indirect block acts as a *first-level index* of more data blocks. It can point to 1024 more data blocks (with 4-byte pointers and 4 KB blocks). - **Larger files:** The double indirect block is a *second-level index* of single indirect blocks. - **Huge files (GB–TB):** The triple indirect block can theoretically address terabytes. > [!NOTE] > The choice of having many direct pointers (12) is **deliberate**: empirical studies (e.g., Satyanarayanan's 1981 measurements) showed that most files are **small**, and even files larger than that typically have a *small* "hot" region near the beginning. Direct access to that region avoids extra disk seeks. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation used:** Let $B$ = disk block size (bytes), $P$ = pointer size (bytes), $n$ = number of pointers per index block = $\lfloor B/P \rfloor$, $d$ = number of direct pointers in the inode. | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|---|---| | Pointers per index block | $n = B / P$ | dimensionless | | Capacity of direct region | $C_{direct} = d \times B$ | bytes | | Capacity of single-indirect region | $C_{single} = n \times B$ | bytes | | Capacity of double-indirect region | $C_{double} = n^2 \times B$ | bytes | | Capacity of triple-indirect region | $C_{triple} = n^3 \times B$ | bytes | | **Maximum file size** | $S_{max} = (d + n + n^2 + n^3) \times B$ | bytes | | Maximum file size (no triple indir.) | $S_{max} = (d + n + n^2) \times B$ | bytes | | Maximum file size (only direct + 1 indir.) | $S_{max} = (d + n) \times B$ | bytes | | Inode table storage | $I_{table} = N_{inodes} \times \text{InodeSize}$ | bytes | | **Inode table address** | $Addr(k) = I_{start} + (k-1) \times \text{InodeSize}$ | bytes | | Average file access seeks | $1 \text{ (inode)} + \text{tier-of-block}$ | disk seeks | > [!WARNING] > **Critical pipe-symbol warning:** In your answer scripts, **never** write $\vert B \vert$ or $P \vert n$ in plain text — the vertical bar `|` is interpreted as a markdown table cell separator. Always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\lfloor B \mid P \rfloor$ is wrong; write $\lfloor B/P \rfloor$). ### 2.3.1 Worked Constants for a "Classical Unix" System For the textbook-standard configuration used in Silberschatz's *Operating System Concepts*: - $B = 4096$ bytes (4 KB block) - $P = 4$ bytes (32-bit pointer) - $d = 12$ direct pointers This gives $n = 4096 / 4 = 1024$ pointers per index block. | Region | Capacity | |---|---| | 12 direct blocks | $12 \times 4\text{ KB} = \mathbf{48 \text{ KB}}$ | | 1 single indirect | $1024 \times 4\text{ KB} = \mathbf{4 \text{ MB}}$ | | 1 double indirect | $1024^2 \times 4\text{ KB} = \mathbf{4 \text{ GB}}$ | | 1 triple indirect | $1024^3 \times 4\text{ KB} = \mathbf{4 \text{ TB}}$ | | **Maximum file size** | $\approx \mathbf{4 \text{ TB}}$ | ## 2.4 The Role of Directories in the Inode Model A **directory** in a Unix file system is itself **a special file** whose data blocks contain a list of **directory entries** (`dirent`). Each entry has the form: $$\text{dirent} = \langle \text{filename (variable length)}, \text{ inode\_number (fixed)} \rangle$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key insight:** A directory does *not* "contain" files in a parent-children sense. A directory contains a **mapping** from human-readable names to inode numbers. The file's data and metadata are reached by following that inode number into the inode table. ## 2.5 Hard Links vs Symbolic Links — An Inode Perspective | Feature | Hard Link | Symbolic (Soft) Link | |---|---|---| | What it is | Another directory entry pointing to the **same inode number** | A new file of type `symlink` whose data is the **pathname string** of the target | | Shares inode? | **Yes** — same i-number | **No** — new inode of its own | | Link count effect | `nlink` of original inode increments | Unaffected | | Survives original deletion? | **Yes** (data persists as long as `nlink > 0`) | **No** (becomes a "dangling" symlink) | | Cross file system? | No (inode numbers are local to a file system) | Yes (pathname is global) | | Can link to directories? | Restricted (only superuser) | Yes | The system call `link(oldpath, newpath)` creates a hard link by adding a new directory entry that maps `newpath → same_inode_number`, then increments the inode's `nlink` counter. The call `unlink(path)` removes the directory entry and decrements `nlink`; the inode and its data blocks are reclaimed **only when `nlink == 0` AND no process has the file open**. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database storage engines (e.g., PostgreSQL, Oracle):** Use similar "metadata + block-list" page structures to manage huge table files efficiently. - **Version control (Git):** Internally stores content-addressed "blob" objects in a way that mirrors inode design — the SHA-1 hash is the "inode number" and tree objects are the "directories". - **Cloud object storage (S3, GCS):** Translates user-friendly object keys into internal storage handles analogous to inode numbers. - **Digital forensics:** Recovering deleted files relies on understanding that an inode can be unlinked (nlink=0) but its data blocks may remain on disk until overwritten. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum File Size with Full 4-Tier Inode **Given:** - $B = 4096$ bytes (block size) - $P = 4$ bytes (pointer size) - $d = 12$ (number of direct pointers in the inode) - The inode has 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect pointer. **Step 1: Compute the number of pointers that fit in one index block.** $$n = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{4096 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes/pointer}} = 1024 \text{ pointers/block}$$ **Step 2: Compute the data capacity addressed by the direct region.** Each direct pointer references one data block, and each data block holds $B$ bytes. $$C_{direct} = d \times B = 12 \times 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 48 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 3: Compute the data capacity addressed by the single-indirect pointer.** A single-indirect block holds $n$ pointers, each pointing to a data block. $$C_{single} = n \times B = 1024 \times 4096 = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 4: Compute the data capacity addressed by the double-indirect pointer.** A double-indirect block holds $n$ pointers, each pointing to a single-indirect block, and each of *those* holds $n$ data block pointers. $$C_{double} = n^2 \times B = 1024^2 \times 4096 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}$$ **Step 5: Compute the data capacity addressed by the triple-indirect pointer.** $$C_{triple} = n^3 \times B = 1024^3 \times 4096 = 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ TB}$$ **Step 6: Sum all four regions for the maximum file size.** $$\begin{aligned} S_{max} &= (d + n + n^2 + n^3) \times B \\ &= (12 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824) \times 4096 \\ &= 1{,}074{,}791{,}436 \times 4096 \\ &= 4{,}402{,}345{,}643{,}776 \text{ bytes} \\ &\approx 4.00 \text{ TB} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > In many practical KTU problems, the triple-indirect tier is ignored (treated as unbounded or omitted). In that case, the maximum file size simplifies to $S_{max} = (d + n + n^2) \times B = 4 \text{ GB} + 48 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ MB}$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Disk Accesses to Read a Byte in Each Tier The classical KTU-style question asks: *"How many disk accesses are required to read a byte from a file given its inode is in memory?"* | Logical Location of Byte | Number of Disk Reads | Reason | |---|---|---| | Block addressed by a direct pointer | **1** | The block address is read directly from the (in-memory) inode | | Block addressed by single-indirect | **2** | (a) Read the single-indirect block, (b) Read the data block | | Block addressed by double-indirect | **3** | (a) Read double-indirect block, (b) Read single-indirect block, (c) Read data block | | Block addressed by triple-indirect | **4** | (a) Read triple-indirect block, (b) Read double-indirect block, (c) Read single-indirect block, (d) Read data block | > [!IMPORTANT] > The inode is assumed to be **already in memory** (cached after the directory lookup). If the inode itself must be read from disk, add **+1** access. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inode Address Calculation **Given:** Inode table starts at sector 1000, inode size = 256 bytes, file's inode number = 42. **Step 1:** Compute the byte offset of inode 42 from the start of the table. $$\text{offset} = (42 - 1) \times 256 = 41 \times 256 = 10{,}496 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2:** Convert bytes to sectors (assume sector size = 512 bytes). $$\text{sectors\_to\_skip} = \lceil 10{,}496 / 512 \rceil = 21 \text{ sectors}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the absolute disk address. $$\text{address} = 1000 + 21 = \mathbf{1021 \text{ (starting sector of inode 42)}}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with FAT File System | Property | FAT (File Allocation Table) | Inode-based (UFS/ext4) | |---|---|---| | Location of metadata | In directory entry | In central inode table | | File name stored | In directory entry | In directory entry | | Data block pointers | In FAT chain (linked list) | In inode (multi-level index) | | Random access to block $k$ | $O(k)$ — must walk FAT chain | $O(1)$–$O(4)$ — direct or bounded indirect lookup | | Directory listing speed | Slow (must walk FAT for every file) | Fast (all metadata in one inode read) | | Disk space for empty file | Still 1 directory entry | Inode + at least 1 block reservation (varies) | | Maximum file size | Limited by FAT size | Effectively unlimited (TB scale) | | Crash recovery | Relatively easy (FAT is small and central) | Harder (requires journaling or fsck) | > [!TIP] > **For KTU exams:** When asked to compare, emphasize that **FAT is bad for random access** (sequential chain walk) while **inode is excellent for random access** (direct pointers handle 99%+ of the working set with 1 disk read). --- # 4.
