KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** > *Define an inode. List any four pieces of information stored in an inode.* **Model Answer:** An **inode (Index Node)** is a fixed-size on-disk data structure used by Unix-like file systems to store all administrative information (metadata) about a file — except its name and actual data. Four pieces of information stored in an inode: 1. **File type** — regular file, directory, symlink, device, etc. 2. **Owner and group identifiers** — UID and GID for access control. 3. **File size and block count** — logical size in bytes plus the number of 512-byte sectors actually used. 4. **Timestamps** — last access (`atime`), last modification (`mtime`), and last inode change (`ctime`). *(Other acceptable answers: link count, mode/permission bits, data block pointers.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** > *Why are filenames not stored inside inodes?* **Model Answer:** Filenames are not stored inside inodes because: 1. **It enables hard links.** A single inode can be referenced by multiple filenames (in different directories). If the name were inside the inode, two files could never share the same data and metadata. 2. **Variable name length.** Filenames can be up to 255 bytes (or more in modern systems). Storing them inside a fixed-size inode would waste space for short names and limit the maximum name length. 3. **Decoupling concerns.** Storing the name in the parent directory cleanly separates "what the user calls the file" (a directory-level concern) from "what the file actually is" (an inode-level concern). Filenames are therefore stored in **directory entries (dirents)**, which map ` → `. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]** > *(a)* Explain the structure of an inode with a neat diagram. Describe the roles of direct, single-indirect, double-indirect, and triple-indirect pointers. **(7 Marks)** > *(b)* A file system uses **4096-byte** blocks and **8-byte** pointers. The inode contains **12 direct pointers**, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect pointer. Calculate the **maximum file size** supported. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Inode Structure (7 Marks) An **inode** is a fixed-size (typically 128 or 256 bytes) on-disk data structure that stores file metadata and the addresses of disk blocks containing the file's data. The general layout of an inode is: | Field | Purpose | |---|---| | Inode number (i-number) | Unique index of this inode in the inode table | | File type | Regular, directory, symlink, device, etc. | | Mode / Permissions | rwx bits for owner / group / others | | UID, GID | Owner's user and group IDs | | Link count | Number of hard links | | File size | Logical size in bytes | | Timestamps | `atime`, `mtime`, `ctime`, `birthtime` | | Block count | Disk sectors used (in 512-byte units) | | **15 pointer slots** | First 12 are **direct**, 13th is **single-indirect**, 14th is **double-indirect**, 15th is **triple-indirect** | **Roles of the pointer tiers:** - **Direct pointers (12 of them):** Each directly references a data block. A small file (up to $12 \times 4096 = 48$ KB on a 4-KB-block system) is read with **just one** disk access (the inode itself) for its data — the most common and fastest case. - **Single-indirect pointer:** Points to an **index block** (a block whose content is a table of more data block pointers). With a 4-KB block and 4-byte pointer, this index block holds $4096/4 = 1024$ pointers, so the single-indirect region can address $1024 \times 4$ KB $= 4$ MB of additional data. - **Double-indirect pointer:** Points to an index block of *single-indirect blocks*. It can therefore address $1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576$ data blocks, i.e., $4$ GB of additional data. - **Triple-indirect pointer:** Adds another level of indirection, addressing $1024^3 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$ data blocks, i.e., $4$ TB of additional data. (Include a diagram similar to Section 4.1 of these notes.) ### Part (b) — Maximum File Size Calculation (7 Marks) **Given:** - Block size $B = 4096$ bytes - Pointer size $P = 8$ bytes - Direct pointers $d = 12$ **Step 1: Pointers per index block.** $$n = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{4096}{8} = 512 \text{ pointers/block}$$ **Step 2: Direct region capacity.** $$C_{direct} = 12 \times 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 48 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 3: Single-indirect region capacity.** $$C_{single} = 512 \times 4096 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 4: Double-indirect region capacity.** $$C_{double} = 512^2 \times 4096 = 262{,}144 \times 4096 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ GB}$$ **Step 5: Triple-indirect region capacity.** $$C_{triple} = 512^3 \times 4096 = 134{,}217{,}728 \times 4096 = 549{,}755{,}813{,}888 \text{ bytes} \approx 512 \text{ GB}$$ **Step 6: Maximum file size.** $$\begin{aligned} S_{max} &= (12 + 512 + 512^2 + 512^3) \times 4096 \\ &= (12 + 512 + 262{,}144 + 134{,}217{,}728) \times 4096 \\ &= 134{,}480{,}396 \times 4096 \\ &= 550{,}837{,}702{,}656 \text{ bytes} \\ &\approx 512.001 \text{ GB} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to add the **direct-pointer region** ($d \times B$) when summing. You must sum **all four** regions: $d + n + n^2 + n^3$ multiplied by $B$. Also, do not convert $B$ and $P$ into KB or MB before doing the division — keep units consistent until the final conversion. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]** > *(a)* With a neat diagram, explain how Unix directories and inodes together implement file lookup. How does this design enable **hard links** and what is the role of the link count? **(7 Marks)** > *(b)* Consider a file system with **512-byte** blocks and **4-byte** pointers. Each inode has **10 direct pointers**, 1 single-indirect, and 1 double-indirect pointer (no triple-indirect). >     (i) How many disk accesses are required to read a byte from the **1025th** block of a file (assuming the inode is in memory)? **(3 Marks)** >     (ii) Calculate the **maximum file size** in bytes. **(4 Marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Directories, Inodes, and Hard Links (7 Marks) A Unix **directory** is a special file whose data blocks contain a sequence of **directory entries** (dirents). Each dirent has the form: $$\text{dirent} = \langle \text{filename (variable length)}, \text{ inode number (4 bytes)} \rangle$$ **File lookup procedure** (e.g., for the path `/home/alice/report.txt`): 1. The system reads the inode of the **root directory** (inode 2) — already cached in memory. 2. It scans the root directory's data block for an entry whose name is `home` → say it maps to inode 50. 3. It reads inode 50 (the `/home` directory). Scans its data block for `alice` → say inode 1042. 4. It reads inode 1042. Scans its data block for `report.txt` → say inode 4827. 5. It reads inode 4827 → obtains the file's metadata and the 10 direct pointers (and indirect pointers). 6. It uses those pointers to fetch the file's data blocks. So **at every directory level, two disk reads** are required: one to read the directory's data block (to find the next inode number) and one to read the next inode (to find the next level's data block). Plus a final read for the data. **How hard links work:** A hard link is created by simply adding a **new directory entry** somewhere else in the file system that maps a different name to the **same inode number**. The kernel then increments the inode's `nlink` (link count) field. - File `/home/alice/report.txt` maps to inode 4827. - Hard link `/home/bob/copy.txt` also maps to inode 4827. - Inode 4827 has `nlink = 2`. The **link count** plays two crucial roles: 1. **Reference counting for deallocation.** When `unlink()` removes a directory entry, the kernel decrements `nlink`. The inode (and its data blocks) are freed **only when `nlink == 0` AND no process has the file open**. 2. **Orphan prevention.** Because data is preserved as long as any link (directory entry) or any open file descriptor points to the inode, accidental deletion of one path does not destroy the file's data. ### Part (b) — Disk Access and Maximum File Size (7 Marks) **Given:** $B = 512$ bytes, $P = 4$ bytes, $d = 10$ direct, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, no triple-indirect. **Step 1: Pointers per index block.** $$n = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{512}{4} = 128 \text{ pointers/block}$$ **(i) Disk access for the 1025th block (3 Marks):** - Blocks 1 to 10: covered by direct pointers. - Blocks 11 to 138 (= 10 + 128): covered by the single-indirect region. - The **1025th block** lies in the **double-indirect region**. Reading it requires: 1. Read the **double-indirect block** (1 access). 2. Read the appropriate **single-indirect block** within it (1 access). 3. Read the actual **data block** (1 access). **Total = 3 disk accesses.** **(ii) Maximum file size (4 Marks):** $$\begin{aligned} S_{max} &= (d + n + n^2) \times B \\ &= (10 + 128 + 128^2) \times 512 \\ &= (10 + 128 + 16{,}384) \times 512 \\ &= 16{,}522 \times 512 \\ &= 8{,}459{,}264 \text{ bytes} \\ &\approx 8.07 \text{ MB} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes to Avoid in Exam Scripts:** > 1. **Forgetting the inode-table lookup cost** in sequence. The "disk accesses" question must assume the inode is in memory (the standard assumption) — *do not* add an extra access for the inode. > 2. **Confusing block numbering.** The "1025th block" means block at **index 1024** (zero-indexed) or block number 1025 (one-indexed). Be consistent and clearly state your convention. > 3. **Mixing units in the final formula.** Express everything in bytes, then convert to MB/GB **only at the very end**. Premature conversion causes arithmetic errors. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "File Organization: The Inode"** - [ ] **Inode = metadata container.** Stores everything *about* a file except its name and content. - [ ] **Filename lives in the parent directory**, not in the inode. This is what makes hard links possible. - [ ] **Inode number is an index** into the inode table. `Addr(k) = Start + (k-1) × InodeSize`. - [ ] **Pointer tiers: 12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect** (classical Unix). - [ ] **Pointers per index block:** $n = B/P$. - [ ] **Maximum file size formula:** $S_{max} = (d + n + n^2 + n^3) \times B$ (omit a tier if not present). - [ ] **Disk accesses for reading a byte:** 1 (direct) / 2 (single-indirect) / 3 (double-indirect) / 4 (triple-indirect). - [ ] **Hard link shares the inode; symbolic link does not.** `nlink > 0` keeps the data alive even after `unlink()`. - [ ] **Directory is a special file** whose data blocks are sequences of `(filename, inode_number)` pairs. - [ ] **FAT vs Inode:** FAT = linked-list chain, slow random access; Inode = indexed, fast random access. - [ ] **Most files are small** — most accesses are satisfied by direct pointers (single disk read). - [ ] **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX** for absolute value bars in markdown — never the raw `|` symbol in tables. - [ ] **Always keep units consistent** in derivations; convert KB/MB/GB only at the final answer. - [ ] **Inode-based file systems** include UFS, ext2, ext3, ext4, HFS+, ReiserFS, and most Unix derivatives. > [!TIP] > **One-line takeaway for viva:** *"An inode is the index node — a fixed-size metadata record that turns a numeric handle into a file's permissions, timestamps, and the addresses of its data blocks, allowing hard links and constant-time random access."*

topics.title: The Multi-Level Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Multi-Level Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **File Systems** (Module 4 of PEAMT595), a **Multi-Level Index** is a hierarchical file allocation scheme that combines the advantages of **Contiguous Allocation** (fast sequential access) and **Linked Allocation** (no external fragmentation) by using a tree-structured index block architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A *Multi-Level Index* is a method of organizing file allocation information in which the index of data blocks is itself indexed recursively across multiple levels of blocks. The **index node (inode)** contains a fixed number of direct block pointers and a small number of pointers to other index blocks (single, double, and triple indirect blocks), enabling efficient addressing of very large files while keeping the inode size constant.

In a **Single-Level Index**, all pointers to data blocks reside in one index block. In a **Multi-Level Index**, this index block is itself referenced through a hierarchy, allowing the file system to support files that are far larger than would otherwise fit in a single index block.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Catalogue

Imagine a massive library with **millions of books** stored across hundreds of rooms:

- A **Single-Level Index** is like a *single librarian who has memorized the shelf location of every book*. Once the librarian leaves, the system collapses, and there is a hard limit on how many books the librarian's memory can hold.
- A **Two-Level Index** is like a *head librarian* who knows the *floor number* of each book. The head librarian points you to a *floor librarian*, who in turn points you to the *exact shelf and row*. The head librarian only needs to remember a small set of floor numbers.
- A **Multi-Level Index** generalizes this: a *building directory* points to *floor directories*, which point to *wing directories*, which finally point to the *individual bookshelves*. Each directory only stores a few pointers, but the system can locate **billions of items**.

> [!NOTE]
> The same principle is used in the **Unix File System (UFS)** and the **Berkeley Fast File System (FFS)**, where the inode is a small, fixed-size structure that can address files up to **several gigabytes or terabytes** depending on the block size and pointer width.

### 1.3 Why Single-Level Indexing Is Insufficient

| Limitation | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Fixed index block size** | A single index block can hold only a finite number of block pointers (e.g., 256 pointers of 4 bytes each in a 1 KB block). |
| **Upper bound on file size** | Maximum file size = (pointers per block) × (block size). For 1 KB blocks: 1 KB × 256 = **256 KB** — too small for modern files. |
| **Wasted internal fragmentation** | A small file still consumes an entire index block on disk. |
| **Inefficient for large files** | A single index block cannot be grown dynamically; another allocation strategy is required. |

The **Multi-Level Index** solves these limitations by allowing the *index space itself to grow* recursively, while the *inode remains a constant, small size*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Inode Pointer Architecture (Unix Standard)

The classic Unix inode (used as the canonical KTU exam reference) contains exactly **15 pointers**, traditionally organized as:

- **12 direct block pointers** (pointing to the first 12 data blocks of the file)
- **1 single-indirect pointer** (pointing to an index block)
- **1 double-indirect pointer** (pointing to a block of single-indirect blocks)
- **1 triple-indirect pointer** (pointing to a block of double-indirect blocks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus standard structure uses **13 pointers**: *10 direct*, *1 single indirect*, *1 double indirect*, *1 triple indirect*. We will adopt this KTU convention in all derivations below.

### 2.2 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic

**Level 0 — Direct Pointers (10 in number):**
The first 10 pointers in the inode point directly to the first 10 data blocks. Access requires **1 disk I/O** to read the block. This handles the *common case of small files* extremely efficiently.

**Level 1 — Single Indirect Pointer (1 in number):**
The 11th pointer points to a **single-indirect index block**. This block is a regular disk block filled entirely with data-block pointers. The OS must first read the single-indirect block (1 I/O), then read the target data block (1 I/O). Total: **2 disk I/Os**.

**Level 2 — Double Indirect Pointer (1 in number):**
The 12th pointer points to a **double-indirect block** containing pointers to single-indirect blocks. Access requires **3 disk I/Os** (double-indirect → single-indirect → data).

**Level 3 — Triple Indirect Pointer (1 in number):**
The 13th pointer points to a **triple-indirect block** containing pointers to double-indirect blocks. Access requires **4 disk I/Os**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Let the following parameters be defined:

- **B** = Disk block size (in bytes)
- **P** = Size of a block pointer (in bytes; typically **4 bytes** for 32-bit, **8 bytes** for 64-bit)
- **N** = Number of pointers that fit in one block = $B / P$

| Component | # of Pointer Slots | Data Blocks Addressable | Max Bytes Addressable |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct pointers (10) | $10$ | $10$ | $10B$ |
| Single indirect | $1$ index block → $N$ data pointers | $N$ | $NB$ |
| Double indirect | $1$ → $N$ single-indirect → $N^2$ | $N^2$ | $N^2 B$ |
| Triple indirect | $1$ → $N$ double-indirect → $N^3$ | $N^3$ | $N^3 B$ |

**Total Maximum File Size ($S_{\max}$):**

$$
S_{\max} = (10 + N + N^2 + N^3) \cdot B
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners typically use **B = 1024 bytes** and **P = 4 bytes**, giving **N = 256**. Always re-read the question carefully because the examiner may vary the pointer size to test your understanding.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Multi-Level Indexing

1. **Database Systems:** Large tables and indexes are stored in files whose size far exceeds single-level limits; multi-level indexing enables this transparently.
2. **Video and Image Servers:** Modern multimedia files routinely exceed 4 GB; only a hierarchical index can address them with a fixed-size inode.
3. **Cloud Storage Backends:** Distributed file systems (HDFS, GFS) extend the multi-level concept across nodes, where each level of the index may live on a different machine.
4. **Operating System Kernels:** The VFS (Virtual File System) layer in Linux abstracts the underlying on-disk inode structure, allowing multiple file system types to coexist.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical KTU Problem Parameters

Assume the most common KTU exam configuration:
- **Block size** $B = 1024 \text{ bytes} = 2^{10} \text{ bytes}$
- **Pointer size** $P = 4 \text{ bytes} = 2^2 \text{ bytes}$
- **Inode structure** = 10 direct + 1 single + 1 double + 1 triple indirect

### 3.2 Derivation 1 — Number of Pointers Per Block ($N$)

$$
N = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{1024 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes}}
$$

$$
N = 256 \text{ pointers per block}
$$

> **Logic:** A single block of 1024 bytes can hold 256 separate 4-byte pointers, each of which references a different data block. This is the *fundamental unit of addressability* at every indirect level.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Maximum File Size ($S_{\max}$)

Using the master formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
S_{\max} = (10 + N + N^2 + N^3) \cdot B
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the polynomial $10 + N + N^2 + N^3$ with $N = 256$:**

$$
10 + 256 = 266
$$

$$
N^2 = 256 \times 256 = 65536
$$

$$
N^3 = 256 \times 65536 = 16777216
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all four terms:**

$$
10 + 256 + 65536 + 16777216
$$

$$
= 266 + 65536 = 65802
$$

$$
= 65802 + 16777216 = 16843018
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by block size $B = 1024$:**

$$
S_{\max} = 16843018 \times 1024
$$

$$
= 16843018 \times 2^{10}
$$

$$
= 16843018 \times 1024 = 17,247,250,432 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to gigabytes (1 GB = $2^{30}$ = 1,073,741,824 bytes):**

$$
S_{\max} \text{ in GB} = \frac{17{,}247{,}250{,}432}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 16.06 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** With a 1024-byte block and 4-byte pointers, the classic 13-pointer Unix inode can address files up to approximately **16.06 GB** in size.

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Number of Disk I/Os to Access a Block

Let $k$ denote the level of indirection ($k = 0$ for direct, $k = 1$ for single indirect, etc.).

$$
\text{I/O count} = k + 1
$$

| Access Type | $k$ | I/Os Required | Typical Block Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct | $0$ | $1$ | Blocks 0 – 9 |
| Single Indirect | $1$ | $2$ | Blocks 10 – 265 |
| Double Indirect | $2$ | $3$ | Blocks 266 – 65,801 |
| Triple Indirect | $3$ | $4$ | Blocks 65,802 – 16,843,017 |

> **Logic:** Each indirection level adds one index-block read before the actual data block is fetched. This is why **direct access is the fastest path** and why the OS keeps the direct pointer count generous (10 or 12) — most files are small and benefit from the fastest access mode.

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Effect of Increasing Block Size

Suppose we double the block size to $B = 2048$ bytes, keeping $P = 4$ bytes:

**Step 1 — Recompute $N$:**

$$
N = \frac{2048}{4} = 512
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $N^2$ and $N^3$:**

$$
N^2 = 512 \times 512 = 262144
$$

$$
N^3 = 512 \times 262144 = 134217728
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_{\max}$:**

$$
S_{\max} = (10 + 512 + 262144 + 134217728) \times 2048
$$

$$
= 134480394 \times 2048 = 275,415,847,424 \text{ bytes} \approx 256.4 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Doubling the block size **multiplies $S_{\max}$ by approximately 16×** because the cubic term $N^3$ scales as $(B/P)^3$. This illustrates the classic **space-time tradeoff** in file system design — larger blocks mean larger maximum files but also larger internal fragmentation for small files.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inode Pointer Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inode["INODE Block 13 Pointers Total"]
    p0["P0 Direct"]
    p1["P1 Direct"]
    p2["P2 Direct"]
    p3["P3 Direct"]
    p4["P4 Direct"]
    p5["P5 Direct"]
    p6["P6 Direct"]
    p7["P7 Direct"]
    p8["P8 Direct"]
    p9["P9 Direct"]
    si["P10 Single Indirect Pointer"]
    di["P11 Double Indirect Pointer"]
    ti["P12 Triple Indirect Pointer"]

    siblock["Single Indirect Index Block N Pointers"]
    diblock["Double Indirect Index Block N Pointers"]
    tiblk["Triple Indirect Index Block N Pointers"]

    dblock1["Data Block 0"]
    dblock2["Data Block 9"]
    dblock3["Data Block 10"]
    dblock4["Data Block 265"]
    dblock5["Data Block 65801"]
    dblock6["Data Block 16843017"]

    inode --> p0
    inode --> p1
    inode --> p2
    inode --> p3
    inode --> p4
    inode --> p5
    inode --> p6
    inode --> p7
    inode --> p8
    inode --> p9
    inode --> si
    inode --> di
    inode --> ti

    p0 --> dblock1
    p9 --> dblock2
    si --> siblock
    siblock --> dblock3
    di --> diblock
    diblock --> siblock
    siblock --> dblock4
    ti --> tiblk
    tiblk --> diblock
    diblock --> siblock
    siblock --> dblock5
    siblock --> dblock6
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The 13-pointer inode sits at the root. The 10 direct pointers lead straight to data blocks 0–9. The single-indirect pointer leads to an index block which in turn leads to a chain of data blocks. The double-indirect pointer inserts one more index level, and the triple-indirect adds yet another.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Application Request file block N"] --> B["OS Examines Inode"]
    B --> C{"Block N within first 10 blocks?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Use Direct Pointer P N"]
    C -- No --> E{"Block N within single indirect range?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Read Single Indirect Block 1 IO"]
    F --> G["Read Data Block 1 IO"]
    E -- No --> H{"Block N within double indirect range?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Read Double Indirect Block"]
    I --> J["Read Single Indirect Block"]
    J --> K["Read Data Block"]
    H -- No --> L["Triple Indirect Path 4 IOs Total"]
```

> **Reading the architecture:** When the OS must read block N of a file, it walks the index hierarchy top-down, performing a single I/O at each level. The most common case (small files) is served by direct pointers in a single I/O, which is why this design is so performant in practice.

### 4.3 Pointer-Level Decision Matrix

| Block Index Range | Path Taken | Number of I/Os | Index Block Reads |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0 \leq N \leq 9$ | Direct | $1$ | $0$ |
| $10 \leq N \leq 265$ | Single Indirect | $2$ | $1$ |
| $266 \leq N \leq 65801$ | Double Indirect | $3$ | $2$ |
| $65802 \leq N \leq 16843017$ | Triple Indirect | $4$ | $3$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is a multi-level index? How does it differ from a single-level index in terms of file size support?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **single-level index** uses one index block to store all data block pointers, limiting the maximum file size to (pointers per block × block size). A **multi-level index** organizes the index recursively — the inode contains pointers to other index blocks (single, double, triple indirect), so the address space grows *exponentially* with the number of levels while the inode itself remains a fixed small size. The multi-level scheme therefore supports files many orders of magnitude larger than a single-level index can. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the components of a typical Unix-style inode. State the number of I/Os required to access a block via each type of pointer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A typical Unix inode (KTU standard) contains 13 pointers: **10 direct, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect**. I/O requirements:
- Direct → **1 I/O**
- Single indirect → **2 I/Os**
- Double indirect → **3 I/Os**
- Triple indirect → **4 I/Os**

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a multi-level index used in file allocation. With a neat diagram, describe how a file block is accessed through direct, single-indirect, double-indirect, and triple-indirect pointers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a file system with **block size B = 512 bytes** and **pointer size P = 4 bytes**. Using the standard 13-pointer inode (10 direct, 1 single indirect, 1 double indirect, 1 triple indirect), compute:
   (i) The maximum number of data blocks addressable.
   (ii) The maximum file size in bytes and in kilobytes.
   (iii) The number of I/Os required to read the very last block of the file. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure Explanation (7 marks):**

A multi-level index in a Unix-style file system is implemented through an **inode** (index node) that contains a fixed set of pointers. The KTU-standard inode uses **13 pointers** distributed as follows:

- **Direct pointers (10):** Each points directly to a data block. Access requires one disk I/O. Used for the first 10 blocks of the file.
- **Single-indirect pointer (1):** Points to an *index block* that contains pointers to data blocks. Access requires 2 I/Os (1 for the index block, 1 for the data block).
- **Double-indirect pointer (1):** Points to an index block that contains pointers to *single-indirect index blocks*. Access requires 3 I/Os.
- **Triple-indirect pointer (1):** Points to an index block that contains pointers to *double-indirect index blocks*. Access requires 4 I/Os.

The diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1** above. The inode itself remains a constant small size, but the address space grows recursively through the indirect levels. (7 marks)

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $N$ (pointers per block):**

$$
N = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{512}{4} = 128
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum number of data blocks:**

$$
\text{Blocks} = 10 + N + N^2 + N^3
$$

$$
= 10 + 128 + 128^2 + 128^3
$$

$$
= 10 + 128 + 16384 + 2097152
$$

$$
= 2{,}113{,}674 \text{ data blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum file size in bytes:**

$$
S_{\max} = 2{,}113{,}674 \times 512 = 1{,}082{,}200{,}576 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilobytes (1 KB = 1024 bytes):**

$$
S_{\max} \text{ in KB} = \frac{1{,}082{,}200{,}576}{1024} = 1{,}056{,}836 \text{ KB} \approx 1.008 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5 — I/Os for the last block:**

The very last block lies in the **triple-indirect region**, requiring **4 disk I/Os** (triple-indirect block → double-indirect block → single-indirect block → data block).

**(Full 7 marks: 1 mark for $N$, 2 marks for block count, 2 marks for size, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for I/O reasoning.)**

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare **single-level indexing** and **multi-level indexing** with respect to: (i) maximum file size, (ii) access time for small files, (iii) internal fragmentation, and (iv) implementation complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A file system uses a **block size of 4096 bytes** and **64-bit pointers** (8 bytes each). If the inode contains **12 direct, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect** pointer, calculate the maximum file size. How does this compare with a 32-bit pointer scheme (4-byte pointers) of the same configuration? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Single-Level Index | Multi-Level Index |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Maximum file size** | Limited to $N \times B$ (e.g., 256 × 1024 = 256 KB) | Effectively unlimited; cubic growth via $N^3 \cdot B$ (e.g., 16 GB+) |
| **(ii) Small file access time** | Fast (1 I/O) but needs full index block | Faster for small files (direct pointers) — 1 I/O for first 10 blocks |
| **(iii) Internal fragmentation** | One full index block per file is wasted even for tiny files | Only direct pointer slots wasted; index blocks allocated on demand |
| **(iv) Implementation complexity** | Simple flat structure | More complex — requires recursive index allocation and tree traversal |

(7 marks: ~1.75 per criterion)

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $N$ with 8-byte pointers and 4096-byte blocks:**

$$
N_{64} = \frac{4096}{8} = 512
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $S_{\max}$ with 12 direct pointers and triple indirect:**

$$
S_{\max, 64} = (12 + N + N^2 + N^3) \cdot B
$$

$$
= (12 + 512 + 512^2 + 512^3) \cdot 4096
$$

$$
= (12 + 512 + 262144 + 134217728) \cdot 4096
$$

$$
= 134480396 \times 4096
$$

$$
= 550{,}991{,}302{,}144 \text{ bytes} \approx 512.6 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $N$ with 4-byte pointers:**

$$
N_{32} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S_{\max, 32}$:**

$$
S_{\max, 32} = (12 + 1024 + 1024^2 + 1024^3) \cdot 4096
$$

$$
= (12 + 1024 + 1048576 + 1073741824) \cdot 4096
$$

$$
= 1074791436 \times 4096
$$

$$
= 4{,}402{,}345{,}401{,}536 \text{ bytes} \approx 4.10 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$
\frac{S_{\max, 32}}{S_{\max, 64}} = \frac{4.10 \text{ TB}}{512.6 \text{ GB}} \approx 8\times
$$

> The 32-bit pointer scheme supports approximately **8× larger files** because the cubic term scales as $(B/P)^3$, and halving the pointer size doubles $N$ and hence multiplies $N^3$ by 8. (7 marks: 1 mark $N_{64}$, 2 marks $S_{64}$, 1 mark $N_{32}$, 2 marks $S_{32}$, 1 mark for the comparison ratio.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students often forget to convert bytes to KB/MB/GB. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Pointer count mistake:** The classic KTU inode has **13 pointers (10+1+1+1)**, not 15 (which is the *older* Unix version with 12 direct). Read the question stem carefully.
> 3. **Off-by-one in block range:** The single-indirect region covers blocks 10 through $10 + N - 1$, *not* blocks 10 through $N$. KTU examiners award partial credit for boundary accuracy.
> 4. **Forgetting the multiplier $B$:** A frequent error is to report $N^3$ as the answer without multiplying by block size. The polynomial gives *block count*, not *byte count*.
> 5. **I/O count confusion:** Remember I/Os = indirection level + 1. Direct = 1, single = 2, double = 3, triple = 4.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Multi-Level Index**

- ✅ **Definition:** A hierarchical file allocation scheme where index blocks themselves are indexed recursively across multiple levels, allowing arbitrarily large file support with a fixed-size inode.
- ✅ **Standard KTU inode structure:** **13 pointers** = 10 direct + 1 single indirect + 1 double indirect + 1 triple indirect.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $S_{\max} = (10 + N + N^2 + N^3) \cdot B$, where $N = B/P$.
- ✅ **Typical KTU parameters:** $B = 1024$ bytes, $P = 4$ bytes → $N = 256$ → $S_{\max} \approx 16.06$ GB.
- ✅ **I/O formula:** Number of I/Os = indirection level + 1 (so 1, 2, 3, 4 for direct, single, double, triple).
- ✅ **Cubic growth:** $S_{\max}$ grows as $N^3 \cdot B$, so doubling the block size or halving the pointer size can multiply the maximum file size by ~8×.
- ✅ **Advantage 1:** Supports very large files with a constant-size inode.
- ✅ **Advantage 2:** Direct pointers give fast access (1 I/O) to small files — the common case.
- ✅ **Advantage 3:** Index blocks are allocated on demand, reducing internal fragmentation.
- ✅ **Disadvantage 1:** Large files incur multiple I/Os per access through deep index levels.
- ✅ **Disadvantage 2:** Implementation is more complex than single-level or linked allocation.
- ✅ **Block range mnemonic:** Direct = [0, 9], Single = [10, 10+N-1], Double = [10+N, 10+N+N²-1], Triple = [10+N+N², 10+N+N²+N³-1].
- ✅ **Real-world usage:** Unix File System (UFS), Berkeley FFS, Linux ext2/ext3/ext4, HFS+, NTFS (variant).
- ✅ **Examiner's hot keywords:** "inode," "index block," "indirect block," "disk I/O," "maximum file size," "pointer width," "block size tradeoff."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: VFS Objects and Their Data Structures - The Inode Object
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAMT595 - OPERATING SYSTEM CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: VFS Objects and Their Data Structures - The Inode Object

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **Virtual File System (VFS)** layer of the Linux kernel, an **Inode (Index Node)** is the fundamental kernel data structure that represents a single file system object — a regular file, directory, symbolic link, character device, block device, named pipe (FIFO), or socket — in memory. It is a filesystem-independent abstraction that encapsulates the *metadata* (administrative information) of a file rather than its actual data content.

The inode is the bridge between the *logical file view* (seen by user processes) and the *physical on-disk representation* (handled by concrete file systems such as ext4, NTFS, FAT, or procfs). Each inode carries a unique numeric identifier within its file system called the **Inode Number (i_ino)**, which the directory entries use to reference the file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Inode Object is one of the **four primary VFS objects** along with the *Superblock Object*, the *Dentry Object*, and the *File Object*. Together, they form the Object-Oriented abstraction that allows the kernel to support multiple file systems uniformly.

The C structure `struct inode` is defined in `<linux/fs.h>` and contains approximately 100 fields. For KTU purposes, the structure is grouped into six logical categories:

| Category | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **File Statistics** | Inode number, links count, file size, timestamps |
| **Block Layout (I/O)** | Block pointers for direct, indirect, double-indirect addressing |
| **State Flags** | Dirty, locked, in-use flags |
| **VFS Layer Bookkeeping** | Reference count, list pointers, hash table linkage |
| **Filesystem-Specific** | `i_sb` pointer, `i_fop`, `i_op` operation tables |
| **Access Control** | Permission bits, owner UID/GID, security labels |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Land Registry

Imagine a city's **Land Registry Office** that maintains records of every property:

- The **land itself** (bricks, soil, building) corresponds to the *file's data blocks* stored on disk.
- The **registry entry** for that property — containing the survey number, owner's name, area, registration date, mortgage details, and a unique *property ID* — corresponds to the **inode**.
- The **property ID number** is the **Inode Number (i_ino)**.
- The **address** used by people to locate the property (e.g., "12, MG Road") is the **directory entry (dentry)** — a *human-readable* name that maps *to* the inode number.

Crucially, just as a property can be *referenced* by multiple people (owners, tenants, banks) but has only one registry record, a file can have multiple directory entries (hard links) but is identified by exactly one inode. The registry office (VFS layer) doesn't care whether the land is in Kerala or Karnataka — the records look the same regardless of the actual local land laws (concrete file system).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The inode is **not a directory entry** and it does **not store the filename**. The filename is stored exclusively in the directory entry (dentry) structure, which is a separate VFS object. This distinction is a common KTU exam trap.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Typical on-disk inode size:** **128 bytes** (ext2/ext3) up to **256 bytes** (ext4). Total inodes per filesystem = fixed at format time (default: **1 inode per 2 KB** of disk space in ext4).
- **Inode Number width:** Traditionally **32-bit** (max ~4.29 billion inodes per FS); ext4 supports **64-bit** inode numbers.
- **Default inode cache size (Linux):** dynamically scaled; typically 25% of physical RAM.
- **Maximum filename length (ext4):** **255 bytes**; (VFS abstract limit): **PATH_MAX = 4096 bytes** for full path.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the inode size matters in exams:** When a question asks "What limits the maximum number of files in a Linux filesystem?" the textbook answer is *the number of inodes allocated at format time*, NOT the free disk space. A filesystem can have free space but no free inodes, and vice-versa.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of `struct inode`

The inode object is one of the largest structures in the kernel. Below is a logically grouped decomposition suitable for KTU answers.

### 2.1.1 Identification & Statistics Fields

| Field Name | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `i_ino` | `unsigned long` | Inode number — unique within the file system |
| `i_nlink` | `atomic_t` | **Hard link count** — number of directory entries pointing to this inode |
| `i_uid` / `i_gid` | `kuid_t` / `kgid_t` | **Owner User ID** and **Group ID** |
| `i_mode` | `umode_t` | **File type + permission bits** (e.g., 0644, 0755) |
| `i_size` | `loff_t` | File size in **bytes** |
| `i_atime` | `struct timespec64` | Last **access** time (read) |
| `i_mtime` | `struct timespec64` | Last **modification** time (data write) |
| `i_ctime` | `struct timespec64` | Last **inode change** time (metadata write) |
| `i_blocks` | `loff_t` | File size in **512-byte blocks** (NOT 1 KB!) |
| `i_bytes` | `unsigned short` | Tail bytes in the last partial block |

### 2.1.2 On-Disk Block Mapping Fields

These are the pointers that allow the inode to locate the file's data blocks on disk. They implement the classic **multi-level indexed allocation** scheme.

| Field | Count | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `i_block[0..11]` | 12 entries | **Direct block pointers** — point directly to data blocks |
| `i_block[12]` | 1 entry | **Single-indirect pointer** — points to a block of pointers |
| `i_block[13]` | 1 entry | **Double-indirect pointer** |
| `i_block[14]` | 1 entry | **Triple-indirect pointer** (ext4 only) |

### 2.1.3 Operational Pointers (VFS Method Tables)

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `i_op` | `struct inode_operations *` | Inode-level methods: `create`, `lookup`, `mkdir`, `link`, `unlink`, `symlink`, `rename`, `setattr` |
| `i_fop` | `struct file_operations *` | File-level methods bound to opened file descriptors: `read`, `write`, `open`, `release`, `mmap` |
| `i_sb` | `struct super_block *` | Back-pointer to the **superblock** of the filesystem owning this inode |

### 2.1.4 State, Locking, and Cache Fields

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `i_count` / `i_refcnt` | `atomic_t` | **Reference count** — number of kernel entities holding a pointer |
| `i_state` | `unsigned long` | Bitwise OR of flags: `I_NEW`, `I_DIRTY`, `I_LOCK`, `I_FREEING`, `I_WILL_FREE` |
| `i_hash` | `struct hlist_node` | Linkage in the **inode_hashtable** (for fast lookup by superblock + ino) |
| `i_list` | `struct list_head` | Linkage in the **superblock's inode lists** (dirty / in-use / unused) |
| `i_lock` | `struct rw_semaphore` | Protects most inode fields from concurrent access |
| `i_mutex` / `i_rwsem` | `struct mutex` / `rw_semaphore` | Serializes filesystem-specific operations on the inode |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design — The Object-Oriented VFS Model

The VFS layer treats every filesystem object as an **abstract base class** with *inheritance* implemented in C through **operation tables** (function pointer structs). This is how the same `open()` system call works on ext4, FAT, NFS, and procfs:

1. The user calls `open("/etc/passwd", O_RDONLY)`.
2. The VFS layer **resolves the path**, walking dentry objects to reach the target.
3. The target's `inode->i_fop->open` is invoked.
4. For ext4, this is `ext4_file_open`; for FAT, it is `fat_file_open`; the VFS layer never needs to know which.

The inode is therefore the **object** whose *virtual method table* (`i_op` and `i_fop`) dispatches the correct concrete implementation. This is pure polymorphism in kernel C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 14-mark answers:** Always draw the relationship diagram showing: *User Process → File Descriptor Table → File Object (struct file) → Dentry → Inode → Superblock → Concrete Filesystem Driver*.

## 2.3 Inode Lifecycle — The Six States

An inode progresses through well-defined states managed by the kernel:

1. **Unallocated** — On disk only; no in-memory copy exists. The inode number is reserved.
2. **Allocated on disk** — File exists; metadata is persisted. Still no in-memory copy.
3. **In-core (in-use)** — Kernel has loaded the inode into memory; it is on the `s_inodes` list and has `i_state` clear (or `I_DIRTY_SYNC`).
4. **In-core (dirty)** — `I_DIRTY` / `I_DIRTY_SYNC` / `I_DIRTY_PAGES` set; changes must be flushed to disk.
5. **In-core (unused / clean)** — Not referenced by any file descriptor; eligible for eviction. On `s_inode_unused`.
6. **Freed** — Returned to the inode slab allocator; on-disk copy may be unlinked.

> [!NOTE]
> The state transitions are controlled by three key functions: `iget()` / `iget_locked()` (load), `mark_inode_dirty()` (mark), `write_inode()` / `sync_inode()` (flush), `iput()` (release), `clear_inode()` (destroy).

## 2.4 Inode Cache (Inode Hash Table)

To avoid repeatedly reading the same inode from disk, the kernel maintains a **global inode hash table** keyed on the pair `(superblock pointer, inode number)`.

### 2.4.1 Hashing Formula

$$
H = \text{hash}(sb\_ptr, i\_no) \mod \text{inhash\_size}
$$

- **`inhash_size`** is a compile-time constant (typically 8192 or larger, scaled with RAM).
- Collisions are resolved by **chaining** (each hash bucket is a doubly linked list).
- Lookup uses `iget()` → `find_inode()` → `__find_inode_locked()`.
- Insertion uses `inode_init_owner()`, `__insert_inode_hash()`.

### 2.4.2 Inode Slab Allocator

Inodes are allocated from a dedicated **slab cache** named `inode_cachep` (created by `alloc_inode()` and `inode_init_once()` in `fs/inode.c`). This cache:

- Pre-allocates `struct inode` objects to avoid `kmalloc` fragmentation.
- Initializes default fields once via constructor `inode_init_once`.
- Allows rapid allocation (`alloc_inode`) and freeing (`destroy_inode`).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max file size with 12 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect (4 KB blocks, 4-byte pointers) | $B = (12 + 1024 + 1024^2) \times 4096$ bytes $\approx \mathbf{16 \text{ GB}}$ | ext2/ext3 classic |
| 2 | Max file size ext4 (4 KB block, 4-byte pointer, triple-indirect) | $B_{max} = (12 + 2^{30} + 2^{20} + 2^{10}) \times 4 \text{ KB} \approx \mathbf{16 \text{ TB}}$ | with 64-bit i_size |
| 3 | Max inodes per ext2/3 FS | $I_{max} = 2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ | 32-bit `i_ino` |
| 4 | Max inodes per ext4 FS | $I_{max} = 2^{32} \cdot 2^{32} = 2^{64}$ | 64-bit addressing |
| 5 | File size in blocks (as stored) | $B = \left\lceil \frac{\text{file\_size\_bytes}}{512} \right\rceil$ | Note: 512 not 1024 |
| 6 | Direct addressable bytes (12 direct) | $D = 12 \times B_{size}$ | $B_{size}$ = block size |
| 7 | Single-indirect addressable bytes | $S = \left(\frac{B_{size}}{P_{size}}\right) \times B_{size}$ | $P_{size}$ = pointer size (4 or 8 B) |
| 8 | Reference count required to keep inode alive | `i_count > 0` | `iput()` decrements; frees at 0 |
| 9 | Hash table bucket index | `bkt = ((unsigned long)sb + i_ino) & (inhash_size - 1)` | Power-of-two table size |
| 10 | Hard link max for one inode | $L_{max} = 65535$ | `i_nlink` is 16-bit (ext2) |

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark numerical questions, **memorize the ext2/ext3 max file size formula**:
> $(12 + 1024 + 1024^2) \times \text{Block Size}$
> Block size variants: 1024 B → 16 GB, 2048 B → 32 GB, 4096 B → 64 GB (with double-indirect only).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum File Size in ext2/ext3 with Double-Indirect Addressing

**Given Parameters (Standard ext2 Configuration):**
- Block size $B_{size} = 4096$ bytes $= 2^{12}$ bytes
- Pointer size $P_{size} = 4$ bytes $= 2^{2}$ bytes
- Number of direct block pointers $N_{dir} = 12$
- One single-indirect pointer
- One double-indirect pointer
- 64-bit file size limit (effective for calculation purposes)

**Step 1 — Compute the number of pointers that fit in a single block.**

A single block is used to store an array of pointers when implementing indirect addressing. The number of pointers per block is:

$$
N_{ptr} = \frac{B_{size}}{P_{size}} = \frac{2^{12}}{2^{2}} = 2^{10} = 1024
$$

**Step 2 — Bytes reachable through 12 direct pointers.**

Each of the 12 direct pointers points directly to a data block. Total bytes:

$$
D = N_{dir} \times B_{size} = 12 \times 2^{12} = 12 \times 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes} \approx 48 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Bytes reachable through the single-indirect pointer.**

The single-indirect block contains 1024 pointers, each pointing to a data block:

$$
S = N_{ptr} \times B_{size} = 2^{10} \times 2^{12} = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Bytes reachable through the double-indirect pointer.**

A double-indirect block contains 1024 pointers, each pointing to a single-indirect block. Each of those contains 1024 pointers to data blocks:

$$
T = N_{ptr} \times N_{ptr} \times B_{size} = 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{12} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Total maximum file size.**

$$
F_{max} = D + S + T
$$

$$
F_{max} = (12 \times 2^{12}) + (2^{10} \times 2^{12}) + (2^{20} \times 2^{12})
$$

$$
F_{max} = 2^{12} \times (12 + 2^{10} + 2^{20})
$$

$$
F_{max} = 2^{12} \times (12 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576)
$$

$$
F_{max} = 2^{12} \times 1{,}049{,}612
$$

$$
F_{max} = 4096 \times 1{,}049{,}612 = 4{,}299{,}210{,}752 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to GB.**

$$
F_{max} = \frac{4{,}299{,}210{,}752}{2^{30}} = \frac{4{,}299{,}210{,}752}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 4.004 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the classical ext2/ext3 ceiling of **4 GB** per file. ext4 removes this limit by introducing the **triple-indirect pointer** (`i_block[14]`), raising the limit to **16 TB** with 4 KB blocks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Inode Bit-Map Position Calculation

**Given:**
- A block group number $G$ (0-indexed)
- An inode number $N$ (1-indexed, starting from 1; root inode is typically 2)
- Inodes per block group $I_{g}$
- Inode bit-map block number for the group $B_{g}$

**Step 1 — Determine the block group containing the inode.**

Since inode numbers are allocated sequentially per group:

$$
G = \left\lfloor \frac{N - 1}{I_{g}} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the local index within the group's inode table.**

$$
L = (N - 1) \mod I_{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert local index to bit-map bit position.**

The bit-map is a sequence of bytes. To find the byte offset $B$ and bit position $b$:

$$
B = \left\lfloor \frac{L}{8} \right\rfloor
$$

$$
b = L \mod 8
$$

The bit at position $b$ of byte $B$ indicates the allocation state (1 = allocated, 0 = free).

**Worked Example (ext2):** Suppose $N = 100$, $I_{g} = 8192$, and $B_0 = 1000$.

$$
G = \left\lfloor \frac{100 - 1}{8192} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 0.01208 \right\rfloor = 0
$$

$$
L = (100 - 1) \mod 8192 = 99
$$

$$
B = \left\lfloor \frac{99}{8} \right\rfloor = 12
$$

$$
b = 99 \mod 8 = 3
$$

Therefore, the inode is described by the 4th bit (0-indexed) of the 13th byte of the inode bit-map located in block 1000 of the file system.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Inode vs Other VFS Objects

| Property | Inode | Dentry | File | Superblock |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Represents** | A file (data + metadata) | A name in a path | An open file instance | A mounted filesystem |
| **Unique Key** | `i_ino` within `i_sb` | Path component name | File descriptor (per process) | Mount point |
| **Created When** | File is created on disk | Path is resolved | `open()` is called | Filesystem is mounted |
| **Destroyed When** | Last link removed + `i_count`=0 | Cache eviction (LRU) | `close()` syscall | `umount()` syscall |
| **Stores Name?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No (stores label) |
| **Stores Data?** | ❌ No (data is in blocks) | ❌ No | ❌ No (just offset) | ❌ No (just metadata) |
| **Holds Blocks?** | ✅ Yes (block pointers) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (free block bitmap) |
| **Caching Structure** | Inode hash table | Dentry hash + LRU | File descriptor table | Mounted FS list |
| **Counters** | `i_count`, `i_nlink` | `d_count` (refcount) | `f_count` | `s_count` |
| **Number per file system** | Equal to inodes allocated | Variable (cache-bound) | Equal to active opens | Exactly one per mount |

Notice how the `File Object` (per-open instance), the `Dentry` (path-name resolution), and the `Superblock` (mounted filesystem) all connect *into* the inode. ## 4.3 Inode State Transition Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unallocated : Format FS
    Unallocated --> AllocatedOnDisk : Create file mkfs
    AllocatedOnDisk --> InCoreInUse : iget or iget_locked
    InCoreInUse --> InCoreDirty : write modifies metadata
    InCoreDirty --> InCoreInUse : sync writeback complete
    InCoreInUse --> InCoreUnused : iput and refcount zero
    InCoreDirty --> InCoreUnused : iput then reclaim
    InCoreUnused --> InCoreInUse : iget reacquire
    InCoreUnused --> Freed : evict and clear_inode
    Freed --> AllocatedOnDisk : reused for new file
    AllocatedOnDisk --> [*] : Last hard link removed

    note right of InCoreInUse : i_state clear
    note right of InCoreDirty : I_DIRTY flag set
    note left of InCoreUnused : On s_inode_unused LRU list
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Examiners:** The `I_DIRTY_*` flags in `i_state` are the mechanism by which the kernel decides which inodes to flush during `sync()`, `fsync()`, or background writeback. The state machine is a recurring topic in KTU OS papers.

## 4.4 Inode Hash Table Lookup Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[System Call: open path] --> B[VFS Path Lookup]
    B --> C[Hash Computation\n key = sb plus i_ino]
    C --> D{Hash Bucket Exists?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Walk hlist Chain]
    D -->|No| F[Allocate new inode\n via iget_locked]
    E --> G{Inode Match?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Increment i_count]
    G -->|No| F
    H --> I[Return Inode to VFS]
    F --> J[Read from disk\n via superblock read_inode]
    J --> K[Initialize i_state = I_NEW]
    K --> L[Insert into hash table]
    L --> I
```
